question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the impact of Emergency Department overcrowding on patient safety at Causeway Area Hospital.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department ensures coordination with other public bodies when changes to a Universal Credit claim result in reassessments across multiple systems.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to increase social care provision in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to reduce delays in discharging patients from Causeway Area Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the impact of delayed discharges within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust on patient flow through the Emergency Department at Causeway Area Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of ambulance handover times at Causeway Area Hospital, including the impact on ambulance availability across the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether any consideration has been given by her Department to introducing any increase in firearms licensing fees on a phased basis.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to ensure stability of support for claimants with disabilities, particularly where interruptions to payments may have wider impacts on health or housing.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been made of how Universal Credit supports claimants with disabilities who enter further or higher education as a pathway towards employment.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department has given to the administrative burden placed on claimants with disabilities when required to repeatedly re-engage with Universal Credit processes.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any notice provided to claimants in advance of a Universal Credit claim being closed, following a six-month nil payment period.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has given to (i) recent policy developments in England in relation to allergy safety in schools; and (ii) introducing similar measures in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how the proposed firearms licensing fees in Northern Ireland compare with those in other UK jurisdictions and Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has undertaken of the impact of proposed firearms licensing fee increases on rural communities and farm businesses.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration her Department has given to the role of firearms as an essential tool within agriculture, including for vermin control and livestock protection, in developing proposals to increase firearms licensing fees.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any consideration her Department has given to the impact of proposed firearms licensing fee increases on individuals who hold a personal protection weapon; and (ii) whether any mitigations or exemptions are being considered.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any assessment has made of the implications of the proposed staff reduction scheme at Ulster University for regional campuses, including Coleraine.

To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has made any assessment of whether current arrangements for managing severe allergies in schools adequately support equal access to education for affected pupils.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any mandatory training currently required for school staff in recognising and responding to anaphylaxis.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) current guidance provided to schools on the use of adrenaline auto-injectors; and (ii) plans to update this guidance.
To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has considered introducing statutory guidance for schools on the management of severe allergies and anaphylaxis.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the requirement for drivers of funeral limousines to hold a Public Service Vehicle licence is being considered as part of the review of Class C taxi licensing.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any steps her Department is taking to progress changes to the licensing requirements for funeral limousines; and (ii) whether this work will be completed within the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department is taking to ensure the long-term sustainability of regional higher education provision, including the Coleraine campus of Ulster University.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether pupils who require a smartphone to operate medically necessary diabetes technology should be permitted to keep that device on their person during the school day; and (ii) any guidance issued to schools on related alarms, monitoring, and emergency intervention.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of higher education funding models in other UK jurisdictions including how this is informing the development of a revised funding model in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any plans her Department has following the Financial Needs Assessment of the higher education sector; and (ii) when she expects to bring forward proposals for a revised funding model.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether any assessment has been made of the impact of reported shortages of Flixotide Evohalers on veterinary care; and (ii) any steps he is taking, including with the Department of Health and pharmaceutical suppliers, to ensure continuity of supply for animals requiring this medication.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps the Housing Executive takes to balances the need to support tenants with complex needs with the need to protect other residents from ongoing antisocial behaviour.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any preventative measures being developed, alongside enforcement powers, to reduce persistent anti-social behaviour.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department is working with (i) the PSNI; and (ii) local councils, to ensure a coordinated response to antisocial behaviour.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been made of the time taken by the Housing Executive to resolve persistent antisocial behaviour cases.

To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the development of the legislative proposals to reform anti-social behaviour law, following the joint Department of Justice and Department for Communities consultation.
answer_text: Responses to a joint DoJ and Department for Communities (DfC) public consultation on ASB legislation, including issues of street drinking, were published in April 2025 and suggested a need for legislative reform. In relation to the issues of drinking in public and the possibility of granting the police additional powers to tackle ASB, I have been in direct communication with the Chief Constable and the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, whilst my officials have engaged with  other stakeholders seeking views on proposals developed with DfC exploring the potential of powers allowing for the seizure and disposal of open and closed containers of alcoholic drinks in designated areas and in certain public order situations. As drinking in public is a cross-cutting issue, my Department and DfC will continue to work in conjunction with the PSNI and other key partners to determine next steps towards identifying a proportionate and workable solution.As was detailed in the consultation report further work has been, and will be, required with respect to any potential amendment to Anti-Social Behaviour Orders (ASBOs). Initially this was taken forward by an ASB Working Group comprising of statutory partners together with representatives from the community and voluntary sectors. My officials are now considering all the issues raised throughout this exercise and the recommendations of the working group to inform the final direction of the ASBO. My Department has also committed to conducting an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) on this and all proposals brought forward by the ASB Working Group with a view to bringing forward legislation in the next Assembly mandate, subject to Executive and Assembly approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any progress made in delivering the previously announced support package for households impacted by rising fuel and home heating oil costs; and (ii) when payments will be issued.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration she has given to introducing broader-based energy or fuel support for households who do not qualify for means-tested benefits, but are experiencing financial pressure due to rising fuel costs.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any contingency planning is in place to ensure access to healthcare services, including ambulance provision and hospital appointments, is maintained during planned fuel price protests.
answer_text: Healthcare Services in Northern Ireland have the ability to deal with a range of emergencies or potential emergencies through an ongoing process of assessment, planning, testing and review. This aligns with the Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework structures for Prepare, Respond and Recover. This preparation includes planning for unexpected, short-term emergencies and longer-term ‘rising tide’ events. Part of this preparation is to have robust Business Continuity Plans to maintain critical business as usual services.In line with the procedures for alerting agencies in the Respond Phase (NI Civil Contingencies Framework) the Department of Health and HSC Trusts attended the Local Impact Assessment Call facilitated by the Regional Resilience Team on 10th April 2026. This provided an updated assessment of the intelligence surrounding the proposed fuel protests, their potential locations, duration and scale.As an outcome of this assessment call, PSNI established a Strategic Co-ordinating Group and three local Tactical Co-ordinating Groups to share the latest assessments and information on the proposed fuel protests with partners. These provided a Strategic overview and local impact assessments.Departmental staff and NIAS were represented on the Strategic Co-ordinating Group and NIAS and the appropriate Trust were represented on the geographical Tactical Co-ordinating Groups located in Belfast, Ballymena and Newry. These groups met at regular intervals from 12th April to 14th April 2026. This multi-agency partnership working is critical to HSC preparedness and response.Receiving the latest briefings on the proposed fuel protests allowed Trusts to assess the potential impacts on scheduled or unscheduled services and staffing/resourcing because of the proposed protests. Agreed messaging was shared on various platforms across agencies, internally and externally.With these measures in place, Trusts were able to manage business as usual and to be prepared to activate business continuity measures or escalate any impacts as required. There were no reported impacts on services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any additional financial support her Department will bring forward to assist (i) businesses; and (ii) the farming sector, affected by rising fuel costs.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with the UK Government regarding temporary reductions in fuel-related taxation, including excise duties, to reduce costs for consumers.

To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been carried out of the impact of the Education Maintenance Allowance on (i) educational attainment at Key Stage 5; and (ii) progression from Key Stage 4 to Key Stage 5.
answer_text: The policy in respect of the Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) is a joint policy between my Department and the Department for the Economy and is administered on behalf of both Departments by the Student Loans Company (SLC). EMA is intended to support participation in postcompulsory education by young people from lowincome backgrounds. While many recipients will be studying at Key Stage 5, eligibility for EMA is determined by age, household income and fulltime enrolment on an eligible academic or vocational course up to and including Level 3, rather than by Key Stage.No assessment has been undertaken to determine the impact of EMA on educational attainment at Key Stage 5, or on progression from Key Stage 4 to Key Stage 5. However, work is progressing on plans to require young people to remain in education or training until age 18 and the role and impact of the EMA scheme will be revisited in the context of the crossdepartmental thematic work supporting the 14–19 strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to improve school attendance among pupils entitled to free school meals prior to Key Stage 5.
answer_text: Regular pupil attendance at school is an ongoing challenge which has been significantly impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic and, more recently, by what is referred to as Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance. Subsequently, my Department is developing a new Pupil Attendance Strategy to support all children and young people to attend school every day. The public consultation on the draft strategy concluded on 6 March, and officials are currently considering all responses and preparing recommendations for my consideration. The final Attendance Matters strategy will be published as soon as possible thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department is taking to address underperformance and low educational attainment at Key Stages 1 to 3.
answer_text: Addressing low educational attainment is central to the Department’s TransformED Strategy, which provides a coherent, systemwide approach to improvement based on the evidence that highquality teaching has the greatest impact, particularly for pupils experiencing educational disadvantage.TransformED focuses on early intervention, equity, and excellence in teaching.  It prioritises literacy, numeracy and inclusion, supported by clearer curriculum expectations, improved assessment practice and strengthened professional learning in order to raise standards and reduce the attainment gap.  Fundamental reviews of the curriculum, statutory assessment and external qualifications have been completed and are now moving into implementation. I have very recently published my formal response to the Independent Review of Assessment alongside a new policy framework for statutory assessment in primary schools and at Key Stage 3 which will provide lowstakes, formative assessment to help teachers identify learning gaps earlier and tailor support more effectively.The TransformED delivery plan also provides targeted support for schools where outcomes can be improved, including enhanced leadership capacity, evidenceinformed interventions and better use of systemlevel data. It includes action to strengthen legislation to ensure the independent inspection process operates as intended, safeguarding this key accountability mechanism that evaluates the quality of teaching and learning, supports effective selfevaluation, identifies schools that would benefit from additional support, and shares effective practice across the system.  Taken together, TransformED aims to raise consistency and quality across the system, support schools serving disadvantaged communities, and ensure underachievement is identified and addressed early, enabling more children to make strong progress through the foundational stages of education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to address the underrepresentation of pupils entitled to free school meals in grammar schools.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any arrangements in place to monitor and evaluate the effectiveness of measures implemented by individual schools to address underperformance among disadvantaged pupils.
answer_text: In recent years, the absence of consistent pupillevel data at Key Stages 1 to 3 has limited the Department’s ability to assess attainment at system level. To address this, the Department has introduced systemlevel sample assessments to provide reliable nationallevel evidence on attainment. Pupils sat these low workload, low stakes tests in March.  The data currently being analysed will help identify trends, strengths and areas for improvement, informing future policy and targeted support.The Independent Review of Assessment set out a longerterm approach to reform. On 15 April 2026, the Department published its formal response alongside a new Policy Framework for Statutory Assessment. The new Framework establishes a clear assessment pathway from Year 1 to Year 10, supported by short, curriculumaligned assessments, providing consistent information on pupils for schools and parents. The new system will be less high-stakes, reflecting the Department’s commitment to publishing system-level data only, and will support greater equity, inclusion and fairness.  A stronger emphasis on formative assessment will help teachers identify gaps in learning earlier - particularly in literacy and numeracy - and put targeted support in place.  This is especially important for children from disadvantaged backgrounds.  More reliable systemlevel information will also enable the Department to better target policy and interventions to reduce the attainment gap.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the effectiveness of recent road safety advertising campaigns, including any evidence of changes in driver behaviour.
answer_text: It is globally recognised that three elements working together are required to improve road safety and help reduce the number of people killed or seriously injured in road traffic collisions. These elements which are commonly referred to as the three E’s are Engineering, Education, and Enforcement; my Department’s road safety campaigns contribute to the education element of this fundamental frameworkStatistics tell us that over 95% of collisions are caused by human error, this statement refers to the most recent five-year period (2020-2024).  This being the case, my Department’s road safety campaigns play a significant role in influencing road user behaviour contributing to a reduction of death and serious injuries on roads here.In the past 3 years, the Department has produced and launched 4 major advertising campaigns that have either been evaluated with independent tracking research or have been scheduled for this evaluation: Careless Driving – “Driver or Passenger” – 85% of those surveyed agreed the advertising made them reconsider the consequences of careless driving, while 89% said it made them think about their own behaviour. 71% of people who were aware of the campaign reported having taken action, e.g. driving more attentively. Drug Driving – 76% of people aware who were aware of the campaign reported the campaign challenged their thinking on driving under the influence of drugs. The campaign also significantly increased awareness of Drug driving as a problem in the north and of police testing drivers for drugs. Tracking research is scheduled for the new “Control or Speed” and “Priorities List” campaigns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure that road safety advertising campaigns are trauma-informed, particularly for those who may have been affected by road traffic collisions.
answer_text: All TV advertisements go through the Broadcast Clearance process with Clearcast. During this process, Clearcast provides instructions as to the time of day an advertisement is permitted to be broadcast. Some TV advertisements receive instructions to be broadcast all day, some after 7.30pm, and the more graphic advertisements after 9pm, to take account audience age. The Department always complies with these instructions. Advertisements online are targeted to adults only in the north of Ireland, unless the content is intended for a younger audience. Cinema advertising receives age classifications, which again the department always complies with. Road safety messages are relevant to all, including those families affected by road traffic collisions. However, if anyone finds a particular campaign traumatic because of their own personal circumstances if they contact my officials within the Road Safety Promotion team, we can make them aware of the TV schedule so that they can avoid the campaign.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any consideration her Department has given to the timing and placement of road safety advertisements across television and online platforms; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made of the impact the advertisements have on children and young viewers.
answer_text: All TV advertisements go through the Broadcast Clearance process with Clearcast. During this process Clearcast provides instructions as to the time of day an advertisement is permitted to be broadcast. Some TV advertisements receive instructions to be broadcast all day, some after 7.30pm, others are permitted all day except around children’s programme and the more graphic advertisements after 9pm, so as to take account of audience age. My Department always complies with these instructions. Advertisements online are targeted to adults only in the North of Ireland, unless the content is intended for a younger audience. Cinema advertising receives age classifications, which again my department always complies with.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost of road safety advertising campaigns, in the last five years, broken down by media type.
answer_text: Various platforms have been utilised for road safety advertising campaigns, including, TV, Radio, Online/social media, Cinema and Outdoor.
