question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance for healthcare professionals on involving family members or carers in the care of patients presenting with mental health concerns, particularly where there is a risk of self-harm or suicide.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any training or guidance that is provided to healthcare professionals to support decision-making on when it is appropriate to involve family members or carers in cases where a patient may be at risk of self-harm or suicide.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to ensure a consistent approach to the delivery of mental health services across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Action 31 within the Mental Health Strategy commits to the development of a Regional Mental Health Service, operating across the five HSC Trusts, with professional leadership across the region that is responsible for consistency in service delivery and development. The intention is to achieve these objectives by putting appropriate structures in place rather than altering existing organisational boundaries. A key priority for the RMHS is the development of a blueprint for a regionally consistent community mental health service model, addressing existing unwarranted variation in provision and further expanding area and local collaboratives to enable local providers to better collaborate to address mental needs within communities.  A significant scoping exercise is currently underway to establish a baseline of existing community mental health provision across all Trusts.  The findings from this exercise will inform the components of the developed blueprint.  To inform this work, the RMHS team has undertaken a programme of engagement with Trusts. Whilst the initial focus has been on secondary care, the team is now also engaging with both primary and secondary care stakeholders to gather input and perspectives.  It is anticipated that the blueprint will be developed by Autumn 2026.  The RMHS also has an appointed a Regional Service User Consultant, to ensure that the views of service users and carers are proactively sought, consistently represented and incorporated into the decision-making processes. Working Together: A Pathway for Children and Young People through CAMHS (2018) sets out the standards that are applied regardless of whatever level of service is required in response to children and young people presenting with emotional and mental health difficulties.  The Pathway describes the services available within Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services, (CAMHS) for all children and young people up to the age of 18 and uses the stepped care model as an operational framework designed to ensure that young people gain access to services aligned to assessed need and delivered in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has made of the adequacy of mental health support for young people identified as being at risk of suicide.
answer_text: There are a wide range of issues that may increase the risk of later suicidal behaviour. This can include bullying, body image, eating disorders, exam stress, abuse and adverse childhood experiences. A wide range of policies and programmes are in place to address these issues.The National Confidential Inquiry into Suicide and Homicide recommends that agencies that work with children and young people (particularly those in the health, social care, youth justice and education sectors) can contribute to suicide prevention by recognising the pattern of cumulative risks and stress that lead to a suicide attempt. Improved recognition of these features by families and children themselves would also enhance suicide prevention. A number of actions within the Protect Life 2 Strategy are targeted at younger age groups including: suicide prevention training for teachers, youth workers and sports coaches; emotional resilience building and responding to critical incidents in schools; the schools counselling service; and child focused bereavement services.Under the Protect Life 2 Strategy the Public Health Agency currently fund the Self Harm Intervention Programme (SHIP) service which is available for anyone aged 11+ who has self-harmed and have needs at Step 2 of the You in Mind care pathway. Health and Social Care Trusts provide services for those requiring support at Step 3 and Step 4.  Referrals are made to SHIP by HSC Trusts, and mental health services through a formal referral pathway.In recognition of data in relation to higher rates of self-harm recorded in younger women, the Public Health Agency (PHA) has scheduled a programme of work to develop a pilot project for young women who have self-harmed or who are at risk of self-harm for the 2026/27 year, subject to funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any consideration has been given to introducing a system whereby patients engaging with mental health services can nominate a support person to be involved in their care.
answer_text: Under the current partial implementation of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, there is no universal statutory system for a nominated support person.  However, the Act does include provision for Nominated Persons, which will apply more broadly on full implementation, particularly in relation to serious interventions, enabling individuals to formally nominate someone to support and represent their views.HSC trust give consideration to the involvement of nominated support persons to be involved with patients engaging with mental health services including:Mental health services adopt a person-centred approach. Through routine assessment and care planning, practitioners seek - where appropriate and with consent - to identify and involve informal supports such as family members, carers, or other trusted individuals.Patients participate within the ‘You in Mind Pathway’ which focuses on personalised, collaborative and recovery orientated support. Within this, a partnership approach is encouraged, and all patients are invited to involve support persons such as family/friends. Carers assessments are also offered/provided to these nominated individuals to ensure they too are adequately supported throughout this process to optimise outcomes for both the patient and the nominated individual. HSC Trusts support this via the Northern Ireland Protect Life 2 Strategy (PLIG2 Strategy) and adoption of the Share Guidelines:	SHARE Guidelines Northern Ireland | Department of HealthInpatient mental health treatment will be actively involved in their plan of care.  Where possible, with consent of the patient, next of kin/carers/nominated others are involved in providing collateral history, updates during treatment and in preparing for discharge.  staff work in partnership with advocacy services and encourage patients to seek support, especially if they do not have an identified nominated person.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out any assessment of the impact on children and families of any potential reduction or withdrawal of funding for the TinyStart programme, delivered by TinyLife.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026/27 have not yet been set by the Executive, I am therefore not presently in a position to provide any commitments or timescales to funding for the TinyStart programme. Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026/27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures. On that basis, no formal assessments of the impacts on children and families of any potential reduction or withdrawal of funding has been undertaken at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail a timeline for the laying of legislation on the sale and supply of puppies and kittens.
answer_text: My officials are currently drafting the necessary legislation to provide for the new requirements for sale and supply of puppies and kittens in Northern Ireland.It is important that local councils, which will be legally responsible for enforcing the legislation, have sufficient opportunity to prepare for implementing the new requirements. Officials therefore continue to engage with councils and remain open to their input to ensure the legislation can be executed as intended. This work will help inform appropriate timelines.I remain committed to introducing legislation to protect puppies and kittens for sale in Northern Ireland, at the earliest opportunity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) an update on his decision regarding funding for the TinyStart programme delivered by TinyLife; and (ii) when a final decision is expected.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026/27 have not yet been set by the Executive, I am therefore not presently in a position to provide any commitments or timescales to funding for the TinyStart programme. Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026/27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures. On that basis, no formal assessments of the impacts on children and families of any potential reduction or withdrawal of funding has been undertaken at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any (i) assessment has been made of the condition and remaining operational lifespan of Holywell Hospital; and (ii) risk assessment has been undertaken in relation to a potential gap in inpatient mental health provision in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust in event it becomes unfit for purpose prior to the opening of the Birch Hill mental health facility.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the impact on staff retention at Causeway Hospital following the proposed transfer of inpatient mental health beds from the Ross Thomson Unit, including any measures being taken to address this.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when a decision will be made regarding emergency general surgery provision at Causeway Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether any assessment has been made of the capacity of community mental health services in the Causeway area to meet increased demand following the proposed transfer of inpatient beds from the Ross Thomson Unit; and (ii) to detail any additional resources that will be provided to support this transition.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to introducing a more graduated, or tapered, approach to financial support, to address potential issues faced by households whose income places them just above eligibility thresholds.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any specific measures within the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which will address the needs of low to middle income working households, who are not in receipt of benefits, but are experiencing financial hardship.
answer_text: The Executive’s draft Anti-Poverty Strategy aims to address poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage by means of a joined up, cross-departmental, long-term approach.Addressing the needs of low to middle income working households, who are not in receipt of benefits is a key priority for me. The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy contains a number of interventions which will help a wide range of people experiencing financial hardship. These include supports to help people increase income, address debt or other issues which impact on their lives and increase their incomes. Some of the most relevant commitments, which are delivered by Departments across the Executive, include: Working with delivery partners to scope an NI Debt Respite Scheme. Working with the Money and Pension Service to implement a local delivery plan for the UK Financial Wellbeing Strategy.Continuing to deliver the Extended Schools Programme which provides a range of support in the form of breakfast clubs, after school activities, parental engagement, literacy and numeracy support, healthy eating etc.Introducing legislation to ensure that school uniforms are affordable for all families.Developing and delivering a Warm Healthy Homes StrategyBringing forward a draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for public consultation. Continuing to support the advice sector to ensure that good quality, free, independent, advice is available to those living in or at risk of poverty. This list is not exhaustive, and final content of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be dependent on budgetary priorities across all departments, and subject to Executive Agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any steps she is taking to address the impact of unadopted developments on homeowners who are unable to sell their properties.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with Executive colleagues regarding financial support, or relief measures, for working households who are not eligible for benefits but are experiencing financial pressure.
answer_text: While any additional funding is welcome, the £17.2 million that the British Government have made available to the Executive in response to the significant rise in global oil prices falls well short of what is required, particularly given the widespread reliance on home heating oil here and the scale of increase in fuel costs to date. I raised the matter with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in person at the F:ISC meeting on the 19 March and I will continue to press the British Government for further support. Given the limited funding provided, in my view this must be focussed on supporting those on the lowest income. Therefore, I have written to the Communities Minister to ask that he brings forward proposals as a matter of urgency to the Executive setting out how he could use this funding to support households impacted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the number of working households, who are not in receipt of benefits, but are experiencing financial hardship due to rising living costs.
answer_text: Whilst I remain committed to ensuring that the Anti-Poverty Strategy is taken forward in the most effective and expeditious way possible, my Department does not hold specific information on households not in receipt of benefits who are experiencing hardship due to rising living costs. My Department publishes the Northern Ireland Poverty and Income Inequality Report, which presents annual estimates of the proportion of people, children, working-age adults and pensioners in Northern Ireland living in poverty, as well as other statistics on household income and income inequality. This report uses data from the Northern Ireland Family Resources Survey and includes a range of low-income poverty indicators including relative poverty, absolute poverty, and combined low income and material deprivation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of rising living costs on low-to-middle income working households, including whether current wage growth is keeping pace with inflation.
answer_text: My Department is closely monitoring the financial pressures facing households across the North.  The Consumer Council NI Household Expenditure Tracker (Northern Ireland Household Expenditure Tracker | Consumer Council) highlights the scale of these pressures and how heavily everyday essentials weigh on family finances. For example, in Q3 2025, the lowest-earning households spent 45.1% of their total basic spending on food and non-alcoholic beverages, housing, electricity, gas and other fuels, and transport. 		Against this backdrop, the latest NISRA labour market statistics shows strong wage growth, with median monthly pay increasing by £43 (1.8%) over the month to £2,493 in February 2026, an annual rise of £189 (8.2%). After accounting for CPI inflation of 3.0%, the real annual growth in median monthly pay was around 5.2%. However, inflationary pressures remain uncertain. The Bank of England has warned that ongoing global energy disruption linked to conflict in the Middle East will place upward pressure on households’ fuel and utility prices. As a result, CPI inflation is expected to rise in the near term.		I recognise that, although real wage growth has been positive, continued inflationary pressures and the risk of further price shocks mean that real household incomes particularly for low-to-middle income working families are likely to remain under strain. 		I will continue to monitor developments closely and press the British Government to take further action to shield consumers and businesses from additional financial pressures as this situation evolves.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his Department’s engagement with health and justice colleagues in Northern Ireland, and with counterparts in England, regarding the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill; and (ii) whether any provisions may inform policy in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware of the Children’s Wellbeing & Schools Bill currently progressing through the Parliamentary stages at Westminster.  The Bill applies to England only and brings forward a broad package of provisions relating to children’s social care and the school system.  While elements of the Bill may provide useful context for future policy development, and officials are engaging with colleagues from across health and justice in terms of relevant areas of commonality, it is important to recognise the distinct legislative framework and differing social care and education structures in place in Northern Ireland.The Bill is relevant to safeguarding and wellbeing in education policy responsibilities, this includes work in respect of increasing online safety intervention, strengthening early identification, information sharing and multi-agency working.  This aligns with our focus on preventative, whole-system approaches where schools play a key role in identifying risk and promoting wellbeing.As part of our ongoing refresh of safeguarding guidance and the wellbeing framework, we will consider relevant elements of the Bill to support greater clarity, consistency and effective practice across schools, ensuring safe and supportive environments for all children and young people. On Children Missing Education (CME), officials are currently reviewing potential legislative gaps and are working to develop comprehensive Departmental guidance on the issue. Engagement has taken place with counterparts in England on CME policy and relevant provisions within the Children’s Wellbeing & Schools Bill.  Addressing CME effectively requires a coordinated, multi-agency approach.  To that end, my officials continue to engage closely with colleagues in the Department of Health and the Education Authority (EA) to explore opportunities for improved information sharing and collaborative action in this policy area.  Work is underway to examine the processes and policies in place across other jurisdictions, with a view to identifying best practice and informing local approaches. My officials have engaged with their counterparts in England about the school uniforms provisions in the Children's Wellbeing and Schools Bill.  The School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Act (Northern Ireland) 2026 came into operation on 20 February 2026.  Engagement with England continues to ensure that there is two-way sharing of information as we implement our legislation and as their Bill works through the Parliamentary process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the 16 to 18 Legislation, to detail (i) his Department's reasoning for proposing that 16 and 17 year olds should not be permitted to choose elective home education; and (ii) whether further consideration has been given to the policy position regarding elective home education for children under the age of 16, referenced in the consultation document.
answer_text: The legislation being developed provides for all young people to continue their education or training for two years following compulsory school age. This can be achieved through education or training in school, FE College, CAFRE or home education; or education in parallel with working or volunteering. The proposed legislation will strengthen the system’s ability to monitor participation and intervene early when a young person disengages. Given the purpose and scope of the Bill, it would not be appropriate to add substantive provisions relating to elective home education at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department would consider using the 16 to 18 Legislation to address safeguarding issues relating to elective home education, identified in the consultation document.
answer_text: The legislation being developed provides for all young people to continue their education or training for two years following compulsory school age. This can be achieved through education or training in school, FE College, CAFRE or home education; or education in parallel with working or volunteering. The proposed legislation will strengthen the system’s ability to monitor participation and intervene early when a young person disengages. Given the purpose and scope of the Bill, it would not be appropriate to add substantive provisions relating to elective home education at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's plans to address safeguarding relating to elective home education, highlighted in the consultation on the 16 to 18 Legislation, including any plans to improve the current practice ahead of potential legislation.
answer_text: The legislation being developed provides for all young people to continue their education or training for two years following compulsory school age. This can be achieved through education or training in school, FE College, CAFRE or home education; or education in parallel with working or volunteering. The proposed legislation will strengthen the system’s ability to monitor participation and intervene early when a young person disengages. Given the purpose and scope of the Bill, it would not be appropriate to add substantive provisions relating to elective home education at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department routinely collects and analyses sex-disaggregated data on cardiovascular diagnosis, treatment and outcomes.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure women are adequately represented in cardiovascular clinical research.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve awareness of cardiovascular disease risk among women.
To ask the Minister of Health whether women diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome are routinely advised of their increased risk of cardiovascular disease.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of potential gender bias in the diagnosis, treatment or referral pathways for cardiovascular disease.
answer_text: There is growing evidence internationally of a systemic gender gap in relation to how cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) have traditionally been diagnosed and treated within healthcare systems. Although this is not yet fully understood, it is now accepted that – while CVDs are more prevalent among men – women can present differently with early symptoms which can be misdiagnosed, resulting in fewer diagnostic investigations, fewer cardiac medications being prescribed and fewer interventions. Studies have shown that women are more likely to be interrupted, have their symptoms psychologised, or be advised to “wait and see”, and that traditional risk factors such as diabetes, cholesterol, age and smoking may have different weights and relevance in men and women. Coupled with the challenge of improving awareness and prevention of cardiovascular risk factors among women and girls, this represents an important issue which must be understood and reflected in clinical education, triage tools and risk assessments in order to mitigate any disparities in diagnosis and treatment.Currently, however, the relevant NICE guidelines on cardiovascular health apply to all adults and do not include specific guidance on diagnostic and treatment disparities affecting women. They focus on population level risk tools, lifestyle risk modification, and treatment escalation. The Northern Ireland Cardiac Network also works to ensure that the entire population has equitable access to highquality cardiac care. My Department will continue to work closely with the Network to identify and implement any improvements that can strengthen existing diagnostic and treatment pathways for women.  My Department has published data on acute, episode-based activity at HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland during the year ending 31 March 2025 at: Hospital Statistics: Acute Episode-Based Activity Statistics 2024/2025. This publication presents information on diagnosis and procedures broken down by several variables including sex, however does not include information on outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any steps her Department is taking to protect consumers in Northern Ireland from volatility in the home heating oil market.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of the ongoing threat level, faced by retired prison officers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I hope the member will appreciate that it would be counterproductive to disclose any information about the security arrangements for serving or retired staff into the public domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has considered reviewing the policy on protective security arrangements for retired prison officers.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service does not disclose any information about the security arrangements for serving or retired staff into the public domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the reason why protective security arrangements, available to serving Northern Ireland Prison Service staff, are not formally extended to retired officers who may continue to face security risks arising from their former role.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service does not disclose any information about the security arrangements for serving or retired staff into the public domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale for the policy, whereby the Northern Ireland Prison Service ceases maintenance or replacement of security equipment, issued to prison officers six months after retirement.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service does not disclose any information about the security arrangements for serving or retired staff into the public domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any consideration was given to pupil visibility and road safety when developing the School Uniform Guidelines, published in February 2026.
answer_text: Claire Sugden MLAParliament Buildings BallymiscawStormont26 March 2026AQW 42174/22-27Claire Sugden MLA has asked:To ask the Minister of Education whether any consideration was given to pupil visibility and road safety when developing the School Uniform Guidelines, published in February 2026.										     AQW 42176/22-27AndTo ask the Minister of Education whether schools are permitted, under the School Uniform Guidelines published in February 2026, to incorporate reflective or high-visibility elements into uniforms where they believe it would improve pupil safety.ANSWERThe answer to both questions is yes.  We can all agree that the safety of our children and young people is of paramount importance.  The School Uniforms Guidelines, which formally issued on 23 February 2026, set out that the Department considers it reasonable for such safety considerations to inform the design of a school uniform policy.  The guidelines use the example of reflective strips on uniform items, but it is open to schools to determine how best to manage this aspect of their uniform design - including any high-visibility or safety element a school considers will be helpful for their pupils.  Paul Givan MLAMinister of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment his Department has made of the safety risks faced by pupils who must walk along rural roads to access school transport, particularly during darker autumn and winter months.
answer_text: Matters relating to the safety of footpaths, road conditions and street lighting fall within the remit of the Minister for Infrastructure. Officials in my Department and the Education Authority are contributing to the work of the Safer Journeys to School Programme which was established by the Minister for Infrastructure to explore and develop measures aimed at reducing risks for children travelling to school. Under the Home to School Transport Policy, parents can be expected to assist with, and accompany their child on, the journey to the bus stop which should be no more than two miles from home for primary school pupils and three miles for post primary school pupils. As responsibility for this part of the journey rests with parents, the Education Authority does not routinely assess these routes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has carried out any assessment of the safety of pupils who must walk along rural roads to access school bus stops, particularly during darker autumn and winter months.
answer_text: Keeping children safe on their journeys to and from school is a priority for my Department. This is reflected in the dedicated Safer Journeys to School Programme, which is taking a wholesystem approach to improving safety.As part of this programme, officials will be examining the safety of rural bus stops and the environments in which children wait for, board and alight from buses, including where factors such as visibility, lighting and road layout may increase risk.A Bus Stop Design Guide was jointly issued by Roads Service and Translink in October 2005 -https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/bus-stop-design-guide-october-2005-internal-017.pdf  This document is currently under review for updating. The current guide covers formal bus stops and is primarily focused on the placement and physical layout of bus stops and guidance for Translink on signage.When assessing requests for rural footway provision / extensions, my Department does take into consideration an assessment of children walking along roads to reach any particular bus stops.  In the past, my Department has provided footways to formal bus stops from a nearby road junction, sometimes as part of a visibility splay improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether schools are permitted, under the School Uniform Guidelines published in February 2026, to incorporate reflective or high-visibility elements into uniforms where they believe it would improve pupil safety.
answer_text: Claire Sugden MLAParliament Buildings BallymiscawStormont26 March 2026AQW 42174/22-27Claire Sugden MLA has asked:To ask the Minister of Education whether any consideration was given to pupil visibility and road safety when developing the School Uniform Guidelines, published in February 2026.										     AQW 42176/22-27AndTo ask the Minister of Education whether schools are permitted, under the School Uniform Guidelines published in February 2026, to incorporate reflective or high-visibility elements into uniforms where they believe it would improve pupil safety.ANSWERThe answer to both questions is yes.  We can all agree that the safety of our children and young people is of paramount importance.  The School Uniforms Guidelines, which formally issued on 23 February 2026, set out that the Department considers it reasonable for such safety considerations to inform the design of a school uniform policy.  The guidelines use the example of reflective strips on uniform items, but it is open to schools to determine how best to manage this aspect of their uniform design - including any high-visibility or safety element a school considers will be helpful for their pupils.  Paul Givan MLAMinister of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any departmental guidance that exists regarding the placement and safety of school bus pick-up and drop-off points, on rural roads.
answer_text: Keeping children safe on their journeys to and from school is a priority for my Department. This is reflected in the dedicated Safer Journeys to School Programme, which is taking a wholesystem approach to improving safety.As part of this programme, officials will be examining the safety of rural bus stops and the environments in which children wait for, board and alight from buses, including where factors such as visibility, lighting and road layout may increase risk.A Bus Stop Design Guide was jointly issued by Roads Service and Translink in October 2005 -https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/bus-stop-design-guide-october-2005-internal-017.pdf  This document is currently under review for updating. The current guide covers formal bus stops and is primarily focused on the placement and physical layout of bus stops and guidance for Translink on signage.When assessing requests for rural footway provision / extensions, my Department does take into consideration an assessment of children walking along roads to reach any particular bus stops.  In the past, my Department has provided footways to formal bus stops from a nearby road junction, sometimes as part of a visibility splay improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to support GP practices to adopt technology that improves appointment management and patient access, including telephone triage and online booking systems.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.  At this point in time it is not mandatory for GP practices to make online appointments available to their patients.  While all practices have the technology available to enable this, it remains their choice in how they manage and deliver their services within their individual practice.  To support GPs in improving service delivery, my Department has issued a best practice guide, ‘Making All Contacts Count’, which includes recommendations on how practices could maximise their telephony and technology to help meet patient demand. I have been clear that improving access to GP services for patients is a key priority for me.  My Department has made available significant funding to help General Practice meet the growing demands for primary care services and enable practices to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and increase staffing levels to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.  In addition, my Department has announced investment of £61m over the next 4 years to support the wider rollout of the Primary Care MDT model which will provide access to a wider range of services in Primary Care and will help stabilise GP services.My Department has commenced negotiations with the NIGPC on the GMS contract for 2026/27. Developing robust measures to improve access to GP services for implementation in 2026/27 will be a core requirement in seeking agreement on the Contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to the barriers some older people may face when accessing GP appointments, including challenges with telephone triage and online booking systems.
answer_text: I recognise that some older people may face challenges when accessing GP services.  This may be due to difficulties using digital options such as online appointment systems, text messaging or virtual consultation, mobility issues, or other needs.  All GP practices are required to comply with the Disability Discrimination Act and make reasonable adjustments to accommodate affected patients, for example, those with mobility issues. GPs can address these requirements based on their in-depth knowledge of the patient’s situation and that of their family support or carers and adjust to recognise any known limitations.  For example, GPs and district nurses will often make home visits to accommodate elderly patients who are unable to attend the practice, including where a patient is unable to manage public transport and no relative is available to assist. For elderly patients who are unfamiliar with digital systems, prescriptions can continue to be ordered by phone or at the reception desk and patients can obtain advice on their complaint by phone or in person, as well as by home visit, where considered appropriate. A range of support is available for individuals with additional communication needs, for example, language translation services, Text Relay for patients who are hearingimpaired, and the use of induction loops.  Improving access to GP services for patients is a key priority for me. My Department has commenced negotiations with the NIGPC on the GMS contract for 2026/27.  Developing robust measures to improve access to GP services for implementation in 2026/27 will be a core requirement in seeking agreement on the Contract. In addition, to support GPs in improving service delivery, my Department has issued a best practice guide, ‘Making All Contacts Count’, which includes recommendations relating to the needs of vulnerable groups, including older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the impact that difficulties accessing GP appointments can have on pressures within Emergency Departments.
answer_text: My Department recognises that difficulties in accessing GP appointments have the potential to influence how patients engage with urgent and emergency care services.  However, at present we have no evidence which demonstrates a direct causal link between access to primary care and increased demand in emergency departments (EDs).Over the last number of years there have been a number of changes to urgent and emergency care services, in particular the development of urgent care centres (UCCs) and minor injuries units (MIUs) which provide alternative and more appropriate services for patients presenting to secondary care with less serious conditions. These changes make direct comparisons for EDs attendances over the last number of years less accurate as they are no longer like-for-like services. However, when the numbers presenting at EDs, UCCs and MIUs are added together, it is possible to make more accurate comparisons in terms of total numbers as shown in the table below.New and unplanned attendances at EDs/ UCCs/ MIUsYearTotal attendances2022/23790,2152023/24800,8992024/25802,495It should be noted that total ED attendances pre-pandemic for 2019/20 were over 815,000, and so the figures for 2024/25 have not yet reached pre-pandemic levels. The Phone First model which was introduced in the last number of years and is now in place across all Trusts, could be a factor in reducing overall attendances.In addition to these new services in secondary care, measures such as the continued development of multi-disciplinary teams and non-medical support roles are intended to improve access and ensure patients receive appropriate care in the community. My Department will continue to monitor this issue as part of the broader urgent and emergency care reform programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department takes to ensure that older people are able to access GP services easily, particularly those who may have mobility issues, limited transport options or difficulty using digital systems.
answer_text: I recognise that some older people may face challenges when accessing GP services.  This may be due to difficulties using digital options such as online appointment systems, text messaging or virtual consultation, mobility issues, or other needs.  All GP practices are required to comply with the Disability Discrimination Act and make reasonable adjustments to accommodate affected patients, for example, those with mobility issues. GPs can address these requirements based on their in-depth knowledge of the patient’s situation and that of their family support or carers and adjust to recognise any known limitations.  For example, GPs and district nurses will often make home visits to accommodate elderly patients who are unable to attend the practice, including where a patient is unable to manage public transport and no relative is available to assist. For elderly patients who are unfamiliar with digital systems, prescriptions can continue to be ordered by phone or at the reception desk and patients can obtain advice on their complaint by phone or in person, as well as by home visit, where considered appropriate. A range of support is available for individuals with additional communication needs, for example, language translation services, Text Relay for patients who are hearingimpaired, and the use of induction loops.  Improving access to GP services for patients is a key priority for me. My Department has commenced negotiations with the NIGPC on the GMS contract for 2026/27.  Developing robust measures to improve access to GP services for implementation in 2026/27 will be a core requirement in seeking agreement on the Contract. In addition, to support GPs in improving service delivery, my Department has issued a best practice guide, ‘Making All Contacts Count’, which includes recommendations relating to the needs of vulnerable groups, including older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve access to GP services, particularly in areas where patients report difficulty obtaining timely appointments.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.  At this point in time it is not mandatory for GP practices to make online appointments available to their patients.  While all practices have the technology available to enable this, it remains their choice in how they manage and deliver their services within their individual practice.  To support GPs in improving service delivery, my Department has issued a best practice guide, ‘Making All Contacts Count’, which includes recommendations on how practices could maximise their telephony and technology to help meet patient demand. I have been clear that improving access to GP services for patients is a key priority for me.  My Department has made available significant funding to help General Practice meet the growing demands for primary care services and enable practices to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and increase staffing levels to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.  In addition, my Department has announced investment of £61m over the next 4 years to support the wider rollout of the Primary Care MDT model which will provide access to a wider range of services in Primary Care and will help stabilise GP services.My Department has commenced negotiations with the NIGPC on the GMS contract for 2026/27. Developing robust measures to improve access to GP services for implementation in 2026/27 will be a core requirement in seeking agreement on the Contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether eligibility criteria for existing energy efficiency or fuel poverty schemes are kept under review, in light of rising home heating oil prices.
answer_text: I am aware that the price of home heating oil has risen considerably in recent weeks. I am very concerned about the potential impact of this on lower income or vulnerable households.My Department leads on energy efficiency policies to help reduce household energy use and lower bills, with support available through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP).NISEP eligibility criteria are set out in a formal Framework Document approved and published by the Utility Regulator for each programme period, most recently for 2025-27 with schemes delivered annually in line with that framework.Officials continue to work closely with the Department for Communities, which has lead responsibility for addressing fuel poverty. Their newly launched Warm Healthy Homes Strategy sets out pathways to support low-income households and improve energy-inefficient homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any monitoring arrangements her Department has in place to (i) track volatility in the home heating oil market; and (ii) assess its impact on consumers.
answer_text: The escalation of conflict in the Middle East has increased volatility in global energy markets.The Consumer Council actively monitors heating oil prices across the North and publishes weekly average prices. This provides consumers with clear, comparable information on prevailing prices, helping them to make informed purchasing decisions and to shop around between suppliers.  The Trading Standards Service works to protect consumers from illegal trading practices, such as unfair pricing, while supporting legitimate businesses. It provides guidance to oil retailers on their obligations and if consumers have a complaint about oil deliveries, they should contact the Department for the Economy’s consumer advice helpline Consumerline on 0300 123 6262 or log on to Consumerline.  I welcome the Competition and Markets Authority’s announcement of a market study into home heating oil. It will serve to strengthen competition, improve transparency and consumer protection, and ensure pricing reflects genuine costs.In recent weeks I have met heating oil, natural gas, National Energy Action, and wider business organisations to understand first-hand what cost pressures are in the market and the impact these are already having on lower income and vulnerable households and businesses.I am meeting regularly with the Ministers from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, Michael Shanks and Martin McCluskey. I continue to press for urgent consumer support, as the £17million confirmed on Monday 16 March is clearly inadequate to help those who are struggling with rising energy prices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department is considering introducing any additional consumer protection measures, in response to rising home heating oil prices.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any assessment her Department has carried out of energy support schemes used elsewhere in the UK; and (ii) whether any UK schemes could be applied in Northern Ireland, especially for households that rely on home heating oil.
answer_text: When the conflict in the Middle East, began, the reduction in electricity bills of around £30 per year had already been announced and delivery was being progressed by my Department.Many of our households and businesses are already under pressure from the cost-of-living crisis, and this new energy shock is compounding those pressures. I am particularly concerned about the impact on low-income and vulnerable households.I have pressed the British government for support and I continue to do so. I spoke to the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero’s Energy Minister, Michael Shanks on 4th March to highlight the impact of the immediate rise in heating oil costs here.I subsequently spoke and wrote to Department for Energy Security and Net Zero’s Energy Consumers Minister, Martin McCluskey about the urgent need for support.The £17 million announced by the British government is clearly inadequate, therefore, it is important that whatever support is available, or becomes available going forward, is targeted at those in most need.I am meeting regularly with the Ministers from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, Michael Shanks and Martin McCluskey, and I will continue to argue for additional support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any recent engagement her Department has had with the Utility Regulator regarding increases in home heating oil prices; and (ii) any actions arising from those discussions.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the impact of recent rises in oil prices on households and businesses here due to the conflict in the Middle East.I met with the Utility Regulator, the Consumer Council, heating oil and natural gas representatives on 5 March to discuss energy prices. While home heating oil is sold in a competitive but unregulated retail market and does not fall within the Utility Regulator’s statutory remit, my Department has ongoing engagement regarding potential impacts on gas and electricity prices.I spoke to the British Minister for Energy Consumers, Martin McCluskey on 14th March, he told me that the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) is conducting a formal study of the oil retail market. This was formally launched on Friday 20th March and is expected to take around 3 months.I met with the CMA on Thursday 19th and assured them my officials will provide data to support this study. I will carefully review any recommendations from the findings of this study.The Consumer Council provides invaluable information for consumers by monitoring heating oil prices and publishing weekly average prices. Consumers are encouraged to use its website to compare suppliers pricing and find information on oil buying clubs and Fuel Stamp schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Department of Justice in relation to funding the PSNI holiday pay liability, as an exceptional pressure.
answer_text: There has been an ongoing engagement between myself and the Justice Minister, as well as my predecessor, on this issue. My officials have also been actively engaging with counterparts in Department of Justice (DoJ), to ensure that my department remains aware of the potential costs that may be incurred.In terms of the funding for the financial settlement DoF will continue to engage with counterparts in DoJ to determine the quantum of funding that is required and the time period in which payments fall due.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any progress made in relation to PSNI holiday pay, including whether the business case has now been advanced to the Department of Finance or the Executive for consideration.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering the PSNI Holiday Pay business case which must satisfy the requisite approvals process, including approval by the Department of Finance. While I cannot give a definitive timeframe at this stage, I want to reassure you that my officials are working closely and constructively with the PSNI to progress the Holiday Pay business case as quickly as possible.  Holiday Pay is an exceptional pressure that is wholly unaffordable under any circumstances within existing or future PSNI and DoJ baseline budget Furthermore, Holiday Pay is an issue that has an impact across all departments and, therefore, a collective solution at Block level will be required.Further engagement will be required with the Executive and the DoF to explore potential funding options when the liability crystallises.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with Executive colleagues regarding the consideration of PSNI holiday pay as a cross-departmental issue, requiring a block-level approach.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has been made of the impact of anti-social behaviour on footfall, business confidence and the evening economy, in town centres, such as Coleraine.
answer_text: Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, play a key role in the Department’s operational response to community safety and ASB across all communities in Northern Ireland. PCSPs liaise with local communities and other key partners, including local statutory bodies/agencies and the voluntary and community sector, to develop appropriate actions to deal with, and reduce, the impact of ASB.To create their 2026-27 Action Plan, Causeway Coast and Glens PCSP carried out a strategic assessment to confirm local priorities. The result of this assessment was Theme One being identified as ASB, with the top four behaviours identified as: Alcohol / Drugs Abuse; Youth causing annoyance; Litter; and Vehicle nuisance.Specific projects  overseen by Causeway Coast and Glens PCSP to tackle issues of ASB include: a graffiti removal scheme; talks at schools; the local Support Hub; events held by PCSP involving Crime Prevention Officers; Neighbourhood Watch Schemes (with specific support groups focusing on ASB); Safer Driving Projects aimed at reducing anti-social driving and; a Grant Scheme providing constituted groups up to £999 to develop localised solutions to ASB.Whilst my Department has not made a specific assessment on the impact of ASB on footfall, business confidence and the evening economy my officials are currently considering ASB legislation with a view to bringing forward amendments to the current framework in the next assembly mandate. You may wish to contact the Causeway Coast and Glens PCSP directly to discuss any issues of particular concern in the context of your question: pcsp@causewaycoastandglens.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the role of the evening economy, including food and hospitality provision, in supporting footfall and business viability in town centres, in East Londonderry.
answer_text: My Department, alongside Tourism NI and the three Belfast Business Improvement Districts, has commissioned Ipsos to conduct research to better understand the issues affecting the night-time economy.		While the final report is anticipated soon, the interim findings suggested that in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area the night-time economy made a significant contribution in terms of Gross Value Added and supporting jobs.		The interim findings also found that Tourism's potential within the night-time economy is significant but underutilised. Promoting unique night-time experiences and addressing access and safety issues could attract more visitors who stay longer and spend more.		A safe, thriving and diverse night-time economy will contribute to footfall and business viability across our urban areas. I remain committed to engaging with stakeholders to achieve this ambition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support increased residential accommodation in town centres, including in the East Londonderry constituency, as a means of improving footfall and sustaining local services in towns such as Coleraine.
answer_text: A key objective of the Housing Supply Strategy is to create thriving, inclusive and sustainable communities by providing a range of housing options which are also located close to services such as employment, recreation, community services and access to public transport. Delivery of the Strategy is intended to supplement the work of Local Councils and the Housing Executive’s place-shaping teams on the development of Local Development Plans.  Through the plan-making process, councils are responsible for establishing necessary zonings and policies to meet specific local spatial growth needs, including opportunities for increased town-centre living.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department has given to the role of parking and accessibility in supporting footfall and town centre regeneration, in towns such as Coleraine and Limavady.
answer_text: My Department has fully considered the role of parking and accessibility in supporting footfall and town centre regeneration in towns such as Coleraine and Limavady.  This is reflected in the Masterplan Reviews completed for both towns in partnership with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council.The 2020 Coleraine Masterplan Review identified parking and accessibility as essential to increasing footfall, and these factors are embedded within the Outline Business Case for the Coleraine Leisure and Wellbeing Centre, a key Causeway Growth Deal project led by my Department.  Similarly, the 2024 Limavady Masterplan Review highlighted parking pressures and the importance of improving accessibility.My Department continues to work closely with the Council to prioritise regeneration projects, ensuring that parking and accessibility considerations are integral to public consultations and the development of all business cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to support high street businesses in the East Londonderry constituency, including measures to improve footfall and long-term sustainability in town centres, such as Coleraine and Limavady.
answer_text: While responsibility for town centre regeneration rests with the Department for Communities and development of local economic policies primarily falls to the relevant district council, my Department and its agencies will work with local partners to ensure that economic development meets local needs and priorities. In March 2022 the Executive Office published its Report with recommendations on the High Street Task Force. My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. This recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Work is progressing to implement this recommendation and includes the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promoting wider adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaborating with the Labour Relations Agency to develop a Good Employment Charter. These measures will underpin improved job quality and workplace standards across local economies, including town centres such as Coleraine and Limavady.My officials are also working with all eleven Councils to develop Local Economic Partnerships to address economic need on a sub-regional scale.  Causeway Coast & Glens Local Economic Partnership’s Action Plan was approved in December with funding of £4.51m to support its delivery through to March 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how safeguarding considerations are reflected in guidance to schools on responding to bullying, particularly where bullying behaviour may be linked to wider emotional distress, or vulnerability.
answer_text: Safeguarding considerations are embedded throughout the guidance provided to schools on responding to bullying through the Education Authority’s Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT).ABSIT’s leadership training and resources place strong emphasis on the integration of safeguarding policy and practice, highlighting the direct link between addressing bullying type behaviour and safeguarding legislation. The training handbook makes clear that safeguarding begins with preventative education, early intervention and support to families, and that the addressingbullying policy is a key part of the wider safeguarding framework required in schools.Schools are guided to use a wide range of safeguardingrelated evidence, including survey results, incident logs, wellbeing data, safeguarding records, attendance patterns, pupil voice activities and parent and staff feedback when reviewing or developing policies. These records are identified as essential for understanding the nature and extent of bullying type behaviour and ensuring appropriate safeguarding responses.ABSIT’s targeted advice reinforces the use of Bullying Concern Assessment Forms so that interventions are codeveloped with pupils and parents, prioritising safeguarding needs and enabling multiagency engagement where required. At Levels 3 and 4, where bullying behaviour may be more sustained or complex, staff are expected to consider the risk to all involved, with cases escalated to senior pastoral, safeguarding and SEN staff for coordinated support.Safeguarding is also built into ABSIT’s policy template and checklist, which emphasise the school’s duty to create a safe, inclusive, and relational environment consistent with statutory measures. Governors are required to maintain a standing safeguarding agenda item for reporting on bullying type behaviour, ensuring governance oversight of both policy and practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any mechanisms in place to review how the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 is being implemented in practice across schools.
answer_text: My Department has a number of mechanisms in place which inform assessment of how the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (The Act) is being implemented in practice and to monitor the statutory guidance issued under the Act.  Since the Act came into operation in 2021, schools are required to record all incidents of bullying, including their motivations and outcomes.  It is the responsibility of the Board of Governors (BoGs) for the development, implementation, monitoring and periodic review of their schools addressing bullying policy.  These duties form a key mechanism through which the Department can assess implementation across schools in Northern Ireland.  The Department issued statutory guidance in March 2021 to principals and BoGs, which supports the implementation of the Act and sets out how schools should fulfil their legal obligations.  Schools are required to keep their addressing bullying polices and preventative measures under regular review, and to maintain transparent and detailed records of all alleged and confirmed bullying incidents, including how these were addressed.  These record-keeping duties allow the Department to monitor compliance with the statutory guidance and provides an oversight of how consistently schools apply the correct procedure and requirements.  Recognising that access to comprehensive data is key to informing postlegislative scrutiny of the Act and effective, evidence-based policy-making, over the last two years months the Department has taken forward a number of measures to improve the bullying-related data to which the Department has access. These measures include the introduction of bullying-related questions in the Northern Ireland School Omnibus Survey, engaging with the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) to review how schools are implementing the Act, and receiving regular Outcome Based Accountability reports from the Education Authority’s (EA) Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT).  The ETI is also undertaking further visits to primary schools to review how strategies for addressing bullying are being implemented, and the Department is working with the EA with the aim of digitising the Bullying Concern Assessment Form through the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) Programme with a view to facilitating access to comprehensive anonymised regional data on schools’ bullying related data.The emerging evidence from the available data sources demonstrates high levels of compliance with the Act and strong evidence of resulting positive impacts.  The Act has clearly helped to provide greater consistency in how all schools respond to bullying incidents and allegations, helping to build on schools’ duty of care for their pupils, and strengthening the protection pupils can expect if they experience bullying type behaviour in school by ensuring all pupils are protected to the same, best practice standards. Together, the mechanisms implemented by the Department e

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department monitors the application of statutory guidance issued under the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
