question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance available to schools on early intervention, in cases of bullying.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA’s) Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) provides clear guidance and support to schools on early intervention in cases of bullying. ABSIT places a strong emphasis on early identification and early intervention. Its introductory training (Tier 1) for all staff includes recognising signs and symptoms of bullying type behaviour and the importance of having structures in place for early reporting. Leadership training (Tier 2) also reinforces early intervention across modules on ‘Effective Prevention and Response’. Schools are further supported through ABSIT’s intervention framework, which ranges from early responses at Level 1 through to Level 4 for more complex or sustained situations, complemented by resources including ‘Effective Responses to Bullying Behaviour’.  A wide range of practical resources is provided to schools to support early intervention, including policy templates and checklists, targeted referral advice, Level 1–4 intervention tables, training materials, rightsbased information leaflets, parental support via the Safer Schools App, template tools such as the Bullying Concern Assessment Form (BCAF), Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing (SBEW) plans and reallife case studies. These are designed to strengthen early action, consistency and best practice.ABSIT supports schools through complex referrals, offering advisory input that may involve multiagency engagement. ABSIT also provides rightsbased guidance for parents, carers and pupils, and works with external partner agencies to ensure that families are signposted to appropriate support in line with statutory and multiagency frameworks. In all of this work, ABSIT reinforces the importance of early intervention when dealing with cases of bullying-type behaviour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance his Department provides to schools on trauma-informed approaches when responding to bullying behaviour.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) provides schools with advice, guidance and training to address concerns related to alleged bullying type behaviour in their setting within a trauma informed framework.Trauma Informed Practice (TIP) is central to ABSIT’s training programme and their advisory model, contributing to effective safeguarding through advocating preventative measures and early intervention. ABSIT support is developed on six foundational pillars which promote and inform effective prevention and response to bullying type behaviour. These are: Trauma Informed Practice, Safe Supportive Learning Environment, Nurture Principles, Steps to Wellbeing, Restorative Practice and Positive Approaches to Social Behavioural and Emotional Wellbeing. ABSIT provides three key forms of training which embed traumainformed approaches across the school system:Foundational Online Training – equipping all staff with an understanding of safeguarding duties, legislative requirements and traumainformed best practice. Staff report feeling more confident and consistent in responding to concerns, supporting early intervention.EightModule Leadership Training – enabling principals, senior leaders and governors to build wholeschool cultures that prioritise safety, wellbeing and traumainformed approaches. Participants are encouraged to complete the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) traumainformed toolkit to strengthen wholeschool implementation.Targeted TraumaInformed Advice – ABSIT provides structured advice to schools in complex bullying cases, ensuring responses are aligned with safeguarding legislation and departmental guidance. This helps schools assess concerns, identify gaps and ensure that support plans reflect traumainformed practice.Furthermore, ABSIT officers are all SBNI TIP trainers and currently are participating in the SBNI Leading with a Trauma-Informed Lens leadership training programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact that delayed cataract assessment can have on patient confidence, mobility and quality of life, particularly for older people.
answer_text: My Department recognises that delays in cataract assessment and treatment can have a significant impact on all patients, but particularly older people.Cataract surgery is one of the highest volume surgeries carried out across the UK, including in Northern Ireland. It is almost always daycase, not usually requiring general anaesthesia, and the intervention enjoys excellent Quality of Life outcomes. Although sight-limiting, it is reversible, and not generally a time-critical procedure.I acknowledge that delays longer than clinically-indicated to accessing care, including for cataract assessment, are unacceptable and can have negative implications for patient confidence, mobility and quality of life. This can be particularly true for older people and those living in rural communities.In the event that vision deteriorates whilst on a waiting list, support for patients suffering with impaired vision is available from voluntary sector organisations such as the RNIB, where a range of resources to help patients and service users can be accessed. “Waiting Well” is an important element of a patient’s care journey, and strategies are in place to support patients on outpatient waiting lists.Delays to accessing appropriate care are due to demand, much of it demographically-driven, outstripping capacity to deliver against need. In recognition of the sustained demand-capacity gap which impacts on treatments, including those for cataract, my officials are currently working with providers around business cases to build capacity within clinical teams with additional recruitment. I acknowledge that capacity-building is a key strategic pillar to address waiting lists sustainably, and prioritisation of ECF new and re-purposed funding, including investments to support in-reach Independent Sector activity, is ongoing. This additional capacity will have a positive impact on waiting times reducing any potential negative impact on patients confidence, mobility and quality of life.My Department will continue to monitor waiting times for cataract assessment and treatment and work with providers to improve timely access.  Due to the impact these delays have on patients, addressing these care pathways remains an important step in efforts to reduce avoidable sight loss and support healthy aging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department takes to ensure that people with cataracts are able to access NHS assessment before deterioration in sight begins to affect day-to-day safety and independence, including the ability to drive, work or care for others.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department gives to the impact of delayed cataract care on people living in rural areas, where loss of vision may result in loss of the ability to drive and access essential services.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department takes to support community optometrists, given they are relied upon to manage patients with cataracts for extended periods before referral.
answer_text: My Department is committed to making it easier for patients to access the care they need. Community optometry shows how services delivered closer to home can detect problems early, help to manage waiting lists, and ease pressure on hospitals, all while improving outcomes for patients.It would not be an expectation that community optometrists are relied upon to manage patients with cataracts for extended periods before referral. When referral is clinically-indicated, and in the patient’s best interest, referral for specialist advice and treatment would be anticipated.Through Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) communications and protocols, “Guidance Notes for Community Optometrists” have been developed, shared, and hosted. These guidance and support protocols lay out best practice around level of vision, quality of life impacts and indications for referral, including any comorbidities. Driving status, caring and independent living needs and a discussion on the nature of surgery also helps to make an informed decision on referral.Community optometrists are further supported by development of a cataract-specific electronic referral template into the encompass electronic patient system, and associated clinical decision-making aids hosted within that system.As I take forward the Reset agenda, our priority is to build services that are sustainable, patient-centred and responsive to need. That means moving more care into the community where it can be delivered quickly and effectively.  The main focus will be rebalancing the health and social care system so that care is delivered closer to home, hospitals are used for those who need them most, and services are planned for long-term sustainability. In Northern Ireland we have over 700 optometrists who are highly skilled, local, and ready to help with 95% of the population living less than 5 miles from a practice.The Reset agenda also emphasises the importance of prevention and early intervention. With Northern Ireland’s ageing and rural population increasing demand for eyecare, community optometry has a crucial role to play. By putting in place strategies to improve access and uptake of HSC-funded eye examinations, though Universal Credit legislative reform, awareness-raising campaigns and initiatives such as #EyeCareWeCare, earlier case-finding and informed decision-making can help to manage both patient expectations and system demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of whether delays or barriers in accessing non-molestation orders, discourage victims from seeking legal protection.
answer_text: No formal assessment has been made of whether victims have been discouraged from seeking legal protection if unable to access a non-molestation order in a timely way.However, ensuring all victims of domestic abuse are supported to access effective protection through the courts is essential. To that end, my Department works closely with support organisations through the relevant frameworks and partnerships to ensure that victims can access advice and information about court procedures and the protections available. Legal aid is also available to anyone, regardless of means, who needs to apply for the protection of a non-molestation order.  While individuals may need to make a modest contribution towards the cost of their legal representation, this will generally be considerably less than they might pay privately, and is payable over time, on terms designed to be affordable in light of their financial circumstances.Furthermore, while the Department of Finance has policy responsibility for the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998, the legislation governing non molestation orders, I brought forward provision, in the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, to enhance the protections available to victims of domestic abuse giving evidence in family cases, including non-molestation order proceedings. This included provision for the use of special measures, enabling evidence to be given from behind a screen, or from outside the courtroom.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of whether the current legal aid framework for non-molestation orders is sufficiently resilient to protect victims during periods of widespread withdrawal of legal services.
answer_text: Legal aid is available to anyone seeking the protection of a non-molestation order, though some applicants will need to make a modest contribution towards the costs of their representation.  This is intended to ensure that no-one is denied access to the protection that they need as a result of financial considerations. The representation provided through the legal aid system is, of course, delivered by independent legal professionals, and the system is critically dependent on the availability of such professionals willing to provide these services.  Until now, non-molestation order proceedings have not been affected by any withdrawal of services.  Given the acute and serious nature of the danger that non-molestation orders protect against, I would not expect them to be affected by any future withdrawal.I am conscious of the serious impact of the current action of the Criminal Bar Association, in particular of its impact on vulnerable victims and witnesses involved in criminal proceedings. In addition to the work currently under way to resolve this issue, I have tasked Departmental officials with developing a range of options to protect users of the justice system from similar issues should they arise in future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the timescale she expects for the introduction of (i) Domestic Abuse Protection Notices; and (ii) Domestic Abuse Protection Orders, in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Domestic Abuse Protection Notices (DAPNs) and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders (DAPOs) potentially represent an important next step in strengthening protections for victims of domestic abuse and enhancing the justice system’s ability to intervene at an earlier stage.Officials are continuing to develop the detailed policy and operational model in close partnership with the PSNI. This work has been informed by learning from pilot activity in England and Wales, while ensuring that any approach adopted reflects the particular legal and operational context in Northern Ireland.My Department is working with criminal justice partners to develop a pilot to test the use of DAPNs and police-led DAPOs in one police district in Northern Ireland.  This approach will help to inform the development and roll-out of DAPNs and DAPOs across Northern Ireland. Through roundtable engagement with voluntary and community sector organisations, it was clearly articulated that a key advantage of DAPNs and DAPOs is that they remove the onus from victims to make protective applications themselves and that is why the pilot will focus on police-led DAPOs. This approach is intended to reduce pressure on victims at a point of crisis and ensure that protective action can be taken swiftly where necessary.Subject to funding being made available, my Department is working towards the commencement of a two-year pilot during the 2026–27 financial year. The two-year pilot period will allow for structured monitoring and evaluation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department will take to strengthen protection for victims, assessed as being at high risk of harm, while Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders are not yet in force.
answer_text: I am acutely conscious of the heightened vulnerability of victims assessed as being at high risk of harm. Ensuring that strong and effective safeguards are in place for those individuals remains a central priority for me as Minister of Justice.While Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders (DAPNs and DAPOs) are not yet operational, victims assessed as high risk continue to be protected through a well-established framework of civil and criminal measures. Existing provisions, including Non-Molestation Orders, Occupation Orders, Restraining Orders and the use of bail conditions, provide significant legal safeguards. Breach of these orders constitutes a criminal offence.The domestic abuse offence introduced under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 has further strengthened the justice response to coercive and controlling behaviour. The Act also provides for specific statutory aggravators, including where children are involved, ensuring that the courts can reflect the full seriousness and impact of offending behaviour.Where a victim is assessed as being at high risk of serious harm, their case will be referred into a structured multi-agency risk management process. Information is shared in a controlled and lawful manner to build a comprehensive picture of risk, and a coordinated safety plan is developed. This can include enhanced police monitoring, safety planning, emergency accommodation, safeguarding arrangements for children, and proactive engagement with the perpetrator where appropriate. Actions are tracked and reviewed to ensure that risk is being actively managed and reduced wherever possible.  Additionally, my Department has been leading multi-agency work, both to strengthen and replace existing multi agency risk assessment conference arrangements. It is planned to implement a number of practical changes in the coming months and incrementally throughout 2026/27.Anyone who is a victim of domestic abuse, or who has concerns about the safety of someone else, should report the matter to the PSNI, who can take immediate action to help protect those at risk.Once introduced, DAPNs and DAPOs will provide an additional layer of protection, including enabling applications to be brought by the police, as currently proposed under plans for the two-year pilot, thereby helping to reduce the burden on victims. Until that time, victims assessed as being at high risk of harm continue to benefit from the range of protections already available, supported by coordinated multi-agency action and a strengthened legislative framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department has given to ensuring that housing policy for older people applies coherently across all tenures, including the private rented sector.
answer_text: The Executive Housing Supply Strategy which I published in 2024 recognises the changing age profile of our population and includes a long-term policy commitment to support the delivery of a more diverse range of housing options across all tenures, which meet housing needs and takes into account demographic change. Implementation of the Strategy, through a series of short-term action plans, will therefore consider actions to increase housing supply and provide a range of housing solutions to meet all needs, including those of older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any guidance his Department provides to housing bodies regarding changes in housing suitability as tenants age.
answer_text: The Department’s Housing Association Guide contains various guidance applicable to the design of housing for older people. The basis of any design of housing for older people should normally be a universal solution that can be easily adapted to suit a wide range of users now and over the life of the property. All new build social homes are currently built to ‘Lifetime Homes Standards’, ensuring that homes can be easily adapted to meet the changing needs of tenants. Guidance is also available for specific wheelchair housing design standards to improve the range, quality and flexibility of accommodation for wheelchair users, many of whom are older; and on CAT 1/CAT 2 design. Housing Associations are also delivering schemes to enhance older peoples living under ‘Housing our Ageing Population Panel for Innovation’ (HAPPI) pilot schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any ways his Department’s housing policy balances supporting older people who wish to move to more suitable accommodation, with ensuring such moves are voluntary, and not driven by pressure or insecurity.
answer_text: The Supporting People programme funds a range of housing related support services for older people including sheltered/continued independent living in later life accommodation, supported accommodation and floating support. These services provide person-centred support, designed around individual needs and preferences, and are focused on empowering older people to make informed decisions about their housing. As set out in the Supporting People Policy Framework NI, provisions are made to support older people to remain in their own home or to transition to a form of supported housing accommodation which endeavours to maintain independent living.This issue is also an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive operates a housing solutions and support model when customers approach us for housing assistance. Housing Advisors engage with customers to understand their individual needs and to help them explore their housing and support options. Typically, the main options for any customer will be to stay in their current accommodation, or to move accommodation, with support as required for both of these options. In the case of older applicants, the ‘stay’ option might be facilitated through the provision of floating support or through delivery of an adaptation to their current dwelling. The ‘move’ option could be a move to ‘Independent Living Plus’ accommodation (Sheltered) or from a multi-story dwelling to a level access property. Housing solutions and support advice should be offered in a balanced manner. Housing Executive policy is clear that the decision is for the customer to make, having been provided with comprehensive advice on the practical and realistic options available. Our Housing Advisors will guide and support the customer to make an informed decision about their preferred option. They will not lead or unduly influence the customer to favour one viable option over another. Housing Advisors will not allow third parties to make decisions on behalf of customers unless a lack of capacity is confirmed and, in these instances, the third party must be empowered by the Office of Care and Protection to make the decision in question. Where a Housing Advisor suspects there may be undue pressure being placed on an older or vulnerable customer, they will follow our internal Safeguarding Policy.” My Department has provided £8 million in Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) funding to Co-Ownership, our primary delivery partner for intermediate housing, to operate its Co-Own for Over 55s pilot. This novel affordable housing option was designed in response to our ageing population, providing an innovative housing solution for older people, supporting them to live as independently as possible, for as long as possible. With Co-Own for Over 55s, Co-Ownership can help homeowners aged 55 or over to purchase a home that is rig

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any ways his Department’s housing policy is adapting to demographic change, including an ageing population.
answer_text: The Executive Housing Supply Strategy which I published in 2024 recognises the changing age profile of our population and includes a long-term policy commitment to support the delivery of a more diverse range of housing options across all tenures, which meet housing needs and considers  demographic change and an ageing population.  Delivery will be through a series of action plans. The Department is currently providing , £8 million in Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) funding to Co-Ownership, to operate its Co-Own for Over 55s pilot which was designed in response to our ageing population, providing an innovative housing solution for older people, supporting them to live as independently as possible, for as long as possible. With Co-Own for Over 55s, Co-Ownership can help homeowners aged 55 or over to purchase a home that is right for them as they age.In addition, work on bringing forward a new Active Ageing Strategy Action Plan is continuing at pace. This work is considering a range of issues, including housing, that have been identified by stakeholders as impacting on the ageing population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any ways his Department works with the Department of Health to reflect the impact of housing conditions and suitability, on the health and wellbeing of older people.
answer_text: My Department, and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE), work with the Department of Health (DoH) in relation to housing for older people in a number of ways, which reflects the impact housing conditions and suitability will have on health and wellbeing:My Department and DoH co-chair the Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum, which sets strategy and policy and strengthens collaboration and decision-making on home adaptations. We are working together on a review of NIHE-administered Private Sector Grants focusing on streamlining processes and ensuring people can access the adaptations they need as quickly as possible.  My Department provides funding for the Supporting People Programme, which is commissioned by the NIHE. The Programme delivers housing support and works closely with the Department of Health on improving access to supported living accommodation for vulnerable adults, including older people. My Department chairs the Supporting People Joint Policy Forum —comprising DoH, the Department of Justice, and NIHE — which strengthens strategic cooperation and engagement across the statutory stakeholders involved in Supporting People services. My Department collaborates with DoH’s Social Care Collaborative Forum to identify and undertake actions to address the needs and challenges related to supported living for individuals with diverse abilities and support requirements.My Department is overseeing work with the NIHE, with engagement with stakeholders and other departments including DoH, on Proposal 20 of the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations, which is considering the allocation process of specialised properties including for later in life.The assessment of needs for older people is led by Health and Social Care. Any accommodation needs, or needs for housing related support, are agreed through cross-departmental partnership working forums including the NIHE’s Supporting People Strategic Advisory Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of difficulties faced by community pharmacies in sourcing commonly prescribed medicines, including medicines for pain relief, cardiovascular conditions and blood pressure.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, the UK medicines regulator and others in the supply chain to ensure patients have fair and equitable access to treatments across the UK. I recognise the impact medicine supply shortages can have on patients and community pharmacy teams in Northern Ireland (NI), however, there are over 14,000 licensed medicines in the UK, and the majority are in good supply and are available in sufficient quantities to meet patient needs. Community pharmacies in NI have access to these medicines through numerous pharmaceutical wholesalers and medicine licence holders as part of the UK-wide medicine supply chain. My Department has seen no evidence that patients or community pharmacies accessing medicines in NI are more negatively impacted when compared to other regions of the UK.However, medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons such as manufacturing issues or increases in demand globally. When a medicine is not available to meet overall patient demand, such as the current supply disruption affecting the pain relief medication, co-codamol 30mg/500mg tablets, there are well-established tools and processes that can be applied to ensure patients have access to appropriate treatments, including but not limited to providing healthcare professionals with advice and guidance on the actions they can take. Additionally, if NI community pharmacies have concerns about accessing medicines in relation to pricing and reimbursement arrangements but the products remain available to meet overall patient need, these can be raised through the Drug Tariff Liaison Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of whether current community pharmacy payment and reimbursement arrangements remain fit for purpose.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to ensure fair, accurate and prompt payment for HSC commissioned pharmaceutical services, and the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff sets out the remuneration and reimbursement arrangements for medicines supplied by community pharmacies.Since April 2018, funding has increased by almost £60m (57%). This is the highest level of funding to date and is comparable with the rest of the UK. Funding for community pharmacy includes payment for dispensing services and provision of other pharmacy services to the public.The prices of medicines fluctuate daily, and with over 45m items dispensed in 2024/25, its simply not practical to reimburse pharmacies with the exact amount they paid to purchase each item. That means, at an individual prescription level, pharmacies may sometimes receive less than they paid for the product, and at other times more than they paid.An annual margin survey is completed to ensure that, on average, the reimbursement arrangements for community pharmacy cover the cost of purchasing medicines plus a guaranteed level of margin, or ‘retained purchase profit’, meaning that HSC dispensing should be a profitable activity overall. For 2025/26, the retained minimum margin threshold has been increased from £31.5m to £34m.The actual annual margin achieved by community pharmacies in Northern Ireland has significantly exceeded the minimum threshold. And importantly, unlike other UK regions, any excess in margin is not recouped, meaning additional income for pharmacy contractors above the agreed financial envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the sustainability of community pharmacy dispensing services is being considered, given ongoing public messaging encouraging patients to access care through their local community pharmacy.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to ensure fair, accurate and prompt payment for HSC commissioned pharmaceutical services, and the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff sets out the remuneration and reimbursement arrangements for medicines supplied by community pharmacies.My Department publishes an annual review of access and delivery of commissioned community pharmacy services in Northern Ireland. The 2024/25 review found that Northern Ireland continues to be well provided in respect of access to community pharmacy services and that services are delivered in line with a commissioning plan which is agreed between the Department and Community Pharmacy NI, the representative and negotiating body for community pharmacy contractors. My officials have engaged with CPNI to develop a new commissioning plan for 2025-2028. These new commissioning arrangements will progress the ambitions set out in the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030, which outlines a vision for delivery of more clinical services that utilise the skills of pharmacy teams and offer safe, convenient and timely access to care. To provide stability to the community pharmacy sector and ensure the continued supply of medicines to patients, my Department has invested significantly in recent years. Since April 2018, funding has increased by almost £60m (57%). This is the highest level of funding to date and is comparable with the rest of the UK. Funding for community pharmacy includes payments for dispensing services and also the provision of other pharmacy services to the public for a range of services including Pharmacy First Services such as Sore Throat, Management of Urinary Tract Infections, Emergency Hormonal Contraception and Shingles.My Department actively and regularly collaborates with CPNI through established stakeholder fora. This engagement helps to inform the annual funding level for community pharmacy services in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given any consideration to pension provision for community pharmacists delivering health service-commissioned services.
answer_text: Access to the HSC Pension Scheme is legislated for under The Health and Social Care Pension Scheme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 and is limited to health service workers who are: employed by HSC employers; are an HSC contractor/provider or those employed by them – this includes GPs and dentists. For private employers that are too small to have their own pension scheme, they can meet this obligation through the National Employment Savings Trust (NEST) Pension Scheme.  NEST is a government-backed workplace pension scheme accountable to Parliament through the Department for Work and Pensions and is run on a not-for-profit basis. It is used by employers across the UK, including Northern Ireland (NI), to meet their automatic enrolment obligations. The purpose of the scheme is to ensure that all eligible workers have access to a workplace pension. Information on the scheme can be accessed at  https://www.nestpensions.org.uk/schemeweb/nest.html

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to the financial impact on community pharmacies of dispensing medicines at a loss where reimbursement does not reflect market prices.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to ensure fair, accurate and prompt payment for HSC commissioned pharmaceutical services, and the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff sets out the remuneration and reimbursement arrangements for medicines supplied by community pharmacies.The prices of medicines fluctuate daily, and with over 45m items dispensed in 2024/25, its simply not practical to reimburse pharmacies with the exact amount they paid to purchase each item. That means, at an individual prescription level, pharmacies may sometimes receive less than they paid for the product, and at other times more than they paid.An annual margin survey is completed to ensure that, on average, the reimbursement arrangements for community pharmacy cover the cost of purchasing medicines plus a guaranteed level of margin, or ‘retained purchase profit’, meaning that HSC dispensing should be a profitable activity overall. In recent years, actual margin achieved has exceeded the agreed minimum threshold. For 2025/26, the retained minimum margin threshold has been increased from £31.5m to £34m.If there is significant price instability or price fluctuations in-month for medicines, then a “concessionary price” is activated to help reset the price. Furthermore, since 1 April 2024, all drugs awarded concessionary prices have been subject to Zero Discount, i.e. discount is not deducted from these products. In Northern Ireland, unlike other parts of the UK, there is also a special advance in the system for drugs on concession. The advance is currently set at £3,000 per contractor.  I have listened to the recent concerns from contractors in relation to the financial pressures arising from current price fluctuations for a number of medicines, and in order to support the network, the Department is taking steps to review the special advance payable to contractors.The Department remains committed to working constructively with the sector to ensure that community pharmacies are supported within a fair and sustainable framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider the provision of a dedicated car park at Castlerock Train Station, given the absence of on-site parking and the distance many users are required to walk to access the station.
answer_text: Castlerock Train Station is a historic facility, constructed in the mid1800s, and is located within the centre of the village. Given its setting, there is no current opportunity to provide dedicated onsite parking for commuters without the loss of important public amenity space, which is already in limited supply in Castlerock.I would continue to encourage all public transport users to consider using multimodal travel, including the use of bus, rail, and active travel options, to help reduce congestion and support sustainable transport objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of increased use of rail services for commuting and leisure travel, whether her Department will consider expanding the parking facilities at Coleraine Train Station.
answer_text: I welcome the increased use of rail services for both commuting and leisure purposes at Coleraine Train Station. However, given the towncentre location of the station, there is no suitable land available that would allow for an expansion of the existing parking facilities.Alongside the 74 free Park & Ride spaces provided by Translink, my Department also provides a further 477 lowcost parking spaces adjacent to the station, which support commuter access within the constraints of the area. Local bus services also operate from the station, and I would continue to encourage all public transport users to consider using multimodal travel, including the use of bus, rail, and active travel options, to help reduce congestion and support sustainable transport objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of the condition of the road surface between Bellarena and Benone; and (ii) whether any resurfacing or tarmac works are planned for this route.
answer_text: Any resurfacing scheme would  be co-ordinated with the proposed Signature Active Travel Scheme.Details of all resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year are provided in my Department’s annual report to Council. In the meantime, my Department will continue to undertake routine inspections and actionable defects will be prioritised for repair in accordance with the Limited Service Policy in so far as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the £3.8 million estimated cost of the proposed park and ride facility at Bellarena railway halt, including a breakdown of costs for (i) design; (ii) land acquisition; (iii) construction; and (iv) associated works.
answer_text: The table below provides a breakdown of estimated costs to deliver the new Park and Ride facility at Bellarena Rail Halt. ItemCostConstruction £2,794,701 Project management, design and statutory fees £259,419Optimum Basis and sunk costs £547,836Inflation  £221,924 Total £3,823,880

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how public transport provision across East Londonderry is being planned to improve integration between bus services and rail halts and stations, including the coordination of timetables and service frequency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Coleraine Integrated Bus and Rail Station operate an hourly rail service with trains timed to connect with bus services to Portrush and the very popular tourist locations situated within the Causeway Coast and Glens Council area, functioning as a key transport hub for the northwest and providing substantial onward connections across the wider North West region. The 402 Causeway Rambler bus service operates on a year round basis providing transport connectivity to key tourism attractions between Coleraine and Ballycastle and coupled with this the 140 bus service provides connectivity between the towns of Coleraine, Portrush and Portstewart. The North West Transport Hub facilitates integrated bus and rail services. All trains are met by a connecting dedicated bus link to Foyle Street bus centre thereby providing opportunities for interchange to the bus network and a range of journey options, including the 212 Goldline service between Derry and Belfast serving intermediate towns along that route, the X3/X4 service between Derry and Dublin, Foyle Metro services to the University of Ulster campus at Magee, and rural bus services operating between Derry and Limavady and connecting the communities along that route. Translink continuously review all bus and rail services to ensure they remain safe, sustainable, reliable and meet its customers' needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any interim actions that are planned by his Department to maintain health service dental provision, pending the publication of the General Dental Services Cost of Service Review, in March 2026.
answer_text: To improve access to dental care for both registered and unregistered patients, and to support primary care capacity as part of the broader ‘shift-left’ agenda, I have invested £7 million in General Dental Services in 2025/26.The £7million investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme; a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions, and root canal treatments; and a further £1.6million allocation to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care to patients.An additional £2.5million has also been made available for General Dental Practitioners to help offset the increased cost of employers’ National Insurance contributions for Family Health Service providers.I am clear that General Dental Services will require a sustained effort to ensure stability over the long term. The Department’s ‘Health and Social Care NI -Three-year plan’ includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.A General Dental Services Advisory group is being established to provide independent, expert advice to the Department to inform the development of an Action Plan, and ensure that reform aligns with Departmental priorities and addresses patient needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the impact of reduced access to routine and preventative dental care on demand for emergency dental, GP, and hospital services.
answer_text: At present, all registered patients can access emergency dental treatment at their registered practice, where their General Dental Practitioner has an obligation to provide the required treatment to in a timely manner.The Dental Access Scheme (DAS) has been in operation since 1 August 2024. The DAS provides appointments to unregistered patients who require treatment to deal with urgent or pressing dental problems during the working week.  My Department provided funding for up to 500 patients to be seen under the DAS per week. The performance data shows that there has been a high demand for this service with approximately 800 patients now accessing the service per week.For any patient experiencing an urgent or emergency dental condition at weekends or public holidays, dental care can be obtained at one of the three Emergency Dental Clinics (EDCs) based in Belfast, Armagh and Ballymena. Patients are triaged remotely and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged. Again, there is a high demand for this service. The treatment appointments are currently limited to 55 patients per day but my Department has made funding available to the Southern HSC Trust to expand their EDC service which will increase the number of emergency appointments to 66 per day.  My Officials are also working on a proposal to commission an Emergency Dental Clinic for the Western area to meet the demand for patients requiring out-of-hours emergency dental care, subject to the necessary funding being identified.  In addition, undergraduate dental students, based in the urgent student clinic (USC) in the Belfast Dental School provide emergency dental appointments for unregistered patients under the supervision of dental teaching staff.  Patients can self-refer to this service, which is available during the university term time, i.e. 39 weeks per year. This service is heavily over-subscribed with 48 patients applying for the 16 appointments available per day. My Department has recently provided additional funding to the Belfast Trust to increase the number of appointments available to 20 per day and extend the opening of the USC by 10 weeks beyond term time. My Department does not hold data for demand relating to emergency dental care provided by GPs or within hospital settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail expenditure on health service dentistry per head of population, in the last five years.
answer_text: Health service dentistry is carried out both by Health and Social Care Trusts and Dental Practices. The total cost of health service dentistry per head of population in Northern Ireland over the last five years is shown in the table below:YearCost of HSCNI Dental Services per head of population2024/25 £60.50 2023/24 £55.09 2022/23 £59.92 2021/22 £68.73 2020/21 £65.91

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of the financial sustainability of dental practices delivering predominantly health service care.
answer_text: In December 2024, I commissioned a General Dental Service Cost of Service review to provide a robust evidence base on the costs associated with delivering Health Service dentistry. This review is expected to be completed at the end of this financial year and will help to inform future decisions on the reform of General Dental Services. While this work is underway, I have made available additional funding to help offset the rising costs associated with National Insurance Contributions, and to support practitioners who continue to deliver Health Service dentistry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any preparatory work that has been undertaken to enable timely policy and funding decisions following publication of the General Dental Services Cost of Service Review.
answer_text: A General Dental Service Advisory Group is being established to provide independent expert advice, to ensure that reform aligns with Departmental priorities and addresses the needs of the patient.  The outworkings of this group and the cost of service review will allow for the development of an Action Plan in line with the timeline set out in the Three-Year Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department uses whole-life costing to assess the long-term cost and effectiveness of road repairs; and (ii) whether investing in more durable resurfacing is being considered as a more cost-effective alternative to repeated patching.
answer_text: The current impacts from winter weather, especially the very high levels of rainfall over the past few months, have exposed the weakness in our road network, resulting from many years of underinvestment.I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern. I also recently launched ‘Better Roads’ - the draft Roads Maintenance Strategy, which outlines my Department’s intention to repair defects like potholes and protect the structural integrity of our roads by doing things in a new and different way.This new Roads Maintenance Strategy will use cutting edge AI technology to gather a full and evidence-based survey of our road network, thereby identifying the stretches of road in the worst condition. This information will allow the improved targeting of resources to increase the effectiveness of road repairs, allowing localised areas of road to be resurfaced as a more cost-effective alternative to repeated patching.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of funding allocated to road (i) maintenance; and (ii) resurfacing, in East Londonderry, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: My Department’s capital and resource structural maintenance expenditure, by council area covering the East Derry constituency, for each of the last five financial years, is set out in the table below:District Council AreaStructural MaintenanceYear 20-21Year 21-22Year 22-23Year 23-24Year 24-25     £m Causeway Coast & Glens Resource1.21.311.71.7Capital10.310.110.39.110.9Derry City & Strabane Resource11.21.61.72.1Capital6.76.59.87.48.2Expenditure is determined by budget allocation and capacity in any given year. Resource Structural Maintenance includes patching of the carriageway and footways.Capital Structural Maintenance includes resurfacing and reconstruction, asphalt patching, surface dressing, capital investment in both drainage infrastructure and in the structural stability of embankments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has a preventative road maintenance strategy in place.
answer_text: The current impacts from winter weather, especially the very high levels of rainfall over the past few months, have exposed the weakness in our road network, resulting from many years of underinvestment.I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work her Department is currently undertaking to ensure the road network is adapted to the impacts of climate change, including heavier rainfall, freeze-thaw cycles and flooding.
answer_text: The current impacts from winter weather, especially the very high levels of rainfall over the past few months, have exposed the weakness in our road network, resulting from many years of underinvestment.I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to reduce the need for repeat interventions on the same sections of road.
answer_text: The current impacts from winter weather, especially the very high levels of rainfall over the past few months, have exposed the weakness in our road network, resulting from many years of underinvestment.I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern. I also recently launched ‘Better Roads’ - the draft Roads Maintenance Strategy, which outlines my Department’s intention to repair defects like potholes and protect the structural integrity of our roads by doing things in a new and different way.This new Roads Maintenance Strategy will use cutting edge AI technology to gather a full and evidence-based survey of our road network, thereby identifying the stretches of road in the worst condition.
