question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how frequently his Department reviews housing and homelessness strategies, to assess whether they remain effective in light of changing housing market conditions.
answer_text: A strategic review of the Housing Supply Strategy will be carried out every five years. Progress reports on the delivery of agreed Strategy Action Plans will also be undertaken annually.The Housing (NI) Order 1988 requires the Housing Executive to publish a new homelessness strategy every five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of access to the private rented sector in areas with limited housing supply, including East Londonderry.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has the statutory responsibility to regularly examine housing conditions and need.Information produced as part of this work can be found at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research.  This includes a recent Housing Market Review Report on the Private Rented Sector, which can be found at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/northern-ireland-housing-market-data-hub/housing-market-review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of whether existing homelessness prevention measures are sufficient to prevent homelessness arising from landlord sales in the private rented sector.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is unable to prevent landlords from selling their properties, and there is no specific prevention measure open to the Housing Executive in this regard.In the event an applicant presents to the Housing Executive having received a Notice to Quit due to property sale, the Housing Executive will provide advice and assistance as part of our Housing Solutions and Support approach.This may include exploring alternative housing options to prevent homelessness such as the private rented sector and co-ownership models, making referrals to support services as appropriate, and carrying out a homeless assessment under the Housing (NI) Order 1988, where an applicant is threatened with homelessness within 28 days.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any interim policy measures in place within his Department to reduce prolonged stays in unsuitable temporary accommodation, while longer-term housing supply initiatives are delivered.
answer_text: I am looking at innovative ways to increase housing supply across all tenures. Following the additional funding I secured in December Monitoring, the Social Housing Development Programme is expected to start 1,750 and complete 1,500 new social homes this year. I also introduced the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation (LAMA) scheme in December 2024 to enable charities to purchase properties and provide secure, affordable tenancies for those with low support needs. This scheme allows those who are tenancy ready, but who can not access social housing, or the private rented sector, to move out of temporary accommodation.Already, £4m of the £10m of financial transaction capital available has been allocated and officials continue to engage with sectoral partners to progress further business cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions available to his Department where monitoring shows that housing or homelessness strategies are not delivering improved outcomes for families.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy is being implemented through a series of short-term Action Plans.  Each Plan will be subject to regular review with reports published which track progress against defined measures. Progress against Strategy indicators will also be reviewed at the end of each Action Plan period which will help inform the development of actions in subsequent Plans. The Homelessness Strategy is delivered via Annual Action Plans. Progress is reviewed each year enabling the Housing Executive to adjust their Action Plans as needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any steps he will take to ensure guidance and application materials for the Warm Healthy Homes Fund will be designed to allow accessibility for older people, including those with limited digital experience, and (ii) whether materials will be available in (a) clear language; (b) large print; and (c) alternative formats.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing the scheme design and delivery requirements for the new Warm Healthy Homes Fund: an energy efficiency scheme to improve warmth, health and energy affordability for low-income households. The scheme will be shaped by further public consultation in the next few months. A key aspect of implementation will involve close collaboration with the appointed delivery partner to ensure all scheme materials adhere to accessibility standards and are straightforward for applicants to use. This will include providing information in large print and other alternative formats. While the scheme is expected to adopt a ‘digitalfirst’ approach, it is recognised that applications must also be available through nondigital channels, including by telephone or in writing, and with additional support such as translation services, video relay or signlanguage assistance where required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any steps he will take to ensure the Warm Healthy Homes Fund will prioritise older people with cold-related health risks; and (ii) whether grants will support a whole-house approach incorporating (a) insulation; (b) heating upgrades; and (c) ventilation improvements.
answer_text: As part of the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy consultation, views were sought on the policy for the Warm Healthy Homes Fund. There was widespread agreement that the fund prioritises low-income and vulnerable households living in the poorest-performing properties and that a whole house approach should be taken. The Warm Healthy Homes  Fund will offer a package of measures such as insulation, draught proofing and ventilation, alongside heating systems incorporating low-carbon heat solutions and renewable technologies. The final scheme design will be shaped by further public consultation which will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any proposals he intends to bring forward to raise energy-efficiency standards, in the private rented sector.
answer_text: Work has commenced to develop and introduce Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards Regulations for the private rented sector. It is, however, too early in the policy development process to provide specific detail on proposals. Progress will be reported on as part of the implementation of the Housing Supply and Warm Healthy Homes Strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy will ensure support reaches low-income working households. who may not qualify for means-tested benefits. but who still face fuel poverty, including those affected by high childcare and transport costs.
answer_text: The Warm Healthy Homes Strategy adopts a needs-based approach to prioritise support to households in or experiencing the greatest risk of fuel poverty, rather than relying solely on benefit entitlement. A cornerstone of the Strategy is the ‘Warm Healthy Homes Fund’, launching in 2027. The Fund will deliver energy-efficiency upgrades with support aimed at low income and vulnerable households, not just benefit recipients.   This will sit alongside the Strategy’s commitment to coordinating all future energy efficiency interventions across government to ensure that they are consistent, flexible, and targeted at those most in need.The Strategy commits to strengthening access to trusted, community-based energy advice and information. This will broaden the ways in which all households can access trusted advice, ensuring support is easier to reach as the Strategy develops.Improving housing standards and enforcement across all tenures is another integral element of the Strategy. By raising housing standards, the Strategy aims to create warmer, healthier and more efficient homes, initially in the social housing sector and then for the private rented sector, which will reduce the conditions that contribute to damp, mould and poorer health, whilst helping to lower energy bills for those households.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional capacity for gynaecology assessment and treatment that has been commissioned in the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Within the last 12 months, each of the Trusts have accessed non-recurrent funding to reduce the >3-year outpatient backlog. This has been achieved with additional capacity being made available via Waiting List Reduction (WLR) funding to send patients to the Independent Sector (IS), as detailed below.AssessmentsTreatmentsDiagnosticsActivity SpendActivity SpendActivitySpendBelfast Trust1,518£484, 388160£51,616Northern Trust557£849,550South Eastern Trust538£106,74425£66,03935£11,975Southern Trust1,141£857,335Western Trust357£94,8286£15,343Total4,111£2,392,843192£132,998£11,975To support earlier validation and prioritisation of patients on the waiting list, some Trusts commissioned additional administrative sessions dedicated to telephone validation. This enabled administrative teams to work through substantial numbers of cases, ensuring that patient information, clinical need, and waiting list status were reviewed efficiently and accurately.These initiatives have collectively enhanced service capacity and contributed to targeted reductions in gynaecology waiting lists. This has been progressed as part of the regionally coordinated Gynae Collaborative with funding support from my Department.Nurse Led colposcopy/ hysteroscopy services have been extended by recurrent funding being made available for a model of two Lead Nurses for colposcopy and hysteroscopy in each Trust.Further investment is currently being planned for Clinical E-Triage, additional sonography and Advanced Nurse Practitioners in each Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the impact of prolonged waiting times for gynaecology services on unplanned care, including repeat GP attendances and emergency presentations.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women currently waiting more than 52 weeks for a first outpatient gynaecology appointment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Accredited Official Statistics for the number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Gynaecology specialty is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’. Figures broken down by HSC Trust, specialty, and time band are published in the accompanying data files. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 September 2025 and is available here:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/outpatient-waiting-times

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any routine clinical review arrangements that are in place for women who have been waiting more than 52 weeks, for a first outpatient gynaecology appointment.
answer_text: Trust are currently undertaking a waiting list reduction process for first outpatient gynaecology appointments. To achieve this, a number of steps are currently underway. Firstly, an administrative validation process is undertaken to cleanse the new outpatient list. Secondly, each patient is clinically validated by a consultant gynaecologist to ensure that the most appropriate appointment is organised for the patient based on the initial referral to gynaecology.Following consultant clinical validation, there are various options of appointments for these patients, which include: A face-to-face outpatient appointment with a consultant; A telephone consultation with a consultant; Onward referral to a nurse / pessary service; Or referral to physiotherapy for further management. As part of a regional Gynae Collaborative programme aimed at addressing gynaecology waits exceeding three years, many Trusts have initiated telephone consultation clinics for all patients waiting more than three years. Once this cohort is completed and dependent on resources available, the programme will be extended to those waiting over two years and thereafter to patients waiting over one year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment made of the capacity of early learning and childcare providers to support children with special educational needs, particularly where settings do not have the financial resources to fund specialist training or additional staffing.
answer_text: The draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy | Department of Education, launched on 17 December 2025, has been informed by a comprehensive engagement process, the department’s End-to-End Review of early intervention specific to the early years and the RSM (2023) DE Review of Childcare Services in Northern Ireland. In response to these, a range of measures  to support early learning and childcare providers to support children with special educational needs and/or disabilities (SEN/D) have already commenced with the initial Executive £80m investment on early earning and childcare provision in the last two financial years,  these include the Pre-school Inclusion Fund assisting pre-school settings to support children with SEN and the extension of the Department of Health’s (DoH) Fair Play grant scheme.  Continuation and expansion of these schemes, as well as support for specialist training for providers, are set out in the draft strategy.  However, the scale and pace of implementation of the proposals are subject to Executive agreement and the availability of the funding required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will address situations where children who receive one-to-one support in school settings, do not receive equivalent support in early learning or childcare provisions.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy,  currently issued  for public consultation Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy | Department of Education,  includes specific proposed initiatives to support early learning and childcare providers to respond and meet the needs of all children including those with additional needs/disability. Proposed actions include:a plan to develop of a specific SEN CPD framework for education settings and childcare providers; agreed protocol for sharing of information on children’s developmental journeys; development of a holistic approach to child development bringing together Allied Health Professionals, Speech and Language Therapists and others to develop a targeted programme for children with emerging additional needs; the establishment of a Strategic Childcare Accessibility Fund inclusive of provision for children with additional needs;extension of the Department of Health (DoH) Fair Play grant scheme, provide high-quality support services and professional advice to both non-statutory and statutory PSEP providers to develop good practice and support them to meet the needs of all children; andcontinuation of the Pre-school Inclusion Fund which I established in November 2024 and expanded in November 2025. The full range of initiatives set out in the draft strategy, if agreed and resourced, will support comprehensive, needs based early learning and childcare provision for all children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration that has been given to allocating one-to-one special educational needs support on the basis of the child’s needs, rather than the setting attended, to support continuity of care across early years, childcare and school provision.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy,  currently issued  for public consultation Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy | Department of Education,  includes specific proposed initiatives to support early learning and childcare providers to respond and meet the needs of all children including those with additional needs/disability. Proposed actions include:a plan to develop of a specific SEN CPD framework for education settings and childcare providers; agreed protocol for sharing of information on children’s developmental journeys; development of a holistic approach to child development bringing together Allied Health Professionals, Speech and Language Therapists and others to develop a targeted programme for children with emerging additional needs; the establishment of a Strategic Childcare Accessibility Fund inclusive of provision for children with additional needs;extension of the Department of Health (DoH) Fair Play grant scheme, provide high-quality support services and professional advice to both non-statutory and statutory PSEP providers to develop good practice and support them to meet the needs of all children; andcontinuation of the Pre-school Inclusion Fund which I established in November 2024 and expanded in November 2025. The full range of initiatives set out in the draft strategy, if agreed and resourced, will support comprehensive, needs based early learning and childcare provision for all children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration given to enabling early learning and childcare providers to make referrals for special educational needs assessments, where concerns are identified.
answer_text: All registered early years and childcare providers must adhere to the Department of Health (DoH) Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12. Standard 9 of these stipulates the requirements placed on the registered person, person in charge or childminder regarding identification of a child in their care as possibly having additional needs.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dhssps/early-years-standards-full-version.pdf .	The draft ELC Strategy launched on 17 December 2025 for public consultation, seeks to further enhance identification of and response to the needs of all children. It sets out a range of proposed actions to this effect. These include a holistic approach to child development through integrated family-centered services which brings together Allied Health Professionals, Speech and Language Therapists, early years practitioners, family support teams, and others to develop a targeted programme for children with emerging additional needs, and extended access to developmental support for children aged 2–3, initially prioritising children facing disadvantage and those with additional needs.	As with all initiatives proposed within the draft ELC Strategy, the extent to which they can be delivered will be dependent on the resources made available by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of rising operational costs, including rates, energy and staffing, on the sustainability of early learning and childcare providers.
answer_text: The independent Review of Childcare Services in Northern Ireland (RSM, 2023) identified clear financial pressures across the sector, including workforce recruitment and retention challenges linked to relatively low pay, increased operating costs and declining profitability. A copy of the report can be found here:RSM (2023) DE Review of Childcare Services in NI - Final Report.PDFSubsequently, the Department of the Economy's Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Market Research further examined business sustainability challenges. This report identified sustained pressures from energy, insurance and, in particular, staffing costs, including the April 2025 increases in the National Minimum Wage and Employers’ National Insurance contributions. However, the research also recognised that there is no single childcare business model in operation across the sector, with each provider operating under different cost and income structures. A copy of the report can be found here:Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Market ResearchThe draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy aims to reduce childcare costs for parents by expanding the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, while also enhancing the long-term sustainability of providers. Proposals include the creation of a Childcare Business Advisory Service, offering tailored guidance to strengthen viability and to gain a better understanding of the financial needs of the sector, so that future supports can be tailored over time.  The Strategy also proposes a Strategic Childcare Accessibility Fund to improve access to early years services where demand outstrips supply, including disadvantaged communities, rural areas, and services for children with additional needs. In addition, a PSEP Strategic Support Fund will help address short-term pressures in strategically important non-statutory pre-school settings.Additional measures focus on workforce sustainability, including improved pay structures, commitments toward the Real Living Wage, clearer career pathways, and expanded professional development and training to address recruitment and retention challenges

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any specific measures, proposed within the Draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, to address recruitment and retention challenges within the early years workforce.
answer_text: The draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, currently issued for public consultation, sets out proposals that will support the recruitment and retention challenges facing the sector under Theme 3: Supporting the Early Years Sector and WorkforceKey proposals include:-The Development of a comprehensive early learning and childcare Workforce Plan to support the development of the current workforce and incentivise early learning and childcare as a valued career choice.Establishing a clear qualification and career framework with enhanced CPD and leadership pathways.Promoting professionalisation of the sector, including a representative body.In conjunction with the ‘Good Jobs’ priority in DfE’s Economic Vision, promote the Real Living Wage with early learning and childcare employing organisations.The full Strategy can be accessed here:draft Northen Ireland Early Learning and Childcare Strategy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment his Department has made of whether recent childcare subsidy uplifts have improved the financial viability of providers, as opposed to being absorbed by rising operating costs.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is designed to reduce the net cost of childcare for families. It is not intended to improve the financial viability of providers. The 15% subsidy is applied directly to parents’ bills and has remained unchanged since the scheme began in September 2024; no uplifts have been made to this rate.In April 2025, the maximum subsidy cap was uplifted by 10%, from £167 to £184 per child per month. This was to ensure that families whose childcare costs were close to the cap continued to receive the full 15% subsidy in the context of expected price increases. This adjustment benefitted parents only and did not alter the subsidy rate.At the same time, a separate uplift was applied to the administrative fee paid to providers. This payment is intended solely to offset the costs of administering NICSS, such as onboarding children, invoicing claims and verification. It is not designed to address wider operational cost pressures or to improve the financial viability of providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department plans to take to ensure that the expansion of wraparound childcare does not inadvertently undermine the sustainability of established after-school services.
answer_text: The draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, published on 17 December 2025,  sets out a long-term vision that supports quality, accessibility and sustainability across all early learning and childcare provisionto meet the needs of children and families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy seeks to balance improving affordability for families, with ensuring providers are able to operate on a financially sustainable basis.
answer_text: The independent Review of Childcare Services in Northern Ireland (RSM, 2023) identified clear financial pressures across the sector, including workforce recruitment and retention challenges linked to relatively low pay, increased operating costs and declining profitability. A copy of the report can be found here:RSM (2023) DE Review of Childcare Services in NI - Final Report.PDFSubsequently, the Department of the Economy's Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Market Research further examined business sustainability challenges. This report identified sustained pressures from energy, insurance and, in particular, staffing costs, including the April 2025 increases in the National Minimum Wage and Employers’ National Insurance contributions. However, the research also recognised that there is no single childcare business model in operation across the sector, with each provider operating under different cost and income structures. A copy of the report can be found here:Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Market ResearchThe draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy aims to reduce childcare costs for parents by expanding the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, while also enhancing the long-term sustainability of providers. Proposals include the creation of a Childcare Business Advisory Service, offering tailored guidance to strengthen viability and to gain a better understanding of the financial needs of the sector, so that future supports can be tailored over time.  The Strategy also proposes a Strategic Childcare Accessibility Fund to improve access to early years services where demand outstrips supply, including disadvantaged communities, rural areas, and services for children with additional needs. In addition, a PSEP Strategic Support Fund will help address short-term pressures in strategically important non-statutory pre-school settings.Additional measures focus on workforce sustainability, including improved pay structures, commitments toward the Real Living Wage, clearer career pathways, and expanded professional development and training to address recruitment and retention challenges

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement his Department has had with veterinary professionals in Northern Ireland in relation to the operation of the new regulatory arrangements for veterinary medicines since 1 January 2026.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland, including the operation of the new regulatory arrangements, falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has made any assessment to changes in the price of veterinary prescription medicines, following the end of the transitional arrangements governing their supply from Great Britain on 31 December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has identified any veterinary prescription medicines that are no longer supplied to Northern Ireland, following the end of the transitional arrangements on 31 December 2025.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment his Department has made of the availability of veterinary prescription medicines, since the new regulatory arrangements governing supply from Great Britain came into effect on 1 January 2026.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines, including any assessment of their availability in Northern Ireland, falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has identified any impacts on animal welfare arising from changes to the supply arrangements for veterinary prescription medicines since 1 January 2026.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.  		I am currently not aware of any impacts on animal welfare arising from changes to the supply arrangements for veterinary prescription medicines since 1 January 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any procedures followed by her Department where, a developer bond for private streets, is no longer sufficient to meet the cost of completing the streets to an adoptable standard.
answer_text: On the 25th September 2025 I announced the introduction of the new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance. The guidance establishes a threshold for the initiation of enforcement action of 2 years following 80% occupation of houses in a development.My Department has also published new developer information which amends the bond release thresholds. This change will enable my department to retain a larger proportion of the bond which provide the funds required to bring new developments roads infrastructure to an adoptable standard should enforcement action be necessary.The new guidance, implemented alongside the changes to the bond release thresholds, will protect homeowners by greatly reducing the risk of new developments remaining unadopted.It has to be remembered that all private streets sites are the responsibility of the developer until they are formally adopted into the public road network. However, my officials continue to engage with developers and encourage them to complete the sites to the required specification and proceed to adoption.My Department may consider enforcement when a developer has defaulted through the issuing of a Notice under Article 11 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980, outlining the outstanding work, and allowing the developer to take action towards completion of the site within 28 days The developer has a right to appeal under Article 12.In instances where there is non-compliance with the Article 11 Notice, my Department may take forward the necessary works and recover the costs under the provisions of Article 15 and adopt the road on completion of the work.If the Developer does not pay costs as detailed in Department's request or the developer is no longer trading, the Article 15 demand is served to the appropriate Bank or Insurance Company acting as surety for payment of costs incurred in bringing the development to an adoptable standard.If a site is not brought to an adoptable standard by the developer, and the funds in the bond are insufficient to complete the infrastructure to an adoptable standard, my Department is not funded to carry out such works. If the developer is no longer trading, the householders become the responsible person. If householders are willing to, they can pay for the works to compete to an adoptable standard.There are no time limits under current legislation for a developer to complete a site.Householders will be aware of the status of a housing development when going through the conveyancing process to purchase a property, through the details on a property certificate. All responsibility for a site, including the time frame to complete construction lies with the developer.The contents of recent revised enforcement guidance are for the department to issue notices to a developer, 2 years after 80% occupation of a site.Although there are no immediate plans to amend the legislation, the revised enforcement guidelines and uplift of bond calculation rates greatly enhance the department’s standing

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any evidence that underpins the introduction of a minimum mandatory six-month minimum learning period of Graduated Driver Licensing, before a driving test can be taken.
answer_text: The Mandatory Minimum Learning Period (MMLP) of six months is established by the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016.  Its purpose is to provide learner drivers with sufficient time to progressively and safely develop their knowledge and skills by completing a structured Programme of Training, evidenced through a logbook.  When combined with supervised practice, this increases the likelihood that novice learners will encounter night-time driving, poor weather, busy traffic, and other more complex situations, helping them become safer drivers.    Research supports that an MMLP provides a strong foundation for road safety.  Evidence drawn from data, international practice, and policy evaluations indicates that the aim of the learning period is not only to develop technical driving competence but also to support safer behavioural development.  This includes building an understanding of attitudes, emotions, and risk. A longer supervised period reinforces good habits, reduces risky behaviours, and importantly, helps ensure a learner’s confidence is more closely aligned with their actual ability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any exemptions that will apply under the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme for new drivers who are required to drive during night-time hours for employment, education or caring responsibilities.
answer_text: New drivers are permitted to drive at night, including for employment, education or caring responsibilities.  While doing so, however, night-time passenger restrictions will apply to new drivers for the first six-months after passing their test (drivers only, it does not apply to motorcyclists).  New drivers aged 17 to 23 will be restricted from carrying more than one passenger aged 14 to 20, between the hours of 11pm and 6am.  There is an exemption for immediate family members or where the driver is entitled to claim carer’s allowance in respect of a passenger. Additionally, the passenger restrictions will not apply if there is an accompanying person in the front seat who is aged 21 years or older, and who has held a full driving licence for three years.  No other exemptions are provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the Graduated Driver Licensing proposals will apply to new motorcyclists.
answer_text: Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) will apply to new learner motorcyclists from 1st October 2026.  Learning to ride a motorcycle can be enhanced by measures such as the Programme of Training and Logbook, motorway lessons when accompanied by an Approved Motorcycle Instructor (AMI) and the removal of the 45mph limit. Motorcyclists will not be subject to the Mandatory Minimum Learning Period as they must complete Compulsory Basic Training before undertaking the training introduced by GDL.  Newly qualified riders will be required to display the new 2-part ‘R’ plate for two years.  Passenger carrying restrictions will not apply to newly qualified riders.Given the complexities of age-related progressive access routes to the different categories of motorcycle, I have asked my officials to provide additional information through the Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) on GDL, published on the Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the proposed Graduated Driver Licensing restrictions will apply to individuals who already hold a provisional driving licence, prior to the commencement date of the new scheme.
answer_text: There will be a 6-month transitional period after GDL is introduced.  Learners with a provisional licence entitlement to drive a category B vehicle that commenced before 1st October 2026 and who pass the practical driving test by 1st April 2027, will be subject to existing restrictions, ie a 45mph speed limit and display of an amber R plate for 12 months. However, if such a learner does not pass a practical driving test by 1st April 2027, they will then be subject to GDL scheme rules.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has been made of the potential financial impact of the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme on learners and their families, including increased lesson costs and affordability in rural communities.
answer_text: The main cost in learning to drive is the cost of instruction. The introduction of the Mandatory Minimum Learning Period and the Programme of Training will encourage learners to take a steady and progressive path to learning to drive and learner drivers may, as now, still choose to learn to drive with a supervising driver, an approved driving/motorcycle instructor or a combination of both. While the training may require more lessons, depending on the individual, there is no requirement to take paid lessons. There will be an additional cost of £17 for the use of the app for the Programme of Training and Logbook.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department has taken to facilitate discussions between (i) Northern Ireland universities; (ii) the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs; and (iii) the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons, regarding the establishment of a veterinary medicine degree in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As autonomous institutions, the universities have discretion to offer any course, including veterinary medicine, from within existing student numbers and funding allocations.		However, should a proposal be put forward, I am open to working in partnership with all relevant stakeholders to explore the matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department has taken since publication of the January 2022 Strategic Investment Board situational analysis on a veterinary school in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Strategic Investment Board’s (SIB) situational analysis and baseline report in 2022, it was recognised that further work to advance a business case was required and this now rested with the third level educational institutes.  To date, whilst both Queen’s University, Belfast and University of Ulster (UU) have voiced an interest in offering a veterinary course, either separately or together, no viable proposals for a veterinary school in NI supported by a funded business case have emerged. I met with the Vice Chair of the UU to discuss their ambitions including any plans for introducing veterinary related courses in May 2024, and any considerations they had around a veterinary school within NI.No further discussions have been sought or undertaken by the third-level institutions with my Department on this matter however, DAERA remain committed, where requested, to discuss with and support educational institutes on relevant aspects related to DAERA’s responsibilities should they seek to progress a business case for a veterinary school in NI.  In addition, at a meeting with the Working Group for Reform in Veterinary Education in August 2024, the key causes of the challenges facing the veterinary profession were discussed, including the limited numbers of vets studying and qualifying in Ireland. After the announcement in 2024 of two new veterinary schools in Ireland by Patrick O’Donovan, Minister for Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science, and Charlie McConalogue, Minister for Agriculture, Food and Marine, I raised the issue of student mobility at the North South Ministerial Council so as to allow access to veterinary places offered in Ireland to students coming from NI.My Department contacted the Department for the Economy (DfE) in February 2025, who advised that if required they are happy to work closely with DAERA to provide support to the Universities to advance a business case for  the establishment of a veterinary school in NI, however they also confirmed that it would not be for the DfE to take a decision on, or to fund, a veterinary school.To date, no further actions, other to those outlined above, have been taken by my Department regarding a veterinary school in NI, and no viable proposals supported by a funded business case have emerged from the tertiary education sector. However, Queen’s University, Belfast have recently announced the launch of a BSc (Hons) Veterinary Biosciences degree programme which will be available from September 2026 and will respond to the growing demand for skilled professionals in veterinary science and animal health.Whilst DAERA is a key employer within the veterinary sector and as such has a vested interest in securing such a talent pipeline, issues around workforce planning for the veterinary sector sits within the remit of the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the (i) planning; and (ii) allocation, of Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools aligns with his Department’s Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda.
answer_text: The Department is committed to ensuring every child with Special Educational Needs (SEN) receives the right support and is placed in the right setting to help them reach their full potential. There is clear strategic direction to tackle both the immediate need for SEN placements and the long-term transformation of provision across the education system.The planning and allocation of specialist provision classes is guided by EA’s Strategic Area Plan 2022–2027, with Special Schools Plans of Action providing the roadmap for delivering the additional places needed over the coming decade. The Special Schools’ Plans of Action identify the need for over 5,000 additional specialist school places in addition to 6000 specialist provision places over the next decade, and projections show requirements could more than double by 2031/32.EA’s planning also accounts for a projected decrease in overall pupil numbers against an increase in the number of pupils with SEN and the complexity of their individual needs. The need for capital investment, however, continues to grow. Placements in the last two years have required investment of over £109 million, and the Department simply cannot meet this pressure and children’s needs within its existing level of capital funding whilst keeping all schools open and safe.Securing adequate investment remains vital to maintain the pace of this transformation and ensure Northern Ireland's education system can effectively support all children to reach their full potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any oversight arrangements in place to review decisions to establish new Specialist Provision in Mainstream classrooms by the Education Authority,
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The EA has comprehensive oversight arrangements to review decisions regarding the establishment of new Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools The EA maintains internal oversight through its Senior Leadership Team, ensuring that all proposals are subject to thorough scrutiny before progression. This provides an essential first layer of assurance that decisions are evidence-based and aligned with strategic priorities.All specialist provision proposals undergo a consultation process over an eight week period, engaging with relevant stakeholders and published on the EA’s consultation page. This ensures that decisions reflect the needs of pupils, families, schools and local communities. EA continues to work with all stakeholders when considering proposals in accordance with the established Framework and Strategic and Operational Plans.Following the consultation process, a consultation report is presented to the EA’s Strategic Planning and Policy Committee for approval of Specialist Provisions. This committee-level governance ensures informed and transparent decision-making.The EA provides a Statement of Assurance to DE confirming that appropriate consultation has taken place.These arrangements collectively ensure that decisions address geographical, sectoral and linguistic inconsistencies whilst responding to growing demand for specialist provision places across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools that currently have (i) no pupils; or (ii) significantly fewer pupils than anticipated, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Education Authority has advised that:The most recent (2024/5) verified census information including enrolments in specialist provision is available at School enrolment - school level data 2024/25 | Department of EducationWhere pupil numbers are below typical capacity, this may reflect a change in pupil profile or a strategic decision to maintain smaller class sizes to support wellbeing and effective learning. Any remaining available capacity is used by schools for reverse integration to support pupils within mainstream settings and to ensure in-year capacity remains available for those requiring a change of placement or to provide additional capacity for the incoming academic year.SEN provision is not a "one size fits all" model. As pupil needs evolve, demand for certain types of provision may decrease in some areas while increasing in others. The Education Authority continues to respond to these changes to ensure equitable access to education across all council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) demand modelling; or (ii) forecasting, undertaken by the Education Authority before approving the establishment of new Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority undertakes both demand modelling and forecasting to inform decisions on establishing new Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools.In planning for September 2026, forecast figures were derived using assumptions based on historical data, expert insights, and anticipated trends. Current data relating to children with special educational needs, referrals for assessment, and pupils at various stages of the Code of Practice have been considered. This data continues to be reviewed as milestones are reached and pupil mapping is completed.Planning assumptions project need in advance of all relevant information becoming available, allowing pressures to be identified early. The modelling accounts for children in transitional year groups, children recommended for placement changes, and new children going through statutory assessment. A key unknown factor remains the type of placement required, identified only through Annual Review or statutory assessment completion.While the overall school-age population is decreasing, the percentage of children with SEN is increasing. The EA is working to create approximately 900 places required for September. New places already in planning, combined with allowances for forecasting variability and demand being met through other means, reduces this by around 500. A shortfall of circa 350-400 placements remains, particularly across Belfast, Derry City and Strabane, Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon, Lisburn and Castlereagh, Mid Ulster, and North Down and Ards. Work continues at pace to progress additional projects and assess accommodation solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how changes in draft Net Annual Values, under Reval2026, for pubs and hospitality premises compare with changes for retail premises.
answer_text: Reval2026 to take stock and allow sectors the space to provide the evidence needed for a full examination of the issues. My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of FinanceHowever, based on the draft valuation work completed prior to that decision, it is possible to indicate how movements in Net Annual Values (NAVs) for pubs and hospitality premises compared with those for retail properties.Draft figures show that NAVs for retail properties increased by around 8% overall compared with the 2023 valuation List. In contrast, the hospitality sector showed significantly larger uplifts, with draft NAVs for pubs rising by approximately 47% and hotels by around 84%. These broad percentages are not representative of the changes at individual property level, as the table below demonstrates.AddressClass2023 NAVDraft 2026 NAVPercentage changeThe Diamond ColeraineShop£25,100£23,500-6.37%Bridge Street ColerainePub£8,000£10,000+25%The overall draft outcomes reflected property which had experienced both postpandemic trading recovery and, in some cases, physical extensions or improvements that increased the rateable value of individual properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether operating costs, including energy and staffing costs, are taken into account in the valuation of pubs and hospitality premises, for rates purposes.
answer_text: I can confirm that operating costs, including energy and staffing costs, are taken into account when valuing pubs and other hospitality premises for rates purposes. These properties are assessed using the Receipts and Expenditure (R&E) method, which is the same standard approach across England, Scotland and Wales for sectors where rental values are driven by trading performance rather than physical characteristics.Under this method, valuers estimate the Fair Maintainable Trade (FMT) at the statutory valuation date and deduct typical costs of sales and working expenses, including staffing and energy costs, to determine the divisible balance. A proportion of that balance represents the Net Annual Value (NAV), in accordance with Schedule 12 of the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977.I would direct the Member to my oral statement to the Assembly and subsequent debate in the House on 2 February:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of FinanceOfficial Reports I also placed a paper on the Receipts and Expenditure Methodology in the Assembly Library.Deposited Papers – reference 2034

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any valuation methodologies used by Land and Property Services when assessing pubs, and licensed premises, for the purposes of Reval2026.
answer_text: Land & Property Services uses the industrystandard Receipts and Expenditure (R&E) valuation method to determine the rental value of pubs and licensed premises, reflecting how these properties are let in the real market. As set out in my statement to the Assembly of 2 February 2026, I have stopped Reval 2026 in response to the concerns raised by businesses, including those in the hospitality sector, about the implications of proceeding with the draft valuations at this time. My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of FinanceA paper detailing the Receipts and Expenditure valuation method for valuing licenced premises was deposited in the Assembly Library on 9 February.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any (i) mitigation; or (ii) transitional arrangements, currently in place to support businesses facing significant increases in rates liability, as a result of Reval2026.
answer_text: I refer the member to my Oral Statement of 2 February 2026 at the link below:-https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/finance-minister-oral-statement-reval-2026

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department is monitoring the impact of increased Net Annual Values, under Reval2026, on eligibility for Small Business Rates Relief, for pubs and hospitality businesses.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my oral statement, and the subsequent debate in the Assembly on 2 February, regarding Reval 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he is taking to ensure people at the end of life are not required to wait three months to access the limited capability for work-related activity element of Universal Credit.
answer_text: I can confirm that benefit claimants who advise of an end of life condition where they have 12 months or less to live, and provide supporting medical evidence, are not required to serve the relevant waiting period for payments of Limited Capability for Work and Work Related Activity on Universal Credit. Further information is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-if-you-have-health-condition-or-disability

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will reintroduce a two-year longitudinal dental foundation programme, including a single lead employer model.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency has been exploring options for the potential reintroduction of longitudinal dental training programmes. Any such opportunities will need to be developed in consultation with key stakeholders, including trainees. Consideration will also take into account the longitudinal training pathways currently being piloted in England.My officials have recently approved a business case to expand NIMDTA’s educator capacity and to support the modernisation of the dental training programmes for early career dentists. Foundation Dentists are not included within the existing Single Lead Employer arrangements in Northern Ireland; however, this may be considered should these arrangements be expanded to other areas in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to address the limited exposure to Community Dental Services among newly qualified dentists.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans in place to secure the long-term future of Sandelford School, Coleraine.
answer_text: The school enhancement project at Sandelford continues to progress.  The original business case was approved by my Department in January 2024.  However, following my removal of the funding threshold for school enhancement projects in April 2024, the scope of this project has now been expanded.The initial scheme would have delivered two general classrooms, four specialist classrooms, and the removal of outdated mobile accommodation.  The expanded project will now provide an additional four classrooms, ancillary accommodation, and enhanced school meal facilities.  The project is currently expected to complete by September 2028, at which point the site will be at capacity. The long-term plan for the school is therefore the expansion of the school on a new site to provide places for up to 150 pupils.  Work is underway to identify available and suitable sites.  However, I must emphasise that both the current project and the longterm development plan are entirely dependent on the availability of capital funding. On 9 September 2025, I circulated a draft Executive Paper to other Ministers outlining that my department’s existing capital budget cannot absorb the £1.7 billion required over the next decade to deliver more than 6,000 new special school places and over 500 specialist provisions in mainstream settings. That paper set out proposals for a flagship, Executive-led and Executive-funded Special Educational Needs (SEN) capital investment programme. I am deeply disappointed that we have not yet had the opportunity for a meaningful Executive discussion on these proposals and I am even more concerned that the draft budget fails to ring-fence any funding for SEN capital investment, despite the scale and urgency of the need. I will continue to emphasise the need for cross-party support for my proposals to ensure that the long-term plans for Sandelford, and all special schools can be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any mechanisms in place to ensure consistent delivery of Relationships and Sexuality Education across all schools, particularly in relation to content on violence against women and girls.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) evaluates the quality of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) in schools. Council for the Curriculum Examinations and Assessment (CCEA)’s RSE Hub provides free, highquality resources, including materials on violence against women and girls. The nonstatutory CCEA RSE Progression Framework offers a pathway from Foundation Stage to post16, outlining appropriate topics for each Key Stage and directing schools to further guidance. It also includes information on domestic and sexual violence and abuse.(AQW 38707/22-27)CCEA provides a wide range of ageappropriate RSE resources which includes lesson plans, activities and short films to support a preventative curriculum. These cover topics such as violence against women and girls (VAWG), domestic and sexual abuse, coercive control, online harassment and misogyny, and are available on the CCEA RSE Hub.Key areas include:Consent:Six postprimary units explore communication, decisionmaking, risk, myths, misogyny, gender stereotypes, and the consequences of nonconsensual behaviour.Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse:Eight units cover types of abuse, its impact, online abuse, the law, VAWG (including SEN), and the Active Bystander approach. A new unit on upskirting, downblousing, cyberflashing and sextortion will be added, with further units on male victims and masculinity in development.Healthy Relationships:Resources help pupils recognise positive, unhealthy and romantic relationships, set boundaries, understand responsibilities, and gender issues in relationships.Internet Safety:Materials support teaching about online risks and how to protect yourself online, challenging harmful behaviour, and relevant laws. Topics include cyberbullying, sexting and the impact of pornography, with cyberflashing and sextortion addressed in the Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse section.The CCEA RSE Progression Framework provides a nonstatutory pathway from Foundation Stage to post16, outlining suitable topics, including Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse and VAWG. It also links to the Department of Health’s Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and The Executive Office’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.Coercive control is addressed at Key Stage 4 and revisited post16.(AQW 38708/22-27 & AQW 38710/22-27)	 	CCEA provides webinars to support teachers, featuring expertise from organisations working in domestic violence, consent and online abuse.		With support through the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy Programme fund 2025/26, CCEA is currently working with my Department to plan an event for school leaders in March 2026. The event is aimed at highlighting the role of leadership in embedding a whole school approach to tackling this issue at a school level through the promotion of healthy relationships, alongside showcasing best practice to shape and inform interventions that can be of use to schools when addressing

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the extent to which landlord sales of private rented properties are contributing to homelessness presentations.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an assessment of the extent to which landlord sales of private rented properties are contributing to homelessness presentations.According to the Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin, of the 8,217 households who presented as homeless between April to September 2025, the ‘loss of rented accommodation’ accounted for 1,197 (14.6%) cases. The most common reason for presentation was ‘accommodation not reasonable’ with 1,866 (22.7%), followed by ‘sharing breakdown/family dispute’ with 1,777 (21.6%).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 31507/22-27, to detail any interim measures in place to reduce the number of families with children in unsuitable, or hotel-style, temporary accommodation, while the planned 600 units are being acquired over a four-year period.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to addressing the supply and demand for temporary accommodation, including seeking to reduce the number of families with children in non-standard temporary accommodation, is outlined in the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation which can be found here: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/0d1aee01-f02c-40d6-b95b-a58c7213c2c0/Temporary-Accommodation-Strategic-Action-Plan-2022-27.pdf As a result of this work, approximately 324 self-contained properties have been added to the Housing Executive’s portfolio this financial year.The Housing Executive seeks to avoid placing families with children in non-standard temporary accommodation for more than two weeks and will identify alternative options at the earliest possible opportunity. The majority of families with children are placed in ‘own front door accommodation’ such as Single Lets, with approximately 97% of children in temporary accommodation living in such placements.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any guidance issued to the Housing Executive on ensuring that temporary accommodation placements for households with children are, as far as practicable, close to schools, childcare, employment and family support networks.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The decision regarding the suitability of temporary accommodation offered in the discharge of the homelessness duty is made in accordance with the Housing (NI) Order 1988. In determining suitability, Designated Officers within the Housing Executive will consider individual circumstances, the needs of the applicant, and will take on board any representation made on behalf of the applicant. The consideration of individual circumstances will include proximity to schools, childcare, employment and family support networks. The Housing Executive will then seek to provide a placement in a location that best fits the circumstances of that applicant.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average length of time households spend in temporary accommodation in (i) Northern Ireland; and (ii) the Causeway Coast and Glens area.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April - September 2025 contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2025. Section 3 provides data on households in temporary accommodation including current placements by banded length of stay and accommodation type (Table 3.5).I am unable to provide this information by Council area although Table 3.2A details the number of placements by Council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any current (i) targets; (ii) timescales; and (iii) funding commitments, to increase the supply of social and genuinely affordable homes throughout East Londonderry.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is committed to addressing housing need in the East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency through support for the provision of affordable housing including social rented housing delivered by housing associations via the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP).The Housing Executive carries out Housing Need Assessments (HNAs) on an annual basis. The total social housing need projection for the period 2024-29, based on HNA areas in the East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency, is 1,432 units.Much of the East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency falls within Causeway Coast and Glens Council. Currently, the Housing Executive is seeking a quota of social housing (a minimum of 20%) on residential sites in areas of social need across the Borough, as facilitated by Policy HOU2 of the Northern Area Plan 2016. A smaller area of the East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency falls with Derry City and Strabane District Council (DC&SDC), the Housing Executive is seeking a quota of affordable housing (a minimum of 20%) on residential sites in areas of social need across the Borough, as facilitated by Policy HOU5 of the DC&SDC Local Development Plan ‘Plan Strategy’. This will help to increase housing supply and support sustainable communities.In the East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency:There have been 35 new social housing completions to-date in 2025/26. There are currently 253 social housing units under construction. There are 458 social housing units programmed to start as part of the SHDP 2025/26 – 2027/28:102 units programmed to start in 2025/26.314 units programmed to start in 2026/27.42 units programmed to start in 2027/28.Funding is available to support the delivery of 1,750 new social housing starts across Northern Ireland via the SHDP in the 2025/26 programme year, including the 102 units programmed to start in the East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency.”More widely the Executive Housing Supply Strategy has an ambition to deliver 100,000 homes in NI over 15 years, of which a third should be social. Local Councils are responsible for considering their own affordable housing needs for their areas, in conjunction with NIHE place shaping teams, through the development and delivery of Local Development Plans.I continue to help people into home ownership and last year, I announced a further £153M in funding for Co-Ownership to support 4,000 people in buying a home over the next 4 years through its Co-Own product.All Co-Ownership products operate on a “DIYSO” (Do It Yourself Shared Ownership) basis where customers can select a home of their choice anywhere in Northern Ireland up to the property value limit of £210,000.In East Londonderry there were 30 Co-Ownership completions in 2024/25 and 23 completions between April 2025 and Septemb

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to reduce ambulance handover delays at Emergency Departments, to ensure ambulances return to service promptly.
answer_text: My Department recognises the challenges faced by emergency departments (EDs) and has worked closely with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays. To help alleviate pressures in EDs, my Department has implemented a range of targeted initiatives. These include the expansion of urgent care centres, Phone First Services and minor injury units, offering patients timely alternatives to ED attendance.Ahead of the present winter period, my Department worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and Trusts to appoint an additional 18 emergency medicine consultants to work in EDs across Northern Ireland. A further ten consultants completed their training in December 2025 and it is anticipated that they will also be recruited locally to provide further resilience and senior decision-making capacity to our EDs.I am determined to find better ways to work as a system to tackle waiting times in EDs. That is why I initiated a series of winter planning workshops to identify solutions to the system flow issues that we collectively face. I attended the first of these events on 4 March 2025 and was encouraged by the engagement and enthusiasm from HSC leaders across the system. The workshop saw attendance from a diverse group of stakeholders within our system, including Royal Colleges, clinicians, and other frontline services. In August 2025, my Department held a regional workshop with senior management and clinicians from Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), which focused on improving ambulance handover times. Input was provided from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital in London where they have been successful in reducing ambulance handover delays.  My Department is working with NIAS and the Trusts to finalise ambulance handover guidance, based on the experience of the London model in order to improve ambulance handover times and release ambulances more quickly to respond to calls in the community.Trusts in Northern Ireland have been asked to implement changes to move towards this protocol. Incremental improvement targets for Phase 1 were agreed with Trusts that by 1 December 2025 there would be no ambulance handover delays in excess of two hours outside an ED, whilst also ensuring there was no deterioration in delays in excess of 15, 30 and 60 minutes. Once the 2-hour milestone is achieved, we will move towards meeting the 15-minute standard.Since the introduction of the phased approach, there have been around 800 fewer handover delays exceeding two hours compared to the same period in 2024. In December, several hospitals demonstrated strong performance against the 2hour threshold, with less than 20% of ambulance arrivals delayed over two hours. This included the Mater Hospital (8%), South West Acute Hospital (10%), Altnagelvin Hospital (13%), Daisy Hill Hospital (15%), and the Royal Victoria Hospital (16%), which reflects f

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of ambulance staff vacancies; and (ii) the number of ambulance operational hours that were lost due to hospital handover delays, in the last 12 months;, and (iii) any plans in place to reduce this figure.
answer_text: I acknowledge the outstanding dedication of staff in the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS). These professionals deliver a high-quality service under extremely challenging circumstances, often supporting people at the most critical moments of their lives. My Department is working with NIAS to ensure sufficient ambulance and staffing capacity to meet Category 1 and Category 2 response time standards. An independent review of the NIAS Clinical Capacity and Demand has just been completed, and this will inform the service model in the future. NIAS are finalising an implementation plan, and some of the key strands - including the development of the advanced paramedic role - have already commenced, with the first 15 staff having started their training in Autumn 2025. As part of this commitment, my Department has increased the number of paramedics on the Degree Programme from 50 to 65.  The number of current NIAS staff vacancies, as at 23 January 2025, is shown below.Table 1GradeDivisionParamedicEMTTotal Emergency VacanciesACATotalBelfast10.488.7619.2415.2053.68Northern-1.2914.5213.235.0031.46Southern2.671012.6714.7240.06Western2.025.497.513.4018.42South Eastern19.5622.2541.813.4787.09NIAS33.4461.0294.4641.79136.25The number of lost operational hours, due to handover delays, for the period 1 January 2025 – 31 December 2025 (12 months) is shown in the table below.Table 2Ambulance Handover DelaysDateLost HoursJan-2513290Feb-2510075Mar-259499Apr-259555May-2510366Jun-259852Jul-259995Aug-259497Sep-2510131Oct-2511303Nov-2510463Dec-25118472025 Total125872Reducing ambulance handover delays at emergency departments (EDs) is a key priority due to the impact on NIAS’ ability to respond to calls in the community. The importance of timely ambulance responses was highlighted in the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine.Average and longest ambulance handover waits were lower in 2025/26. It should be acknowledged that there was an early and significant flu season with cases starting to increase in November 2025 and peaking in the first week of December. Flu cases remained high throughout December with episodes higher than 2024/25. Seasonal peaks in respiratory illness will inevitably place additional pressure on hospitals.Vaccination rates, especially for those over the age of 65 (72.4%) and in care homes (79.3%) were good. Additional support has also been provided to care homes by Trusts in the administration of antivirals to residents where flu outbreaks occurred. This intervention is aimed at reducing admissions from care homes.Ambulance handover delays are influenced by system-wide pressures, particularly during periods of high demand. The recent flu surge contributed to increased hospital admissions and reduced bed availability, which in turn affected the ability to offload patients promptly at EDs.Reducing ambulance handover delays is a key priority for my Department, as prolonged delays impact NIAS’ capacity to respond to calls

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a 24/7 urgent care triage service, similar to NHS 111, across all Health and Social Care Trusts to reduce pressure on ambulances and Emergency Departments.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether measures such as (a) Hospital Ambulance Liaison Officers; (b) rapid handover protocols; and (c) cohorting areas, are being used consistently in Emergency Departments; and (ii) any plans in place to ensure their effective implementation.
answer_text: Hospital Ambulance Liaison Officers (HALOs) have a key interface role between the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and Trusts ensuring good communication and helping to expedite patient handover. In the largest emergency departments (EDs) - the Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH), Ulster, Antrim, Craigavon and Altnagelvin Hospitals - NIAS provide HALO cover on Monday to Friday from 7.00am to 11.00pm (16 hours), and either 7.00am to 7.00pm or 9.00am to 9.00pm (12 hours) Saturdays and Sundays. HALOs located at these sites also provide oversight of smaller sites in each of the respective Trusts. There will be periods where, from an operational perspective, NIAS are not always able to provide a HALO presence on the hospital sites. When this happens, NIAS will work to provide other cover through station managers and the NIAS control room. NIAS and the Trusts also join daily calls seven days a week, and the Regional Control Centre (RCC) manages any interface issues, dealing with them in a timely manner.Regional Ambulance Handover Protocols aim to ensure a standardised, safe and efficient transfer of patient care from ambulance crews to ED clinical staff, minimising delays and associated risks to patient outcomes and ambulance service capacity.The national standard for ambulance handover states that patients arriving by ambulance should be handed over to ED staff within 15 minutes, with the ambulance cleared and ready to respond within a further 15 minutes.  Any handover that exceeds 15 minutes is considered a delay.  Reducing handover delays is a critical priority, given the direct impact on the NIAS’ ability to respond to emergency calls in the community.In August 2025, my Department held a regional workshop with senior management and clinicians from Trusts and NIAS, focusing on improving ambulance handover times. Input was provided from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital in London, where they have been successful in reducing ambulance handover delays. My Department is working with NIAS and the Trusts to finalise the ambulance handover guidance/protocol for Northern Ireland, based on the experience of other ambulance services including the London Ambulance Service. In order to support improved ambulance offload and response times, my Department has repurposed £10 million to fund targeted initiatives across Trusts. These measures include:The expansion of Hospital at Home services in Belfast, South Eastern, and Northern Trusts, increasing community-based capacity;Additional short stay inpatient beds (for example, eight in Altnagelvin) and intermediate care beds (for example, 12 in the Western Trust) to facilitate timely discharge;Frailty assessment and multidisciplinary team reviews at the front door of acute hospitals;Expansion of Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways across acute medicine, respiratory, cardiology, and surgical specialties; and, Enhanced capacity for Early Review Teams and Discharge Coordination.Ultimate

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of private ambulance providers that are contracted by the health and social care system; and (ii) the amount that private ambulance provision has cost, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Independent Ambulance Services (IAS) are procured by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) through a regionally agreed Framework Agreement administered by the Business Services Organisation’s Procurement and Logistics Service (PaLs).The Framework Agreement was implemented in November 2023 following a regional tendering process involving NIAS and all five Health and Social Care Trusts.
