IAS providers support scheduled care services and loweracuity unscheduled care through a nonemergency framework.There are currently 15 IAS providers contracted under this framework.The cost to NIAS of IAS provision (after recharges to other Trusts) in each of the last three years is shown below.Table 1YearCost2022-23£ 8,883,6742023-24£ 7,098,4772024-25£ 9,917,540Independent providers are primarily used for nonemergency patient transport, which helps protect NIAS urgent and emergency care resources. Work is ongoing with my Department to reduce dependence on this provision through the development of updated eligibility criteria for nonemergency transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered automatically enrolling adults with a learning disability onto Universal Credit, when they are required to move from Employment and Support Allowance.
answer_text: Anyone moving to Universal Credit since migration commenced in October 2023 has been required to make a fresh claim. This is necessary to ensure all aspects of entitlement are captured as Universal Credit replaces a range of legacy benefits and Tax Credits. People who are unable to make or manage their own Universal Credit claim due to a learning disability may have another individual or corporate body appointed to act on their behalf.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department has taken to ensure that Universal Credit communications issued during the move from Employment and Support Allowance are accessible for adults with a learning disability, including the use of clear language, and formats designed to reduce distress, and confusion.
answer_text: The Move to Universal Credit of legacy benefit claimants is supported through a range of communications, primarily migration notification letters, an awareness campaign and information online at nidirect.gov.uk. The migration notification letters were developed following extensive user testing and Advice Sector consultation to ensure the language and messaging used was as clear and easily understood as possible. Alternative formats of communication materials are available on request and all web content is considered in line with the appropriate Accessibility Regulations and the language used is suitable for a reading age of 9 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any training Universal Credit advisers receive to support vulnerable adults with a learning disability.
answer_text: All Universal Credit front line staff receive comprehensive training to ensure they can support claimants with complex needs, including vulnerable adults with a learning disability. This training includes detailed material on identifying and responding to complex needs, guidance on the range of support available and skills-based learning designed to enhance their ability to provide tailored assistance to ensure that claimants receive appropriate and effective support throughout their Universal Credit journey.To further strengthen this learning and enhance capability, my Department’s Work Psychology Service, in partnership with Mencap, developed and delivered staff capacity building events on supporting customers with learning disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how claimants with a learning disability are identified, when moving from Employment and Support Allowance to Universal Credit; and (ii) any safeguards in place for those without family, or informal support.
answer_text: When a claimant makes a new claim to Universal Credit they will be asked to declare their circumstances which will include any health conditions they may have. All claimants moving from Employment and Support Allowance to Universal Credit will receive a migration letter which includes details of a range of support available including the Universal Credit helpline, and signposting to independent advice. As part of the migration journey, enhanced support arrangements are also in place, with additional touch points both prior to and after making a claim, to ensure claimants are able to make and maintain their Universal Credit account. This can include additional support telephone calls, appointments in a local Jobs & Benefits office and a home visit if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any specific support currently in place to assist adults with a learning disability, who are required to move from an Employment and Support Allowance award to Universal Credit, particularly at the point of initial application.
answer_text: All claimants moving from Employment and Support Allowance to Universal Credit will receive a migration letter which includes details of a range of support available to make their application including the Universal Credit helpline and signposting to independent advice. As part of the migration journey, enhanced support arrangements are also in place, with additional touch points both prior to and after making a claim, to ensure all claimants are able to make and maintain their Universal Credit account. This can include additional support telephone calls, appointments in a local Jobs & Benefits office and a home visit if required.Tailored support can be accessed by adults with a learning disability or their appointee by contacting their local Jobs & Benefits office. This support is available at the point of initial application and at any stage thereafter. The full list of Jobs & Benefits office locations is available on nidirect and the Department for Communities websites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average distance from the home of individuals placed in respite or interim care due to a lack of domiciliary care; and (ii) the number of such placements, (a) over 20 miles; and (b) over 40 miles, from home, in the last year, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trusts have indicated that to provide this information, a manual trawl of case notes would be required. This trawl would require a significant investment of resource and time, therefore this information is not readily available.  The Trusts do seek to ensure that when an interim care home placement is considered due to a lack of domiciliary care that the following factors are always considered:Patient choice Carer preference e.g. for ease of visiting Actual bed availability and/orAvailability of appropriate category of care bedWhilst I am unable to provide specific data in respect of individuals being placed at lengthy distances from their homes and communities, I trust the response has provided a measure of reassurance as to the factors taken under consideration by the Health and Social Care Trusts in approaching such decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients who had a delayed discharge due to the unavailability of domiciliary care; and (ii) the average length of such delays, in each of the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Officials contacted each of the HSC Trusts for the response set out below;BHSCTNumber of Patients delayed due to unavailability of domiciliary careAverage length of delay (over 48 hours in days)Jan-25597.1Feb-25658.5Mar-25888.5Apr-25818.5May-25739Jun-25729Jul-25827.8Aug-258511.1Sep-25949Oct-259010.3Nov-25938.3Dec-25879.6*Belfast Trust note that they discharge approximately 200 patients who require homecare per month in under 48 hours. NHSCTNumber of Patients delayed due to unavailability of domiciliary careAverage Length of delay (in days, to include the 48-hour target timeframe)Jan-25404.7Feb-25365.5Mar-25465.1Apr-25584.3May-25715.6Jun-256671Jul-25863Aug-25724.4Sep-25733.9Oct-25884.2Nov-25664.3Dec-25745.8SEHSCTNumber of Patients delayed due to unavailability of domiciliary careAverage length of delay (over 48 hours in days) Jan-251162.83Feb-251143.75Mar-251072.54Apr-251093.63May-251173.21Jun-251022.04Jul-25993.96Aug-25953.46Sep-251102.13Oct-251262.17Nov-251192.17Dec-251382.67*SET note, there are many variables and caveats to be factored in which raw data cannot account for, i.e. the length of time delayed may not have solely been due to unavailability of a homecare package. Transport issues and orders for equipment and medication are all reasons which could have contributed to delayed discharge without being coded as the primary reason.SHSCTNumber of Patients delayed due to unavailability of domiciliary careAverage length of delay (over 48 hours in days) Jan-254435.69Feb-253945.72Mar-25578.88Apr-256214.48May-255314.49Jun-255912.29Jul-256313.32Aug-255611.16Sep-255316.88Oct-2511213.72Nov-256815.07Dec-257415.46WHSCT*WHSCT have indicated they are unable to provide the information as requested as the Trust is still working on a data validation process following implementation of Encompass.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that (i) individuals do not lose their domiciliary care package following hospital admission; and (ii) care can be reinstated promptly on discharge.
answer_text: Earmarked funding is provided for previous Executive commitments, such as Flagship Projects and City and Growth Deals.In addition, my draft Budget proposes some earmarked allocations for Programme for Government (PfG) priorities. It is anticipated that departments will also use a proportion of their non-earmarked, or general, budget towards PfG priorities. The York Street Interchange does not fall into the category of a previous Executive commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken to improve recruitment and retention of domiciliary care workers, including in rural areas, given that travel time and coverage can present additional challenges.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts have an ongoing recruitment process in place for home care staff.  In addition, the Trusts and the Independent Sector Providers run targeted recruitment in areas of high demand as and when required, this includes rural areas where it can be difficult to secure adequate staffing levels.  There are a number of factors that present particular challenges to the recruitment and retention of home care staff. These challenges include the unsocial element of the role (a service that is deployed 365 days per year), the challenges of lone working, the requirement to be a car driver with access to a car and of accruing very high mileage and the increasing complexity of service user’s needs and the associated responsibilities involved for home care care staff.   Over the past five years, my Department has focused on providing additional financial support to the independent home care sector across Northern Ireland with a view to attracting more staff into this area of work.  In 2023/2024 my predecessor, Minister Swann had approved a 7.93% uplift for the Independent Sector Home Care Providers, which uplifted the hourly rate for home care to £18.54 per hour.  Subsequent to this, in 2024/2025 a £70 million support package for social care providers and hospices was agreed.  This led to a further uplift in the hourly rate for home care to £20.01.  This decision had been made in recognition of the important role played by Independent Sector providers and their requirement as an employer to pay the National Living Wage.In March 2025, I approved a 6.7% uplift for the Independent Sector Home Care minimum hourly rate which brought the minimum hourly rate for homecare to £21.35.  There is a continuing focus on the need to further uplift this rate in line with the Real Living Wage.  This is based upon a recognition of the valued work that homecare workers carry out for many of our most vulnerable people and also in recognition of the need to both stabilise the current homecare workforce and to attract more people to work in this sector.The initiatives put in place by the work of the Social Care Collaborative Reform Board (led by my Department), have adopted a particular focus on maximizing and growing home care.  Some of these initiatives include: Right-sizing packages of care for individuals and re-cycling care hours back into the system to create more capacity.Focus on prevention and early interventionProvision of support through multi-disciplinary teams attached to an individual’s GP and local neighbourhood networks.Promotion of all elements of Self-Directed Support to empower individuals to manage their own care needs.Introduction of the Social Care Workforce Strategy 2025-2035Notwithstanding the work being carried forward in this area, the challenges continue to be considerable. I would ask that you and other MLAs continue to offer assurance to your constituents that improving services for our families and communities remains a prio

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department takes to ensure that interim placements, including for respite care made due to a lack of domiciliary care, are located as close as possible to a person’s home.
answer_text: The responsibility for provision of interim and respite placements rests with the host Health and Social Care Trust.The Belfast Trust has a range of contracts in place with care home providers which are within the Belfast geographical area. Where it is possible to do so, the Trust strives to facilitate placements as close to people’s homes as possible.The Northern Trust has embarked on an ambitious Strategic Reform of Home Care Services, with a focus upon increasing and maximising home care capacity.   If a contingency placement is required when a package of care is not available, the Trust will endeavour to ensure a care home is secured in the service user’s home of choice. However, due to challenges with care home capacity and suitability, this is not always possible. In such instances, the Trust will work with service users and families to source a placement as close as possible to their preferred location.In the South Eastern Trust, when it is not possible to secure a homecare package for the delivery of care in a person’s own home, it is at times necessary to arrange an interim placement to await the care package.  Whilst every effort is made to secure an interim placement close to the person’s home, this is not always possible as options are dependent upon care home availability.  Any interim option will be discussed with the person and/or next of kin, and the Trust will continue to prioritise these requests to facilitate a return home as soon as possible.In the Southern Trust, access to respite or an interim care bed due to a lack of access to a home care package is dependent on a number of variables including; Patient Choice, Carer preference e.g. for ease of visiting Actual bed availability andAvailability of appropriate category of care. The placement selected is agreed in partnership and with consent from the individual service user and family, as appropriate.The Western Trust makes every effort to ensure that if a domiciliary care package is not immediately available, clients are placed in a care home that is as close to their home address as possible and will involve the client and their family/carer in these discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of increased reporting of domestic and sexual violence, on the criminal justice system capacity.
answer_text: Between 20 December 2025 and 2 January 2026, the PSNI received 1,407 domestic abuse calls, an increase on the 1,290 calls recorded during the same period in 2024–25. This represents an increase of approximately 9.1%.The figures show that demand for police support was consistently high across the two-week period, with call volumes exceeding those recorded across most days in the equivalent periods in 2023–24 and 2024–25. New Year’s Day recorded the highest number of calls, at 116, followed closely by Christmas Day with 115 calls, and 27 December with 114 calls.These figures underline that the heightened risk associated with domestic abuse does not end on Christmas Day but continues throughout the holiday period and into the New Year.In relation to the impact of increased reporting on criminal justice system capacity, my Department keeps demand and pressures across the system under ongoing review, working closely with the PSNI, the PPS, NICTs and NIPS. Increased reporting is, on the one hand, a sign that more victims are coming forward to seek help and protection, which is vitally important. However, it also inevitably places additional pressure on police, prosecutors, courts, prisons and support services.Through the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which my Department is delivering jointly with the Department of Health, we are taking a whole-system approach focused on prevention, early intervention, supporting victims and holding perpetrators to account. This includes work to improve pathways through the justice system, strengthen victim support, and ensure that services are better equipped to respond to both rising demand and complexity.It is also important to note that reported crime figures do not necessarily provide a direct measure of prevalence, as changes may reflect greater confidence in reporting as well as changes in underlying offending. My Department will continue to work with justice partners to monitor trends, assess pressures on capacity, and ensure that the limited resources available are targeted where they are most needed.Any level of domestic and sexual abuse is unacceptable, and these figures reinforce the importance of sustained, year-round action across government and the justice system to protect victims and prevent abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any specific delivery milestones, set for 2025 under the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, (i) that have been achieved; (ii) where shortfalls remain.
answer_text: The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024–2031 launched in September 2024 and is being delivered through a multi-year action plan covering Years 1 to 3. Many of the actions are deliberately phased and sequenced, reflecting the fact that a number of the actions require preparatory work, engagement with delivery partners and, in some cases, legislative decisions or funding before they can be fully implemented. The Strategy is jointly led by my Department and the Department of Health, with the Department for Communities, the Department of Education and The Executive Office acting as key delivery partners.In terms of strategic governance, substantial progress was made during 2025 across a wide range of key commitments:Governance and oversight arrangements for the Strategy have been fully embedded, with the Strategy Oversight Board and Expert Reference Group meeting regularly and reporting aligned with the Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) structures;Funding for 2025/26 has been allocated; a further Small Grants Scheme has been launched, enabling voluntary and community sector partners to deliver projects that support victims and survivors of domestic and sexual abuse or help prevent abuse in the first place.In relation to criminal justice delivery my Department and its partners continue to make strong progress:The Children’s Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (CSOLA) service was launched in February 2025 and is already providing specialist support to children and families. The adult SOLA scheme has been extended until 2027 and continues to support thousands of victims. New advocacy support service contracts commenced in 2025 for both adults and children and young people.My Department is continuing to oversee the Domestic Homicide Review process under which a new multi-agency group has been established to drive system-wide improvements in line with recommendations. The Department has also published its statutory training statement under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act to show that professional involved in policing, prosecution, courts and victims support are trained to understand domestic abuse.The Department secured additional PPS resources for Domestic Abuse Contest Courts, which are achieving improved conviction rates. Within the prison system, specialist Women’s Support and Trauma Support Worker services are now in place to support women who have experienced domestic and sexual abuse.My Department also provided funding for and working in partnership to develop the Power to Change campaign which seeks to tackle the underlying behaviours and attitudes that can facilitate gender based violence.  The campaign was launched jointly by the Chief Constable, Junior Minister Cameron and me in January 2025.Significant work has also been progressed towards the establishment of new arrangements to replace the existing Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conferences (MARAC).  This has included significant engagement on a cross-secto

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the effectiveness of specialist sexual violence support provision in East Londonderry; and (ii) whether any gaps have been identified.
answer_text: My Department has a current contract with Nexus NI who provide a regional service which offers counselling for adult victims and survivors of sexual abuse. This service which is available in the East Londonderry area provides a period of counselling for victims and survivors of sexual abuse. Following an initial assessment each individual will be offered up to 12 counselling sessions, based on presenting need. Counselling services in the region are delivered from two locations in Coleraine and one location in Derry/Londonderry.Referrals to the service will be from a range of agents, including GPs, Health and Social Care Trusts, and individuals who self-refer. This provision is currently specifically focused on supporting individuals who have been victims of sexual abuse and are at a stage of their journey where therapeutic interventions are most likely to have the best outcome. In addition to face-to-face counselling, Nexus NI also offer telephone counselling and virtual counselling via Microsoft Teams. At the point of referral, clients can select their preferred method of delivery and service location. This choice is not always linked to home address; for example, individuals may choose to attend a service location closer to their workplace.From 1 April 2025 to date, a total of 425 individuals (adults and children combined) have been supported through counselling services in the East Londonderry area. Outcomes continue to be positive, with most clients who engage in counselling reporting improvements in wellbeing and overall functioning.At present, the waiting list in the East Londonderry area comprises:31 adults awaiting counsellingNo children or young people currently on the waiting listRegionally the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services work therapeutically with children and young people who have experienced mental health issues secondary to having experienced child sexual abuse. My Department currently procures specific therapeutic support for children and young people who are potentially vulnerable to Child Sexual Exploitation through Barnardo’s who operate regionally. My Department also supports the National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NSPCC) through the Department of Health’s Core Grant. Although this Grant does not specifically procure a Therapeutic Service, it does support the NSPCC to deliver their services which includes Therapeutic Support.The Moving On and Recovery Service is based in the Western Health and Social Care Trust area which covers the East Londonderry constituency.Adult counselling services are primarily funded by my Department through the Nexus contract (up to June 2027).  A procurement exercise is underway to commission this service thereafter.There is currently no confirmed funding beyond March 2026 to sustain the adult crisis de-escalation service delivered by Nexus NI, which is currently being funded by the Department of Justice. Counselling provision for children and young people is re

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any short-term actions that have been delivered under the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls framework, since its launch.
answer_text: Since the launch of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework we have delivered a wide range of actions. The Local and Regional Change Fund are driving meaningful change across communities and organisations, with visible impact in schools, sports clubs and the community and voluntary sectors. The Power to Change Campaign, jointly launched by TEO, DoJ and the PSNI in January 2025, targets harmful attitudes and behaviours of men and boys and uses the active bystander approach. The pilot stage of the coercive control campaign has now been completed, and it is planned for regional rollout in 2026. We have also established key-sectoral working groups to identify actions to improve outcomes for women and girls in educational, workplace, social and nighttime settings. We will continue to prioritise actions that deliver the long term, transformational change needed across society to end violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any immediate measures her Department has put in place to improve the safety of women and girls in East Londonderry, following recent reports of alleged serious sexual offences in Coleraine.
answer_text: I am very aware of the understandable concern in the local community following recent reports of alleged serious sexual offences in the Coleraine area. However, I want to be clear that ensuring the safety of women and girls is a priority for both my Department and for the Police Service of Northern Ireland.I am aware that, operationally, the PSNI keeps local threat, risk and harm under constant review and, where necessary, deploys additional visible policing, targeted patrols and other protective measures in response to emerging concerns. Decisions about such deployments are, quite properly, a matter for the Chief Constable based on the professional assessment of risk and intelligence.From a Departmental perspective, my officials continue to work closely with the PSNI and with partners across government and the voluntary and community sector to ensure that victims have access to support and protection. This includes ongoing investment in domestic and sexual abuse support services and funding for specialist support organisations.More broadly, my department along with the Department of Health is delivering the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024–2031, alongside the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework led by The Executive Office. These frameworks are focused on prevention, protection, justice and support, and on improving both the immediate and long-term response to violence and abuse.I would strongly encourage anyone who has information about these or any other offences, or who feels unsafe, to contact the PSNI, and I would also encourage victims to seek support from the specialist services that are available to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his decision in relation to the removal of emergency general surgery from Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: On 18 December 2025, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group chaired an Emergency General Surgery Causeway Summit. This meeting brought together senior leaders from the Northern Trust, Western Trust, and NIAS at Chief Executive and Director level.The purpose of the summit was to consider the potential impact on these organisations should emergency surgery at Causeway Hospital close and to enable system-wide planning and collaboration ahead of any Ministerial decisions regarding changes to Causeway Hospital’s emergency surgical services. At the meeting it was agreed that further work was required to review patient flows, and an independent analysis will be commissioned to model the way forward. This includes consideration of the opportunities afforded within Causeway Hospital, taking account of available theatre capacity.It should be noted that it is planned that there will continue to be surgical services on the Causeway Hospital site including a consultant surgical presence.  Plans also include Causeway continuing to have a Surgical Assessment Unit and the Trust aims to extend its opening hours to enable as many surgical patients to be assessed and treated in Causeway Hospital as possible. National bodies, such as GIRFT suggest that emergency surgical assessment at the front door of hospitals result in 30% fewer general surgery emergency admissions each year, as patients are managed in an ambulatory way. Causeway will also maintain its elective ward and theatre sessions and discussions are well underway with the SPPG to expand the elective surgical capacity. These discussions are around commissioning a third inpatient theatre at Causeway Hospital. This will allow the Causeway Hospital to be become a Regional Elective Overnight Stay Unit. A further update from this work will be due in early March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any provision of Relationships and Sexuality Education that will be delivered under the Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls strategy.
answer_text: Developing healthy, respectful relationships is essential to ensuring that children and young people gain the knowledge, understanding, and skills needed to make informed life choices and act responsibly in their relationships and behaviour.The Curriculum already places a strong emphasis on healthy relationships and through Relationship and Sexuality Education (RSE), schools can play a key role in addressing emerging issues from the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, such as violence, misogyny, coercive control, and sexual harassment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether the Executive Office plans to prioritise data collection on coercion, intimidation, and blackmail, in cases of sexual abuse in departmental monitoring, and strategy implementation.
answer_text: The Executive Office does not have a role in collecting data on cases of sexual abuse. This sits with the Department of Justice and its agencies.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any measures their Department will take to ensure that the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls strategy is fully implemented and adhered to across all relevant Departments.
answer_text: The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework sets out a cross-government approach to tackling gender-based violence, with success reliant on coordinated action and clear accountability. As a key priority within the Programme for Government (2024–2027), it underscores the Executive’s firm commitment to driving meaningful change.We remain fully committed to delivering the Framework, with oversight provided through the EVAWG Oversight Board, chaired by Junior Minister Reilly and Junior Minister Bunting. Membership includes Permanent Secretaries of all NICS Departments which ensures informed decision making and accountability.Departments are embedding EVAWG principles in all relevant policies, strategies, and service delivery. Progress is subject to regular reporting to Ministers, the Executive, and Assembly Committees. An update on progress of the Strategy is made to the Executive twice a year. We will continue to work collaboratively with all Departments to ensure that the Strategy is not only implemented but embedded as a core part of government policy and practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the data her Department holds on the use of (a) blackmail; (b) coercion; and (c) intimidation, to silence victims of sexual abuse; and (ii) whether this relationship is being considered as part of the Executive’s work to address sexual violence.
answer_text: I recognise that these types of behaviour may be present in some cases of sexual violence. Each case is unique and is considered on its own merits by the courts, where such behaviours are taken into account when determining the circumstances and the impact of the offence. However, the way in which data is currently collected is limited to offence descriptions. As such, identifying and capturing these behaviours in cases of sexual violence would require a manual review of each individual case, which would be at a disproportionate cost.My Department holds data on offence descriptions such as blackmail. However, there is currently no mechanism in place within our data systems to identify whether an offence of this nature is linked to a sexual abuse context. In other words, while offences are recorded according to their legal classification, it is not possible to disaggregate or cross-reference those cases to determine if the behaviour of blackmail, coercion, or intimidation was used specifically to silence or control a victim of sexual abuse.While this type of information is not currently available, I recognise that perpetrators of sexual abuse may employ a range of behaviours to threaten, manipulate, or deter victims from coming forward. These may include acts of intimidation or blackmail, which are criminal offences in their own right. Such tactics are part of the wider pattern of control and fear that can prevent victims from seeking help or engaging with the justice process. Working with partners, my Department has already put in place, or is working towards, a range of measures under the Gillen Review, the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and under the draft Victims and Witnesses Strategy that are intended to support victims of crime, including victims of sexual violence, through the criminal justice process in order to encourage them to continue to engage with the criminal justice system.Anyone who believes that they have been a victim of any form of crime, including those linked to sexual abuse, is strongly encouraged to report this to the Police Service of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans her Department has to improve public and survivor confidence in the criminal justice system.
answer_text: It is a key priority for me as Justice Minister to increase public confidence in the justice system and my Department is working with criminal justice partners to deliver a significant body of transformational work to achieve this.I recognise the importance of tackling delay, as one of the biggest challenges facing the criminal justice system, and as an issue which directly impacts confidence in the system. The Criminal Justice Board has prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay, which will include full delivery of committal reform, work in the digital arena and early engagement, court remits, and out of court disposals.I also intend to publish a new Victims and Witnesses of Crime Strategy in the New Year.  This is focused on delivering tailored support to victims and witnesses at the right time, and building a balanced, trauma-informed and rights-compliant justice system.  One of the strategy’s five pillars is focused on victim rights and raising confidence. The strategy’s Action Plan sets out a detailed programme of work to address the priority areas that were identified through engagement and consultation with victims, witnesses, representative organisations and stakeholders.  In addition, the Victim and Witness Charters set out the entitlements and services that victims and witnesses of crime in Northern Ireland can expect to receive from the criminal justice system.  There have been significant efforts to increase awareness of the Charters by my Department, the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime, and partner organisations.  Work is also well advanced on developing a framework for collating and publishing statistical victim data. This will drive performance improvement, but will also help to demonstrate how criminal justice organisations deliver Charter rights, with the aim of increasing transparency and improving confidence.I also intend to introduce a Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill to the Assembly next April, which will focus on a small number of very significant measures which will have a positive impact on victims and witnesses of crime. The Bill will establish a statutory Commissioner for Victims and Witnesses of Crime to represent all victims and witnesses of crime, with robust legislative powers and duties, and with a focus on identifying systemic issues across the criminal justice system and making recommendations for change. It will also include new provisions and protections for victims and witnesses in cases involving serious sexual offences and others in hate crime cases. Much work is also being done to protect, support and improve the experiences of particularly vulnerable victims of crime:measures under the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy are intended to protect those at risk, but also to support increased reporting and reduced attrition, by ensuring that victims know they will be listened to and supported, and that robust measures are in place to hold perpetrators to account;transform

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what measurable progress has been made against the ten-year Substance Use Strategy in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, since its publication.
answer_text: In line with the regional direction set by the Department’s Substance Use Strategy, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust has partnered with the Public Health Agency (PHA), the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), and other regional stakeholders to advance a broad range of actions identified as priorities within both the Strategy and the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan. Key areas of progress include:Piloting the Treatment Outcomes Profile (TOP) tool—the regionally agreed outcome measurement tool to be used within the HSC Encompass system. This NHSCT pilot aims to support more outcome-focused data collection and promote effective information sharing and governance across substance use services.Improving access to treatment and support services for vulnerable women and individuals in the pre- and post-natal period. NHSCT substance use and maternity services are working collaboratively across service boundaries to strengthen links between maternity (including neonatal) and substance use services, ensuring that treatment services actively reduce barriers for women and those with childcare responsibilities.Collaborating with SPPG and regional partners to implement key recommendations from the Independent Review of Regional Tier 4 Services. This includes working with SPPG and other stakeholders to develop a new service specification in line with a 3-year procurement plan, that will address inconsistencies in referral pathways, improve access to Tier 4 rehabilitation beds, and support the development of a Regional Specialist Assessment, Treatment, and Recovery Service for Substance Use.Reducing substance-related harm through an innovative pharmacy-based pilot, which will provide brief interventions and referrals for individuals at risk from over-the-counter (OTC) analgesic medication in the NHSCT area.Contributing to the Regional Co-occurring Mental Health and Substance Use Task and Finish Group to develop a comprehensive work plan. This plan will build capacity in both mental health and substance use services, ensuring they are equipped to meet the full needs of individuals with co-occurring issues in the NHSCT area and regionally.In support of the strategy, the Department also publishes a regular update on the indicators, available online at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/substance-use-strategy-indicators-summary-report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of addiction treatment beds available in the (i) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of funding allocated to addiction support services in the (i) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) Western Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last three years.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment she has made of the effectiveness of the interdepartmental work with the Department of Health in reducing drug-related deaths, since the launch of the Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery Substance Use Strategy.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) leads on the Substance Use Strategy “Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery” which was co-produced across government departments, including my Department, agencies, health professionals, the community and voluntary sector and service users. This strategy has a specific outcome focussed on harm reduction, including drug deaths.To this end, my Department works closely with the PSNI, the Organised Crime Taskforce (OCTF) Drug Sub-Group and DoH on a range of key actions.Through the work of the OCTF, my Department supports the ongoing efforts of law enforcement partners to tackle drug-related harm in our communities. The OCTF operates under a strategic structure comprising a Strategy Group—made up of senior representatives from statutory and law enforcement bodies—which provides oversight across several thematic subgroups. One of these, the OCTF Drugs subgroup, is chaired by the PSNI and includes representation from my Department and DoH. Meeting quarterly, the Drugs subgroup brings together key agencies to coordinate a strategic response to emerging threats related to drug misuse. It also facilitates joint enforcement activity and ensures that those involved in the illicit supply and distribution of drugs are effectively targeted. Notably, drug-related deaths are a standing agenda item at each meeting, reflecting the group's commitment to addressing the most serious consequences of substance misuse.Another specific action within the Substance Use Strategy addresses the reduction of violence and intimidation towards drug users by paramilitary and vigilante groups. In support of this, the Public Health Agency was commissioned by the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, alongside DoH, to undertake a scoping exercise on drug-related intimidation across Northern Ireland. This involved extensive engagement with affected individuals, families, and professionals across justice, health, housing, and youth sectors. The resulting Drugs Related Intimidation report NI Drug Related Intimidation in Northern Ireland: Nature, support needs and how to respond | A Summary Report was launched last month at a joint event attended by myself and the Health Minister.The DoH-led Drug and Alcohol Monitoring and Information System (DAMIS) is also supported by the Public Health Agency, the PSNI, Forensic Science Northern Ireland, and my Department. DAMIS serves as an early warning mechanism to detect emerging trends in substance use, enabling timely and informed responses. It monitors a range of indicators, including sudden increases in the use of specific drugs, changes in consumption methods, the appearance of new substances, and the emergence of drugs with unexpected or harmful effects. This collaborative approach ensures that relevant information and advice can be rapidly disseminated to those at riskMy Department is also a member of the Substance Use Strategy Programme Board, chaired by DoH, with this engagement helping to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of drug-related deaths recorded in East Londonderry, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Drug-related deaths are defined using the UK National Statistics definition where the underlying cause of death recorded on the death certificate is drug poisoning, drug abuse or drug dependence.  Drug-related deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision) using codes F11–F16, F18–F19, X40–X44, X60–X64, X85 and Y10–Y14. The most recent annual statistics on drug-related deaths cover the registration year 2023. Figures for 2024 are currently being compiled and are expected to be published in early 2026.Table 1: Number of drug-related1 deaths registered in the East Londonderry Constituency by registration year, 2019 – 2023Year of RegistrationNumber of Drug-Related Deaths2019142020720211220221220236Drug-related deaths are defined using the International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision) codes F11–F16, F18–F19, X40–X44, X60–X64, X85 and Y10–Y14.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the timeline and objectives of the General Dental Services Cost of Service Review; and (ii) whether its recommendations will be implemented to ensure the sustainability of NHS dentistry.
answer_text: The cost-of-service review is underway and is expected to be completed by the end of March 2026. It is being carried out by Professor Ciaran O’Neill, Professor of Health Economics at QUB.The objective of the review is to identify the cost per hour of running a dentist’s chair to deliver Health Service treatment within a dental surgery in Northern Ireland.The outcome will be a report detailing the cost per hour of running a dentist’s chair to deliver Health Service dentistry in a majority Health Service practice in Northern Ireland. The review is intended to provide a robust evidence base to inform and shape future policy and investment decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review and update General Dental Services fees to reflect the appropriate cost of providing dental care.
answer_text: A cost-of-service review of General Dental Services is currently underway. The review is intended to provide an independent assessment of the actual costs incurred by dental practices in delivering Health Service dental treatments and will inform future decisions on fee structures as part of  evidence-based reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any immediate measures he will introduce to prevent further decline in access to NHS dental care.
answer_text: To improve access to dental care for both registered and unregistered patients I have invested £7million in General Dental Services in 2025/26. The £7million investment is funding the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions, and root canal treatments, and an additional £1.6million to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care. Officials are also continuing to scope further actions and initiatives aimed at improving access to Health Service dental care for both registered and unregistered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to improve (i) morale among; and (ii) retention of, health service dentists.
answer_text: I acknowledge the challenging environment that dentists are operating under and appreciate the commitment of practitioners who have continued to provide an essential service under difficult circumstances.While I am pleased to have been able to make additional investment in General Dental Services in the last two years, it is clear that significant reform is required to improve the service for both patients and practitioners.The opportunities to progress reform in recent years have been limited by a number of factors including available resources and the need to address immediate pressures. I have been clear in my commitment to make progress in this area.The reform of General Dental Services has been included in my Department’s Health and Social Care three-year plan. The first step in this process is a cost-of-service review, which is currently underway and will provide a robust evidence base to inform and shape future policy and investment decisions.Creating an environment that allows dentists to thrive and remain motivated to treat Health Service patients will be key to securing the long-term future of General Dental Service and meeting the Oral Health needs of the people of Norther Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the British Dental Association’s evidence that current Health Service fees do not cover the cost of providing treatment; and (ii) to detail any action he will take to address this.
answer_text: I acknowledge the British Dental Association’s (BDA) longstanding concerns regarding the adequacy of Health Service dental fees in covering the cost of providing treatment and ensuring the financial viability of Health Service dentistry. That is why I commissioned a cost-of-service review which will help inform the future direction of General Dental Services. The review is intended to provide an independent assessment of the actual costs incurred by dental practices in delivering Health Service dental treatments and represents a critical step towards evidence-based reform. I am clear that in common with other services, General Dental Services will require sustained effort to ensure sustainability over the longer term.  The ability to implement transformative change however is significantly constrained by the Department’s £600 million budget deficit, which limits the scope for transformative change.Despite this, in June this year I announced £7m investment for 2025/26 aimed at supporting dental practices while longer-term reform is developed.  In addition, a further £2.5million has been made available for General Dental Services to offset the impact of increased National Insurance contributions on General Dental Services. These measures reflect my Department’s ongoing commitment to sustaining dental provision, despite significant financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment that has been made of the effectiveness of the slurry spreading closed period, in improving water quality.
answer_text: The closed period for slurry spreading is implemented by the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations which are reviewed and amended where necessary on a four yearly basis to ensure they remain effective.A comprehensive scientific review of the current closed period for slurry spreading was carried out by Agri Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) in 2020 which concluded that the current evidence base supports the closed period as the most effective strategy for minimising the risks associated with slurry spreading from 15th October to 31st January.The fourth ongoing NAP review is based on this scientific evidence and proposes that the current closed spreading period dates remain effective for the region.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department plans to ensure that slurry spreading regulations remain appropriate, in light of changing weather and climate patterns.
answer_text: The slurry spreading regulations are contained within the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations which contain a range of measures on the storage, spreading and management of livestock manures and chemical fertilisers. The NAP Regulations are reviewed on a four yearly basis to ensure they remain effective and are amended where necessary.The fourth NAP review is currently underway and has assessed the slurry spreading components of the regulations to ensure they remain appropriate. Several revisions have been proposed including a reduced maximum application rate in October and February and increased use of Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment (LESSE).A slurry spreading information system is also being considered. This would help support implementation of the requirement in the current regulations which prohibits slurry spreading when heavy rain is forecast within 48 hours.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department ensures consistency and fairness in enforcement between agricultural and non-agricultural pollution incidents.
answer_text: My Department’s approach to enforcement is underpinned by the DAERA Enforcement Policy, which includes the principles of proportionality and consistency in relation to the significance of the offence. All reports of water pollution are investigated as potentially criminal offences with appropriate enforcement applied regardless of the business sector involved.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions his Department has had with (i) the Department for Infrastructure; and (ii) NI Water, on taking a coordinated approach to reducing agricultural and wastewater pollution, in the last two years.
answer_text: In June 2025, I discussed my department’s proposal to withdraw from the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent (SORPI) with the Minister for the Department for Infrastructure (DfI). My department had notified the DfI and Northern Ireland Water (NIW) in April 2025, inviting comments; both responded. I will continue discussions with the DfI Minister on this matter in the coming weeks.I engaged with the DfI Minister during preparations for the Third Cycle River Basin Management Plan, published in June 2025. My officials are collaborating with DfI and NIW to address wastewater-related water quality impacts. This work will be strengthened by the outputs from the Northern Ireland Environment Agency’s wastewater regulatory reform programme. I have met with the DfI Minister on these matters and will continue discussions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement his Department has had with farmers regarding the practical challenges of meeting slurry spreading deadlines, during recent wet conditions.
answer_text: My Department has had no representations from, or engagement with, farming representatives or farmers about practical challenges related to slurry spreading regulations during recent wet conditions. In September, the average recorded rainfall in Northern Ireland was 144mm, which is higher than average, while the first half of October was generally dry. The summer months prior to this were generally favourable for slurry spreading, allowing most applications to be completed before the autumn and the subsequent closed period. While the closed period is approximately 16 weeks duration, farms are required to have a minimum slurry storage capacity of 22 weeks. This is to ensure farms have sufficient storage capacity to allow for when weather and soil conditions are unsuitable for slurry spreading either side of the closed period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure consistency in clinical standards between private and health service autism assessment services.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment his Department has made, of the impact of delays in autism diagnoses, on children’s access to special educational needs support in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that with the implementation of the Local IMPACT Teams, a delay in autism diagnosis should not prevent a child or young person from receiving appropriate support in school.Local IMPACT Teams offer a wide range of support to schools at both the whole-school level and Stage 1 and 2 of the SEN Code of Practice. This includes access to autism advice lines for educational settings and parents, autism-specific training for staff, drop-in clinics, and solution-focused hubs. These resources are designed to help schools respond effectively to the needs of autistic children and young people, regardless of where they are in their diagnostic journey. This process is needs-led, meaning that a diagnosis of autism is not required for a child to receive support. Instead, provision is based on the presenting needs of the child or young person.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment he has made of the impact that reliance on private autism diagnoses is having on equality of access to services.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to increase assessment provision and reduce waiting times for autism diagnosis across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: On the 22 May my Department published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation. The Framework promotes a person centred and needs based care pathway that will allow for a holistic early offer of support to help with presenting needs.Health and Social Care Trusts are reviewing and refining assessment pathways to reduce clinical time per case while maintaining quality. Assessments are aligned with NICE guidelines to ensure consistency and clinical robustness. Plans are ongoing with PHA and Child Health colleagues to develop regionally defined neurodevelopmental pathways. This will allow earlier intervention needs to be assessed and help define service capacity requirements.To promote a specific focus on Adult pathways, a dedicated project lead for Adult Autism was appointed in March 2025. Since recruitment, they have been working with Trusts to map current diagnostic and support pathways, and hosted a regional workshop on 2nd July to explore steps that can be taken to manage waiting lists and reduce variation within existing resources. Department officials meet monthly with HSC Trusts Heads of ASD Services. These meetings are structured separately for adult and children’s services and provide a forum where professionals can participate in regional discussion to explore demand-capacity challenges and consider opportunities for standardised pathways. Services are observed to be adapting to increasing complexity in referrals characterised by more children presenting with comorbid ASD and ADHD and requiring more intensive assessment accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department ensures that individuals who receive a private autism diagnosis have access to the same supports and services as those diagnosed through Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Health and Social care Trusts follow the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP), to manage autism referrals. IEAP is a regional guidance document issued by the DHSSPS, updated in June 2020.The purpose of the Protocol is to define the roles and responsibilities of all those involved in the elective pathway, to set out how data should be recorded and reported, and to outline good practice to assist staff in the effective management of outpatient appointments, diagnostic, elective admissions and Allied Health Professional (AHP) bookings, including waiting list management. Referrals are managed in line with clinical and chronological need.IEAP allows for clinical discretion to be made as to whether to discharge or reappoint patients following non-attendance or repeated cancellations. Flexibility can be exercised by administration staff for unforeseen circumstances which result in non-attendance e.g. family bereavement.IEAP guidelines encourage the timeliness, equity and appropriateness of treatment for all patients/clients. Referrals for autism assessment are defined as routine, and therefore under IEAP are managed in chronological order by referral date. Access to HSC ASD services and supports is through the waiting list in each Trust. Service users on an ASD waiting list who seek a private diagnosis or are accepted onto an ASD waiting list with a private diagnosis are managed in chronological order of their referral, in line with IEAP guidelines. Trusts will evaluate the private assessment process against compliance with National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines. If the diagnostic assessment complies with NICE guidelines, the diagnosis will be accepted and ongoing support will be provided. If the assessment does not meet with NICE guidelines, HSC clinicians will complete the diagnosis. Having a private diagnosis will not affect an individual’s position on the waiting list for assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider further restricting the use of fireworks to specific times of year or licensed community events, to ensure they are used safely and to reduce distress to animals and vulnerable people.
answer_text: My Department is not currently considering a review of the legal framework, which seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public.  The Department grants licences for fireworks displays throughout the year for various celebrations.  Applications are made for private functions, community events and also from those who wish to have a display at their private address.  Halloween is the busiest time of year for fireworks applications from the general public.  Appropriate checks are carried out, and a licence will only be issued when it has been deemed safe to do so.  A fireworks licence includes conditions on use, including date and time restrictions on when the fireworks can be used.There is a balance to be struck between those who enjoy fireworks and those who do not.Information on firework safety and the availability of low noise fireworks is available all year round on the government services webpage nidirect.  In a recent press release I urged people to be mindful of the whole community particularly vulnerable people and animals when using fireworks.Any concerns about the misuse of fireworks or the purchase of fireworks without a valid licence should be reported directly to the PSNI who enforce any breaches of regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the effectiveness of current fireworks licensing arrangements, in preventing misuse and protecting animal welfare.
answer_text: I have no plans for an assessment of the existing fireworks licensing arrangements.  The current regulations provide a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public.  A condition can be added to any licence, where appropriate, to notify neighbours and/or animal owners e.g. farmers in advance of the display.  Information is available year-round on the nidirect fireworks information webpage reminding the public to be considerate to others when using fireworks, especially vulnerable people and animals.The licensing system aids partners, including PSNI, to identify unlicensed and illegal fireworks use.  Offences committed under the firework regulations may result in a fine of up to £5,000, or a prison sentence, or both.Any concerns about the misuse of fireworks should be reported to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure the public clearly understand the laws governing fireworks use and the potential impact on animals and vulnerable people.
answer_text: Information on fireworks, including the licensing requirements and fireworks safety, is available all year round on the government services webpage nidirect.	Halloween is the busiest time of year for firework displays and in September I issued my annual reminder to be considerate to others, especially vulnerable people and animals, when using fireworks.  As Halloween approaches, regular reminders are being issued by the Department and other agencies, such as PSNI, NIFRS and local councils, promoting the firework safety messages via social media platforms.	The Department’s messages encourage the public to follow the law, keep fireworks safe and be considerate to others and also advises that low-noise fireworks are available and, by making a simple change it could help make Halloween more enjoyable for neighbours and reduce the distress to animals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will engage with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to develop a coordinated approach to reducing the impact of fireworks on animals.
answer_text: The Department is conscious of the impact that fireworks can have on animals.  Where circumstances require, a fireworks licence may be issued with a condition that landowners/farmers are informed in advance of the firework display.Through my annual Halloween messages, and our government services webpage nidirect, my Department reminds the public to be considerate to neighbours and animals when using fireworks, as well as raising awareness that some fireworks manufacturers have a range of low noise fireworks available to purchase.My Department welcomes the views or input from colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs; however, I am not currently considering a change to the law on fireworks in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what assessment has been made of the impact of fireworks on farm animals and wildlife; and (ii) whether further measures are being considered to mitigate that impact.
answer_text: I am aware of the potential impact from using fireworks on all animals, especially during the Halloween season.  My Department conducts a social media campaign prior to Halloween to inform the public about the impact of fireworks on animal welfare and to offer advice on reducing stress and anxiety for animals.The Department of Justice is the licensing authority for fireworks in Northern Ireland. It has advised that it is conscious of the impact that fireworks can have on animals and that where circumstances require, a fireworks licence may be issued with a condition that landowners/farmers are informed in advance of the firework display. Through annual Halloween messages, and information available all year round on the government services webpage NI Direct, the Department of Justice also reminds the public to be considerate to neighbours and animals when using fireworks, as well as raising awareness that some fireworks manufacturers have a range of low noise fireworks available to purchase.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps her Department is taking to align apprenticeship availability with regional workforce demand, as identified in the Skills Barometer 2023–2033.
answer_text: My Department’s funded apprenticeship programmes are demand-led, with employers establishing apprenticeship opportunities in line with business need.Apprenticeships are delivered by a regional network of training providers who are in tune with local labour market requirements and are able to respond to current and anticipated demand from employers.  The number of training providers delivering the ApprenticeshipsNI programme was recently expanded by a further 10 organisations, helping to further increase regional provision.The Department collaborates with 18 established Sectoral Partnerships to design apprenticeships, from Level 2 to Level 7, that address current industry needs and future labour requirements highlighted by the Skills Barometer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department provides dedicated support or incentives for employers who take on apprentices with special educational needs.
answer_text: In my Statement to the Assembly in June, I outlined proposed reforms which, subject to the necessary investment, will improve and expand my Department’s pathways and supports for school leavers with special educational needs (SEN).  I am committed to making progress on this important issue, which is also reflected as a priority in the Programme for Government.  Securing funding for new and enhanced provision is crucial. My officials have led on the development of a collaborative bid to the Transformation Fund, in partnership with the Departments of Health, Communities, Education and Infrastructure. That Bid includes provision for the development of enhanced Transitions support and a SEN Apprenticeships Model. I have also ensured it is reflected in my Department’s Spending Review bids.On the current apprenticeship model, an incentive payment is available for employers upon the successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework. This payment ranges from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area. Whilst there are currently no additional dedicated payments specifically for employers taking on SEN apprentices, apprentices with a disability are supported. Disability Support Suppliers are contracted by the Department to work in conjunction with training suppliers delivering the ApprenticeshipsNI Programme and, a disability supplement may be payable to the Training Contractor to provide additional resources in terms of training time, equipment, or support for the apprentice to benefit fully from training.Similarly, disability support is made available to eligible participants to help them overcome the barriers that they may face when accessing the off the job training element of the Higher-Level Apprenticeship programme.  Training Providers may submit a request on behalf of participants for support such as non-medical help, specialist equipment, other general support, or a combination of the above.Additionally, apprentices with disabilities, including SEN, as employees are eligible for work-based support from the Department for Communities (DfC) where required, such as Access to Work funding for workplace adjustments. DfE also provides training support to address disability-related needs, ensuring employers receive the necessary resources to support these apprentices effectively.Looking forward, my Apprenticeship Action Plan, published in August 2025, includes the Supported Apprenticeship pilot, which will be designed to maximise apprentices’ potential within employment, considering the range of factors that impact participation, employment and achievement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the action her Department is taking to ensure that young people with special educational needs are not excluded from apprenticeship opportunities, due to difficulties in securing employer placements.
answer_text: In my Statement to the Assembly in June, I outlined my proposed reforms which, subject to the necessary investment, will improve and expand my Department’s pathways and supports for school leavers with special educational needs (SEN).  I am committed to making progress on this important issue, which is also reflected as a priority in the Programme for Government.  Securing funding for new and enhanced provision is crucial. My officials have led on the development of a collaborative bid to the Transformation Fund, in partnership with the Departments of Health, Communities, Education and Infrastructure. That Bid includes provision for the development of enhanced Transitions support and a SEN Apprenticeships Model. I have also ensured it is reflected in my Department’s Spending Review bids.Apprenticeships are built on the principle of employment first, meaning individuals are hired into substantive roles from the outset and gain skills through a combination of work-based learning and formal training.However, we know employment rates for SEN leavers are too low. To address this, our Apprenticeship Action Plan, published in August 2025, includes the Supported Apprenticeship pilot, which will be designed to maximise apprentices’ potential within employment, considering the range of factors that impact participation, employment and achievement.Additionally, the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund, supports collaborative projects to design innovative practices for under-represented groups, including those with disabilities, to improve participation and address placement barriers. The fund has very intentionally brought together employers, educational institutions, voluntary and community organisations, and government to bring collective expertise to the table to create pathways to success and supportive environments that enable individuals to thrive and reach their full potential.My Department has also worked closely with the Department for Communities on the development of the draft Disability and Work Strategy which is currently out for consultation.  The Strategy includes several actions and commitments for which my Department has ownership under the theme of “Inclusive Skills, Careers, and Educational transitions”, aligning with efforts to enhance opportunities for all, including those with special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she plans to introduce new incentives, such as wage subsidies or tax relief, to encourage employers to take on apprentices, particularly in trades and technical sectors experiencing shortages.
answer_text: My Department funds the directed, off-the-job training component of apprenticeships, with annual funding in the region of £48 million across the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes. This funding is focused on ensuring that employers are not financially liable for the delivery of apprenticeship training.  Businesses are also eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the apprentice’s successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area. This is a 20% increase under ApprenticeshipsNI 2025 compared to the previous ApprenticeshipsNI 2021 programme. This incentive is designed to encourage employers to support their apprentices through to full apprenticeship completion.  In addition, employers in the North do not need to pay Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under 25, provided certain conditions are satisfied. Tax relief sits outside the remit of my Department. However, in the recently published Apprenticeship Action Plan there are a range of measures to support the continued growth of the apprenticeships system including piloting new Apprenticeship Navigators help support business employ their first apprentice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has assessed the financial or administrative barriers preventing small and medium-sized employers from engaging in apprenticeship schemes.
answer_text: My Department works closely with employers to develop the apprenticeship system through a wide range of sectoral partnerships and regional groups to support the design and promotion of apprenticeships.Participation in the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeship programmes is at an all-time high, with ApprenticeshipsNI starts rising by 70% from 2019/20 to 2023/24. Higher Level Apprenticeship starts at FE colleges have increased by almost 200% from 2017/18 to 2023/24, while HLA starts at our universities have risen by over 130% from 2018/19 to 2022/23.	This suggests that many employers are turning to apprenticeships. However, a key benefit of apprenticeships is that employers are developing talent in line with their needs. Whilst the delivery model of centrally contracted providers seeks to minimise adminstative burdens, the development of talent through on- the-job learning brings a degree of financial and development focus within employers – which is more challanging for smaller employers. 		The Apprenticeships Action Plan seeks to build on the success of the apprenticeship system to enable more employers to access and benefit from apprenticeships. It includes actions to help more employers recognise that investing in an apprentice is of longer term beneft to the business; a commitment to undertake a strategic review and research into the effectiveness and experience of apprenticeships, including any barriers experienced by employers; and the need to provide the specific support employers need with a commitment to pilot Apprenticeship Navigators to support SMEs employing their first apprentices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any data his Department currently collects on second home ownership and short-term holiday lets; and (ii) whether his Department intends to improve the quality, and availability, of this data to better inform housing policy.
answer_text: Officials from my Department are currently carrying out research on second home ownership to improve our understanding of the issues. Regarding short-term holiday lets, my officials have been working closely with colleagues in the Department for the Economy and the Department for Infrastructure to scope out research on the nature, scale and potential impacts of short-term holiday lets on local housing markets, which in turn will help to identify further policy options.  It is hoped to commission this research shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the Primary one intake numbers ,for each of the last five academic years for schools in (a) Portstewart; and (b) Portrush.
answer_text: Please see table below for year one enrolment figures for primary schools located in the Portstewart and Portrush areas 2020/21 – 2024/25.School Name 2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Carnalridge Primary School, Portrush1125141714Mill Strand Integrated Primary School, Portrush5356554836Portrush Primary School1624282115Portstewart Primary School2830342536St Colum's Primary School, Portstewart2521261926St Patrick's Primary School, Portrush3291611Source: NI school census

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions his Department will take to support the long-term sustainability of coastal communities experiencing housing pressures from second home ownership, and short-term holiday lets.
answer_text: My Department is currently carrying out research on second home ownership to improve our understanding of the issues. In addition to this, officials from my Department are working with colleagues in DfE and DfI to scope and commission research on the nature, scale and potential impacts of short-term holiday lets on local housing markets. Action to address locally specific issues and ensure long term sustainability of local communities is primarily taken forward through the Local Development Plan process. Local Councils are responsible for bringing forward appropriate policies for their own areas in their Local Development Plans to support the creation of balanced communities and ensure the spatial growth requirements of the local area can be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has assessed the economic and social impact of short-term holiday lets on year-round communities, particularly in coastal areas.
answer_text: The self-catering accommodation sector has seen a significant evolution in recent years, including short-term, self-catering holiday lets.
