Short-term holiday lets have contributed to the growth in tourism across the North, attracting visitors who may not have otherwise considered certain destinations. They provide flexibility for visitors and income for property owners.NISRA statistics from 2024 indicated that 10% of overnight trips taken by external visitors used ‘Rented Accommodation’.The positive economic impact filters through local communities, with accommodation owners utilising local services and supply chains, and guests spending in local businesses which boosts local economies.I recognise that the significant increase in the number of self-catering properties, and broader challenges impacting on housing supply, has led to an increased interest in how short-term lets for visitors interact with local housing markets and communities.I am committed to supporting the Executive's Housing Supply Strategy. My officials are working with officials in the Departments for Communities and Infrastructure as DfC prepares to commission research on how short-term lets may affect local housing markets. The completion of this research will help inform the development of any potential policy interventions by Executive Departments in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work her Department has undertaken, to date, in the cross-departmental group considering short-term holiday lets, including any recommendations or proposed actions for her Department, to help protect housing availability in coastal towns, such as Portstewart.
answer_text: While the lead role on housing policy rests with the Department for Communities (DfC), my officials continue to engage on the cross-departmental working group comprising DfC, DfI and DfE.  The group is currently working to finalise Terms of Reference to enable DfC to commission research. The purpose of the research is to provide a more informed understanding of the relative impact, if any, of short-term/holiday lets in the North on housing supply.  It is the current intention that a final research report will be available later next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to review and strengthen the management of storm drainage infrastructure located near rivers, to reduce the risk of harmful discharges.
answer_text: My Department is currently bringing forward the Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill which includes additional powers for NI Water in relation to drainage miscommunications and private drainage infrastructure which will help improve protection of the environment from pollution.I have also secured £15 million from the Public Sector Transformation Fund to develop and deliver a pilot project that will use nature-based solutions to help transform our urban drainage systems.  The aim is to provide a range of nature-based drainage and flood management solutions to demonstrate their effectiveness in managing the flow of water through urban areas, helping to reduce flood risk and reduce pollution.NI Water also works closely with the NIEA in the investigation and rectification of the misuse of surface water drains which leads to pollution of surface water outfalls (PSWO).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any storm drains or discharge pipes, managed by her Department or its agencies, have been assessed as part of the investigation into the recent chemical pollution incident in the Ballykelly River.
answer_text: I have been advised by Minister Muir that DAERA was aware of the recent water pollution incident in the Ballykelly River, with the Loughs Agency leading on the investigations. DAERA staff have supported the Loughs Agency on checks of the local stormwater system, testing of foul sewers, and door-to-door checks, which have found no evidence of pollution.Investigations indicate the incident being caused by a short duration discharge of a domestic chemical agent that quickly passed through the system.NI Water was aware of the investigations conducted by NIEA and the Loughs Agency. Further investigations were carried out by NI Water in the surrounding area, with the storm sewers working as designed and no evidence of any chemical agents or other inappropriate material.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any investigations under way to determine how a cleaning substance entered the Ballykelly River.
answer_text: The Loughs Agency led the investigation, supported by my staff. Checks of the local stormwater system, testing of foul sewers, and door-to-door checks found no evidence of pollution. The incident appears to have been caused by a short duration discharge of a domestic chemical agent that quickly passed through the system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether additional water quality testing will be carried out in the Ballykelly River following the recent chemical pollution incident.
answer_text: My department has no plans for additional water quality testing. The Loughs Agency led the investigation, supported by my staff. Investigations pointed towards the incident being caused by a short duration discharge of a domestic chemical agent that quickly passed through the system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action being taken to confirm (i) that water in the Ballykelly River is safe for public use; and (ii) that local wildlife is protected, following the recent chemical pollution incident.
answer_text: (i) My department currently has no concerns with drinking water quality of either public supplies through Northern Ireland Water Ltd or private supplies, following this recent incident.(ii) The Ballykelly River is not in itself a protected area; however, does flow into the Lough Foyle Area of Special Scientific Interest (ASSI)/Special Protection Area. Following the pollution incident, my officials, at low tide, visited the Lough Foyle ASSI mudflats, where the Ballykelly River discharges into the sea. No concerns for wildlife, birds or ASSI protected features were noted.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31824/22-27, to detail what specific measures his Department is taking to ensure that women are clearly informed that cervical screening does not detect ovarian, womb, or other gynaecological cancers, given the common misconception that smear tests check for all women’s cancers.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency’s (PHA) public facing information and resources for all screening programmes are kept under regular review and are updated as necessary. The PHA has confirmed they will update the information provided on the NIDirect website to advise that cervical screening does not detect ovarian cancer. PHA will also be making similar changes to their patient leaflets for the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme at their next review. In addition, the PHA will continue to increase public awareness of the signs and symptoms of Ovarian Cancer, through regular social media channels to promote cancer awareness messaging. This includes information on signs and symptoms, and healthy changes people can make to help reduce the risk of developing cancer in the future.  In addition, the PHA website provides information about signs and symptoms of a range of cancers, including ovarian cancer, which explains what people can do if concerned, and signposts to recommended sources of support or further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will recognise the menopause as a public health issue and allocate specific resources to improve education, awareness and early intervention.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that menopause can have on a woman and, for some, it is more than just a biological milestone and can impact on every aspect of their lives.  Work is already ongoing to improve gynaecology services across the region, including menopause care. A Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and Public Health Agency representation, has been established to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level.  Although work is at an early stage of development and will require additional funding, this will include mapping clinical pathways from presentation at GP services into secondary and tertiary care, which will benefit both patients and clinicians.  This work includes a specific task and finish group to consider menopause care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the forthcoming Women’s Health Action Plan will address the development of regional menopause services.
answer_text: I recognise that menopause can be a time of profound transition for women and for some it is more than just a biological milestone and can impact on every aspect of a woman’s life, from physical health to emotional wellbeing and workplace performance. I welcome that as a society we are beginning to open up these important conversations. Through the Department's stakeholder engagement for the Woman's Health Action Plan, menopause has, as expected, emerged as a priority area for women. Whilst the Action Plan is still in development, work is ongoing to improve menopause services.  A Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and PHA representation, has been established to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level.  Although work is at an early stage of development and will require additional funding, this will include mapping clinical pathways from presentation at GP services into secondary and tertiary care, which will benefit both patients and clinicians.  This work includes a specific task and finish group to consider menopause care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the proposed Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: Work on a Women’s Health Action Plan is continuing with a current focus on engagement with harder-to-reach groups of women. I recognise that many women have felt overlooked within our health and social care system for a long time, and that some services have not met their needs. Work is ongoing to address these issues, and the Action Plan aims to further raise the profile of women’s health and accelerate positive change.It is important that women’s voices and experiences are reflected in the plan. To support its development, my Department has contributed £20k to the large-scale public listening exercise being undertaken by Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast.  Over 2,300 women responded to the Phase 1 health survey, and I am grateful to them. Phase 2 is currently underway in the form of focus groups with harder-to-reach women.This work is expected to conclude early next year and will form a key part of the evidence base for the Women’s Health Action Plan. Whilst development of the Action Plan continues, I want to assure Members that work on many of its key initiatives and actions are continuing – we are not waiting for the publication of the plan to begin to address important challenges in the here and now. This is, of course, very challenging in view of resource constraints across the Health and Social Care system, however I am pleased that GP elective gynaecology services have been re-established at 100% capacity from April this year, and  a further £3.5m of elective care funding has been earmarked for capacity building for red flag and time critical gynaecology. I aim to publish the Action Plan early next year after the findings from the listening exercise are known.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps he is taking to ensure GPs and primary care staff receive mandatory, up-to-date training in menopause diagnosis and treatment, including safe prescribing of HRT.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including menopause.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.My Department have endorsed the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines on the identification and management of menopause, updated in November 2024.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of PBL is to encourage dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GPs and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then Health & Social Care Board (HSCB) for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) commissions the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31823/22-27, to detail what measures are in place to ensure consistent and equitable access to BRCA1 and BRCA2 genetic testing.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department is committed to ensuring consistent and equitable access to BRCA1 and BRCA2 genetic testing across Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland Regional Genetics Service receives referrals from both primary and secondary care providers from across the region, operating outreach clinics and virtual clinics to provide equitable access across Northern Ireland. Patients and families affected by breast and ovarian cancer are assessed using UK-wide clinical guidelines. Eligibility for BRCA1 and BRCA2 testing is determined in accordance with the National Genomics Test Directory, which provides clear and consistent criteria for genomic testing. The National Genomics Test Directory, developed by the Test Evaluation Working Group, includes representation from all UK devolved nations, including Northern Ireland, to ensure that regional needs are considered in the development of national testing policies. In addition, the Regional Genetics Service has established Molecular Tumour Boards and has dedicated clinic capacity to assess genetic variants identified through tumour sequencing that may also be present in the germline. The service is planning a systematic rollout of this approach, starting with ovarian cancers and eventually breast cancers, with the aim of improving the detection of familial cancer cases.These measures collectively aim to ensure that individuals in Northern Ireland have equitable access to BRCA1 and BRCA2 genetic testing, particularly those with a relevant family history or clinical indication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether his Department has assessed regional variation in access to miscarriage and pregnancy loss services, and (ii) how it is working to ensure equitable provision across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department are aware that there is variation across Trusts in relation to  access to miscarriage and pregnancy loss services.The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families.  My Department has been overseeing a major programme of work on maternity and neonatal services, with an immediate focus  on improving governance and safety. Collectively, this work will result in a consolidated regional action plan to improve maternity care, including bereavement support. This work is informed by the recent independent review of midwifery services and reflects our commitment to ensuring that all women and families receive safe, respectful, and compassionate care.A range of compassionate and dedicated support options have been developed and are available in each Health and Social Care Trust, including, Bereavement support midwife posts, counselling services, early pregnancy units and importantly information and signposting to bereavement support groups, which offer specialised resources and peer support so that grieving families can receive one to one care they need during this difficult time.In addition, a regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis.  Where appropriate, care is coordinated with maternity units and health visitors to ensure continuity and holistic support.You may also be aware that the Department of Finance has introduced  primary legislation in the Assembly, which will enable the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for NI - The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill. ‘This initiative would enable a pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, to be recognised.  The Department of Health Northern Ireland and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss. SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently developing the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, opportunities for memory-making, and dignified support for grieving families. As part of the project healthcare professionals will be equipped with training, resources, and materials to deliver compassionate and informed care. Engagement with families who have a lived experience will be integral to the development of the pathways for NI, ensuring that bereavement support services are both clinically sound and emotionally responsive.My Department do however acknowledge the gaps that exist for Specialist Psychological Support for Bereaved Parents and remain committed to improving services.  As yo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail when she will approve the submitted PSNI business case to settle outstanding holiday pay claims for officers.
answer_text: While I cannot give a definitive timeframe at this stage, I am committed to progressing this matter as quickly as possible.  My officials are currently considering the PSNI Holiday Pay business case which must satisfy the requisite approvals process, including approval by the Department of Finance (DoF). Holiday Pay is an exceptional pressure that is wholly unaffordable within existing or future PSNI and DoJ baseline budget. Further discussions will be needed with the Executive and the Department of Finance (DoF) to consider options for funding this exceptional pressure.Furthermore, Holiday Pay is an issue that has an impact across all departments and, therefore, a solution at Block level will be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether the forthcoming Baby Loss Certificate Scheme will include provision for data collection, to support public health policy.
answer_text: As part of the development of the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme my officials in the General Register Office (GRO) are considering the possibility of other potential uses for the data collected as part of the application process. To better understand how those who have experienced baby loss would feel about the question of the retention and wider use of their data it was included in the recently completed public consultation exercise.My officials are currently analysing the responses to the consultation, the findings of which I will publish as soon as possible. Once this is complete, I will reconsider the question of other possible data uses in more detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department will use data from the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme to inform maternal health policy and service planning.
answer_text: The Baby Loss Certificate Scheme is being developed by the Department of Finance (DoF). It is not yet known whether the scheme will be suitable for gathering comprehensive data on pregnancy loss.As participation in the scheme is voluntary, it is unlikely to capture the full extent of pregnancy loss across Northern Ireland. However, my Department will continue to work closely with DoF to support the development of the scheme and to consider its potential role in improving data collection.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration been given to aligning miscarriage and pregnancy loss care pathways with the recommendations of the UK Pregnancy Loss Review.
answer_text: The UK Pregnancy Loss Review, published in July 2023, made several key recommendations aimed at improving care and recognition for individuals experiencing pregnancy loss before 24 weeks. These include:Introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate to formally acknowledge early pregnancy loss.Implementation of the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) to ensure consistent, compassionate bereavement care.Improved access to psychological support, including counselling and specialist services.In Northern Ireland, you will be aware that the Department of Finance has introduced  primary legislation in the Assembly, which will enable the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for NI - The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill. ‘This initiative would enable a pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, to be recognised.  The Department of Health Northern Ireland and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss. SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently developing the NBCP in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, opportunities for memory-making, and dignified support for grieving families. As part of the project healthcare professionals will be equipped with training, resources, and materials to deliver compassionate and informed care. Engagement with families who have a lived experience will be integral to the development of the pathways for NI, ensuring that bereavement support services are both clinically sound and emotionally responsive.A range of compassionate and dedicated support options have been developed and are available in each Health and Social Care Trust, including, bereavement support midwife posts, counselling services, early pregnancy units and importantly information and signposting to bereavement support groups, which offer specialised resources and peer support so that grieving families can receive one to one care they need during this difficult time.In addition, a regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental health illness, including postpartum psychosis.  Where appropriate, care is coordinated with maternity units and health visitors to ensure continuity and holistic support.The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families.  My Department has been overseeing a major programme of work on maternity and neonatal services, with an immediate focus  on improving governance and safety. Collectively, this work will result in a consolidated regional action plan to improve maternity care, including bereavement support. This wor

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken (i) to reduce reliance on locum staff within the Emergency Department at Causeway Hospital; and (i) to recruit permanent medical and nursing staff.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail what engagement his Department has had with the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and local representatives, including SOS Causeway, to provide clarity about the hospital’s long-term future.
answer_text: Departmental officials have regular and ongoing engagement with the Trust about a range of issues, including the Trust’s strategy for Causeway Hospital. The Trust restated its commitment to the long-term future of Causeway as an acute hospital site in its Strategic Vision for Causeway, and the Department’s Hospitals: Creating a Network for Better Outcomes indicates that consideration should be given to designating Causeway as an elective care centre. The Trust has engaged with SOS Causeway representatives regarding the future of the hospital on a number of occasions, with a meeting planned in the short term to specifically discuss mental health services. The Trust has established a Causeway Hospital Partnership Forum with internal and external stakeholders, and SOS Causeway have a standing invitation to this Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he expects to make a final decision on the Northern Health and Social Care Trust’s proposal to remove emergency general surgery from Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: On 18 December, Tracey McCaig, Chief Operating Officer of SPPG, chaired an Emergency General Surgery Causeway Summit. The meeting brought together senior leaders from the Northern Trust, Western Trust, and NIAS at Chief Executive and Director level to consider the potential impact on these organisations should emergency surgery at Causeway Hospital close.The purpose of the summit was to enable system-wide planning ahead of any Ministerial announcements. It was agreed that further work is required to review expected patient flows, which will inform the Minister’s decision.  An independent analysis has been commissioned from Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) to support this modelling exercise, test the underlying assumptions and assess the potential impact.A further update is expected by March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to invest in the Emergency Department at Causeway Hospital to support its long-term sustainability as a 24-hour service.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what diagnostic and elective services will be delivered as part of the development of Causeway Hospital as a diagnostic and elective hub; and (ii) how this model will improve access to care and reduce waiting times for patients in East Londonderry.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including menopause.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.My Department have endorsed the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines on the identification and management of menopause, updated in November 2024.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of PBL is to encourage dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GPs and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then Health & Social Care Board (HSCB) for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) commissions the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what plans his Department has to improve access to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services in East Londonderry, in light of the pressures on capacity identified in The Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy: A Review of the deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions 2026-2029.
answer_text: The WHSCT CAMHS continues to be challenged, like other CAMHS regionally, in meeting IEAP access targets with respect to 9-week routine Choice (new patient) appointments. The reasons for this are multi-faceted. It is no coincidence that the unique social economic, political landscape of NI coupled with high levels of social deprivation in the Western Trust area accounts for increasing trends in referral rates to the Service. CAMHS has witnessed increasing clinical acuity levels and risk laden co-morbid presentations following the Covid pandemic and to date referral rates have continued to increase both in numbers and complexity of presentations and co-morbid mental health difficulties.The Referral Co-ordinating team has established links with all areas in our community and voluntary sector.  The Co-ordinator’s role involves liaising within local community voluntary groups and signposting young people and their families to the most appropriate service that will meet their needs and offer support. Within the service the Referral Co-ordinators liaise with our Step 2 services in CAMHS.  The service also links with local Hubs in the community in continually providing up to date information in respect of other community and voluntary sector services that can provide psychological support to young people and their families.To improve access targets CAMHS continues to work in close partnership with Senior Trust Managers and Trust Quality Improvement and Performance colleagues with regular monitoring meetings taking place with SPPG/DOH regarding waiting times associated with access to service and ensuring positive outcomes for young people in receipt of the service.CAMHS received recurrent additional funding of £466,000 in 2020-21 and 2022-23. This additional funding supported recruitment to strengthen Step 2 and Step 3 core CAMHS. The provision at this level provides specialist assessment and targeted therapeutic intervention to children and young people experiencing moderate to severe mental health conditions. Recurrent funding of £169,000 through the Delivering Care Framework supporting the provision of high-quality care which is safe and effective for the Nursing and Midwifery workforce saw the appointment of a CAMHS Consultant Nurse and a CAMHS Lead Nurse in 2022-23.  These posts have been filled and this has enhanced nursing structures within the service. The CAMHS/Youth life Partnership has also been established, delivering therapeutic support at Step 2 level through counselling. This model has been successfully rolled out in the Omagh/Enniskillen sectors of the Trust for 5 – 18year olds.  The Partnership is currently ongoing with recurrent funding which includes monthly audit meetings with Youth life.A recurrent funding allocation in 2022/2023 of £285,000 also saw the establishment of the CAMHS Emotional Health & Wellbeing Team in School project, 9EWTS).  This investment has supported an innovative CAMHS-led Regional response at a universal Ste

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what specific measures are being put in place to ensure that rural constituencies, including East Londonderry, are not disproportionately affected by delays in implementing the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, as highlighted in The Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy: A Review of the deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions 2026-2029.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 is underpinned by a commitment to ensuring equitable access and consistent delivery across all areas in Northern Ireland.  While funding constraints have necessitated the completion of the Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy: Review of the deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions 2026-2029, the process has sharpened our focus and ambition. The review recommends a phased approach to delivery, ensuring that efforts remain targeted and achievable within available resources.The Review of Deliverability highlights the principle of Eradicating Inequity within Mental Health Services as a central focus. It reinforces that any actions prioritised for additional funding must be progressed in line with the Strategy’s original commitment to equality of access and consistent provision, ensuring that improvements benefit all communities fairly.The approach to implementation therefore remains centred on regional consistency and equitable access across Northern Ireland, with a clear commitment to ensuring that no area - including rural constituencies such as East Londonderry - is disproportionately impacted or disadvantaged.This is a central tenet of the work underpinning the ongoing development of a regionally consistent mental health service (Action 31), alongside the implementation of a Regional Mental Health Outcomes Framework (Action 34). Both were identified and progressed as priority enabling actions at the time of the Strategy’s publication. The former seeks to establish systems that ensure equitable access to high-quality, consistent mental health services across all areas of Northern Ireland, through collaborative efforts that shape services to meet local needs while maintaining regional standards. The latter supports evidence-based decision-making and enables monitoring of service effectiveness and consistency across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding allocated to the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, in each year since 2021.
answer_text: No additional funding has been provided to my Department to support implementation of the Strategy. As a result, existing Departmental resources have been used to support progress on the Strategy’s actions.The table below shows the funding allocated by my Department from 2021/22 to 2025/26 to support delivery of the Mental Health Strategy:YearFunding Allocation(£m)Requirement within Funding Plan (£m)2021/220.650.652022/234.19.592023/245.524.382024/255.9422025/265.961.72There has also been capital spend of £4.7m from 2020/21 to 2023/24 for the Northern Trust Mental Health Inpatient Unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how the findings in The Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy: A Review of the deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions 2026-2029 on disparities across Health and Social Care Trusts will inform his Department’s future decisions about inpatient mental health provision at the Ross Thompson Unit in Coleraine.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline how his Department is addressing the higher prevalence of mental health conditions in Northern Ireland compared to the rest of the UK, particularly among older people and those affected by the legacy of conflict.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 identifies the region’s higher prevalence of mental health conditions as being driven by a combination of factors including the legacy of the Troubles, social inequality, and adverse childhood experiences. The Strategy was developed to address these challenges through a whole-system, whole-life approach.However, in light of the funding constraints faced, it has been necessary for my Department to undertake a review of the deliverability of the Mental Health Strategy’s actions from 2026 - 2029 to focus and prioritise our ambition, whilst recognising that the Strategy in its entirety is still needed.  The Review report, published on 9 October 2025, recognises that the mental health system must focus on fewer priorities and proposes a phased approach for the Strategy's deliverability dependant on the availability of additional resources. While the immediate focus for 2026/27 will be on workforce development and crisis services, the review acknowledges the importance of addressing older people's mental health and proposes it for action in 2027/28. This reflects a recognition of the growing need in this demographic. Any additional resources we secure for mental health will be directed into priority areas identified through the review.  In the absence of additional funding, we will focus on making the most of the resources we already have.Mental health outcomes are shaped by broader social factors like poverty, housing, education, and employment. My Department is committed to enhancing cross-Government collaboration, working closely with the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience, and Suicide Prevention to strengthen partnerships and deepen cooperation.The Regional Trauma Network, overseen jointly by my Department and The Executive Office, was established to support the mental health and wellbeing of individuals affected by the Troubles. It adopts an innovative approach working collaboratively with statutory, community and voluntary sector organisations to provide specialist trauma care to individuals. Phase 1, focused on victims and survivors of the Troubles, formally began on 1 April 2023 and will run for at least three years. It will be evaluated from April 2026 to identify any unmet or ongoing needs, with lessons learned informing the design and rollout of Phase 2.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the consultation her Department has had with organisations, such as Women’s Aid, regarding the commencement and operation of (i) Domestic Abuse Protection Orders; and (ii) Domestic Abuse Protection Notices.
answer_text: I fully recognise the vital role that voluntary and community sector partners, including Women’s Aid, play in supporting victims and survivors of domestic abuse. Their knowledge, experience, and the work they do on the frontline are invaluable in helping shape effective policy and practice.Departmental officials have been engaging with these partners as part of the development of the model for the introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders in Northern Ireland. A roundtable meeting was held in June this year with representatives from the PSNI and a number of voluntary and community organisations, at which officials provided an update on learning from the pilot area in Croydon. Colleagues from Scotland also attended to share insights from their own experience of implementing Domestic Abuse Protection Orders and Notices, including the learning and challenges being encountered within that jurisdiction. A visit to a second pilot area has been arranged and the delegation includes a representative from the voluntary and community sector.As work to develop and implement the Northern Ireland model continues, officials will maintain regular engagement with voluntary and community sector partners to ensure their views and expertise continue to inform the approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact on victims of domestic abuse as a result of the delay in commencing (i) Domestic Abuse Protection Orders; and (ii) Domestic Abuse Protection Notices.
answer_text: I am deeply aware of the serious impact that domestic abuse has on victims, their families and communities. Protecting victims and preventing further harm is, and will remain, a key priority for me as Minister of Justice. In bringing forward regulations to give effect to Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders (DAPNs / DAPOs) my focus has been on ensuring that there are effective and sustainable protections in place for those experiencing abuse, supported by a justice system that responds swiftly and safely to their needs.My Department has previously undertaken significant preparatory work to bring forward DAPNs and DAPOs. However, in progressing this work with operational partners, a number of significant operational challenges were identified. It has therefore been important that we take the necessary time to design and deliver a model that is workable and sustainable within a Northern Ireland context and that will provide real and effective protection for victims. It would not be in anyone’s interest to introduce a system that is unworkable or ineffective in practice.In the meantime, there are already a range of protections available to victims of domestic abuse, including non-molestation orders, restraining orders and bail conditions. Anyone who is a victim of domestic abuse, or who has concerns about someone else, should report this to the PSNI, who can take immediate action to help keep victims safe.It is intended that, once introduced, DAPNs and DAPOs will add an additional layer of protection for victims and to help break the cycle of abuse. Work continues to develop a model that meets the needs of victims, is operationally deliverable, and commands the confidence of both victims and justice partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the timeline for the implementation of the provisions for (i) Domestic Abuse Protection Orders; and (ii) Domestic Abuse Protection Notices, legislated for under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: Protecting victims of domestic abuse and holding perpetrators to account remain key priorities for me as Minister of Justice.  The introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders (DAPNs and DAPOs) will represent a new safeguard for victims, allowing for immediate protection in cases where there is a risk of abusive behaviour.Department of Justice officials, in partnership with the Police Service of Northern Ireland, continue to progress detailed work to develop a DAPN/O model that is both effective and sustainable within the Northern Ireland context.  This work is taking account of operational capacity across the justice system, estimated costs, and, crucially, the needs and safety of victims.To inform the design of the Northern Ireland model, officials have undertaken fact-finding visits to pilot areas in England, including Croydon and shortly, Greater Manchester, to gather learning from their experiences.  Roundtable discussions have also been held with voluntary and community sector partners, whose expertise and on-the-ground insight have helped to inform the development of a model that will work in practice and deliver meaningful improved protection for victims of domestic abuse.  Continued engagement is also taking place with officials in Scotland, who are facing similar challenges in the development and implementation of the approach in that jurisdiction.Given the issues that have been encountered in developing the DAPN / DAPO model, including uncertainties around the scale of qualifying cases, I have concluded that it is essential to undertake a Northern Ireland pilot before wider roll-out.  This will allow for a robust assessment of operational processes, the scale of demand, and the associated cost and resource implications across agencies.The aim is to agree a preferred pilot model with delivery partners by the end of this financial year.  Subject to that agreement, it is anticipated that a pilot could commence during the second half of 2026/27.  The learning from that pilot will inform decisions on future roll-out and ensure that when DAPN/Os are introduced, they will deliver real, practical and sustainable protection for victims of domestic abuse across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any consideration her Department has given to the interaction between (i) Domestic Abuse Protection Orders and Domestic Abuse Protection Notices; and (ii) existing protective measures, such as Non-Molestation Orders.
answer_text: Ensuring that there are effective and proportionate protections in place for victims of domestic abuse remains a key priority for me and my Department. We recognise that victims may require different forms of protection depending on their individual circumstances, and it is therefore important that a range of protective measures are available.In developing proposals for the introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders (DAPN/Os), my officials have given careful consideration to how these new measures will interact with existing protective mechanisms such as Non-Molestation Orders (NMOs), Occupation Orders and Restraining Orders.The DAPN/Os are being designed to provide immediate protection to victims of domestic abuse. The intention is that they can be applied for, not only by the victim themselves, but also by police or by another relevant third party, thereby helping to remove the onus from victims to initiate proceedings. I believe this is an important distinction that will help to enhance the available protections for victims of domestic abuse. It is intended that the regulations giving effect to DAPNs and DAPOs will empower PSNI and the courts, respectively, to place certain restrictions and / or positive requirements on the individual subject to the DAPN or the DAPO, where these are considered necessary to prevent further abuse. For example, in the case of a DAPN, this might include an immediate requirement for them to leave the family home, or, in the case of a DAPO, a requirement for them to complete a specific programme designed to address the underlying drivers for abuse, such as a substance misuse programme.  They are intended to offer protection to victims aged 16 and over.By contrast, NMOs can provide protection to both the victim and to any relevant child(ren), preventing the perpetrator from harassing, intimidating, or threatening them. Victims may also qualify for an Occupation Order, which sets out terms restricting the offender from entering or residing in the home. NMOs and Occupation Orders are civil orders, however a breach of either constitutes a criminal offence;  the intention is that that DAPOs will also be a civil protection order, however breach of a DAPO would be a criminal offence.In addition, other protective measures such as Restraining Orders are also available within the existing framework, which provide further avenues of support and safety for victims of domestic abuse.The development of the DAPN/O model is being carefully designed to complement, rather than duplicate, these existing provisions, ensuring a coherent and victim-centred system of protection in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how the current transport policy is addressing public transport provision in East Londonderry, particularly in rural areas where services remain limited.
answer_text: The draft Transport Strategy sets out a new vision and strategic priorities for our transport system through to 2035 aiming to support economic growth, regional balance, and improved connectivity across both urban and rural areas. The public consultation closed at the end of September 2025, and officials are now analysing responses to help shape a system that meets the needs of all communities.In addition, East Derry is covered by two Local Transport Plans: The Northwest Transport Plan and the Causeway Coast and Glens Transport Plan. These transport plans are being developed in an integrated manner with each of the council’s Local Development Plans. For this reason, these plans are at very different stages in their development. My department is currently progressing Stage 1 of the Northwest Transport Plan (NWTP), which will guide future transport investment across the Northwest region. The Plan will identify proposals to address local transport issues, including local bus services, active travel, local road network and parking. It will provide most local detail in Derry and Strabane and this will be for the plan period up to 2035. A Baseline Report has been initially prepared to support the development of the NWTP.  However, the Plans work is not sufficiently advanced at this stage to provide commentary on proposed transport measures.  The Causeway Coast and Glens Transport Plan is currently at the earlier mobilisation phase and is not yet sufficiently advanced at this time.  My Department provides funding to Translink to provide public transport services and facilities. This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people of all ages and abilities. Both the Translink website and App can provide transport options from any location, address or stop, urban and rural, where transport links exist.  The website and app allow for address-to-address planning, park & ride planner and a bike it option.  All major hospitals have public transport stop listings.My department also provides grant support for Dial-a-Lift, a member only rural door to destination transport service which improves access to local goods and services, helping people travel independently and connected to their local communities.  Grant support is also provided to support the Disability Action Transport Scheme which is a member only urban door to destination transport service operating in Limavady and Coleraine.I have allocated an additional 26% in 2025/26 to support the rural Dial-a-Lift and urban Disability Action Transport schemes across all areas in the north.  The level of funding was commensurate with the level of funding requested by those providing these services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail her Department's assessment of Invest NI’s support in Causeway Coast and Glens over the last five years; and (ii) whether this reflects her Department’s commitment to balanced regional economic growth.
answer_text: There are currently 6,340 registered businesses in the Causeway Coast and Glens Council area, accounting for 8% of all businesses in the North. The area accounts for 3% of the region’s exports and is home to 6% of the region’s exporters. Over the last five-year period Invest NI has assisted 619 investments, including job creation projects, providing £14m of financial assistance to businesses in the council area. The assistance provide by Invest NI is expected to unlock £95m of investment in the council area and lead to the creation of 550 new jobs. Invest NI published its new strategy last year entitled ‘Our Future in Focus’ (Invest NI Business Strategy 2024-2027 (PDF)). This strategy is directly aligned with my department’s commitment to balanced regional growth. To deliver against this strategy, Invest NI is implementing a new regional approach that is seeing it increasing the capacity and capability across its regional office network, assisting it to seek out, identify and engage with more companies than ever before. This involves attracting businesses that are new to Invest NI, enhancing the support, advice and guidance it provides to companies, as well as implementing a new purposeful partnership approach with its stakeholders and partners to align and maximise the impact of all of our work. To measure its success, Invest NI have set ambitious targets, including a new target to increase the number of investments outside the Belfast Metropolitan Area to 65% by the final year of their Business Strategy, 2026/27. Over the previous 5 years the average was c.56%. This is one of the first steps in a long-term commitment to better shape economic performance right across the North to provide better outcomes for all our people. Many of our regional challenges are deep seated, requiring multi-agency responses and investments, and will therefore not be addressed within Invest NI’s current business strategy period.  Nevertheless, Invest NI and my department are fully committed to working with all partners to play our part in effecting real, meaningful change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the delivery of Causeway Coast and Glens Growth Deal projects; and (ii) how her Department is ensuring these commitments deliver tangible benefits for local businesses.
answer_text: My Department is currently supporting the Causeway Coast & Glens Growth Deal with four projects. These are The Centre for Food & Drug Discovery (CfFDD), Business Innovation and Incubation Hub (BIIH), Foodovation & Skills Centre and Connected Causeway.  The Centre for Food & Drug Discovery is at the final stages of approval while the other projects are progressing in business case development. 		This collaborative approach between the Department, the Councils and its partners is central to ensuring the Growth Deal delivers maximum impact for the region, including supporting job creation, fostering innovation, and enhancing regional competitiveness. Through Invest NI and partners we will focus on creating new opportunities for local firms to engage in supply chains, supporting small and medium-sized enterprises to access innovation facilities, and helping to develop the skills needed by local employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the current higher education policy is ensuring the long-term viability of Ulster University’s Coleraine campus, given recent investment at other university sites.
answer_text: My Department works in partnership with Ulster University to deliver investment across all of its campuses, including Coleraine, ensuring the appropriate skills needs for the region continue to be met. A procurement exercise is currently underway to appoint external consultants to carry out an independent assessment of the costs associated with the university’s multi-campus model. My Department is also working with Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council and Ulster University to develop proposals for a Centre for Food and Drug Discovery at Coleraine, as part of the Causeway Coast and Glens Growth Deal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how current regeneration policy is supporting small businesses in East Londonderry; and (ii) whether recent programmes have addressed the needs of local town centres.
answer_text: My Department continues to develop a range of regeneration schemes with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council throughout East Londonderry such as the current Revitalisation Scheme providing £150,000 to small businesses in Limavady.  Furthermore, small businesses in rural areas such as: Garvagh, Greysteel, Kilrea, Magilligan and Drumsurn are benefitting from approximately £300,000 part-funding from my Department under the COVID Recovery Small Settlements Programme.  My Department has also funded approximately £2,800,000 on Public Realm Schemes in Portrush since 2020 and sixty small businesses have benefited from a £300,000 Revitalisation Scheme in Coleraine Town Centre in 2023. My Department is also leading on the Shaping Sustainable Places Programme, in collaboration with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department for Infrastructure.  This programme will require NI Executive approval, and if approved, will focus on regeneration interventions prioritised by Local Councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has given consideration to extending water quality monitoring at bathing sites on the North Coast beyond the summer season, in response to changing weather conditions and bathing patterns.
answer_text: Currently the bathing season runs from the 1 June to the 15 September each year. Responses to the last Bathing Water Review in 2022/23 indicated public support for an extension to the season. Given the potential for significant change to the policy, my department has been sampling outside of the bathing season to inform future policy direction. This data is published on the department’s website and is available via the NI Bathing Water Dashboard.  I have also recently committed to a new Bathing Waters Policy Review for Northern Ireland which will commence shortly. The length to the bathing season will be considered as part of this review.In addition, due to the public health implication of blue green algae, monitoring continued this year outside of the bathing season at sites likely to be at risk on the North Coast.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of Pollution Risk Forecasts issued for designated bathing waters, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The bathing water quality prediction app, ‘Swim NI’, provides daily pollution forecasts of bathing water quality at six beaches, enabling bathers to make an informed choice about potential exposure to bacteria that can cause illness.  This project has provided predictions for four bathing seasons (2022-2025) and is a collaboration between my Department, Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute and Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful. The number of predictions over the last 4 bathing seasons per constituency are as follows:Constituency (Bathing water(s) in italics)Year2022202320242025East LondonderryCastlerock, Portrush Curran214214214214East AntrimWaterfoot107107107107North DownBallyholme107107107107StrangfordBallywalter107107107107South DownNewcastle107107107107
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department intends to review the level of fines and sanctions available for illegal discharges into waterways.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a public consultation on policy proposals aimed at strengthening the protection of the water environment. This consultation, which closes on 6 November 2025, will inform the development of the forthcoming Fisheries and Water Environment Bill.The proposed policies seek to enhance the enforcement regime by introducing more robust measures to deter non-compliance, improve regulatory control, and strengthen enforcement capabilities. Through the Bill, I intend to increase existing fines and penalties to bring them into alignment with those in other jurisdictions for a range of offences, such as illegal discharges into waterways. Additionally, the Bill will provide the power to introduce Fixed Penalty Notices and both Fixed and Variable Monetary Penalties for a spectrum of offences impacting the water environment. These measures are designed to provide a more immediate and proportionate deterrent for minor and moderate breaches of environmental legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how improvements from wastewater infrastructure upgrades in East Londonderry are measured, in terms of reduced sewage discharges and improved bathing water quality.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The Northern Ireland Environment Agency’s Summary Guidance Document in relation to intermittent discharges (storm overflows), identifies that assets within 2km of identified bathing waters have the potential to impact bathing water quality and sets standards for assets accordingly.In the North, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is responsible for monitoring bathing water quality, to ensure that coastal waters meet safety standards for public bathing. DAERA’s efforts are in line with the Quality of Bathing Water Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2008, which require regular testing and reporting of water quality results to the public. The NI Direct website lists the results for the 2024 bathing season  (bathing water quality) and all bathing waters within East Derry are classified as having excellent status. The results for the 2025 bathing season are due to be published in the near future.In addition, to date, NI Water has not upgraded any assets within 2km of a designated bathing water in East Derry in the current Price Control 21 period.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider introducing legislation to require NI Water to provide continuous or real-time monitoring of sewage discharges, in line with the approach taken in Great Britain under the Environment Act 2021.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is responsible for monitoring water quality, including carrying out sampling, monitoring and investigations, and would therefore be best placed to consider if new legislation is required.However, NI Water has advised that it is currently working with DAERA to develop a pilot project for near real time Event Duration Monitoring (EDM) reporting to support DAERA in making assessments of public health risks for water users, particularly bathers. In addition, as of September 2025, NI Water has approximately 655 EDMs installed and sufficiently verified to support effective use for operational maintenance and monitoring. Of these, 222 EDMs have been fully quality assured for both operational maintenance and release of spill count data to the public, via NI Water’s storm overflow webpage (NI Water's Storm Overflows).By the end of Price Control 21 (31 March 2028), NI Water aims to have approximately 40% of its 2,430 storm overflows fitted with EDMs. This ongoing programme of installation of EDMs will continue into Price Control 28 (2028-2034), subject to funding. 	For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what dedicated services are available for adults with ADHD.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting lists across Trusts.Although there is no commissioned Adult ADHD service, Belfast; Northern and Southern Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts provide a level of provision. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust reconfigured a small resource (0.5 Consultant Psychiatry) from within adult teams to process these referrals. Current waiting is in excess of 3500, waiting times for an ADHD assessment based on current provision is 8 years from the point of referral.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) in response to demand for Adult ADHD services; decided to progress with a small, unfunded pilot for service users who had been referred to the Trust. This pilot captured need by placing people on a waiting list and then offering an appointment with the Trust. Part of the process detailed pre-screening to ensure assessments are offered; where there is a high likelihood of Adult ADHD. All patients on any waiting lists remain under the care of their GP. This pilot approach has taken time to establish given the preparatory work required. Communication with individuals on the register commenced in December 2023, in chronological order of date of original referral.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) in response to demand, has progressed a small unfunded service for those patients who have transferred with an existing diagnosis and who are prescribed medication and require monitoring as per NICE Guidelines.  For patients awaiting a diagnostic assessment, the demand for ADHD assessment far outweighs capacity, which is resulting in long waiting times of several years.SEHSCT receives referrals for Adult Mental Health Services via Mental Health Assessment Centres located in Newtownards, Lisburn and Downpatrick.  Individuals, including those with a potential/confirmed co-morbid ADHD diagnosis are offered a mental health assessment and are managed via the adult mental health core care pathway, where appropriate.There is no adult ADHD diagnostic service in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, therefore there is no waiting list for ADHD diagnostic assessment.  If a person is diagnosed with ADHD in children’s services and required ongoing medication management by a Consultant Psychiatrist, they will continue to receive regular reviews by a Consultant Psychiatrist in adult mental health services.The Western Health and Social Care Trust is not commissioned to deliver ADHD assessment. The pathway for ADHD into WHSCT adult services is through transition from CAMHS to Adult Recovery Services where Trust diagnosis has been delivered within CAMHS and concordance with medication is evident.The National Institut

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has engaged with (i) service users; (ii) clinicians; and (iii) charities, to develop proposals for adult ADHD services.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting lists across Trusts.The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including proposed referral pathways and potential implementation and staffing costs. The needs assessment includes consideration of the impact of untreated ADHD on wider services.A wide selection of stakeholders have contributed to the development of the needs assessment, including representatives from the Department of Health, HSC Trusts, clinicians, the Community and Voluntary sector and people with lived experience, including service users and carers, amongst others.  While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis required, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment his Department has made of the impact of untreated ADHD in adults on wider services, including employment, education, mental health, and criminal justice.
