question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider incorporating measures such as (i) a carer’s essential payment; (ii) reforms to Carer’s Allowance; and (iii) improved workplace rights, when finalising the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: In June this year, the Executive agreed to consult on a draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which is both sustainable and deliverable and which prioritises the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives throughout NI, including the needs of unpaid carers. 		Following the close of the Public Consultation on the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy on 19 September, my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received. I will report the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues, and we will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy.	At this time there are no plans for additional payments for unpaid carers. Decisions around the provision of any additional payments, including the funding arrangements, is a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Anti-Poverty Strategy will respond to the poverty experienced by unpaid carers, many of whom are unable to remain in paid work due to their caring responsibilities.
answer_text: I agree that unpaid carers fulfil an essential role. Where possible, I want to ensure that my Department and the cross-Executive work that my Department leads reflects and supports this role. Carer’s Allowance, for example, is the main benefit support targeted towards certain unpaid carers, aiming to provide a measure of financial support for people who give up the opportunity of full-time employment in order to provide regular and substantial care.In June this year, the Executive agreed to consult on a draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which is both sustainable and deliverable and which prioritises the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives throughout NI, including the needs of unpaid carers. 		The Anti-Poverty Strategy Public Consultation closed on 19 September and my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received throughout the public consultation process. I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues, and we will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he will take to ensure that the lived experiences of unpaid carers, including financial hardship, stress and isolation, are reflected in the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: In June this year, the Executive agreed to consult on a draft Anti-Poverty Strategy. This consultation involved input from, a wide range of stakeholders from across the community and voluntary sector including organisations which have members who are carers and those who represent carers directly. 	The Anti-Poverty Strategy Public Consultation closed on 19 September and my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received throughout the public consultation process. I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues, and we will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy for Executive consideration and approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with unpaid carers, and their representative organisations, in developing the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: I am aware of the challenges faced by carers having met with representatives of Carers NI earlier this year. In addition to this, the public consultation on the draft Anti-poverty Strategy which ran from June to September involved input from a wide range of stakeholders from across the community and voluntary sector. This included organisations which have members who are carers and those who represent carers directly.	The Anti-Poverty Strategy Public Consultation closed on 19 September, and my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received throughout the public consultation process. I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues, and we will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy for Executive consideration and approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether unpaid carers will be explicitly recognised in the final Anti-Poverty Strategy, given their absence from the current draft.
answer_text: In June this year, the Executive agreed to consult on a draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which is both sustainable and deliverable and which prioritises the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives throughout NI, including the needs of unpaid carers. Following the close of the Public Consultation on the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy on 19 September, my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received and I will report the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues.Decisions on the final content of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to agreement by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the role her Department will have in the (i) registration; and (ii) oversight of Lasting Powers of Attorney, when implemented under the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The 2016 Act provides for a Public Guardian, to be appointed and resourced by my Department. The Public Guardian’s functions will include establishing and maintaining a register of lasting powers of attorney, receiving reports from attorneys and dealing with complaints about the way in which powers are being exercised by attorneys. Under the legislation, oversight of lasting powers of attorney is ultimately a matter for the High Court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement his Department has had with people living with dementia and their representative organisations regarding the implementation of lasting powers of attorney.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage on a regular basis with stakeholders to understand the impact that phased implementation of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 has. The MCA contains provisions which fall under the remit of both my Department and the Department of Justice and as a result, officials have been working collaboratively to scope the implications of full implementation. This is with a view of developing a full implementation plan, to guide future decision making and inform me on the impact, implications and likely costings of commencing these provisions. Although work on this area had slowed due to significant resourcing challenges, the work will recommence early next year.Whilst no specific assessment has taken place by my Department, as part of this work my officials, alongside DoJ officials, met with Dementia NI colleagues in June 2025, where the discussion focused on provisions related to lasting powers of attorney.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of lasting powers of attorney under the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, including the expected timetable for introducing health and welfare provisions.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016 (MCA), a piece of legislation brought forward by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice (DoJ), provides a framework for people who lack capacity to make a decision for themselves and for those who now have capacity but wish to make preparations for a time in the future when they may lack capacity. The MCA has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to deprivation of liberty, research and money and valuables commenced to date. Further work is needed to understand the resourcing implications for full commencement and so there are currently no indicative timings for full commencement of the MCA. Any decision regarding provisions related to lasting powers of attorney would need to be considered in collaboration with DoJ.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has assessed the impact on patients’ rights of the continued absence of health and welfare lasting powers of attorney, particularly for people living with dementia.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage on a regular basis with stakeholders to understand the impact that phased implementation of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 has. The MCA contains provisions which fall under the remit of both my Department and the Department of Justice and as a result, officials have been working collaboratively to scope the implications of full implementation. This is with a view of developing a full implementation plan, to guide future decision making and inform me on the impact, implications and likely costings of commencing these provisions. Although work on this area had slowed due to significant resourcing challenges, the work will recommence early next year.Whilst no specific assessment has taken place by my Department, as part of this work my officials, alongside DoJ officials, met with Dementia NI colleagues in June 2025, where the discussion focused on provisions related to lasting powers of attorney.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the internal policy and legislation that facilitates emotional support animals in Northern Ireland prisons.

To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how she is ensuring that the legal framework for Lasting Powers of Attorney in Northern Ireland, will provide the same protections and safeguards as in other UK jurisdictions.
answer_text: The legal framework for lasting powers of attorney in Northern Ireland is provided for in the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, although the relevant provisions have not yet been commenced. The protections and safeguards provided for in the 2016 Act are the same as those in the Mental Capacity Act 2005 for lasting powers of attorney in England and Wales, and are broadly similar to those under the corresponding legislation in Scotland. The commencement powers for the Act rest with the Department of Health and my officials will be working with colleagues in that Department on an implementation plan for the 2016 Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has engaged with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on how the treatment of animals in domestic abuse cases could be better reflected in family court proceedings.
answer_text: While I recognise that domestic abuse can involve mistreatment of animals, policy responsibility for the substantive law applied by courts in private family law cases sits with the Department of Finance. This includes the legislation (the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (Northern Ireland) Order 1998) under which courts can make non-molestation and occupation orders. It would, therefore, be for that Department to engage with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs regarding how the legislative framework could be enhanced in relation to family cases which raise concerns about animal welfare.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has considered enabling family courts to include pets in non-molestation and occupation orders, in cases involving domestic abuse.
answer_text: Under the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, a court must have regard to all the circumstances when deciding whether to exercise its discretionary power and make a Non-Molestation Order or Occupation Order. In reaching that decision, the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 requires a court to take into account the impact of its decision on the health, safety and well-being of the parties and any children involved. That consideration can include issues raised by the applicant regarding their companion animals. Courts can therefore presently consider circumstances regarding pets in situations of domestic abuse and coercive control.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any guidance issued to family courts in Northern Ireland, recognises the use of harm or threats to pets as a form of coercive control.
answer_text: The principle of judicial independence means that the issue of guidance to family courts is a matter for the Lady Chief Justice.I am not aware of any judicial guidance that expressly references the use of harm or threats to pets as a form of coercive control; however, the Lady Chief Justice issued guidance in June 2025 to set out the approach of the court in an application for a residence or contact order (under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995) where it is alleged that a party, or the child, has experienced domestic abuse perpetrated by another party. The definition of “domestic abuse” for the purpose of the guidance includes behaviour falling within the meaning of section 2 of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 when read with section 4 of that Act, and section 4(3) of the 2021 Act refers to behaviour  that is directed at a “person” through “conduct concerning or towards property” which includes pets or other animals, whether belonging to the victim or others.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will consider consulting on the role of pets in domestic abuse and the barriers this presents for victims accessing family court protections.
answer_text: While I recognise that pets can be used to perpetrate domestic abuse, policy responsibility for the substantive law applied by courts in private family law cases sits with the Department of Finance. This includes the legislation (the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (Northern Ireland) Order 1998) under which courts can make non-molestation orders and occupation orders. It would, therefore, be for the Department of Finance to consult on whether the legislative framework could be enhanced for victims where the behaviour in respect of which protection is needed involves pets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any consideration her Department has given to recognising harm or threats to pets as a form of coercive control in domestic abuse cases.
answer_text: I acknowledge the very real and distressing impact that harm, or the threat of harm, to pets can have on victims of domestic abuse. For many people, pets or other animals, are a deeply loved part of the family, and perpetrators who use them as a means of intimidation, coercion, or control can cause significant emotional trauma. Section 1 the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 provides for the offence of domestic abuse against an individual, their child or any other person which is intended to cause a person “to suffer physical or psychological harm,” with “psychological harm” including fear, alarm and distress”.  Section 4(3) expressly refers to behaviour that is directed at a “person” through “conduct concerning or towards property” which includes pets or other animals (for example agricultural livestock) whether belonging to the victim or others.  My Department continues to engage with stakeholders such as animal welfare charities, and victim support services to strengthen recognition of this issue and to ensure victims know that such abuse is taken seriously. I recently met with the Links Group and remain in contact with them to explore options for further collaborative working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any long-term strategy he is developing to ensure sustainable special educational needs transport provision.
answer_text: I recognise that there is a need for improvement in transport provision for pupils with Special Educational Needs (SEN). In recent years, there has been a significant rise in the number of children with statements of SEN who need specialist school placements. Consequently, the number of pupils requiring bespoke transport arrangements has increased which has placed additional pressure on the school transport network and led to higher associated costs. Currently, the EA provides transport assistance to over 13,000 pupils with additional SEN transport requirements. Home to school transport can only be arranged once a school placement has been confirmed. Ongoing work within my Department to improve the statementing and annual review processes is anticipated to support earlier confirmation of school placements. This will enable the EA to plan and coordinate its SEN transport services with greater efficiency, contributing significantly to overall sustainability.    The distance that children travel to school is also a key factor in the sustainability of school transport provision. I am committed to enabling children with SEN to attend school as close to home as possible, however, this is dependent on placements being available locally. My Department and the EA have a shared vision that, in time, every school in Northern Ireland will be able to offer specialist provisions, allowing all children to attend school close to home.In addition, I have allocated funding to the EA to support the expansion of a pilot project introducing smaller, 7-seater vehicles to its fleet to enhance flexibility in the provision of SEN transport. The findings from this pilot will inform future considerations on the delivery of SEN transport services. Furthermore, I have instructed my officials to collaborate closely with the EA in developing proposals aimed at ensuring a sustainable approach to SEN transport delivery, thereby ensuring that home to school transport can continue to be provided for pupils who need it to enable them to attend school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting times for red flag breast referrals; and (ii) what action he is taking to increase capacity within breast clinics to reduce waiting lists.
answer_text: (i) The most recent official statistics for red flag breast cancer referrals were published on 2 October 2025 referring to the quarter end of June 2025. Figures for median waiting times for red flag breast cancer referrals are given in Table 1 which follows.Table 1. Median waiting times for red flag breast cancer referralsat quarter end June 2025.Regional breast cancer service 1, 2Number of patients 3Median wait time (days) 3, 4, 5Median wait time (weeks) 3, 4, 5Belfast, Northern and South Eastern Trusts1,947385.4Source: Department of Health Hospital Waits Information Branch, Cancer Waiting Times Dataset.Notes:1 A new regional breast cancer service was launched on 8 May 2025 and breakdowns by Trust are no longer applicable. Data for quarter ending June 2025 include the transition period from a Trust to a regional service.2 Given the relatively recent transition of Southern and Western Trusts to encompass on 8 May 2025, validated breast cancer referrals data for these Trusts were not available for inclusion at the time of publication.3 Data sourced from the encompass system.4 The figures above refer to waiting times for red flag breast cancer referrals during the reporting period. They do not indicate expected future waiting times.5 To avoid distortion due to long waits, the Department reports on the median (the ‘middle’ waiting time) instead of mean waiting time.Due to essential data validation processes, there is a reporting delay.  Therefore, data beyond June 2025 is unavailable.  The next Cancer Waiting Time Statistics bulletin, covering the period from 1 July to 30 September 2025, is scheduled for publication on 15 January 2026.(ii) My Department recognises that the current regional waiting time for red flag breast assessment across the region is unacceptable. My Department is working closely with Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts to increase capacity through the delivery of additional clinics.  In response to the immediate pressures, my Department is working closely with Trusts to implement regional waiting list clinics over the next few months; evenings and Saturday sessions have been confirmed for all Trusts for October. Trusts have also been instructed to prioritise red flag referrals within existing core capacity to ensure timely access for the most clinically urgent cases. The Independent Sector will also be used to supplement short-term capacity.As part of the Elective Care Framework (ECF), targeted investment has been identified to enhance breast service provision across the region. In collaboration with HSC Trusts, we are actively progressing a range of solutions including drawing on best practice from other jurisdictions, exploring innovative workforce skill mix models, and redesigning care pathways, all aimed at improving service efficiency and delivering better outcomes for patients.My Department continues to monitor the situation closely and remains committed to improving access to timely and appropriate care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of applications for special educational needs transport drivers and escorts processed, in each of the past three years; and (ii) the average time taken to complete compliance and Access NI checks for these positions.
answer_text: (i) The Education Authority (EA) has advised that all drivers and escorts providing home to school transport services for the EA are required to undertake an enhanced Access NI check. The EA is unable to differentiate those working solely with children who have special educational needs; however, it has provided information below on the total number of Access NI applications processed:   Number of Access NI applications processed in respect of drivers and escorts providing home to school transport on behalf of the EA 2022202320242025 (Jan to Sept)EmployerDriverEscortDriverEscortDriverEscortDriverEscortEA693679411005411168Private Operators1,6124871,7335712,0648561,695743Total1,6815231,8126122,1649101,8068112,2042,4243,0742,617(ii) The EA has provided information below on the average time taken to process Access NI Enhanced Disclosure Checks: Average time taken to process Access NI Enhanced Disclosure Checks   EA  Private Operators Drivers6.5 days4.5 daysEscorts6 days5.5 days

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any information the Education Authority holds on the number of school days missed by children with special educational needs, in the current academic year, due to lack of transport provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority does not hold information detailing the number of school days missed by children with Special Educational Needs due to lack of transport provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of contracts for special educational needs transport not secured, in each of the past three years; and (ii) the reasons identified for this.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport and it has advised me that it does not hold the requested information for the 2023/24 academic year.  In the 2024/25 academic year, the EA was unable to secure contracts for 21 routes to provide private hire transport for children with Special Educational Needs. These routes were tendered on two occasions, but the EA received no bids and alternative transport assistance was put in place. To date in the 2025/26 academic year, the EA has successfully secured contracts for all routes and transport arrangements have been put in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the engagement her Department has had with the (i) Department for the Economy; and (ii) Department for Communities, in the last three years, regarding how the unreliability of rural transport affects disabled people in accessing and sustaining employment.
answer_text: My Department supports the provision of transport services across the region via public transport services provided by Translink as well as through the Dial-a-Lift and the Disability Action Transport schemes. The grant funded Dial-a-Lift scheme can assist people, including those with a disability access employment, but it is a member only service which operates in defined geographical areas and due to the nature of the service (demand led with limited resources available) it may not always be possible to meet everyone's needs.There has been no specific engagement with either the Department for the Economy or the Department for Communities, regarding how the unreliability of rural transport affects disabled people in accessing and sustaining employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will review funding arrangements for community transport to ensure providers can meet growing demand in rural areas.
answer_text: I have allocated an additional 26% in 2025/26 to support the rural Dial-a-Lift and urban Disability Action Transport schemes across all areas in the north.  The level of funding was commensurate with the level of funding requested by those providing these services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure the outline (i) the data her Department holds on unmet transport need in East Londonderry; and (ii) how this informs investment decisions.
answer_text: My department, through the application process for the Dial-a-Lift service, receive information from the Rural Community Transport Partnerships, which provides data on the level of demand they expect to fulfil in terms of this transport service. This includes information from the North Coast Community Transport partnership that primarily covers East Londonderry. In addition, East Londonderry is covered by two Local Transport Plans: The Northwest Transport Plan and the Causeway Coast and Glens Transport Plan. These transport plans are being developed in an integrated manner with each of the council’s Local Development Plans. For this reason, these plans are at very different stages in their development. My department is currently progressing Stage 1 of the Northwest Transport Plan (NWTP), which will guide future transport investment across the Northwest region. The Plan will identify proposals to address local transport issues, including local bus services, active travel, local road network and parking. It will provide most local detail in Derry and Strabane and this will be for the plan period up to 2035. A Baseline Report has been initially prepared to support the development of the NWTP.  However, the Plans work is not sufficiently advanced at this stage to provide commentary on proposed transport measures.  The Causeway Coast and Glens Transport Plan is currently at the mobilisation phase and is not yet sufficiently advanced at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any steps she is taking to ensure the long-term sustainability of community transport services in East Londonderry.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of community transport in improving the lives of individuals within our communities who experience social isolation because they are unable to access suitable transport.  Community transport provides access to local goods and services, maintains connections to friends and families and offers independence to those with few travel options.My Department has funded community transport services in excess of 20 years and I have allocated an additional 26% in 2025/26 to support the rural Dial-a-Lift and urban Disability Action Transport schemes in all areas including East Derry.  The level of funding was commensurate with the level of funding requested by those providing these services.I hope to move to multiyear budgets which would provide certainty around future budgets to community transport providers, assisting them in planning future investment in services and activitiesTranslink has an ongoing partnership with the local community transport provider, North Coast Community Transport. This partnership provides users of community transport services with access to Coleraine bus and rail station for onward journey options, thereby providing opportunities for connections between community transport and the mainstream public transport network. This model of interchange between modes maximises the efficiency of the combined resources, enabling community transport to provide demand responsive solutions from rural locations and connections to the main public transport network for onward journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the steps her Department is taking to improve rural transport connectivity in East Londonderry.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of both community and public transport in improving the lives of vulnerable groups within our communities.  It provides access to local goods and services, maintains connections to friends and families and offers independence to those with few travel options.I have allocated an additional 26% in 2025/26 to support the rural Dial-a-Lift and urban Disability Action Transport schemes in all areas including East Derry.  The level of funding was commensurate with the level of funding requested by those providing these services.  It should provide additional connectivity in the form of more local journeys within that area.While Translink are responsible for the operational delivery of public transport services they provide under the Public Service Agreement, they have advised the network is designed to enhance connectivity throughout the area.  The Translink website and Journey planner app would be the best resource for viewing the extensive available services in the area.The draft Transport Strategy 2035 is currently out for its public consultation. I recognise that the transport needs in rural areas are very different to those in urban areas and in remote areas it can be challenging to provide the same frequency of services along direct routes to make public transport a realistic alternative to private vehicles. These areas of the draft strategy are very important, and I look forward to receiving people’s views on this issue as part of the public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any discussions he, or his Department has had with the Department for Work and Pensions regarding the Third State Pension age review; and (ii) how he intends to ensure that the implications for people due to retire between April 2026 and April 2028 are taken into account.
answer_text: I have been assured by the Minister for Pensions in the Department for Work and Pensions, Torsten Bell MP, that the differences between the countries and regions of the UK, including Northern Ireland, will be considered during the State Pension age review. I have asked my officials to collaborate with their counterparts at the Department for Work and Pensions throughout this process to provide any relevant Northern Ireland data requested. On 15 September, I, along with officials from my Department, met with Dr Suzy Morrissey, who was appointed by the UK Government to prepare an Independent Report to examine factors the Government should consider in determining the State Pension age. Other key stakeholders in attendance on the day were representatives from Advice NI and Age NI, as well as the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland.The scope of the third State Pension age review, and the resulting Independent Report, is to consider the key factors the UK Government should take into account in determining State Pension age for future decades, balancing consideration of fairness and sustainability.The current timetable for Northern Ireland and Great Britain, as per the Pensions Act 2014, is that State Pension age will be raised to age 67 for everyone from 6 April 2028. This is to be increased gradually between 6 April 2026 and 5 April 2028, with people who reach State Pension age during this transition phase having been previously notified by letter.Individuals who reach State Pension age during this transition phase will be unaffected by this latest review.  The final report is not due until March 2029, and State Pension age during the period in question has already been legislated for.Further details on the scope and terms of the Independent Report, part of the third review of State Pension age, can be found at:https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/third-review-of-state-pension-age-independent-report-terms-of-reference

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Education Authority has secured a permanent site for Causeway Academy, and what the projected timeline is for completion.
answer_text: Causeway Academy will open in September 2026 across three existing sites. The Education Authority is currently working with the Principal Designate on the configuration of the three sites.   Whilst a new school formed and operating on a split site following an amalgamation would be well placed to receive funding for a new build in a future major capital call, there can be no guarantees that this would be the case. Given the constrained capital budget position, I cannot confirm when I will be in a position to make a new call for major works projects. I will continue to make the case for increased and sustained investment in education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider permitting Health and Social Care Trusts to pay the Northern Ireland-equivalent cost of treatment directly under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, so that patients only need to cover any additional charges.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is a patient led Scheme based on the previous RoI Reimbursement Scheme and EU Cross Border Healthcare Directive. In choosing to arrange healthcare themselves, the patient is effectively stepping outside of the HSC system entirely.  At this point, the patient is taking individual responsibility for ensuring that the service they obtain is appropriate and safe within the laws of ROI or EU (not under UK legislation). The Department of Health, under this scheme, will not be formally commissioning services from private providers abroad and therefore will not be liable for the outcome of the treatment provided. The HSC Trusts are not involved with this process and on that basis this is not something my Department will be considering.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority has decided how pupils will be allocated across the three sites of Causeway Academy from September 2026, and whether this will be determined by year group.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), Project Board, the Interim Board of Governors and the Principal Designate are currently finalising the plans for Causeway Academy to open across the three existing school sites from September 2026. As soon as the operating arrangements are finalised, they will be immediately communicated to all stakeholders.The Principal Designate has been working with the Interim Board of Governors, the Principals of the three schools and the EA to ensure that everything is in place for pupils and staff in relation to curriculum, transition planning and branding of the new school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how parents will be kept informed of site allocations for their children ahead of the opening of Causeway Academy in September 2026.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it is working with the Principal Designate of Causeway Academy on the configuration of sites for opening the new school on 1 September 2026.  When this has been confirmed, the Principal Designate will issue relevant communications, by newsletter and social media, to relevant stakeholders.A Communications Plan is also in place to provide updates to current and prospective pupils, parents, staff, governors and the wider school community at key times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether menopause education programmes for women include information on differentiating potential cancer symptoms from normal menopausal changes.
answer_text: Public health information about the menopause, including both the Public Health Agency’s Sexual Health NI website and NI Direct, include common signs and symptoms to look for as well as advice about when to seek medical help.  Public health information is also widely available regarding the signs and symptoms of various types of cancer, and when to seek medical opinion. Healthcare professionals are trained to identify and diagnose these conditions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they are planning strategically for the long-term implications of an ageing population.
answer_text: Older people make such a valuable contribution to our local communities and it is vital we do everything we can to support them.  We understand the importance of meeting the challenges we face with an ageing population, but we must also embrace the opportunities.  Our Programme for Government includes commitments to wellbeing and inclusion that apply directly to older people. And a new Active Ageing Action Plan, based on the framework of the previous Strategy, is being led by the Department for Communities. This plan will help address the needs and priorities for older people and alongside our PfG commitments, will ensure we can make an immediate and lasting impact on the quality of the lives of our older people. We are also developing strategies on data and the use of analytics to improve policy making and future proofing. This will prove critical in the planning and investing in the right areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider additional screening measures to improve early detection of non-HPV related gynaecological cancers.
answer_text: All screening programmes in Northern Ireland are based on the advice and recommendations of the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific advisory committee, that advises the four UK Health Departments on all screening matters; and on the advice from the Northern Ireland Screening Committee (NISC). Almost all cervical cancers are linked with persistent infection with a high-risk HPV and since December 2023, in line with the current UK NSC advice, which was endorsed by the NISC, the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme has implemented primary HPV testing.  This is considered to be a more accurate and effective test at detecting those at higher risk of developing cervical cancer. The same process is in place in the rest of the UK and Ireland.I will consider any future recommendations from the UKNSC in relation to additional screening measures to improve early detection of non-HPV related gynaecological cancers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will review current eligibility criteria for BRCA1 and BRCA2 genetic testing to ensure that all individuals with a strong family history of breast or ovarian cancer can access testing consistently.
answer_text: The NI Regional Genetics Service is based at Belfast City Hospital and receives referrals from both primary and secondary care across the whole of Northern Ireland. Patients and families affected by breast and ovarian cancer in Northern Ireland are assessed against UK-wide clinical guidelines. Eligibility criteria for BRCA1 and BRCA2 genetic testing are set out in the National Genomics Test Directory to provide guidance on which patients may benefit from genomic testing. The criteria are developed by the Test Evaluation Working Group which includes representatives from devolved nations, including Northern Ireland. This ensures that the needs of all UK nations are considered in the development of testing guidelines

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will publish data on regional variation in waiting times for gynaecological cancer referrals and diagnostics.
answer_text: Data on waiting times for gynaecological cancer referrals and diagnostics for each Trust is available on the My Waiting Times NI website. https://online.hscni.net/my-waiting-times-ni/My Waiting Times NI website provides the average outpatient waiting time information for outpatient referrals, by Trust and by speciality across Northern Ireland.  Under the speciality of ‘gynaecology’ patients can find the average outpatient wait time by referral priority. Red flag priority show appointments allocated to patients with suspected cancer diagnosis and are the highest priority.This section will also show the average await in weeks for gynaecology colposcopy in each Trust.  The data presented in the website reflects the average waiting time for those patients who are actively waiting for their appointment or procedure. Some patients will wait less time than the average and some patients will wait longer than the average waiting time indicated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of red flag gynaecological oncology appointments that have been cancelled in the last 12 months; and (ii) the reasons for the cancellations.
answer_text: There is currently no centralised system for the regional collation of data on gynaecological oncology appointment cancellations. The information held by individual Trusts is variable and recorded across different systems this is in part due to the phased implementation of the Encompass electronic patient record system over the past two years. As a result, the data cannot be easily collated or used to produce a consistent, accurate regional figure.Looking ahead, the Encompass system will be further explored to assess how cancellation data can be more effectively coded, collated, and analysed to support future regional reporting and service improvement. While all Trusts diagnose gynaecological cancers, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) provides the specialist regional Gynae- oncology service. All Trusts transfer patients requiring specialist gynecologically surgery to this service. Table 1 below shows the number of red-flag cancellations over the last 12 months for the Belfast Service (local and regional) and the reasons for these cancellations.828 cancellations were made of which 527 were cancelled by the service and 297 were cancelled by patients.  Table 1 – No. of Gynae oncology cancellations and reasons for these cancellations in the last 12 months: BHSCT local and regional gynae-oncology service.No of CancellationsReason for cancellationCancellation Initiator 52Clinician not availableservice 14Clinician medical reasonservice 2Clinician Patient Unitservice 1Added to Inpatient / Day Case W. Listservice 39Admin Errorservice 2Ambulance unavailable / Not bookedservice 1Awaiting further investigationsservice 91Clinic Cancelled / Reducedservice 38Clinic Reorganisationservice 3Currently admittedservice 15Industrial Actionservice 1Insufficient noticeservice 40No longer requiredservice 143Otherservice 5Patient Discharged from Trustservice 1Patient seen separately for same conditionservice 28Appoint rescheduled (Put back)service 55Appoint Rescheduled (Brought Forward)service 6Refusal of Reasonable OfferPatient162Date unsuitablePatient1Lack of TransportationPatient17Not now requiredPatient84OtherPatient27UnwellPatientTotal – 828It should be noted that cancelled appointments cannot be directly correlated to reduced activity. BHSCT appointment teams will maximise all capacity and ensure even last-minute cancellations are filled. The BHSCT is proactively managing the reduction of outpatient cancellations and the percentage of patients who ‘do not attend’ to focus on maximising productivity within outpatients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) how Operation Encompass is being evaluated; and (ii) the measures in place to assess its effectiveness.
answer_text: The Operation Encompass response is undertaken by the PSNI. My department has no role in assessing its overall effectiveness Officials will, however, be commissioning an independent evaluation of the Education Authority role in supporting schools who receive an Operation Encompass referral during this academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support available for children, following an Operation Encompass referral, to ensure their wellbeing is monitored beyond the immediate response.
answer_text: Support for children following an Operation Encompass notification is the responsibility of the school's Designated Teacher. They will assess the child and co-ordinate support based on the child’s identified needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the training provided to designated teachers in schools, to respond to Operation Encompass referrals.
answer_text: Designated Teachers in Northern Ireland are provided with the following specialised training/support, alongside some additional training to support Operation Encompass referrals: Initial two-day training for Designated Teachers/Deputy Designated Teachers provided by the Education Authority's Child Protection Support Service (CPSS). This will be followed by a half-day refresher training course every three years. The specific Operation Encompass ‘Key Adult' online training module, with completion certification being verified by Police Service of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Operation Encompass referrals which have resulted in onward referrals to social services or other safeguarding interventions.
answer_text: The department does not hold this information. These questions would be a matter for the Minister of Justice to answer as the Operation Encompass response is undertaken by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children referred through Operation Encompass in (i) 2023-24; and (ii) 2024-25, broken down by constituency
answer_text: The department does not hold this information. These questions would be a matter for the Minister of Justice to answer as the Operation Encompass response is undertaken by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department intends to reduce diagnostic delays for ovarian cancer, particularly the delays in obtaining and processing tests such as CA125 blood tests and ultrasounds.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the critical importance of early detection and timely diagnosis in improving outcomes for women affected by ovarian cancer.   Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) referral guidance recommends measuring CA125 in primary care in women with symptoms that suggest ovarian cancer and, if levels are raised, to arrange an ultrasound scan of the abdomen and pelvis and/or refer to gynaecology as a red flag.    Department officials have been advised that there is no systemic problem with delays in processing CA125 results.  There are however recognised challenges in the available capacity for diagnostic imaging including non-obstetric ultrasound (NOUS) and gynaecology services in secondary care which are impacting on the timely delivery of diagnostics.The recently published Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan sets out clear proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards and how we will transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in the future.   Examples within the plan that may support the diagnosis of ovarian cancer include:Gynaecology (£3.5m) - Demand for outpatients, daycase and inpatient red flag and time critical gynaecology services is significantly above capacity available. There is a need to expand gynaecology capacity to manage red flag and time critical patients and expand capacity in medical, nursing and allied health profession patient pathways. This investment will allow for 4,100 assessments and 900 treatments. Diagnostic Imaging - Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Computed Tomography (CT) and Non-Obstetric Ultrasound (NOUS) (£12m) – based on the current spend, use of existing capital equipment, and available staffing infrastructure, there is the potential to reduce the current demand/capacity gap in regional imaging. This will potentially include an additional 5,880 funded CT/MRI sessions and 2,000 funded NOUS sessions per annum.In addition, my Department’s has established a Regional Health and Social Care (HSC) Gynaecology Services Forum to oversee the implementation of service improvement recommendations from the “Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report for Northern Ireland," published in January 2024.  GIRFT recommendations include streamlining patient pathways to improve diagnostic pathway and efficiency in patient care.  This includes development of pathways that use a range of skills mix of nursing and allied health professionals to assist with triage and diagnostic procedures including colposcopy and hysteroscopy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to roll out government-funded awareness campaigns to increase recognition of lesser-known symptoms of ovarian cancer, such as urinary symptoms.
answer_text: Given the current financial position, there are no plans to roll out government-funded awareness campaigns to increase the recognition of lesser-known symptoms of ovarian cancer at this time. However, the Public Health Agency (PHA) works closely with the Department of Health, local Health and Social Care Trusts, and other partners to support a range of activities to raise awareness of ovarian cancer symptoms and services, including:• Ovarian Cancer Awareness Month - each March the PHA issue a press release and social media messaging to increase awareness of ovarian cancer (https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/news/ovarian-cancer-awareness-month-be-aware-signs-and-symptoms-0). • Social media - the PHA posts regularly through social media channels to promote cancer awareness messaging, providing information on the signs and symptoms of cancer, including ovarian cancer, and healthy changes people can make to help reduce their risk of developing cancer in the future. • ‘Be Cancer Aware’ Website - all PHA cancer awareness messaging signposts to its ‘Be Cancer Aware’ website. The website provides information about the signs and symptoms of a range of cancers, including ovarian cancer, and signposts to recommended sources of support or further information (https://www.becancerawareni.info/signs-and-symptoms/ovarian-cancer).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will mandate that information given at cervical screening appointments clearly states that the screening does not detect ovarian, or any other gynaecological, cancer.
answer_text: The current public information on cervical screening does not reference other forms of cancer. The Public Health Agency’s (PHA) public facing information and resources for all screening programmes are kept under regular review and are updated as necessary. The PHA will consider the recent changes made to the information in England and if it is appropriate will incorporate similar messaging locally at the next review of their patient leaflets for the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to increase access to and uptake of genomic and germline testing for ovarian cancer patients.
answer_text: Patients in Northern Ireland who meet the criteria outlined in the National Genomic Test Directory criteria for germline testing related to ovarian cancer are eligible for testing via the Northern Ireland Regional Molecular Diagnostics Service (RMDS), based within the Belfast HSC Trust. This approach ensures equity across the UK.In addition, Action 27 of the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy provides a commitment to integrate genomic testing into cancer pathways, in line with NICE recommendations. This action is designed to improve access to genomic testing and enhance diagnosis, treatment and outcomes for cancer patients, including those with ovarian cancer. Under the action, the RMDS sequences 3500 solid tumour samples per year. Where a potential germline disposition is identified, patients are fast tracked for confirmatory testing, allowing for personalised treatment and the identification of at-risk family members. Northern Ireland was the first region in the UK to undertake this revolutionary pathway at a population scale, enabled by investment from the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy. However, additional funding will be necessary to further extend this pathway to increase the number of solid tumour samples genomically sequenced per year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the potential benefits of implementing Jess’s Rule for earlier cancer diagnosis.
answer_text: My Department expects medical professionals to act in line with the General Medical Council’s “Good Medical Practice” guidance, which requires medical professionals to adequately assess a patient’s condition(s), taking account of their history, carry out a physical examination where necessary, and promptly provide (or arrange) suitable advice, investigation or treatment where necessaryThis should be the case whether a patient consultation takes place in person, online or via telephone. As part of the Cancer Strategy, within Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDC) my Department has established a Vague Symptom Pathway which offer GPs a way to refer patients with non-specific but worrying symptoms that might indicate cancer but don't fit existing "red flag" pathways. Patients receive a coordinated, one-stop assessment to investigate their symptoms, and results are reported quickly, enabling earlier diagnosis and onward referral Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres are currently in operation, located at South Tyrone Hospital in the Southern HSC Trust and Whiteabbey Hospital in the Northern HSC Trust. The Vague Symptom Pathway became a fully regional service in July 2024 with patients across all five Trusts in Northern Ireland now able to be referred into either of the two RDC sites directly by their GP, regardless of their location.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the consideration they will give to incorporating responsible media reporting of domestic abuse into future iterations of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Delivery Plan.
answer_text: Responsible media reporting is an important issue for all violence against women and girls’ incidents, including those of domestic abuse.There is already a focus on improving standards of reporting in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework. The intention is to progress this work in future iterations of the delivery plan.We are supportive of the work Women’s Aid Federation NI has done with local journalists to develop ‘Responsible Reporting Matters’ guidelines for media outlets here, and these have recently been updated.This has been enabled through support from the Department of Justice (DoJ) and Department of Health (DoH) who have funded the update of these resources, as well as funding workshops and training sessions for journalists.We will continue to collaborate with our Community and Voluntary Sector partners and stakeholders across departments to improve responsible media reporting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department is working with the Department of Justice and the Department of Health to ensure the implementation of the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, supports the Executive’s wider strategy to tackle domestic abuse.
answer_text: My officials engage with their Health and Justice counterparts to ensure that the actions relevant to my Department within the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031 Action Plan Year 1-3 are taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she plans to introduce the legislation required to implement the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, during the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Yes, it is my intention to introduce the legislation during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when her Department expects to publish the findings of the 2024 public consultation on the implementation of the Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: Work to finalise the consultation analysis is at an advanced stage and I hope to publish the findings as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how her Department will (i) support the relaunch of Women’s Aid Federation NI’s Responsible Reporting Matters guidelines; and (ii) reflect their principles in its domestic abuse policies and communications.
answer_text: I was pleased, along with the Permanent Secretary of the Department of Health, to open the relaunch of Women’s Aid Federation NI’s Responsible Reporting Matters guidelines on 3 March 2025. My Department, working jointly with the Department of Health, provided funding through the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Small Grant Scheme 24/25 to support the revision and re-launch of the guidance, and to enable Women’s Aid to deliver a series of workshops to journalists. I believe that these guidelines will encourage more responsible reporting within the media, help to dispel myths and narratives that can perpetuate victim-blaming, and make victims and survivors more comfortable in coming forward to seek support.I believe the updated guidelines are crucial in creating a wider understanding of domestic abuse and violence against women and girls within the media. My Department will continue to refer to these principles in the development of new policies and communications, ensuring that our approach remains trauma-informed and supports all victims and survivors in the most appropriate way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timeline for purchasing the 600 temporary accommodation units for the Housing Executive which he announced in March 2025.
answer_text: No properties have yet been purchased under this scheme however NIHE estimate a purchasing profile of: 25 Yr1 2025/26, 125 Yr2 2026/27, 225 Yr3 2027/28 225 Yr4 2028/29

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Housing Supply Strategy 2024-2039 will support pathways from temporary accommodation into permanent housing.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy sets out the need to prevent homelessness. It recognises the inextricable link between housing, health and social care and support system and the necessity of a continuum of support from temporary to permanent accommodation. Actions aligned to the objectives of the Strategy will be developed throughout its lifetime, taking into account actions also being delivered through the Homelessness Strategy for NI.One action already being taken forward by my Department to support those moving from temporary accommodation into permanent housing, is the development of the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation (LAMA).The Fund, which was launched in December 2024, is a £10m fund running for five years and is available to Homelessness charities in Northern Ireland looking to provide well managed, long term, private rented sector accommodation at an affordable rate.The aim over the five years of the Project is to provide 80-100 units of accommodation as a route into housing for those who cannot access permanent social housing or private rented accommodation, supporting the Homelessness Strategy for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how he will ensure that the 600 temporary accommodation units announced in March 2025 are allocated fairly across council areas, according to local need.
answer_text: NIHE has advised me that the principal considerations in the geographical distribution of these units will include:Geographical areas where exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation is most prevalent.The nature of the temporary accommodation client groups (single persons, couples, families).The availability of suitable property available to purchase that align with the above criteria.Value for money considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how his Department will improve pathways from temporary accommodation into permanent housing in East Londonderry.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to supporting applicants in temporary accommodation, including assisting with timeliness of move-on options, is outlined in the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation which can be found at:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/0d1aee01-f02c-40d6-b95b-a58c7213c2c0/Temporary-Accommodation-Strategic-Action-Plan-2022-27.pdfThe Year 4 Actions can be found here:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/93744f51-9819-46ba-85a3-723da092667e/Year-4-Actions-Strategic-Action-Plan-Temporary-Accommodation-2022-27.pdf”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to reduce the length of time households spend in temporary accommodation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s approach to supporting applicants in temporary accommodation, including assisting with reducing the length of time households spend in temporary accommodation, is outlined in the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation which can be found here https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/0d1aee01-f02c-40d6-b95b-a58c7213c2c0/Temporary-Accommodation-Strategic-Action-Plan-2022-27.pdf The Year 4 Actions can be found here https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/93744f51-9819-46ba-85a3-723da092667e/Year-4-Actions-Strategic-Action-Plan-Temporary-Accommodation-2022-27.pdf”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether emotional wellbeing and meaningful stimulation are included as core standards in dementia care.
answer_text: Unpaid Carers of people with dementia in Northern Ireland have access to a wide range of physical and emotional support services. Each HSC Trust will offer a carers assessment and each Trust has a carer co-ordinator to support carers with a wide range of physical and emotional supports.  Praxis care offers a floating support service in the Coleraine, Foyle Londonderry and lisnaskea Fermanagh area. They visit, carers and people living with dementia in their homes to assist with daily tasks and emotional well-being. Respite services can be offered to carers to provide a short break from their caring role. The C&V sector also provide a range of physical and emotional supports to unpaid carers.Dementia NI runs empower and support group across Northern Ireland allowing carers and people with dementia to share experiences and receive peer support.Alzheimer’s Society dementia advisors offers awareness sessions to carersCarers NI provide advice and guidance to unpaid carers with expert information, guidance and support.A Regional Dementia Project Board was established in May 2024. Its priorities include working collaboratively with all relevant stakeholders to improve services for people with dementia and their carers. It focuses on service delivery within the context of the Regional Dementia care pathway. It has developed a Service User and Carer workstream which will incorporate the voices of service users and carers.The Reginal Dementia Care Pathway is the current strategic tool that has a number of core values that underpin Dementia care these are Enabling experience, Early intervention, Personalisation and Integration. The pathway adopts a person-centred holistic approach ensuing emotional and social needs are met. There are 5 pillars of the Pathway and the 3rd pillar ‘Living Well with dementia’ promotes emotional well-being through meaningful activities, community engagement and psychological support. It encourages social inclusion and independence to reduce social isolation, maintain dignity and promotes stimulation. The 4th Pillar ‘Coping with Change’ offers emotional and psychological support for both individuals with dementia and their carers. This would include access to support groups, counselling and peer networks to help manage the emotional impact of dementia.The Department of Health has acknowledged the issue of delays in dementia diagnosis and their impact on the health and social care system. My Department has taken some key actions in recognition of the delays in dementia diagnosis.My Department has set a 9 -week target for first assessment from referral to memory services and Trusts’ performance continues to be monitored against this target with improvement plans in place to address areas of underperformance.My Department leads on a number of working groups with Trust Dementia Services personnel to continually seek to make improvements to dementia services within the statutory sector. This includes a focus on service delivery from

answer_text: Unpaid Carers of people with dementia in Northern Ireland have access to a wide range of physical and emotional support services.
