This includes a focus on service delivery from

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when his Department plans to publish an updated dementia strategy; and (ii) whether that strategy will include measures to improve emotional wellbeing and prevent isolation.
answer_text: The strategic tool for Dementia is the Regional Dementia Care Pathway. The Pathway adopts a person-centred, holistic approach to Dementia care, ensuring that emotional and social needs are addressed alongside clinical support. The Pathway was developed with input from people living with Dementia and their carers, ensuring that emotional needs and social inclusion were not overlooked but embedded throughout the care journey.Implementation of the Pathway has, regrettably, been delayed due to funding pressures, significant service capacity challenges to deliver existing care pathways with gaps in available workforce and increased demand. To address this, a Regional Dementia Services Project Board has been established to refocus attention on Dementia care as a priority across Northern Ireland.Our Health and Social Care Trusts, working with community partners, remain committed to raising awareness and providing support for those living with Dementia and their Carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what specific support is available to unpaid carers of people with dementia to assist them to provide both physical and emotional care.
answer_text: Unpaid Carers of people with dementia in Northern Ireland have access to a wide range of physical and emotional support services. Each HSC Trust will offer a carers assessment and each Trust has a carer co-ordinator to support carers with a wide range of physical and emotional supports.  Praxis care offers a floating support service in the Coleraine, Foyle Londonderry and lisnaskea Fermanagh area. They visit, carers and people living with dementia in their homes to assist with daily tasks and emotional well-being. Respite services can be offered to carers to provide a short break from their caring role. The C&V sector also provide a range of physical and emotional supports to unpaid carers.Dementia NI runs empower and support group across Northern Ireland allowing carers and people with dementia to share experiences and receive peer support.Alzheimer’s Society dementia advisors offers awareness sessions to carersCarers NI provide advice and guidance to unpaid carers with expert information, guidance and support.A Regional Dementia Project Board was established in May 2024. Its priorities include working collaboratively with all relevant stakeholders to improve services for people with dementia and their carers. It focuses on service delivery within the context of the Regional Dementia care pathway. It has developed a Service User and Carer workstream which will incorporate the voices of service users and carers.The Reginal Dementia Care Pathway is the current strategic tool that has a number of core values that underpin Dementia care these are Enabling experience, Early intervention, Personalisation and Integration. The pathway adopts a person-centred holistic approach ensuing emotional and social needs are met. There are 5 pillars of the Pathway and the 3rd pillar ‘Living Well with dementia’ promotes emotional well-being through meaningful activities, community engagement and psychological support. It encourages social inclusion and independence to reduce social isolation, maintain dignity and promotes stimulation. The 4th Pillar ‘Coping with Change’ offers emotional and psychological support for both individuals with dementia and their carers. This would include access to support groups, counselling and peer networks to help manage the emotional impact of dementia.The Department of Health has acknowledged the issue of delays in dementia diagnosis and their impact on the health and social care system. My Department has taken some key actions in recognition of the delays in dementia diagnosis.My Department has set a 9 -week target for first assessment from referral to memory services and Trusts’ performance continues to be monitored against this target with improvement plans in place to address areas of underperformance.My Department leads on a number of working groups with Trust Dementia Services personnel to continually seek to make improvements to dementia services within the statutory sector. This includes a focus on service delivery from

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current average waiting time for a memory clinic appointment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) what steps are being taken to reduce delays in dementia diagnosis.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts do not routinely report on the average waiting time for a memory clinic appointment. As part of the Encompass roll-out, the Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system. As the Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, the data generated within the context of service delivery is subject to a process of extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data that Trusts are able to submit for reporting purposes.My health officials work closely with the HSC Trusts and I can advise that the steps taken to reduce delays for  a memory clinic appointments include;Exploring waiting list initiatives.Raising awareness of Dementia and early symptoms and the importance of seeking earlier diagnosis;Exploring the education and recruitment of Advanced Nurse Practitioners into Memory Services to aid with diagnosis. The WHSCT and the NHSCT currently have employed an Advanced Nurse Practitioner within their memory services. Active recruitment being undertaken by Trusts that are facing challenges with recruitment of Psychiatrists.Exploring the use of bank staff to increase capacity for diagnostic assessments in the Memory Service.Ongoing administration and clinical validation exercises to cleanse current waiting list.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether funding allocated to the Affordable Warmth Scheme for 2025-26 has been released to the Housing Executive.
answer_text: I can confirm that the funding allocated to the Affordable Warmth Scheme for 2025-26 has been released to the Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department is taking to prevent lengthy delays in approvals for applications made to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, particularly for pensioners and other vulnerable applicants.
answer_text: I committed £7.5m to the Affordable Warmth Scheme this year covering £5m of committed approvals from last year and £2.5m for new approvals this year meaning approximately 570 new approvals can be issued by NIHE.  I requested additional funding for the Affordable Warmth Scheme in the June Monitoring Round; however, that bid was not met. The Housing Executive prioritise eligible applications in line with criteria, as agreed with my Department, prioritising those households without an operational heating system. I will continue to press for additional funding for this important scheme through the monitoring round process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the expected timeline for the delivery of Winter Fuel Payments for winter 2025-26.
answer_text: Winter Fuel Payment in Northern Ireland is administered and paid by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP). Payments are made automatically to those eligible and it is expected that the majority of payments will be made during November and December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any targeted measures being developed to support older people in rural areas facing higher heating costs.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of finalising the new Fuel Poverty Strategy for Northern Ireland. With a vision of “a warm, healthy home for everyone,” the strategy has three objectives: make homes more energy efficient, collaborate and build capacity and protect consumers. The aim is to support low income households with better access to trusted energy and energy efficiency advice and with energy saving measures for their homes.  My Department also delivers the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS) for owner occupiers and those in the private rented sector most at risk of fuel poverty. The scheme is directed at low-income households, though not specifically targeted to older people. It provides a range of energy-efficiency improvement measures including cavity and loft insulation, new and replacement heating systems and windows, where appropriate. The Department is also currently developing a new more ambitious Warm Healthy Homes Scheme that will replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends. It will provide support to low-income households experiencing fuel poverty, offering a range of measures including insulation, low carbon heating solutions and renewable technologies. The Scheme will be open to applications from all households that meet the eligibility criteria including those with older occupants in rural areas.Work is also ongoing on a number of activities to promote the uptake of Pension Credit, which can help older people access extra support with their heating costs, through the Cold Weather Payment.  This includes:regular social media and broadcast campaigns, with a further advertising campaign scheduled for October across radio and press channels; distribution of information leaflets to libraries and GP surgeries; collaboration with other statutory bodies such as the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and Northern Ireland Health and Social Care Trusts;  identifying Pension Credit entitlement at the point where a person makes their State Pension claim.   My Department continues to work closely with community and voluntary sector partners to reach out to those who are eligible but not currently claiming. This approach helps ensure older people, particularly in rural communities are informed about, and able to access the financial support available to help them with their heating costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Anti-Poverty Strategy will address the needs of older people, including (i) fuel poverty; and (ii) the rising cost of living.
answer_text: I remain committed to developing a sustainable, deliverable Anti-Poverty Strategy, prioritising the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives throughout NI, including the needs of older people.The draft Strategy contains a number of strategic commitments which will help address the needs of older people including -Lobbying Westminster to maintain the triple lock guarantee in relation to pension payments.Developing and delivering a Fuel Poverty Strategy.Providing advice, increasing awareness and providing focused support to ensure that older people can access their full benefit entitlement. (This will include a programme of work to promote Pension Credit uptake and delivery of the Make the Call wraparound service.)	In addition to this the Strategy also contains work which, while not targeted solely towards the needs of older people, will help improve their lives alongside many others across our communities. These include, the development and delivery of a new Safer Communities Strategic Approach, a new People and Place Strategic Framework, and the implementation of the Executive Housing Supply and Disability Strategies. 		While the Department for Communities is leading on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, it remains an Executive Strategy. My officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received throughout the public consultation process.  I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues and we will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to improve (i) pay, (ii) career progression,; and (iii) working conditions for childcare staff, as part of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: The provision of high-quality inclusive childcare for children with additional needs and to support parents working non-standard hours has been identified by stakeholders as a priority.  Proposals will be included within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy I plan to bring forward to the Executive in the coming weeks.While the Department of Education does not have a role in setting pay or terms and conditions for childcare staff for a sector which is largely made up of private providers, the draft Strategy will include proposals to support the childcare workforce, including training needs, recruitment and retention challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will expand the Women’s Centre Childcare Fund to better meet the needs of low-income families.
answer_text: The Women’s Centre Childcare Fund (WCCF) is aimed at assisting low income families by providing two hour childcare sessions to parents who are in receipt of Income Support, or Unemployed and registered for JSA (income based), Employment Support Allowance or Universal Credit and engaged in part-time (less than 16 hours per week) paid voluntary / community development work or engaged in vocational / educational development or training or engaged in personal development training. Should none of these apply, have within the previous 12 months, progressed into employment following a period of unemployment.There are no plans to expand the current eligibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration being given to the provision of childcare support during non-standard working hours, to enable parents in shift work to remain in employment.
answer_text: The provision of high-quality inclusive childcare for children with additional needs and to support parents working non-standard hours has been identified by stakeholders as a priority.  Proposals will be included within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy I plan to bring forward to the Executive in the coming weeks.While the Department of Education does not have a role in setting pay or terms and conditions for childcare staff for a sector which is largely made up of private providers, the draft Strategy will include proposals to support the childcare workforce, including training needs, recruitment and retention challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the investment being made in training and skills development for the childcare workforce.
answer_text: High-quality, affordable childcare remains a strategic priority for the Executive. Supporting skills and education pathways for those working within the childcare and childminding sectors is essential to achieving this aim. My Department currently facilitates a range of training and qualification pathways through   Further and Higher Education institutions, as well as vocational routes including apprenticeships, traineeships and academies. This year, I have secured over £350,000 to support Further Education Colleges to develop a series of online accredited childcare specific micro courses, at Levels 2 and 3, building on the £740,000 invested last year. A second collaborative Assured Skills Academy in Early Learning and Childcare has also recently launched to support participants with the training needed to gain entry level roles in the childcare sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he will ensure that all children have access to high quality and inclusive childcare, including those with special educational needs or language barriers.
answer_text: The provision of high-quality inclusive childcare for children with additional needs and to support parents working non-standard hours has been identified by stakeholders as a priority.  Proposals will be included within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy I plan to bring forward to the Executive in the coming weeks.While the Department of Education does not have a role in setting pay or terms and conditions for childcare staff for a sector which is largely made up of private providers, the draft Strategy will include proposals to support the childcare workforce, including training needs, recruitment and retention challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the July 2025 Northern Ireland Audit Office report Waste Crime in Northern Ireland finding that waste crime costs approximately £34 million annually; and (ii) what steps are being taken to strengthen enforcement and deterrence.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of this report and I can provide assurance that its recommendations will be considered very carefully by officials in the Northern Ireland Environment Agency and the Department.I am conscious also that, when it comes to reports from the NI Audit Office, the general principle is that departments should restrict comments until the Assembly process is complete.   It is now for the Public Accounts Committee to consider the report’s findings and to consider whether it wishes the Departmental and NIEA Accounting Officers to give evidence and it is important that I abide by the Department of Finance’s guidance that no comment should be made that might pre-empt or pre-judge PAC consideration. Waste crime poses a serious threat to our environment, to public health and to the integrity of our waste management systems.  It also undermines law-abiding operators and damages public confidence.Strengthening our environmental governance structures is a top priority for me and I recently took receipt of and published the interim report from the independent panel that I set up to bring forward proposals for a more robust model of environmental governance. Whether within existing structures or new structures, tackling waste crime is also a priority for me and for the NIEA and we will continue to work hard on both prevention and enforcement, with a clear focus on delivering results.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of livestock worrying incidents recorded, in each of the last three years; and (ii) what measures are in place to support farmers affected.
answer_text: Councils are responsible under The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (the Order) for investigating incidents of livestock worrying. The Department collates this data from all 11 councils and publishes on our website.  Table A sets out the number of recorded livestock worrying incidents for each of the last three years up to March 2025.I take the matter of livestock worrying very seriously and my Department continues to remind dog owners of the need to keep their dogs under control around livestock. Farmers are also encouraged to report all incidences of livestock worrying to their local council dog wardens for investigation. Additionally, my Department has produced specific guidance on the issue which is available on the DAERA website. 		Table A: Number of incidents of livestock worrying recorded in last three yearsYearNumber of incidents of livestock worrying recorded by councilsApril 24 – March 25115April 23 – March 24147April 22 – March 23122
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any steps being taken to improve firearm security in rural areas, and (ii) how breaches are monitored and enforced.
answer_text: Firearms security in rural and urban areas are all treated with the same high level of individual attention by PSNI.  The PSNI’s Firearms & Explosives Branch (FEB) are the firearms licensing authority in Northern Ireland.Security inspections are completed by trained PSNI staff, in person, at the home of the applicant/FAC holder and agreed standards, in accordance with the Firearms Security Handbook, must be met in order that an application may be progressed towards approval.  PSNI FEB can review (and potentially revoke) a person’s FAC, subject to PSNI being made aware of any breaches of a FAC, including safe storage, or any concerns about the fitness of a FAC holder to continue to possess firearms and/or ammunition.  PSNI FEB can also issue warnings, when appropriate, to FAC holders.Upon notification of any concerns, PSNI FEB will act promptly and, whilst a FAC holders’ fitness is under review e.g. following the breach of any FAC conditions, any firearms and/or ammunition will be seized and remain in police safekeeping until a final decision is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any steps being taken to address under-reporting of domestic abuse and coercive control in rural areas; and (ii) how access to refuges and specialist services is being improved in rural communities.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what assessment his Department has made of the impact of livestock and machinery theft on farm businesses and families.
answer_text: Rural crime, particularly the theft of farmed animals, is a direct threat to the livelihood of our farmers and to the integrity of the traceability system which is vital in providing assurance on the safety, integrity and quality of our food. My Department takes this threat very seriously. Officials from my Department’s Veterinary Service Animal Health Group sit on the Rural Crime Partnership forum. Rural Crime Partnership is a valuable networking, information sharing and problem-solving forum established to help address rural agri-crime. The  Department of Justice, Police Service of Northern Ireland, Policing and Community Safety Partnerships, Ulster Farmers’ Union, National Farmers’ Union Mutual, Federation of Small Businesses and the Young Farmers’ Clubs of Ulster are also represented on the Rural Crime Partnership.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) how the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy is being implemented in respect of cases where victims of domestic abuse die by suicide; and (ii) what steps the Executive Office is taking to ensure cross-departmental learning and prevention in such cases.
answer_text: The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework focuses on working better together, through a coordinated approach. This aligns with the Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Strategy led by the Departments of Justice and Health which recognises the significant impact that domestic abuse has on victims.The introduction of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 has changed the way the criminal justice system views and responds to domestic abuse. Protection is no longer limited to physical abuse, and the legislation criminalises a course of abusive behaviour including controlling or coercive behaviour, which may be psychological, emotional or financial in nature. Domestic homicide reviews (DHRs) are in place to ensure that there is system-wide learning to improve the overall response to domestic abuse.  DHRs in Northern Ireland do not currently extend to cases where a victim of domestic abuse dies by suicide as a consequence of that abuse. The Department of Justice has indicated the intention to expand the remit of DHRs, in due course, to ensure that Departments and Agencies are able to learn from such tragic circumstances in order to safeguard other victims. However these cases are, by their nature, highly complex and it will be important that any policy around domestic suicides is properly informed and fully scoped.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he will take to ensure that suicides related to domestic abuse are incorporated into suicide prevention policy and strategy.
answer_text: During the recently completed review process of the Protect Life 2 Action Plan the principles for the strategy were re-evaluated and feedback from a wide variety of stakeholders, including those impacted by domestic violence was acknowledged and incorporated.The new Action Plan includes a specific action 2.2, to implement place or setting based strategies to effectively raise awareness of available support, both universally and through targeted outreach; tailored to the needs of families, communities and groups of interest. This action contains a deliverable around support for victims of domestic and sexual violence.The Public Health Agency also fund a small grant programme throughout Northern Ireland. As part of the 2025/26 programme 7 projects supporting those impacted by domestic abuse have been funded. There may be potential for further targeting future PHA small grants opportunities on activities focusing on domestic violence suicide prevention and support. However, I must stress that full delivery of all the actions within Protect Life 2 will only be possible subject to the necessary funding being secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how his Department records suspected suicides linked to domestic abuse; and (ii) whether he intends to improve data collection in this area.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not have responsibility for the recording of deaths in Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA)which falls under the remit of the Department of Finance are responsible for the collation of death statistics. The outcome of the coronial investigation, including the formulation of the cause of death, the recorded study category and the NISRA coding process reflects the statutory focus on how the deceased died, rather than why they died. Any decisions around enhancing data collection, for example through academic research, would require consideration by the Coroners Service.The Coroner, an independent judicial office holder, investigates unexpected, sudden, violent, or unnatural deaths to establish the cause of death and investigates based on the individual circumstances of each death. Each case reported to the Coroner is considered on a case-by-case basis. The Coroner has a statutory obligation to determine, as far as is possible, on the balance of probabilities, the medical cause of death and the four statutory questions, namely who the deceased was, when and where they died and how the deceased came by their death, taking into account all relevant, available material. Coronial findings are evidence-based at the civil standard, not on the basis of suspicions. An inquest will be held where the Coroner considers it is necessary. The Coroner is legally required to focus on answering the four statutory questions, including ‘how’ rather than ‘why’ the deceased person died (albeit all relevant prevailing circumstances including mental health, situational stressors and any other relevant factors may be determined by the Coroner to fall within the scope of the inquest).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline (i) how the recommendations from Domestic Homicide Reviews are being implemented across relevant agencies; and (ii) what monitoring is in place to ensure they lead to change.
answer_text: I recognise that the learning arising from each Domestic Homicide Review (DHR) is invaluable, and I acknowledge the need for DHR recommendations to be effectively implemented. Each DHR report includes agency recommendations, where the individual  agencies involved have identified specific areas where their practices and responses could be improved. Each individual agency is responsible for implementing the recommendations directed to them and for ensuring that the necessary changes to their policies and practices are taken forward. In addition to agency-specific recommendations, each DHR report also includes overarching recommendations made by the Independent Chair and multi-agency Panel. Given the complexities involved in such cases, these overarching recommendations are often made on a multi-agency basis and often fall to a number of agencies, working in partnership, to implement across many sectors including Health, Justice, Education and Housing. My Department is not responsible for overseeing how recommendations outside of the justice sphere are implemented; that falls to each of the agencies respectively.  However, it receives regular updates from agencies on progress against each action, in order to ensure that the multi-agency DHR Senior Oversight Forum is able to track and monitor progress against implementation.Where recommendations have been made for my Department, these have been completed or are in progress and expected to be completed in the near future. However, there has been some delay to the implementation of overarching, multi-agency recommendations. In order to address this, a multi-agency working group, chaired by PSNI, has been established to drive implementation of those recommendations which cut across organisational boundaries. This group brings together representatives from the key statutory partners to ensure that shared challenges are addressed collectively and that cross-cutting recommendations are implemented in a consistent and coordinated way. The group also provides a mechanism for monitoring progress and ensuring that the learning from Reviews is embedded across the system as a whole.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she will review current law to ensure that perpetrators of domestic abuse can be held criminally responsible, where their actions result in a victim’s suicide; and (ii) to outline what consideration she has given to creating a specific offence of causing or encouraging suicide through coercive control or abuse.
answer_text: This is an extremely serious and sensitive issue. The devastating impact that domestic abuse can have on victims including the potential for it to contribute to suicide, cannot and should not be underestimated. The introduction of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 was a milestone in protecting people from violent and abusive behaviours.  The  2021 Act has changed the way in which the criminal justice system views and responds to domestic abuse. Protection is no longer limited to physical abuse, and the legislation criminalises a course of abusive behaviour including controlling or coercive behaviour, which may be psychological, emotional or financial in nature.  Section 15 of the 2021 Act provides that any offence (apart from the domestic abuse offence) may be aggravated because it involves domestic abuse. Where the offence as well as the aggravation is proved, the court must take the aggravation into account when determining sentence. In principle, this could apply to manslaughter where it was established that someone had died by suicide as a result of domestic abuse.I believe that the current legislation provides the courts with sufficient powers to hold perpetrators of domestic abuse to account. However ending domestic abuse in all its forms remains a key priority for my Department and I will continue to keep this under review, informed by learning and other relevant developments in neighbouring jurisdictions. There is currently no specific offence of causing or encouraging suicide specifically through domestic abuse including coercive control in any part of the UK; however, under sections 13 and 13A of the Criminal Justice Act (NI) 1966 (the 1966 Act), it is an offence to encourage or assist with the suicide or attempted suicide of another person, or to intend to do so; and s.13A further provides that this includes where a person has encouraged or assisted by threatening or otherwise putting pressure on another person to commit or attempt suicide.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is addressing the link between domestic abuse and suicide within service provision.
answer_text: The new Protect Life 2 Strategy Action Plan includes a specific action 2.2, to implement place or setting based strategies to effectively raise awareness of available support, both universally and through targeted outreach; tailored to the needs of families, communities and groups of interest. This action contains a deliverable around support for victims of domestic and sexual violence.The Public Health Agency also fund a small grant programme throughout Northern Ireland. As part of the 2025/26 programme 7 projects supporting those impacted by domestic abuse have been funded. There may be potential for further targeting future PHA small grants opportunities on activities focusing on domestic violence suicide prevention and support. However, I must stress that full delivery of all the actions within Protect Life 2 will only be possible subject to the necessary funding being secured.Supporting victims to disclose domestic abuse in the first place will help identify the appropriate supports required for the individual and may prevent further harm / suicide.Routine Enquiry involves asking all women who are accessing services, regardless of whether there are any indicators or suspicions, about abuse, so that they can be offered supportive interventions including information, safety planning and referral. In Northern Ireland this approach has been established across Maternity, Health Visiting and Family Nurse Partnership Services.The PHA fund Lifeline, Northern Ireland’s crisis response helpline for people experiencing distress or despair or who are suicidal.  Victims of domestic abuse can avail of the service 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, every day of the year and provides compassionate, trauma-informed support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any financial support his Department provides to community-based and voluntary organisations that deliver bereavement services for children affected by suicide.
answer_text: The recently published Protect Life 2 Implementation Plan contains a specific action, 7.2, in relation to the provision of a consistent, compassionate approach to supporting those bereaved/ affected by suicide. The development of recommendations for a regionally consistent postvention service for adults and the development, implementation and evaluation of a regional postvention service for under 18 year olds, are included as deliverables under this action. Scoping for this service is at an advanced stage. I must stress that full delivery of all of the actions and deliverables in the Protect Life 2 Action and Implementation Plans will be subject to the necessary funding being secured.Funding for bereavement services is currently provided under Protect Life 2 as follows:Organisation Description Finance Barnardo’s NIChildren & Young People Bereavement by Suicide Support£347,616 for 12 months (which has been pro-rated to £260,712 for 9 months due to ongoing tendering process.) PIPS Hope & SupportPIPS - Bereavement Support Services  Range of services which includes the provision of counselling / art therapy sessions for children and young people. It is not possible to quantify the exact investment in children’s services. £45,882ExternFamily Support and Suicide Prevention Response Services & Crisis ResponseProvide a range of activities for individuals bereaved through suicide and includes 1:1 support for young people up to 25 years old.   £223,501LighthouseLighthouse Family Support ServicesProvides 114 hours of Art and Personal Development for children and young people bereaved through suicide. £3,990 Action Mental Health Community CounsellingProvides counselling to individuals bereaved by suicide which may include children and young people. It is not possible to quantify the exact investment in children’s services. £288,302The Public Health Agency also fund a small grant programme throughout Northern Ireland. As part of that programme four projects supporting children and families bereaved by suicide have been funded. Organisation Description Finance Youthlife InspiresProviding young people who have been bereaved with confidence building and inspiring projects£5,000Circle of Friends - Carers Support GroupProvision of creative programmes for individuals who have lost family members through suicide, substance use, road traffic accidents and family breakdown.£4,365Recharge CICThe provision of creative programmes for individuals bereaved through suicide or living with mental ill health.£5,000Cruse Bereavement SupportVolunteer Bereavement Training to increase bereavement support within the SEHSCT Area£4,473

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how (i) Emergency Departments; and (ii) other statutory agencies such as the PSNI, meet the needs of those in suicidal crisis.
answer_text: Implementing the new Regional Mental Health Crisis Service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. My Departmental officials and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are: accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. Future state work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. A draft Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Lifespan Pathway has been presented to relevant stakeholders. Stakeholders have provided feedback on the draft pathway and how best to take forward the next phase of work of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service model. A Mental Health Liaison and Crisis Response Home Treatment Care Network has recently been formed which includes all key stakeholders. This Care Network’s aim is to ensure consistent evidence-based practice, equitable access to care and improve service user outcomes.As an alternative to ED attendance two initiatives were piloted for individuals with mental health needs but not requiring acute physical care. Firstly, The Multi Agency Triage Teams (MATT); which is currently only operational in two Trust areas with PSNI being one of the stakeholders within MATT. Secondly,  The NI Ambulance Service pilot initiative Hear and Treat (SEHSCT area); which includes mental health professionals in their control rooms. These two services offer a suitable alternative to transporting patients with mental health issues to busy Emergency Departments. The Department needs to await the receipt of the evaluations to consider how best to proceed with these regionally.In order to develop and roll out the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, additional funding will be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s plans to establish a dedicated crisis service under the Mental Health Strategy 2021–2031.
answer_text: Implementing the new Regional Mental Health Crisis Service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. My Departmental officials and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are: accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. Future state work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. A draft Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Lifespan Pathway has been presented to relevant stakeholders. Stakeholders have provided feedback on the draft pathway and how best to take forward the next phase of work of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service model. A Mental Health Liaison and Crisis Response Home Treatment Care Network has recently been established which includes all key stakeholders. This Care Network’s aim is to ensure consistent evidence-based practice, equitable access to care and improve service user outcomes.In order to develop and roll out the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, additional funding will be required. My officials within the Department are working in partnership to prioritise areas for next steps. This will include standardisation of services and levelling up and roll out of service areas across the region, to ensure a more equitable provision to those in need across the lifespan.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission mental health crisis services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures in place to ensure families and children bereaved by suicide can access support, particularly in rural constituencies such as East Londonderry.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline how his Department is communicating the application process for the EU Treatment Scheme to patients and to healthcare providers who may be supporting them.
answer_text: The Department ensured to establish a central information hub as part of the Waiting List Scheme launch. The online information hub provides all necessary procedural guidance, parameters of the scheme and key performance indicators for application submission and approval processes. Timelines have been clearly articulated to help support applicants across the application journey with a reinforcement that this readily accessible information hub provides all necessary information.  This hub managed by a dedicated Scheme team who are available Monday to Friday from 10am to 4pm. A dedicated helpline has been set up to provide guidance on the process from start to finish, assisting with any and all ad hoc questions prospective applicants may have. Applicants can seek assistance through telephone, email and were appropriate face to face scheduled assistance. The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme was launched on 2 June 2025 with Phase one covering the Republic of Ireland. Phase two, launched 15 September 2025, extends access to the wider European Union. Through a well-established communications and engagement plan, details of the Scheme, both Phase one and Phase two launches, were communicated to both internal and external stakeholders inclusive of HSC Trusts, General Practitioners and other professional bodies.The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is a patient led Scheme. In choosing to arrange healthcare themselves, the patient is effectively stepping outside of the HSC system.  At this point, the patient is taking individual responsibility for ensuring that the service they obtain is appropriate and safe within the laws of ROI or EU (not under UK legislation). The Department of Health under this scheme will not be formally commissioning services from providers abroad and therefore will not be liable for the outcome of the treatment provided.The Waiting list Scheme is a reimbursement only scheme and one of many initiatives I have in place to tackle the overall waits within Northern Ireland. Discussions regarding alternative mechanisms to pay for treatment had been tabled, however, in the context of deliverability for patients and under requirement and readiness to go live by June 2025, a reimbursement scheme similar to that of previous Republic of Ireland reimbursement Scheme and the EU Cross Border Directive was agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of the EU Treatment Scheme on reducing elective surgery waiting lists.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the mechanisms his Department has explored to improve the affordability of the EU Treatment Scheme for patients who cannot meet the upfront costs, such as alternative reimbursement arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what assessment her Department has made of the link between police officer (i) sickness absence rates; (ii) recruitment and retention challenges; and (ii) public confidence in policing.
answer_text: The management of resources, including staffing, within the PSNI remains a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I respect the Chief Constable’s operational independence and the role of the Policing Board; therefore, it would not be appropriate for me to make an assessment of matters relating to police sick absence rates.  I am encouraged by the results of the recent Northern Ireland Safe Community Telephone Survey, published in July, that confidence in the police and police accountability arrangements remains high. I am committed to working to ensure that public confidence in policing is not eroded by issues relating to officer numbers and the additional pressure and responsibility that this places on fewer officers. I also believe that there is a role for us all to play in ensuring that confidence in policing is not damaged by unsubstantiated and negative rhetoric about the PSNI and wider justice system’s performance.  My department has regular engagement with the PSNI on these, and other issues and I will continue to work with the Chief Constable to ensure we have a police service that is properly resourced in all aspects for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how her Department is supporting the PSNI to address high sickness absence rates, alongside challenges in recruitment and retention.
answer_text: I have regular discussions with the Chief Constable regarding the pressures facing the PSNI, including the challenges in managing sick absence, and the pressure this creates in delivering effective policing for our communities. However, the management of staff and sickness absence is primarily the responsibility of the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Policing Board for such operational matters.I have confidence that it is a priority for the Chief Constable, and I support the efforts he is making to address the issues around absence management, including the development of an in-house mental health service for officers and staff and the introduction of a new absence management policy. I am also fully committed to supporting the PSNI in order to secure adequate funding for policing in Northern Ireland to support recruitment and retention and avoid discouragement among officers who continue to perform a difficult role in challenging circumstances. The PSNI Recovery Business Case has been approved on value for money grounds and I continue to engage regularly with the Department of Finance and wider Executive on the funding needed to deliver it in full. All parties around the Executive table have said that policing is a priority and it is vital that that commitment is matched by the funding the PSNI needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how her Department monitors the effectiveness of the PSNI’s occupational health and wellbeing provision, given the level of public funding provided.
answer_text: The provision of the PSNI’s Occupational Health and Wellbeing service is a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  It would not, therefore, be appropriate for me to monitor the effectiveness of this service.  The Department does, however, fund the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) to provide police officers and former police officers and their families with clinical and support services. This is funded from the grant in aid awarded to PRRT and the effectiveness of the work of the PRRT is monitored by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is addressing barriers to police officers accessing statutory mental health services, including stigma and delays in referral pathways.
answer_text: The Department of Health acknowledges the significant pressures faced by police officers and the potential impact on their mental health and well-being due to the nature of the work they undertake. The Department is committed therefore to ensuring timely and appropriate access to statutory mental health services when required.When any citizen requires mental health support there are a range of services which can provide assessment, formulation and intervention as appropriate to the presentation. These include Community Mental Health Teams, Psychological Therapy Services and the Regional Trauma Network Team in each Trust. These services are applicable to people with a potential diagnosis of Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD),Psychological services within Adult Mental Health HSCT services provide psychological therapies for all adults who require this service. Referrals to psychological therapy services are prioritised based on clinical risk and need. These services are skilled in delivering trauma informed interventions and specific psychological therapies appropriate to adults across a range of backgrounds and experience. The Regional Trauma Network, overseen jointly by my Department and The Executive Office, adopts an innovative approach working collaboratively with statutory, community and voluntary sector organisations to provide specialist trauma care to individuals.Phase 1 focuses on victims and survivors of the Troubles and formally commenced on 1 April 2023 for a period of at least 3 years.  Phase 1 is subject to evaluation to consider any unmet needs or ongoing needs, with lessons learned from the evaluation informing the roll out of Phase 2. The Phase 1 evaluation is scheduled to commence in April 2026. Community and Voluntary agencies also provide a wide range of interventions and support for individuals who experience trauma.There is currently limited data on the prevalence of PTSD in Northern Ireland. However, I am mindful that there may be unmet need in this area with people not presenting to services or being within services but not yet having received a diagnosis of PTSD.I remain committed to the continued integration of a trauma informed approach throughout my Department’s work and to ensure that people who require our services receive timely access and appropriate intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how her Department (i) ensures that the PSNI is adequately resourced to deliver statutory post-incident debriefs, following a death or serious injury after police contact; and (ii) works with the (a) Policing Board; and (b) the Police Ombudsman, to monitor delivery.
answer_text: The prioritisation of policing resources, including the delivery of statutory post-incident debriefs following a death or serious incident, is an operational matter for the PSNI which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the PSNI and the oversight role of Policing Board, it would not be appropriate for me to comment.  You may, therefore, wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what formal guidance exists for the Northern Ireland Prison Service on the introduction and care of therapy or emotional-support animals in custodial environments.
answer_text: The Prison Service takes its duty of care for animal safety and wellbeing very seriously. This was reinforced when the Governor of Magilligan invited Animal Welfare Officers from Mid & East Antrim Borough Council to carry out a full review of the care and welfare of an emotional support dog recently introduced at that establishment. These Officers were satisfied with every aspect of Bailey’s care and welfare.Given the variation of the prisons estate and those held in custody in each, I am not of the view that formal guidance should override the contextual, careful and professional consideration required and applied when animals are introduced and used in prisons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to consult with veterinary and animal-welfare organisations to develop standards for therapy or emotional-support animals.
answer_text: At present, I have no plans to introduce standards specifically for therapy or emotional support animals. The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 is a comprehensive piece of legislation that establishes minimum welfare requirements for all animals in every context. To date, I have not received any evidence indicating a necessity for distinct standards relating to therapy or emotional support dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what role local councils play in monitoring the welfare of therapy or emotional-support animals.
answer_text: Local councils are responsible for enforcing animal welfare legislation in relation to companion animals. They have full autonomy in determining how best to deliver the animal welfare service; any related queries should be directed to them. Animal welfare concerns should be reported promptly to the relevant agency for investigation and enforcement action as necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will engage with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to address the legislative gap in the recognition and regulation of therapy or emotional-support animals.
answer_text: The lead department for issues around animal regulation is the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA). I would encourage the Member to write to DAERA if she wishes to raise this matter.  My department follows all the appropriate guidance in relation to the welfare and well-being of animals in our care and will continue to prioritise that.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what assessment her Department has made of the risks associated with introducing animals into custodial environments, including potential links between animal abuse and wider patterns of violence.
answer_text: The use of animals by the Northern Ireland Prison Service is an operational matter for the relevant Governor in each prison establishment. I am content that Governors have a thorough understanding of the risks that can present in custodial environments, and how they should be managed in respect of the use of animals.It is worth noting that emotional support animals have been deployed in prison settings in Northern Ireland for the last 25 years, providing a wide range of proven benefits in relation to the wellbeing and rehabilitation of offenders. The Prison Service takes its duty of care for animals’ safety and wellbeing very seriously and has also an established record of working with a range of animal charities to offer a caring home and a purposeful life to many animals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total length of time that traffic management measures have been in place in Ballykelly, as a result of roadworks, in the last three years.
answer_text: Over the past three years there have been approximately 230 days of restricted hours lane closures on the A2 Clooney Road in Ballykelly and Walworth.In addition, there have been 10 days of full night-time carriageway closure with a diversion route for carriageway resurfacing. The majority of the works are attributable to my department and involve investment of over £3m in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has carried out an updated traffic flow assessment for Ballykelly, since the preferred A2 bypass route was first announced in 2010.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which includes the A2 at Ballykelly.A preferred route for Ballykelly Bypass was announced in 2010 and since then no further development work has been undertaken on the project. If the A2 Ballykelly scheme is prioritised within the Transport Plan, this route would then be reassessed as part of the plan for scheme delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any engagement she intends to have with residents and businesses in Ballykelly, as part of the development of the (i) Transport Strategy 2035; and (ii) Transport Plan.
answer_text: The draft Transport Strategy was launched for public consultation on 24 June 2025 and is currently open for public consultation. The public consultation is an important first step and officials welcome feedback during this consultation period to help shape a transport system that works for all and safeguards the environment for future generations. Public engagement on the vision and objectives of the North West Transport Plan was carried out from 27 August 2024 to 5 November 2024. Further engagement will be carried out during the development of the Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the timetable for the publication of the new Transport Strategy 2035.
answer_text: The draft Transport Strategy has been launched for public consultation.This Strategy is the beginning of a bold and necessary journey to ensure that our transportation system meets the demands of tomorrow, while reflecting the values and priorities of our community today.The public consultation is an important first step and feedback is welcomed during this consultation period to help shape a transport system that works for all and safeguards the environment for future generations. All responses will be reviewed alongside all other consultations before the Transport Strategy is finalised.  Publication will therefore be dictated by the level and scale of engagement responses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has undertaken, or plans to undertake, an assessment of the impact of traffic congestion in Ballykelly on local economic development.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely assess the impact of traffic congestion on economic development. My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which will include the strategic network at Ballykelly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any legislative proposals currently proceeding following the Sentencing Policy Review in Northern Ireland announced in 2016.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 11 - AQO 2366/22-27		  ANSWERWith your permission Mr Speaker I will answer Questions 11 and 14 together.    The Sentencing Bill will deliver on a number of the recommendations arising from my Department’s review of sentencing policy which commenced in 2016, the findings of which were subject to public consultation in 2019.    This will include: a statement of the principles and purposes of sentencing;  provision to allow further use of community community-based sanctions in sentencing;  statutory starting points for life sentence tariff calculation;  expansion and simplification of the current unduly lenient sentence referral provisions;  creation of a new offence of assaulting those providing a service to the public or performing a public duty;  creation of a statutory aggravator for vulnerable victims; and increasing maximum penalties for offences causing death by driving. The Bill will also include measures to encourage the disclosure of the location of victims’ remains, to be known as “Charlotte’s Law”, and provision for a statutory aggravator for hate crimes. I received Executive approval to the proposed content of my Sentencing Bill on the 19 December 2024. It is intended that the Bill will be introduced to the Assembly before the end of 2025. In relation to cases of death by dangerous driving, the Bill will ensure that disqualification from driving is served after release from custody.    The grant or refusal of bail when a person is awaiting trial should always be based on the facts of the case. Any decision to refuse bail should only be taken where this can be justified under domestic legislation and in compliance with the European Convention on Human Rights.    Any decisions on bail or remand into custody in individual cases are, therefore, matters for the independent courts and judiciary and are not matters that involve me or my Department.    There are no current plans to include new guidance on the granting of bail in the forthcoming Sentencing Bill; however, it is a matter which I intend to keep under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of sexual offence cases, currently in the court system, which have been delayed for more than two years; and (ii) the longest current delay.
answer_text: At 1 September 2025, there were 361 live cases at court stage in the justice process, where the main offence was a sexual offence.  Of these, in 216 cases, it had been over 2 years since the offence was initially reported, and, in the case with the longest period since the offence was reported, it had been 21 years 8 months.In 48 cases, it had been over 2 years since the first court date after PPS decision to prosecute, and, in the case with the longest period since the first court date after PPS decision to prosecute, it had been 12 years 9 days.Note:1. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, NCA, Harbour, Airport police and other Governmental bodies in Northern Ireland.2. Figures include cases at Crown Court and at magistrates’ courts.3. Figures relate to cases where the main offence is a sexual offence.4. Cases in the court system has been interpreted as cases which have had a first court date after PPS decision to prosecute but which have not yet been completed.5. Figures are derived from management information systems.It is important these figures are seen in context.  For example, for these cases, the median time from date offence was reported was 2 years 4 months.  Similarly, the median time since the first court date after PPS decision to prosecute was 7 months 10 days. There may be a number of reasons that a case may have been unable to be completed, such as where the defendant has health related issues or where a defendant is already in custody in another jurisdiction.  The Department of Justice is working with criminal justice partners to deliver a Speeding Up Justice programme, which seeks to reform the handling of cases, getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, and dealing with cases effectively and proportionately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will consider (i) piloting specialist or fast-track sexual violence courts in Northern Ireland; and (ii) assessing their potential to reduce the current median of 630 days of processing time, including the effectiveness of similar courts in England.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the ongoing Specialist Sexual Violence Court pilot in England and will consider what, if any lessons there are for Northern Ireland when the evaluation is published.  The listing and scheduling of court business is a matter for the independent judiciary; however, my Department is currently working with the judiciary and criminal justice stakeholders to support and further develop the voluntary protocol to expedite sexual offence cases involving young complainants and will continue to support such positive initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the action her Department is taking to address the median 630 days for processing sexual offence cases in Northern Ireland courts.
answer_text: The timely progression of criminal cases is central to the effective delivery of justice and delays can have significant consequences for victims, defendants, and for public confidence in the system. While efforts are ongoing to reduce avoidable delay, some types of cases can take significantly longer to conclude.Sexual offence cases due to their serious nature often tend to be prosecuted in the Crown Court which generally involves a longer process. Whilst the most recent available data demonstrates these cases are currently take longer to complete than any other offence category, there has been a gradual improvement in case processing times. Median durations have reduced from 757 days in 2022–23, to 675 in 2023–24, and now 630 in 2024–25, which is a reduction of nearly 17% over three years. While this progress is encouraging, I recognise there is much more to do. It is important to understand the underlying reasons why these cases can take longer to conclude. They are among the most complex cases with several factors contributing to the longer case durations observed:They include significant volumes of evidence, including growing use of digital evidence. Where they are historical offences, the time between the alleged incident and its reporting can create additional challenges in building the case, securing corroborating evidence, and supporting victims and witnesses through the process.Serious sexual offences are rightly subject to rigorous process and therefore are predominantly heard in the Crown Court - 57.1% of cases were disposed there in 2023–24. This reflects both the seriousness and complexity of these cases and includes additional procedural stages such as committal from the magistrates’ courts. These steps contribute to the overall case duration. In contrast, other offence types are more likely to be prosecuted in the magistrates’ courts. Therefore, while sexual offence cases may appear to take longer to complete, comparisons between across offence types can be misleading without accounting for the level of court in which they are heard. It is more appropriate to assess trends over time within an offence category.My Department and justice colleagues are currently engaged in a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay across the system. This work is driven by the Criminal Justice Board and takes forward existing work on Committal Reform, existing and emerging work in the digital arena and explores other areas for efficiencies such as Early Engagement between key stakeholders to help drive case progression, increased sentencing powers for magistrates’ courts and expanding the use of Out of Court Disposals. My Department has also recently been successful in a bid to secure Transformation funding which will help to drive forward work in relation to Early Engagement and Out of Court Disposals.Taken together, these reforms aim to rebalance the justice system, ensuring a more proportionate approach to offending, and freeing up c

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the recommendations in the Gillen Review Report into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland, regarding improving the handling of serious sexual offences.
answer_text: While there remains work to be done, good progress has been made towards the implementation of the Gillen Review recommendations. Implementation of the is complex and cannot be achieved by DOJ alone as it requires a collaborative and partnership approach to achieve the transformational change required.I am pleased to report that 174 (69%) of the original 253 Gillen Review recommendations are assessed as complete and a further 25 (10%) are partially completed, meaning that while provision is in place, further work will be required to mainstream the service. However, I do not believe that simply reporting on the number of completed/in progress recommendations conveys the true scale of the impact of this progress in terms of outcomes for victims.My approach has been to prioritise recommendations that deliver real and meaningful benefits for victims of sexual crime, including:Access to free legal advice for all victims of serious sexual offences, through the SOLA and Children’s SOLA schemes. The adult service has been in place since 1 April 2021 and has provided advice and support to over 2,000 victims of sexual crime. The Children’s SOLA (CSOLA) service launched on 5 February 2025, and has assisted over 150 young victims and their families. Feedback on both schemes continues to be overwhelmingly positive with many service users reporting that it has increased their confidence and understanding of the justice system, and importantly, ha encouraged them to remain engaged within the justice system when they might otherwise have withdrawn. Remote Evidence Centres (RECs) allow vulnerable witnesses to give evidence directly to the court from a secure and comfortable facility, away from the courthouse. Importantly, attending the REC ensures that the witness will not encounter the defendant or the defendant’s family or supporters in or around the court building on the day of the trial. Two new RECs have been established in Belfast and Craigavon, in addition to the existing NSPCC-led facility in Derry ~ Londonderry. The outcomes are encouraging:there was an increase of over 600% in REC trial bookings in 2024 compared to 2023;this increase in demand has continued in 2025 with a further 90% increase in bookings so far this calendar year compared to 2024;and, in 2024, 344 witnesses (199 adults and 145 children) attended a REC to give evidence with that number set to reach around 500 in 2025. The Gillen Review anticipated that RECs would enable victims to give their best evidence and increase their confidence in the justice system. This is supported by conviction rates for REC cases that, so far this year, stand at 90%, and for the whole of 2024 was 92%. Over half of these convictions have been by guilty plea, meaning that the victim has not had to go through the daunting experience of having to provide any oral evidence at the trial. An evaluation of the RECs is to be taken forward and will help to inform  recommendations to roll out wider access to REC facilit

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the steps her Department is taking to improve communication and support for victims of sexual offences during lengthy court delays.
answer_text: I recognise the unique challenges facing all victims of crime, but particularly victims of sexual offences, as a result of lengthy and complex court processes. I recognise that it is important that victims are provided with appropriate and timely information, advice and support to help minimise these impacts.  The Victim Charter sets out the entitlements and services that victims of crime in Northern Ireland can expect to receive from criminal justice organisations. This includes their right to receive updates on their case. The Charter also provides information about support services available, and what to do should anyone feel their Charter rights are not being upheld. The planned Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill includes a provision requiring criminal justice organisations to demonstrate compliance with Victim Charter entitlements, which I am confident will help to drive transparency and best practice across the criminal justice system.In addition, my Department funds specialist advocacy services for high-risk victims and survivors of domestic and sexual abuse, which are delivered by ASSIST NI. This service helps victims to engage with statutory and voluntary services and in doing so, helps to ensure victims receive regular updates on the status of their case, which is so important during lengthy investigations or court delays.  The advocates will help explain processes, anticipated timeframes, and any changes to trial schedules in clear, trauma-informed language. Victims are assigned a dedicated support worker who acts as a single, trusted point of contact.  This continuity builds trust, reduces re-traumatisation, and prevents victims from having to repeatedly share their story.My Department also funds Victim Support Northern Ireland (VSNI) to provide a range of support services to all victims, including those who have experienced sexual crime. This includes the provision of emotional support, information, advice, assistance with claiming Criminal Injuries Compensation, court support services and signposting to other specialist services. VSNI’s Witness Service helps witnesses to prepare for court, and by accompanying witnesses as they give evidence can provide reassurance in advance of and during the trial process. The service ensures that victims feel supported, listened to, and confident in navigating the criminal justice system, even when proceedings are prolonged.My Department also funds the NSPCC’s Young Witness Service, which provides support and reassurance to young people, children and their families, including those who have experienced sexual offences.The Victim and Witness Care Unit (VWCU) within PPS also provides victims of crime with a caseworker from the point that an investigation or charge file is submitted to the PPS, through to and including the outcome of any court proceedings. VWCU Case Officers keep victims updated on their case as well as referring victims for specific supports provided by other organisations.Collecti

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients red flagged for breast cancer assessment who have waited longer than the 14-day target in each of the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The numbers of patients waiting more than 14 days following urgent breast cancer referrals, by Health and Social Care Trust, are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Times’ statistics bulletin. The most recent figures are included on the ‘14 Day Wait - Breast Cancer’ worksheet of the associated ‘Cancer Waiting Times Statistics by HSC Trust and Tumour Site January – March 2025’ Excel file on the Department’s website at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesPlease note Red Flag breast assessments are equivalent to urgent breast cancer referrals.I acknowledge that the current waiting times for red flag breast cancer are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average waiting times for red flag gynaecology referrals across all Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) what action is being taken to bring these within clinically-safe timescales.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) what measures are in place to ensure patients with urgent diagnostic needs, including MRI and CT scans, are seen consistently across all Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) whether his Department has identified reasons for disparity in waiting times between the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The recently published Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan sets out clear proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards, and how we will transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in the future.As part of the Implementation and Funding Plan, £12m has been secured to expand diagnostic imaging capacity across the region.  This includes increasing capacity for CT, MRI and ultrasound.  The current demand and capacity gaps in each Trust have been taken into consideration during this exercise.  Newly commissioned CT and MRI scanners have also been funded at both the Southern Trust and Northern Trust’s Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDCs) and are being utilised to image patients from across the region.  Since becoming operational in 2025/26, the RDC’s CT capacity has already demonstrated a significant reduction in Trust variation. Weekly monitoring is in place to ensure all RDC capacity is currently utilised for the longest regional waits. The RDC MRI scanners are anticipated to be operational in the next few months.The reasons contributing to disparity in imaging waiting times are multifactorial and vary across each Trust. The reasons include:Historical funding gaps;Diagnostic radiographer workforce shortages;Consultant radiologist workforce shortages;Capital equipment;Demand variation;Complexity of workload.My Department continues to work to increase imaging capacity, and a bid has been submitted for significant investment in regional imaging capital equipment. If successful, this will facilitate expansion of the imaging equipment base across all Trusts.  However expansion will be subject to funding approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he will introduce transparent performance reporting for red flag referrals across all specialities; and (ii) how his Department plans to rebuild public confidence in urgent referral pathways.
answer_text: Cancer waiting time targets in Northern Ireland aim for at least 98% of patients to start treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat, and at least 95% to start within 62 days of an urgent GP referral. Additionally, all urgent breast cancer referrals should be seen within 14 days. Current reporting on performance falls in line with these targets and is provided on a quarterly basis, available at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-times.Whilst actions 13 and 14 of the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032 refer to the introduction of a 28-day standard for suspected cancer and a review of current cancer targets respectively, there are no immediate plans to review cancer targets or performance reporting at this time. Reducing waiting lists remains a key priority for me and my Department.  The plan to tackle our elective care waiting lists is detailed in the Elective Care Framework that was updated and published again in May last year, available at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-05/doh-elective-care-framework-funding-plan-may-2025.PDF.  My Department continues to undertake work to rebuild public confidence in referral pathways and to tackle the unacceptable backlog of patients waiting. This has included the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and mega clinics, which have collectively delivered tangible improvements to the waiting list position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what actions are being taken to address workforce shortages in diagnostic specialities, including radiology; and (ii) how this is being factored into long-term workforce planning.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for ensuring sufficient staffing to provide those services commissioned from them by my Department.
