As employers they carry out day to day, operational workforce planning, including vacancy management, recruitment and retention.Long term, strategic workforce planning is led by my Department, in partnership with relevant stakeholders in health and social care.  Diagnostic professions are included in both operational and strategic workforce planning.Regarding pathology, the Northern Ireland Regional Strategic Workforce Review in Pathology Services was published February 2025.  Its aim is to provide a high-level picture of the current workforce and consider future requirements.  Implementation of the Review’s 12 recommendations will depend on a number of factors, including availability of resources.  A Regional Implementation Board has been established and tasked with prioritising these recommendations going forward.In terms of clinical radiology, my Department has responded to increasing demand by expanding commissioned medical specialty training places from 37 (2015/16) to 55 places (2025/26) – a 49% increase. Diagnostic radiographers are also a key part of the imaging workforce.  There has been progress in the last few years in securing additional funding to expand the number of diagnostic radiography training places within Ulster University, from 48 (2017/18) to 94 places – a 96% increase.  This was secured following a workforce gap analysis, which included current and future predicted requirements for diagnostic radiography within the region. Nevertheless, the imaging workforce in Northern Ireland continues to be constrained by insufficient numbers.  A costed workforce plan for imaging services has been developed, which outlines requirements to bring training within imaging teams up to date over three to five years.  However, currently this remains unfunded within the context of the known deficiencies in my budgetary allocation.Plans are underway for a multi-professional regional Imaging Academy.  This will significantly increase HSC imaging capacity, by providing a continuous supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.  While this is a priority area for the Department, progress remains subject to business case approval and the identification of additional recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what assessment her Department has made of the investment required to upgrade wastewater treatment infrastructure, to reduce nutrient discharges into rivers and coastal waters in East Londonderry.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:During each Price Control, NI Water works closely with the environmental regulator, the NIEA, to agree the priorities for wastewater investment, to deliver water quality improvements, which includes consideration of any capital works upgrades to meet nutrient standards, such as ammonia and phosphorus limits.NI Water does not record its information by constituency.  However, it has identified and set out below, the relevant wastewater schemes in the East Derry area which were identified for investment in the Price Control 21 Final Determination, subject to the availability of funding. Relevant Wastewater Schemes in the East Derry areaAghanloo Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTW) Ballykelly WwTW Dunloy WwTW Donnybrewer Drainage Area (DA) Eglinton Cottage Way Wastewater Pumping Station (WwPS) Kilrea WwTW Limavady Catchment Upgrades Limavady DA - Bovally WwPS Limavady DA UID-Ballyclose Street CSO North Coast DA Ballycairn CSO CAI Playing Fields North Coast DA Strand Road WwPS Rossbay & Caw Park, Storm Sewer Extension West Area Essential Base Maintenance and Optimisation Year 5 and 6For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department is working with other agencies to ensure wastewater and drainage systems do not contribute to nutrient build-up, leading to algal blooms affecting the River Bann and north coast beaches.
answer_text: In July 2024, the DAERA Lough Neagh Report and associated Action Plan was published and endorsed by the Executive, in response to the widespread levels of blue green algae in Lough Neagh. The River Bann flows through Lough Neagh and any consequential impact from blue green algae in Lough Neagh can manifest on the North Coast beaches.To oversee the delivery of the actions in the Report,  DAERA established the Lough Neagh Stakeholder forum in December 2024 and my Department continues to participate in this forum, alongside NI Water.An action in the Report relates to the upgrade of wastewater treatment works around Lough Neagh and the investment needed to deliver this work has already been identified, as part of the Price Control process and is subject to budget availability.	Nutrient levels are reduced through the wastewater treatment processes.  NI Water operates wastewater treatment works in accordance with consent discharge standards, set by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA), undertaking an accredited programme of sampling, testing and reporting of wastewater quality to NIEA.  During the bathing water season, NI Water also operates a protocol with DAERA for the immediate investigation of high bacterial levels detected in bathing water, to assist with the management of bathing water quality. Furthermore, for each Price Control, NI Water works closely with the environmental regulator, NIEA, to agree the priorities for wastewater investment to deliver water quality improvements, which includes consideration of any capital works upgrades to meet nutrient standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what cross-departmental arrangements are in place to ensure a joined-up response to algal blooms impacting East Londonderry; and (ii) what actions have been taken, or plan to be taken, to limit future algal blooms.
answer_text: The Interagency Blue Green Algae Monitoring Protocol has been developed and is being implemented operationally for the 2024 and 2025 seasons. My Department’s Interagency Protocol groups are currently established and are responding to reports of blue-green algae. Guidance is available in the Interagency Protocol which can be accessed at: www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/inter-agency-blue-green-algae-monitoring-protocolThe Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan, agreed by the Executive in July 2024, contains science led actions to tackle the blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland. Of the 37 actions included in the plan, 14 have been completed, 22 are currently underway, and one is pending due to its reliance on another action being implemented.I remain fully committed to driving forward implementation of the Action Plan, working closely with other Departments, particularly the Department of Infrastructure and the Department of Justice, where their input is required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what assessment her Department has made of the impact on (i) tourism; and (ii) hospitality businesses in East Londonderry, following recent beach closures such as at Benone Strand, due to toxic algal blooms.
answer_text: Through continued engagement with both businesses and with DAERA officials, Tourism NI is aware of the challenges presented by the recent algal blooms to tourism and hospitality businesses in the affected areas. It will remain a challenge and Tourism NI officials will continue to engage with DAERA colleagues on clear communications and information for visitors and potential mitigating actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what consideration his Department has given to introducing new incentive schemes for farmers in high-risk catchment areas to support locally-appropriate nature-based solutions, such as constructed wetlands or riparian planting, to improve water quality while sustaining family farms.
answer_text: Protecting and restoring our natural environment is one of my key priorities and my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) is incentivising enabling actions to support the scaling up of nature friendly farming, delivering environmental public goods including improvements in water quality, alongside sustainable food production.Examples of the schemes within SAP that will provide support for nature-based farming include: the Farming with Nature Package, the Farm Sustainability Payment, Farming for Sustainability - Knowledge Transfer, the Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, and the Protein Crop Scheme.The first phase of the Farming with Nature Package, the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, launched in June 2025 to increase the extent of habitats on agricultural land by creating new habitats, protecting watercourses, providing green infrastructure for nature corridors and increasing carbon sequestration.Five environmental actions were offered: planting new hedgerows, creating riparian buffer strips, farmland tree planting, retention of winter stubble and establishing multi-species winter cover crop.  Supporting items available included protective fencing, gates and drinking water troughs. Other elements of the Farming with Nature Package, focusing on support for Designated Areas, Priority Habitats and Landscapes Projects are currently being progressed.The Sustainable Catchment Programme (SCP) currently operates in five river catchments and aims to reduce agricultural pressures on water quality in these areas. The SCP is delivered by my Department in partnership with the Rivers Trust. It invites farmers to work with Rivers Trust advisors, to ensure the right measures are implemented in the right places on each individual farm to maximise both water quality and farm benefits. Interventions funded include nature based solutions such as creating riparian buffer zones and strategic tree and hedge planting which helps combat nutrient runoff, aid carbon sequestration, and enhance biodiversity. The expansion of the SCP into other catchments is currently being scoped as an action under the Lough Neagh Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many school-age children live in households receiving Universal Credit, but do not currently qualify for free school meals.
answer_text: The department does not hold information on households receiving or not receiving Universal Credit. The Department collects pupil-level data on free school meal entitlement (FSME) through the annual School Census. These pupils have been confirmed as ‘entitled’ following completion of the Education Authority’s FSM application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures he is considering to protect Personal Independence Payment claimants affected by the proposed changes to daily living criteria.
answer_text: During the second reading of the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill in Parliament, the UK Government indicated that any future changes to the eligibility conditions for Personal Independence Payment will now be subject to the outcome of a review in relation to the assessment criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures are in place to support pupils who experience food insecurity, but do not meet the current eligibility threshold, for free school meals.
answer_text: The Department continues to provide nutritious free school meals to children entitled to these meals during the school term to ensure families most in need get the support they require.Families who do not meet the eligibility criteria are encouraged to contact the “Make the Call Service” to check if they are receiving all the benefits, services and supports that they are entitled to. Details of this service can be found at www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department intends to raise the upper age limit for eligibility for Disability Living Allowance for children from 16 to 18.
answer_text: This was a welfare reform proposal that was included in the UK Government’s recent Green Paper consultation exercise which closed on 30 June 2025.  No decisions have yet been taken in relation to potential changes to the upper age limit in Disability Living Allowance for children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to introduce formal targets to reduce stillbirth, neonatal death, and extended perinatal mortality in line with other UK regions.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to introduce formal targets to reduce stillbirth, neonatal death, and extended perinatal mortality in NI.As you will be aware, there is a focus via the adaption of the Savings Babies Lives care bundles version 3 developed by NHS England, to reduce stillbirths and neonatal deaths by improving the health of pregnant women and identifying areas of care where there is evidence or best practice aimed at reducing perinatal mortality. The care bundle consists of 5 elements:Element 1: smoking cessationElement 2: fetal growth restrictionElement 3: reduced fetal movementsElement 4: fetal monitoring Element 5: prevention of preterm birth. All stillbirth, neonatal death, and extended perinatal mortality rates in NI continue to be reported via the MBRRACE- UK perinatal programme. The programme conducts national surveillance of all eligible late fetal losses, stillbirths and neonatal deaths in the UK. It also conducts confidential enquiries on selected topics related to perinatal mortality and serious infant morbidity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what emotional and psychological support services are available across Health and Social Care Trusts for individuals and couples who experience miscarriage.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:- Bereavement Midwives: Specially trained midwives are available in many Trusts to provide compassionate, one-to-one support following miscarriage or baby loss.- Counselling Services: Trusts offer access to psychological therapies, either directly or through referral, to support individuals experiencing grief, trauma, or mental health challenges following pregnancy loss.- Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to external organisations such as the Miscarriage Association, SANDS, and Cruse Bereavement Support, which offer specialised resources and peer support.- Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs): These units often serve as the first point of contact for women experiencing miscarriage and can provide immediate emotional support and follow-up care.- Information and Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.In addition, my Department is actively supporting the development of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for Northern Ireland. This initiative, currently under public consultation, aims to offer formal recognition of pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, acknowledging the emotional significance of such losses.Furthermore, my Department is considering options to progress with the establishment of a Maternity and Neonatal Partnership, which will oversee the implementation of a regional action plan to improve maternity care, including bereavement support. This work is informed by the recent independent review of midwifery services and reflects our commitment to ensuring that all women and families receive safe, respectful, and compassionate care.We remain committed to listening to those with lived experience and to ensuring that support services are accessible, equitable, and responsive to the needs of all who experience pregnancy loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will introduce miscarriage registration to improve data collection and inform future policies.
answer_text: There is currently no legal provision for the registration of miscarriage, which is defined as the loss of a pregnancy before 24 completed weeks of gestation. Legislation underpinning the requirements around registration falls under the remit of the Department of Finance (DoF).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what fertility treatments are currently publicly-funded in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how access and provision compare with those available in other UK regions.
answer_text: My Department commissions fertility services from the Regional Fertility Centre in Belfast Trust for the population of Northern Ireland.  Fertility services within local Health and Social Care Trusts are provided as part of the gynaecology service for women rather than commissioned separately and are dependent on skilled staff availability and capacity, in particular, consultants with a specialist interest in fertility services.Patients accessing local fertility services are assessed on a case by case basis, the next stage in the treatment pathway is dependent on multiple factors such as whether or not there is a need for surgical intervention/investigations, onward referral to the Regional Fertility Centre in Belfast HSC Trust, or if the patient is deemed suitable to commence ovulation induction treatment (if offered in the local Trust).    I outline below fertility treatments which are available in each Trust:BHSCTFertility services to local population Baseline investigations are carried outSemen analysisRegional Fertility Centre (tertiary referrals) providing treatments including 1 full IVF cycle; ICSI; Donor IUI; Fertility preservation (oncology and gender reassignment)NHSCTNo dedicated fertility service / do not offer fertility treatmentsBaseline investigations are carried outSETConsultant clinicBaseline investigations are carried outTreatments include ovulation inductionSHSCTConsultant triage / Nurse led fertility clinicBaseline investigations are carried outTreatments include ovulation induction WHSCTFertility/ reproductive outpatient clinic provision (part time Consultant / nurse) Baseline investigations are carried outTreatments include Ovulation induction(ii) How access and provision compare with those available in other UK regionsAs can be seen in the detail above, there is variation in the provision of fertility treatment in HSC Trusts.  My Department is actively overseeing gynaecology service improvement and pathway development at both a local and regional level as outlined in the recommendations arising from the recent Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology.  As part of this remit my Department are actively working with Trusts and General Practice to consider the current fertility services pathways from general practice through to secondary and tertiary care.  My Department will seek to ensure that pathways to the Regional Fertility Centre are timely and appropriate, given the time-sensitive nature of this service and that there is equity of access for women from across the region.I understand that there are varying levels of provision across other regions the UK which have endorsed the NICE CG156 or equivalent guidelines which are primarily due to issues of capacity and affordability. My Department is working closely with Belfast Trust to explore the necessary additional capacity and funding within the Regional Fertility Centre to deliver on the commitment to providing up to three IVF cycles (in line with NICE C

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what assessment has been made of progress in reducing stillbirth and neonatal mortality rates since 2010; and (ii) how these outcomes compare with other UK regions.
answer_text: Along with the other UK administrations, the Department funds the Health Care Quality Improvement Partnership (HQIP) to commission the Clinical Outcome Review Programmes (CORP), which includes the Maternal, Newborn and Infant Programme provided by MBRRACE-UK (Mothers and Babies: Reducing Risk through Audits and Confidential Enquiries across the UK). The aim of the programme is to provide robust information to support the delivery of safe, equitable, high quality, patient-centred maternal, newborn and infant health services. MBRRACE-UK achieves this by:Surveillance of all maternal deathsConfidential enquiries into maternal deaths during and up to one year after the end of the pregnancyConfidential enquiries into cases of serious maternal morbidity on a rolling basisSurveillance of perinatal deaths including late fetal losses (22-23 weeks gestation), stillbirths and neonatal deaths Confidential enquiries into stillbirths, infant deaths and cases of serious infant morbidity on a rolling basisThe most recent MBRRACE-UK report is published at this link https://timms.le.ac.uk/mbrrace-uk-perinatal-mortality/surveillance/#perinatal-mortality-in-the-uk Figure 1 in the report shows the Stillbirth, neonatal and extended perinatal mortality rates for the UK and by country of residence, 2013 to 2023.  Extended perinatal mortality rates decreased across the UK in 2023 (UK extended perinatal mortality rate: 4.84 per 1,000 total births). The long-term reduction in perinatal mortality is driven largely by a reduction in stillbirths.Compared with rates in 2022, stillbirth rates per 1,000 total births in 2023 were lower across the UK: 3.22 (UK); 3.27 (England); 2.95 (Scotland); 3.32 (Wales); and 2.51 (Northern Ireland).There were decreases in the neonatal mortality rate per 1,000 live births in England, Wales and Northern Ireland compared with 2022: 1.63 (UK); 1.62 (England); 1.61 (Scotland); 1.79 (Wales); and 1.66 (Northern Ireland).As would be expected, the highest numbers of deaths are associated with babies born in the regional maternity unit in Belfast Trust. Like elsewhere in the UK, in Northern Ireland the mothers of the smallest and sickest babies are normally transferred to the regional neonatal intensive care unit at the Royal Jubilee Maternity Hospital (RJMH) Belfast. On most occasions the transfer will take place antenatally with the result that baby is both born and dies in RJMH.   The largest burden of neonatal mortality is therefore associated with tertiary units.Implementation of guidance on assessment of signs of life before 24 weeks is continuing to ensure that there is consistency in reporting of neonatal deaths.Training and funding (£25K recurrent funding per HSC Trust in Northern Ireland) has been provided to support Trusts to develop skills and embed processes for timely multidisciplinary review of all perinatal deaths using a standardised tool (PMRT). A process being established to facilitate PMRT reporting at regional level via the Public He

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what policies and procedures are in place to hold boards of governors to account when a member is found to have acted unlawfully or engaged in misconduct in a way that could undermine equality or inclusion within the school community.
answer_text: All governors, once appointed, are a member of the body corporate; decisions are made collectively either by the board or by a committee thereof in accordance with the school’s scheme of management. As a corporate body, the Board of Governors is legally liable for all decisions and actions taken in its name by a governor (and the Principal) or committees to which it has delegated functions.  Governors are expected to work together to fulfil the Board of Governors statutory functions in relation to the school. The Board of Governors is accountable for ensuring that its decisions support the best interests of the school and its pupils. It must also ensure that its decisions are consistent with the principles of corporate and collective responsibility as per Chapter 2 para 2.36 and 2.37 of the Governors Guide, and the ‘Policies, Schemes and Procedures required by schools’ which are listed within Appendix 3 of the guide.Responsibility for taking decisions on school-based matters (including complaints) normally rests with the Board of Governors, unless there has been a failure on the part of the Board of Governors to comply with some requirement in education legislation. Investigating and responding to complaints about schools is the responsibility of each school’s governing body.If the complaints procedure has been exhausted, there is a right to refer the matter to the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman. The Ombudsman will, however, only consider a case once the school’s internal complaints process has been fully exhausted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) what mechanisms are in place to ensure that Business Improvement Districts are accountable to all businesses within their area, particularly small and independent traders; and (ii) what recourse is available to businesses that feel their interests are not being represented.
answer_text: Individual Business Improvement District Management Boards have their own avenues for complaints and feedback and Levy payers have opportunities to scrutinise the performance of the Board.  Council officials can also be asked to mediate for more serious complaints.  A business owner can also vote for the renewal of a Business Improvement District’s term of office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what guidance his Department provides to local councils to ensure that Business Improvement District proposals and operations are inclusive of the full business community.
answer_text: The Department does not provide specific guidance to BIDs but they are governed by the Business Improvement Districts Act (Northern Ireland) 2013, the Business Improvement Districts (General) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2014 and The Business Improvement Districts (Miscellaneous) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2014.BIDs should be controlled and managed by local businesses that are paying the levy. Most BIDs are delivered through Companies Limited by Guarantee with Directors elected from the BID levy payers. This organisation will be responsible for the delivery of the BID projects and directly responsible to all its business membership through an elected board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether businesses in financial hardship can apply for relief from Business Improvement District levies.
answer_text: Businesses experiencing financial hardship cannot apply for relief from Business Improvement District levies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what role his Department plays in the administration or oversight of Business Improvement District levies.
answer_text: The administration and oversight of Business Improvement District levies is undertaken by the relevant Business Improvement District board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider a pilot programme of farmer-led nutrient management approaches ahead of finalising the Nutrients Action Programme 2026–2029.
answer_text: I am keen to hear all views on how best to finalise the proposals for the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2026–2029. It is essential that we work together to move forward with improving water quality. I welcome constructive input from stakeholders, so that I can consider how we can collaboratively reverse the decline in water quality. I would encourage anyone with an interest in the NAP to provide their feedback through the consultation process. If they have any alternative suggestions to the proposed measures, these can be submitted along with supporting evidence.One of the proposals included within the consultation is for a pilot focused approach, in specific high-risk areas with focused measures. The consultation responses on this proposal will help inform how this focused approach is taken forward.  As I stated in the Assembly on 10 June the NAP proposals are just that, no final decisions have been made. The consultation process and further stakeholder engagement will feed into any final decisions on the NAP for 2026-2029.  I am listening to the genuine concerns and my officials and I will continue to engage with stakeholders during the consultation period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will consider establishing an independent tribunal or disputes resolution service for leaseholders to challenge service charges without needing to go through the courts.
answer_text: The detailed scoping report to which I referred in the answer to AQW 28755 contains an assessment of leaseholder protections in these jurisdictions. Those differences will form part of the consideration of any future policy development on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to review the aspects of the Private Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 which address the obligations of landlords and tenants.
answer_text: I have no plans to review the Private Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 during the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has assessed how leaseholder protections compare with those in England, Wales and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The detailed scoping report to which I referred in the answer to AQW 28755 contains an assessment of leaseholder protections in these jurisdictions. Those differences will form part of the consideration of any future policy development on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) support; and (ii) hardship measures, which are available for leaseholders who are unable to pay high service charges issued by the Housing Executive for major works.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 28756/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail: (i) any performance standards or timeframes which are in place to regulate how long major works should take in leasehold buildings; and (ii) how leaseholders can raise concerns about delays.
answer_text: There are no specific statutory performance standards or timeframes applicable. However, landlords still have legal duties under lease terms, common law principles and, potentially, general contract law. Adherence to health and safety standards, building control regulations and planning approvals may also be relevant.Leaseholders may raise concerns based on such lease terms or common law duties, and should check their lease carefully, asking for information relating to such work from their landlord or managing agent. If necessary, legal advice should be sought, or alternatively, Housing Rights NI can provide assistance in relation to concerns raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any action her Department is taking to address online abuse as a form of coercive control.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 12 - AQO 2224/22-27			   ANSWERAt the outset I should explain that telecommunications legislation, including the regulation of social media platforms, is a reserved matter and, therefore, responsibility rests with the UK Government. However, my Department has always sought to take a proactive approach towards supporting online safety, and I too remain committed to playing my part in addressing this issue.  The domestic abuse offence, which I brought forward through the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, covers online and technological abuse where abusers exploit the use of technology, social media or other online means to control or coerce someone that they are personally connected to.  This legislation ensures that repeated acts of abuse can be prosecuted effectively and I would encourage anyone experiencing this type of abuse to report it to the police.  The Online Safety Act 2023 introduced new UK-wide laws that protect both children and adults from online harm by creating a new regulatory framework for online platforms and search services. It puts the onus on service providers to take measures to protect all users from illegal content and to also protect children from harmful content and activity.  Illegal content includes harassment, stalking, controlling or coercive behaviour and intimate image abuse.  The Act also created two new communication offences, a false communications offence and a threatening communications offence.    In addition, the action plan for the new seven year Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which I jointly published with the Health Minister last September, contains an action, which is led by the Department of Health, to continue to implement actions arising from the Online Safety Strategy to empower children and young people, parents/carers and those working with children and young people to recognise, prevent and respond to abusive behaviours online.  I understand that one initiative under this Online Safety Strategy has led to the development of an Online Safety Hub, which offers clear guidance on healthy relationships and helps users, including young people, identify signs of abuse, including coercive control.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what assessment her Department has made of the impact of physical punishment on children’s (i) mental health; (ii) development; and (iii) long-term outcomes.
answer_text: I met with the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health following publication of their report in 2024 on the impact of physical punishment on children and young people. I continue to endorse the findings of the report, which found that the use of physical punishment has a negative impact on children’s physical and mental health, as well as their social, emotional and behavioural outcomes. The report also outlined evidence to suggest that children who are physically punished are more likely to believe that violence is accepted and encouraged by society, which may lead to them behaving in a more violent manner in adolescence and adulthood.Despite my efforts to do so, I have not been able to secure Executive agreement to deliver a change in the law in the current Justice Bill, but I will continue to work with my ministerial colleagues to seek their support to repeal the defence of reasonable chastisement here and move towards a position of equal protection for children in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to introduce provisions in the Justice Bill to remove the defence of reasonable chastisement in law.
answer_text: I am fully supportive of removing the defence of reasonable chastisement from the law here, however any such amendment requires Executive agreement.Despite my best efforts to do so, I have not been able to secure Executive agreement to deliver a change in the law in the current Justice Bill, but I will continue to work with my ministerial colleagues to seek their support to repeal the defence of reasonable chastisement here and move towards a position of equal protection for children in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement her Department has had with children’s rights stakeholders, including the NSPCC and the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People, regarding the defence of reasonable chastisement.
answer_text: Both I and my Department have engaged and continue to engage with the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People, the Children’s Law Centre and the Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission regarding equal protection for children.  These organisations have made representations that the current law is contrary to the European Convention on Human Rights and Equal Protection rights for children.  They continue to make the call for the defence of reasonable chastisement to be abolished, which I fully support. I am happy to engage with other children’s rights stakeholders on this and other issues; however, to date, and despite best efforts, it has not been possible to secure Executive support for legislative change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what legal protections are currently in place for children who are physically punished under the defence of reasonable chastisement.
answer_text: Children who are physically punished under the defence of reasonable chastisement have legal protections in that an adult’s defence in such a case can only be successful if the harm to the child is minor: anything which causes more than transitory or minor discomfort is unlawful and can result in prosecution.  This is an independent decision for the court to make depending on the evidence provided by the prosecution.  The current position is, in my view, unacceptable in that children do not have the same legal protections as adults, where the defence of reasonable chastisement is obviously not available. I am in agreement with the views of experts such as the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health, who have warned of the adverse impacts on children who are, under the law as it stands, “reasonably chastised” and of the risk of the availability of that defence making safeguarding more subjective and complex for professionals. That is why I support equal protection and will continue to strongly encourage all of my Executive colleagues to agree to a change in the law.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what guidance is currently issued to the (i) PSNI; (ii) Public Prosecution Service; and (iii) safeguarding professionals, on the application and limits of the reasonable chastisement defence.
answer_text: As Minister of Justice, my role is independent of the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) and the Police Service of NI (PSNI).  Further, the Minister of Health has responsibility for safeguarding professionals.No guidance is issued by my Department in this regard; however, it is important to note that Article 2 of the Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions (NI) Order 2006 has the effect of limiting the grounds on which the defence of reasonable chastisement can be used.This means if a parent or adult smacks a child and is prosecuted, they can defend themselves in terms of “reasonable chastisement” but only provided the harm is minor.  Anything which causes more than transitory or minor discomfort is unlawful and can result in prosecution.  This will be an independent decision for the court to make depending on the evidence provided by the prosecution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what estimate his Department has made of the potential cost of extending Winter Fuel Payments to all pensioners with an income under £35,000.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the Statement I made in the Assembly on 10 June 2025, Update on Winter Fuel Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department has undertaken an equality or poverty impact assessment on the current eligibility thresholds for free school meals, in light of recent statistics showing a rise in both relative and absolute child poverty.
answer_text: As part of my Department’s review of the free school meals and uniform grant eligibility criteria, an equality screening, rural needs impact assessment and a child’s rights impact assessment were completed. These documents are available on my Department’s website at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/consultations/review-free-school-meals-and-uniform-grant-eligibility-criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he intends to match the expanded eligibility criteria for Winter Fuel Payments announced by the Chancellor for pensioners in England and Wales.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the Statement I made in the Assembly on 10 June 2025, Update on Winter Fuel Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department  has received Barnett consequentials following the UK Government’s expansion of Winter Fuel Payments in England and Wales.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the Statement I made in the Assembly on 10 June 2025, Update on Winter Fuel Payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has given to extending free school meal eligibility to include all children in households in receipt of Universal Credit, in line with England.
answer_text: The announcement by the Department for Education in England that it will be expanding access to free school meals to all children in families in receipt of Universal Credit from September 2026 is a major policy change in England as at present families in receipt of Universal Credit would only be eligible for free school meals if they had a net household income of less than £7,400 per year. This is significantly lower than the £15,000 income threshold that currently applies in Northern Ireland. My Department recently concluded a twelve-week public consultation exercise on the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants. The consultation modelled options including widening access to more low-income families by raising the current income threshold but did not examine the option to remove the income threshold entirely. I intend to make my decision on the way forward in the near future. This decision will be informed by the consultation feedback, the potential impact on children and their families, schools and school caterers as well as the Department’s current significant budgetary pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has considered the introduction of increased rates for properties used as holiday lets.
answer_text: The level of rates paid by “holiday lets” will vary depending on the frequency of letting. Those that are commercially let are valued as non-domestic properties and are charged business rates. Non-commercial holiday lets are valued differently and charged domestic rates. These may include second homes or holiday homes which are let out on a more occasional basis. Applying a levy to domestic properties in that cohort was previously considered at Executive level as part of a previous formal review of domestic rating. Such a levy was ruled out on the basis that it would be easy to evade, impossible to vet at reasonable cost and would be unlikely to make any difference to the decisions taken by those seeking second homes. I am aware of similar policy interventions being attempted in Britain in recent years which my officials have been monitoring closely. Those policy interventions elsewhere have not been without significant problems but nonetheless will be assessed by my Department as part of the systemic assessment of rate supports within the Strategic Review of Rating.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department ensures that environmental responsibilities are applied fairly across all sectors, including public infrastructure and agriculture.
answer_text: Ensuring that environmental responsibilities are applied fairly across all sectors involves policy, regulation and monitoring. Environmental policy and regulation set clear, enforceable standards and baseline environmental responsibilities that apply across all sectors, ensuring a consistent and transparent approach. Environmental regulation and monitoring is carried out across all impacting sectors in a fair, firm and reasonable manner in accordance with the relevant legislation, any regulatory position statements in force and the relevant Northern Ireland or UK guidance if available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) what monitoring requirements the Northern Ireland Environment Agency places on consented sewage discharges; and (ii) whether there are plans to strengthen these.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates wastewater discharges by assessing the quality of the wastewater discharged against set conditions and standards within the Water (NI) Order Discharge Consent. Compliance and monitoring requirements are dependent on the nature of the consented wastewater treatment operation, but in general they include; targeted site inspections, operator self-monitoring and reporting. Sites are also routinely sampled as part of NIEA’s sampling rota in respect of compliance monitoring. Small discharges are not routinely monitored but remain subject to compliance with consent conditions. NIEA uses all available data to assess compliance against the conditions of the Discharge Consent. In situations where non-compliances are identified, enforcement action may be taken in line with DAERA’s enforcement policy.NIEA, through its Wastewater Regulatory Reform programme, will continue to review and update its consenting methods to ensure appropriate measures are in place, through Water Order discharge consents, to protect the environment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether NI Water intends to introduce continuous or real-time monitoring at sewage discharge points, as is now required of water companies in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:	The Environment Act 2021 imposes a duty on water companies in Great Britain to publish wastewater overflow data from storm overflows within an hour of the discharge beginning however, this legislative requirement does not apply here.NI Water continues to work with the Utility Regulator and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency regarding reporting requirements for storm overflows.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many sewage discharge consents are currently active; and (ii) whether his Department assesses their cumulative impact on water quality.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates discharges to the water environment under the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999. In total, the Department has issued 147,859 discharge consents for sewage related discharges.  NIEA is unable to confirm if all of these consents are active. The consideration of Consent to discharge applications is carried out on a risk-based approach.Discharges from a single domestic property and low volume (less than 3m3/day) discharges are treated as low risk. NIEA does not currently assess cumulative impacts of these low volume discharges. For discharges more than 3m3/ day, discharge consent standards are calculated based on existing water quality and the classification of the water body under the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017. The technical assessment therefore accounts for the cumulative impact of all inputs into that waterbody. For those discharges proposed which may have an impact on a designated site, applications are subject to a Habitats Regulation Assessment (HRAs) under the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995. These HRAs include an assessment of the cumulative, and in combination impact of other environmental authorisations. NIEA is currently reviewing all of the consenting methods applied to ensure the most up to date data and information is used to inform the consideration of applications and to ensure sufficient controls are in place to drive improvement in water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how NI Water’s sewage discharge data is currently collected; and (ii) whether any independent verification of that data takes place.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:	Sewage discharges are generally considered under 2 categories: (a) discharges that occur on a continuous basis from a wastewater treatment works (WwTW), when wastewater has been treated to a standard that is considered safe to return to the environment, and (b) discharges that occur on an intermittent basis from storm overflows when dilute wastewater is discharged direct to the environment. (a) With regard to treated effluent discharges from WwTWs, that are required to meet the standards set out in discharge consents issued by the NIEA, NI Water employs an Operator Self-Monitoring scheme as agreed with NIEA and in line with other water companies in Britain. This includes NI Water collecting data on the daily volume of treated discharges at some larger WwTWs via externally verified flow measurement systems. (b) To monitor and record intermittent spills from storm overflows, Event Duration Monitors are being installed across NI Water’s wastewater network over a multi-year investment programme. These measure the start and stop times when a spill is occurring allowing the number and duration of spills to be determined for a calendar year.None of the data collected is independently verified. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026-2029 aligns with voluntary measures under the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme.
answer_text: The development of the draft proposals has been guided by science and evidence and the information in the Implementation Report for the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2020 – 2023. This was produced in accordance with regulation 34 of the NAP (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2019 and it was published in June 2024. The final Proposals will take account of the wider policy landscape including the Farming with nature scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department engaged with agri-food stakeholders in developing the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 - 2029.
answer_text: Stakeholders were updated on the findings in the Implementation Report for the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2020 – 2023 and the current science and research, which supports the NAP proposals, in June and November 2024. Presentations from these meetings were made available to representatives on the Stakeholder Group and are published on the DAERA website.In parallel with the consultation, my officials have delivered a series of information events including two public meetings, two online public information events and three themed meetings with key stakeholder representatives.My officials will continue to meet and engage with stakeholders and I will also continue to meet with key stakeholder representatives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps he is taking to ensure the final Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 - 2029 reflects the views of farmers and the realities of food production.
answer_text: The development of the proposed Nutrients Action Programme 2026-2029 has been guided by science and evidence.  The draft proposals are underpinned by a broad range of analysis and research studies.   There are processes in place to ensure decisions on any of the draft proposals are informed by robust scientific evidence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what economic assessment has been made of the impact on small and medium farms from the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026 - 2029.
answer_text: In line with statutory process, an initial Regulatory Impact Assessment has been published with the Nutrients Action Programme consultation and a final Regulatory Impact Assessment will be completed, informed by the consultation.The final Regulatory Impact Assessment will be complemented by a full economic impact assessment to ensure any decisions on the draft proposals are informed by a full understanding of the potential impact.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps have been taken to ensure the scientific basis for the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026-2029 is robust and reflects on-farm conditions.
answer_text: The development of the proposed Nutrients Action Programme 2026-2029 has been guided by science and evidence.  The draft proposals are underpinned by a broad range of analysis and research studies.   There are processes in place to ensure decisions on any of the draft proposals are informed by robust scientific evidence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the Planning (Use Classes) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 to introduce a distinct category for short-term holiday accommodation, separate from residential dwellings.
answer_text: A cross departmental group of DfC, DfI & DfE has very recently been established to consider this issue. However, I will keep under consideration are view the Planning (Uses Classes) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans she has to strengthen Tourism NI’s enforcement powers to ensure booking platforms only list certified holiday accommodation.
answer_text: Tourism NI’s powers to regulate the visitor accommodation sector are set out in the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 (“the 1992 Order”). These include enforcement powers for accommodation establishments which have not been certified.Tourism NI engages regularly with representatives of the main online booking platforms, including Airbnb and Booking.com, to encourage them to align the properties listed on their sites with properties certified by Tourism NI in the relevant statutory categories of visitor accommodation. As a result of this engagement, local law disclosure information for the North has been added by Airbnb whilst booking.com highlight the certification requirement to local operators on their platform.Tourism NI also routinely undertakes enforcement action against uncertified operators in accordance with the 1992 Order. There are 324 certified operators who were previously trading without a certificate as a result of Tourism NI engagement.My Department and Tourism NI are reviewing the visitor accommodation certification scheme operated by Tourism NI. This will include consideration of whether any future legislative change is required, including with regard to enforcement powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has considered a cross-departmental review with the Department for the Economy to assess the regulatory gaps in managing short-term holiday accommodation.
answer_text: A cross departmental group of DfC, DfI & DfE has very recently been established to consider this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the support available through his Department to help grassroots sports clubs secure access to appropriate playing grounds.
answer_text: Responsibility for provision of playing fields and other recreation facilities lies with local councils under Article 10 of the Recreation and Youth Service Order (Northern Ireland) 1986.Sport NI has drafted a Sports Facility Audit and is working with councils and governing bodies to identify gaps.Proposals are also currently being developed as part of The Northern Ireland Football Fund for my consideration.I also would draw the Member’s attention to the Your School Your Club initiative, which seeks to open up school sports facilities for community use, including grassroots sports clubs.  I will shortly be making a formal announcement about investment for Your School Your Club for the 2025/2026 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how Project Stratum is addressing any remaining broadband not-spots in rural communities.
answer_text: The build phase for Project Stratum is scheduled to finish at the end of this month. Project Statum has made gigabit services available to 80,000 premises, and a further 1,100 will be connected by the end of June. This means that 93% of premises here can access a gigabit-capable service compared to 83% in the rest of Britain.Project Gigabit is the next phase of broadband infrastructure investment and will further improve access a gigabit-capable services. This project is in procurement. I unable to confirm which premises will be connected until the procurement phase is complete. I am conscious that not all premises are expected to be included in Project Gigabit. This is for a variety of reasons, including high costs for individual premises, and limitations in identifying which premises require public subsidy and which will be served commercially. My Department is taking steps to ensure we maximise the reach of Project Gigabit. My officials are working to identify any premises that need to be added to the project. There is a process to include such premises in Project Gigabit where it is represents value for money, and subject to funding being made available. I am also aware that Building Digital UK is developing schemes for high cost, or very hard to reach, premises. However, these schemes are in early stages. I will continue to make the case for premises in the north to have access to these schemes when they become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will consider introducing legislation, similar to the Republic of Ireland’s Planning and Development Act 2024, which would require booking platforms to verify that listed properties are properly registered as short-term tourist accommodation.
answer_text: Tourism NI’s powers to regulate the visitor accommodation sector are set out in the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 (“the 1992 Order”). These include enforcement powers for accommodation establishments which have not been certified.Tourism NI engages regularly with representatives of the main online booking platforms, including Airbnb and Booking.com, to encourage them to align the properties listed on their sites with properties certified by Tourism NI in the relevant statutory categories of visitor accommodation. As a result of this engagement, local law disclosure information for the North has been added by Airbnb whilst booking.com highlight the certification requirement to local operators on their platform.Tourism NI also routinely undertakes enforcement action against uncertified operators in accordance with the 1992 Order. There are 324 certified operators who were previously trading without a certificate as a result of Tourism NI engagement.My Department and Tourism NI are reviewing the visitor accommodation certification scheme operated by Tourism NI. This will include consideration of whether any future legislative change is required, including with regard to enforcement powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider issuing strategic planning guidance to councils on how to manage the growing number of short-term holiday lets in high-pressure housing areas.
answer_text: The Department’s Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035 and the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) support the creation of balanced communities and, in relation to all types of housing, the policy approach is to facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality housing to meet the needs of everyone. Council local development plans (LDPs) are the main vehicle for delivering this by assessing future housing land requirements and managing housing growth in their areas, including in high pressure housing areas, to achieve sustainable patterns of residential development. This process allows councils to address local housing issues within their communities, including in areas that may be affected by increasing numbers of short-term holiday lets. Where appropriate, LDPs can include specific policies to protect the distinctive nature and character of settlements. Councils can prepare local policies and guidance to address certain planning matters in their areas, such as short-term holiday lets, if considered necessary. Councils are also responsible for operating effective development management in respect of planning applications for all types of housing development and in doing so, the provisions of regional strategic planning policy must be taken into account. I have, therefore, no plans to revise the Department’s regional strategic planning policy approach on housing or provide specific guidance on short-term holiday lets at this time.Notwithstanding this, I can advise that discussions, comprising a cross departmental group of officials (DfC, DfI & DfE) have recently commenced to consider the matter of short-term lets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he expects to publish full details of the new Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Scheme has now launched 2 June 2025, and gives patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland and have been on an HSC hospital treatment waiting list for two years, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to Health and Social Care (HSC) in Northern Ireland.Details of the application process and accessing funding are now available on the Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the criteria that will be used to assess eligibility for treatment under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Scheme has now launched 2 June 2025 and applies the following strict eligibility criteria:Patients who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland; And Patients who are on a treatment or surgical waiting list within HSC services in Northern Ireland.The Scheme will be initiated on a phased approach. The first phase will allow reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland. The second phase will be introduced at a later date subject to remaining available funding and will see the geographical scope of the Scheme expanded to all countries in the European UnionPatients require prior approval from my Department before accessing the Scheme. As the Scheme is only available to pre-authorised treatments, my Department cannot accept retrospective applications. Details of the application process and accessing funding are now available on the Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to allowing reimbursement for patients who access treatment in private hospitals or clinics within Northern Ireland under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which allowed patients to get reimbursed for healthcare treatment outside Northern Ireland and GB.  As I outlined in my previous statement there is other work ongoing with Independent Sector Providers in Northern Ireland as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the new Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme will include provision for patients with comorbidities or higher dependency needs.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what specific initiatives have been considered to improve breast screening uptake; and (ii) whether there are any funding commitments to improve breast screening uptake.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) is responsible for ensuring that all eligible women in NI can make an informed choice about attending for breast screening and that the service is as accessible as possible. The uptake rate for routine breast screening in Northern Ireland, for those invited, meets the national acceptable standard of greater than, or equal to 70.0%, as set out in the NHS Breast Screening Programme screening standards. PHA works in partnership with other stakeholders to raise awareness of the breast cancer screening programme and to promote informed choice. Specific initiatives include the following:the PHA chairs a regional group with representation from all Breast Screening Units in the region and Trust health promotion staff. The remit of the group is to identify opportunities to promote informed choice in the Northern Ireland Breast Screening Programme (with a particular focus on women from disadvantaged communities; women who have learning/physical/ sensory disabilities; women from minority ethnic groups; older women and other women considered to have special needs); to identify and share good practice; and to advise on the provision of information to the public and health care professionals;the PHA breast screening team includes standalone meetings related to promoting informed choice in breast screening in the Quality Assurance (QA) visits to breast screening units;the PHA collaborates with Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure that comprehensive, up to date, information on screening is available on their website.the PHA currently commissions the Women’s Resource and Development Agency (WRDA), a local not-for-profit community organisation, to raise awareness of the Breast, Cervical and Bowel Cancer Screening Programmes and to promote informed choice among individuals from communities and populations with historically lower cancer screening uptake rates compared with the general population. The aim of the WRDA’s programme of work is to provide individuals with sufficient information to enable them to make an informed decision about participating in cancer screening programmes. The WRDA recruit, train and support Peer Facilitators to deliver Educational Awareness Sessions to targeted service user groups, including; people from deprived areas, those from ethnic minorities, those from the LGBT+ community, homeless individuals and those with physical disabilities, learning disabilities or mental health issues. WRDA also carry out Bespoke Workshops for those latter groups with additional support needs.In relation to working with charities to improve breast screening uptake, I can advise that during February and March 2025, Cancer Research UK (CRUK) ran a multimedia campaign to promote the three NI cancer screening programmes. The PHA worked closely with CRUK to develop this campaign. In addition, the PHA is working with Breast Cancer Now and the Rainbow Project to promote informed choice.  There are no specific funding commitments

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what evaluation methods are in place to measure the effectiveness of breast screening uptake improvement plans or initiatives.
answer_text: Effectiveness of uptake improvement plans and initiatives are measured through a review of the Breast Screening Programme uptake data.  Uptake is monitored regularly by the Public Health Agency, on a quarterly and annual level. Where there are specific initiatives or interventions, uptake can be reviewed over specific timescales, or geographical locations.The four Breast Screening Units in Northern Ireland each work to develop Promoting Informed Choice action plans with specific actions at local level. Local uptake improvement initiatives can be monitored internally by the Breast Screening Unit using data e.g. at GP practice level. The uptake rate for routine breast screening in Northern Ireland, for those invited, meets the national acceptable standard of greater than, or equal to 70.0%, as set out in the NHS Breast Screening Programme screening standards. Uptake rates for Northern Ireland since 2020, are as follows:Year2020/212021/22 2022/232023/24Uptake Standard*72.2%74.8%74.3%73.9%*Acceptable level: greater than or equal to 70.0%; Achievable level: greater than or equal to 80.0%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered implementing new legislative provisions to enable data to be used to (i) inform and deliver service improvements; and (ii) facilitate benchmarking with other UK regions.
To ask the Minister of Education what guidance is provided to schools on identifying, preventing, and responding to incidents of upskirting among pupils.
answer_text: My department provides guidance for educational settings on all child protection and safeguarding matters.In 2022, the Department of Justice introduced new legislation which established new offences, including up-skirting. My department’s guidance was reviewed to include information on these new offences and also how schools can identify harmful sexual behaviour, alongside providing advice on dealing with these issues as they emerge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of reported incidents of upskirting in Northern Ireland in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on offences reported to the police in its datasets.  The recording of information on the number of incidents reported to the police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to offences reported to police, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether there are any plans to review how data is collected in order to achieve an outcome-based accountability policy approach.
answer_text: An outcomes-based approach is valuable in developing and monitoring the implementation of policy, but other ways of measuring progress are also needed.  We have learnt a great deal about outcome-based approaches over recent years, and an important development in this regard was the Executive’s Programme for Government Wellbeing Framework.  The Framework builds on previous work on improving outcomes and wellbeing for all, and covers a combination of social, environmental, economic, and democratic factors, which are considered essential for society to flourish. When first launched, the framework had 47 indicators, and we’ve since added another four to address gaps that were identified following our engagement with key stakeholders. Work is continuing to add new data, notably to help us identify inequalities across different groups and to add more indicators. We collaborate with NISRA colleagues to ensure that we have robust, timely and reliable data to inform the work of the whole Executive, as we look to improve wellbeing for everyone living here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what plans she has to raise public awareness around upskirting and other forms of image-based sexual abuse.
answer_text: A dedicated public awareness campaign to promote the introduction of the up-skirting offence, as well as the other new intimate-image based offences of down-blousing and cyber-flashing, was carried out by the Department from November 2023 to the end of March 2024.  This formed part of the wider communication and promotional strategy which was used to deliver implementation of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, in which the three offences are contained.  Promotion was extended across a range of social media platforms, as well outdoor placement advertising, to coincide with commencement of the new offences.I continue to keep potential further public awareness raising exercises in this area under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any support provided to children and young people who are victims of upskirting; and (ii) whether these measures are age-appropriate and trauma-informed.
answer_text: I am aware of the particular harm and distress caused by the abhorrent and intrusive behaviour of up-skirting and other forms of voyeurism, not least to our children and young people, in an evolving society where access to, and advances in, technology provides more opportunities for its perpetrators.  I recognise the need for access to age appropriate and trauma-informed support and I am pleased that a range of such services is available for victims, either partly or fully funded by my Department. These include: a 24-hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Helpline; Victim Support NI (VSNI) services; and the NSPCC Young Witness Service (YWS).DSA Helpline staff receive specialised training to ensure they can respond sensitively to the needs of all victims, including children, young people, and those with additional vulnerabilities.  Age-appropriate responses are central to the service, ensuring that support is tailored to the developmental stage, understanding, and needs of each individual.  The approach is trauma-informed and prioritises safety, empowerment and trust-building and minimises re-traumatisation.VSNI has a number of services that can provide relevant support to children and young people who have been victim to this offence, including its Community Service and its Young Victim Service.  Its staff and volunteers provide emotional support, advocacy, information and advice to victims after the crime has occurred.  This support is based on the World Health Organisation’s ‘psychological first aid’ model, which is used worldwide to support people in the aftermath of a stressful event and is very much age-appropriate and trauma-informed.  When appropriate, VSNI also signposts/refers victims to other specialist services.The YWS, whose staff have social work training, provides individually-tailored support to all young prosecution witnesses under the age of 18 years old who are called to give evidence, to enable them to give their best evidence at the court hearing.  Support for victims of crime, including victims of sexual abuse, continues to be a key priority for me as Justice Minister.The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which I jointly published in September 2024 with the Health Minister, recognises the damage that all forms of sexual abuse can have on children and young people, including non-contact sexual abuse such as upskirting and downblousing.  The Strategy’s Children and Young People Pillar sets a clear direction to help prevent all forms of domestic and/or sexual abuse from happening and to ensure that children and young people, who are victims, are seen and heard; and can access services and receive support to help them to recover.My Department has also recently published a consultation on proposals for a new Victims and Witnesses of Crime Strategy 2025-2030. One of the Strategy’s five delivery pillars focuses specifically on children and young people. It aims to deliver a child-centred approach by providing tailored, responsive an

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many people have been supported by the Ross Thompson Unit in the Causeway Hospital in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below details how many staff are currently employed within the Ross Thompson Unit, Causeway Hospital, compared to the last five years.202520242023202220213941413736The table below outlines the number of patients supported by the Ross Thompson Unit in the Causeway Hospital, following admission in the last five years, from 1st April – 31st March for each financial year.2020/20212021/20222022/20232023/20242024/2025167151138138140A stepped care approach is taken to supporting service users with their mental health, matching a person’s need with the right level of support. The You in Mind, Regional Care Pathway, explains how a person who may require mental health support, can access mental health care, and the steps involved. This pathway includes specialist mental health services. There are a range of specialist mental health services accessible to service users, including for example the Forensic Mental Health Service, Psychological Therapy Services, Peri Natal Mental Health Service and Eating Disorders Service. Where following assessment, clinical and risk factors indicate that admission is necessary, Trusts endeavour to arrange admission to an acute mental health unit within their own Trust, closest to the address of the patient. Where there is no immediately available bed, the Trust will consider alternative options within their Trust facilities. In exceptional circumstances, where no suitable bed can be found, and where deemed critically necessary for the safety and wellbeing of the patient, a bed may have to be sourced from another Trust. Patients will be involved in all decisions involving their care and treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how she ensures that her Department treats teaching roles across further education equitably with teaching roles in schools, particularly in terms of (i) remuneration; and (ii) recognition.
answer_text: Remuneration and recognition for those with teaching roles in schools falls under the responsibility of the Department of Education, whilst my Department holds responsibility for the Further Education colleges as employers of those with teaching roles across further education. My predecessor provided a budget envelope last year which allowed for an 11.2% pay increase for Further Education lecturers for 2023/24, which aligned with that provided for schoolteachers. I am pleased that I was able to facilitate a multi-year pay offer which again took cognisance of the pay increase agreed for schoolteachers for 2024/25 as a further step towards parity for Further Education lecturers. I remain fully supportive of equitable remuneration and recognition for Further Education lecturers.
