question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what consideration she has given to the long-term impact on (i) recruitment; and (ii) retention, in further education if the pay disparity with school teachers remains unresolved.
answer_text: I am pleased to update that I was able to facilitate a multi-year pay offer as a further step towards pay parity with teachers and I remain fully supportive of equitable remuneration for Further Education lecturers. I understand Further Education colleges, as the employers, are proposing a range of agreed workstreams as part of the pay offer, including on school teachers’ pay scale parity.  I hope that the pay offer is accepted as it will have a positive impact on recruitment and retention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the role of further education lecturers in delivering her Department’s skills agenda; and (ii) to detail how this is reflected in current pay and workforce policy.
answer_text: I strongly believe that the Further Education lecturers play a vital role in helping to provide individuals with the necessary skills and education to deliver on my skills agenda, and my wider vision for the economy. In recognition of this role, I am pleased that I was able to facilitate a multi-year pay offer for Further Education lecturers. In relation to workforce policy, the Further Education colleges, as the employers, are progressing a range of agreed workstreams, including workload, in conjunction with Trade Unions in the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Department has assessed public confidence in Non-Molestation Orders as a protective measure, particularly among victims of domestic abuse.
answer_text: ANSWERNon-Molestation Orders (NMOs) are significant in the context of tackling domestic abuse and safe-guarding victims. The cross-Departmental Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy is led by the Departments of Justice and Health, and my Department retains an interest in relation to civil measures of this nature. The Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime has commissioned a researcher to carry out some quantitative analysis on data relating to the application, granting and outcomes in cases of breach of NMOs. My officials will liaise with colleagues in other Departments and will carefully consider the findings of that work. The Department of Justice is also progressing work to develop an effective model for Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders, and my officials will continue to monitor developments relating to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether data on Non-Molestation Order breaches is used to inform improvements in domestic abuse policy and enforcement.
answer_text: Responsibility for policy and legislation relating to Non-Molestation Orders (NMOs) rests with the Minister of Finance: however, I recognise their role in tackling domestic abuse and safeguarding victims and I acknowledge the importance of using data and evidence on a range of interventions, including NMOs, to inform improvements to domestic abuse policy and enforcement.I am aware that the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime has heard concerns from victims and victim support organisations regarding inconsistencies in the application and granting of Non-Molestation Orders (NMOs) and Occupation Orders (OOs), as well as the criminal justice response when such orders are breached. I understand that the Commissioner Designate has subsequently commissioned research to gain a deeper understanding of how these orders are being used and their perceived effectiveness in safeguarding victims of domestic abuse.I understand that the first part of this research is in its final stages.  This is a quantitative analysis of available data on NMO and OO applications which looks at grant rates, breaches, and criminal justice outcomes following breaches. I have been advised that, following completion of the data analysis, the Commissioner intends to take forward a qualitative exercise to engage with victims, support organisations, police and legal professionals. This will explore the statistical findings in more depth and seek practical solutions to strengthen the protective order regime and improve outcomes for victims.It is anticipated that the research will be completed and published during the summer months. My Department will carefully consider the findings and use the learning to inform and develop future domestic abuse policy responses, including identifying opportunities for improving the protective measures available to victims.My officials are also working closely with the PSNI and key stakeholders on the development of a viable model to support the introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders, ensuring that it will meet the needs of victims in Northern Ireland. The findings of the Commissioner Designate’s research will help to inform this work and support the design of a victim-centred and meaningful protective order and notice framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether there are plans to review or strengthen the legislative framework underpinning Non-Molestation Orders to ensure they provide meaningful protection.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department was responsible for the primary legislation governing Non-Molestation Orders (NMOs), the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998.Although there are no current plans to review the Order, following a previous review by my Department, the sentencing relating to breaches of non-molestation orders was increased from 3 to 6 months. Pursuant to AQW 24847/22-27, the Minister of Justice advised that her Department is actively working to identify where gaps in protection exist and is currently exploring how Domestic Abuse Protection Orders could be introduced with the aim of providing enhanced protection for victims. My officials continue to liaise with colleagues in other Departments who have responsibility for domestic abuse policy and will take account of other examinations of the effectiveness of NMOs, with a view to exploring this issue further with relevant colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what steps are being taken to (i) prevent repeated breaches of Non-Molestation Orders; and (ii) ensure that victims feel adequately safeguarded.
answer_text: I recognise the concerns that have been raised around of the operation and effectiveness of Non-Molestation Orders (NMOs), including in relation to breach of these Orders. Responsibility for policy and legislation relating to NMOs rests with the Minister of Finance, but I recognise their critical role in tackling domestic abuse and safeguarding victims.I understand that the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime is carrying out quantitative analysis on data relating to the application, granting and outcomes in cases of breach of NMOs in order to assess their effectiveness and identify any potential weaknesses. I await the outcome of that work and will consider its findings carefully, in conjunction with the Minister for Finance, to identify any necessary improvements.In addition, my Department is continuing to progress work to develop a viable model to support the introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders (DAPNs/DAPOs) to Northern Ireland. My officials are working closely with the Police Service of Northern Ireland to ensure any new system is effective and appropriate for our legal and operational context. This work has included learning visits to pilot areas in England and engagement with frontline officers and stakeholders, with plans for further visits and for a roundtable policy workshop with interested voluntary and community sector Organisations.This evidence-led, stakeholder-informed approach underlines my commitment to strengthening protection for victims and ensuring any new measures are both operationally effective and responsive to victims’ needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how many breaches of Non-Molestation Orders have been recorded in each of the last five years; and (ii) how many of these breaches resulted in a prosecution.
answer_text: The recording of information on the number of breaches of Non-Molestation Orders recorded by them is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct part (i) of your question to the PSNI. In relation to the information requested in part (ii) of the question, information for prosecutions and convictions at courts for breaches of Non-Molestation Order offences that were dealt with in the period 2020 to 2024, the most recent five years for which information is available, has been provided in the following table.  Prosecutions and Convictions at courts for ‘Breach of Non-Molestation Order’ offences, 2020 to 2024YearProsecutionsConvictions202025518120214412832022380252    20233392332024324185Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 2. Figures relate to defendants who were prosecuted or convicted at court for at least one count of the offence ‘Breach of a Non-Molestation Order’.3. Figures for 2024 are provisional.4. Figures provided relate to the time period in which cases were dealt with.  The offences themselves may have occurred outside of the five years reported.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether data is being collected on the mental health outcomes of mothers up to one year postpartum; and (ii) how this data is informing the design and delivery of services.
answer_text: I can confirm that (i) data is being collected on the mental health outcomes of mothers up to one year postpartum. This data collection is part of a broader effort to monitor and improve perinatal mental health services. The Perinatal Mental Health Service has been gathering pre and post-intervention questionnaires for various support groups such as the Postnatal Support Group, Emotional Regulation Group, and Incredible Babies Group. These questionnaires are collected when a patient enters the service, during treatment, and on discharge, which includes up to one year postpartum.We are also exploring how Perinatal Mental Health Outcomes can be reported at both regional and trust levels, to support more comprehensive monitoring and service improvement.In summary, the collection and analysis of data on the mental health outcomes of mothers up to one year postpartum are integral to our efforts to enhance and tailor perinatal mental health services across Northern Ireland.The data collected is crucial for informing the design and delivery of services. It helps in understanding the effectiveness of current interventions and identifying areas for improvement. For example, the Southern Trust Perinatal Mental Health Team has completed surveys with women on their caseload, which includes assessments on admission and discharge, and at various points during treatment.  This ongoing assessment helps tailor the services to the needs of the women and informs continuous service improvement Furthermore, the data informs broader strategic initiatives such as the development of community perinatal mental health teams across all Trusts, which aim to provide specialist services for mothers experiencing mental health issues during the perinatal period. This is part of a phased approach to enhance perinatal mental health services regionally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support available for mothers who do not meet the threshold for clinical intervention but continue to experience mental health challenges after childbirth.
answer_text: There are a wide range of services in existence in maternity services, primary care and the community and voluntary sector that adopt a trauma focused approach and are dedicated to supporting women to parent their children and support women across the spectrum of mental health issues that occur that do not meet the threshold for clinical intervention.  These include specialist midwifery and health visiting initiatives. One such initiative is the Continuity of Midwifery Carer which was launched in May 2023.  This new model of maternity care provides integrated, appropriate and seamless care for all women through their journey from pregnancy and birth to early parenthood. Research has concluded that midwife continuity of carer models save babies’ lives, reduce interventions and improve clinical outcomes and women’s experiences. Specialist Perinatal Mental Health teams are now operational in each Health and Social Care Trust and provide a comprehensive service dedicated to treating illness in the mother alongside supporting and enhancing the relationship between the mother and her infant. These teams are also involved in training initiatives with a wide range of professionals for those women who do not meet threshold for specialist services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Regional Perinatal Mental Health Pathway; and (ii) for his assessment of whether it adequately addresses the long-term psychological impact of childbirth on women.
answer_text: (i) Since becoming Health Minister, I have repeatedly stated that improving mental health services, including perinatal mental health, is one of my top priorities.  I am delighted that all five Trusts now have a fully operational community perinatal mental health team in place, each accepting referrals and providing a range of interventions to women experiencing mental health problems during the perinatal period.  Action 29a of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31, made a commitment to continue to roll-out perinatal mental health services, including inpatient services.  The first phase of this was the establishment of these perinatal mental health teams within each Trust. I was delighted to launch the updated Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathway in March 2025 which has integrated the community perinatal mental health teams into the pathway of care.  It provides guidance to all health and social care professionals who come into contact with women in the antenatal and postnatal period across all settings, inclusive of primary care.(ii) The specialist perinatal mental health teams that are now operational in each Health and Social Care Trust provide a comprehensive service dedicated to treating illness in the mother alongside supporting and enhancing the relationship between the mother and her infant. The teams have a range of professionals with skills and expertise in this area, including parent infant psychotherapists, health visitors, midwives and psychologists.  The new specialist teams support women up to one-year post childbirth (with some flexibility).  At the point of discharge, referral on to other services, both statutory and voluntary, can be made if required.Each team is already starting to see very positive outcomes from these interventions.  The teams are also involved in training initiatives with a wide range of professionals for those women who do not meet threshold for specialist services. The second phase of this programme of work includes the delivery of a Mother and Baby Unit (MBU).  Work is now at an advanced stage in developing a business case to progress the investment required to take this forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women that have accessed support from community perinatal mental health teams in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The table below details the number of women that have accessed support from community perinatal mental health teams in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.Trust202220232024Belfast TrustData not available, team was not fully operational131143Northern TrustData not available, team was not fully operational101175South Eastern TrustData not available, team was not fully operational95120Southern TrustData not available, team was not fully operational92104Western TrustData not available, team was not fully operational(Accepting referrals from October 2023)1075Total-429617

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the establishment of the regional Mother and Baby Unit in Belfast, including the expected timeline for completion and opening.
answer_text: The business case for the Mother and Baby Unit is well-progressed with further work currently being carried out to strengthen the proposal and to future proof the Unit. It is almost nearing completion and will be submitted to the Department’s commissioning team in SPPG for scrutiny. However, progression of the project will remain subject to the necessary funding being identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department ensures women experiencing financial control or coercive control are not disadvantaged in legal aid assessments due to a lack of independent income.
answer_text: I am aware that victims of domestic abuse often face financial barriers when seeking legal assistance, particularly where they have limited or no independent income due to the abusive relationship.  Section 28 of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings (Northern Ireland) Act 2021 provides the Director of Legal Aid the discretion to disregard the financial eligibility thresholds for victims of domestic abuse where an evidentiary letter is provided in response to Article 8 Children Order (Northern Ireland) 1995 proceedings brought by the perpetrator of their abuse exactly for this reason.In addition, the legal aid financial eligibility threshold tests are automatically disregarded in relation to non-molestation orders for victims of domestic abuse.I am committed, through the Enabling Access to Justice Delivery Plan, to improving support for this group, by building new, simpler routes by which people can access comprehensive legal help to navigate the Justice issues that domestic abuse victims face, including in both the criminal and the family courts.  My officials will be engaging directly with stakeholders on this vital issue over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will review legal aid eligibility rules where a nil Universal Credit award prevents automatic entitlement for victims of domestic abuse.
answer_text: The system of “passporting” people on certain means tested benefits, including those in receipt of Universal Credit, is an administrative procedure based on the premise that people entitled to these benefits have already had their means assessed by the Department for Communities (DfC) who established that their income levels are such that they would more than likely meet the financial means test for legal aid – thereby avoiding duplication of effort and resources on assessment.A nil award is made where a person’s income from employment, other income, or their capital or savings exceed relevant limits.  For this reason, there is no automatic award of legal aid in these circumstances. If they are not eligible for passporting in this way, an applicant may be eligible for legal aid through either the waiver for non-molestation order applications, or the waiver for victims of domestic abuse who are respondents to contact or residence orders relating to children.The issue of financial eligibility for legal aid, including the rules relating to victims of domestic abuse is being considered as part of my Department’s ongoing Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on when a decision will be made regarding the initial site for Causeway Academy.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of requiring financial contributions on women seeking non-molestation orders under Regulation 10 of the Civil Legal Services (Financial) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: Victims of domestic abuse may face financial barriers when seeking legal assistance, particularly where they have limited or no independent income due to the nature of the abusive relationship. Although the normal full test of financial eligibility is automatically disregarded in relation to non-molestation orders, there can be situations whereby a small contribution is required. We have no evidence of these contributions presenting a meaningful barrier to justice for this group. The current rules say that, where people are not in receipt of a passporting benefit, they must undergo a test of their means, however, the contributions are typically small and they are also payable over time, rather than up front. Based on the evidence gathered through the foundational review of civil legal services, and two independent research projects looking specifically at the experience of victims of domestic abuse,  the main barriers to uptake of the non-molestation waiver are lack of awareness and solicitors being unwilling to take on legal aid work, however the issue of financial eligibility, including for the rules applying to vulnerable groups including victims of domestic abuse, and the remuneration rates for representatives, are being considered as part of my departments ongoing reform process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement her Department has had with women’s support organisations in the last five years to assess the practical impact of legal aid eligibility rules on access to justice for domestic abuse victims.
answer_text: During the development and passage through the Assembly of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 (DACPA), Department of Justice officials consulted with and met a range of women’s support organisations, and other organisations involved in supporting people who have experienced domestic abuse.  This engagement helped to shape the provisions of that Act, and specifically with reference to legal aid, resulted in the adoption of provision for a waiver of financial eligibility limits for legal aid in certain circumstances for victims of domestic abuse.Since then, my Department has commissioned two pieces of research aimed specifically at understanding the experiences of victims of domestic abuse with the justice system in general and with legal aid in particular.  These are the research commissioned to inform the Department’s statutory report on legal aid and domestic abuse, as required by s. 29 of DACPA, and a further review and rapid priority setting exercise conducted by researchers at Ulster University, to inform the Department’s own foundational review of civil legal services, which itself involved direct engagement with organisations supporting victims of domestic abuse.As a result of this engagement, the Department has a strong evidence base that clearly indicates a need for additional support for domestic abuse victims.  These additional support measures will be advanced as part of the implementation of the Enabling Access to Justice Delivery Plan, and will be developed in collaboration with all relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any immediate action being taken to investigate and address recent media reports of illegal puppy farmers euthanising dogs due to reduced demand.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the media report and has been in contact with local councils to ascertain whether the claim in relation to euthanising dogs is substantiated. However, no council has received reports to support the claim, nor is the Department aware of any evidence indicating such activities are occurring. Local councils are responsible for enforcing legislation pertaining to animal welfare and dog breeding and any concerns should be addressed to them for further investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the proposed expert review of dog breeding regulations will include an assessment of the adequacy of enforcement mechanisms to prevent illegal breeding practices.
answer_text: The Expert Advisory Group, when established, will be assigned to conduct a comprehensive review of current dog breeding regulations and their implementation in Northern Ireland. As part of this, the Group will be engaging with key stakeholders, including local councils through their statutory enforcement role, to collect pertinent evidence to inform its report and recommendations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures being considered to regulate canine fertility services.
answer_text: My Department has recently launched a recruitment competition to appoint an Expert Advisory Group to review the current dog breeding regulations in Northern Ireland. This will include examining whether canine fertility clinics need regulation. Once the review is complete, I will then consider the Group's recommendations and decide on any necessary next steps.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps being taken to enhance collaboration with local councils and animal welfare organisations to identify and shut down illegal puppy farms.
answer_text: My Department maintains regular engagement with councils, by liaising with members of the Northern Ireland Dog Advisory Council and through councils participation in the Paws for Thought initiative, which aims to tackle the breeding and trafficking of low welfare pups.  Officials also maintain contact with the Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group (NICAWG) and I would encourage all animal welfare organisations are to report any evidence of illegal breeding to the relevant local council for investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has engaged with the Department of Justice to strengthen penalties for those involved in illegal dog breeding or animal cruelty.
answer_text: I, along with officials from my Department and the Department of Justice, recently met with the USPCA, to discuss their recent report on animal welfare sentencing in Northern Ireland. My officials have also engaged with the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) to examine how animal welfare prosecutions are processed, particularly in relation to understanding the factors which determine the mode of trial. The penalties available for animal welfare offences, under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, do already afford a high degree of protection to animals. The maximum penalty available is five years imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine. This is in line with other jurisdictions, and when introduced, were the most stringent across the UK.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the monitoring mechanisms in place to ensure private providers comply with financial arrangements when delivering publicly-funded treatment.
answer_text: HSC Trusts engage directly with the Independent Sector using the standard financial contracting arrangements.  All contracts are assessed for value for money before being agreed and payments are only made once invoices are received and the work is verified.  Monitoring of this process will include the checking of invoice tariffs against contracted rates and the identification of duplicate invoicing.  This will be based on the individual contractual arrangements agreed in advance and may vary in nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of money recouped by his Department through the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive; and (i) the amount written off due to non-recovery while the scheme was active.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 25972/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is considering expanding the cross-border elective healthcare scheme to include diagnostic assessments for ASD and ADHD.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.The Scheme will allow patients, who have been placed on a Health and Social Care waiting list for elective surgery or treatment, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector and have the cost reimbursed. Diagnostic assessments for ASD and ADHD will be outside the parameters of the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total amount of money recovered by his Department for healthcare provided to patients not Ordinarily Resident in Northern Ireland in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The total amount of money recovered for healthcare provided to patients not Ordinarily Resident in Northern Ireland in each of the last five years, as reported by HSC Trusts, is summarised in the following table.Year£m*2020/212.12021/221.82022/232.42023/242.02024/25**2.7*Note: figures above have been rounded to the nearest £0.1m.** Note: 2024/25 not finalised, early estimate of final value included in table.This income includes items such as overseas patients, UK cross border emergency treatments and income relating to European Health Insurance Card (EHIC).  Contract activity with HSC for Republic of Ireland patients is not included.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any barriers his Department has identified in recouping costs associated with the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive; and (ii) how these barriers will be addressed in future schemes.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) was a limited administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to Northern Ireland (NI) following the United Kingdom’s exit from the European Union.  The CBHD set out a framework of rules which allowed patients to seek and pay for treatment in either the state or private sectors in an EEA country and apply to for reimbursement of the cost (up to the cost of treatment in Health and Social Care in NI).Under the RoIRS, patients were able to seek, and pay for, routinely commissioned treatment in the private sector in the Republic of Ireland and have the cost reimbursed (up to the cost of treatment in Health and Social Care in NI). To use the scheme patients had to receive prior authorisation before travelling for treatment.As these were both reimbursement schemes, the Department did not recoup any money through either the CBHD or RoIRS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department has undertaken a review of current training capacity within Northern Ireland’s universities for nursing and allied health professions; and (ii) whether this aligns with projected local workforce demands.
answer_text: Commissioning of both pre-registration and post-registration training for nursing, midwifery and allied health professions is a collaborative process between my Department, higher education providers and HSC Trusts. Commissioning plans are informed by HSC Trusts’ training and workforce planning needs analysis that is undertaken on an annual basis.My Department has not undertaken a review of current training capacity within the universities for nursing and allied health professions. Under the current contract for the commissioning of pre-registration nursing and allied health professions training programmes, assessment of training capacity is a matter for the universities. For post-registration arrangements, the Department works with universities via the established DoH Nursing and Midwifery and Allied Health Professions post-registration education commissioning process to ensure education commissioning plans are drawn up which reflect service needs in line with strategic priorities.  The Department maintains a close working relationship with the universities and provides as much foresight as possible on future commissioning intentions, subject to affordability considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support mechanisms in place to assist internationally-recruited health and social care staff in adapting to the communication, cultural and clinical expectations of the health service in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: International recruitment is a key avenue in addressing our workforce resource issues and ensuring that the health & social care service can deliver the safe and effective service for patients. The international recruits who arrive to work here across our HSC system provide an immensely valuable contribution to the delivery of health and social care services, let alone to our communities. The provision of robust pastoral support and professional induction for international recruits across our HSC workforce is vitally important to ensure they are able to adapt to their arrival in a new country, workplace and community.A regional HSC Working Group meet regularly to consider all aspects of the recruitment and retention of our international recruits, and this includes how best to assist them to settle within the workplace. Each HSC Trust has a dedicated resource who will support with social induction, pastoral care and professional training, including working with recruits on their communication skills, practicing using the telephone, role plays, and understanding local colloquialisms. The HSC Clinical Education Centre runs Objective Structured Clinical Examination preparation programmes designed to support international recruits with clinical expectations whilst HSC Trusts maintain strong links with Professional Bodies and their international ‘Welcome’ programmes. As well as helping our international recruits to understand our local cultures, the HSC Trusts support our locally trained workforce with cultural competency training, promoting open communication and celebrating the diversity of our international staff and creating an inclusive environment for all of our HSC workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the barriers facing people from Northern Ireland in pursuing careers in the health and social care sector.
answer_text: Currently the main route into a career in registered healthcare professions is through pre-registration undergraduate training.  While the number of pre-registration training places commissioned by my Department has increased in recent years, these still fall below the numbers my Department have identified through strategic workforce planning as necessary to grow the health and social care workforce to the level required to deliver the most effective treatment and care to the people of Northern Ireland.  There remains a high interest in these professions, which means that the volume of applications for places on these undergraduate programmes regularly exceeds the numbers of available places. Many people from Northern Ireland who wish to pursue a career in health and social care are therefore unable to do so.  Additional recurrent funding would enable my Department to increase the numbers commissioned to the levels required, providing greater training opportunities for local people who wish to pursue a career in health and social care.  I am also mindful that the financial commitment required to undertake undergraduate training may be a barrier to pursuing a career in health and social care for some sections of the population here.  I am currently exploring with my officials possible future changes to the financial support provided to students on the pre-registration healthcare programmes commissioned by my Department. I anticipate that the associated process of policy development will provide an opportunity to assess any financial barriers to individuals commencing careers in health and social care.In addition, my Department is also exploring opportunities to expand training and recruitment pathways into registered healthcare professions beyond existing pre-registration training routes.  Ongoing work in this area includes the commissioning of pre-registration nursing and social work education at the Open University and the proposed development of a pilot public sector apprenticeship in diagnostic radiography. It is hoped this pilot will enable an assessment to be made of the suitability of apprenticeships to the HSC more generally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his Department’s assessment of the factors that have contributed to the increased reliance on an international workforce by Health and Social Care Trusts in recent years; and (ii) to detail any measures being taken to address domestic workforce shortages that have led to such reliance.
answer_text: International recruitment is a key avenue in addressing our workforce resource issues and ensuring that the health & social care service can deliver the safe and effective service for patients. The international recruits who arrive to work here across our HSC system provide an immensely valuable contribution to the delivery of health and social care services, let alone to our communities. My Department’s vision for the health and social care workforce is outlined in the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People.  The Strategy was published in May 2018 following detailed engagement with stakeholders from across the health and social care system with the aim of ‘meeting our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce’.The multi-faceted approach to workforce development outlined in the Strategy includes effective workforce planning, commissioning appropriate levels of pre- and post-registration training, better attraction and recruitment of staff, developing alternative training pathways into a career in health and social care such as apprenticeships, reducing agency dependency, developing initiatives to improve staff retention and provision of effective staff health and wellbeing services.This strategic approach to workforce development has supported a 17.2% increase in whole time equivalent staff directly employed across the HSC since the Strategy was published.  This amounts to 9,786 additional whole time equivalent staff across the HSC, including 989 (+23.6%) more medical and dental staff, 2,891 (+19.1%) more registered nurses and midwives and 1,928 (+23.7%) more professional and technical staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how Health and Social Care Trusts ensure that all staff, including those recruited from different countries, are trained to engage appropriately with non-verbal patients to prevent communication shutdown and support ongoing mental stimulation.
answer_text: Health and Social Services are provided in accordance with a person’s care plan, aligned to their assessed need. Care plans contain all necessary information to enable staff to meet the needs of the person. If there are tasks that require specific professional input, this will be clearly stated. All staff must familiarise themselves with a person’s care plan, prior to working with that person. If specialist communication methods are required, these will be clearly outlined within the care plan, with instructions to support staff to communicate with that person. Staff who regularly work in environments with individuals with complex communication needs, undertake additional training in communication methods, such as Makaton. Speech and language professionals also provide support to staff in all service areas, to optimise communication with people who have complex needs. Additional training is available to support staff if required. Hospital passports have been implemented in Northern Ireland to support people who have communication needs relay their wishes. The Hospital passport ensures that within the busy and complex environment of the hospital, communication can continue to be optimised. Health and Social Care Trusts do not differentiate between staff based on their country of origin.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children that were unable to secure a funded pre-school place in each of the last five academic years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for administering the pre-school admissions process, as well as securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas.  The EA has advised that at the end of stage two of the pre-school admissions process for each year, the number of children that had not been offered a funded pre-school place in a setting of their parents’ preference is as set out below. However, sufficient provision was available in local areas to accommodate each child and children who did not receive the offer of a funded place at the end of stage two could still access one; the EA provides details of pre-school settings with places still available to parents at the end of stage two for this purpose.Parliamentary Constituency2024-252023-242022-232021-222020-21Belfast East00000Belfast North00000Belfast South****8Belfast West00000East Antrim00000East Londonderry00***Fermanagh & South Tyrone*000*Foyle00000Lagan Valley*0**0Mid Ulster*****Newry & Armagh**0**North Antrim0**0*North Down00000South Antrim*000*South Down00000Strangford00000Upper Bann00**13West Tyrone00**8TOTAL7511940* denotes fewer than 5 pupils suppressed due to sensitive nature and potential identification of individual pupils

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any steps being taken to ensure the financial and operational sustainability of voluntary pre-school providers; and (ii) how their viability compares to statutory providers.
answer_text: Since my appointment as Education Minister, I have significantly increased funding for non-statutory pre-school providers..        In August 2024 I approved the payment of a one off non-consolidated stabilisation payment of £100 per pupil as part of a package of additional measures to support the early years and childcare sectors.  I also approved two increases to the Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) funding rate for non-statutory settings effective from September 2024.  The most recent uplift increased the rate to £2,550 per pupil per year and represents the largest single increase in the non-statutory PSEP funding rate that my Department has ever made.  It recognises the vital role which non-statutory providers play within the programme.  As a result of these increases an average setting with 16 funded places will have received approximately £8,000 additional funding in the 2024/25 academic year.Should any non-statutory setting consider its funded PSEP places to be at risk due to financial or other pressures they can contact the EA PEG to discuss their concerns and, if appropriate, will be supported to apply for additional funding from the Strategic Support Fund (SSF).   The Common Funding Formula is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to statutory pre-school settings funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements.  The current pupil Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) for statutory pre-school settings is set out in the table below, alongside the current rate for non-statutory providers.  Statutory settings are funded based on the enrolment data on the school census immediately preceding the financial year to which funding relates (i.e. 2024/25 CFF is based upon the October 2023 census).Per-Pupil Funding – 2024-25 Financial Year Part-time (£)Full-time (£)Statutory nursery school2,6924,093Statutory nursery unit2,5233,806Non-statutory2,550N/A

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of basing Published Admissions Numbers for pre-schools on birth rate data, rather than live application demand from families.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), in cooperation with the EA Pre-School Education Group, is responsible for setting Pupil Allocation Numbers for non-statutory pre-school settings.  The EA wrote to providers on 7 February to set out the process for the allocation of initial PANs, as well as the process followed in reviewing those allocations. This process includes consideration of a range of factors, including live application demand. Each year, at least 99.8% children receive the offer of a funded place in a pre-school setting of their parents’ preference by the end of stage two of the pre-school admissions process.  We are currently at the beginning of the second stage of the admissions process for the 2025-26 academic year.  To date, 97% of children have already received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. Should additional funded places be required to meet demand in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings where necessary.Nonetheless, I have requested that the EA review the PAN allocation process so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he is ensuring transparency and accountability in the Education Authority’s decision-making process in relation to Published Admission Number allocations.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), in cooperation with the EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG), is responsible for setting Pupil Allocation Numbers for non-statutory pre-school settings; my Department plays no role in this process.  The EA wrote to providers on 7 February to set out the process for the allocation of initial PANs, as well as the process for reviewing those allocations.  A copy of this letter was previously placed in the Assembly Library in response to AQW 24601/22-27. This process does include specific consideration of rural pre-school settings and those that are the sole provider in a locality. Nonetheless, I have requested that the EA review the PAN allocation process so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department holds the Education Authority to account for ensuring that Published Admission Number decisions are fair and equitable, particularly for rural or voluntary pre-school providers that are the sole local provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), in cooperation with the EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG), is responsible for setting Pupil Allocation Numbers for non-statutory pre-school settings; my Department plays no role in this process.  The EA wrote to providers on 7 February to set out the process for the allocation of initial PANs, as well as the process for reviewing those allocations.  A copy of this letter was previously placed in the Assembly Library in response to AQW 24601/22-27. This process does include specific consideration of rural pre-school settings and those that are the sole provider in a locality. Nonetheless, I have requested that the EA review the PAN allocation process so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the availability of; and (ii) funding for psychological and trauma-informed services for survivors of sexual violence, including access times in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: i) NEXUS NI provides a regional counselling services to adult victims and survivors of sexual abuse and violence, commissioned by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).  Following an initial assessment each individual will be offered up to 12 counselling sessions, based on presenting need.    This provision is currently specifically focussed on supporting individuals who have been victims of sexual abuse and are at the stage of their journey where therapeutic interventions are most likely to have the best outcome.  This service accepts referrals from a number of agents, including, self-referrals, GPs and Health and Social Care Trusts.  NEXUS apply a 13-week target but mostly engage within the first number of weeks.  A longer wait can occur based on the client’s preference for counsellor and geography.The regional Sexual Assault Referral Centre (SARC) for Northern Ireland is based at the Rowan Clinic in Antrim Area Hospital site.  The centre is jointly funded by Department of Health and Department of Justice.  The team delivers a range of support and services 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, to children, young people, women and men who have been sexually abused, assaulted or raped, whether this happened in the past or more recently. The Rowan receives victims following allegation of sexual assault for forensic medical and therapeutic examinations including sexual health examination. For further specialist psychological support victims are referred, if they consent, to Nexus for counselling services.  All those aged under 18 years attending The Rowan are referred to social services. The Rowan will also discuss with the child and their family other referral agencies such as NSPCC, Letting the Future In and referral, if consented, to Child Independent Sexual Violence Advocate, Victim Support Northern Ireland.ii) There is variation across Trusts; with some providing support to survivors of sexual violence either directly, or via contacts with third sector providers, or a combination of direct service provision and contracts; or via more general psychological services.  For example, Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) have services to support service users who have experienced sexual violence. For psychological assessment and intervention, referrals can be made to the most appropriate Psychological Services department within Adult Mental Health. Clinicians are trained and skilled in delivering trauma informed and trauma specific psychological interventions.  Western Health and Social Care Trust’s (WHSCT) Moving On and Recovery team provide trauma informed support and therapy to children and their parents who have experienced sexual abuse, with varying wait times between 0-8 weeks. Psychological services within Adult Mental Health receive referrals and provide psychological therapies for adults who have experienced trauma, to include sexual violence. They are skilled in delivering trauma informed interventions and specific psycho

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether guidance has been issued to schools on how to deal with incidents of peer-on-peer sexual abuse, including online abuse.
answer_text: My department’s Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools – A Guide for Schools and its associated Circular 2022/02 - Children Who Display Harmful Sexual Behaviour have been issued to educational settings to provide advice as to how these matters should be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the reported comments of the Deputy Chief Constable that the PSNI can only deliver the service it is funded to deliver, for her assessment of whether communities are safe with the current numbers of police officers.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how her Department is working with criminal justice partners to address the long-standing disparity between the number of sexual offences reported and those resulting in conviction.
answer_text: Achieving convictions in sexual offence cases can be particularly challenging and can involve complex evidential and legal issues, including more recently, a significant increase in digital material. The bar is high to obtain a conviction and there is often an absence of physical evidence and corroborative witnesses, meaning the evidence frequently comes down to the accounts of the parties involved. However, there is ongoing engagement between my Department and criminal justice partners that is intended to support victims and increase their confidence in the criminal justice system. I anticipate that this work should impact positively on conviction rates. These measures include:research into attrition to understand the scale and the reasons for victims of sexual offences disengaging from the criminal justice process. This research is being conducted in partnership with the PSNI and PPS with an initial report expected soon. The report will be used to inform future policies aimed at reducing attrition and therefore potentially increasing convictions; and,new interventions, including those under the Gillen Review implementation programme, that are helping to support victims and to raise their confidence in the justice system. Examples of these include:the establishment of Remote Evidence Centres (RECs) in Belfast and Craigavon, in addition to the existing NSPCC-led facility in Derry/Londonderry. RECs allow vulnerable witnesses to give their best evidence from a secure and comfortable location and importantly, they help reduce the risk of re-traumatisation by removing the possibility that victims might encounter the defendant or their supporters in or around the courthouse on the day of the trial. In 2024, 344 witnesses (199 adults and 145 children) attended a REC to give evidence. The conviction rate for cases where the witness was in a REC was 91%, with 62% by guilty plea without the need for the victim to give oral evidence;new legislation to protect the anonymity of complainants and victims of serious sexual offences by excluding the public from Crown Court hearings; provision of advocacy support services for high risk victims of domestic and sexual abuse; and,the introduction of the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) service, which my Department launched in April 2021 and the corresponding Children’s SOLA (CSOLA) service which launched in February of this year. These schemes provide free, independent legal advice to complainants in serious sexual offence cases. The adult service has so far provided advice to over 2,000 complainants and since its recent launch, around 200 referrals have already been made to the CSOLA service. Feedback has been overwhelmingly positive, with many service users telling us that without SOLA support they would have not continued with their case. I am also aware that that delay is a major source of stress that contributes to high attrition rates. Significant work is therefore underway to reduce unnecessary delay in t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure all frontline health professionals receive training to recognise and respond appropriately to disclosures of sexual violence or abuse.
answer_text: My Department has implemented several initiatives to ensure that frontline health professionals are adequately trained to recognise and respond appropriately to disclosures of sexual violence or abuse.In September 2024, my Department, in collaboration with the Department of Justice, launched the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031. One of the key priority areas under Pillar 2 (Prevention) of this comprehensive strategy is to build capacity among people working in frontline and public-facing roles to identify and respond effectively to domestic and/or sexual abuse.Health and Social Care Trusts have training pathways which aim to ensure anyone who has contact with people who use Trust services will have an understanding of domestic and sexual abuse to support their engagement and intervention with victims, perpetrators and their children. Those staff working with people and families affected by this issue can avail of more specialist training to equip them to undertake their role. The Permanent Secretary has recently written to the Chief Executives of our ALBs to encourage uptake of the Domestic Abuse Context and Change e-Learning training across Health and Social Care.My Department is currently piloting the Identification and Referral to Improve Safety (IRIS) programme in Belfast and Newry and District GP Federation areas. IRIS is a specialist domestic and sexual abuse training, support and referral programme for primary care. The core elements include training and supporting general practice to identify and refer victims of domestic and sexual abuse to appropriate services. The IRIS pilot has been subject to an evaluation and the findings are being considered. Any future roll-out of IRIS will be subject to funding. Through these initiatives, my Department aims to ensure that frontline health professionals are adequately trained to recognise and respond to disclosures of sexual violence or abuse, thereby providing victims with the necessary support and care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures in place to ensure that survivors of sexual violence, who do not wish to report to police, still have access to advocacy, legal and therapeutic support.
answer_text: Much of the work that my Department is progressing is focused on supporting and improving the experiences of victims of sexual crime within the criminal justice system so that they will have the confidence to come forward and report sexual crime to police.However, I recognise the challenges that many victims of sexual crime face and so my Department does fund a number of services for survivors of sexual violence which are not dependent on the survivor having made a report to police.Core funding of around £1.9M is provided to Victim Support NI who provide emotional support and practical advice to help victims recover from the impact of crime and trauma experienced, even if they do not want to make a report to the police. Victim Support NI works in partnership with a range of organisations and will refer or signpost victims to other specialised services who can provide the necessary support and information victims require, dependant on their individual needs. The Sexual Offences Legal Advisers, (or SOLAs), and Children’s SOLAs are solicitors employed by Victim Support NI who can provide qualified legal advice to all victims of serious sexual offences, and in the case of children and young people, their families, either prior to or without making a report to police. The SOLAs can provide support and information on victims’ legal rights and options and, should they wish to go on to make a report to police, what they might expect on their journey through the criminal justice process. My Department, with PSNI, jointly funds the provision of advocacy support services delivered by ASSIST NI. It is a domestic and sexual abuse advocacy support service available to victims of sexual abuse crime who primarily are engaging with the criminal justice system. ASSIST NI will provide advocacy support to victims who have self-referred to The Rowan Sexual Assault Referral Centre and are yet to engage with the Criminal Justice System. ASSIST NI provides trauma-informed guidance, support and information and works with other service providers, advocating on the victim’s behalf, tailoring the service to the victim’s needs.In addition to the services that my Department funds, there are other supports and services, including counselling and therapeutic services, that are provided by other Departments, including the Department of Health, that victims of sexual crime may be able to avail of.  The Department of Health jointly funds the 24-hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline with the Department of Justice and the Department for Communities (NEXUS NI is the current provider). The Helpline is a confidential, Freephone service available 24/7. The Department of Health provides a range of therapeutic supports and investigative services for children and adults. While these support services do not fall within the responsibilities of the Department of Justice, nonetheless, the services that my department funds, such as Victim Support NI and ASSIST NI, can help to signpost victims

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24166/22-27, to detail (i) the impact that the regional day procedure centres and single waiting lists have had on reducing waiting times across Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) how his Department is monitoring the experience of patients who may need to travel further for treatment.
answer_text: The regional day procedure centres deliver capacity across a number of high-volume specialties, with this capacity accessed by patients from all Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts. Whilst the ongoing implementation of the Epic system makes it difficult to directly compare to pre-Epic data for those Trusts now live on the system, comparisons can be made for the Southern and Western Trusts. Official statistics show general daycase waiting list reductions within those Trusts as at 31 December 2024, compared with 31 December 2021. This includes a 35% reduction in General Surgery, and a 6% reduction in Urology. Both of these specialties have access to regional day procedure centre capacity. Monitoring of patient experience is common practice within HSC Trusts. As an example of how this is conducted at the regional day procedure centres, Lagan Valley Hospital use the Care Opinion platform to collate feedback from patients. The majority of patients who are treated at Lagan Valley come from other Trust areas, and have travelled to access treatment. This feedback is analysed and shared with staff. The feedback received is generally positive, with patients indicating they are happy to travel, particularly when it results in receiving treatment faster than may otherwise be the case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) target waiting times; and (ii) current average waiting times for breast screening services, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) advise that data on the waiting times for breast screening services is not collected.For screening mammography, PHA measures the round length, which is the proportion of eligible women, whose date of first offered appointment is less than, or equal to 36 months, from their previous episode. The acceptable standard is greater than, or equal, to 90.0%.The most recently available NI data for the round length, is from Quarter 3 of 2024/25:Breast screening unitQuarter 3 (2024/25) round length figures (%) Annual figures (2023/24) for round length  figures (%)Belfast (covers women resident in both Belfast and South Eastern Trusts)10099.1Northern10093.5Southern81*99.8Western83*98.3*While PHA has provided quarterly figures as the latest figures available, official reporting is on an annual basis. The most recent annual report is for 2023/24. For screening assessment following abnormal findings on a screening mammogram, the standard measured is the proportion of women referred for assessment whose date of first offered appointment at an assessment centre is less than or equal to 3 weeks (less than or equal to 21 calendar days) from attendance for the screening mammogram. The acceptable standard is greater than or equal to 95%.The most recently available NI data for screening assessment is from Quarter 3 of 2024/25:Breast screening unit%Belfast (covers women resident in both Belfast and South Eastern Trusts)97Northern100Southern100Western100

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to rebuild public confidence in health and social care services following long delays in treatment and diagnosis.
answer_text: It is understandable that public confidence in Health and Social Care services has been eroded due to the unacceptable waiting lists here, however, I am determined to rebuild that confidence.  My Department is striving to deliver improvements in elective care services here and the roadmap to do so is detailed in the Elective Care Framework, which focuses on a wide range of measures and initiatives needed to improve and reform elective care services and address those long delays in treatment and diagnosis.  Positive achievements have already been made, through a number of initiatives, including; the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and mega clinics. As I outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, following the Executive’s ringfencing of £215m in this year’s Health budget for waiting list activities, I plan to introduce a package of measures to reduce waiting list backlogs including; targeting long waiting patients, expansion of elective services in Primary Care, investment in Mega Clinics and a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme.Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix and it will not be resolved in a year or two. Investment at this level will need to be sustained for up to five years to bring hospital waiting times down to acceptable levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for a timeline for the full implementation of single regional waiting lists across all specialities; and (iI) whether interim performance targets are in place during the roll-out.
answer_text: The implementation of single regional waiting lists is a key component of our strategy to streamline patient care and reduce waiting times across Northern Ireland. The implementation of the Encompass programme has provided the opportunity to develop single, regional waiting lists which will ensure patients are assessed in chronological order on a regional basis, rather than within their local Trust area. This will allow patients to be assessed in the next available clinic slot, which may be in any Trust, mitigating Trust specific service pressures that can impact waiting times. The specific timeline for the full implementation of these single, regional waiting lists will be dependent on the rollout and embedding of Encompass across all Trusts. The concept of a single regional waiting list is a part of broader initiatives to improve the efficiency of our health service delivery. There are examples of regional waiting lists already in place.  For example, on 8 May 2025, a new single regional waiting list for breast assessment launched. This initiative aims to allow patients across Northern Ireland to access the earliest available appointment, regardless of their Trust area. The new regional booking system equalises waiting times across the region. Lessons learned from introducing the regional breast single waiting list will help inform the planning of any future initiatives.Further, there are single waiting lists for specialist services including spinal surgery, vascular surgery, neurosurgery and paediatrics. Work has also progressed to establish single waiting lists in subspecialty areas including bladder outflow procedures, complex stone surgery and penile cancer in the urology specialty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is supporting Health and Social Care Trusts to increase treatment capacity in high-demand specialities such as orthopaedics, dermatology and gynaecology.
answer_text: I am committed to addressing the challenges faced by Health and Social Care Trusts in managing high-demand specialties such as orthopaedics, dermatology, and gynaecology. My Department has implemented several strategies to increase treatment capacity and reduce waiting times in these critical areas.Non-Recurrent Funding for Waiting List Initiatives (WLI): As outlined in my statement to the Assembly on 6 May 2025, £215m has been ringfenced for Health in this year’s budget to be invested across three areas for waiting list activities: to expand core capacity to reduce the red flag/time critical gap (£85m); increase core routine capacity (£80m); and, non-recurrent funding (£50m) to start tackling the backlog of patients waiting.The package of measures which will be taken forward to tackle waiting lists include targeting long waiting patients, expansion of elective services in primary care, investment in mega clinics and a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme.The package of measures which will be taken forward to tackle waiting lists include targeting long waiting patients, expansion of elective services in primary care, investment in mega clinics and a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme.Inter-Trust Patient Transfers: To facilitate the equalisation and reduction of waiting times, my department continues to support the inter-Trust transfer of patients. This approach allows patients to receive treatment more swiftly by utilising capacities available across Trusts, where possible, rather than patients being delayed waiting for procedures in hospitals of high demand.Department of Health Oversight: The Department meets quarterly with Trust colleagues to review performance against specific targets and pathways, including those for high-demand specialties. This group plays a crucial role in maximising efficiencies within current service provision and ensuring that Trusts are adhering to performance optimisation plans.Implementation of GIRFT Recommendations:Following the "Getting It Right First Time" (GIRFT) reports, such as the one for gynaecology, my Department has established forums with representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency (PHA), and General Practitioners (GPs). These forums oversee the implementation of recommendations aimed at improving service delivery and patient pathways in specialties like gynaecology and orthopaedic.Support for Patient Self-Management: Recognising the importance of patient well-being while on waiting lists, the updated Elective Care Framework (ECF) outlines the need for policies that support patients in managing their conditions and overall health. This framework is particularly relevant as we develop strategies to manage waiting times in high-demand specialties.Consideration of where is the most appropriate place to manage patientsMy Department is working with between primary and secondary care to consider what patients are best managed in which arena. This can be seen in dermatology where

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for Communities, since taking office, in relation to the lack of financial support for postgraduate students in Northern Ireland, particularly those who are ineligible for Universal Credit due to full-time study and are also unable to access maintenance loans.
answer_text: My officials engage with their counterparts across other departments on a regular basis. This includes discussions on a number of topics, including how the benefits and skills systems interact. Plans are currently being developed for a proposed review of higher education funding, including financial support to students.  Officials are due to brief me in coming weeks regarding the review. I am always happy to engage with other executive Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the impact of ongoing work coach requirements on full-time university students with caring responsibilities; and (ii) whether his Department has considered introducing greater flexibility within Universal Credit to support these individuals.
answer_text: Universal Credit work coaches are trained to provide a supportive, tailored service to all claimants. Full time students who are eligible to claim Universal Credit should discuss their caring responsibilities with their work coach who will develop and agree a claimant commitment with them that is both flexible and reflective of the demands of their caring responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support in place through Universal Credit to encourage mature students with caring responsibilities to pursue higher education and, ultimately, move into employment.
answer_text: Universal Credit staff are trained to provide tailored support to all claimants including mature students with caring responsibilities.  They will answer their queries, give them information about additional training and/or education to support them into the workplace, and will signpost them to seek advice from Advice NI. Alongside this they will tell them about the additional financial support available for the Department’s Adviser Discretion Fund.  This is a non-repaybale grant to help claimant’s complete additional training to support them into employment/self-employment for such things as clothing, work equipment, childcare costs and training costs if they are eligible for support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any financial support available for mature students with children who do not qualify for a student loan; and (ii) whether she will consider expanding access to targeted hardship funds.
answer_text: Mature students who are undertaking an eligible full-time undergraduate course but find themselves ineligible for student loan support may still be able to apply for targeted support such as the childcare grant, parents’ learning allowance or adult dependants’ grant. Disabled Students’ Allowance may also be available to mature students regardless of whether they are studying an undergraduate or postgraduate full-time or part-time course.In relation to the funding the Department provides to institutions to support students facing financial hardship, maximum support of up to £3,500 is currently available for eligible full-time or part-time undergraduate and postgraduate students. The Department sets out the terms and conditions for this funding and from academic year 2022/23 removed priority groups from the terms and conditions, the intention being to allow the institutions greater flexibility when awarding this support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with (i) universities; (ii) further education colleges; and (iii) student unions to understand the impact of Universal Credit on mature students.
answer_text: My officials engage with the universities through the Universal Credit front line adviser’s forum which meet on a quarterly basis to provide the advice sector and the Universities with an opportunity to raise any student related concerns or issues they are experiencing.  Universal Credit also has an escalation route in place providing contact details that is regularly updated and shared with the Advice Sector, Law Centre and other organisations who form part of the Department’s Trusted Partner Forum.Regional Managers engage on a monthly basis with colleges as part of the local council labour market partnerships and will readily provide information and answer any queries relating to concerns from all students, this includes mature students.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the delayed review of general surgery at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: Over the past months, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust has continued to develop plans for the reconfiguration of general surgical services across its hospital sites and has been developing and appraising options for a more compliant, sustainable surgical configuration in the context of the Department’s Review of General Surgery.The public consultation on the Trust Review of General Surgery Service has now concluded, and it was the intention of the Northern Trust to bring the outcome and a proposed way forward to their Trust Board later this month.The Trust has been liaising closely with colleagues in the Department of Health throughout and following the consultation process to look at the impact of any proposed service change on staff, patients and the wider community.  It is important that all views and responses have been considered and implications across the service included before the final recommendation of this review is completed.Northern Health and Social Care Trust is finalising the work on the wider implications of any proposed service change and anticipated impacts to ensure all feedback and implications have been considered.  Northern Trust anticipates that the consultation outcome and associated proposals will be brought to the Trust Board in May for agreement.  Following this, the Trust will bring forward its proposals to the Department for consideration and a recommendation on any proposed service change will be brought forward for decision.The final decision on any permanent change to general surgery services rests with me as Minister of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any cross-departmental work being carried out to improve young people's understanding of nutrition and healthy cooking.
answer_text: My Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) work across departments and agencies to improve young people's understanding of nutrition and healthy cooking through nutrition training and education programmes such as ‘Nutrition matters for the early years.’ A range of community-based nutrition education programmes, such as ‘Cook It!’, ‘Food Values’ and ‘Food and Health Essentials’ are commissioned to increase knowledge and understanding of healthy eating and develop cooking skills, building both confidence and competence. My department also works closely with the Food Standards Agency (FSA) which has responsibility for the development, implementation and evaluation of dietary health policy in Northern Ireland. As part of this, the FSA works with other government departments across the UK to promote dietary guidelines through the Eatwell Guide. The Eatwell Guide is included in the school curriculum in Northern Ireland and helps children learn how to achieve a healthy balanced diet. The FSA supports this learning through the provision of free resources for schools and community groups.The Department of Education chairs a cross-departmental ‘Food in Schools’ forum which provides strategic leadership and guidance on the joint Northern Ireland Food in Schools policy, nutritional standards for school meals and supports the inclusion of nutrition in the school curriculum. Officials from my Department, PHA, the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, and the FSA, alongside other stakeholders, work together as members of this Forum. My Department, through the PHA, and the Department of Education jointly fund a Food In Schools coordinator to carry out a programme of work to support schools to create a healthy food environment. The PHA works closely with DE through the Food in Schools Forum to implement the ‘Health Food for Healthy Outcomes’ policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the long-term public health costs associated with poor nutrition in young people due to over-consumption of fast food.
answer_text: A 2024 report from the World Health Organization, based on data from 44 European countries, revealed a concerning picture of dietary habits, with a particular focus on the decline in healthy eating behaviours and the rise of unhealthy choices. Fewer than 2 in 5 young people (38%) eat fruit or vegetables daily, and these figures decline with age (from 45% of 11-year-olds to 33% of 15-year-olds for fruit, and from 40% to 36% for vegetables). More than half of young people reported eating neither fruit nor vegetables every day (56% of boys and 51% of girls aged 15).
