The report also reveals a concerning link between socioeconomic status and unhealthy dietary habits, with adolescents from lower-income families more likely to consume sugary drinks (18% versus 15%) and less likely to eat fruits (32% versus 46%) and vegetables (32% versus 54%) daily.For Northern Ireland, the NISRA Young Persons’ Behaviours and Attitude Survey 2022-23 found that over three fifths of young people (63%) thought they should be eating 5 or more portions of fruit and vegetables each day to be healthy. However, only 16% recorded that they usually eat 5 or more portions of fruit or vegetables each day. The Public Health Agency is currently working on a whole systems approach to obesity prevention in Northern Ireland as obesity is a complex chronic condition which is cause by multiple factors such as, the environments we live and work in along with psychosocial and genetic variants. There is an acknowledgement that inequalities and poor nutrition in young people contribute to overall inequalities in health and vice versa. The annual cost of obesity to society in Northern Ireland is estimated at £500m annually.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions being taken to (i) reduce obesity; and (ii) improve dietary health among young people, particularly in relation to regular consumption of fast food.
answer_text: The new Healthy Futures strategic framework has been developed to enhance obesity prevention efforts, informed by research and stakeholder consultations. This framework emphasises a whole system approach, targeting specific groups and addressing health inequalities. It aims to prevent and manage obesity through education, treatment services, and a person-centred approach. The Public Health Agency (PHA) works across departments and agencies to improve young people's understanding of nutrition and healthy cooking through nutrition training and education programmes such as ‘Nutrition matters for the early years.’ A range of community-based nutrition education programmes, such as ‘Cook It!’, ‘Food Values’ and ‘Food and Health Essentials’ are commissioned to increase knowledge and understanding of healthy eating and develop cooking skills, building both confidence and competence. The PHA also commissions an Early Years Obesity Prevention Programme which provides obesity prevention training to Health and Social Care Trusts and SureStart staff across Northern Ireland. The Department of Education chairs a cross-departmental ‘Food in Schools’ forum which provides strategic leadership and guidance on the joint Northern Ireland Food in Schools policy, nutritional standards for school meals and supports the inclusion of nutrition in the school curriculum. Officials from my Department, PHA, the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, and the FSA, alongside other stakeholders, work together as members of this Forum. My Department, through the PHA, and the Department of Education jointly fund a Food In Schools coordinator to carry out a programme of work to support schools to create a healthy food environment. The PHA works closely with DE through the Food in Schools Forum to implement the ‘Health Food for Healthy Outcomes’ policy.The Active School Travel Programme is jointly funded by PHA and DfI and has delivered an increase on the number of children completing physical activity for at least 60 minutes each day from 25% at baseline (Autumn/Winter 2023) to 35% after one academic year of engagement (2024/2025).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans to expand access to practical cooking and nutrition education in post-primary schools.
answer_text: I have commissioned a strategic review of the curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan, which will focus on the purpose, design, specification and implementation of the Curriculum and how we can learn from high performing education systems to bring about necessary reform. Ms Crehan is due to provide her report to my Department in May 2025, at which point I will consider the recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider funding community-led healthy eating initiatives, including free basic cookery classes, as part of preventative health strategies.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency currently commissions and funds a number of community-led healthy eating initiatives which include ‘Nutrition matters for the early years,’ ‘Cook It!,’ ‘Food Values,’ and ‘Food and Health Essentials.’ These programmes are contracted to increase knowledge and understanding of healthy eating and develop cooking skills, building both confidence and competence. Safefood funds the Community Food Initiative across the island of Ireland. Since 2010, over 50,000 people have taken part in Community Food Initiative activities gaining practical tips in food and healthy eating, healthier shopping, and cooking skills for healthy eating. They support local initiatives with a range of online, video and print resources to encourage people to develop healthy eating skills and knowledge. The 2025-2028 programme is funding 28 community initiatives. The Food Standards Agency (FSA) has a Making Food Better programme which supports food businesses to make the food environment healthier by reducing calories, saturated fat, sugar, and salt in the food they produce sell or serve, reduce portion size, provide nutritional information, and deliver responsible promotions. The FSA also collaborates with partners to implement nutritional standards in Health and Social Care, public sector, and local council settings to ensure healthier options are available for staff and visitors. The FSA provides guidance for a range of food businesses, including fast food outlets, on providing and promoting healthier food and drink. The FSA recently commissioned research to investigate the availability of fast-food outlets and grocery retailers in Northern Ireland, and their proximity to secondary schools which will help to inform the FSA’s work on the food environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to increase the number of optometrists with independent prescribing qualifications, given the success of the NIPEARS Plus pilot in treating 90 per cent of patients without the need for referral back to Eye Casualty.
answer_text: The small but growing number of primary, and indeed secondary, care optometrists with independent prescribing (IP) qualifications has undoubtedly helped to build capacity across the wider ophthalmic workforce.Optometrists with an independent prescribing (IP) qualification are able to clinically assess a patient, establish a diagnosis, determine the clinical management required and prescribe where necessary, freeing capacity in both general practice and ED/Eye Casualty.In recognition of this, my Department has made recurrent funding available to facilitate the necessary clinical placements (22 sessions per trainee) for up to twelve IPs in-training annually, with these placements currently taking place under supervision of consultant ophthalmologists across Belfast and Western Health and Social Care Trust ophthalmology services.This funded arrangement has been in place since 2016 and there are currently 52 community optometry IPs listed with SPPG across the region.The limiting factors in increasing the number of optometrists with independent prescribing qualifications centre on the number of trainees that universities with approved qualifications can enrol, and the physical and clinical capacity which Trust ophthalmology departments have to facilitate trainee placements.With respect to availability of suitably-qualified supervisors for clinical placements, it is anticipated that this might be improved by recent changes to Rules for this requirement by the General Optical Council. Trainees upon or shortly after admission to an approved qualification must have identified a suitably experienced and qualified designated prescribing practitioner (DPP) who has agreed to supervise their 90 hours of learning and experience in practice. This is a shift from the current requirement of a designated medical practitioner (DMP), in line with other regulators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the cost savings to the health service from the NIPEARS Plus pilot, which utilised independent prescribing optometrists to manage acute eye conditions in the community.
answer_text: A novel NI PEARS (Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service) Plus service, which aimed to build on the successful regional core NI PEARS service, was piloted within the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust (BHSCT) catchment area, from April-December 2023, with a brief extension into 2024 to support Trust with roll-out of encompass.The service aims included making optimum use of clinically-skilled primary care optometrists, facilitation of appropriate access to eye care for patients with specific conditions rapidly and close to home, and to reduce the number of patients requiring specialist secondary care ophthalmology services.A total of 841 patients accessed the service during the pilot, generating 1,331 optometry primary care appointments, including follow-up. BHSCT triaged out 628 of the patients, representing 10.6% of 5,914 referrals into BHSCT Eye Casualty during the pilot period (the remainder being primary care intra-professional referrals).Those patients managed to completion in primary care released 650 appointments in in Eye Casualty, excluding follow up appointments, in the 32-week period of the pilot. This would equate to the release of approx. 1000 appointments annually in Belfast HSC Trust (RVH) Eye Casualty and approx. 330 appointments in the Western HSC Trust (Altnagelvin) Eye Casualty annually.The pilot cost a total of £85,600 in clinical fees (£90 for first appointment, £20 per follow-up) to community optometry providers. This sum does not, however, reflect administration, validation, and evaluation costs for the pilot, which were absorbed at Trust and SPPG level prior to, throughout, and after the pilot.The current absence of tariff makes cost-saving analysis challenging, although ongoing work around Patient Level Information and Costing System (PLICs) should help to make future comparative benchmarks realistic.Preliminary PLICs data demonstrates a total of 13,638 attendances at Eye Casualty in the period 2022/23 with a maximum 94 patients per day and an average of 40.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to address the occupational health risks for community optometrists identified during the NIPEARS Plus pilot, including the absence of cover for needlestick injuries.
answer_text: The occupational health risk to optometrists identified during the NIPEARS Plus pilot were in relation to the potential for needle stick injuries during a removal of foreign bodies, which in many cases require the use of a small needle.To date there have been a number of barriers for Trust Occupational Health services including optometrists in their needle stick injury service.  These include; capacity, the absence of a Service Level Agreement and the absence of the necessary governance arrangements. A Regional Review of Occupational Health recommended a review of the Service Level Agreement to Primary Care. This review is underway and will include consideration of future provision of the service to optometrists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered rolling out the NIPEARS Plus pilot regionally, following its successful implementation in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A novel NI PEARS (Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service) Plus service, which aimed to build on the successful regional core NI PEARS service, was piloted within the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust (BHSCT) catchment area, from April-December 2023, with a brief extension into 2024 to support Trust with roll-out of encompass.The service aims included making optimum use of clinically-skilled primary care optometrists, facilitation of appropriate access to eye care for patients with specific conditions rapidly and close to home, and to reduce the number of patients requiring specialist secondary care ophthalmology services.A total of 841 patients accessed the service during the pilot, generating 1,331 optometry primary care appointments, including follow-up. BHSCT triaged out 628 of the patients, representing 10.6% of 5,914 referrals into BHSCT Eye Casualty during the pilot period (the remainder being primary care intra-professional referrals).Pilot evaluation shows access, patient experience and clinical outcomes to be strong, with the capacity-releasing aim achieved and clinical safety non-inferior.Any regional roll-out will need to address recommendations outlined in the pilot evaluation, including requirements for improved booking and administration systems and protocols, establishment of a primary care optometry needlestick injury policy, contractual agreement over fees and value for money, and training and support for the primary care optometry providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on efforts to align Northern Ireland with the rest of the UK in ensuring that people on Universal Credit can access their entitlement to NHS eyecare.
answer_text: Work on the proposed changes to the legislation is a key priority for my Department and I am committed to ensuring that this work is completed without undue delay. 		This is a complex area, as it is not the case that in GB all Universal Credit (UC) recipients are automatically eligible for help with health costs. 				The public consultation on the proposed eligibility criteria for passporting UC recipients closed on 10 March 2025. I welcome the openness, feedback and engagement from all who have participated in the consultation. 				Officials are currently analysing the responses and compiling a Consultation Summary Report which will be published on the Departmental website in the coming weeks. It is intended that the legislative process will be completed by summer.				My aim is to ensure that those most in need automatically receive help to access these very important health services. 				Whilst the legislative process is ongoing, an interim measure remains in place for UC recipients who need financial assistance with their health costs. This is via the Health Service Low Income Scheme (LIS). This scheme was already established before UC, and is to help those who had a low income but were not a recipient of eligible benefit for Help with Health Costs.  It is a paper-based scheme, processed by DfC, where UC claimants are required to apply annually to gain a potential exemption or remission from their health costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department ensures that individuals beyond the eligible screening age are made aware of self-referral options where available.
answer_text: There are currently three cancer screening programmes in Northern Ireland for asymptomatic individuals: Bowel, Breast and Cervical.  Age cut-offs in cancer screening programmes aim to maximize the benefits of screening while minimizing potential harms. If a person has any concerns or symptoms, they are advised to consult their GP immediately.  There is no self-referral option available outside of the current eligible screening age for bowel cancer screening in Northern Ireland between 60-74 years old.   In respect of the cervical screening programme, eligible women aged between 25-64 years old are invited routinely to participate in cervical screening. Women with previous abnormal results who require ongoing surveillance past the age of 64, will remain within the screening programme until they can be safely discharged. While there is no self-referral process, any cervical screening samples taken by GPs from women outside the screening age group will still be processed and reported by the laboratory. Women over the age of 70 years old are not routinely invited to breast screening but at their last screening appointment within the eligible age cohort of 50-70 years old, women are advised that they can self-refer for screening by contacting their local breast screening unit: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/breast-screening-overview#toc-1. Women are given an over 70s leaflet and a reminder card, with additional leaflets and posters being made available to the Breast Screening units to display and in other locations e.g. GP surgeries. The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee, makes recommendations to the four nations in relation to screening matters, to include age thresholds. The UK NSC routinely reviews the evidence available for the population screening programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the data collected on cancer diagnosis outcomes in individuals who fall outside the age eligibility for screening programmes.
answer_text: Data collected on cancer diagnosis outcomes in individuals who fall outside the age eligibility for screening programmes is outlined in the NI Cancer Registry (NICR) “Routes to Diagnosis” report published on https://www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/CancerInformation/RoutestoDiagnosisReport/This report, published in 2024 as part of the work being progressed through the Cancer Strategy NI 2022-32 aims to provide an indication of the key event in each cancer patient’s pathway that most directly led to their cancer diagnosis. The “Routes to Diagnosis” report provides data on patient classification at diagnosis, including gender, age, stage and a statistical analysis of trends over time. Additionally, the report provides data on survival statistics, with comparative data provided for survival based on diagnosis route, along with detailed analysis for each tumour site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for age cut-offs in cancer screening programmes, including breast, cervical and bowel screening.
answer_text: The age thresholds for population screening programmes are based on recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific advisory committee which advises the four nations, on all aspects of screening. The UK NSC regularly reviews its recommendations on screening as new research and evidence becomes available. When considering who to screen, the benefits of offering a screening programme are weighed up against the possible harms. The UK NSC only recommends screening when it has concluded that the evidence shows that the benefits of screening outweigh the potential harms, for each age cohort.  There is ongoing research to examine the benefits and harms of extending breast cancer screening to women aged 71-73 and 47-49 years of age (the age X trial). The first report from this trial is expected in 2026. Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to review the upper age limits for cancer screening services, in line with changes in population health and life expectancy.
answer_text: The age thresholds for population screening programmes are based on recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific advisory committee which advises the four nations, on all aspects of screening. The UK NSC regularly reviews its recommendations on screening as new research and evidence becomes available. When considering who to screen, the benefits of offering a screening programme are weighed up against the possible harms. The UK NSC only recommends screening when it has concluded that the evidence shows that the benefits of screening outweigh the potential harms, for each age cohort.  There is ongoing research to examine the benefits and harms of extending breast cancer screening to women aged 71-73 and 47-49 years of age (the age X trial). The first report from this trial is expected in 2026. Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether current age thresholds for cancer screening services reflect the healthcare needs of older people.
answer_text: The age thresholds for population screening programmes are based on recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific advisory committee which advises the four nations, on all aspects of screening. The UK NSC regularly reviews its recommendations on screening as new research and evidence becomes available. When considering who to screen, the benefits of offering a screening programme are weighed up against the possible harms. The UK NSC only recommends screening when it has concluded that the evidence shows that the benefits of screening outweigh the potential harms, for each age cohort.  There is ongoing research to examine the benefits and harms of extending breast cancer screening to women aged 71-73 and 47-49 years of age (the age X trial). The first report from this trial is expected in 2026. Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will undertake an urgent update of Dial-a-Lift and Disability Action Transport Scheme service maps, to ensure they reflect current demographic needs, particularly in rural parts of East Londonderry.
answer_text: A phased review of Community Transport is currently underway. As part of phase 2 of the review, the existing scheme criteria will be considered including factors such as operational areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the sustainability of community transport services in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether she will consider establishing a multi-year funding model for Rural Community Transport Partnerships.
answer_text: Services provided by community transport provide a lifeline for individuals and communities, connecting people to essential services that underpin their health, well-being, and independence across both our rural and urban communities.  I recognise the important role they play for those who have limited transport opportunities and the value that users, their families and their wider support networks attach to these services.I recently launched my Department’s Draft 2025-26 Budget Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) consultation.  I have not taken any final decisions on how the draft budget will be spent.  I am not yet in a position to establish a multi-year funding model in respect of community transport schemes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will commit to facilitating cross-departmental collaboration on Community Transport provision as part of the Executive’s work on older people, following the inclusion of ageing in the Programme for Government 2024-2027 priorities.
answer_text: The PfG commits to collaborative working across all Executive Departments.Our Executive colleague, John O’Dowd MLA, when Infrastructure Minister, along with other executive colleagues Minister Nesbitt MLA and Minister Muir MLA met on 9 September 2024 to establish a Community Transport Cross Departmental Working Group. Through this Group, officials are exploring what options are there for community transport to support the work/objectives of other departments and how it can meet the needs of users, including older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with the Department for Infrastructure to develop a regional strategy for non-emergency patient transport; and (ii) whether community transport operators will be included in future hospital discharge planning.
answer_text: I met with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on 9 September 2024 to discuss the Cross-Departmental Working Group on Community Transport. As part of this work, I have asked officials from my Department to undertake a review of the existing non-emergency transport policy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland, with the aim of completing this work in the 2025/26 financial year. It is important to note that the provision of transport services for the discharge of patients is a matter for individual Health and Social Care Trusts, where there is a defined medical or financial need. Under the existing non-emergency transport policy, Trusts have access to a wide range of transport options, including the use of community transport services, as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any plans to review the current driver licensing requirements for community transport operators, particularly in relation to Category D1 licensing; and (ii) whether consideration will be given to aligning with the approach in Great Britain to support recruitment and retention.
answer_text: My officials have previously engaged with the sector on this issue. The current licensing arrangements for drivers of not-for-profit community transport providers are a legal requirement in support of road safety and at this point in time, I have no plans to change them

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps she is taking to ensure that surplus public land is prioritised for social and affordable housing development.
answer_text: My Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) identifies Local Development Plans (LDPs) as the primary planning vehicle to meet social and affordable housing needs identified through a Housing Needs Assessment and Housing Market Analysis. It indicates that LDPs should do this by zoning land for social/affordable housing, or by indicating, through key site requirements, where a proportion of a site may be required for social and affordable housing. This will not preclude other sites coming forward through the development management process.   My officials continue to work closely with housing officials in the Department for Communities, Council officers, and the NIHE to ensure that collectively, we can facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality homes to meet the needs of everyone.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of instances recorded in the last five years where developers have failed to deliver social housing units agreed under Section 76 planning agreements of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: Councils here are also responsible for determining the majority of planning applications in their areas. The enforcement of planning control and section 76 planning agreements is therefore primarily a matter for each individual council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has considered introducing planning restrictions on the conversion of residential properties into short-term holiday lets in high-demand areas, such as coastal towns, in order to protect the availability of long-term housing.
answer_text: A cross departmental group DfC, DfI & DfE has very recently been established to consider this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the process for establishing a veterinary school in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Question 5 AQO 1814/22-27As autonomous bodies, the universities have the discretion to offer any course, including veterinary subjects, from within existing student numbers and funding allocations.There is currently no opportunity at the local Higher Education Institutions to study for a Veterinary Medicine degree.  Should a specific proposal for a veterinary school be put forward I am open to working in partnership with all relevant stakeholders to consider the implications of such a proposal.  Any proposal would, of course, need to be considered by all those who have stake in the delivery of veterinary medicine here including the relevant professional bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the specific statutory duties of her Department in ensuring local councils enforce the 20 per cent social housing requirement in planning approvals, including whether her Department has a legal obligation to intervene if a council fails to enforce compliance; and (ii) any instances where her Department has taken action under the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 in the last five years.
answer_text: The enforcement of conditions attached to the approval of planning permission and any local development plan policies that guided that decision is a matter for the local planning authority. Sections 139 and 151of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, provides my Department with the power to issue enforcement and stop notices, however only after consulting the appropriate council. My Department’s reserve powers are not an alternative to the councils much broader enforcement powers and it is only envisaged that these powers should be used in exceptional circumstances. In respect to enforcement action taken in the context of delivering social housing provision, my Department has not exercised its reserve powers to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether her Department has assessed the impact of delays in the planning process on social housing delivery; and (ii) any actions her Department is taking to address such delays.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the challenges facing the supply of social housing in the North and acknowledge the important role of the planning system in helping to meet current and future housing needs.  To this end my Department has been working closely with local government, who are responsible for over 99% of all local and major planning applications as well as bringing forward local development plans, and other stakeholders to improve the planning system through the Planning Improvement Programme.The programme has already delivered positive changes including legislation to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists to improve the quality of planning applications entering the system, and in turn reduce the potential for delay through the application process. My Department is also conducting a review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 to enable the planning application process to be further streamlined. Ongoing work with statutory consultees has already resulted in performance improvements and this engagement will continue.My officials are also currently engaging with individual council planning authorities and other key stakeholders in the planning system with a specific focus on identifying challenges in current development management (planning application process) practice and procedures and the local development plan process, as well as individual performance related matters, to inform further opportunities for efficiencies and improvements to the system.  I am committed to ensuring that the planning system in the North operates in an efficient and effective way and delivers for all stakeholders including those needing social housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department plans to communicate changes resulting from the proposed Westminster welfare reforms to affected claimants in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to work closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to understand the full implications for Northern Ireland of the changes announced on 18 March. However, it will take time to understand the impacts of these changes. Once further detail is known, my department will communicate the changes to all relevant stakeholders and claimants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions that have taken place with counterparts in Westminster to ensure the unique needs of Northern Ireland are considered in the implementation of the proposed welfare reforms.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to work closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to understand the full implications for Northern Ireland of the changes announced on 18 March. Calculations on the UK wide impact of the Green Paper are expected to be published along with the Chancellor’s Spring Statement on 26th March.  It will take time to understand the implications of the announced welfare changes. I want to ensure that Northern Ireland has a welfare system that is fair for claimants and taxpayers and protects the most vulnerable. I will continue to engage with the UK Government to ensure that this happens, and also with Executive colleagues as Northern Ireland does not have the resources to mitigate against these changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department plans to support young people under the age of 22 in Northern Ireland who will be affected by the proposed removal of the health element of Universal Credit.
answer_text: I am extremely concerned about the proposed welfare changes announced on 18 March, which I raised during a meeting I had with Hilary Benn, Secretary of State for Northern Ireland on 20 March. I, along with Executive colleagues, have also written to the Prime Minister to record our concerns and disappointment at the changes announced.I understand the shock and uncertainty caused by the proposed changes, but my department does not yet know the full impact. 	My officials are continuing to work closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to understand each of the changes and their implications for Northern Ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any discussions have taken place with the Minister for the Economy on expanding job and skills programmes to accommodate those no longer entitled to benefits due to proposed welfare reforms.
answer_text: My Department continues to analyse the potential impact of the Welfare changes announced by the Work and Pensions Secretary. Aligned with the Department for Economy, my Department will be bidding for necessary funding to support those people impacted and no longer entitled to benefits under the proposed changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any impact the increased frequency of reassessments for disability benefits will have on processing times and appeals in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is currently engaging with the Department for Work and Pensions to assess the impact for Northern Ireland of the proposed changes to disability benefits outlined in the Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working Green Paper. I am committed to ensuring that Northern Ireland has a welfare system that is fair for claimants and taxpayers and protects the most vulnerable. I will continue to engage with the UK Government to ensure that this happens, and also with Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will introduce specific funding mechanisms and incentives to support the establishment of a veterinary school in Northern Ireland, given the high costs associated with veterinary education.
answer_text: Policy concerning the supply of locally trained graduates to support the veterinary profession has not been led by my Department.  However, as autonomous bodies, the universities have the discretion to offer any course, including veterinary subjects, from within existing student numbers and funding allocations. My Department is open to working in partnership with the universities to consider the funding implications of such an initiative should a relevant proposal be forthcoming.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the long-term economic benefits that a veterinary school in Northern Ireland could provide.
answer_text: Policy concerning the supply of locally trained graduates to support the veterinary profession has not been led by my Department. Therefore, my Department has not carried out an assessment of the long-term benefits that a veterinary school could provide. My Department is open to working in partnership with the universities to consider the implications of such an initiative should a relevant proposal be forthcoming.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is taking to address the shortage of veterinarians beyond facilitating access to external study opportunities.
answer_text: The responsibility for workforce planning for the veterinary sector as a whole rests with the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS).The NICS through my Department is, of course, a significant employer of veterinary professionals and therefore works closely with the Department of Finance in addressing the challenges associated with the recruitment and retention of veterinarians into DAERA.  Recent measures deployed have included the launch of recruitment competitions as well as securing additional veterinarian resource from Government Agency Frameworks and specialised agency contract providers.Work is also continuing, again in partnership with the Department of Finance, to improve structures, resourcing, working practices and systems to ensure that the NICS, and DAERA in particular, remains a responsive and attractive employer of professional veterinary staff.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the Strategic Investment Board’s report on the extent and nature of the needs for veterinary manpower and veterinary education will be made available to the public.
answer_text: The Strategic Investment Board’s situational analysis and baseline report on the extent and nature of the needs of veterinary manpower and veterinary education within Northern Ireland was published on DAERA’s internet site on 18 May 2023. The document can be accessed by the public using the following link: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/daera/DAERA%2022-343%20DLR.PDF
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he will take to proactively encourage universities in Northern Ireland to develop a business case for a veterinary school.
answer_text: I am conscious that responsibility for higher education provision rests with the Department for the Economy and that responsibility for workforce planning for the veterinary sector sits within the remit of the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS).However, as the NICS through my Department is a significant employer of veterinary professionals, we take a close interest in any steps that can help secure an ongoing supply to meet the needs of Northern Ireland, including any proposals for a veterinary school.My Department engaged some time ago with the Strategic Investment Board on this issue and in 2022 it completed a situational analysis and baseline report to inform any future strategic business case for a veterinary school for Northern Ireland, which was subsequently published and is available on my Department’s website.  That report recognised that further work to advance a business case was required and this work rests with the third level educational institutes.I have also recently been able to hear directly from the Vice Chair of the Ulster University (UU) about that university’s ambitions and intentions in relation to a veterinary school.If any of our third level educational institutes wish to take plans forward to progress a business case, my Department remains happy to engage on relevant aspects that are related to DAERA’s responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to restore and expand respite services for individuals with learning disabilities and dementia, given the significant strain on families and carers.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of health and social care services in accordance with population and individual assessed need. HSCTs utilise both statutory and independent sector providers to provide respite/short breaks for adults with a learning disability and dementia, in accordance with assessed need. HSCTs have procedures in place to optimise capacity within available resources. The allocation of respite/short breaks is determined through completion of a Carer’s Assessment. All requests for respite/short breaks are assessed and managed by HSCTs based on individual need, with statutory and independent sector care homes facilitating both planned and emergency respite/short breaks. In addition, there are alternative options to respite/short breaks in a care home setting which include for example, day care, a sitting service in the individual’s own home or a Direct Payment. Shared Lives Services are also available within Older People and Learning Disability Services, offering day support for older people including those with dementia. Shared Lives day support and respite/short breaks services are also available for adults with a learning disability in the Shared Lives Carer’s home. Carer Assessments continue to be offered by HSCTs to assess the carer’s individual situation and provide appropriate support.It is recognised that there is complexity of need and demand for respite/short breaks for adults with a learning disability and dementia. My Department is leading on the reform of adult social care under the auspices of the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The Commissioning and Contracting workstream is taking forward the review of the regional care homes contract and specification which will include provision for respite/short break placements within the care home setting for adults in all Programmes of Care, including individuals with learning disabilities and dementia. The Maximising Capacity Workstream is working to make most effective use of the available regional care home resource, which is expected to assist with the provision of respite/short breaks to support individuals and their carers. Work is also ongoing within my Department to finalise the Learning Disability Service Model. Throughout 2023/24, a body of work has been undertaken, including thematic review of published research and clinical excellence guidelines, consideration of data to better understand population need and service capacity, alongside feedback from people supported, families/carers, professionals and providers. This work and feedback informed a Draft Learning Disability Model which sets out the Department of Health’s vision regarding home, transitions, health & wellbeing, meaningful lives & citizenship, families & carers (including respite/ short breaks), and mental health & behavioural support. Public Consultation is anticipated in coming months, pending finalisation of the model.My Department remains commit

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department will prioritise respite care funding in the next budget, given its critical role in supporting families and reducing long-term healthcare costs.
answer_text: As the Department of Health has responsibility for providing respite care, this is a matter for the Health Minister to consider in line with his other priorities, once the final 2025-26 Budget has been agreed by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether additional funding has been allocated to support the reintroduction and expansion of respite services.
answer_text: In answering this question, this has been interpreted as relating to Children with Disabilities (CwD) services, and the response is being provided specifically in respect of the additional funding announced by my Department in October 2024 to improve services for Children with Disabilities.To date, a total of circa £1.38m additional funding has been allocated to HSC Trusts for the 2024/25 financial year, for a range of services and supports for CWD, which will include respite / short breaks services. Officials are currently working to finalise the allocations to individual HSC Trusts for 2025/26 from the circa £13.1m additional funding available on a recurrent basis. The additional funding being provided has been specifically ringfenced in order to directly improve short breaks capacity, increase residential capacity and rapidly expand a range of family, therapeutic and behavioural supports, and the services to be commissioned from the funding will be within these priority areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps taken to ensure that carers can access (i) financial support; and (ii) practical support while respite provision remains limited.
answer_text: My Department provides Carer’s Allowance, a benefit for unpaid carers aged 16 and over who provide 35 hours or more of care per week. Carers Allowance can be claimed online quickly via any device with internet access. The Department’s Make the Call service also provides entitlement checks to ensure a carer is accessing all the support available to them. In addition, a range of financial assistance may be available from the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts including: a one-off carers payment to meet an identified need; use of Direct Payments to support a carer in their caring role; and within Children and Disability Services, the cost equivalent of residential short breaks may be offered if the family can identify an appropriate carer.  The Department of Health has confirmed that all HSC Trusts have well-established carers’ services offering a range of practical and emotional support. Trusts are responsible for the planning and delivery of short break services, aligned to assessed needs and within the financial resources available. For adult services alternatives to bed based care such as day care, a sitting service in the person’s own home or a Direct Payment can be offered. Adults with a learning disability can also access respite/a short break in a Shared Lives Carer’s home. Trusts also signpost to community and voluntary supports. Within Children’s Services, Trusts can offer alternatives such as befriending, Share the Care/Foster Care, Direct Payments and short day breaks through community and voluntary groups.  Emergency short breaks are available for children and adults in critical or unforeseen situations when primary caregivers are unexpectedly unavailable, through illness or emergencies, or there may be a safeguarding concern which may mean that care needs provided outside of the family home.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is ensuring that respite services are equitably distributed across Northern Ireland, including in rural areas.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of health and social care services in accordance with population and individual assessed need. HSCTs endeavour to ensure that respite/short breaks services are equally distributed across Northern Ireland, including across rural and urban areas.HSCTs advise that a person-centred approach is undertaken in the provision and commissioning of respite/short break care. At an individual level, an assessment of need is completed with service users and their carers. Respite/short break options are then reviewed, agreed in partnership with service users, carers and Trust keyworker/s. An assessment of need will include consideration of factors such as geographical location and appropriate environment.HSCTs utilise both statutory and independent sector providers to provide respite/short breaks. However, to ensure that respite/ short break services are not limited to the location of available beds within buildings-based facilities, respite/short breaks can also be provided through domiciliary care support within people’s own homes and also through fostering services for children. Direct Payments are also available to facilitate respite/short breaks for adults within their own local community, this helps support service provision across both urban and rural areas.   Shared Lives is also an option for older people and for people with a learning disability.  Shared Lives provides community-based care in a Shared Lives Carers home.  My Department remains committed to working with HSCTs and other agencies to facilitate appropriate respite/short breaks for individuals, delivering support for individuals, families and carers in accordance with assessed need across all areas of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether curriculum reform will include updated Relationships and Sexuality Education to ensure young people receive education on consent and healthy relationships.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan, will consider all areas of learning across the curriculum.  Given the nature of the Review, it is for Ms Crehan to determine the level of detail regarding any recommendations on specific subject content.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the key benchmarks that will be used to measure the success of the TransformED NI strategy, including whether these will be aligned with international standards for educational excellence.
answer_text: A detailed implementation plan to support the TransformED NI strategy will be published in Spring 2025 identifying lead organisations and delivery mechanisms, as well as funding requirements. Embedding a culture of monitoring, evaluation and quality assurance will be woven through all aspects of implementation. Following publication of the implementation plan, the Department will report at key milestones notably through an Annual Progress Report. Integral to success will be consistent improvement in pupil attainment and we will consider appropriate system measures and targets, including international standards for educational excellence, as we take forward work on curriculum and qualifications review, Key Stage assessment and literacy and numeracy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will take a cross-departmental approach to addressing educational disadvantage.
answer_text: The ‘A Fair Start’ Report and Action Plan, which seeks to address educational disadvantage, was endorsed by the Northern Ireland Executive in May 2021.  A number of actions contained in it are cross-departmental and the delivery of the Action Plan is overseen by a cross-departmental board.The extent of the implementation of the recommendations will of course be dependent on funding, which is currently significantly lower than recommended by the Expert Panel on Educational Underachievement. Ensuring a cross-departmental approach is also aligned to key legislation and policy/strategy relevant to all Departments:The Children’s Service Cooperation Act 2015 places obligations on all ‘children’s authorities’ to cooperate with each other in order to contribute to the improvement of outcomes for children and young people. All government departments in Northern Ireland are children’s authorities.The Executive Children and Young People’s Strategy provides a strategic framework to encourage and support cooperation across departments to improve outcomes for children and young people.  My department has established the cross-departmental Monitoring and Reporting Board to support implementation of the Children and Young People’s Strategy. The Board draws together senior officials from across departments. The role of the Board is to provide strategic direction, with a strong emphasis on collaboration and co-ordination, on policy matters affecting children and young people, particularly where there are significant challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will review teacher retention and recruitment, including workload and pay, as part of the TransformED NI strategy.
answer_text: High-quality teachers are the most important feature of a successful education system and I am determined to make teaching in Northern Ireland an attractive, high-status profession.As outlined in my Department’s Education Strategy, TransformED, attracting, developing and retaining the best teachers is the greatest challenge that education systems face. Northern Ireland is extremely fortunate that historically we have recruited high performing school leavers and graduates to teaching. Teaching has been popular amongst our young people and the profession held in high esteem. Yet in recent years there have been increasing challenges with teacher recruitment and retention.A number of key commitments are, therefore, included in TransformED to address these challenges which will be progressed as a matter of urgency. These include medium and long-term strategic monitoring of teacher supply to anticipate and remediate issues and reviewing how best to attract high-quality graduates to subject shortage areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps that will be taken to enhance the status and perception of vocational education and apprenticeships, ensuring they are recognised as equal in value to academic routes.
answer_text: My priority is to ensure that learners in schools have access to high quality qualifications that meet the needs and interests of the pupils and that will facilitate progression along their chosen pathway.While policy responsibility for vocational education and apprenticeships rests with Department for the Economy, the Entitlement Framework policy ensures that all learners have access to a broad range of qualifications, both general (academic) and applied (professional/ technical or vocational). Legislation requires that schools offer access to 21 courses at both Key Stage 4 and Post 16, with at least one third of the offer being general and one third applied. Careers education, information and guidance provided by teachers and careers advisors ensures that learners have access to advice to help them make informed decisions about the most appropriate qualifications and courses to facilitate progress along their chosen routes. In support of this, every post-primary school in Northern Ireland is a member of an Area Learning Community (ALC) This provides a mechanism for schools to work collaboratively with other schools and FE colleges to deliver the requirements of the EF. Each ALC also has a careers sub-group comprising careers teachers and coordinators from the participating schools and representatives from the Careers Service.  They aim to share best practice, develop joint events for their pupils, and work together on issues such as engaging with local employers and identifying opportunities for apprenticeships in their local area.A number of local Labour Market Partnerships and ALCs are currently working in partnership to enhance career connections and potential pathways between schools and local industry. My officials are working with the Department for Communities to replicate this across all ALCs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with healthcare professionals and patient representatives on the feasibility of a cross-trust waiting list model, including any feedback received.
answer_text: Addressing waiting times continues to be a key priority for me, and the Elective Care Framework (ECF), published May 2024, sets out my Department’s plan to tackling those waiting times. A key objective of the ECF is for the development of regional waiting lists. At present, there is not one single cross Trust waiting list model however, there is much work being taken forward in this area by the various clinical networks that have been established to support regional working, with examples across specialities such as breast, urology and general surgery.  For example, in September 2024 I announced the development of a regional breast assessment waiting list.  This announcement was based on proposals that had been co-produced with key stakeholders including service user representatives, health care professionals and the charity sector. There was broad consensus among these groups that a regional waiting list was the correct approach to address inequities of access to Breast Cancer Services.I recognise that there are a number of benefits in having single waiting lists, which include the ability to maximise patient equity for the care they need, irrespective of where they live. Work will continue in this area to ensure patients are assessed as quickly as possible, on a regional basis. Opportunities to develop single waiting lists will be considered through engagement with all relevant stakeholders, where clinically appropriate and where resources are available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the impact increased cross-trust collaboration would have on reducing long-term waiting lists for key procedures; and (ii) whether a cost-benefit analysis has been conducted.
answer_text: Reducing waiting lists requires a multifaceted approach. Regional cross Trust collaboration has been fundamental in supporting efforts to address waiting lists here and deliver much-needed reform of services to improve outcomes for our patients. The Elective Care Framework (ECF), May 2024, sets out the path for the development and reform of elective care services and details measures needed to address the unacceptable waiting lists here. A key component to successful delivery is the continued regional collaboration to ensure effective and efficient use of our resources and build on the good progress and improvements made to date to reduce waits here. Much good work is already underway through, for example; regional elective care centres, mega clinics and rapid diagnosis centres.The investment needed to reduce waiting lists is clearly set out in the Framework and if fully funded and implemented, would address the waiting list backlog, close the demand capacity gap and deliver reform for the benefit of our patients.Whilst the Programme for Government includes a commitment to invest up to £215m a year on the waiting list backlog, in line with the required investment levels detailed in the ECF, the draft 2025/26 NI budget does not contain any additional funding for waiting lists. Without this investment, there is no realistic possibility of reducing waiting times in Northern Ireland to acceptable levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any safeguards in place to ensure that patients transferred between Health and Social Care Trusts do not experience delays in follow-up care or aftercare in their home Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The onus is on the discharging Health and Social Care Trust to make a timely referral as appropriate to the home Health and Social Care Trust.The ability to provide care in the home Trust will be dependent on service capacity within the home Trust at the time of the referral.The individual is central in this process and it is important that this is done in a timely way to ensure the least possible inconvenience to the person and or their family

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to a more collaborative approach to waiting list management across Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure more efficient use of capacity.
answer_text: A collaborative approach to waiting list management is well developed across all Health and Social Care Trusts. The Elective Care Management Team meets regularly and provides a forum for Trusts to highlight pressures that may require assistance, alongside discussion of opportunities to equalise waiting times across Trusts. All Trusts are represented at a senior level.This regional approach has been further embedded by the development of the regional day procedure centres (DPCs), which provide capacity to treat patients who require low acuity procedures across a range of specialties. Patients travel from all Trusts to access this capacity. This supports the efficient use of core Trust capacity, which often focuses on more complex procedures. The DPCs have supported reductions in the number of patients waiting and the associated waiting times for procedures such as endoscopy. In addition, a number of specialist centres have been created for Urological conditions which can be accessed by patients from all Trusts, with the complex stone service in Craigavon Area Hospital and the bladder outflow service at the Ulster Hospital examples of this. This collaborative approach continues to be strengthened through service and technical developments. The implementation of the encompass programme has provided the opportunity to develop single regional waiting lists that will ensure patients are assessed in chronological order on a regional basis, rather than within their local Trust area. This will allow patients to be assessed in the next available clinic slot, which may be in any Trust, mitigating Trust specific service pressures that can impact waiting times. Work is ongoing to implement this process for women referred for urgent suspected breast cancer assessment, with plans in place to develop this in other specialties such as dermatology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any mechanisms currently in place to allow patients to receive treatment in a different Health and Social Care Trust area if waiting times are significantly shortened.
answer_text: A collaborative approach to waiting list management is well developed across all Health and Social Care Trusts. The Elective Care Management Team meets regularly and provides a forum for Trusts to highlight pressures that may require assistance, alongside discussion of opportunities to equalise waiting times across Trusts. All Trusts are represented at a senior level.This regional approach has been further embedded by the development of the regional day procedure centres (DPCs), which provide capacity to treat patients who require low acuity procedures across a range of specialties. Patients travel from all Trusts to access this capacity. This supports the efficient use of core Trust capacity, which often focuses on more complex procedures. The DPCs have supported reductions in the number of patients waiting and the associated waiting times for procedures such as endoscopy. In addition, a number of specialist centres have been created for Urological conditions which can be accessed by patients from all Trusts, with the complex stone service in Craigavon Area Hospital and the bladder outflow service at the Ulster Hospital examples of this. This collaborative approach continues to be strengthened through service and technical developments. The implementation of the encompass programme has provided the opportunity to develop single regional waiting lists that will ensure patients are assessed in chronological order on a regional basis, rather than within their local Trust area. This will allow patients to be assessed in the next available clinic slot, which may be in any Trust, mitigating Trust specific service pressures that can impact waiting times. Work is ongoing to implement this process for women referred for urgent suspected breast cancer assessment, with plans in place to develop this in other specialties such as dermatology.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review existing planning policy to assess the impact on the balance between apartment developments and the preservation of family housing in coastal towns.
answer_text: The Department’s Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035 and the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) support the creation of balanced communities and, in relation to all types of housing, the policy approach is to facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality housing to meet the needs of everyone. Council local development plans (LDPs) are the main vehicle for delivering this by assessing future housing land requirements and managing housing growth in their areas to achieve sustainable patterns of residential development. This process allows councils to address local housing issues within their communities, such as the balance between family housing; apartments; holiday homes; and short-stay rental properties. Where appropriate, LDPs can include specific policies to protect the distinctive nature and character of settlements. Councils are also responsible for operating effective development management in respect of planning applications for housing and in doing so, the provisions of regional strategic planning policy must be taken into account. I have no plans to revise the Department’s planning policy approach on housing at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any enforcement measures in place to ensure that developers comply with the 20 per cent social housing requirement in Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council.
answer_text: The interpretation and application of the policies contained in the Northern Area Plan 2016 is a matter for Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council as the local planning authority. Following the approval of a planning application, any conditions attached to that permission, and any local development plan policies that guided the decision, can then be enforced if a developer fails to comply with their permission. There are a wide range of powers and penalties in the Planning Act (Northen Ireland) 2011 available to councils enabling them to take enforcement action, where they consider it expedient to do so, having regard to the provisions of the local development plan and to any other material considerations. Whether to take enforcement action is matter for the council, as the local planning authority, to decide.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has undertaken a review of Section 76 Planning Agreements to assess their effectiveness in securing social housing contributions.
answer_text: Planning agreements are voluntary but legally binding and some councils will use them more than others.  My Department does not review councils’ use of planning agreements. A planning agreement can potentially be used to ensure a development is occupied by a defined use or user.  For example, it may be used to secure a proportion of affordable housing or social housing in a new development or a mix of tenure in a housing development. This could be where a need has been established, possibly through a policy requirement or as a key site requirement of a Local Development Plan and where a planning condition may not give the appropriate level of detail or security of outcome to be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social housing units added to the Housing Executive stock as a result of Section 76 planning agreements in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. With regard to the Housing Executive, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Section 76 (S76) planning agreement is a legally binding agreement, usually between the applicant or landowner and a council, to secure specific planning obligations necessary to make proposed development acceptable. One of the uses of such an agreement is to secure affordable housing (social or intermediate housing). The planning agreement must be signed and completed before planning permission will be issued by Council.The Housing Executive does not currently build social housing stock, therefore there have been no units added to Housing Executive stock as a result of S76 planning agreements.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered introducing planning restrictions on the construction of apartment developments in coastal towns, such as Portrush and Portstewart, to protect the character of these areas.
answer_text: Councils across the North are responsible for bringing forward housing policies for their own areas in their Local Development Plans (LDPs). In doing so, they must take account of the Department’s Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035 and the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) which supports the creation of balanced communities and the provision of adequate and available supply of quality housing to meet the needs of everyone. This process allows councils to address local housing issues within their communities, such as the balance between family housing, apartments and holiday homes. Where appropriate, LDPs can include specific policies to protect the distinctive nature and character of settlements. My Department performs an important oversight role in seeking to ensure that LDPs take account of regional policy and guidance and has been encouraging of LDP approaches that support the creation of balanced communities and an appropriate mix of tenures. In addition to plan making, councils are responsible for determining planning applications for housing in their local areas and in doing so, the provisions of regional strategic planning policy must also be taken into consideration. I have no plans to revise the Department’s planning policy approach on housing to restrict the construction of apartment developments in coastal towns at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures currently in place to ensure that mainstream teachers and classroom assistants receive adequate support in assisting children with special educational needs who may display challenging behaviours.
answer_text: All schools have a statutory responsibility within their scheme of management (The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998:  Articles 3-6) for developing their own policies to promote good behaviour and discipline.Article 6 of the 1998 Education (Northern Ireland) Order, tasks the Education Authority (EA) with preparing a statement setting out the arrangements it has made or proposes to make for the education of children with behaviour difficulties including the arrangements for the provision of advice and support to schools. The updated Behaviour Plan for 2024-2025 highlights the EA’s arrangements in respect of supporting positive behaviours in schools, including Telephone Advice Helpline, teacher professional learning opportunities, risk management processes, relevant reporting arrangements and recording arrangements and can be found at: https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services/behaviour-support-post-primary/information-schools/support-available-schools.A range of whole school capacity building training is available from the EA Primary and Post Primary Behaviour Support and Provisions services, which encourages schools to establish early intervention, preventative, trauma informed and nurturing approaches to support behaviours that challenge and promote a culture of positive emotional health and wellbeing.Schools can also refer directly to the EA Primary and Post Primary Behaviour Support to request staff professional development, pupil intervention and request placement within EOTAS through the respective EOTAS Panel for the phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to introduce mandatory continuous professional development programmes for all teachers focused on special educational needs.
answer_text: Investing in and supporting the teacher workforce is a key priority in my Department’s TransformED NI: Transforming Teaching and Learning strategy. This strategy aims to ensure high-quality professional development is provided throughout teachers’ careers.  It is closely aligned with my recently published Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and five-year Delivery Plan, which set out an ambitious programme for the transformation of services to support children and young people with SEN.To support SEN-related professional learning, the Education Authority (EA) provides a range of Continuous Professional Development (CPD) opportunities. While participation is not mandatory, my Department has facilitated attendance by providing financial support for substitute cover in schools.The EA also offers a wide range of SEN-focused resources, including CPD packs for school-wide use and online professional development programmes, accessible via its website. Additionally, the Higher Education Institutions in Northern Ireland provide ongoing SEN-related professional learning opportunities for teachers. I will consider further the approach to SEN-related professional development over coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the specific measures his Department is implementing to enhance teacher training programmes, as part of the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda, to ensure educators are equipped to support children with special educational needs.
answer_text: One of the four pillars of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda is ‘Right People’, which focuses on ensuring we have a highly skilled workforce, equipped and empowered to build supportive learning environments for pupils with SEN.  The SEN Reform five-year Delivery Plan includes a range of specific measures in this regard.  Actions relating to teacher training include defining and implementing:SEN/Inclusion subject content requirements for Initial Teacher Education courses (both PGCE and B.Ed);Early Professional Development training for Newly Qualified Teachers offer to facilitate regular training in relation to SEN, disabilities and broader inclusive practice;a Professional Learning Programme for teaching staff;a Leadership Programme for SEN Learning Support Co-ordinators/ SENCos;a Resource Portal for Teachers;a universal SEN Training Programme targeted at all front-line staff The Education Authority (EA) will also offer a professional development programme and supporting resources for teachers through the implementation of the child-centred Graduated Response Framework.As set out in the SEN Reform Delivery Plan, the EA, Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) (who determine content of Initial Teacher Education programmes) and the Middletown Centre for Autism will be key delivery partners in taking this work forward.The design and trial of a teacher and classroom assistant professional learning programme is one of the projects funded under NICS Transformation Board funding which the Finance Minister confirmed on 4 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether schools are subject to a limit on the number of children they can refer for educational psychology assessments each year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) plays a vital role in supporting schools at all stages of the Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Code of Practice. However, the service operates with finite resources, which limits the amount of time that can be allocated to schools, pupils, and parents. To ensure fair and equitable distribution, schools receive an allocated portion of EPS time based on a formula that considers 50% school enrolment and 50% Free School Meal Entitlement (FSME), ensuring that all schools have access to support.While this system provides a structured approach, flexibility is built in to allow children with complex and significant needs to receive support beyond the standard school allocation. However, increasing demand on the service means that it is not possible to meet all requests. Therefore, it is essential to prioritise children with the highest level of need to ensure resources are directed where they are most urgently required.To maximise impact within these constraints, the service has been working to develop creative and agile solutions to support schools and pupils more efficiently. This includes the introduction of Solution-Focused Hubs and Early Years Clinics as pilot initiatives. The outcomes of these pilots will help inform future service delivery and ensure the service remains responsive to the evolving needs of schools and pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department ensures that all newly-qualified teachers receive adequate teacher training in special educational needs support before entering the classroom.
answer_text: The Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in Northern Ireland deliver Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes that are aligned with, and prepare future teachers for the delivery of, the Northern Ireland Curriculum. All ITE courses include a range of mandatory and optional modules for student teachers focused on Special Educational Needs (SEN). These programmes are designed to ensure, amongst other things, that new teachers achieve a set of professional competences identified by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI). All aspects of ITE programmes can be mapped against one or more of these competences and SEN is specifically referenced in competences 9, 15 and 21.A key action in TransformED NI is to develop a core content framework for initial teacher education. This will ensure a consistent and unified approach that clearly identifies the essential skills and knowledge that all teachers should acquire before entering the classroom.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has considered strengthening legislation to ensure enhanced protection for older victims of various forms of abuse, including financial exploitation.
answer_text: The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031, which is jointly led by my Department and the Department of Health, adopts an inclusive approach, recognising the diversity of all victims of domestic and sexual abuse and their needs. The Strategy acknowledges that there will be specific challenges faced by older victims of domestic and sexual abuse and the Strategy’s Expert Reference Group (ERG) includes representation from the organisation ‘Hourglass’ to ensure that the voices and perspectives of older victims help to inform policy decisions and the development of services.  One of the actions included in the Strategy’s Action Plan is to consider intersectionality and the barriers faced by specific groups (including older people) and then to identify and implement measures to address these, subject to funding.  My officials have spoken to Hourglass who have agreed to be involved in this work, in respect of older people.  You may also be aware that my Department also funds the delivery of a Northern Ireland wide advocacy service, by ASSIST NI, to support victims of domestic and sexual abuse who are engaged with the criminal justice system.
