ASSIST NI offers a service tailored to the individual needs of each victim, they provide guidance, information, work with other service providers and advocate on victim’s behalf to secure the help and resources they require.  Advocates are trained to understand and recognise the barriers faced by specific groups, including older people, and will work with them to provide support that meets their needs.The Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 introduced a new domestic abuse offence which criminalises behaviour that is controlling or coercive or amounts to psychological, emotional, technological or financial abuse. It captures patterns of physical and/or psychological abuse by a partner, ex-partner or close family member and covers behaviour that is physically violent, threatening or intimidating.When the new offence was introduced, my Department launched a public awareness campaign including a series of online videos detailing the various types of domestic abuse; financial abuse is depicted in these powerful videos and they sign post victims on how to report the abuse.  Additional advice on the domestic abuse offence and the videos remains available on the NI Direct website under the title ‘Still Abuse’.My Department also helps to address harm and vulnerability caused by fraud and supports partnership working to tackle the threat of scams and financial exploitation through its membership of the ScamwiseNI Partnership, which is chaired by the PSNI.  The Partnership was established eight years ago and now has over 45 partners who work collaboratively to provide valuable information and crucial advice to keep the public informed, aware and vigilant.  ScamwiseNI's most recent, six-month campaign, featured radio and newspaper advertisements, along with posters in public transport spaces,

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps being taken to ensure a coordinated approach across all Departments in addressing elder abuse, in line with the priorities in the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031, which is jointly led by the Department of Justice and the Department of Health, adopts an inclusive approach, recognising the diversity of all victims of domestic and sexual abuse and their needs. The Strategy acknowledges that there will be specific challenges faced by older victims of domestic and sexual abuse and the Strategy’s Expert Reference Group (ERG) includes representation from the organisation ‘Hourglass’ to ensure that the voices and perspectives of older victims help to inform policy decisions and the development of services.  Department  of  Justice along with NI Policing Board, via a Joint Committee, provides funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) which are statutory stand-alone bodies that engage with their local communities to identify and help address local community safety concerns. Some PCSP initiatives that can help support older people, rather than specifically address elder abuse, include:the provision of call blocking schemes via Truecall telephone devices installed to stop nuisance calls and scammers;commissioning of services through Ageing Well which provides support to older people to reduce their fear of crime and vulnerability to crime, particularly those older people who may be alone and/or infirm;projects which raise awareness among older people of the risks of bogus callers, internet and telephone fraud, and other relevant issues which older people and/or their carers might identify.The Executive are committed to working together to support all Ministers to deliver the priorities as laid out in the Programme for Government priorities and to support Departments in their own initiatives going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals aged 55 and over who have been referred for safeguarding interventions in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to raise public awareness of the different forms of abuse that older people can experience, such as financial, physical, emotional and neglect.
answer_text: My Department is developing an Adult Protection Bill which will introduce additional protections to strengthen and underpin the adult protection process.  The Adult Protection Bill will see the introduction of a number of duties and powers to assist in inquiries and the protection of all adults at risk from harm, including those experiencing financial abuse.  The Adult Protection Bill will also see the introduction of new offences regarding ill-treatment and neglect.Furthermore, questions on the issue of safeguarding are being developed for the Health Survey and have already appeared in the Northern Ireland Life and Times survey, which will help raise awareness and gauge public opinion.The new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031 was formally launched on 25th September.  The Strategy recognises that anyone can be a victim of domestic and/or sexual abuse regardless of their age, ethnicity, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation or background.The Strategy also recognises that while anyone can experience domestic and/or sexual abuse, some people may be more at risk or may face additional barriers in accessing services and will therefore require specialist support and intervention.  For example, older people face a number of additional barriers to seeking support including generational views about faith, loyalty, shame and guilt.  In some cases, they may find it difficult to report if the person is abusing them is also their carer or they are financially dependent on them. The Strategy acknowledges the importance of identifying and responding to the intersectional needs of victims, to both prevent abuse from happening and to provide appropriate support in the event that it does occur.Health and Social Care staff are provided with training to identify and respond effectively to domestic abuse.  Staff are supported to consider and discuss the contexts in which domestic abuse can manifest, dispel myths about where domestic abuse can occur and who can experience domestic abuse.  This will include acknowledgement of the safety strategies and resilience that older victims develop over time, increasing physical and social dependencies of both the victim and the alleged perpetrator, family and social perception/support or lack of and isolation.  Training supports staff to be aware and recognise those potentially more vulnerable groups of people that could be at higher risk of abuse, such as those with reduced mental or physical capacity.The Department of Health fund the 24 Hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline jointly with the Department for Communities and the Department of Justice.  The Helpline designed and executed a comprehensive digital and offline campaign targeting individuals aged 55+ who may be experiencing abusive relationships.  The campaign, which ran from 17th June to 18th August 2024, involved a combination of bus and radio advertisements, social media ads, and a press release, all implemented with joint branding between the DSA

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has undertaken any reviews or consultations on whether the current consent-based safeguarding system adequately protects individuals who may be experiencing abuse.
answer_text: Following the widely publicised safeguarding failings at Muckamore Abbey Hospital and Dunmurry Manor Care Home, it was recognised that there was a need to review and improve the Department’s Adult Safeguarding policy.  In September 2020, following the independent review into the situation at Muckamore Abbey Hospital, Minister Swann announced his intention to legislate, to provide a statutory footing to the policy around Adult Safeguarding.  The Adult Protection Bill would introduce additional protections to strengthen and underpin in the adult protection process and would bring Northern Ireland in line with other parts of the UK where such legislation already exists.A consultation on the proposed Adult Protection Bill ran for a period of 16 weeks from 17 December 2020 to 8 April 2021 where during this time there was 19 stakeholder sessions and 89 responses received.  Officials from the Department of Health received, on 21 October 2021, Executive approval to start drafting.  My Department is developing an Adult Protection Bill which will introduce additional protections to strengthen and underpin the adult protection process.  The Adult Protection Bill will see the introduction of a number of duties and powers to assist in inquiries and the protection of all adults at risk from harm, including those experiencing financial abuse.  The Adult Protection Bill will also see the introduction of new offences regarding ill-treatment and neglect.It is intended that the Adult Protection Bill will be introduced to the Assembly during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department has considered to improve access to dental care for vulnerable populations, including children requiring general anaesthetic extractions and elderly patients in care homes.
answer_text: Children, young people and adults receive dental care across a range of settings in Northern Ireland: General Dental Services (GDS), Community Dental Services (CDS), and the Hospital Dental Services (HDS). All five Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland provide CDS, which deals with patients who require standard dental treatments, but are not suitable to receive their care in General Dental practice. In addition to referrals from the GDS, the CDS also undertake regular screening sessions of day care facilities for patients with physical, sensory and learning disabilities and care homes for older persons. Through this process CDS identify those individuals who require follow-up treatment.Many child patients referred into the CDS only require the service for one-off specialist or complex interceptive procedures, such as General Anaesthetic (GA). Regionally, in 2023-24, the number of children receiving dental treatment under GA was approximately 90% of the 2019-20 figure. In 2023-24, the CDS saw over 3,400 children for extractions under GA.All dental services were markedly affected by the Covid-19 pandemic, with many services stood or scaled down, and services are still in recovery.  GA waiting lists in all Trusts were severely impacted and access for CDS staff to theatre lists has still not returned to pre-pandemic levels. Reductions in the number of patients waiting for a GA assessment and GA treatment have been made through prioritisation of staff for GA aspects of the CDS service as well as proactive utilisation of any available HSC Trust lists. Attempts are also being made to maximise the use of alternatives to GA.Dental access enhancement initiatives which my Department has funded in 2024/25 include £1m for an Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, £4.3m for a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments, and £3.9m for a Dental Access Scheme for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients. Approximately 500 patients per week are currently seen under the Dental Access Scheme.A Dental Survey of care home residents, commissioned by my Department, is currently underway. The results of the survey will help inform the design of future dental services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken to implement the recommendations of the Oral Health Improvement Plans for (i) children; and (ii) the elderly.
answer_text: The recommendations in the Children’s and Older Persons Improvement Plans are ambitious and funding these will be a significant challenge in the current financial climate. (i) Recent initiatives such as the re-introduction of the Enhanced Child Examination in general dental practice have been underpinned by the recommendations made in the Child Oral Health Improvement Plan.  I am committed to improving the oral health of our population and will continue to seek opportunities for this which will be guided by these recommendations. I am very pleased that oral health has been included in the path finder of my Live Better Initiative in the North West and I look forward to the lessons learned from this initiative to inform our longer term priorities. Empowering children and their families to improve oral health is key to reducing oral health inequalities. (ii) I am aware that our Community Dental Service teams across Northern Ireland are currently reviewing how best they can assess the oral health of older people in care homes and work with care home staff to improve the oral health of this vulnerable population. This reflects recommendations in the Older Persons Oral Health Improvement Plan.Any future work related to the General Dental Services (GDS) contract will take into consideration how best practices can be supported to provide care for older persons retaining natural teeth into later life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any proposals under consideration to top-up fees for NHS dental services in the 2025-26 financial year, particularly in relation to incentivising care for children.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures his Department is taking to ensure that any financial interventions provide sustainable long-term support rather than short-term relief.
answer_text: In Health our primary goal is to direct all available funding to the areas of greatest need. The current financial position can sometimes of necessity mean that a less attractive option needs to be implemented due to affordability constraints.However, the Department has process in place requiring that all funding decisions are appraised to ensure they provide value for money over the status quo whether they are providing long-term support or short-term relief.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure the General Dental Contract is reformed and fully implemented by the end of the 2022-2027 mandate to prevent the movement of practitioners towards private care.
answer_text: My Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan included a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.The first step on this path is to determine the real cost of providing dental services so that realistic options for reform of the service can be developed.To achieve that I have approved the commissioning of a cost-of-service review to be completed in 2025/26. The outworking of the review will inform the next steps, and it would be counterproductive to pre-empt the conclusion of the review by predetermining those next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department ensures that endometriosis patients receive clear and timely communication in relation to their condition, treatment options and waiting times.
answer_text: In each of the Trusts, waiting times fluctuate depending on the specific needs of each patient and the next step in their pathway.  The next steps for the patient’s treatment pathway may be conservative management, further investigations or to be added to the inpatient or day case waiting list for surgical treatment.In Belfast Trust patients are provided with patient information leaflets in relation to their condition.  Options for onward treatment is discussed as part of the consent process at outpatient appointment.  Waiting times fluctuate depending on the specific needs of each patient and the next step in their pathway, e.g., conservative management, investigations or to inpatient/day case waiting list.Patients who are new referrals for an outpatient appointment are advised if their case has been added as urgent/routine and approximate waiting times can be obtained from Waiting list offices in Belfast Trust.  Patients for review are informed when they should expect their clinically indicated review appointment and those added to Inpatient Waiting Lists are advised of the approximate length of wait.Currently in Southern Trust, endometriosis patients are given information at the time of their diagnosis by consultant overseeing their care.In South Eastern Trust information leaflets are provided on third sector support.In Northern Trust information available from Endometriosis UK is used in gynaecology clinics as a point of reference for patient information.  Treatment options are patient specific and individual to each woman’s condition and management plan.In Western Trust patients can contact the Endometriosis Nurse Specialist in relation any queries to their condition, treatment options and waiting times

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any psychological and emotional support services available for those with a diagnosis of endometriosis.
answer_text: As you may be aware, my Department has published the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology – this signals our commitment to working towards improving services for women which includes those suffering from endometriosis.The GIRFT report has identified 20 general recommendations to improve services through coordinated regional action, as well as a list of specific recommendations for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to take forward. These recommendations have been accepted in principle with a view to implementing the required improvements as soon as it is feasible; however, it is acknowledged that some recommendations will require additional funding and lead-in time to deliver fully.A Task and Finish Group to develop and implement an approved pathway for endometriosis has been established, and work is underway with key stakeholders to provide a holistic improved pathway for women.  Trusts have advised that their current services have the following psychological and emotional support services available for those with a diagnosis of endometriosis.Belfast Trust have an Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialist who can provide emotional support for women diagnosed with endometriosis. Patients can be referred to general psychological and pain services for further support although it is recognised that these services also have significant waiting time.In Southern Trust there is currently no dedicated psychological and emotional support services available for those with a diagnosis of endometriosis.In South Eastern Trust there is no dedicated psychology service for women suffering from endometriosis.  There is however access to the chronic pain team but again this is not a dedicated service.Northern Trust does not have any specific or ring-fenced psychological or emotional support services for women with a diagnosis of endometriosis. If indicated, a referral to general psychological services can be made by the attending clinical team.The Western Trust would advise that they are not currently commissioned to provide specific psychological support to patient diagnosed with endometriosis.  Patients can however contact the Endometriosis Nurse Specialist, who can provide emotional support. The Nurse Specialist can also sign-posted patients to services within the community or back to their GP for community-based support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting times for endometriosis diagnosis and treatment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to reduce diagnostic delays for endometriosis.
answer_text: Assessment, diagnosis and treatment for Endometriosis is part of General Gynaecology waiting times, so Trusts are unable to currently provide a specific waiting time for the assessment, diagnosis and treatment of endometriosis specifically.  Trust have provided the following response to this question.The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is unable to distinguish which patients are requiring an endometriosis diagnosis as this information is not recorded on the hospital systems.  The Trust has an Endometriosis Clinic and could provide average waiting times for new appointments at this clinic although not all patients diagnosed with Endometriosis attend this clinic as many also attend general gynaecology clinics.Within the Southern Health and Social Care Trust the waiting time for endometriosis is unknown as this is not coded at triage.In the South Eastern Trust, they are unable to extrapolate individual waiting times for women with endometriosis, however referrals are triaged as urgent and time dependent.The Northern Trust are not able to provide a response to this, as the information requested is not available from their systems.  Women referred for suspected endometriosis or related symptoms are triaged and reviewed within general clinics.In Western Trust not all referrals from GPs are for suspected endometriosis in the first instance and in these cases they are triaged for any Gynaecology consultant to see, according to clinical priority.  The Elective Overnight Stay Centre has been established at the Mater Hospital and this has enabled a reduction in diagnostic delays for endometriosis.Funding has been made available via DoH/SPPG for Waiting List Initiatives and this has led to extra validation of outpatient and inpatient waiting lists, increase in Multi-Disciplinary Meetings for complex gynaecology/endometriosis which has cleared the backlog of patients awaiting discussion and extra outpatient clinics (especially backlog and telephone review clinics) and inpatient/day case theatre lists.After consultation, women are then referred for diagnostic procedures for definitive diagnosis, and the waiting times for a diagnostic laparoscopy or MRI can also contribute to the wait

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work being undertaken to improve GP awareness and training on recognising the symptoms of endometriosis.
answer_text: My Department does not hold lists or information regarding GPs and their training.  As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including endometriosis.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.My Department have endorsed the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines on the diagnosis and management of endometriosis.   Last year the National Confidential Enquiry into Patient Outcome and Death (NCEPOD) published A Long and Painful Road, a review of the quality of care provided to adult patients diagnosed with endometriosis and this was shared with all GP practices.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2024/25 is £50.5k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of consultant gynaecologists that have specialist training in advanced endometriosis treatment.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, as women referred will be under the care of a consultant gynaecologist.  The service model for endometriosis further recognises the impact of this condition on the health and well-being of women.  The basis of the model is holistic management for the needs of women undergoing diagnosis and treatment by delivering care through a specialist multidisciplinary team best able to provide responsive, appropriate care throughout the patient journey.  Each member of the team is functioning as a specialist from within their discipline whether as a consultant, nurse, or other professional lead in delivering care. Belfast Trust have two consultants who have a special interest in endometriosis.In the Southern Health and Social Care Trust there are no consultants with specialist training in advanced endometriosis treatment.South Eastern Trust has 4 gynaecologists with specialist training in endometriosis treatment and access to a consultant surgeon with a special interest who often performs joint cases with gynaecologists.Northern Trust do not have any consultant gynaecologists who have specialist training in advanced endometriosis treatment.The Western Trust currently has two Consultants with specialist training in endometriosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve mental health services for older people in (i) rural areas; and (ii) coastal areas.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 launched in June 2021 sets the vision and future strategic direction for mental health services in Northern Ireland over the next decade. This strategy is for all people of Northern Ireland regardless of their geographical location. Everyone in Northern Ireland is expected to benefit from this strategy as it is a holistic societal approach to mental health and wellbeing with a whole life approach to ownership. Action 1 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to increase public awareness of the distinction between mental wellbeing, mental ill health and mental illness, encouraging public understanding and acceptance of how life can impact upon mental wellbeing, and recognition of the signs of mental ill health and mental illness.  Action 2 of the Mental health Strategy commits to create an action plan for promoting mental health through early intervention and prevention, with year on year actions covering a whole life approach, reaching from infancy to older age. The action plan must consider groups disproportionally affected by mental ill health who often struggle to access early intervention services and seek to reduce stigma associated with mental ill health. Action 31 of the strategy is to, “Develop a regional mental health service, operating across the five HSC Trusts, with regional professional leadership that is responsible for consistency in service delivery and development”. This is to be achieved not by changing organisational boundaries but by ensuring there are structures in place to deliver regional consistency, quality and access across Northern Ireland. Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, as set out in Action 27:     Create a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service that is fully integrated in mental health services and which will help and support for persons in mental health or suicidal crisis.Work in this area is progressing as a lifespan approach ensuring an equitable crisis response service in all geographical areas of Northern Ireland.It is co-produced with significant engagement across a broad range of stakeholders including people with lived experience as service users and as carers. It sets out the structures and arrangements necessary for a Regional Mental Health Service encompassing Trusts’ adult mental health services, primary care and services delivered by community and voluntary sector partners. CAMHS and Mental Health Services for Older People managed care networks will link in as part of the arrangements for a regional mental health service supporting an overall life span approach.  The Strategy is person centred, takes a whole life approach and a whole system focus and the key aim is to ensure long term improved outcomes for people’s mental health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve access to social housing for older people in (i) rural areas; and (ii) coastal areas.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There are four categories of accommodation for older people - Categories 1,2,3 & 4. The main category in the delivery of new build social housing is Category 1 ‘Cat 1’. This is self-contained accommodation for more active older people, which may include an element of scheme supervisor support and/or additional communal facilities (active elderly).A summary of the current Social Housing Development Programme delivery on ‘Cat 1’ accommodation is detailed below. (i) Rural AreasThere are 14 ‘active elderly – cat 1’ Social Housing Units Completed in 2022/23 - 2024/25. There are currently 10 ‘active elderly – cat 1’ social housing units under construction. There are 36 ‘active elderly – cat 1’ social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2027/28. (ii) Coastal AreasThere are 417 ‘active elderly – cat 1’ Social Housing Units Completed in 2022/23 - 2024/25. 57 of these units completed in 2024/25. There are currently 486 ‘active elderly – cat 1’ social housing units under construction. There are 246 ‘active elderly – cat 1’ social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2027/28. Programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.Please note that ‘Older People’ applicants are also eligible to occupy general needs units”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is taking to address the shortage of domiciliary care workers in (i) rural areas; and (ii) coastal areas.
answer_text: The issue of shortage of homecare (domiciliary care) workers in rural areas is largely because of two things:  Either there are none or few people who live in this area that are interested in applying for work in this field or because provider organisations are not willing to travel to rural areas.  The latter can be for a number of reasons, such as:  some providers do not pay a travel allowance; some providers can find delivery of packages of care economically unviable and may choose packages of care in urban areas where the travel distance between calls is less and therefore more profitable; some care workers do not have a car or indicate a preference to work only in a specific area(s).One of the HSC Trusts are trialling paying an enhanced rate for rural areas to encourage providers to pick up these calls.  This is at an early stage, but results are positive so far.  Another Trust is hoping to enhance their team profile in a very rural part of Fermanagh where there is a high level of unmet need to include a reablement focus to ensure people have an opportunity to reach their full potential and therefore may not need a long-term homecare package.It is hoped that the introduction of the real living wage for homecare workers will attract more staff into this area of work.The issues referenced above would be similar for coastal areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has assessed the impact of limited public transport options on older people’s ability to access healthcare in (i) rural areas; and (ii) coastal areas.
answer_text: My Department funds Translink which works to provide public transport services and facilities. This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people of all ages and abilities.Recognising Translink cannot reach all, I also provide funding for community transport which serves both rural and coastal areas, delivering access to transport for those, including older people, who find it difficult to access mainstream public transport.  The rural Dial-a-Lift service assists many people in rural and coastal areas to remain connected to their communities and get to the places they need to go including local health care.My predecessor, Minister O’Dowd MLA met with Executive colleagues to explore if community transport could support other transport activities across government.  This work is being taken forward by officials, including those from Health, with the next meeting of the group scheduled to take place in April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to improve (i) rural; and (ii) coastal transport options for older people.
answer_text: My Department funds Translink which works to provide public transport services and facilities. This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people of all ages and abilities.Recognising that Translink cannot reach every person, I also provide funding to community transport which also serves both rural and coastal areas, delivering access to transport for those, including older people, who find it difficult to access mainstream public transport.  The rural Dial-a-Lift service assists many people in rural and coastal areas to remain connected to their communities and get to the places they need to go locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures her Department is putting in place to mitigate the impact of the upcoming increase in the Minimum Wage and employers' National Insurance contributions on her Department’s budget and operations.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the upcoming increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions (ERNI) will create additional pressures of approximately £15m for my Department and its Non-Departmental Public Bodies (NDPBs). It is not currently anticipated that the increase in the Minimum Wage will lead to significant additional costs.It is reasonable to assume that some additional funding will be received in due course from the Executive for ERNI costs. However, this amount has not yet been confirmed by the Department of Finance and is unlikely to cover the full costs.Decisions on prioritisation and service provision will therefore be required across the Department and its NDPBs to manage the remaining pressures in order to live within the budget allocation. All business areas will be required to implement cost reductions and efficiencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures his Department is putting in place to mitigate the impact of the upcoming increase in the Minimum Wage and employers' National Insurance contributions on his Department’s budget and operations.
answer_text: I will consider the impact of the upcoming increase in the Minimum Wage and employers’ National Insurance contributions alongside all other pay pressures facing the Education sector, when agreeing my Department's 2025-26 resource budget strategy.In relation to employers’ National Insurance contributions, the Chancellor to the Exchequer has stated that support will be provided for public sector employers.  The actual quantum of funding provided through the Barnett formula will not be confirmed for the Executive, and thereby Northern Ireland departments, until the Westminster Main Estimates process concludes (i.e. usually around June). However, it is anticipated that this additional funding will fall short of the Executive’s pressures and work remains ongoing to seek funding from HM Treasury that meets Northern Ireland’s public sector pressures in full.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has considered releasing additional funding to each Department to mitigate the impact of the upcoming increase in the Minimum Wage and employers' National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: ANSWERI appreciate that departments are facing additional pressures as a result of the British Government’s decision to increase employers National Insurance Contributions from April 2025. The Treasury has advised that the Executive will receive Barnett consequentials on the additional funding provided to Whitehall departments for the increased costs.These will not be confirmed until Westminster Main Estimates, which usually conclude around June. Therefore, this funding will be allocated to departments as part of the June Monitoring round.However, our current planning assumption, informed by engagement with the Treasury is that the Barnett Consequentials will fall far short of the pressures identified by departments. I have raised this with the chief Secretary to the Treasury.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures his Department is putting in place to mitigate the impact of the upcoming increase in the Minimum Wage and employers' National Insurance contributions on his Department’s budget and operations.
answer_text: To mitigate the impact on my Department's Budget and operations, officials are engaging with DoF on pay pressures and Barnett Consequential due to the Executive for employers’ National Insurance contribution increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is committed to making provisions for a cap on school uniforms as part of the School Uniforms (Guidance and Allowances) Bill (NIA Bill 12/22-27).
answer_text: The School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill which passed second stage on 3 March 2025 contains provisions for a cap on school uniforms.Clause 3 enables a limit – a cap- to be put in place on numbers of branded items.  Clause 4 enables a cap to be put in place on the cost of individual items or an entire uniform.  The flexibility is built into the Bill for a cap to be on any of these elements or a combination of them.In response to the consultation on the policy proposals underpinning the Bill there was a high level of support for a cost control measure and an even higher level of support was expressed for ensuring a competitive market for school uniforms.  Open competition is the means by which expert bodies such as the Competition and Markets Authority suggest driving and keeping costs down.  So, it may be the case that a cap is brought in for a timebound period, or if needed once we see the impact of the statutory guidelines.  The important point in relation to the Bill is that the necessary powers are in place via clauses 3 and 4.These clauses are drafted to come into operation via commencement orders – this means that further work on the detail of how a cap might operate can be taken forward in parallel with – but without delaying – the implementation of statutory guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is putting in place to mitigate the impact of the upcoming increase in the Minimum Wage and employers' National Insurance contributions on his Department’s budget and operations.
answer_text: My Department anticipates in year funding will be received for the National Insurance increase to cover those staff who are directly employed.However, I recognise that the increase in employer’s National Insurance contributions announced in the Autumn Budget as well as the increase in National Minimum wage will significantly increase the cost-base for Family Health Service and Social Care providers.  Currently I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these additional costs, however I have written to the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care on the issue of National Insurance, to urge him to work to ensure the treatment of these providers faced with these cost pressures is equal to our other providers of health and social care.I’ve also written to the former Finance Minister asking that she also bring her influence to bear on HM Treasury in this regard and I am aware that she has written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in similar terms.Following the announcement of the 2025-26 Draft Budget allocation my department has assessed that we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025-26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.However in recognition of the valuable role played by those providers affected, I will be considering these funding pressures alongside other priorities before I make any final funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to improve access to specialist eating disorder services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i)TrustAdultsChildrenSouthern510Western14<5Note the following:• Reporting for the 2024/25 year has been impacted by Encompass implementation for Belfast/SET & Northern Trusts. No data is available from Belfast/SET or Northern Trust for January 2025.•  Small numbers <5 have been suppressed for non-disclosure purposes•  This is Management Information sourced from Trusts’ manual returns – not official statistics.(ii)Funding to Eating Disorder Services2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Eating Disorders Association NI23,300  18,000  18,000  25,200  13,500 HSC Trusts-    -    1,158,000 1,000,000  -   Total funding allocated23,300  18,000  1,176,000  1,025,200  13,500 (iii)The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 includes a commitment to enhance regional eating disorder services and in line with the Mental Health Strategy Action 29d, a Regional Clinical Lead and Manager for the Regional Eating Disorder Managed Care Network were appointed in late 2024. They are in the planning phase of establishing the Network and a business case to improve effective resource allocation is in its final stages.A precise timescale for further implementation of Action 29d has not been determined as it will depend upon the availability of funding. The Business Case related to Action 29d proposes to enhance the current community-based, evidence-based treatment model in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance, thereby increasing regional availability and accessibility of intensive community and day treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently awaiting treatment for eating disorders, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i)TrustAdultsChildrenSouthern510Western14<5Note the following:• Reporting for the 2024/25 year has been impacted by Encompass implementation for Belfast/SET & Northern Trusts. No data is available from Belfast/SET or Northern Trust for January 2025.•  Small numbers <5 have been suppressed for non-disclosure purposes•  This is Management Information sourced from Trusts’ manual returns – not official statistics.(ii)Funding to Eating Disorder Services2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Eating Disorders Association NI23,300  18,000  18,000  25,200  13,500 HSC Trusts-    -    1,158,000 1,000,000  -   Total funding allocated23,300  18,000  1,176,000  1,025,200  13,500 (iii)The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 includes a commitment to enhance regional eating disorder services and in line with the Mental Health Strategy Action 29d, a Regional Clinical Lead and Manager for the Regional Eating Disorder Managed Care Network were appointed in late 2024. They are in the planning phase of establishing the Network and a business case to improve effective resource allocation is in its final stages.A precise timescale for further implementation of Action 29d has not been determined as it will depend upon the availability of funding. The Business Case related to Action 29d proposes to enhance the current community-based, evidence-based treatment model in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance, thereby increasing regional availability and accessibility of intensive community and day treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures his Department has put in place to ensure that teachers and school staff are equipped to identify and support pupils who may be struggling with an eating disorder.
answer_text: Eating disorders are primarily a mental health issue which are the responsibility of the Department of Health (DoH). It is important to note that our educational staff are not mental health professionals, however it is essential that settings provide caring, supportive environments for children and young people ensuring that where issues arise these are identified and acted upon at the earliest opportunity.The joint Department of Education and DoH ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ provides overarching guidance to empower those working in educational settings in the promotion of positive emotional wellbeing and to strengthen self-esteem and resilience in our children and young people. This is underpinned by an investment of over £5m which supports several emotional health and wellbeing projects and services directly to schools and youth services. Details of the Framework and the projects it supports can be found at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/emotional-health-and-wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to improve awareness and training for GPs and frontline healthcare professionals on the early signs of eating disorders.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.  All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including recognising eating disorders.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.NICE (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence) produce recommendations to help practitioners and commissioners get the best care to people fast, while ensuring value for money.  NICE guidelines are shared with GPs once they have been approved for application within Northern Ireland.  NICE have guidelines for the recognition and early management of eating disorders.My Department have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2024/25 is £50.5k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners. Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding allocated to eating disorder services in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i)TrustAdultsChildrenSouthern510Western14<5Note the following:• Reporting for the 2024/25 year has been impacted by Encompass implementation for Belfast/SET & Northern Trusts. No data is available from Belfast/SET or Northern Trust for January 2025.•  Small numbers <5 have been suppressed for non-disclosure purposes•  This is Management Information sourced from Trusts’ manual returns – not official statistics.(ii)Funding to Eating Disorder Services2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Eating Disorders Association NI23,300  18,000  18,000  25,200  13,500 HSC Trusts-    -    1,158,000 1,000,000  -   Total funding allocated23,300  18,000  1,176,000  1,025,200  13,500 (iii)The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 includes a commitment to enhance regional eating disorder services and in line with the Mental Health Strategy Action 29d, a Regional Clinical Lead and Manager for the Regional Eating Disorder Managed Care Network were appointed in late 2024. They are in the planning phase of establishing the Network and a business case to improve effective resource allocation is in its final stages.A precise timescale for further implementation of Action 29d has not been determined as it will depend upon the availability of funding. The Business Case related to Action 29d proposes to enhance the current community-based, evidence-based treatment model in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance, thereby increasing regional availability and accessibility of intensive community and day treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department intends to support businesses in managing wage pressures associated with the increase in the national living wage and employers' National Insurance contributions.
answer_text: I am aware the local business community has expressed serious concerns about the impact of the British Government’s Autumn Statement. While the Statement brought welcome benefits for workers with increases in the National Living Wage and National Minimum Wage; rather than offsetting this increase in pay with a decrease in taxes, the Budget also increased employer National Insurance contributions.When Minister of Finance, I engaged with businesses on the budget and made continued representations to the Treasury on the subject. I also commissioned research to better understand the cost of doing business including disparities in VAT, and the changes to employer’s national insurance contributions and national minimum wage. The research is due to be completed later this month. It will help build the evidence base for making the case for the Treasury to act on the cost of doing business here.My colleague, the Minister of Finance, highlighted the adverse impacts of the taxation system with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee on 27 February. I will work to support him and advocate alongside him where possible. We will continue to engage with the sector to help us in making persuasive representations to Treasury.Recent calls have been made for the Executive to use our rating system to compensate for some of the budget challenges. This is not practical, nor is it the right solution. The Executive does not have the financial capacity to compensate for decisions made on reserved taxation matters.Delivering for the economy is a priority for the Executive. We want to help create more good jobs, boost productivity, support local innovation and deliver a more sustainable economy.My Department and Invest NI provide a broad range of support to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets. Invest NI also has a portfolio of grants, loans and investment funds, many of which are specifically targeted at early stage and small businesses.In addition, my Department’s Regional Economic Plan is focussed on identifying key economic opportunities and priority actions for each part of the North, supported by a £45m Regional Development Fund.Over the last five years Invest NI has made financial offers of support totalling £474m to businesses in the North. 93% of these offers were to locally owned businesses.The Energy Efficiency Capital Grant (EECG) helps businesses in the North reduce energy costs and build resilience through green efficiency.  Grant support is offered to eligible businesses to buy and install energy efficiency equipment that reduces energy and carbon emissions.  The grant is part of a suite of support, delivered through Invest NI, that helps businesses to make efficiencies in their use of energy and resources.  In addition to the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant, Invest NI also delivers a suite of Energy and Resource Efficiency support, i

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department will take to promote and sustain tourism-related businesses in Northern Ireland’s coastal towns in light of recent cost pressures.
answer_text: I’m aware that the local Tourism and Hospitality business community has expressed serious concerns about the impact of the British Government’s Autumn Statement. These concerns are being examined as part of Cost of Doing Business research which I commissioned as Finance Minister and which is due to be completed later this month.  It will help build the evidence base for making the case for the Treasury to act on the cost of doing business here. My colleague, the Minister of Finance, highlighted the adverse impacts of the taxation system with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee on 27 February. I will work to support him and advocate alongside him where possible. We will continue to engage with the sector to help us in making persuasive representations to Treasury. Recent calls have been made for the Executive to use our rating system to compensate for some of the budget challenges. This is not practical, nor is it the right solution. The Executive does not have the financial capacity to compensate for decisions made on reserved taxation matters. My Department and its delivery partners provide a wide range of support to local companies to generate growth. These include dedicated financial offers of support from Invest NI, upskilling the workforce through FE and HE provision and Tourism NI and Tourism Ireland encouraging visitors to visit and spend here, whilst supporting industry development. Making businesses more sustainable also makes economic sense. My Department leads on the delivery of the Energy Strategy and last year saw the launch of an Energy & Resource Efficiency Programme through Invest NI which provides essential support for businesses to reduce operating costs whilst also creating economic opportunities in the green economy. In addition, my Department’s Regional Economic Plan is focussed on identifying key economic opportunities and priority actions for each part of the North, supported by a £45m Regional Development Fund. Tourism NI provides a suite of advisory supports to help tourism & hospitality businesses reduce costs, improve productivity and be more sustainable. These include programmes such as Leaner and Greener focusing on reducing resource costs, Innovate Tourism, focusing on harnessing innovation in businesses, and the Destination Sustainability Programme, providing a more in-depth approach to embedding sustainability. The recently launched Tourism Vision and Action Plan: 10 Year Plan provides a roadmap for the growth of tourism over the next ten years, to increase tourism expenditure from overnight visitors to £2 billion per annum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement that has taken place between her Department and representatives of the (i) retail sector; and (ii) hospitality sector in relation to the impact of increased labour costs.
answer_text: On 13 January 2025, my predecessor met with representatives of 23 local business groups and chambers of commerce at Parliament Buildings to discuss issues relating to local high street businesses following the British Government’s Autumn Statement. Those in attendance represented Retail NI, Hospitality Ulster and Food NI along with several chambers of commerce.  As Finance Minister, I, alongside a DfE official, met with Hospitality Ulster representatives on 22 January to discuss the implications of the Autumn Statement. My predecessor met with Colin Neill, CEO of Hospitality Ulster, and representatives from four of his member organisations on 29 January to discuss their concerns on the cost of doing business. My officials also regularly engage with Hospitality Ulster through a range of other fora. From this engagement I’m aware that the local retail and hospitality sectors have concerns about the impact of increased labour costs as a result of the British Government’s Autumn Statement. These concerns are being examined as part of Cost of Doing Business research which I commissioned as Finance Minister and which is due to be completed later this month.  It will help build the evidence base for making the case for the Treasury to act on the cost of doing business here. My colleague, the Minister of Finance, highlighted the adverse impacts of the taxation system with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the Finance Interministerial Standing Committee on 27 February. I will work to support him and advocate alongside him where possible. We will continue to engage with businesses to help us in making persuasive representations to Treasury.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has considered sourcing alternative funding streams to continue the provision of therapy to children in primary schools, given the growing concern in relation to children’s mental health.
answer_text: My officials continue to work alongside colleagues from the Department of Health with the continued investment of over £5m per annum to support programmes identified under the joint Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.It is important to note that while our schools are encouraged to provide a supportive and nurturing environment, the provision of mental health and therapeutic services for children, including those or primary school age, is ultimately within the remit of the health service and should be delivered by appropriately qualified and registered health professionals (i.e. counsellors and therapists).Given the challenging financial context across both the health and education systems, I welcome the re-establishment of the ‘Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention’ as I believe that this will provide an opportunity to discuss the continuum of provision for mental health and therapeutic services, and the funding of the same, with Ministerial colleagues.At the same time, schools continue to have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the identified needs of their pupils and in some circumstances they may employ the services of a counsellor or therapist from existing funds.  It is important to stress however, that where a school has any immediate concerns for a primary age child, referral continues to be available through health services, including CAMHS and associated pathways in conjunction with other support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration that was given to the long-term educational and social impacts on children of discontinuing the Healthy Happy Minds programme.
answer_text: As part of the Healthy Happy Minds Counselling and Therapeutic Services pilot which ended two years ago:  702 schools availed of the Healthy Happy Minds pilot, the pilot was available to all primary setting including schools and EOTAS. The number of pupils supported through the pilot is not available, as some participating schools failed to return their monitoring forms.I welcome the re-establishment of the ‘Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention’ as I believe that this will provide an opportunity to discuss the continuum of provision for mental health and therapeutic services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any financial support available for low-income families seeking controllership; and (ii) whether reforms are being considered to reduce the cost burden.
answer_text: (i) While there are normally fees for those seeking a controllership order, exemptions are available for those in receipt of certain benefits, and remissions on the grounds of hardship are available on application.  An information leaflet, ‘Do I have to pay court fees?’, and the form for Exemption or Remission of a Court Fee can be found on my Department’s website.  (ii) The legislative framework for controllership in Northern Ireland, provided for in the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, is a matter for the Department of Health.  Following the 2007 Bamford Review, the Department of Health brought forward the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016. The provisions within the 2016 Act around controllership have yet to be commenced, but they will replace controllers with deputies, who will carry out the same functions but in relation to health and welfare matters, as well as finance and property. Deputies will also be supervised by a public official known as a Public Guardian rather than directly by the court, but the nature of the supervision will essentially be the same. The Department of Health has recently established a working group to identify the actions, resources and time required to enable commencement of the remainder of the 2016 Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how his Department ensures that financial planning services for individuals with disabilities and their families are accessible and aligned with best practices in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: Financial services are reserved to Westminster, with Treasury being the responsible department. This is the same position for Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the resources and training currently provided to healthcare professionals to advise families on the legal options available for decision-making support.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department supports disabled individuals and their families in managing financial and personal affairs when controllership is not available.
answer_text: My Department has processes in place to ensure that those who require support are assisted with Personal Independence Payment (PIP), Disability Living Allowance (DLA) and Attendance Allowance (AA) claims. These include the opportunity to have an appointee or representative to assist with the day-to-day management of claims, and for PIP, the opportunity to be accompanied when undergoing a functional assessment. Where a customer requires additional support, a marker is set on the PIP computer system to ensure that applications are progressed to Capita for assessment where a customer fails to return a claim form to ensure that the customer is not disadvantaged. In addition, all disability benefit customers, including Universal Credit claimants who require support, are referred to the Department’s Make the Call service for assistance, as appropriate.  Make the Call deploys a team of community-based Outreach Officers who conduct home visits with vulnerable people and provide face to face assistance and support with benefit claim completion.All benefit delivery staff also have access to the Department’s advanced customer support guidance which is designed to equip staff to both identify vulnerable customers and those with complex needs and to better support them to access services. Customers are also signposted to independent advice which is available from the Advice NI Helpline. The helpline number is 0800 915 4604 or via email on advice@adviceni.net. This service is free, confidential, independent and impartial.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure that vulnerable adults without family support are provided with appropriate decision-making assistance.
answer_text: There are processes in place for Personal Independence Payment (PIP), Disability Living Allowance and Attendance Allowance to ensure that those who require support are assisted. These include: the opportunity to have an appointee or representative to assist with the day-to-day management of claims, and for PIP, the opportunity to be accompanied when undergoing a functional assessment. Where a customer requires additional support, a marker is set on the PIP computer system to ensure that applications are progressed to Capita for assessment where a customer fails to return a claim form to ensure that the customer is not disadvantaged. In addition, all Universal Credit and disability benefit customers who require advice or support with form completion are signposted to the Department’s Make the Call service and to the independent support provided by Advice NI.  All benefit delivery staff have access to the Department’s advanced customer support guidance which is designed to equip staff to both identify vulnerable customers and those with complex needs and to better support them to access services. Services funded through the Supporting People Programme conduct needs and risk assessments, to ensure individuals receive the right level of decision-making assistance and support tailored to their needs.Additionally, support extends to assistance in engaging with professionals and external organisations, ensuring individuals can navigate services effectively. Where needed, individuals can also be signposted to specialist advocacy services for assistance with complex decision-making.My Department invests around £6.6m annually in independent, community-based advice services which are free at the point of contact. This includes regulated debt advice and support for people impacted by Welfare changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any collaborative work being undertaken with his Executive colleagues in order to prioritise and accelerate the development and implementation of a comprehensive childcare strategy.
answer_text: With the support of my Executive Colleagues, I secured £25m for early learning and childcare provision in 2024-25 and £50m within the draft budget for 2025/26.  The draft Programme for Government also commits the Executive to prioritising the development of an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy by autumn 2025.At an operational level, I have established a senior officials cross-departmental Task and Finish Group to support this work.  In addition to my own department, the group has representation from The Department for the Economy, the Department of Health, the Department for Communities, the Department of the Environment and Rural Affairs, the Department of Finance, Invest Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency.  Sub-groups on specific themes have also been formed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of rising childcare costs on workforce participation, particularly amongst women.
answer_text: I recognise the relationship between the availability of childcare provision and the ability for parents to participate in education and employment, and thus support and grow the economy.  I hear the issues being raised by parents in terms of costs and it’s important that we understand the extent of the issue. With this in mind, my department commissioned research examining key determinants of the gender employment gap, the findings of which supports the evidence that women with children suffer labour market penalties.  I also look forward to the first results of the NISRA childcare survey, expected in May.  The survey collected information on the cost of childcare and the impact of childcare on employment and training decisions.My officials are also assisting the Department of Education with market research on the childcare sector. This research, currently being carried out by Innovation and Consultancy Services (ICS), will determine the key issues impacting the financial sustainability of providers within the childcare sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of a full Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, including (i) the current status; (ii) key milestones; and (iii) expected timelines for publication.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation by autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any considerations to increase the financial support provided through schemes such as the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to better align with the rising costs of childcare.
answer_text: ANSWERIn the Executive’s 2024-25 Budget, £25 million was provided for a range of support measures in respect of early learning and childcare.This included the local Childcare Subsidy Scheme, enabling eligible parents to receive a 15% discount off their childcare bills (in addition to Tax-Free Childcare savings) where their provider is participating in the scheme.  As of the end of January 2025, over 14,000 children had been registered for the scheme, reducing families’ bills on childcare by £5.3 million since its launch in September 2024.A further £50 million has been allocated to early learning and support measures in the draft budget for 2025-26, and it is for the Education Minister to seek Executive agreement on his proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when Housing Executive staff can expect to receive the National Joint Council for Local Government Services National Pay Award.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 21575/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the Executive’s commitment to improving mental health, to detail any steps being taken to ensure that frontline organisations like Samaritans Northern Ireland remain financially viable.
answer_text: The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 was co-designed in 2024 with Community and Voluntary Sector representatives.259 bids totalling over £13m were submitted to a Scheme with a budget of £1.8m. Demand was therefore always going to outstrip supply, leaving more losers than winners. The general standard of applications was incredibly high, and the selection of 25 high scoring applications to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The purpose of the Core Grant scheme is to help Community and Voluntary sector bodies cover their fixed costs and does not directly fund service delivery.The Department’s Core Grant Scheme is a small part of the total health service expenditure which goes to the Community and Voluntary sector, as most money is for the direct provision of services.For example, in 2023/24, 84% of Core Grant recipients were also in receipt of HSC service delivery contracts worth £53m. The Samaritans have not been successful in their application for Core Grant in 2025/26, which I understand will be disappointing for the organisation. My officials will be in contact with all applicants to offer feedback over the coming weeks, which will assist them in applying again in future funding rounds. I expect to issue another call for applications this Autumn for 2026/27 funding.I can assure you that I value the work of the Samaritans and I want to place on record my thanks for their continuing work to support those in need.

Demand was therefore always going to outstrip supply, leaving more losers than winners. The general standard of applications was incredibly high, and the selection of 25 high scoring applications to stage 2 of the assessment process was based solely on agreed scoring criteria, rather than a departmental determination of the value of the applicants. The purpose of the Core Grant scheme is to help Community and Voluntary sector bodies cover their fixed costs and does not directly fund service delivery.The Department’s Core Grant Scheme is a small part of the total health service expenditure which goes to the Community and Voluntary sector, as most money is for the direct provision of services.For example, in 2023/24, 84% of Core Grant recipients were also in receipt of HSC service delivery contracts worth £53m. The Samaritans have not been successful in their application for Core Grant in 2025/26, which I understand will be disappointing for the organisation. My officials will be in contact with all applicants to offer feedback over the coming weeks, which will assist them in applying again in future funding rounds. I expect to issue another call for applications this Autumn for 2026/27 funding.I can assure you that I value the work of the Samaritans and I want to place on record my thanks for their continuing work to support those in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures his Department has in place to ensure that planning policies support the delivery of affordable and social housing.
answer_text: My Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) identifies Council Local Development Plans (LDPs) as the primary planning policy instrument to meet affordable housing need identified through Housing Needs Assessment/Housing Market Analysis. It indicates that LDPs should do this by zoning land for affordable housing, or by indicating, through key site requirements, where a proportion of a site may be required for social/affordable housing. This will not preclude other sites coming forward through the development management process.   My Department performs an important oversight role in seeking to ensure that LDPs take account of regional policy and guidance and also that they add value to and assist in the delivery of higher level Executive priorities, such as the recent Housing Supply Strategy. In this context the Department has been encouraging of LDP approaches that maximise affordable housing provision and support the creation of balanced communities and an appropriate mix of tenures. I can advise that those Councils who have published Plan Strategy documents have included requirements for housing/mixed use planning applications above a specified threshold of dwelling units to provide a proportion of those units as affordable properties.My officials continue to work closely with housing officials in the Department for Communities and colleagues in councils and the NIHE to ensure that collectively, we can facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality homes to meet the needs of everyone.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any specific measures within the Housing Supply Strategy that will address the impact of second home ownership on the availability of affordable housing in coastal communities in East Londonderry.
answer_text: I recognise that the issue of second home ownership is causing concern in some specific areas in relation to the potential impact on housing affordability.  There is very limited data available on second homes which makes it difficult to establish their extent, location and what impacts they may have.For example, the latest Census for Northern Ireland showed that six percent of all houses had no usual residents. There are a range of possibilities for this including that they are truly vacant, being renovated, self-catering/short-term lets or second homes.My Department is carrying out research on second home ownership to improve our understanding of the issues.More broadly, the first objective of the Executive Housing Supply Strategy aims to ensure we deliver more affordable options across all tenures and work is underway to take forward the first action plan.   This work will supplement action to address locally specific issues that may impact on housing requirements in an area, which is primarily taken forward by Local Councils working closely with the Housing Executive Place shaping teams. This includes through the delivery of Local Development Plans, which are responsible for establishing policies to ensure the spatial growth requirements of the local area can be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to address the shortage of affordable and social housing in East Londonderry, particularly in areas where demand is highest.
answer_text: Housing need is generally addressed through the re-let of existing social housing accommodation (Stock turnover). Where the volume of re-lets is insufficient to meet housing need, then construction or acquisition of further (new) social housing is required.In the East Londonderry constituency:  •	51 social housing units have been completed in 2024/25 to date.  •	There are currently 76 social housing units under construction.  •	There are 591 social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2027/28.  It should be noted that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf propertiesCo-Ownership is the Department’s main delivery partner for intermediate housing. In 2023/2024, 43 Co-Ownership homes were delivered in East Londonderry. Wider than this, the Executive Housing Supply Strategy published on 9th December identifies the need for a whole system approach to address housing supply issues. The Strategy recognises there is not one single overarching challenge impacting on supply. As we move to implementation of the Strategy, addressing these challenges will depend on Executive commitment to invest in the actions required to deliver the supply we need.
