question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the actions planned under the Housing Supply Strategy within the next three years to address housing shortages in East Londonderry.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Executive Housing Supply Strategy on 9th December, work is underway to establish cross-Departmental structures to develop the first action plan which will be focussed on addressing the whole system issues that impact on overall supply across all of Northern Ireland.This work will supplement action to address locally specific issues that may impact on housing requirements in an area which is primarily taken forward by Local Councils working closely with the Housing Executive Place shaping teams. This includes through the delivery of Local Development Plans, which are responsible for establishing the zonings and policies to ensure the spatial growth requirements of the local area can be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with housing associations to discuss barriers to delivering social housing in smaller towns and rural villages; and (ii) any financial or regulatory support being considered to overcome these challenges.
answer_text: I am keenly aware that the provision of social housing delivery in rural areas presents significant challenges and requires different solutions than those in urban areas. Engagement with Housing Associations suggests that in addition to difficulties identifying suitable land, there are barriers relating to community infrastructure and sustainability, specifically connections to wastewater systems, adequate roads, NI Water capacity and proximity to schools. Housing Associations have also indicated that it can be difficult to get support for viable schemes due to a lack of evidence of housing need in rural areas.My Department has set up a Steering Group which includes representatives from Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, NI Housing Executive, and Land & Property Services to explore the barriers to rural housing development and develop a plan to address them. Research commissioned by the group has recently been received and is currently being evaluated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on the future of Limavady Courthouse.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the funding available to further education colleges to provide specialist support for students with special educational needs.
answer_text: My Department provides £4.5 million, per annum, ring-fenced funding through the Additional Support Fund (ASF) to Further Education colleges towards the necessary additional technical and/or personal support required to allow students with learning difficulties or disabilities to participate in mainstream Further Education courses, or in discrete programmes.The additional personal and technical support provided through the ASF enables students to gain the skills and qualifications needed to help them meet their goals in education, progress to employment, training, or towards independent living.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made in relation to the impact of the transition from school on the mental health of young people with special educational needs.
answer_text: Transition is recognised to be a time of increased risk of stress and negative emotions for families and young people. The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 is cognisant of transition from education as part of a wider series of interfaces as children enter adult health and social care (HSC) services. Action 13 of the Mental Health Strategy sets out the Department's commitment to develop proposals for transitions between CAMHS and adult mental health services, engaging widely with all relevant stakeholders. Work is ongoing to take this forward.In addition to this, a regional protocol to better manage HSC transitions has been developed with a pilot due to take place later this year

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has engaged with the Department for the Economy and the Department of Education to ensure coordinated support for young people with special educational needs during their transition to adulthood.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with Economy and Education to improve support and services for young people with special educational needs (SEN) as their transition to adult services. A cross-departmental Transitions working group has undertaken work to review and improve the SEN Transitions process and to update the existing Code of Practice through the SEN End to End Review.  The working group includes representatives from Health, Education, Communities, and Economy.  To date, the group has conducted a survey, and the outputs will be used to improve the transitions process which will inform further work to provide children with much greater choice and control on their lives after school, while recognising that Health has a pivotal role to ensure care and health needs are supported. Ongoing cross-departmental work will continue as part of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan. In terms of legislative change, I have committed my support to working with the Department for the Economy as they explore options and review what support is currently available for children over the age of 19 years with SEN.Health and Social Care will support a cohort of children with SEN throughout their life in school and transition to adult services.  Supports provided are dependent on assessed need, although it is likely that HSC services will have a continued presence in the lives of children with disabilities and their families. This may include day activity, transport, short breaks, accommodation-based support, and self-directed support. My officials have worked closely with Trusts to develop a regional protocol for Transitions to ensure that the supports we provide are regionally consistent. The next phase of this work is to commence a localised pilot ahead of regional scale up. The draft protocol has been shared with Economy and Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her Department’s assessment of the accessibility of further educational colleges for those with (i) special educational needs; and (ii) physical disabilities, to ensure these students have the same opportunities as their peers.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of equality and social inclusion within Further Education and there are currently a number of measures in place which aim to remove barriers for students with special educational needs gaining access to, participating in and achieving their goals with Further Education. Ring-fenced funding of £4.5 million, per annum, is provided by my Department through the Additional Support Fund to Further Education colleges to help provide the necessary additional technical and/or personal support required to allow students with learning difficulties or disabilities to participate in mainstream Further Education courses, or in discrete programmes.The fund allows colleges to provide students with technical and personal support to enable them to gain maximum benefit from their courses by removing barriers to study. Examples of technical support include specialised enabling equipment, such as braillers, specialist software and hearing loops. Personal support includes specialist tutors/advisers, such as hearing or visual impairment tutors, interpreters, classroom assistants and note-takers. The level of support provided depends on both the nature of the disability and the course of study.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his Department’s assessment of the current provision of day centres, particularly in rural areas; and (ii) to detail the measures his Department is taking to ensure all young people with special educational needs are able to access this support.
answer_text: (i)Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of health and social care services in accordance with population and individual assessed need. Across all HSCTs adults with a learning disability aged 18 years plus, may access Day Centres where this is in accordance with their assessed need. HSCTs advise that Day Care Services are predominantly located in towns/cities across Trust geographical areas, with some Day Centres located in a number of villages. Where possible and if in accordance with assessed need, adults with a learning disability avail of Day Centre provision most closely located to their home address, to reduce the need for lengthy journeys. Day Care Services are available in the following Trust locations.Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT):17 Statutory Adult Day Care Centres offer support to adults with learning disabilities across the Trust geographical boundary in the following areas:Maybrook: LondonderryOaktree Centre: LondonderryValley Centre: LondonderryEvergreen: LondonderryBenbradagh: LimavadyWillow Group: LimavadyGlenside: StrabaneOmagh Centre: OmaghStrathroy: OmaghDromore Centre: DromoreCastlederg Centre: CastledergKesh Centre: KeshTir Na Var: EnniskillenLackaghbouy 1: EnniskillenLackaghbouy 2: EnniskillenShare Centre: EnniskillenEPC, Lisnaskea: LisnaskeaNorthern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):10 Statutory Adult Day Care Centres offer support to adults with learning disabilities across all four Trust localities as noted below:Mid UlsterMagherafelt Adult CentreCookstown Adult CentreCausewayMountfern, ColeraineRathmoyle, BallycastleMillbrook, BallymoneyAntrim/Ballymena localityAntrim Adult CentreGeorge Sloan Adult Centre, BallymenaEast AntrimHawthorns, CarrickfergusDrumross, NewtownabbeyLarne Adult CentreSouth Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):10 Statutory Day Centres offer support to adults with learning disability in the following Trust locations, 3 in Downpatrick area, 3 in Lisburn and 4 in North Down and Ards.  In addition to this there are 6 independent providers who offer Day Centre facilities across the Trust area.  Southern Health and Social Care Trust: (SHSCT)10 Statutory Day Centres offer Day Care Services to adults with a learning disability, with 2 independent providers also providing Day Care to adults with a learning disability in the following Trust locations. Newry & Mourne– Windsor, Laurels and Crossmaglen (Statutory). Cairnmount and Mourne Stimulus (Independent Sector). Craigavon & Banbridge– Manor, Bannvale and Linenbridge (Statutory). Armagh & Dungannon – Appleby, Oakridge, McCague, and Islandview (Statutory). Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT):8 Statutory Day Centres offer Day Care Services to adults with a learning disability across the Belfast Trust area. In addition, there are 4 offsite community Based Clubs managed and run by people with a learning disability for people with a learning disability supported by BHSCT staff; 2 Soc

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has given to raising the age that the Department of Education has legal responsibility for children with special educational needs to 25, similar to the current position taken by England.
answer_text: Work is on-going within my Department to scope legislation within this Assembly mandate to require young people to continue their education or training until they are age 18.  My officials are currently engaging with stakeholders including officials from the Department for the Economy on the wide range of issues associated with the proposed legislation including those which may impact on children with special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on his plans to introduce legislation to raise the compulsory school age in Northern Ireland to 18; and (ii) when the public consultation will be launched.
answer_text: I do not propose to increase compulsory school age to 18.  Work is on-going within my Department to scope proposals for legislation within this Assembly mandate which would require young people to continue their education or training until they are age 18.  This will require Executive and Assembly approval.  As part of this process, my officials will engage with a wide range of stakeholders. A public consultation is likely to take place this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has engaged with (i) parents of children with special educational needs; and (ii) special schools, in relation to legislation to increase the compulsory school age to 18.
answer_text: I do not propose to increase compulsory school age to 18. Work is on-going within the Department to scope legislation to be enacted within this Assembly mandate which will require young people to continue in education or training until they are age 18.My officials are engaging with stakeholders currently on the wide range of issues associated with this proposed legislation.  Subject to approval by the Executive, engagement with parents of children with special educational needs and special schools will form an important part of the anticipated public consultation in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has undertaken an equality assessment of how legislation to raise the compulsory school age to 18 will impact children with additional needs.
answer_text: I do not propose to increase compulsory school age to 18.  Work is on-going within the Department to scope legislation for enactment during this Assembly mandate which will require young people to continue education or training until they are age 18.  As part of this work, my officials are currently conducting an Equality Screening of the proposed legislation together with a Rural Impact Assessment and Child Rights Impact Assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, as part of legislation to increase the compulsory school age to 18, whether he has considered extending the compulsory school age for children with special education needs to 19.
answer_text: I do not propose to increase compulsory school age to 18. Work is on-going within the Department to bring forward legislation within this Assembly mandate to require young people to continue their education or training until they are age 18.  My officials are engaging with stakeholders currently on the wide range of issues associated with this proposed legislation including the impact on young people with special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that schools address the risks posed by Artificial Intelligence, including misinformation and deepfakes, as part of digital literacy education.
answer_text: My Department provides guidance to schools in relation to online safety risks to children. The ‘Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools’ refers to resources such as those provided through the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland and also the Safer Schools Northern Ireland App. These provide advice, guidance and information on a range of safeguarding issues to schools, parents/carers and pupils. The Safer Schools Northern Ireland App has issued a guide this month to all schools, aimed at preventing and addressing exploitation related to images generated by Artificial Intelligence (AI). This guidance includes creating awareness-raising initiatives to inform students, staff and parents about the risks of online image manipulation, responsible image sharing and potential dangers of AI-generated images. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) also offer a range of resources covering online safety and digital literacy including the Digital for Life and Work non-statutory guidance, Digital for Life and Work | CCEA.The statutory curriculum already includes elements of digital literacy and online safety, particularly within the Using ICT and Personal Development and Mutual Understanding areas at primary level and Learning for Life and Work at post-primary level. My officials are working with CCEA to ensure that emerging AI-related risks, including misinformation and deep fakes, are appropriately reflected within digital literacy education. Departmental officials will continue to engage closely with key stakeholders in relation to the emergent challenges and risks posed by AI, ensuring that they also take into account the benefits that AI can bring to education.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls will address technology-facilitated abuse, including (i) Artificial Intelligence-generated content; (ii) deepfake content; and (iii) online harassment and image-based sexual abuse.
answer_text: Online abuse and violence against women and girls is an area of focus of the Executive’s Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework 2024-31. The Strategic Framework will be agile in its delivery to ensure it can respond to evolving and changing culture and practices, such as the expanding forms of online harm and technological abuse, including deepfake imagery.The Department of Health has sponsorship of the Executive’s ‘Keeping children and young people safe: an Online Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland’ and accompanying three-year Action Plan, which were published in February 2021. The Department is currently drafting Actions for years 4 and 5 of the Strategy. Ofcom, which is now the regulator for online safety under the Online Safety Act 2023, was a key co-design partner in the development of the Strategic Framework.Ofcom is developing draft guidance for protecting women and girls online which is a requirement of the Online Safety Act 2023, and that draft Guidance which is due to launch for consultation in February 2025, will focus on the content and activity covered by the Act which disproportionately affects women and girls, such as online harassment, online misogyny and intimate image abuse. Our officials have been liaising with Ofcom, through two UK wide workshops, as they develop this guidance for consultation.Work is also ongoing through the EVAWG Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) Challenge Fund on six proof of concept projects that will use data, tech, and design innovations to identify solutions that make spaces safer for women and girls, including online.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department plans to engage with the technology sector to promote ethical Artificial Intelligence development and safeguards against its misuse.
answer_text: Ensuring that artificial intelligence (AI) is safely and responsibly developed and deployed is vital to ensuring that we can maximise the benefits of this technology to society. 	The work of the AI Collaboration Centre, funded by my Department and Invest NI, in promoting AI awareness and adoption among businesses in the north includes a strong emphasis on ethical and responsible innovation. 		The regulatory frameworks to govern AI are the responsibility of the British government and potentially the EU. My Department will continue to work on a collaborative basis within the north and across these islands, including with the technology sector, to harness the positive potential of the safe and responsible use of this technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions she has had with the (i) UK Government; and (ii) other devolved administrations in relation to cross-jurisdictional efforts to address Artificial Intelligence-generated misinformation and deepfake technology.
answer_text: I recognise the significant harm and distress that can be caused by the use of deepfake technology, both in terms of disinformation and also in the creation of deepfake sexual imagery. It can destroy victims’ lives. Telecommunications and internet legislation are reserved matters.  However, online safety is also a cross-cutting issue where a holistic approach needs to be taken to address the issue more widely.  This is demonstrated in the Executive’s Online Safety Strategy which has been established to assist children and young people to participate in the online world in a positive, safe and responsible way.   Where responsibility falls to my Department, I am committed to strengthening our laws to further protect our community from abusive and fraudulent behaviours facilitated by deepfake technology. I continue to consider all opportunities available to me to do so, including both through the extension of UK Government legislation and through Assembly legislation. I have been in contact with the UK Government on a range of related proposals intended for its upcoming Crime and Policing Bill to explore where these may be extended to Northern Ireland, subject to the agreement of the Assembly.  I understand my Scottish Government counterparts have a similar interest in extension of particular proposals. This had included a proposal to introduce a new offence of creating a sexually explicit deepfake image without consent, which I was keen to extend to Northern Ireland.  However, this proposal has since been brought forward for England and Wales by way of an amendment to the current Data (Access and Use) Bill, tabled by the UK Government on 22 January 2025. As we were not made aware of this change of plan by the UK Government in advance, I am now considering the options available for Northern Ireland, and I remain committed to strengthening the protection for victims of deepfake imagery. I continue to work with counterparts on other aspects of the Bill of particular interest to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure (i) businesses; and (ii) consumers are protected from Artificial Intelligence-driven scams and misinformation.
answer_text: Significant work is ongoing across responsible agencies, such as Ofcom, the Consumer Council and Trading Standards Service to protect businesses and consumers from online harms. The recent UK Online Safety Act provides a range of protections against such scams and fraudulent activity, including many types of AI generated deepfake content, and provides powers of enforcement to Ofcom to deal with malicious actors. My Department’s Trading Standards Service works to tackle online and mass marketing scams and disrupt the operations of perpetrators behind them and they continue to raise awareness of the latest frauds, including AI scams, through media appearances, press releases and social media alerts about. They deliver educational talks to local community groups and businesses while working with banks, charities and law enforcement agencies to identify victims of fraud.The Consumer Council works closely with a range of partners and community sector organisations to provide support for consumers including tailored resources and regional outreach to promote scam awareness and prevention, and this will remain a priority in 2025.The Consumer Council and Trading Standards Service are also members of the ScamwiseNI partnership, alongside other organisations such as the PSNI and Department of Justice, which promotes awareness and provides education to the public on the risks of scams. My department will continue to work with these organisations and others to promote safe and ethical use of AI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to establish local facilities for infant post-mortem examinations to prevent the need for transportation to England.
answer_text: I am committed to finding a better resolution to the issue of local access for local perinatal and paediatric pathology. Whilst my first preference would be the restoration of a dedicated paediatric pathology service for Northern Ireland, such an outcome continues to remain incredibly challenging as the services are under considerable pressure not just here but across the world due to a global shortage of paediatric pathologists.I am keen to explore the feasibility of an all-island approach to these services and, as I confirmed to the Health Committee, I have had a number of conversations with Minister Donnelly about the possibility of future co-operation, which, whilst still at the very early stages of development and consideration, have been very positive.As these services move to more centralised models nationally, my priority in the meantime is to ensure that we continue to have access to high-quality, reliable and timely pathology services at Alder Hey, to ensure that parents can get the medical answers they need following their loss. Access has been secured by a further extension of these arrangements.Officials will continue to work with NHS England and colleagues across the UK and Ireland to explore possible solutions to capacity issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of families have opted to send their child to Alder Hey Children's Hospital paediatric pathology service in the last five years.
answer_text: The number of babies/children that have been assessed by Alder Hey Children's Hospital paediatric pathology service in the last five years are detailed below:YEARNumber of babies/children20201542021217202219820232002024181

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department’s assessment of the current interim arrangement with Alder Hey Children's Hospital paediatric pathology service, mindful of the impact the current process has on grieving parents.
answer_text: The Service Level Agreement (SLA) between Belfast HSC Trust and Alder Hey Hospital Trust for the provision of this service has been in place since January 2019, when the service in Belfast became unsustainable following the resignation of the sole paediatric pathologist in Northern Ireland. The SLA for this agreement has recently been extended on a rolling three-year basis until 2027 with the caveat that we can exit this contract at any stage should we be in a position to re-establish  the service in Northern Ireland.  Post-mortem uptake levels are now almost consistent with those prior to the cessation of the service in Belfast. The numbers of placenta histologies being sent for examination have risen in recent years. Further research is required to understand the underlying reasons for this rise.The Pubic Health Agency (PHA) and my Department gave an undertaking to stakeholders involved in planning of the interim move to Alder Hey to evaluate the service after 1 year. In addition to qualitative measures, it was agreed that an understanding of the experience of staff and most importantly, families, would be undertaken by an organisation external to the HSC.  In light of the specialist and sensitive nature of this evaluation, Queens University Belfast were approached to undertake this work.  The evaluation was supported by my Department and PHA with advice being provided by a stakeholder representative steering group.  Unfortunately, due to Covid pressures this evaluation was paused for a period of time. A full evaluation report has now been developed jointly by my Department and PHA which encompasses the qualitative report produced by Queens University Belfast along with a full-service evaluation and includes key performance indicator information. This report contains 21 recommendations in relation to how services and care could be improved for example in relation to timeliness of reporting, communication, and follow-up for parents. This report is currently going through the necessary approval process before it can be released.  I am committed to finding a better resolution to the issue of local access for local perinatal and paediatric pathology. Whilst my first preference would be the restoration of a dedicated paediatric pathology service for Northern Ireland, such an outcome continues to remain incredibly challenging as the services are under considerable pressure not just here but across the world due to a global shortage of paediatric pathologists.I am keen to explore the feasibility of an all-island approach to these services and, as I confirmed to the Health Committee, I have had a number of conversations with Minister Donnelly about the possibility of future co-operation, which, whilst still at the very early stages of development and consideration, have been very positive.As these services move to more centralised models nationally, my priority in the meantime is to ensure that we continue to have access to high-quality, reliable and t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support in place for families who have suffered the loss of a child through (i) miscarriage; and (ii) stillbirth.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 21687/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to improving the provision of bereavement suites to ensure each family has a quiet and comfortable space to grieve.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have confirmed their individual position on their provision of bereavement suites.Western TrustThe Western Trust has a bereavement suite in both of the Maternity units at Altnagelvin and SWAH. These were designed in partnership with families who had experienced pregnancy loss and the essential works to create these suites were funded between the Trust and charitable funds from SANDS.South Eastern TrustThe South Eastern Trust has Sanctuary Rooms at the Ulster, Lagan Valley and Downe hospital sites; these rooms provide families a quiet and comfortable space to grieve; for noting the room at the Ulster Hospital site was renovated last year. In addition, there is a bereavement suite in their Maternity unit.South Eastern Trust also confirmed that within the Directorate of Surgery, Elective, Maternity & Acute Paediatrics, there are a number of bespoke 'Quiet' Rooms & 'Relatives' rooms for parents, families and relatives adjacent to the clinical areas that can be used at any time.  These areas are available to families and relatives when patients are unwell or have been bereaved.Southern TrustWithin acute Paediatric Services, the CAH neonatal unit has 2 transitional care rooms which can be made available to provide privacy and space to grieving families. The SCBU in DHH also has a transitional care room that can be made available for this purpose, in addition to the Willow room in DHH maternity if not in use.Whilst the Paediatric wards do not have bereavement suites, they do have individual rooms with blinds in the windows that provide bereaved families with privacy and space to grieve. The staff endeavour to ensure the room is in the most suitable area to meet their needs.Both the emergency departments in the Southern Trust have bereavement rooms for families to grieve. These have been in place for a considerable period of time. At present the Trust is developing a business case to extend and improve the facility on the Craigavon site with recognition that for families of a sudden infant death, they may need to remain on site for a longer period of timeWithin Surgery and Clinical Services, in response to patient feedback, work is ongoing with estates to develop bereavement suites in the gynae ward in CAH.Unfortunately they do not have bereavement suites in our non-acute hospitals. The SHSCT have purpose built Bereavement Suites situated in the Delivery Suite where mothers are admitted for labour and birth.  Both these suites are geographically located to avoid additional trauma for parents/visitors by meeting other birthing parents. The area has been sound proofed to avoid any noise disturbance. These rooms are suitable for any gestation usually used for 17-42 weeks gestation. Both suites have soft colours and furnishing to ensure the room appears less clinical and more conducive to the needs of bereaved families. Families remain in these room for labour and delivery and then discharged home.Within Critical care (ICU) they do have tw

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any contingency plans in place to ensure timely access to emergency and general surgery for residents of East Londonderry if those services are removed from the Causeway Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) emergency; and (ii) general surgery procedures that have taken place at Causeway Hospital in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Health produces statistics on the use of operating theatres in Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) hospitals in Northern Ireland, each year, broken down by Hospital and type of case. This data can be found at the link: Statistics on Theatre Utilisation by Hospital, HSC Trust and Classification 2023/24. This data goes back beyond the last five years. Type of cases are broken down as per the National Confidential Enquiry into Patient Outcome and Death (NCEPOD) classifications. The figures do not include obstetric delivery rooms containing a delivery bed, dental treatment rooms (or surgeries containing a dental chair), X-ray rooms, or rooms used only for endoscopies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department anticipates that a final decision will be announced in relation to the future of emergency and general surgery services at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern HSC Trust held a public consultation on proposals to reconfigure general surgery services between 23 August and 29 November 2024.The Trust is currently preparing a consultation outcomes report which will be submitted to the Department of Health in due course, and will consider all likely impacts of proposals. I will consider any recommendation made by the NHSCT at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to enhance or introduce alternative medical services at Causeway Hospital if emergency and general surgery services are removed, to ensure that the hospital remains a key healthcare provider in the region.
answer_text: Information provided by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number (whole-time equivalent) of speech and language therapists employed at 31 December 2024, and how many are based in stroke units, is detailed in the table below.HSC TrustSpeech and language therapists employed (WTE) at 31 December 2024Of which: WTE based in stroke unitsBelfast157.13.8Northern111.20.8*South Eastern113.80.8 Southern116.92.3Western89.30**Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH* 0.8 WTE in Antrim, no S&LT based on Causeway stroke unit with the service provided by Causeway S&LT core team **3.0 WTE based in the community and inreach to the stroke units.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any alternative solutions that have been considered to sustain emergency and general surgery services at Causeway Hospital, including options for recruitment, investment and service reconfiguration.
answer_text: The Northern Trust’s Strategic Vision for Causeway Hospital (March 2024) includes a clear commitment to maintaining a 24/7 Emergency Department and acute inpatient services in Causeway Hospital and sets out an ambitious vision for the development and enhancement of services in Causeway. The Trust’s review of general surgical services has recently been subject to public consultation, and the Trust has been clear that its preferred option does not impact on Causeway’s status as an acute hospital. This is consistent with the Minister’s position that Causeway Hospital will remain a key element of the hospital network in Northern Ireland.The Trust held a public consultation on proposals to reconfigure general surgery services between 23 August and 29 November 2024. The Trust is currently preparing a consultation outcomes report which will be presented to Northern HSC Trust Board on 27th March and then submitted to the Department of Health in due course. I will consider any recommendation made by the NHSCT at that time.   In line with the DoH Circular on Change or Withdrawal of Service, the final decision on a permanent change or withdrawal of service rests with the Minister.During and prior to this consultation process, engagement with stakeholders and staff regarding the vision for Causeway hospital was central.  The staff are enthusiastic about recent service improvements such as the extension of ambulatory care, frailty services and the vision of an elective and diagnostic hub in this area.  The options for recruitment and continued investment into Causeway will continue as Causeway is central to the plans with regard to the configuration of the acute hospital configuration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s position on carers employed via Direct Payments administering medication and feeding via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube.
answer_text: The Carers and Direct Payments Act (Northern Ireland) 2002 and the Direct Payments Scheme guidance both stipulate that Direct Payments are intended to allow service users to commission carers to provide assessed personal social services.  Currently, there is no provision for healthcare interventions to be delivered by carers employed through Direct Payments.Since the administration of medication and feeding via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube (PEG) is a healthcare intervention, this should not be undertaken by a carer employed via Direct Payments. I am aware, however, that across the Health and Social Care sector here there are variances in the approach taken to supporting the delegation of such interventions to carers employed through Direct Payments and recognise that this situation needs to be resolved.  My Department is actively reviewing the arrangements in place across the region for the delegation of healthcare interventions, with the aim of supporting individuals to live as independently as possible.  As part of that process, the Chief Nursing Officer has commissioned the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC) to assess the practices currently in operation and identify viable pathways that will facilitate the safe and effective delegation of healthcare interventions, in line with the governing guidelines and legislation. The review is anticipated to be completed by Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department currently implements to ensure full time carers for those who have complex care requirements receive respite.
answer_text: Monitoring and analysis of short breaks (respite) for mental health, older people, physical and sensory disability services and learning disability services are integrated into standardised performance monitoring arrangements between HSC Trusts and Department of Health officials.   Within children’s services there is ongoing regional monitoring of short break provision for children with disability.  It is acknowledged that there are challenges accessing short break services regionally across children services.  In October 2024, the Minister approved additional regional funding for the children with disability service. It is intended that this funding will assist with increasing short breaks capacity, and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports.      Emergency short breaks are available for children and adults.   It is provided in critical or unforeseen situations when primary caregivers are unexpectedly unavailable, through illness or emergencies, or there may be a safeguarding concern which may mean that care needs provided outside of the family home.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department currently implements to ensure those who require feeding or medication via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube can access Health and Social Care Trust-employed carers who are permitted to carry out such care.
answer_text: My department has sought answers from the five HSC Trusts in relation to their current processes, please see their responses below: -Belfast TrustChildren’s Community Nursing (CCN) currently have approximately 80 children on caseload requiring PEG feeding.  The CCN team support families with provision of required equipment/feeding sets but do not provide carers to administer enteral feeds and medication.  As a rule, parents assume this role and receive the requisite training before their child is discharged from hospital.  Parents, if eligible, can also use Direct Payments to facilitate additional support.  The CCN team can provide training and order equipment for these specific clients.Northern TrustAcross the programmes of care there a number of pathways to ensure those who require feeding or medication via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube have their needs met.With respect to children with a disability, the Trust has a ‘children with a disability’ resource panel. An Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland  (UNOCINI) is completed in respect of any child being presented at panel (which will include recommendations in terms of level of care (hours) and which service will undertake that support including any delegated nursing tasks, such as PEG feeding. If it is a child with complex physical healthcare needs the UNOCINI is also supported by a CMAC (Comprehensive Multidisciplinary Assessment of the Child) – coordinated by the paediatric discharge transitions coordinator. In adult services, PEG feeds and PEG medications following an assessment of need are generally outsourced as per the Delegation of Nursing Tasks to Independent Service Providers & Protocol for Accessing Enhanced Packages of Care policy.Within Adult Learning Disability Services, people who require feeding or medication via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube will have an assessment of their need completed; this informs a determination regarding which service will undertake that support including any delegated nursing tasks. For those individuals attending Trust supported living, short break or day centres PEG feeds (and medication) are available through Trust trained care staff; and for others this may be through commissioning with the independent sector.South Eastern TrustService users who require feeding or medication via a Percutaneous Endoscopic Gastrostomy tube (PEG) will have an individual patient risk assessment completed by the relevant community nursing team. This will determine best interest and if agreed; who is best placed to support the use of the Percutaneous Endoscopic Gastrostomy tube for medication and/or feeding. The individual could be the service user themselves, a family member, a carer; a Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) employed carer or a classroom staff member; identified by an individual school.A programme of education is delivered by the relevant community nursing team. This will include demonstration and observ

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department’s assessment of the impact of the Northern Health and Social Care Trust’s decision to not permit carers employed via Direct Payments to administer medication or feeding via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of health and social care services in accordance with population and individual assessed need. It is therefore not considered appropriate for my Department to comment on the impact of specific operational decisions made by any HSCT.Individuals can choose to avail of Direct Payments, rather than availing of statutory service provision, aligned to assessed need. My Department is aware of the challenges regarding care provision related to the administration of medication or feeding via a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube.My Department is urgently undertaking a review of the delegation of health care tasks in relation to care provision and is engaging with stakeholders, including HSCTs, service users and families to address the issues that have arisen and make recommendations on the way forward.  Pending completion of this work regional consideration will be required in respect of recommendations, to determine implementation of changes as perceived appropriate, to address important care considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on his Department’s review of the delegation of healthcare tasks in relation to care provision; (ii) when he expects the review to be completed; and (iii) whether his Department has engaged with family members who will be directly affected by any proposed changes.
answer_text: My Department is actively reviewing the delegation of healthcare interventions. The initial focus is on the delegation of healthcare interventions under direct payments within Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts, the delegation of healthcare to social care practitioners employed by HSC Trusts and third-party providers, and the delegation of healthcare for school children with complex medical needs.The Chief Nursing Officer commissioned the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC) to lead this important work, with the aim of supporting individuals to live as independently as possible. NIPEC will establish the practices currently in operation and identify viable pathways that will facilitate the safe and effective delegation of healthcare in line with the governing guidelines and legislation. The review is anticipated to be completed by Autumn 2025.My Department is committed to engaging fully with all stakeholders throughout this process, including service users, family members, carers, and health and social care professionals. To date, we have held meetings with key stakeholders, people with lived experience, and advocacy groups, including the Centre for Independent Living in NI. We are also engaging with the Patient Client Council to allow them to fulfil their statutory function of independently representing the interests of the Public in Health and Social Care.In September 2024, Improving Adult Care Together (IMPACT) commenced collaborative work with the Centre for Independent Living in NI, following their identification of service user concerns regarding the safe and effective delegation of complex health and care interventions for children and adults during transitions. NIPEC is working in partnership with IMPACT, maintaining a strong focus on the lived experiences of people drawing on care and support, carers, and the practice knowledge of social care staff in the development of a new model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to ensure that children with special educational needs living in rural areas have equal access to high-quality support and education services.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan sets out a series of actions focused on providing our children and young people with SEN, from all areas, with the Right Support, from the Right People, at the Right Time and in the Right Place.  By tailoring support to the needs of individual children, we will ensure that geographic location does not impact access to the quality of support and education services provided.At present, to ensure that children with SEN in rural areas have access to high-quality support and education services, the Education Authority (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) allocates its resources strategically based on need.Allocation is determined through an annually reviewed formula that considers 50% enrolment and 50% social deprivation (measured by Free School Meal Entitlement (FSME)).  This ensures fair distribution of staff across urban and rural areas, aligning with identified needs.The EA Specialist Setting Support Team (SSST) supports staff in specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS) throughout Northern Ireland to improve outcomes for children and young people.All the EA SEN and/or Disability (SEND) Pupil Support Services (PSS) provide training, advice and support within mainstream schools across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his assessment of the impact that delays in statutory assessments and school placements have had on children with special educational needs and their families; and (ii) any immediate measures being taken to reduce waiting times.
answer_text: My department, along with the Education Authority (EA), recognises the impact that delays in school placements have had on children with special educational needs (SEN) and their families.  The lack of capacity across special schools and available local Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS) has impacted on the time taken to complete the statements for some children as a place must be secured to finalise the statement.  I recognise that this may impact children and their families being able to prepare for a new school placement; transition arrangements for children in getting to know their new teacher and environment and access to other supports that may be provided for within the statement. In order to mitigate such impacts, bespoke contingency arrangements are put in place to ensure that there is no or minimal impact for our children and young people.  Each solution is based on the individual circumstances of the children within each school and may include one or more of the following: alternative classroom provision, home visits, stay and play sessions, dual days. The Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS) has recruited additional SEN Link Officers to support reduction of caseloads and help in ensuring that communication with the families affected is prioritised. During the summer of 2024, Family Support Link Officers were recruited to support families waiting on placements to be secured.I recently published the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.  A key year one action, already underway, is the streamlining of the statutory assessment process and the EA is leading the way on this aspect of reform through its digitisation of the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda will ensure that children with special educational needs in mainstream schools receive the necessary support; and (ii) the safeguards that are in place to prevent children with special educational needs in mainstream schools from being disadvantaged due to resource constraints.
answer_text: My Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan, published on 4 February 2025, set out an ambitious programme for change to ensure the education system can meet the needs of all children with SEN, regardless of the educational setting they attend.  The Delivery Plan contains a wide range of actions to be implemented across mainstream and special education provision.Successful delivery of the SEN Reform Agenda will require investment and, as indicated in the documents, my department awaits the outcomes of a bid to the NICS Transformation Fund which would provide some of the investment necessary to commence implementation of the Delivery Plan.  I have also sought the support of Executive colleagues in securing this much needed investment which would ensure delivery of the draft Programme for Government (PfG) priority ‘Better Support for Children and Young People with Special Educational Needs’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the expected time frame for the implementation of the first steps of the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda.
answer_text: A number of actions from within the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda are already underway or due to begin imminently.  These include:Publication of the Education Authority’s (EA) Operational Plan 2 (OP2) on 16 January 2025 which prioritises strategic area planning for special education.Pre-school education settings from November 2024 have received additional resource for children undergoing statutory assessment, estimated at a cost of £1.2m for this financial year.Streamlining of the assessment and SEN placements process to commence.The EA, in 2025, will implement its Graduated Response Framework (GRF) and new Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) model for Pupil Support Services (PSS).The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) has now been commissioned to evaluate specialist education provision including the early years foundation stage provisions.Dependent on the success of my department’s transformation bid, work will also commence this year on a range of actions focusing on early intervention, speech, language and communication, enhanced nurture programme, inclusive play environments and special schools as centres of expertise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any strategies being implemented to address workforce shortages in key special educational needs services, including educational psychologists, speech and language therapists and specialist teaching staff.
answer_text: I published my Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan on 4 February 2025, which is designed to meet the needs of children with SEN by addressing, among others, the issues raised in your question.As part of the Right People aspect of my Delivery Plan, I am committed to increasing the Educational Psychologist (EP) workforce and to working closely with Health and Social Care (HSC) services to ensure that the necessary resources are available to all children and young people with SEN.Since 2006, the department has funded student places on the Doctorate in Educational, Child and Adolescent Psychology (DECAP) course at Queens University Belfast (QUB).  The DECAP is the only professional training course in educational psychology within Northern Ireland and it provides the relevant qualification to enable registration as a practitioner.The funding of this course ensures a sufficient pool of suitably qualified EPs to enable the Education Authority (EA) to meet its statutory obligations in line with the SEN and Disability (SEND) (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 and the SEND Act 2016 and respond to the good practice guidelines within the department’s Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of SEN.I will be trialling and implementing a Speech, Language and Communication Programme designed to deliver early intervention for emerging needs.  This will be aided by the introduction of toolkits to assist speech and language therapists.The training and development of specialist teaching staff will be informed by my child-centred Integrated Workforce Planning Framework.  I will also explore a wide range of options designed to recruit and develop high-quality SEN practitioners who can offer the right support at the right time for the children and young people of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures her Department is taking to tackle the growing issue of sexual violence and abuse within digital spaces, including the spread of non-consensual intimate images and online misogynistic abuse.
answer_text: Sexual violence and abuse in any form is totally unacceptable.  I am mindful of the growing prevalence of online violence and abuse as our modern technologies continue to advance at a concerning rate, and that the majority of such abuse is targeted against females. While online safety is a reserved matter, I look for opportunities that are available within the devolved space to strengthen the law to help provide further protections from sexual predators.   There is already a comprehensive and robust legal framework for sexual offences in place in Northern Ireland, which is contained, for the most part, within the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008. The offences address both online and offline behaviour.  I strengthened the law in the last Assembly mandate by introducing a range of new offences via the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, to include upskirting, downblousing, cyber flashing as well as four new offences designed to tackle the particular behaviour of an adult pretending to be a child for the purposes of child sexual grooming, which are unique to Northern Ireland. I also reinforced the offence of disclosure of private sexual images, more commonly referred to as ‘revenge porn’, by bringing threatening to disclose within its definition.I have been in contact with the UK Government on a range of proposals intended for its upcoming Crime and Policing Bill. This included a proposal to introduce a new offence of creating a sexually explicit deepfake image without consent which I was keen to extend to Northern Ireland. However, this proposal has since been brought forward for England and Wales by way of amendment to the current Data (Access and Use) Bill tabled by the UK Government on 22 January 2025.  As we were not made aware of this change of plan by the UK Government in advance, I am now considering the implications for Northern Ireland.  I remain committed to strengthening the protection for victims of deepfake imagery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how character references are currently used in the criminal justice system; and (ii) whether her Department has any plans to introduce guidance or legislative reform to limit or regulate the use of good character references in sexual violence and abuse cases, similar to those being considered in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: It is a matter for the judiciary to determine what is relevant to the sentencing process. The purpose of character references under common law is to provide the court with insight into any factors that could mitigate the defendant's sentence.This may be relevant when the defendant does not have a criminal history or a history of the same type of offence, but the courts have made it clear that personal circumstances should not normally rank highly in a serious case.I acknowledge the potential inequalities, highlighted by the Gillen Review,  between complainants and defendants in serious sexual offence cases, including the fact that the complainant’s testimony is often treated with disbelief and the defendant, who can call good character evidence, may seek to undermine their character or actions whereas the complainant is not accorded the right to call character evidence or witnesses on their behalf. It is my preference to end or limit the use of good character references in certain categories of offending, particularly domestic and sexual offending.  My Department is, therefore, actively seeking to address the use of good character references, which will require legislation.  In the meantime, I would urge everyone to exercise personal responsibility when it comes to providing character references for use by a court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has engaged with the PSNI in relation to low conviction rates for sexual offences.
answer_text: There is ongoing engagement between my Department, the PSNI and other criminal justice organisations to address a range of issues relating to sexual offences, including conviction rates. A number of measures have been or are being progressed in partnership with the PSNI. I anticipate that, collectively, these measures will increase victims’ confidence in the criminal justice system, improve their experience and contribute to reducing attrition. Consequently, they are therefore likely to impact positively on conviction rates. These measures include:research into attrition to understand the scale and the reasons for victims of sexual offences disengaging from the criminal justice process. This research is being conducted in partnership with the PSNI and PPS with an initial report expected before the end of March. The report will be used to inform future policies aimed at reducing attrition and therefore potentially increasing convictions; and,new interventions, including those under the Gillen Review implementation programme, that are helping to support victims and to raise their confidence in the justice system. Examples of these include:the establishment of Remote Evidence Centres (RECs) in Belfast and Craigavon, in addition to the existing NSPCC-led facility in Derry/Londonderry. RECs allow vulnerable witnesses to give their best evidence from a secure and comfortable location and importantly, they help reduce the risk of re-traumatisation by removing the possibility that victims might encounter the defendant or their supporters in or around the courthouse on the day of the trial. The overall conviction rate for REC cases stands at 91%, with 62% by guilty plea without the need for the victim to give oral evidence;new legislation to protect the anonymity of complainants and victims of serious sexual offences by excluding the public from Crown Court hearings, as well as any subsequent appeal; and,work with PSNI to raise awareness of the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) service, which my Department launched in April 2021. The scheme provides free, independent legal advice to adult complainants in serious sexual offence cases. It has so far provided advice to over 2,000 adult complainants and feedback has been overwhelmingly positive, with many telling us that without SOLA support they would have not continued with their case. An equivalent service to provide free legal advice to child victims and their families launched on 5 February 2025.My Department has also engaged with PSNI and other criminal justice partners to inform the development and introduction of new specific offences to strengthen the law and provide additional tools for law enforcement in response to sexual offending. This includes the introduction of new laws to deal with behaviours commonly referred to as upskirting, downblousing and cyber-flashing as well as new laws around stalking and non-fatal strangulation. These new laws will provide greater protections to individuals at

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any training and guidance currently provided to (i) judges; and (ii) legal practitioners on the appropriate weight to be given to good character references in cases of sexual violence and abuse.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for the training and guidance provided to judges or legal practitioners.The Judicial Studies Board (JSB), chaired by a Lord Justice of Appeal, is responsible for judicial training in Northern Ireland. The aim of the JSB is to ensure as far as possible that judicial office holders are equipped with the skills and knowledge which they require to discharge their duties effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any measures in place to ensure victims of sexual violence and abuse are supported throughout the court process; (ii) the number of victims that have availed of such services in the last five years; and (iii) any measures her Department has taken to promote such services.
answer_text: Sexual violence and abuse in any form is entirely unacceptable, and my Department is fully committed to collaborating with a range of other departments, agencies, local partnerships, and specialist organisations in the voluntary and community sector to support victims.Last September, the Health Minister and I jointly launched the new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy. One of the key components of this strategy is the Support and Provision Pillar, which focuses on assisting those impacted by domestic and/or sexual abuse, including addressing the broader effects on family and friends. This pillar emphasises understanding and accommodating the diverse needs of victims, ensuring services are victim-centred with an aim to provide tailored, responsive, and specialised support to help all victims recover and rebuild their lives.Other initiatives, services and measures to support victims of sexual crime include:Supporting victim support servicesMy Department annually provides over one million pounds to the Victim Support NI Witness Service and the NSPCC Young Witness Service to ensure that all prosecution witnesses and victims (including victims of sexual violence and abuse) attending court are offered emotional support and practical information.  This includes a chance to visit the court before giving evidence, explaining the court process, accompanying the victim or witness into the courtroom or live link room, and offering the opportunity to talk when the case has ended. The number of victims and witnesses supported over the past five years is set out below (please note, figures are for all crimes, not just offences of a sexual nature).2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24TotalVictim Support NI Witness Service5,2011,7514,0864,3324,25819,628NSPCC Young Witness Service4802064434123481,889Totals5,6811,9574,5294,7444,60621,517From July 2023, my Department has provided funding of around £46K per annum to Victim Support NI for the continued delivery of the Children and Young People’s Independent Sexual Violence Advocate (ChISVA) service.  ‘ChISVAs’ work closely with the PSNI, The Rowan (Sexual Assault Referral Centre), PPS, NSPCC Young Witness Service and other relevant organisations to provide advocacy and support to children and young people and their families who have been victims of sexual violence, including child sexual abuse and child sexual exploitation. From July 2023 to March 2024, 265 victims were supported by the ChISVA service.Victim Support NI and the NSPCC promote their services on their website, on social media and through posters and leaflets in suitable locations such as police stations and courthouses.The Department raises awareness of these services through NI Direct, the Victims of Crime leaflet, the Victim and Witness Charters and other relevant departmental publications.Advocacy ServicesMy Department contracted a new advocacy service for victims of domestic and sexual abuse in September 2021. The contract was awarded to ASSIST NI who w

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how his Department intends to secure and allocate sufficient funding to fully implement the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032, ensuring that resources are effectively distributed between prevention and treatment initiatives.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is taking to ensure equal access to cancer diagnosis and treatment services for patients in rural areas.
answer_text: My department is committed to ensuring equitable and timely access to cancer diagnosis and treatment services for all. The majority of cancer patients have access to diagnostics, cancer surgery and Systematic Anti-Cancer treatment (SACT: chemotherapy and immunotherapy) within their home trusts.  Patients may need to travel outside their Health & Social Care Trust area of residence to access more specialised treatments such as radiotherapy at the NI Cancer Centre and the North West Cancer Centre, or for advanced surgical or tertiary treatments in specialist centres. Since the pandemic, many treatment services have increased efforts to assess and review patients via virtual appointments where feasible. Additionally, where clinically appropriate, prescriptions have changed to oral treatments and longer administration intervals to reduce the frequency of hospital visits. Plans are also being developed for patient self-administration of some SACT treatments. Some trusts also offer SACT medicines delivery service, or a medicine vending facility to reduce frequency of hospital visits, or waits at hospital.As outlined in the ‘Health and Social Care NI: A three-year plan’. I state my commitment to addressing health inequalities. I am seeking to bring care closer to home where feasible. Having easier access to services by locating them in the community will be an important part of achieving greater population health and wellbeing. Out of hospital care is part of the overall long-term direction of health service reform. Where a patient requires on-going treatment and care following a cancer diagnosis, the development of ambulatory care services, or phlebotomy hubs as outlined in the NI Cancer Strategy will help to achieve this, however these developments will be dependent on cancer strategy funding. There has been a significant increase in referrals for suspect cancers since 2019/20. Workforce capacity issues in several specialisms integral to cancer diagnosis including, endoscopy, radiology and pathology have inevitably impacted diagnostic demand and waits, potentially affecting patient outcomes. To ensure patients have faster access to diagnostics, some services will need to operate from a smaller number of sites. New regional initiatives to improve timely access to diagnostics, such as Elective Day Procedure Centres and Rapid Diagnosis Centres have been developed.  Some patients are eligible for financial support with travel costs and this can often be means tested.  Patients who are travelling long distances for appointments will be made aware of travel support available to them (e.g. travel costs if eligible, parking costs, and volunteer driver services). My department has also funded the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry at Queens University to develop a Routes to Diagnosis project. This project aims to identify the key event in each cancer patient’s pathway that most directly led to their cancer diagnosis, whether through GP red flag referral, scr

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any investment his Department is making in community-based cancer care services, to support early diagnosis and reduce the need for patients in rural areas to travel long distances for treatment.
answer_text: My department has provided investment of £10.7 million through the Cancer Charities Support Fund to support cancer charities in providing a wide range of support services. Projects cover all areas in the cancer pathway, from pre-diagnosis through to palliative and end of life care. These are key initiatives towards the implementation of the Cancer Strategy, and important in working towards my Department’s aim of building cancer service capacity in communities. Trust cancer treatment services are enhancing patient care by increasing virtual appointments, switching to oral treatments and extending medicines administration intervals, where clinically appropriate. This will reduce the frequency of hospital visits and need to travel. A number of Trusts also have a Systemic Anti-Cancer Medicines (SACT: chemotherapy and immunotherapy) home delivery service. A policy is also being developed in 2025 for patient self-administration of some SACT treatments.Regional initiatives like Elective Day Procedure Centres and Rapid Diagnosis Centres aim to speed up diagnostics. Some patients may need to travel further, but they will be informed about available travel support, such as help with travel and parking costs, and volunteer driver services.The ‘Health and Social Care NI: A three-year plan: to stabilise reform and deliver’, aims to address health inequalities and aims to bring care ‘closer to home’ where feasible.  For cancer patients, this includes development of ambulatory care services, or phlebotomy hubs as outlined in the NI Cancer Strategy. However, these developments will be dependent on cancer strategy fundingThe new Integrated Care System is forming Area Integrated Partnerships bringing together Health and Social Care, the voluntary and community sector, local Government and service users and carers. These partnerships will focus on prevention, early intervention and community health and well-being. Rollout has commenced and is expected to complete in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans to strengthen early intervention and screening programmes to identify at-risk individuals and prevent the progression of preventable cancers.
answer_text: Health inequalities is one of my key areas of focus and the ‘Live Better’ approach designed to tackle health inequalities by bringing targeted health support to communities which need it most. The approach is focusing initially on Health and Social Care services and will look at better alignment of, and access to, existing resources and services. Early intervention and prevention will be key components of the Live Better approach.There are currently three cancer screening programmes in Northern Ireland: bowel, breast and cervical cancer. A fourth programme, a targeted lung screening programme for those at higher risk of disease, is at the early stages of development.The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland (2022-32) contains three recommendations relating to screening programmes, in line with the UK National Screening Committee’s (UK NSC) recommendations:Action 7: Reduce sensitivity levels and extend the age range for the bowel screening programme;Action 8: Implement HPV testing in the cervical screening programme; andAction 10: implement all UK National Screening Committee recommendations.The NI Cervical Screening Programme introduced HPV testing in December 2023 and I recently announced in my three-year strategic plan for health and social care, targets for the bowel screening and lung screening programmes doh-hsc-3-year-plan.pdf.My Department will continue to be guided by the recommendations of the UK NSC.  However, as with all screening programmes, screening is for people without symptoms. People who are concerned about, or are experiencing any unusual symptoms, should seek advice from their GP. For people experiencing symptoms referred by their GP, in addition to the usual referral routes into specialist hospital services, two Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDCs) are currently in operation, delivering a regional service for patients with vague but concerning symptoms that could indicate cancer.The RDCs aim to see the patient within 14 days from the date of the GP referral which is significantly faster than the majority of tumour specific red flag referrals. Officials are currently seeking to identify and assess several proposals for red flag cancer pathways that could benefit from being delivered through an RDC.  Using the RDC model to deliver elements of these pathways has the potential to diagnose patients faster, shorten treatment pathway times and reduce wating lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any steps being taken to raise public awareness of the risks associated with ultraviolet exposure, particularly amongst (i) outdoor workers; and (ii) young people.
answer_text: Skin cancer is now the most common form of cancer in Northern Ireland, but in the majority of cases it can be effectively treated if detected at an early stage. Over exposure to UV radiation is widely accepted as the underlying cause for skin cancer and experts believe that four out of five cases could be prevented by adopting simple precautions.A key aim of the Department’s Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy and Action Plan is to raise public awareness of the dangers of over exposure to UV. The Public Health Agency host a Care in the Sun Website which promotes sun safe behaviour and UV awareness, and the Department also works closely with Cancer Focus Northern Ireland (CFNI) who also engage the public to raise awareness of the harms of UV and promote good sun safety behaviours. In 2024/25 CFNI promoted key messages to target groups including outdoor workers, sporting clubs and outdoor sports centres. Awareness sessions were delivered across 127 venues to 14,102 people participating in outdoor activities and sports. Furthermore, CFNI presented at an annual Build Health Conference in 2024 to promote the importance of sun safety for outdoor workers.  A Be UV Aware campaign has also seen a variety of media activities targeted at raising public awareness of the dangers of UV exposure in particular adolescents aged 16+. We will continue to liaise with the PHA and Cancer Focus Northern Ireland to co-ordinate future media activities to better raise public awareness of the dangers of UV exposure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the efficacy of current sea defences located on Portstewart's promenade.
answer_text: My Department does not have any sea defences associated with Portstewart promenade.  A retaining wall separates the upper and lower promenade; however, this structure is designed to support the carriageway and not as coastal flooding protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration his Department has given to extending current railway provision to Limavady town centre.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review recommended that consideration be given to developing new stations in the Derry~Londonderry city region, including Limavady. This has been identified as medium-term intervention by the Review i.e. to be delivered between 2030 and 2040. However, more work will be needed to test the feasibility and affordability of the Review’s recommendations, determine an optimum sequencing for the delivery of projects, and secure the necessary funding to take projects forward. The Department is continuing to work with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail and NTA to consider the recommendations and develop a plan for implementation. I will then need to consider which projects to prioritise as funding opportunities become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the redevelopment of HMP Magilligan.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) is continuing to progress business cases for a new kitchen and café plus a 60 cell wing at Magilligan.  The Outline Business Case 1 for the kitchen and café is currently being considered as part of the approval process, with the business case for the 60 Cell wing at an early stage. In addition to these projects, NIPS has developed a masterplan for the wider Magilligan site to determine the location of a new Welcome and Visits Centre, Independent living Units, a new Energy Centre and an Administration/Emergency Control Room building. The progression of these projects will support continued efforts by NIPS to provide an efficient, effective, modern and affordable service which is focused on the development of staff and the rehabilitation of people in its custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when he anticipates the procurement process for Project Gigabit to be completed.
answer_text: The procurement for Project Gigabit commenced on 11 November 2024, with an invitation to interested suppliers to complete a selection questionnaire to demonstrate their capacity and capability to deliver such a contract.  Those suppliers who meet the published criteria of the selection questionnaire will be invited to submit a response to the requirements of the Invitation to Tender for the project. The procurement will seek a supplier to address an initial scope of approximately 11,000 premises. My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit in the coming months, subject to compliant bids being made, bids meeting the published criteria, and all necessary approvals being agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to engage with local businesses to maximise the opportunities that will arise from The Open at Royal Portrush Golf Club 2025.
