answer_text: Tourism NI, on behalf of my Department, is committed to working with key partners to deliver benefits from The 153rd Open in 2025 in excess of those of The 148th Open in 2019, which was over £100 million of combined economic and media benefit. 		The Open in 2025 is expected to attract over 250,000 visitors and will showcase the North as a destination through the huge global broadcast reach and PR coverage generated, that will deliver a legacy for years to come. 		Delivery of an industry engagement programme has begun in the lead up to the event, in partnership with local authorities to assist and advise traders and businesses on how best to avail of opportunities that The Open will present.		Working in close collaboration with the R&A, the intention is to nurture workforce opportunities that will have a legacy beyond July 2025, through skills development and employment before, during and after the event, as well as volunteering across the event week. Discussions are progressing with Derry and Strabane Labour Market Partnership to ensure that opportunities are realised across the region. 		Tourism NI is supporting the industry to recruit and retain staff through its ongoing ‘Make it Here’ careers campaign and e-learning platform. In addition, they are working with Fáilte Ireland to extend its Employer Excellence Programme into the North to foster a culture of excellence in the workplace, making tourism and hospitality sectors rewarding and attractive places to work. 		In partnership with Tourism Ireland, Tourism NI will deliver a comprehensive PR and marketing campaign locally, nationally and globally, in the lead up to, during and after the event. 		Aside from the world-class golf offering, other experiences across the North will be promoted including high quality accommodation, restaurants, visitor attractions and entertainment. In collaboration with Invest NI, a programme of activity will also be delivered to support the presentation of our unique and compelling inward investment proposition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is considering to reduce waiting times for (i) knee replacement surgery; and (ii) hip replacement surgery.
answer_text: The current waiting times for primary joint replacement are unacceptable. Since 2018/19 there has been a significant growth in demand for both trauma and orthopaedic services. Given this increased growth in urgent demand, there has been a need to prioritise both clinic and theatre capacity. This has meant that patients who have been prioritised as routine wait longer for their assessment and treatment.  The 2024 Elective Care Framework and the more recent May 2025 Implementation and Funding Plan, sets out clear proposals for how  we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards.  It also details how we will invest in and transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in the future. This plan includes targeted funding to address some of the longest waiting treatments across a number of procedures including primary hip and knee replacement. In addition, I have also provided funding for a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme which will enable eligible patients the opportunity to reclaim costs for treatments received outside Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to ensuring patients who receive private healthcare abroad are able to avail of NHS-funded services, such as blood tests, as part of their post-procedure monitoring.
answer_text: When an individual procures private healthcare, within or outside of Northern Ireland, the Health and Social Care (HSC) service, which includes GP practices, is not obliged to provide any associated care.Patients should ensure that pre and post-operative care, such as, for example, blood tests and stitch removal, are provided within the package of care sold by the independent sector provider. If this associated care is not provided as part of the private service, then individuals should be prepared to meet the associated costs.In line with normal practice, patients will continue to be able to access emergency care where necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s bid for funding to reinstate the cross-border healthcare scheme.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) was introduced on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce waiting lists in Northern Ireland (NI). The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted during the 2024/25 budget setting process as part of a wider bid for £135million of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. 	Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget, no additional funding was provided for tackling our waiting lists and the focus has had to be on supporting those on cancer and time critical waiting lists. In addition, the draft budget for 2025/26 does not provide any additional funding for waiting list reduction initiatives. 		As such, there is no funding available at present to reinstate a cross-border reimbursement scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure children aged under 18 have adequate access to NHS dental services.
answer_text: I am aware of the challenges currently being experienced within General Dental Services (GDS) and I am committed to taking meaningful action to ensure the service performs for both patients and dental professionals. This is why for many months now, the focus of my Department has been on improving access to GDS, particularly for high-need patients such as children, and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices. In March 2024, a £9.2m investment in GDS was announced, to include:•	 £1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination (ECE) Scheme.•	£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25. This will support public access to priority treatments.•	£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme (DAS).The DAS commenced on 1st August 2024. There are currently 30 practices participating in the scheme, details of which (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/. To date the scheme has enabled the treatment of more than 9100 patients with an urgent or pressing dental condition.From June to December 2024, over 22,400 newly registered children have benefitted from the Enhanced Child Examination scheme.Evaluation carried out to date indicates that these measures have had a positive impact and my Department will continue monitor closely.While interventions such as the DAS and ECE have been necessary to stabilise the service, long-term change will require sustained effort, rather than a single, one-off intervention. In December my officials presented a number of high-level options for the long-term future of dental services in Northern Ireland. To inform the most appropriate approach I have approved the commissioning of a General Dental Service cost of service review to be completed in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of dental practices offering NHS services in East Londonderry in the last five years.
answer_text: The average dental practice in Northern Ireland has three dentists working in it. Individual dentists within a practice may provide only private dental care, only Health Service dental care or a mixture of the two. My Department defines a Health Service dental practice as one in which at least a single dental practitioner has made arrangements with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to provide Health Service dentistry.The table below shows the number of practices in East Londonderry in which a dentist has provided some level of NHS treatment over the last five years.Year1Practices2202117202217202317202417December 202417	Active dental surgeries at 31st March of each year. Latest year count taken at 31st December, the latest published figures, sourced from the Accredited Official Statistics release ‘Dental Services for Northern Ireland, Quarterly Series to Q3 2024/25 - Provisional’.	Practices may include a number of Orthodontic and Specialist Practitioners. Orthodontics is a specialist area of dentistry concerned with the growth and development of the teeth and jaws and the prevention and treatment of abnormalities of this development. Therefore, most patients are children. These practices do not typically have registered patient lists, with instead patients normally seen on referral from a GDS dental practice. Source: Business Service Organisation, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI. (Provided by Laura Baird– 5 February 2025)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of attacks on healthcare staff in hospitals reported in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of attacks on healthcare staff in hospitals reported in each of the last five financial years is shown in the table below.YearBHSCTNHSCTNIASSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTTotal2019/203321857234655152567572672020/21339511003406431323111579162021/223284115139110281213119282592022/23289091838110641443106877642023/2428111198457122818298338356Total157015224180346187333488339562Five-year figures on attacks on staff were last published by the Department on 12 December 2023 as part of the announcement launching the Violence and Aggression in the Workplace Framework. They were figures which included physical attacks only and included all HSC locations, not just hospitals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support services, including counselling, that are available to healthcare staff who have been victims of violence in the workplace.
answer_text: A range of support services are available to healthcare staff who have been victims of violence in the workplace.Initial support includes:Offering the opportunity for affected staff to go off duty (where necessary because of physical injury or emotional trauma).Contacting a relative, friend or Trade Union representativeArranging assistance and accompaniment to hospital (where appropriate) or calling a taxi or making transport arrangements to take them to hospital or home.Signposting to support – Lena by Inspire Confidential Counselling Service and union representative.Referral to Occupational Health and Wellbeing Service (access to OH Nursing, Physiotherapy and Psychology interventions as required).Staff are offered the opportunity to complete a Personal Impact Assessment (PIA) as per the Regional Management of Violence and Aggression Framework.Arrangements are made for ongoing support and contact between the manager and the staff involved, considering;what type of support the member/s of staff think that they might require in the coming days and weeks.when the next contact between the manager and the member/s of staff should take place and the method of contactStaff will also be supported in this initial phase to report an incident to PSNI, should that be appropriate in the circumstances and should the member of staff wish to pursue the matter through PSNI.Ongoing support is also provided to staff involved in incidents of violence and aggression in the weeks and months following the incident.  Examples of this support include;‘Checking in’ with the member of staff (including during subsequent supervision sessions)Following discussions with the staff member, the employer will make a decision on the level of support to be provided and the most appropriate support mechanismsA buddy/identified colleague will be nominated if appropriateIndividual(s) will be kept informed of any investigation process/follow up actions/any learning identifiedAppropriate time off will be granted to attend appointments (as per the Special Leave Policy) made as a result of the incident, for example with Occupational Health or InspireThe incident will be escalated to the Head of Service for consideration as to the appropriateness of a visit to affected staff by the Head of Service/Assistant Director or Director The potential long-term effects of a distressing incident will be considered, this will be by either keeping it under review in supervision or by offering to complete the Talking Toolkit (as per the Recognising and Responding to Stress in the Workplace Policy).  This will be reviewed regularly to ensure that the action plan is appropriate and being implementedThe staff member will be encouraged to contact their Trade Union Side representative where they may require further support from their Trade UnionIndividual or group support such as focus group/team session(s) via occupational health clinical psychology will be offered if appropriate.Advice will be provided on

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions that have taken place between her Department and the Department of Health in relation to improving protections for healthcare workers against violence and abuse in hospitals.
answer_text: I am aware of recent reports of attacks on healthcare workers and am disgusted by these and similar reports from those in other public-facing occupations.  No one should have to go to work in fear of being assaulted.  There are a range of existing assault offences designed to protect any member of the public from harm. In addition, an offence of assault on persons providing ambulance services was introduced in the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.To make it clear that assaults will not be tolerated on any person, while on duty, whose job includes dealing with the public, I intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing a service to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service within my Sentencing Bill. This offence will come with a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that currently available for the existing offences, and, for more serious assaults, the fact that the victim is such a person will become a statutory aggravating factor. Executive agreement to commence drafting the Sentencing Bill was granted on 19th December and I aim to introduce it in the autumn of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any measures being taken to improve the safety of healthcare staff in hospitals; and (ii) whether he has plans to introduce stronger protections for healthcare staff facing abuse or physical assault while on duty.
answer_text: I recognise and acknowledge the issues around Violence and Aggression that Healthcare staff have to deal with in the course of carrying out their duties. Any form of violence or aggression towards staff is unacceptable and the Department will continue to work with Employers and Trade Unions to ensure that appropriate protections are in place for staff.HSC trust employers aim to provide a safe environment for all staff and service users. Each trust has dedicated security staff who maintain static posts and perform routine patrols to try and deter incidents but also to urgently respond to all requests for assistance with violent and aggressive persons as best possible. Trusts also have CCTV and access control systems in place as an additional deterrent to deny unauthorised entry. Security staff all receive the same best practice safety intervention training to de-escalate situations in the first instance and also on how perform restraints as safely as possible for all involved as a last resort.Every service is required to carry out their own internal risk assessments and seek practical advice for management of aggression teams to identify training needs and additional support. This can range from basic courses in disengagement techniques to skilled courses in how to deescalate situations and perform restraints in a safe manner. This can also help identify if staff need any personal protective equipment (PPE) to help protect them. Some examples of this would be body armour, slash / spike & bite resistant gloves or sleeves, protective glasses and panic alarms.NIAS is committed to doing everything that it can to keep its staff safe. The high incidence of violence and aggression towards staff is recorded on the Trust’s Corporate Risk Register and in response, NIAS has introduced a range of measures to help protect staff physical safety and well-being.This includes provision of body-worn cameras which are available to paramedics attending calls. These act as a potential deterrent and allow staff to capture video footage of instances of violence and aggression.NIAS has a dedicated Violence Reduction Officer in post whose role is to raise awareness about the risks and ensure that appropriate steps are taken following an incident, including that staff have access to psychological support.My Department launched the HSC Violence and Aggression in the Workplace Framework on 12 December 2023 which was developed in collaboration with both Employers and Trade Unions and which all HSC organisations are required to adopt in respect to development of their individual organisation’s policies and procedures.This framework enables Trusts to ensure appropriate protections are in place to mitigate the risk of incidents of violence and aggression to staff and that reduction and prevention strategies are reviewed and implemented on an ongoing basis. There are no plans to introduce further measures as the current regional framework allows for each HSC employer to imple

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of healthcare staff currently on sick leave due to an attack that took place in their workplace, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) staff role.
answer_text: The numbers of healthcare staff currently on sick leave due to an attack that took place in their workplace are shown on the tables below. Note that figures have been grouped to avoid personal disclosure. By Health & Social Care TrustHSC TrustNumber of staff on sick leave due to an attack at workBelfast4Northern5South Eastern9Southern / Western / NI Ambulance Service4  By Staff GroupStaff GroupNumber of staff on sick leave due to an attack at workSocial Care / Social Work Support10Nursing & Midwifery / Allied Health Professions / Other12Source: HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support older people during the application process for (i) Pension Credit; and (ii) Attendance Allowance.
answer_text: My Department has a number of measures in place to support older people who require help to make an application for Pension Credit or Attendance Allowance. These include: A choice of channels for making an application - online, by phone, or by post for Pension Credit and online or by post for Attendance Allowance;  The availability of an online Pension Credit calculator which provides an opportunity to check potential eligibility; Assistance from Pension Credit telephony agents who will guide customers or a nominated representative through a telephone application, advise about eligibility and evidence requirements, and signpost to other benefits such as Attendance Allowance, Carers Allowance, State Pension, and Housing Benefit; and Assistance from the Disability & Carers Service by telephone or email for those seeking advice, including a customer’s nominated representative, about making an Attendance Allowance application  In addition, further assistance is available from the Department’s Make the Call service who can visit the customer to provide benefit advice and assistance with application form completion if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered reviewing the current Pension Credit paper application to make the application process succinct and more accessible for older people.
answer_text: My Department reviews the Pension Credit application form (PC1) annually and it is next due for review in February 2025. This is to ensure the application form provides the Department with the customer information needed to accurately establish eligibility. The Department offers a range of channels for those wishing to make a Pension Credit application including online, by telephone or post. Anyone requiring assistance to complete a Pension Credit application can contact my Department’s Make the Call service for assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures his Department is taking to improve the uptake of Pension Credit.
answer_text: My Department undertakes a wide range of activities to promote Pension Credit uptake including: social media and broadcast campaigns; promotion work with age-based stakeholders; displaying information about Pension Credit in public spaces; provision of confidential benefit assessments and help to complete forms; and identifying potential Pension Credit entitlement when a person makes their State Retirement Pension claim.  In addition this year a letter issued to all pensioners advising them of the Winter Fuel Payment entitlement changes which signposted to Pension Credit. My Department also collaborated with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in issuing a letter to all older Housing Benefit recipients not already in receipt of Pension Credit, to make them aware of the benefit. As a result of the Make the Call service, 1,634 people were helped to access over £6.6 million in Pension Credit in 2023/24

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure those entitled to the £100 Winter Fuel Payment receive it as soon as possible.
answer_text: My officials are working at pace to put in place the IT and other logistical arrangements necessary for making the one-off £100 Pension Age Fuel Support payment. The payment remains on schedule to be paid before the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures his Department has taken to ensure that older people, particularly those living in rural areas with limited internet access, are not disadvantaged when seeking information about social security benefits.
answer_text: My Department’s Make the Call Wraparound Service aims to ensure that its community-based Outreach Officers conduct home visits, provide face to face assistance for customers requiring help to make a benefit claim and work with local community, charitable and voluntary groups, to raise awareness of potential benefit entitlements and provide practical help in accessing them.In 2023-24 the Make the Call Wraparound Service helped over 12k people access £62.1 million in additional benefits.  This includes £21.2m for 4,755 people of pension age. Callers to the Make the Call helpline receive a free and confidential needs assessment to identify potential entitlement to benefits, support and services.People who have difficulties attending their Jobs & Benefits office due to vulnerabilities or other barriers may benefit from a visit to their home by an officer from the Department.Each of the Jobs & Benefits offices has internet enabled devices located onsite in digital zones. Staff working in partnership with the Northern Ireland Civil Service Go ON NI are on hand to help claimants develop the digital skills needed to make and maintain their benefit claim.  Information and support is provided through community outreach events where Jobs & Benefits office staff visit local facilities to provide their service. My Department funds free broadband and computers through its Adviser Discretion Fund to help address digital exclusion being a barrier to starting or staying in work.   	Libraries NI, an Arm’s Length Body sponsored by my Department, serves the rural population through its network of 96 libraries and 29 library facilities in designated rural areas providing access to free Wi-Fi, public computers, and digital support. Seven ‘Out of Hours’ facilities are in operation allowing digital services to be accessed at times convenient to the user.  Working in partnership with my Department, Libraries NI offers digital access to government services including Make the Call.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to support students and staff at North Coast Integrated College following the extensive damage to the building caused by Storm Éowyn, considering the upcoming amalgamation of the school in September this year.
answer_text: Departmental officials contacted the school on Monday 28 January and were advised that the school’s contractors were on site undertaking the necessary repairs to enable the school to reopen and operate safely from Tuesday 29 January. Should the school require further Departmental support, they should submit a Minor Works application to minorworks@education-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to ensure older people with health conditions exacerbated by cold weather have access to emergency support and necessary medical care during extreme weather events.
answer_text: HSC trusts have in place emergency planning response plans and oversight structures as part of major incident planning and this includes contingencies for extreme weather events HSC trusts have a comprehensive list of service users who are deemed to have health vulnerabilities who will be supported and contingency arrangements put in place in the event of a power cut such as those dependent on ventilators, respirators, enteral feeding and pressure relieving mattresses.HSC trusts active response teams can be mobilised to support service users who are in receipt of home care, district nursing services and community-based equipment.  Home care providers will advise the trusts of any service users who are found in need of additional supports.   Trusts also engage with all care homes to offer support.It is also acknowledged that any power outages and damage to the road network can necessitate a community response.  HSC trusts work with NIE to identify vulnerable individuals and agree a co-ordinated response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that older people, particularly those living in rural areas, are not left without access to vital communication during power outages, given the move to digital phone lines which require power to operate.
answer_text: Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter, with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) holding responsibility. My officials maintain regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters.The Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) is the traditional copper-based analogue landline, which is being upgraded to deliver voice services over an internet connection via Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technology. This may also be referred to as ‘digital phone’ or ‘digital voice’. Although the migration to VoIP is an industry led initiative, DSIT, along with Ofcom, the communications regulator, is working with the industry to ensure all sectors of the economy are protected and prepared. This includes regularly engaging with communications providers and tracking preparedness across all affected sectors.  Ofcom has issued guidance on how to prepare for the migration, and has rules to protect customers during power cuts, and to require phone companies to provide access to emergency calls at all times. More information is available at: www.ofcom.org.uk/phones-and-broadband/landline-phones/future-of-landline-calls/

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any guidance his Department has issued to the public in relation to the safe operation of generators during power outages.
answer_text: HSENI in discussions with members of the Civil Contingencies Group agreed to place advice on their website on the use of generators. The text is as follows and can be found at https://www.hseni.gov.uk/news/safety-tips-using-portable-generator-emergency .

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their discussions with NIE in relation to potential compensation for those who have been without power for multiple days.
answer_text: We are mindful of the ongoing significant impact on the thousands of people without power as a result of Storm Éowyn.  We met with the Managing Director from Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIE) on Saturday 25 January, and at the Executive meeting on Monday 27 January urged him to consider how NIE could provide support to those families and businesses impacted by the effects of Storm Éowyn. Since the Executive meeting we have received an update from NIE that they have had exploratory conversations with the Department for Economy and the Utility Regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline measures his Department is taking to ensure farmers take steps to mitigate the risk of contracting and spreading Foot and Mouth Disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps are being taken to ensure that the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls and its Delivery Plan are effectively implemented in rural areas.
answer_text: The Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls sets out key actions supporting collaboration across government and wider society, with a focus on prevention and early intervention.	Under the Framework’s first Delivery Plan, which runs until March 2026, we committed to a number of community-led reviews to better understand the needs of groups facing additional barriers or vulnerabilities, including a review on the needs of rural women.This will ensure we can reflect a diverse range of voices, including those living in rural communities. 	 Last month, we announced the Local and Regional Change Fund as part of a wider £3.2 million investment. By working with local councils, we can ensure regional balance as grassroot groups in each locality will have the opportunity to play their part in preventing violence, harm and abuse against women and girls. Local councils are engaging with the community and voluntary sector across their districts to ensure engagement within rural areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of community pharmacies that closed between January 2023 and December 2024; and (ii) how this compares to the total number of closures over the previous five years.
answer_text: The total number of community pharmacies that closed between January 2023 and December 2024 was 17.For comparison purposes, the table below shows the total number of closures over the previous five years from January 2018 to December 2022.YearNumber of closures2018020191202042021220220At a UK level, Northern Ireland continues to have the most pharmacies per 100,000 population. The latest available statistics, which relate to 2022/23, show that the UK average was 20.6 pharmacies per 100,000 people. Northern Ireland’s figure was 27.6, which was 34% higher than the UK average.We do not always know the reason for closures as contractors do not have to give my Department a reason for closing, but we are aware that some of the closures were as a result of business consolidation.There remains good access to community pharmacies across Northern Ireland, with over 99% of the population living within 5 miles of a community pharmacy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to introducing a pension scheme for community pharmacists, similar to the schemes already available for GPs, optometrists and dentists.
answer_text: Access to a pension is an employer responsibility.  Access to the Health and Social Care Pension Scheme is legislated for under The Health and Social Care Pension Scheme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 and is limited to health service workers who are: employed by HSC employers; are an HSC contractor/provider or those employed by them – this includes GPs and dentists.  Community pharmacy owners are the employers of community pharmacists in Northern Ireland and have a legal obligation to provide access to a workplace pension for their employees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to expand the role of community pharmacies in delivering public health campaigns, including the proposed Pharmacy First for Sore Throat Service and the continuation of vaccination services.
answer_text: Community pharmacies in Northern Ireland have an established role in the provision of public health campaigns. In addition to the Pharmacy First Sore Throat Service and Community Pharmacy Vaccination Service, the Living Well and Stop Smoking Services are delivered extensively across the community pharmacy network. The roll out of the Pharmacy First Sore Throat Service this year built upon a successful evaluation of the Pharmacy First Pilot Service for Sore Throat last year (November 2023 – March 2024). Following collaborative discussion with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) DoH has secured £1m non-recurrent funding for a network wide service that commenced on 1 December 2024 and will run until 31 March 2025.Community pharmacies are integral to the delivery of seasonal vaccination campaigns and this service has been established since 2021 following a successful pilot in 2019. The service currently provides seasonal flu and COVID vaccination for care homes and direct access walk-in patients. In 2023/24 during the Autumn and Spring campaigns, 96,186 vaccine administrations were delivered from 338 pharmacies. Over 68,500 vaccines have been administered in the Autumn 24/25 campaign to date. SPPG continues to work collaboratively with the Public Health Agency (PHA) to develop the service as appropriate, to meet the needs of the population and campaign targets. The Living Well Service has been established since 2019 and is delivered in over 500 pharmacies. This service provides key public health messages and advice during an annual schedule of six campaigns, each of which runs for two months. During 2023/24 the campaigns had an estimated reach of 346,400 people and evaluations consistently demonstrate the unique reach and impact of this service. Campaign topics to date in 24/25 have included Cancer Screening, MMR vaccination, Physical Activity, Sexual Health and Staying Well over Winter. Community pharmacy is the largest provider of a Specialist Stop Smoking Service. 6488 people enrolled in the service in 2023/24 and the 4-week quit rate was 57%. The Department funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned HSC services through an agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan, developed with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland. Work will be commencing in early 2025 to update the current commissioning plan and outline the service priorities for the next three years, aligned with the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan which was launched by my Department in May 2024. Public Health services and campaigns will be further considered during this process and in collaboration with PHA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the role community pharmacies play in alleviating pressures on frontline NHS services.
answer_text: Community pharmacy is one of the four pillars of primary care in Northern Ireland and provides vital services for local communities. Community pharmacies are often the first point of contact with the HSC, with an estimated 123,000 patients and members of the public visiting community pharmacies every day in Northern Ireland to access medicines and seek advice from skilled pharmacists and pharmacy staff who are highly trusted by the public. Examples of community pharmacy services that alleviate pressures on other frontline services include the Emergency Supply Service and Pharmacy First services.All pharmacies offer an Emergency Supply Service for situations where people have run out of their medicines and they can’t access their general practice. Up to 16,000 items per month are supplied via this service, avoiding the need for patients to attend Out-of-Hours (OOH) medical services or Emergency Departments to access these medicines or go without essential treatment. Pharmacy First is an umbrella term covering a range of therapy areas which are delivered in community pharmacies obviating the need for patients/clients to access other service providers such as GP practices, GP Out of Hours and Emergency Departments. The range of presentations that can be dealt with through Pharmacy First has expanded from every day conditions such as minor skin infections to management of Urinary Tract Infections and the provision of Emergency Hormonal Contraception (EHC). Evaluation of Pharmacy First has demonstrated displacement of activity. For example, since the introduction in 2022 of the Pharmacy First Emergency hormonal contraception service there has been an overall reduction of 46% in the number of prescriptions for EHC issued by GP practices and a similar reduction of 53% in prescribing by OOH medical services.The Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030 commits to further expanding the range of Pharmacy First services. A new Pharmacy First Sore Throat service commenced on 1 December and will run until 31 March 2025 in the majority of community pharmacies. In addition, a Pharmacy First Treatment of Shingles pilot service is currently running in 50 pharmacies for 3 months. My Department will be engaging with CPNI over the coming weeks to develop a Commissioning Plan for 2025-2028. These new commissioning arrangements will progress the ambitions set out by my Department’s strategic plan for community pharmacy which outlines a vision for delivery of more clinical services that utilise the skills of pharmacy teams and offer safe, convenient and timely access to care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered adopting the Scottish model for claw-back adjustments to provide financial stability for community pharmacies in Northern Ireland, given the reported £24 million reduction in pharmacy budgets due to the claw-back in 2023-24.
answer_text: The community funding models in each of the UK nations differs; in terms of government funding for community pharmacies per head of population (2023-2024), Northern Ireland is 22% higher than Scotland.In Northern Ireland, the opening envelope for 24/25 was £147m; this represented an increase of £19m on 23/24 opening envelope, an increase of 14.8%, not a decrease in funding as detailed in the question.As part of the community pharmacy reimbursement arrangements, discount clawback has been used for many years in NI, England, Wales, and Scotland to reduce reimbursement in recognition that pharmacies are receiving additional profit through their efficient procurement.  Following a consultation completed in March 2022 on proposals to reform aspects of the NI Drug Tariff, including review of discount clawback scales, revised discounting arrangements were introduced on a phased basis from January 2024. These arrangements aim to ensure that clawback is more appropriately applied to pharmacy contractors, whilst maintaining cost neutrality, and are being introduced over an 18-month period to minimise any impact on contractors. In the first 11 months of these transitional arrangements, over £1.42m less has been clawed back from pharmacy contractors under the new arrangements than legacy arrangements, and my Department remains committed to monitoring the impact of these new arrangements on all contractors and to taking actions as needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of hospital admissions for hypothermia of people 65 years of age and over in the last three months; and (ii) how this compares to the same period last year.
answer_text: Admissions1 to Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) hospitals, aged 65 and over with a hypothermia diagnosis2PeriodAdmissionsOct 23 - Dec 2333Oct 24 - Dec 24P8Source: Hospital Inpatient System and encompass, Hospital Activity Information Branch, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI.Notes:1Admissions are approximated using deaths and discharges during the period.  Admissions within the Mental Health and Learning Disability Programmes of Care have not been included.PData for Oct - Dec 2024 are provisional only.  Episodic coding works against the NI Coding Target of 3 months so it is likely that there is activity in this period which has yet to be coded and may fit the criteria requested.2Hospital admissions with hypothermia were identified using the following ICD-10 codes in any of the diagnoses fields of the Hospital Inpatient System and encompass:T68.X - Hypothermia		R68.0 - Hypothermia, not associated with low environmental temperature*Both periods include data extracted from encompass (a new electronic patient record system) for South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust from 9 November 2023 and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust from 6 June 2024.This subset of the data is classed as "Official Statistics in Development" whilst undergoing evaluation. As a result, users should exercise some caution with this data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why the decision to implement cuts to the Winter Fuel Payment was made prior to the publication of the draft Equality Impact Assessment; and (ii) how this aligns with the Department’s statutory equality obligations under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.
answer_text: The decision was made to implement the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment scheme before the draft EQIA to ensure that eligible pensioners in Northern Ireland would receive their payment before the end of 2024. The department’s Equality Scheme provides that in exceptional circumstances, where a policy must be implemented immediately, consultation may be undertaken after the implementation of the policy. This is in line with guidance from the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any additional support he is considering to improve energy efficiency in homes to help mitigate the impact of Winter Fuel Payment cuts, particularly given the high proportion of older and less energy-efficient housing stock in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a consultation on evidence-based options for a domestic energy efficiency programme in line with Action 7 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2024. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support.  In the interim, the department is working with partners such as the Utility Regulator to assess the options for further scaling of up existing energy efficiency programmes. In this context, my department has provided additional funding of £1m to NISEP in 2023-24 and £2.5m in 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been made of the economic and social impacts of cuts to the Winter Fuel Payment on vulnerable groups in Northern Ireland, particularly in rural areas where access to affordable heating options is limited.
answer_text: An Equality Impact Assessment public consultation on the cuts to the Winter Fuel Payments scheme by the Labour government in winter 2024/25 was launched by the department on 9 December 2024 and runs for 12 weeks until 2nd March 2025.  That consultation seeks the public views on the proposed preliminary recommendations with regards to measures to mitigate the adverse impacts of the policy changes. The department will respond to the public consultation after an analysis of the responses received.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any considerations his Department has made to mitigate the disproportionate effects of cuts to the Winter Fuel Payment on households in Northern Ireland, given the higher prevalence of fuel poverty in the region compared to the rest of the UK.
answer_text: I refer to my statement in the Assembly on 19 November 2024 regarding the one-off emergency fuel payment of £100 to pensioner households no longer eligible for the Winter Fuel Payment this winter.		I am also currently consulting on a draft Fuel Poverty Strategy that will provide a long-term framework for reducing fuel poverty.  It will contribute to improving energy efficiency in homes and provide support and protection for those living in or at risk of fuel poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that cuts to the Winter Fuel Payment has had on hospital admissions among older people, particularly where inadequate heating worsens health conditions.
answer_text: The analysis of admissions of older people to hospital where these are a directly related to winter fuel payments would be challenging to determine given what are often multiple variables leading to a decision to admit.  The recent exceptional volume of admissions associated with viral conditions would make an accurate determination further challenging to conclude on this matter.Notwithstanding this position, my Department fully recognises the well-established correlation between poverty and ill health as a key driver in health inequalities across all populations.  My Department remains fully committed to addressing health inequalities through the strategic framework of Making Life Better which provides focused polices and actions across every spectrum of care, supported by multidisciplinary and multiagency arrangements and formally reported on through regular progress reports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of the reduction of the Winter Fuel Payment for older people, particularly those already experiencing fuel poverty.
answer_text: An Equality Impact Assessment public consultation on the changes to the Winter Fuel Payments scheme in winter 2024/25 was launched by the department on 9 December 2024 and runs for 12 weeks until 2nd March 2025.  That consultation seeks the public views on the proposed preliminary recommendations with regards to measures to mitigate the adverse impacts of the policy changes. The department will respond to the public consultation after an analysis of the responses received.In addition, I have proposed within the current draft Fuel Poverty Strategy consultation, which is also currently open, to analyse the impact of the removal of Winter Fuel Payments on pensioners who are above the threshold for Pension Credit to gain a better understanding of who may be at risk of fuel poverty because of the change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered implementing a long-term strategy to protect vulnerable older people from escalating energy costs, particularly in light of the recent reduction in the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: I have launched a 12-week consultation on a draft Fuel Poverty Strategy for Northern Ireland which ends on 6 March 2025. This strategy proposes a vision of “A Warm, Healthy Home for Everyone” and by alleviating fuel poverty, will have positive impacts on both mental and physical health, especially for the most vulnerable in society. The strategy focuses on making homes more energy efficient to address the root causes of fuel poverty and reduce the poor health outcomes associated with cold homes. It will also address how to support those living in or at risk of fuel poverty, improve consumer protection and include proposals for a new fuel poverty energy efficiency scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to improve public confidence in cervical cancer screening services.
answer_text: Over 99% of cervical cancers are caused by the human papilloma virus (HPV). Primary HPV testing, with cervical cytology as a second line test for those women who test positive for HPV, was fully implemented into the NI Cervical Screening programme from 11 December 2023. Primary HPV testing is an automated test which identifies the presence of HPV and was recommended by the UK National Screening Committee because it is a more accurate test, and will save more lives, than cervical cytology alone. The Public Health Agency (PHA) is monitoring awareness, confidence and barriers to participation in cervical screening and will continue to use this information to inform future promotional activities.  Coverage data (used a measurement of participation in the Cervical Screening Programme) is now available for 2023/24 and shows an increase in age appropriate coverage across all Trusts in 2023/24, compared to 2022/23.My Department’s Cervical Screening Oversight and Assurance Group, continues to receive and seek assurances on the overall performance of the cervical screening programme; to include maintaining and building on public confidence in the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any collaborative steps being taken with other Departments to inform young people about the importance of regular cervical screening and cervical cancer prevention.
answer_text: The Cervical Screening Programme is available to women registered with a GP, aged between 25-64 years old and Public Health Agency screening promotional activities target this age group. In addition to the established Cervical Screening Programme, there is also an HPV vaccination school-based programme across NI which has been in place for girls in school year 9 since 2008 and was extended to include all boys in school year 9 from September 2019. Promotional material provided to the schools highlights that while the HPV vaccine reduces the risk of developing cervical cancer, it does not eliminate it and therefore anyone eligible called to attend cervical screening should still attend. Anyone who remains unvaccinated can request the vaccine, via their GP, up until they are 25 years of age.  The PHA collaborates with a number of relevant agencies to inform young people about the importance of cervical cancer prevention.  These include:Development of webinars to promote vaccination and educate teaching staff, young people and their parents on the benefits of the HPV vaccine, in collaboration with Cancer Focus, sporting bodies and NHSCT;Development of social media resources for schools to promote HPV vaccine;Communication to all schools via the Education Authority promoting the HPV vaccine among school staff as well as sharing resources above;International Women's Day celebration event promoting HPV vaccine with Year 8-10 students;World Immunisation Week celebration, including digital creator competition to promote HPV vaccine;Provision of HPV vaccination catch-up clinics at universities, including promotion event during Fresher's week; andTargeted school visit programme to five NI schools with lowest HPV uptake - talks and presentations given by PHA staff to school assemblyThe PHA continues to monitor awareness, confidence and barriers to participation in cervical screening and continues to use this information to inform future promotional activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work (i) specifically aimed at educating older people about domestic elder abuse; and (ii) to increase accessibility to housing and financial support for older victims of domestic abuse who wish to leave abusive environments.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has recently published a Domestic Abuse Action Plan which can be found at www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/089a6b60-dfdf-4a54-ae43-dd04340fc082/Domestic-Abuse-Action-Plan.pdf.The Housing Executive has been engaging with Hourglass in the delivery of actions specific to older people, particularly in raising awareness amongst our staff in relation to domestic elder abuse and the promotion of Hourglass as a means of support and assistance. The Housing Executive will continue to work with Hourglass to ensure the specific needs of older people/older victims of domestic abuse are represented in the delivery of the Domestic Abuse Action Plan.  The Housing Executive also provides support and assistance via our Financial Inclusion Managers who can support customers with money worries, debt advice and/or benefits with an overall aim of assisting them to maximise their income, reduce money worries and thereby sustain current accommodation (where appropriate in cases of domestic abuse) and/or prepare for a new permanent home. Women at risk of domestic violence are supported through Supporting People funded, short-term emergency accommodation-based refuge services, providing support for those in housing need. Support is also provided through short-term Floating Support services for those who choose to remain at home or in a community setting. The aim of support is to help women with needs specific to their domestic abuse situation make informed choices about their accommodation and be able to maintain/sustain a home. Support can also include help to access other agencies and services.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she is ensuring consistent collection and analysis of data on domestic abuse against older people, including age-specific patterns and barriers to reporting.
answer_text: The Department of Justice collates information in relation to prosecutions, convictions and out of courts disposals in relation to domestic abuse.  However, these datasets do not contain information on victim age, in relation to older people.The Department also commissions the Northern Ireland Safe Community Survey, which had included a self-completion module to capture respondents’ experiences of domestic abuse. The module had to be suspended due to a change in survey mode as a result of the Covid-19 pandemic. A further change in mode from 2024/25 means the module has been reinstated and will be included biennially going forward, though the changes have impacted the comparability of findings from the survey. Breakdowns by age would also be subject to sufficient sample size.The Police Service of Northern Ireland record information in relation to domestic abuse related offences reported to them.  Issues around recording and analysis of data recorded by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the recording and analysis of police data, as raised in the question, to the PSNI. Looking forward, my Department plans to take forward additional work in partnership with the community and voluntary sector, and with officials in the Executive Office, to explore how we can better utilise the data held across various sectors. This collaborative work will be taken forward under the multi-year action plan stemming from the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy. By taking this joined up approach, we aim to enhance our understanding of the nature and scale of domestic abuse in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how his Department and statutory agencies are addressing the connection of elder care and domestic abuse, particularly for victims with reduced mental or physical capacity; and (ii) whether health and social care providers are able to recognise signs of domestic abuse among older patients.
answer_text: The Department of Health collaborates with the Department of Justice and other statutory agencies to address the intersection of elder care and domestic abuse. This collaborative effort is encapsulated in the new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy (2024-31) which was formally launched on 25 September 2024 and is jointly led by the Departments of Health and Justice. The Strategy recognises that anyone can be a victim of domestic and/or sexual abuse regardless of age, ethnicity, religion, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, or any form of disability. The Strategy also recognises that while anyone can experience domestic and /or sexual abuse, some people may be more at risk or may face additional barriers in accessing services and will therefore require specialist support and intervention. For example, older people face a number of additional barriers to seeking support including generational views about faith, loyalty, shame and guilt. In some cases, they may find it difficult to report if the person is abusing them is also their carer or they are financially dependent on them. The Strategy acknowledges the importance of identifying and responding to the intersectional needs of victims, to both prevent abuse from happening and to provide appropriate support in the event that it does occur.The Department of Health is developing an Adult Protection Bill which will introduce additional protections to strengthen and underpin the adult protection process.  The Adult Protection Bill will see the introduction of a number of duties and powers to assist in enquiries and the protection of all adults at risk.  Health and Social Care staff are provided with training to identify and respond effectively to domestic abuse. Staff are supported to consider and discuss the contexts in which domestic abuse can manifest, dispel myths about where domestic abuse can occur and who can experience domestic abuse. This will include acknowledgement of the safety strategies and resilience that older victims develop over time, increasing physical and social dependencies of both the victim and the alleged perpetrator, family and social perception/support or lack of and isolation. Training supports staff to be aware and recognise those potentially more vulnerable groups of people that could be at higher risk of abuse, such as those with reduced mental or physical capacity.When staff have concerns that a service user or patient is experiencing domestic abuse, an adult safeguarding referral is progressed on their behalf and a DASH Risk Assessment is completed with the individual.  Should a professional assessment indicate that a service user is at high risk from domestic violence, a referral is made to the MARAC Forum.  Trusts engage in this Forum to ensure that information is shared with multi-agency partners in a timely manner, and that plans are implemented to support victim’s safety.  Should an adult be deemed ‘an adult in need of protection from domestic abuse’, trai

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) how the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls will address the distinct experiences of older female victims of domestic abuse; and (ii) any cross-departmental strategies under the framework to address ageism and its role in exacerbating elder abuse.
answer_text: The Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) is designed to address the whole range of gender-based violence, abuse and harm that is disproportionately experienced by women and girls.  Age NI played a key role in the co-design process for developing the Strategic Framework. The voices of those with lived experience, including older women, were central to shaping its design. This approach will continue throughout implementation through various mechanisms including the Insights and Reference Network. Department for Communities officials were part of the co-design process of the EVAWG Strategic Framework. This will ensure that it complements and supports the ongoing work of the Department for Communities, including on how best to build on the previous Executive Active Ageing Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how the criminal justice system is equipped to meet the specific needs of older victims of domestic abuse.
answer_text: The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031, which is jointly led by my Department and the Department of Health, adopts an inclusive approach, recognising the diversity of all victims of domestic and sexual abuse and their needs. The Strategy acknowledges that there will be specific challenges faced by older victims of domestic and sexual abuse and the Strategy’s Expert Reference Group (ERG) includes representation from the organisation ‘Hourglass’ to ensure that the voices and perspectives of older victims help to inform policy decisions and the development of services.  One of the actions included in the Strategy’s Action Plan is to consider intersectionality and the barriers faced by specific groups (including older people) and then to identify and implement measures to address these, subject to funding.  My officials have spoken to Hourglass who have agreed to be involved in this work, in respect of older people.  You may also be aware that my Department also funds the delivery of a Northern Ireland wide advocacy service, by ASSIST NI, to support victims of domestic and sexual abuse who are engaged with the criminal justice system.  ASSIST NI offers a service tailored to the individual needs of each victim, they provide guidance, information, work with other service providers and advocate on victim’s behalf to secure the help and resources they require.  Advocates are trained to understand and recognise the barriers faced by specific groups, including older people, and will work with them to provide support that meets their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered any short-term programmes to clear lengthy waiting lists, including private health and social care and cross-border initiatives.
answer_text: Long waiting times are unacceptable and addressing this issue is a key priority for me. The Elective Care Framework (ECF) (May 2024) sets out a roadmap for tackling lengthy waiting times.  This Framework sets the strategic direction for the development and transformation of elective care services in Northern Ireland over the next five years and details the measures needed to address the unacceptable waiting lists here.Reducing waiting lists will not be a quick fix. Positive achievements have been made and there have been small but tangible improvements in the waiting list position here over the last few years, but much more still needs to be done.  Full implementation of the ECF, including further development of elective care centres, enhancing Independent Sector engagement and any new reimbursement scheme requires sustainable funding. The £76m made available this year was used to treat cancer/red flag patients and falls far short of the funding required to tackle lengthy waits.  A bid for £80m recurrent funding, as well as an additional £135m per year, for up to five years, to clear the backlog, was made but not met and the position in next year's draft budget is equally bleak.  If the waiting list bid of £135m put forward for 2024/25 had been approved by the Executive, (above the 75-80m required for red-flag and time critical patients), significant progress could be made to improve waiting times.  However, this is regrettably not possible within the current budget allocation. Below is the planning summary of intended spend of the £135m.Specialty/ProcedureApproximate patient numbersApproximate Cost Dermatology new outpatient assessment6,000 patients waiting over 4 years £2.8mCataract procedures2,800 patients waiting £5mPrimary hip/primary knee and other long waiting orthopaedic procedures3,500 patients waiting over 2 years +900 patients waiting over 4 years £42m (2 years waits)+£11m (4-year waits)Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy1,000 patients waiting over 4 years£4.5mTonsillectomies1,100 patients (many of whom are children) waiting over 4 years£3.5mRheumatologyoutpatient assessment 2,100 patients waiting over 3 years £1.4m Gynaecologyoutpatient assessment4,200 patients waiting over 3 years£2.5mHernia Treatment 800 patients waiting over 4 years  £4m Colonoscopy 1,905 patients waiting over 3 years £2.4mOGD1,025 patients waiting over 3 years £0.8m Other1,900 patients across a number of specialities£6mSchemeApproximate patient numbersApproximate CostReimbursement Scheme4,000 patients waiting over 1 year – largely orthopaedics£31mPrimary Care Elective Care17,000 patients across a number of specialities, including dermatology, gynaecology and MSK£3mMega Clinics20,000 patients across a number of specialities£10mOther including validation£5mTotal134.9mI will continue to do all I can, in this constrained financial environment, however, without the investment needed I cannot fund a new cross-border scheme, nor can I address the lack of community capacity sign

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered 24-hour minor injury facilities within communities to ease pressure on Emergency Departments.
answer_text: When establishing minor injury facilities and urgent care services across the province, operating hours were considered and agreed within the context of demand and resources available, and within the context of existing urgent and emergency care service provision in each Trust.All Trusts have minor injury units associated with their acute hospital or attached to a community hospital(s).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to review health and social care pathways, in order to offer patients various options of healthcare that consider individual circumstances and quality of life rather than following a standard pathway for a particular ailment.
answer_text: My Department fully supports the responsive provision of choices and options for patients in where and how they receive their care where this can be done safely and cost effectively within available resources.Clinical care pathways aim to standardise arrangements and reduce variation in care in line with best practice to optimise outcomes.  Notwithstanding this approach, HSC providers will routinely endeavour, where clinically safe and operationally possible to do so, to accommodate patient choice in respect of where and how treatment is delivered.  In most instances these arrangements will relate to providing care as close as possible to the patient’s home.Some types of care pathway will include options and choices consistent with the needs of specific cohorts.  For example, in palliative care, pathways support the delivery of co-ordinated services to enable people with palliative and end of life care needs to have choice in their place of care, greater access to services and improved outcomes at the end of their lives. Highly specialised services, such as liver or heart transplants, are not delivered locally as our small population cannot support the volume of activity to maintain the skills needed for these complex procedures. Highly specialised services are only provided in a very small number of NHS accredited providers in GB.  Although this involves travel and limits choice, these arrangements ensure that patients from Northern Ireland can be assured of equitable access to the best care the NHS can offer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to increase residential nursing provision for younger adults.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the provision of health and social care services in accordance with population and individual assessed need.HSCTs utilise both statutory and independent sector providers to provide residential or nursing home placements for younger adults as required in accordance with assessed need. HSCTs have procedures in place to optimise capacity within available resources. HSCTs advise there are no immediate plans to increase the provision of residential nursing provision for younger adults. My Department is working from a strategic perspective to support adults in line with assessed need to live in their own homes in local communities, rather than in institutional facilities.My Department is also currently leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The Commissioning and Contracting workstream and the Maximising Capacity workstream will support best use of current respite/ short break provision and expansion of service provision within care home settings from a residential and nursing care perspective. My Department remains committed to ensuring young adults receive appropriate service provision in line with assessed need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to redirect and fund additional health and social care services to community practitioners, including pharmacists and opticians.
answer_text: The Department funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned HSC services through an agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan, developed with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland. Work will be commencing in early 2025 to update the current commissioning plan and outline the service priorities for the next three years, aligned with the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan.  An additional £15m, is required to support the delivery of new community pharmacy services once the commissioning priorities are agreed through the refresh of the Commissioning Plan. However, this is dependent on my Department securing the appropriate funding for the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan.The Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan, developed collaboratively between the Department and CPNI, outlines the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030.  The Strategic Plan builds on the foundations already established in the Commissioning Plan for 2022-25 for community pharmacy and aims to develop more community pharmacy services and incorporate independent prescribing and advanced practice to support the goals of the health and social care service. The Strategic Plan will require funding, legislative reform and contractual arrangements to deliver the full vision outlined.  This will not be achieved overnight but my Department is committed to its implementation and will work to develop business cases for funding.My Department is also committed to supporting the role of pharmacists in general practice and to support them to contribute fully to optimising the health outcomes of our population as the clinical leads for medicines within the general practice team. “GPP NI 2030: A strategy for general practice pharmacy in Northern Ireland” sets out a vision for developing the general practice pharmacy workforce, including the introduction of new advanced practice pharmacist roles. Further development of the GP Pharmacy workforce through advanced practice roles will be included in the Multidisciplinary Teams model, once that is rolled out.My Department has, for a number of years, been commissioning a range of enhanced health services from community optometrists. These services reduce pressure on hospital eye services, facilitate patients being treated closer to home, and enable primary care optometry practitioners to fully utilise their skill mix. With respect to potential future funded pathways, a 2023 “NI PEARS Plus” pilot project, which allowed IP optometrists to manage additional conditions in the community, has just been fully evaluated and the outcomes will be considered in line with funding priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the safe access zone at Causeway Hospital; and (ii) whether the zone should be extended as per the legislation.
answer_text: My Department’s assessment of all Safe Access Zones operational in Northern Ireland is set out in the first annual report published in December 2024, as required under Section 8 of the Abortion Services (Safe Access Zones) Act Northern Ireland 2023.Based on the evidence presented from Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and the PSNI and feedback that the Department has received since the introduction of the Safe Access Zones on 29 September 2023, it is the Department’s opinion that, on balance, each of the eight current Safe Access Zones has been effective in protecting the safety and dignity of protected persons.The Department is aware that there have been eight recorded breaches of the Safe Access Zone at Causeway Hospital since its introduction and acknowledges that the ongoing continued protest presence may cause distress to members of the public passing by or attending the hospital.  However, the intention of the legislation was not to remove the right to protest but rather to protect women and staff specifically from harassment, alarm or distress when attending a clinic for abortion services within the immediate vicinity of these premises. My Department is content that the Safe Access Zone is effective in doing this.The Department does not have a role in extending Safe Access Zones.  If an operator (i.e. a HSC Trust) considers that a Safe Access Zone, as established, is not providing safe access to the premises for protected persons, the operator can give notice to my Department that it wishes to extend the Safe Access Zone by a specified distance up to a maximum of 250 metres. Operators must give due consideration to proportionality when determining whether to extend a Safe Access Zone and should engage with the PSNI as part of the determination process.My Department is content that the Northern HSC Trust have fully considered and examined the options available to it including extending the Safe Access Zone to 250m and the Department is content that Northern Trust will continue to keep this under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact on the arts sector in Coleraine and the wider North Coast area if the Riverside Theatre in Coleraine is closed by Ulster University.
answer_text: I have not been involved in any discussions about the Riverside Theatre but, much like the closure of any local venue, it is my assessment that, should it materialise, the impacts will be felt across the local area and beyond. There is work to be done to better understand the way funding is distributed across Northern Ireland.  This will include an informed assessment of the social and economic impact of the arts.  My officials are currently examining this as part of the development of a new Arts Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the plans for The Open at Royal Portrush Golf Club in relation to transport and roads closures; and (ii) any events his Department is planning to inform key stakeholders, including local businesses, to enable them to prepare in advance.
answer_text: The 2025 Open Golf championship at Royal Portrush is organised by the Royal and Ancient Golf Club (R&A), and as such they are responsible for all aspects of the event, including ensuring an agreed Traffic and Transport Plan is in place. I can confirm that Departmental officials have been working as part of a large multi-disciplinary team preparing for the delivery the 153rd Open at Royal Portrush. I am advised that the Traffic & Transport Plan is currently in the final stages of preparation and details of travel arrangements for the event and associated traffic restrictions will be publicised in due course.  I understand a presentation on the event was made to Portrush Town Forum on the 20th January, an official drop-in event is planned for Portrush Town Hall on the 19th February from 12.00 to 18.00 and a webinar on the 25th February from 14.00 to 15.30, and my officials will be in attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions with the Minister for the Economy in relation to the need for a veterinary school in (i) Northern Ireland; and (ii) the Ulster University, Coleraine.
answer_text: I can advise that there have been no discussions with the Minister for the Economy in relation to the need for a veterinary school in (i) Northern Ireland; and (ii) the Ulster University, Coleraine.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he is supporting Ulster University to maintain the Riverside Theatre at the Coleraine campus.
answer_text: Ulster University has not engaged with my Department in relation to support for the Riverside Theatre at the Coleraine campus.However, my Department maintains an ongoing dialogue with the University and is open to discussion of any issue affecting the institution and its campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he is supporting and funding Ulster University to develop a veterinary school at the Coleraine campus.
answer_text: Policy concerning the supply of locally trained graduates to support the veterinary profession is outside the remit of my Department.As autonomous bodies, the universities have the discretion to offer any course, including veterinary subjects, from within the existing student number cap and funding allocations.Ulster University has not recently asked for any support or funding from the Department regarding the development of a veterinary school at the Coleraine campus.My Department is open to working in partnership with others to consider the implications of such an initiative should a relevant proposal be forthcoming.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients that have been diagnosed with conditions related to the Covid-19 vaccine; and (ii) how he is supporting those with conditions related to the Covid-19 vaccine.
answer_text: Specific information in relation to the number of patients that have been diagnosed with conditions related to the Covid-19 vaccine is not gathered.All vaccines and medicines have some side effects although not everybody gets them, and the vast majority of side effects are normally mild and short lasting.  Unfortunately, in some rare cases, an individual may be injured due to receiving a vaccine.  For anyone who has been diagnosed or believes they have a condition as a result of receiving a Covid-19 vaccine, I would encourage them to contact their GP, who will be best placed to consider their patients clinical needs and tailor the most appropriate pathway for their specific condition.  In addition, the UK wide Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme (VDPS) allows people who have been severely disabled as a result of vaccination to apply for a one-off tax-free payment of £120,000.
