Applications for the payment can also be made on behalf of someone who has died after becoming severely disabled because of certain vaccinations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the proposal to transfer Emergency General Surgery (EGS) from Causeway Hospital to Antrim Area Hospital, given the challenges presenting in Emergency Departments; and (ii) how he is reassuring patients that require EGS in this context.
answer_text: The Northern HSC Trust held a public consultation on proposals to reconfigure general surgery services between 23 August and 29 November 2024.The Trust is currently preparing a consultation outcomes report which will be submitted to the Department of Health in due course, and will consider all likely impacts of proposals. I will consider any recommendation made by the NHSCT at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of (i) legislation that extends post-19 special educational needs provision to 25 years old; and (ii) moving responsibility and associated funding from his Department to the Department of Education.
answer_text: As indicated previously, improving support for people with special educational needs is a priority in my Department’s business plan and the Executive’s draft Programme for Government.  My officials are currently reviewing the relevant legislation, as well as the existing training and skills provision, to consider how best we can make improvements. This work is continuing, and I expect cross-Departmental collaboration, rather than transferring functions, will be essential to deliver improvements.   I expect to receive advice from my officials in the Spring, at which point I will outline my intended next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) legislation that extends post-19 special educational needs provision to 25 years old; and (ii) moving responsibility and associated funding from his Department to the Department of Education.
answer_text: Transitions is recognised as a long-standing challenge in NI and work is underway across Departments to improve support for children with disabilities and/or Special Education Needs. A regional protocol for HSC Trusts has been finalised to standardise the transitions process, which will be piloted in the coming months. My officials are fully engaged with colleagues across government to review and improve the SEN transitions process, including consideration for legislative change. The Chronically Sick and Disabled Person (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 provides the legislative basis to provide health and social care services for disabled and sick children as they transition into an adult service. However, it is recognised that this is only applicable to the health and social care needs that are known to Trusts and have an assessed need. There is currently no legislation in Northern Ireland to provide special educational needs pupils with an Education and Health Care plan after the age of 19.  The Department of Health has responsibility for the health & social care needs of children and adults. A change to the transition age to 25 will still require these needs to be met by Health & Social Care Trusts. Therefore, it is unlikely that responsibility and associated funding will transfer to another NI Executive department. I do support the need for exploratory work to assess the benefits of legislative change in Northern Ireland and my officials will work closely with the Department for Economy on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any guidance his Department has issued to schools in relation to the influence of misogynistic social media content on boys and young men.
answer_text: Research published by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) highlights the promotion of misogynistic ideas online as an area of concern. The Board recently launched the Online Safety hub, delivered as part of the Executive’s “Keeping Children and Young People Safe: An Online Safety Strategy” and I can confirm that my Department funds the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app which provides tailored support and information about safeguarding issues and online harms, including extremist content and social media influencers. My Department also issues guidance to schools  in relation to online safety and safeguarding more generally; Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools. My Department participates in a range of cross-departmental strategies such as the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy.  My officials continue to engage and work with the Executive Office, the Department of Justice and Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s Council (CCEA) to take forward work in relation to supporting these strategies. Our curriculum addresses issues such as violence against women and girls, understanding and respecting consent, dealing with misogyny and addressing coercive control.  This provides opportunities for pupils to discuss issues including misogyny, consent and attitudes to women.CCEA’s RSE Hub ensures that teachers can access resources to help address sensitive issues with their pupils, such as violence against women and girls, effects of social media on relationships and self-esteem and male privilege and misogyny.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the public services involved when a child is home-schooled.
answer_text: The legislative position in Northern Ireland is that the responsibility for a child’s education rests with the child’s parents. Parents may choose to educate their children at home rather than register them at a school, however if they choose to home educate their child they take on full responsibility for ensuring their child receives an efficient full-time education suitable to the child’s needs. Parents who choose to educate their children outside of the publicly funded education system do not receive financial assistance towards the education they provide.Parents who choose to home educate their children, or those considering deregistering their child for elective home education, may contact the Education Authority (EA) for advice or support. Parents who are educating a child at home who has special educational needs or a disability may ask the EA to carry out a statutory assessment or reassessment of their child’s special educational needs and this will be considered in the same way, and within the same statutory timescales as for a child attending a school. Home educating parents are also welcome to attend any training programmes delivered by the EA for parents to assist them in understanding and supporting their child’s special educational learning needs.  Should a concern arise with a child’s education, the EA as a public sector organisation will engage with the family regarding the educational concern that has been raised. Any safeguarding concerns are dealt with by social services.The EA published guidance on Elective Home Education in 2020 which outlines the current legislative position, the roles and responsibilities of parents, schools and the EA and details the process of engagement with parents/carers when concerns are raised. It also provides information and signposting for home educating parents on the support available to them.  This guidance is available on the EA website:https://www.eani.org.uk/services/elective-home-education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any of the third-level institutions have sought (i) discussion with; and (ii) support from his Department to progress a business case for a veterinary school in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I met the Vice-Chancellor of the Ulster University on 2 May 2024 to discuss the university’s ambitions and intentions, including its plans for introducing veterinary related courses, and its considerations relating to a veterinary school.  No further discussions have been sought by the third-level institutions on this matter.  In terms of support, decisions on which courses to offer are for the universities themselves, in conjunction with the Department for the Economy, but my Department is happy to engage with the universities on relevant aspects related to DAERA’s responsibilities as needed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any guidance his Department has issued to schools in relation to the influence of misogynistic social media content on boys and young men.
answer_text: Research published by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) highlights the promotion of misogynistic ideas online as an area of concern. The Board recently launched the Online Safety hub, delivered as part of the Executive’s “Keeping Children and Young People Safe: An Online Safety Strategy” and I can confirm that my Department funds the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app which provides tailored support and information about safeguarding issues and online harms, including extremist content and social media influencers. My Department also issues guidance to schools  in relation to online safety and safeguarding more generally; Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools. My Department participates in a range of cross-departmental strategies such as the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy.  My officials continue to engage and work with the Executive Office, the Department of Justice and Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s Council (CCEA) to take forward work in relation to supporting these strategies. Our curriculum addresses issues such as violence against women and girls, understanding and respecting consent, dealing with misogyny and addressing coercive control.  This provides opportunities for pupils to discuss issues including misogyny, consent and attitudes to women.CCEA’s RSE Hub ensures that teachers can access resources to help address sensitive issues with their pupils, such as violence against women and girls, effects of social media on relationships and self-esteem and male privilege and misogyny.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any recent discussions with universities in relation to the progression of a business case for a veterinary school in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The supply of veterinary surgeons is the policy responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and not within the remit of my Department. I have not had any recent discussions with the universities regarding a business case for a veterinary school here.	As autonomous bodies, the universities have the discretion to offer any course, including veterinary subjects, from within existing student numbers and funding allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the current legislation which imposes duties on drivers who injure certain animals, for example dogs, on roads or public places, but does not extend to private land; and (ii) whether causing unnecessary suffering to an animal, as an offence, covers injury through driving on private land.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 places responsibilities on drivers on the occurrence of accidents causing injury to certain animals.The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (the Act) sets out the offence of unnecessary suffering to animals, caused by any action or inaction which causes physical or mental suffering to an animal. The suffering is deemed to be unnecessary if it could have been reasonably avoided, terminated or reduced.The Act does not define specific actions or places which would result in an offence being committed. It is up to the investigating body to determine whether an offence has occurred.  My Department is not responsible for either the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 or the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011. Any assessments of these policies would be a matter for the relevant Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the limitations of securing a manslaughter conviction where the cause of death was dangerous driving which occurred on private land; and (ii) whether she has any plans to extend death by dangerous driving offence to include private property.
answer_text: Manslaughter falls into the two broad categories of involuntary and voluntary. Involuntary manslaughter is unlawful killing without the intent to kill or cause serious harm. Voluntary manslaughter occurs when all the elements for murder are present, including an intention to kill or cause serious harm, but the crime is reduced to manslaughter by reason of loss of control or diminished responsibility. If a death occurs which fulfils the criteria for either of these categories, then a manslaughter conviction may be possible.   The outcome of any charge is a matter for the courts, taking into consideration all the facts and evidence presented in the case.The offence of causing death by dangerous driving is contained within the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, for which my Department is not responsible.  Any plans to extend the offence would fall outside my remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of whether Northern Ireland is an ageing population; and (ii) whether this will underpin the design and delivery of health and social care services.
answer_text: (i) It is widely recognised, and reported, that Northern Ireland has a growing and ageing population, and with this comes the expectancy of people living longer with multiple conditions.  This adds to the complexity of needs that our services must manage, and places additional demands and pressures on the system. Consequently, there is a fundamental need to change how we deliver services if we are to meet these demands and pressures. (ii) It is essential to the successful delivery of health and social care services that detailed consideration is given to the demographics of those who will require care. The design and delivery of future services must, and will, take into account the expected needs of the population now, and in the future.The development of the Integrated Care System NI will support us in doing this, enabling collaborative working across the HSC sector and beyond, placing a focus on people keeping well in the first instance, providing timely, co-ordinated care when they are not, and supporting people to self-care when appropriate. This approach will help support us to ensure that we are maximizing the resource we have available to deliver the best outcomes for our population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process for ensuring roads are gritted prior to dangerous driving conditions.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by the Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Departments website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks and even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed.As road users we all need to be mindful of the road conditions and adjust the way we drive, ride or cycle and during particularly wintry conditions people should only travel if absolutely necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any discussions he has had with the Chief Constable in relation to the regular presence of officers in Emergency Departments assisting vulnerable patients; and (ii) any appropriate Health and Social Care resource that could be deployed to support these patients after check-in to free up PSNI resource.
answer_text: I am aware that currently police officers often accompany individuals in mental health crisis to emergency departments and can spend prolonged periods of time there.  The Right Care Right Person (RCRP) model once implemented will aim to address this issue. I have had a number of discussions in recent months with both the Chief Constable and senior PSNI representatives about the introduction of RCRP in Northern Ireland, in order to ensure close and effective cooperation between health and policing as the implementation is progressed. An RCRP Strategic Oversight Group, chaired jointly by DoH and DoJ senior officials, has been established to ensure that the approach to RCRP implementation is planned and developed through cross-agency partnerships. The Group met for the first time on 29 October 2024.  The focus of the Oversight Group is to identify and address the required operational change, while recognising and respecting the operational independence of PSNI, and ensuring effective future partnership working between all relevant statutory partners in the delivery of health and social care, including mental health care.  This will include how best to address the issue of police time spent supporting vulnerable patients at emergency departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the health and safety of patients waiting for long periods in Emergency Departments for admittance to hospital and treatment.
answer_text: My Department recognises that patients are experiencing significant delays for admission to hospital beds across all of our acute hospital sites leading to crowding in Emergency Departments and delayed ambulance off loads with a subsequent delay for the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) in responding to emergency 999 calls. Although the issues are system-wide, pressures do culminate in Emergency Departments at the front door of the hospital. We are doing everything we can to reduce this pressure. We are continuously working to improve our range of community services, unscheduled care and same day emergency care services to avoid hospital admission and improve patient flow where possible.Unfortunately, the demand for hospital services has resulted in some of our patients being cared for in additional care spaces such as beds, chairs and trollies in non-clinical areas, including corridors within ward and department areas. Whilst this is not the standard we want in providing services, our priority remains to ensure the best possible care and treatment to patients. Our staff are working in difficult circumstances to protect the safety and dignity of patientsAll additional care spaces being used have been risk assessed by Trust staff with the aim to use these areas for the shortest period of time as possible. Senior staff within the Trusts monitor the use and de-escalation of any additional beds and care spaces on a daily basis.As patient safety remains my Department and Trusts’ priority, careful consideration is made in identification of patients who are deemed appropriate for allocation to an additional bed/care space, utilising an agreed set of exclusion criteria.  A patient who is placed in an additional care space receives both medical and nursing care dictated by their clinical need to the same safety standard of all patients placed within a designated ward bed space. Trusts will increase the number of staffs on duty for these respective areas so that these patients have access to appropriate care, although mitigations against deficits in the privacy and dignity of patients are much more challenging and, regrettably, at times insufficient to address privacy and dignity concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to improve accessibility for complete domiciliary care packages for those fit for discharge from hospital.
answer_text: The main reason that some people in hospital cannot be discharged home with a complete domiciliary care package is due to packages not being available in the area they live.  There is some capacity in the whole system but we are finding that this capacity is not in the area where the need exists.  The Health and Social Care trusts have given my department reassurance that where capacity exists in the required area that packages of care are being put in place.  We also know that the trusts are working to review rotas and runs to try to maximize the available capacity.You will remember when I wrote to you back in October I referenced that my department had funded a pilot which was then rolled out permanently for an Early Review Team in each of the five HSCT’s.  These teams continue to generate domiciliary hours that are recycled back into the system.  Some of the trusts have bolstered the team to cover a wider area.  Some trusts have continued to increase their block purchased contracts through the Rapid Response route, this is where the trust pay an enhanced rate to the independent sector who ring-fence domiciliary hours specifically to assist with hospital discharges.Through the Social Care Collaborative Forum, the Fair Work Forum and the work of the Project Steering Group for Home Care there is a recognition for the need to improve this service.  Areas to be addressed include fair pay, staff training and opportunities for career progression for the homecare workforce to improve recruitment and retention.Looking to the near future (2025), the intention is to carry out a review of the regional homecare contract and begin the work to develop a new model for homecare that will better fit our requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to develop a new Active Ageing Strategy.
answer_text: I am currently considering the next steps in this important area, and I have asked officials to set out options for the way forward.I hope to make an announcement in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether she has any plans to publish the report by Judge Burgess on the fundamental review of criminal legal aid in Northern Ireland; (ii) the recommendations in the report; (iii) the recommendations she has accepted and agreed to take forward; and (iv) the recommendations she does not accept or will not fully implement.
answer_text: The Report of the Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid was published on 2 December 2024 alongside a summary of the Department’s analysis and proposed approach to recommendations. Summaries of the other evidence which informed decisions on the Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme was also published on 2 December.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she engaged with key stakeholders, including the Law Society and the Bar Council NI, before her Ministerial Statement Enabling Access to Justice: Reform of Legal Aid.
answer_text: The programme has been informed by widespread evidence gathering and detailed engagement with stakeholders who have had the opportunity to meet directly with officials and to respond to the call for evidence for the Foundational Review of Civil Legal Services and to meet and submit evidence to His Honour Judge Burgess who led the Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid. That includes the Law Society and Bar Council with whom my Department enjoys regular contact, both on an ad hoc basis and through monthly stocktake meetings. I also had the opportunity to meet with representatives of the Bar and the Law Society in advance of my statement to the Assembly. The Programme represents the totality of evidence gathered through that engagement process and other sources, but engagement will continue as I consult on the programme delivery plan and each of the proposals therein.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment on access to justice if solicitors and barristers refuse to take forward criminal and civil legal aid cases, particularly in cases relating to domestic abuse.
answer_text: While I understand the concerns which have driven recent strike action by members of the professions, withdrawal of services causes delay and delay impacts most acutely on witnesses and the victims who are waiting to receive justice and protection. The strike action was also precipitous.  The continuing ongoing investment in the system is considerable. The legal aid budget at the start of the financial year was the highest opening allocation in the history of the legal aid scheme. That follows a spend of £114m last year and I secured additional funding in the June and October monitoring rounds which means we are on track to meet that spend again this year.  I have also announced a significant uplift in fees for advice and representation in criminal and civil and family cases. I would hope the professions would take cognisance of that investment and that there will be no further unnecessary adverse impact on the victims and witnesses waiting to receive justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she is ensuring the independence of the legal system, further to the reforms announced on 2 December 2024 which propose reducing or removing independent functions.
answer_text: The reform programme I announced on 2 December 2024 seeks to ensure the continued provision of high-quality advice and representation and that arrangements are robust and provide sufficient assurance and transparency on quality and value for money. Proposals, therefore, seek to build on the strengths of the existing framework and will ensure the continued availability of independent challenge and appellate functions. All proposals will be subject to consultation to allow intended arrangements to be explored in detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how the plans outlined in her Ministerial Statement Enabling Access to Justice: Reform of Legal Aid compare with other regions of the (i) UK; and (ii) Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Proposals outlined in my Statement on Enabling Access to Justice Reform have been informed by extensive engagement and research including comparative analysis of systems and supports in place in other jurisdictions. Whilst the scope of proposed reform would not allow a detailed comparison of proposals to be provided in a written answer, reform proposals have sought to reflect learning from other UK jurisdictions and Republic of Ireland and, in particular, where operating models appear to provide greater choice, more effective delivery models for citizens, and greater transparency and assurance on value for money. Each proposal outlined in my Statement will be subject to detailed consultation which where appropriate will include comparative information on other jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any specific plans to additionally support women and their children in temporary accommodation due to domestic abuse.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Domestic Abuse Action Plan was launched on 10 December. It contains 16 actions including a commitment to review existing training for frontline staff and the development of new training courses ranging from basic awareness of domestic abuse to identification of, and response to, domestic abuse within the workplace, tenant’s homes, Housing Executive hostels and during interviews for customers. The plan aims to improve communication to ensure those experiencing domestic abuse are aware of the support available and how to access this.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the planned legislation to ensure all children remain in education or training until they are 18 years old, for his assessment of whether this should include a requirement to extend post-19 special educational needs provision to 25 years old.
answer_text: Improving support for people with special educational needs is a priority in my Department’s business plan and the Executive’s draft Programme for Government.  My officials are currently reviewing the relevant legislation, as well as the existing training and skills provision, to consider how best we can make improvements both in terms of services and legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any discussions he has had with the UK Government in relation to the General Product Safety Regulation being introduced by the European Commission, which has resulted in suppliers from Great Britain no longer supplying to Northern Ireland; and (ii) any support he is offering businesses in Northern Ireland relating to challenges of the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: Product safety is a reserved matter for the British Government with the Department for Business and Trade (DBT) holding competence for the General Product Safety Regulation (GPSR). 		My officials have engaged with DBT to ensure that support is made available for businesses seeking to comply with GPSR requirements. DBT have published detailed guidance and offer direct support to queries addressed to OPSS.enquiries@businessandtrade.gov.uk. 	Guidance can be found at: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/general-product-safety-regulations-northern-ireland. 	My Department’s partner agencies, InterTradeIreland and Invest NI, have been providing tailored support for businesses to navigate the new trading and regulatory environment. This support includes, a range of online resources and free tailored consultancy and support services. Further my Department and Invest NI are leading a Dual Market Forum with business representatives to help co-design materials and initiatives to promote the unique trading opportunities provided by the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has any plans to introduce the Warm Home Discount Scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Thank you for your correspondence dated 20 November 2024 asking have I any plans to introduce the Warm Homes Discount Scheme for the North of Ireland.You will be aware of the British Warm Home Discount scheme, which is paid for by a charge levied on all domestic customers in Britain.  The British and North of Ireland Energy markets differ in several ways, most notably size.  The costs of implementing and administering a scheme in the North of Ireland equivalent to the British Scheme would be disproportionately high given our small market size and would lead to higher bills for many customers, including, potentially, those in, or close to, fuel poverty.  This would also introduce additional administrative costs for suppliers which could have unintended consequences for supplier tariffs and competition at retail level. We do already have the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP), which receives £8m of funding raised through electricity charges.  In September 2024 I announced a further £2.5m funding package for NISEP which will allow energy efficiency measures to be installed in more low-income households.You will be aware that the Department for Communities has lead responsibility for fuel poverty and any mitigations which may be put in place to assist with rising fuel costs. The Department for the Economy assists in tackling fuel poverty through setting energy efficiency policy that supports the reduction in household energy use and as a consequence household energy bills.My officials are in regular dialogue with their colleagues in the Department for Communities to identify further interventions that may assist customers facing increases in energy costs.  The Department for Communities will lead the Executive’s response, particularly in relation to vulnerable consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his plans to maximise the opportunities that will arise from The Open returning to Royal Portrush Golf Club in 2025.
answer_text: Tourism NI, on behalf of my Department, is committed to working with key partners to deliver benefits from The 153rd Open in 2025 in excess of those of The 148th Open in 2019, which was over £100 million of combined economic and media benefit. The Open in 2025 is expected to attract over 250,000 visitors and will showcase the North as a destination through the huge global broadcast reach and PR coverage generated, that will deliver a legacy for years to come. An industry engagement programme is being delivered in the lead up to the event, in partnership with local authorities. This will assist and advise traders and businesses on how best to avail of opportunities that The Open will present, including providing reasons for event visitors to extend their stay and explore the wider area, alongside the ongoing support for the development of new and immersive authentic experiences that deliver on the 'Embrace a Giant Spirit' brand. Working in close collaboration with the R&A, the intention is to nurture workforce opportunities that will have a legacy beyond July 2025, through skills development and employment before, during and after the event, as well as volunteering across the event week. Discussions are progressing with Derry and Strabane Labour Market Partnership to ensure that opportunities are realised across the region. Tourism NI will support the industry to recruit and retain staff through its ongoing ‘Make it Here’ careers campaign and e-learning platform. In addition, they will work with Fáilte Ireland to extend its Employer Excellence Programme into the north to foster a culture of excellence in the workplace, making tourism and hospitality sectors rewarding and attractive places to work.  In partnership with Tourism Ireland, Tourism NI will deliver a significant PR and marketing campaign locally, nationally and globally, in the lead up to, during and after the event. Aside from the world-class golf offering, other experiences across the north will be promoted including high quality accommodation, restaurants, visitor attractions and entertainment. In collaboration with Invest NI, a programme of activity will also be delivered to support the presentation of our unique and compelling inward investment proposition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any discussions he has had with the UK Government in relation to VAT for Northern Ireland businesses; and (ii) how he is advocating for better support.
answer_text: My Department has repeatedly raised issues relating to VAT with the British Treasury, in particular highlighting the negative impact of higher rates in the hospitality and tourism sectors here when compared with the South where a lower rate applies. This remains an Excepted matter and unfortunately the British Government has so far been unwilling make any changes to the VAT rate. I meet with British Government ministers on a regular basis and have pressed for a progressive tax system that both properly funds public services, and also supports economic development. I will continue to work with the Minister for the Economy as appropriate to make the case to the Treasury for a VAT regime which enables and supports our businesses to compete within the all-Ireland economy. To better understand the costs faced by business, I have also asked my officials to scope out the potential to commission a sectoral study on the cost of doing business in the North. My officials will continue to highlight the issues faced by local businesses in their regular meetings with Treasury counterparts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to commission new research into mental health in Northern Ireland to aid the development of effective strategies.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the levels of antidepressants prescribed in Northern Ireland compared with other regions of the UK; and (ii) to detail how the use of talking therapies in Northern Ireland to treat depression and anxiety compares with other regions of the UK.
answer_text: As noted in the General Pharmaceutical Services for Northern Ireland Annual Statistics 2023/24, it is difficult to directly compare dispensing levels across the UK due to differences in prescribing practices. It also notes that it is not possible to infer the medical conditions for which patients are being treated on the basis of the medications dispensed to them. Many medications, including anti-depressants, are used to treat multiple conditions.My Department and I remain committed to the implementation of the Mental Health Strategy (MHS) 2021-2031, which fully recognises the benefits of non-pharmaceutical supports, for example talking therapies, for individuals struggling with their mental health, including those impacted by depression and anxiety.  However, full implementation of the action to expand therapy hubs will require additional recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has assessed the impact of VAT burdens on small and medium-sized enterprises in Northern Ireland, particularly those reliant on cross-border trade.
answer_text: VAT remains an Excepted Matter for the British Government and responsibility for all issues on VAT is a matter for Treasury. However, I am aware of the particular challenges for hospitality businesses here given the lower rate of VAT for similar businesses in the South.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans to review the assessment process of non-domestic rates.
answer_text: There are no plans to review the assessment process of non-domestic rates. Non-domestic rates are assessed on the basis of statutory provision, and critically valuation treatment and valuation caselaw which is broadly harmonised both here and England, Scotland and Wales.However, in April 2024, I announced a new non-domestic revaluation, Reval2026. This is an exercise to revalue all non-domestic properties for business rates, marking the fourth revaluation of this type in the last ten years.Regular revaluations are essential due to fluctuations in the property market caused by changes in economic conditions, market sentiment, and local developments. A revaluation serves to re-base property valuations, and the relativity between property types and locations to account for these changes, ensuring the distribution of the rate burden fairly reflects up to date, accurate market values. It is not a means of raising more revenue.Reval2026 valuations are based on a valuation date of 1 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans to coordinate the public, community and medical transport sectors to improve access and connectivity.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they are leading any review and learning opportunities from the Executive response related to the Covid-19 pandemic.
answer_text: We have learned lessons from the Pandemic and have started work to implement changes to ensure we are better prepared for a future pandemic.  We continue to engage and respond to the requirements of the UK Covid Inquiry, including consideration and implementation of recommendations arising from the Module 1 report. In response to a NI Audit Office Report (Overview of the Northern Ireland Executive’s response to the COVID-19 pandemic - Third Report), our department coordinated an exercise across all departments to identify individual departmental reviews on the response to the Covid-19 pandemic. A paper was shared with the NICS Board.  Our department continues to share lessons learned through the work of the Civil Contingences Group (NI) and the Covid Inquiry Cross Departmental Covid-19 Modular Recommendations and Lessons Learned Steering Group.  The Steering Group was established by our department in September 2024 to take forward consideration and implementation of Covid-19 Inquiry Modular recommendations, as well as embedding Lessons Learned from responding to the Inquiry across all departments.  The Executive discussed the Draft Response to Module 1 report on 19 December 2024, the response was agreed at the Executive meeting in early January 2025.  The aim is to publish this in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have had any discussions with the British Government in relation to a post-conflict reconciliation process.
answer_text: We are committed to promoting reconciliation. While we have had no specific discussions with the UK Government in relation to a post-conflict reconciliation process, we have committed, in the draft Programme for Government, to building on the successes of the past twenty-five years to make sure that our peace is meaningful and prosperous for everyone here.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for their assessment of the trauma that exists due to the conflict in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether they have any plans to review the impact of all forms of trauma and the public response across Executive Departments and public bodies.
answer_text: The legacy of the past continues to affect the everyday lives of many people here. The Victims and Survivors Service (VSS) and funded community partners continue to support around 16,000 individuals annually. The impact crosses generations, so it is vital that we work towards breaking the chain of trauma which has resulted from our difficult past. We are committed to embedding trauma-informed approaches within the work of our department and the wider public sector. In the draft Programme for Government, we have committed to developing a trauma-informed public sector and understanding the impact of trauma in society.A Trauma Informed Practice team in the Safeguarding Board (SBNI) is funded by the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime. The SBNI’s Trauma Informed Practice Implementation managers will continue to support member and partner agencies to embed trauma informed approaches building on existing knowledge of adversity and trauma.As part of this cross-Executive Programme, a prevalence study is currently underway to establish a comprehensive understanding of the levels of Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs), childhood trauma and exposure to violence in Northern Ireland. This will be both comparable with other international studies and will also evidence the relationship between these factors and health-related outcomes.  The report is expected to launch in February 2025.  Within The Executive Office, the vision of our new Strategy for Victims and Survivors of the Troubles/Conflict 2024-2034 is of ‘a trauma informed society that addresses the needs of victims and survivors.’ We are committed to ensuring that the right trauma informed, and victim centred services are in place to empower and support victims and survivors. We are pleased that the VSS recently became the first organisation here to be accredited with a Silver ‘Trauma-Informed: Working with Trauma Quality Mark’, offering formal recognition of the VSS trauma-informed approach.In addition, the Regional Trauma Network (RTN) opened in April 2023 with a phased approach.  Phase 1 focuses on victims and survivors with the statutory and community and voluntary sector working collaboratively to provide specialist trauma care. In addition to referrals between statutory and community sectors, significant collaborative work is ongoing within the RTN in wider areas relating to trauma care, including Children and Young People, Training and Development, Research, Trauma and Addictions, and Trauma and Chronic Pain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to fund the service expansion of the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust to meet the demands of poor mental health within the Police Service of Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
answer_text: The Department fund the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) to provide clinical and support services to police officers, former police officers, and their families, prior to and after cessation of their service.  In addition, PRRT provide services to a range of other organisations from within the Justice family, including the NIPS, and recover their costs from those organisations. Given the significant and ongoing financial pressures facing the Department, there are currently no plans to fund PRRT for any expansion of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered developing a public health strategy for trauma and intergenerational trauma, including post-conflict trauma and other forms of trauma, separate to the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: There are a number of different initiatives exploring and addressing the impact of trauma on the population of Northern Ireland.  It is important to have a joined up approach and I am pleased that the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland is leading work in this area,  and has published a report based on research findings from Queen’s University, Belfast earlier this year ‘We are on a journey’: Implementing Trauma Informed Approaches in Northern Ireland.’This research calls for the adoption of a trauma informed approach across all systems, organisations, policies and services in Northern Ireland, for the benefit of all in our society.  To this end they have launched a Trauma Informed Toolkit to embed a trauma informed approach within organisations and systems.Various organisations have now gained specific accreditation  in being trauma informed.The Regional Trauma Network (RTN) has also been developed specifically to address the impact of trauma caused by the Troubles/conflict. This network is envisaged to cover from cradle to grave, across all of Northern Ireland and operates in partnership with the Health and Social Care system, community and voluntary sector partners and the Victims and Survivors Service. In terms of intergenerational trauma, the RTN vision includes separate adult and infant/children/young people’s trauma services in each Trust. Troubles-related trauma can manifest differently in our population of under 18s who have not directly experienced the Troubles/conflict.  To this end the consultants engage in a range of work with other professionals to promote understanding of the impact of trauma via psychoeduation, consultation and training as well as some one to one work with impacted children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of why addressing financial abuse is difficult; (ii) to detail her Department’s work relating to financial abuse; and (iii) to detail any plans to raise public awareness in this area.
answer_text: Ensuring that there are effective protections in place for victims of domestic abuse, including where this takes the form of financial abuse, is a priority for me as Justice Minister.  I have listened to many victims and their families who have shared their painful experiences, and I recognise the courage it takes to speak about them.Addressing financial abuse can be difficult because people may not recognise it as such. Financial matters are often seen as a private and, therefore, people may be reluctant to report it, or may be embarrassed to do so.The Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 introduced a new domestic abuse offence which criminalises behaviour that is controlling or coercive or amounts to psychological, emotional, technological or financial abuse. It captures patterns of physical and/or psychological abuse by a partner, ex-partner or close family member and covers behaviour that is physically violent, threatening or intimidating.When the new offence was introduced, my Department launched a public awareness campaign including a series of online videos detailing the various types of domestic abuse; financial abuse is depicted in these powerful videos and they sign post victims on how to report the abuse.  Additional advice on the domestic abuse offence and the videos remains available on the NI Direct website under the title ‘Still Abuse’.Whilst PSNI training is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, I have been advised that the PSNI has undertaken an extensive training programme in relation to the new domestic abuse offence, including understanding coercive controlling behaviour, the impact of domestic abuse and pathways to support. That training is on-going and PSNI has advised that work is underway to develop a series of case studies that can be used as training materials across the organisation which will help in addressing financial abuse. I am also aware that the draft Adult Protection Bill, which is being developed by the Department of Health, is also likely to make provision that may help with addressing financial abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to (i) support dentists to continue providing a public service; and (ii) encourage dentists back who are no longer providing a public service.
answer_text: I am aware of the challenges currently being experienced within General Dental Services and I am committed to taking meaningful action to ensure the service works better for both patients and dental professionals. This is why for many months now, the focus of my Department has been on improving access to General Dental Services, particularly for high-need patients, and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices – such as the £9.2m investment announced earlier this year to reestablish the Enhanced Child Examination (ECE), establish the Dental Access Service (DAS), and fund the 30% enhancement to the fees paid for fillings.The DAS commenced on 1st August 2024. There are currently 30 practices participating in the scheme, details of which (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/. In the first ten weeks the scheme has enabled the treatment of more than 4700 patients with an urgent or pressing dental condition.From June to November 2024, 18,100 newly registered children have benefitted from the Enhanced Child Examination scheme.Early indications are that these measures have had a positive impact and my Department will be monitoring the results closely.While interventions such as the DAS and ECE have been necessary to stabilise the service, long-term change will require sustained effort, rather than a single, one-off intervention. My officials will be engaging with the British Dental Association on how best to achieve these strategic aims, and address the concerns of the profession, as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the social housing waiting list in Northern Ireland compared with other regions of the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: The housing challenges that we face here are significant and the trend of increasing numbers on the waiting list and in housing stress is not sustainable. My assessment of the issues here has informed my Housing Supply Strategy, which sets out a fifteen-year framework, endorsed by the Executive, for the whole system change that is needed. In terms of how the waiting list in NI compares to other jurisdictions I have sought this information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“It is difficult to compare social housing waiting lists in Northern Ireland with other regions of the UK and Ireland. This is due to the significant variation in how social housing waiting lists operate across the UK and Ireland. There can also be variation in how they operate between local authorities within a country. There are differences in how eligibility for social housing is assessed. For example, in the Republic of Ireland, households must be below a particular income threshold to be considered for social housing. Households in receipt of Housing Assistance Payment in Ireland are not included on the waiting list for social housing (although they can be on a transfer list). In GB and the Republic of Ireland, some local authorities operate a local connections test to assess eligibility for social housing, whilst others do not. In Northern Ireland the Housing Selection Scheme does not refer to local connection. There are also differences in how applicants are prioritised / ranked on a social housing waiting list. In Northern Ireland we operate one points-based system for the whole country. In England, Scotland, Wales and the Republic of Ireland, each local authority has its own allocations policy. In the Republic of Ireland most local authorities operate a “time on list” approach, so those that have been waiting the longest are allocated a property first. In Great Britain (GB), there is variation both across nations and within nations in how priority is determined. This includes the use of a banded approach to allocations (with varying number and types of bands), a points-based approach, or a combination of points and bands. A Choice-based lettings approach to allocations is also used by some local authorities across GB and Ireland.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered any homelessness projections for the next ten years if Northern Ireland continues to not meet demand for social and affordable housing.
answer_text: There are no future projections for Homelessness presentations or acceptances.  The circumstances of each household are considered on a case by case basis by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to determine eligibility for housing assistance in line with the relevant legislation.  Homelessness statistics are published regularly by my Department (See Housing statistics | Department for Communities).  There has been minimal change in the numbers of Homelessness presentations and acceptances over recent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17492/22-27, to provide a timeline of when he plans to begin work at a regional level to take forward a targeted screening programme.
answer_text: As outlined in AQW 17492/22-27, we are at the early stages of scoping out what the requirements of a targeted lung cancer screening programme, for those at high risk of the disease for Northern Ireland will be.  It is anticipated that this work will begin in 2025, subject to financial considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the varying interpretations of mental capacity in medical settings compared with care settings, including domiciliary care; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to address this.
answer_text: (i) The Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) is legislation designed to promote rights and safeguard vulnerable people who are not able to make decisions for themselves. It also includes rules around the Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS).The legislation is designed to support decision making for people who are vulnerable, whilst also promoting autonomy and inclusion. There are five statutory Principles that are to be upheld at all times when working under this legislation. These are:No-one should be treated as lacking capacity unless proven they doNo assumptions can be made (based on any condition the person may have, or any characteristics of the person)Help and support must be providedNo assumptions can be made because of unwise decisionsAll acts and decisions must be made in the person’s best interests.The Act provides staff, carers, senior clinicians and managers, direction on the applicability of this legislation, known as the Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards, and affords protection from liability as long as decision making includes:A reasonable belief that the person lacks capacity to make a particular decision;A reasonable belief that the decision is in the person’s best interests; A belief that failure to detain the person would create a risk of serious harm to that person or someone else; andThat the additional safeguards required for lawful deprivation of liberty, are in place.The Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards are applicable across all settings, including medical, care homes and domiciliary care and decisions taken in relation to Deprivation of Liberty must be authorised by due process.The professionals who work with patients across these settings are required to undertake person centred assessments to determine, if they meet the threshold for a deprivation of liberty authorisation.Mental Capacity legislation can be applied in both medical and care settings to reflect both the needs of the individual and the setting in which the patient is currently in. If it is believed that a person lacks capacity; is unable to leave, and under continuous control and supervision; their confinement is guided by whether an application is made for a Trust Panel Authorisation (TPA) or Short-Term Detention Authorisation (STDA) to provide the authority to detain a person. STDA applications are made for patients who may require this authority whilst they are in an acute setting for a relatively short period. This authorisation, as the title reflects, enables the patient to be cared for, under continual supervision and control, (meaning they are not free to leave), for up to 28 days. People who reside in care homes or domiciliary settings normally require a Trust Panel Authorisation (TPA) which lasts longer than a STDA. The TPA is put in place for up to 6 months with the option to extend for a further 6 months and yearly thereafter. The interpretation and application of mental capacity in Northern Ireland can vary between medical and care settin

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the safeguards in place to prevent domestic abusers from using the health and social care system to further abuse their victims.
answer_text: Within HSC Trusts, access to patient records is provided via a role-based ‘need to know’ security principle, meaning users only see what they need in order to conduct their job safely and effectively.  No additional information is accessible without express consent.  Access is guarded by professional responsibility, and all HSC staff are contractually obligated to ensure that personal data is treated with the highest levels of confidentiality, and they adhere to the HSC Code of Practice on Protecting the Confidentiality of Service User Information, GDPR legislation, and the common law Duty of Confidentiality.  One important benefit of digital records is that access within the system is fully auditable with system-generated reports available to monitor and identify inappropriate access.  Procedures are also in place to deal with any suspected data security breach.Contractual obligations with independent service providers include data protection and confidentiality.In HSCTs where Encompass has been rolled out, Trust Public Protection Teams can use a ‘Public Protection’ flag on Encompass for convicted offenders and ‘Public Protection/Sharing to Safeguard’ flag for MARAC victims and alleged perpetrators heard at MARAC. These flags are only visible to health & social care staff and will therefore alert health and social car staff if a known perpetrator of domestic abuse is utilising any health and social care services.Access to information on My Care is controlled through use of personal usernames, passwords, and identity authentication via nidirect.  My Care cannot be accessed without supplying a personal nidirect username and password.  My Care allows proxy access to patients / service users’ records.  All Adult Safeguarding staff have been reminded to be aware of the potential misuse of this by abusers.  A Proxy Policy has been cascaded to Trust staff.  Trust staff can revoke proxy access, when there are concerns of domestic abuse or coercive control.  My Care currently does not display social care information, including adult safeguarding.  In addition, flags for example to alert for domestic abuse, are not visible on My Care.Furthermore, training pathways are in place which aim to ensure anyone who has contact with people who use Trust services will have an understanding of domestic and sexual abuse. Those staff who are tasked with working with people and families affected by this issue can avail of more specialist training to equip them to undertake their role. The family court in Northern Ireland has a range of powers to deal with cases involving family conflict and separation under The Children (NI) Order 1995. However, the legislation is clear that when considering any change to a child's living and contact arrangements, the welfare of the child is the court’s paramount concern. Contact decisions which come before the court are a matter for that court. Court Children’s Services, located within HSCTs, are responsible for discharging the Tr

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the levels of social workers (a) in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) across Northern Ireland to support children, the vulnerable and their families; (ii) to detail the impact a shortage of social workers will have on service users; and (iii) to detail any plans he has to improve this.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust has reported that their current level of social workers is insufficient to meet the support needs of service users. Ensuring a sufficient level of social workers continues to be a challenge across all Health and Social Care Trusts due to recruitment, retention and sickness absence issues. This impacts on social work availability in some services, resulting in the use of waiting lists/unallocated cases to manage referrals, therefore impacting on waiting times for service users in some areas. While there are protocols in place to manage waiting lists, there is the risk that high priority need will not be identified and met. My department continues to work collaboratively with all five Health and Social Care Trusts to address vacancy and absence levels in social work services.  This has involved a number of Department-led regional initiatives in an effort to stabilise the HSC social work workforce, including: Ceasing the use of recruitment agency social workers in June 2023. The fast-track recruitment of newly qualifying social workers into vacant posts.Participating in regional recruitment exercises for Band 6 social work staff.Developing an evidence base for safer staffing in social work in order to issue guidance to HSC Trusts in 2025.However, despite this activity there continues to be an inadequate supply of social workers to meet current service demands. I have secured funding for an additional 40 training places for social work this year and I will seek to make further places available as funding allows.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 will be fully implemented.
answer_text: Currently there is no indicative timeframe for full implementation of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.A joint Department of Health and Department of Justice project team has recently been established to commence a programme of work over the next 12 months to scope the remaining uncommenced provisions of the Act and develop a costed future implementation plan. This will help inform future decision making around full implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how mothers experiencing (i) trauma; and (ii) postnatal depression are supported to remain with their children.
answer_text: The specialist perinatal mental health teams that are now operational in each Health and Social Care Trust provide a comprehensive service dedicated to treating illness in the mother alongside supporting and enhancing the relationship between the mother and her infant. The teams have a range of professionals with skills and expertise in this area, including parent infant psychotherapists, health visitors, midwives and psychologists.   Each team is already starting to see very positive outcomes from these interventions.The teams are also involved in training initiatives with a wide range of professionals for those women who do not meet threshold for specialist services. In addition there are also a wide range of services already in existence in maternity services, primary care and the community and voluntary sector that adopt a trauma focused approach and are dedicated to supporting women to parent their children,In addition, my officials are also progressing the provision of a Mother and Baby Unit.  In early 2023 my Department commissioned an independent review to make recommendations and provide advice on a suitable model and location for a Mother and Baby Unit, which would best meet the needs of the population.   The Department received the final report from the review team in mid-September 2023.  It provided recommendations on the preferred service model for the unit, and the location of the Mother and Baby Unit, with the Belfast City Hospital site identified as the most suitable location. At that point, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was invited to prepare a business case for the provision of the proposed regional unit.The business case for Mother and Baby Unit is currently being progressed. It should be noted that progression of this project will remain subject to the necessary funding for the unit being identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the current workload of the Office of Care and Protection in relation to Enduring Powers of Attorney registrations; and (ii) to detail any plans she has to (a) streamline; or (b) digitise this process.
answer_text: (i) Statistics on the workload of the Office of Care and Protection (OCP) in relation to Enduring Powers of Attorney (EPA) applications for registration are provided in the table below. 201820192020202120222023EPA applications Received118812101099134114331631The statistics demonstrate that, between 2018 and 2023, there has been an increase in applications of over 37%.  The OCP target is to register 95% of EPA applications received, where no objection has been lodged, within 40 days of receipt of the required information.  Currently, OCP is achieving a rate of 97%.  (ii) While the OCP administers the registration of EPAs, the substantive law contained in the Enduring Powers of Attorney (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 is the responsibility of the Department of Finance. Any review of the 1987 Order or streamlining of the registration process would therefore be a matter for the Minister of Finance to consider in conjunction with relevant stakeholders. However, to improve the experience for applicants, the EPA user guidance in relation to registration is currently in the process of being updated. In addition, the paper application forms needed to register an EPA are being redrafted in a digital format.  It is anticipated that this will make the process more straightforward for applicants and improve efficiency.  These forms will be available via NI Direct for downloading, printing and completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the measures in place to prevent the misuse or abuse of Enduring Powers of Attorney (EPA); and (ii) the oversight process once an EPA is registered.
answer_text: (i) The substantive law contained in the Enduring Powers of Attorney (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 is the responsibility of the Department of Finance. An Enduring Power of Attorney (EPA) enables a person (the Donor), to designate trusted family members or others (the Attorney), for future substitute decision-making over property and finance matters, in the event of a loss of mental capacity. An EPA is a legal document and the Donor has the power to place restrictions or conditions on when and how the Attorney may act. The Donor may cancel or amend the Enduring Power of Attorney at any time while they are mentally capable.    (ii) When a Donor is becoming or has become mentally incapable, the Attorney has a duty to register the EPA with the Court.  Under the registration process, notice parties, such as other family members, have 35 calendar days to object to the registration of the EPA from the date notice is given.  If a concern is raised about the actions of an Attorney, either during the registration process or at any time following registration, the matter will be referred to the Court for direction. The Court has the power to investigate the concerns and revoke the EPA if it is not satisfied that the Attorney is acting in the Donor’s best interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) why the cost of registering an Enduring Power of Attorney (EPA) in Northern Ireland is higher than the cost of registering a Lasting Power of Attorney in England and Wales; (ii) whether she has any plans to review or reduce this fee; and (iii) how she is ensuring the costs associated with creating and registering an EPA are not prohibitive for low-income individuals.
answer_text: (i) There are a number of legislative and administrative differences between Northern Ireland and England and Wales, consequently the processes for the completion and registration of an Enduring Power of Attorney (EPA) and a Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) are different.  Although an EPA only needs to be registered when the Donor loses capacity to manage their financial affairs the position is different for a LPA which needs to be registered once it is made. The current fee for registering an EPA is £180.  While the fee for an LPA is £82, separate applications must be made for a Finance LPA and a Health and Welfare LPA making the total cost £164.  The courts system in Northern Ireland operates  on the principle that those who use the civil or family courts should pay towards the cost of the service they use, if they can afford to do so. The policy on fee charging in Northern Ireland is set out in guidance published by the Department of Finance (DoF) and requires that fees should cover the full costs associated with providing the service including judicial, administrative and overhead costs.(ii) The ability to generate income from fees to fund civil and family courts is a key tool available to the Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunals Service (NICTS) in managing its budgetary pressures.  Court fees were increased by 9% in 2023 and 2024 to help manage the financial position following the significant rise in inflation during 2022 and 2023.  In light of the ongoing financial pressure on public sector budgets, it is important that court fees continue to keep pace with increased costs and, to minimise the cost to the taxpayer.  (iii) The NICTS Help with Court Fees policy is available to provide assistance to individuals who are in receipt of certain means-tested benefits or are experiencing financial hardship.  Under this policy, court fees can be waived or reduced for such individuals so ensuring that court users with limited financial means are not denied access to justice.  In the event that an application for an EPA is received from an individual on low income and they meet these conditions, they would be able to get help with the associated court fee.  An information leaflet, ‘Do I have to pay court fees?’, and the form for Exemption or Remission of a Court Fee can be found on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she has any plans to review the Enduring Powers of Attorney (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 to align with the broader mental capacity frameworks, such as the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016; and (ii) whether she has considered introducing lasting powers of attorney, similar to those in England and Wales, which allow for decisions about health and welfare as well as financial matters.
answer_text: While the Office of Care and Protection administers enduring powers of attorney, the substantive law contained in the Enduring Powers of Attorney (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 is the responsibility of the Department of Finance. Any review of the 1987 Order would be a matter for the Minister of Finance to consider in conjunction with relevant stakeholders. Lasting powers of attorney are provided for in Part 5 of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, which has yet to be commenced. The commencement power rests with the Department of Health, but commencement is dependent upon other provisions in that Act that are the responsibility of my department, in particular Part 7, which provides for the appointment of a Public Guardian. Officials in both departments are currently working on plans to identify the actions, resources and time required to allow for commencement of these and other provisions of the 2016 Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps he is taking to ensure the framework for Enduring Powers of Attorney aligns effectively with the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, particularly in cases where both financial and health decisions are required; and (ii) any discussions he has had with the Minister of Justice in relation to the introduction of health and welfare powers like those in the Lasting Powers of Attorney system in England and Wales.
answer_text: While the Office of Care and Protection administers Enduring Powers of Attorney, the substantive law contained in the Enduring Powers of Attorney (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 is the responsibility of the Department of Finance. Any review of the 1987 Order would be a matter for the Minister of Finance to consider in conjunction with relevant stakeholders. Lasting Powers of Attorney are provided for in Part 5 of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (MCA), which has yet to be commenced. While the commencement power rests with my Department, commencement is dependent upon other provisions in the MCA, which are the responsibility of the Department of Justice, in particular Part 7, which provides for the appointment of a Public Guardian. Officials in my Department are currently working collaboratively with officials in the Department of Justice, as part of a joint project team, to commence a programme of work to identify the actions, resources and time required to allow for commencement of these and the other remaining provisions of the MCA. The development of a costed implementation plan will help inform future decisions regarding future commencement of the MCA.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the recruitment of the Commissioner for Older People, including when an appointment is expected to be made.
answer_text: A public appointment competition for the role of Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland was launched in July 2024 and is well underway. An appointment decision will be announced in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the new digital process for the land registry system, including the new service provider.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the target response times that are in place for his Department when (i) considering whether or not a planning application should be called in; and (ii) processing and deciding a planning application that has been called in.
answer_text: Where a council is required to notify my Department in relation to a particular planning application, a council must not grant planning permission for the application before the expiry of a period of 28 days, from the date of notification.  My Department has the power to extend this 28-day timeframe and, in practice, this can occur where an application is complex and where more time is required to decide whether or not the application warrants referral to it for determination. My Department also considers third party ‘call in’ requests, and whilst there is no legislative timeframe for a response, my Department assesses these requests, as well as all notified applications, as quickly as possible to ensure there are no unnecessary delays to the planning process. The target for regionally significant applications is 30 weeks from validation to recommendation to the Minister. In terms of local and major applications, the statutory target is 15 and 30 weeks respectively. For called in applications, the date the application is called in by my Department is used instead of the date the application is deemed valid. Upon receipt of a called in application, my Department process these applications to a conclusion as expediently as possible.I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way and my Department is continuing to work closely with local government and partners to improve current processes and the performance of our planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he plans to fulfil his Department’s commitment of a clinical audit to measure the effectiveness of diabetes care.
answer_text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable in Northern Ireland. Subsequently, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications of this decision.  Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, are being considered. Given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered, as funding is required for devices as well as resources to upskill and recruit the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders. Diabetes affects nearly 112,000 people in NI, with the number growing. We do not have the proportion of those living with diabetes in Northern Ireland that are not receiving annual check-ups or tests relating to secondary illnesses. Services have been recovering post covid, with additional investment to reduce waiting lists. Delivery of care is provided by diabetes specialist multidisciplinary teams, with patients in higher risk groups prioritised for review first. However, waiting lists remain long, with limited specialist workforce.An integrated response is required for an increasing population presenting with multi-morbidities, utilising all existing assets across workforce, infrastructure, technology, finance and Community Voluntary Sector.Working with key stakeholders, a Regional Integrated Pathway for Diabetes has been defined over the last two years.  This Regional Pathway has been developed in line with the Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016).  It will standardise access and provide equity across Northern Ireland, through the integration of diabetes services, supporting treatment and care now and in the future.A Diabetes Programme of Care Workforce Review is near completion, led by the Department of Health Workforce Policy Directorate.
