This review included diabetes specialist nurses as well as other specialist health care professions directly involved in the delivery of diabetes treatment and care. This 10-year forward view will inform workforce planning needs to ensure equitable access to specialist diabetes professionals across Northern Ireland, in order to provide a safe, effective and quality service for people living with diabetes.                                                      The Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016) included an action to participate in National Diabetes Audit, similar to the Diabetes Audit used in England and Wales.The Department of Health remains committed to working towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.  However, there have been significant

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the proportion of those living with diabetes in Northern Ireland that are not receiving annual check-ups or tests relating to secondary illnesses such as heart failure, stroke, sight loss and loss of sensation in feet; and (ii) whether staffing levels in this area of care are sufficient.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to supply insulin pumps and hybrid closed loop systems as standard to people living with type 1 diabetes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on actions being undertaken by his Department to address the significant delays in the planning process, including within his own Department.
answer_text: It was worth noting,  over 98% of all planning applications are dealt with by Councils which are sovereign democratic authorities .I am committed to ensuring that the planning system in the North operates in an efficient and effective way. My Department, working in collaboration with local councils through the Planning Improvement Programme, has delivered important changes (including fee uplifts, reviews and consultations on policy and procedures, development of legislation and ongoing work on the statutory consultation process) all of which will continue to improve the system’s efficiency and effectiveness. My Department has also made a bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board to support the role of statutory consultees within my Department and to appoint persons to undertake ‘hear and report’ work that the Planning Appeals Commission has been unable to progress. I am hopeful that this bid will ultimately be successful and will assist in increasing the efficiency of the system. I want to build on the positive work that has been taken forward to date and my officials have commenced a series of face-to-face meetings with all planning authorities (including my own Department) which will focus on identifying challenges in current practice and procedure; and inform opportunities for support, shared learning and best practice, with the ultimate aim of improving performance.Planning is a collective issue and all stakeholders, including my Department, must play their part in delivering the necessary changes and improvements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the consultation for the Draft Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: Consultation on the draft Programme for Government closed on Monday 4th November. In total 1,375 responses have been received. Work is now ongoing to analyse all responses received and produce a consultation report for consideration by the Executive. Once this has been considered, we will finalise the Programme for Government and bring it to the Assembly for cross-community approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline reasons for delays in considering contentious planning applications.
answer_text: The member will be aware that local councils are responsible for processing over 99% of planning applications, this includes local and major planning applications. Planning can be controversial and complex and each planning application is assessed on a case-by-case basis, in accordance with Section 45 of the 2011 Act, regard is to be had to the local development plan so far as material to the application, and to any other material planning considerations including objections and concerns from third parties and statutory consultees; it is right and important issues raised by them are carefully reviewed and considered. Within my Department we encourage applicants to engage in Pre Application Discussions (PAD) and we use our legislation powers to expand the consultation and notification around Pre Application Community Consultation (PACC) procedures to ensure issues, which may be contentious, are identified in advance of applications, on appropriate sites, being submitted. Recommendations on applications are made as expeditiously as possible and through review of environmental information, applicants are advised of deficiencies at the earliest opportunity to avoid subsequent delays. There are a number of reasons why the processing time of an application can vary, and sometimes, it is linked to the quality of the submission. The recent changes to the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015, to introduce statutory validation checklists for councils, are designed to improve planning performance with better quality applications entering the system, resulting in shorter processing times, more efficient consultee responses and quicker planning decisions.  I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way. The Planning Improvement Programme is progressing, working closely with local government and partners, to improve current processes and the performance and delivery of the system. This wide-ranging improvement programme has already delivered important changes, including fee uplifts; consultations on policy and procedures and the development of legislation, all of which will continue to improve the overall efficiency and effectiveness of our planning system. I want to build on the positive work that has been taken forward to date and have asked my officials to arrange a series of meetings with Councils with a specific focus on improving performance; identifying challenges; recognising success and promoting key learning across the system. Planning is a collective issue, and all stakeholders must play their part in delivering the necessary changes and improvements, including local councils who have responsibility for a significant number of planning functions.Within my Department, steps have already been taken to increase staffing, mitigate and address performance of consultees. I am pleased during 2023/24 the proportion of statutory consultations responded to on-time (within 21 calendar days or

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail which debts which are given priority over child maintenance payments; and (ii) whether he has any plans to enhance the priority of child maintenance payments to ensure the welfare of the child.
answer_text: The debts that are prioritised before child maintenance deductions are taken include: housing costs, rent and/or service charges, gas and electricity arrears, rates arrears, water charges and fines or compensation orders.The Department for Work and Pensions has recently announced that child maintenance deductions will now be moved up the priority order before repayments of Universal Credit Advances.  This change is at a very early stage. My officials will continue to engage with the Department for Work and Pensions on the implementation timeline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what training exists for everyone involved within court processes, including lawyers and judges, to ensure that any attempts by abusers to manipulate legal systems are recognised and prevented.
answer_text: A range of training is available to staff across the criminal justice system to help them better understand the impact of domestic abuse and how best to meet the needs of victims.   An annual training statement, setting out information about the level of training undertaken in relation to domestic abuse is published annually and available on my Department’s website at; https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/training-statement-domestic-abuse-offence As you will be aware, the judiciary are independent, including with respect to training and I am bound to respect that independence.  The Lady Chief Justice has statutory responsibility for the provision of training for the independent judiciary.  However, my Department is committed to sharing training and awareness-raising resources that have been developed by and for our justice partners with the judiciary, where that is appropriate and helpful.Although my Department is not responsible for training of the legal profession, I understand that both the Law Society and the Bar have arranged training on domestic abuse and coercive control to assist their members in cases involving a victim of domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 has prevented misuse of legal processes since its commencement.
answer_text: The Domestic and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 (the Act) makes provision for a range of safeguards to mitigate against the risk of an abuser using the criminal justice process to further exert control and influence over their victim.  These provisions are intended to minimise the trauma for the victim while ensuring that the proper administration of justice is achieved.  Examples of these measures include:a provision under section 1 of the Act to prevent the accused from electing for trial by jury at Crown Court, in summary proceedings, in relation to the domestic abuse offence; section 24 of the Act prohibits an accused person from cross-examining a partner or connected person in person, where they are to give evidence. This provision applies in relation to the domestic abuse offence under section 1 of the Act, as well as to any other offence (for example, criminal damage, assault, grievous bodily harm, threats to damage property or threats to kill, etc.) where there is a specification that it is aggravated by reason of involving domestic abuse (under section 15 of the Act); and under section 23 (c) of the Act, complainants in cases involving the domestic abuse offence, or an aggravated offence, are also automatically eligible for consideration of special measures when giving evidence, which could include the use of live links, remote evidence centres or screens.  The Act also makes provision to enhance the protection available to victims of domestic abuse who are giving evidence in family proceedings; examples include:court rules, which came into operation in November 2022 and which make specific provision in relation to special measures.  Again, this could include giving evidence from behind a screen, via a remote evidence centre or by live link;   section 28 of the Act introduced a legal aid waiver allowing victims of domestic abuse access to justice when responding to children proceedings brought by their abusers, often employed as a means to further that abuse. Under this measure, if they can provide appropriate evidence of the abuse, and that the perpetrator is the one who is bringing the proceedings, this allows them to apply for legal aid without reference to their means. My Department has been working to ensure that the number of organisations who can provide this information is as wide as possible and is currently exploring options of how it supports victims of domestic abuse more effectively, including efforts to increase awareness and uptake of the waiver, as part of the foundational review of civil legal servicesMy Department intends to publish a report in early 2025 that will include information on the amount of cases that have proceeded though the court since the Act’s introduction. The report will also include the views of service providers who have assisted victims and witnesses during the proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered (i) a dedicated emergency response hotline; (ii) safe zones where women can find immediate help; (iii) a public awareness campaign and community engagement; (iv) an increase to funding and logistical support for local women’s groups and shelters to provide immediate assistance; and (v) other immediate responses, regarding the recent sexually motivated attacks in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area.
answer_text: I strongly condemn the recent attacks in the Derry City and Strabane District, women and girls have a right to live without fear or intimidation. In cases of immediate danger the advice is to call 999. The 24-hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) helpline is also available to connect victims with essential support services.Derry City & Strabane District Council’s Policing and Community Safety Partnership (PCSP) responded swiftly with a Special Council Meeting which has led to increased Community Safety Warden patrols, and attendance at the recent Women’s Safety rally, where personal alarms and other safety resources were distributed.Other relevant initiatives that the PCSP has supported include the provision of funding for Foyle Women’s Aid and support for the delivery healthy relationship education in local schools. The PCSP also supports vulnerable women through the provision of safety packs, security equipment referrals, and CCTV support for high-risk victims, in partnership with the PSNI. In addition, the PCSP also collaborates with Neighbourhood Watch to promote personal safety and provides items like wrist alarms.I am also aware that the PSNI, has increased visibility in the area through foot, bike, and drone patrols .  PSNI is also taking forward implementation of its “Tackling Violence Against Women and Girls” action plan, which I understand includes community outreach and “Safe Zone” considerations.Funding is currently available from my Department, both through Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) and through the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS) for a range of projects which aim to tackle domestic and sexual abuse. These projects provide a selection of crime prevention equipment, which includes personal alarms.  My department is also partnering with The Executive Office (TEO) and PSNI on the “Power to Change” campaign, which seeks to challenge societal acceptance of behaviours that contribute to gender-based violence, promoting zero tolerance toward actions that facilitate violence against women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has any plans to include domestic abuse in financial remedies through divorce proceedings to recognise the profound impact on victims.
answer_text: Plans for legislation to amend the substantive law of divorce and dissolution are at an early stage. A review of divorce law in other jurisdictions was recently completed and officials now plan to meet with key local stakeholders who can make a useful contribution to developing policy proposals. It is intended that this initial work will provide the basis for a full public consultation in 2025 leading to the consideration of future divorce legislation that best meets the needs of those who wish to end their marriage or civil partnership, including those in abusive relationships. While this work will focus on the substantive law, the wider process of divorce and dissolution, including the financial settlement, will be considered where directly relevant. The courts here have recently considered the division of assets in ancillary relief proceedings where a party to the marriage had been a victim of domestic abuse and made an award to reflect the physical and emotional effect on the victim. Officials will keep any further judicial developments in view.I am determined that, in keeping with the Programme for Government commitment to ending violence against women and girls, any reforms to current divorce legislation will be effected in a manner that takes account of victims of domestic violence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to the UK Government’s commitment to fund Growth Deals for Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council and Mid South West Region, to detail how she is supporting the projects to progress.
answer_text: The lifting of the pause on the two Growth Deals was very welcome news. I am delighted that all four Deals can now get on with developing and delivering the range of exciting and transformative projects across the North for the benefits of our citizens, businesses and visitors.City and Growth Deals are an Executive priority, and I have had the pleasure of meeting with all Deal representatives and visiting some of the project sites, ensuring that partners are supported in the projects. I have attended a series of different events and continue to positively influence and champion for all four Deals and their projects.I receive regular briefings on the initiative, with which I, in turn, share with Executive colleagues and the Finance committee to ensure timely updates are provided on progress and developments, as well as any relevant arising issues.Key decisions required of the Executive in relation to City and Growth Deals are brought by me through Executive papers and recommendations. These include funding decisions and relevant approvals.In addition, my Department has an oversight role for the City and Growth Deals initiative. The Department of Finance Permanent Secretary is the Senior Responsible Owner for NICS and there is a dedicated division with the Department to oversee the governance and funding. This team engages on a regular basis with Deals, Councils, Departments, the British Government and a range of other stakeholders to ensure effective collaboration and partnership, implementing consistent policies and approaches and help drive overall progress.My Department is also involved in the business case approvals process to ensure value for money. Construction and Procurement Delivery within my Department assists Accountable Departments involved in the City and Growth Deal projects to ensure value for money in public procurement. Several other Departments are directly involved in the initiative, being accountable for the funding to projects within their policy remit. City and Growth Deals is an excellent example of partnership and collaboration to ensure the many projects across the four Deals are successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how he is improving early detection of lung cancer; (ii) whether he plans to expand screening programs to facilitate earlier diagnosis and treatment; and (iii) how his Department is increasing public awareness of lung cancer symptoms, particularly among high-risk groups, to further support early diagnosis and improved outcomes.
answer_text: Improving early detection of lung cancer is about targeted screening of people at high risk who do not have symptoms, along with raising awareness of the symptoms of lung cancer and what to do if these occur.  Former Minister Swann approved the recommendation from the UK National Screening Committee to introduce a targeted lung cancer screening programme for those aged between 55-74 years old at high risk of disease.Work is now needed at a regional level to take forward the planning for a targeted lung screening programme (including additional diagnostic and treatment services; and the potential service model) and an assessment of the potential resource implications within the current and future budgetary constraints. As well as work to improve early diagnosis my department, along with the Public Health Agency (PHA), has taken forward work to prevent lung cancer. As part of an ongoing programme of communications on smoking the PHA regularly raises awareness of health risks such as lung cancer on PHA social channels, media activity, support materials and website For your health | Stop Smoking. The Tobacco and Vapes Bill was introduced to Parliament on 5th November and offers a once in a lifetime opportunity to address the extensive harms caused by smoking, which include lung cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the HM Treasury Autumn Budget 2024 on businesses in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The impact of the Budget will affect local businesses in a variety of ways, though the nature of that will depend upon the characteristics of each individual business, including its size, which sector it operates in and how it chooses to manage new arrangements around. There are welcome benefits for workers as a result of the increases in the National Living Wage and National Minimum Wage. However rather than offsetting this increase in pay with a decrease in taxes, the Budget increases employer National Insurance contributions. At a time of high costs on businesses this will put many businesses under more strain.My officials and I will continue to engage with local business stakeholders and consider the full detail of the announcements, as well as the possible implications for the North’s economy, in the context of my economic plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to introduce a scheme to repair roads, used by the public, which have fallen into disrepair and pose a threat to safety, where an owner cannot be ascertained and where closing the road would create a negative impact on the surrounding infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department has no responsibility for roads that have not been adopted into the public road network and any proposal to introduce a scheme to repair unadopted roads would require funding to be diverted from the repair of the adopted road network, for which my Department has legal responsibility. Additionally, this would lead to a permanent liability for all defects on those roads my Department repaired but are not part of the adopted network.There are processes whereby my Department can adopt a private road into the public road network, however they require the road to be brought to an adoptable standard prior to the adoption with costs borne by those wishing to have the road adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to align business rates relief with the changes announced for GB business rates in the HM Treasury Autumn Budget 2024, to offset the National Insurance increase for employers.
answer_text: There is no policy linkage between those two measures. National Insurance contribution increases are a reserved matter and business rates are a devolved matter. One is not intended to offset fluctuations in the other.The reduction in Retail, Hospitality and Leisure support in England from 75% to 40%, meaning the cost of support is lower in 2025-26. The Executive has received a reduction of £58.2 million in 2025-26 in relation business rates. The reduction announced by the Chancellor, confirms the affordability issues that I have consistently alluded to with that business rates scheme. Those affordability issues were exacerbated greatly when implanted into our local taxbase and economy, heavily comprised as it is of SMEs. My department will be assessing the details and implications of the policy announcements in the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget Statement and the Transforming business rates paper published by Treasury following that Statement. Further work will then be progressed in this area once the Executive finishes considering my proposals in relation to the strategic short, medium and long-term steps for the rating system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on plans to support childcare for school-age children.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he is aware of any issues in the recruitment of further education college lecturers, particularly in courses related to skills shortages.
answer_text: I am aware of issues in lecturer recruitment and following engagement by my officials with the Further Education Colleges, the Colleges have confirmed difficulties recruiting lecturers in Engineering, Electrical Installation and Computing. Whilst colleges continue to actively advertise, my Department is also working with the colleges on flexing lecturer qualification criteria, where appropriate to alleviate recruitment challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the impact on further and higher education in relation to the Independent Review of Education; and (ii) whether any regional colleges and universities have raised concerns in relation to the Minister for Education's statement and the impact to its course numbers.
answer_text: Whilst a full assessment will be required, I expect that several of the recommendations of the Independent Review of Education have the potential to have an impact on further education colleges and higher education institutions, not least the recommendation to raise the age of educational participation from 16 to 18 years.  My officials have advised in conducting initial discussions with further education college Principals in this regard, Principals have expressed a view of the opportunities this provides for the great utility of colleges and the provision of high quality technical and vocational educational pathways.  Concerns have not been raised by universities in relation to the statement made by the Minister of Education.I look forward to the Minister of Education bringing forward a paper to the Executive and wider discussions be convened on the Independent Review of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has consulted with further and higher education institutions in relation to his decision to make education compulsory to 18 years old.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy (DfE) is responsible for further and higher education. My officials engaged with DfE in advance of my announcement and there will be further discussion on this proposal between departments and key stakeholders as the detail is developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he had with the Minister of Education before the Minister’s Statement on 22 October 2024 on the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: I have had one meeting with the Minister for Education in April this year, where we discussed the importance of both our departments working together to ensure that the learner is put at the centre, especially in relation to the Careers service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether he has had any discussions with the Minister of Health in relation to the transition of those with special educational needs, post-18 years old; and (ii) the setting in which they can continue to be supported in education and skills.
answer_text: (i) A ‘Transitions Working Group’, jointly chaired by officials from my department and the Department of Health (DoH), has been established and is supported by representatives from the Education Authority (EA), the Public Health Agency (PHA) and officials from the Departments for the Communities (DfC) and the Economy (DfE). The aim of the working group is to evaluate current transitions processes and practices, with the aim of identifying improvements to create better ways of working and sharing of information to ensure that there are clear and appropriate pathways to provisions for young people with Special Educational Needs (SEN) or disabilities across further and higher education, training and employment.I will continue to work closely with the Minister of Health and other Executive colleagues as this work progresses. (ii) Currently young people with statements of SEN have access to a number of settings in which they can continue to be supported in education and skills, and this will be agreed as part of their transitions plan. Options will include the EA continuing to maintain a statement of SEN, where there has been one in place, naming a school; progression to a further or higher education setting; a place within a training programme that focuses on fostering independence, social inclusion or skill-building, or directly into employment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their plans to develop a strategy for death, dying and bereavement.
answer_text: Issues of death, dying and bereavement extend far beyond the remit of the Department of Health and would require a cross-Departmental approach. I am aware of calls for death, dying and bereavement to be included in the Programme for Government, which has recently been subject to consultation.  Any future strategy should be considered within the context of the outcome of the consultation and the new Programme for Government.However, I am very aware of the importance of palliative and end of life care in supporting people with palliative care needs to live well and achieve the best quality of life as their illness progresses.  I also recognise the importance of information and support for those who are experiencing grief and bereavement. My Department is committed to improved palliative care and bereavement support. The regional Palliative Care in Partnership Programme, co-led by my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency, brings together a wide range of stakeholders across the statutory, independent and voluntary and community sectors, including hospices, as well as service users, to support the development and implementation of services for adults who are living with palliative and end of life care needs and those important to them. The Partnership’s workplan priorities for 2023-26 focus on 4 priority areas:early identification of and timely intervention for people with palliative care needs; co-ordination & quality improvement in palliative care services; palliative care education and training; and a public health approach to palliative care. These priorities and associated workstreams will inform and support the Palliative Care in Partnership Programme and its member organisations in taking forward a range of actions to further improve palliative and end of life care for adults. In terms of palliative care for children, my Department’s Strategy for Children’s Palliative and End-of-Life Care, published in 2016, sets out 23 objectives across the spectrum of paediatric palliative care from diagnosis through to bereavement care.  The Regional Paediatric Palliative Care Network (RPPCN) identifies the key priorities for delivery in line with the Strategy and puts in place measures to deliver these.  It also acts as a vehicle for the region to identify areas of best practice and share clinical opinion on specific cases.The network also ensures there are close working relations across all Trusts and the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice and offers clinical leadership on the strategic delivery of the key objectives within the Strategy.  Key areas taken forward to date include the development of ante-natal care pathways, rapid discharge plans, advanced care plans and agreed pathways regarding pain relief.  The RPPCN will continue to review and deliver improvements to paediatric palliative care in NI.In terms of bereavement, the Northern Ireland Bereavement Network, which brings together all relevant cross-

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to his oral statement on Maternity and Neonatal Services on 22 October 2024, to provide further detail relating to post-natal support, including post-natal mental health.
answer_text: Initiatives such as the Continuity of Midwifery Carer launched in May 2023 have shown that women who receive continuous care from a midwife have lower intervention rates and better birth experiences. To ensure the full-scale implementation of Continuity of Midwifery Carer, a four phased outcomes-based accountability approach has been created, after initial roll out it is clear that additional funding and resource will be required to ensure this model of care is widely available.All Trusts in Northern Ireland offer a midwifery service to women who chose to birth at home.  Teams based in the community will support a woman through her pregnancy, providing antenatal care and then going on call when a woman reaches 37 weeks gestation to care for her when she is in labour and birthing. These teams follow care through into the postnatal period.In 2021, my predecessor, Minister Swann approved funding to establish a new perinatal mental health service for the region. The first phase in developing this new service was to implement community perinatal mental health teams in all Trusts throughout the region, which would provide specialist services for mothers experiencing mental health issues during the perinatal period. Phase one has now been implemented – each Trust now has a fully operational community perinatal mental health service. The second phase was to consider a Mother and Baby Unit (MBU) for the region. In early 2023 my Department commissioned an independent review to make recommendations and provide advice on a suitable model and location for a Mother and Baby Unit, which would best meet the needs of the population.   The Department received the final report from the review team in mid-September 2023.  It provided recommendations on the preferred service model for the unit, and the location of the Mother and Baby Unit, with the Belfast City Hospital site identified as the most suitable location. At that point, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was invited to prepare a business case for the provision of the proposed regional unit.The business case for Mother and Baby Unit is well-progressed and it is planned that it will be finalised and submitted to my Department for scrutiny by the end of the year.   It should be noted that progression of this project will remain subject to the necessary funding for the unit being identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for a further update on the agreed amalgamation of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College; and (ii) to confirm the site for the new school.
answer_text: The Education Authority is continuing to work towards achieving a day one solution to enable the new Causeway Academy to operate on a single site from 1 September 2026. This is likely to involve refurbishment and additional accommodation on an existing site, either one of the three existing school sites or the former Coleraine High School site at Lodge Road. The feasibility study for September 2026 is well underway and is considering a number of options to enable the school to open on a single site. The subsequent business case will identify the preferred option, and the investment required to implement it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she is aware of any concerns in relation to legal representation of victims in court proceedings that involve coercive control; and (ii) how her Department is working with the Law Society to ensure expertise in this area.
answer_text: While concerns relating to legal representation of victims of domestic abuse have occasionally been raised with me, training and professional development is a matter for the profession. I understand that both the Law Society and the Bar have offered training on domestic abuse and coercive control to equip their members in cases involving a victim of domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will engage with the Equality Commission when drafting Department Bills, as per their statutory responsibilities.
answer_text: Executive Office officials engage with a range of stakeholders, including the Equality Commission, when developing policy underpinning Departmental Bills, in line with best practice and statutory requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how she intends to protect victims of domestic abuse against financial coercion during the no fault divorce process in her proposed legislation.
answer_text: Officials have been reviewing divorce legislation in other jurisdictions and plan to meet over the coming months with key stakeholders who can make a useful contribution to developing policy proposals. It is intended that this initial work will provide the basis for a full public consultation in 2025. This will inform divorce legislation that best meets the needs of those who, for whatever reason and in whatever circumstances, including those highlighted by the member, wish to end their marriage or civil partnership.  In particular, officials have noted reforms elsewhere that enable those in coercive or abusive relationships a reasonably prompt exit without living through years of formal separation in which further financial coercion may occur, and those issues, along with others, will be carefully considered during the policy development process.I am determined that, in keeping with the Programme for Government commitment to ending violence against women and girls, any reforms to current divorce legislation will be effected in a manner that takes account of victims of domestic violence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the increase in badger population in Northern Ireland in the last 20 years; (ii) how this compares with other regions of the UK and Ireland; (iii) how this increase has impacted on other wildlife populations, including hedgehog and bumble bee populations; and (iv) the proportion of the badger population infected with tuberculosis in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i) The last survey of badgers in Northern Ireland was in 2007/8, estimating a total population of 34,100 (7,600 social groups) with no significant change in the badger population from that recorded during the previous survey in 1990-1993. There has been no more recent survey.(ii) There is no directly comparable data available to address this part of the member’s question. (iii) Badgers are known to feed on both hedgehogs and bumblebees, but the impact on those populations is unclear. Factors such as increased use of pesticides, road mortality and loss of suitable habitat are more likely to impact bumblebee and hedgehog populations at a national level.(iv) The proportion of the badger population infected with TB is estimated from the proportion of infected badgers recovered as Road Traffic casualties and has varied from year to year. Over the past ten years, this has ranged from 14.1% in 2015 to 22.9% in 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 14277/22-27, to detail (i) the wildlife interventions he is considering in the Bovine TB Eradication Strategy; and (ii) whether he intends to legislate for a badger cull during the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: In October 2023, a judicial review quashed the former Ministers decision on a wildlife intervention approach as part of the Northern Ireland bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy.  My officials are currently working on possible options for the way forward for my consideration.The current review of bTB being undertaken by the Chief Veterinary Officer will also consider the role played by badgers in the spread and endurance of this disease.  Any wildlife policy proposals will be subject to a full public consultation while any decision to implement a wildlife intervention is also likely to require legislation to be made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail any concerns raised with him by parents and schools in relation to education provision for those over 18 years old with special educational needs; and (ii) for his assessment of enabling special schools as a setting for education and training for those up to 25 years old.
answer_text: The issue of both post-16 and post-19 transitions for young people with statement of special educational needs has been a focus of a specific workstream under the End-to-End Review of SEN. As my officials have progressed this work, I have been made aware, by both young people, their families and school leaders, of the concerns and challenges in regard to ‘leaving school’ and the lack of appropriate supportive pathways, Legislative responsibility to provide for young people beyond the age of 19 falls outside the direct remit of the Department of Education (DE).  This is set out within The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, Part II, Article 3, which states that for the purposes of the provision of special education a ‘child’ is defined as ‘a person who attains the age of 19 years at any time during a school year at any school shall be deemed not to have attained that age until the day after the end of that school year.’Within the Special Educational Needs/Disability (SEND) Act 2016 there are provisions which specify that both the Education Authority (EA) and Health and Social Services Authority must ‘co-operate in the preparation of a transition plan’ for pupils with a statement of SEN and this will set out any arrangements appropriate to help facilitate a satisfactory transition from childhood to adulthood.The scope of a transition plan also falls across the remit of the Department for Communities (DfC) and the Department for the Economy (DfE) and my officials are working with colleagues across these departments to inform and shape any required legislation and policy development in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) current; and (ii) projected annual cost to (a) the public purse; and (b) farmers by not implementing a Bovine TB Eradication Strategy as soon as possible.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is one of the most challenging issues faced by cattle farmers in Northern Ireland (NI). Herd incidence is currently at 10.15% and in the last financial year, the NI bTB programme cost to government was just under £55.7m, with £36.5m of this paid as compensation for the removal of bovines.  Current estimates for this year indicate the cost of the programme could rise to around £60m. In 2021, a business case supporting development of the NI bTB Eradication Strategy estimated farmer costs, resulting from a bTB breakdown in Northern Ireland, to be just over £14m in total per annum.  However, it is acknowledged that rises in bTB incidence will have also resulted in increasing costs to farmers and farm businesses. The costs of controlling this disease are therefore not sustainable for either the public purse or for hard working farm businesses.  I have asked my Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) to undertake a review of TB policy and the TB programme, including the actions contained in the TB Eradication Strategy.  The CVO has engaged with a wide range of stakeholders during this review, and I am looking forward to considering his recommendations shortly.  The review will include a new delivery plan, will seek to address the current high levels of bovine TB incidence in Northern Ireland, the high costs to government and industry, and will clearly set out our priorities over the time ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how he is addressing digital exclusion among older people, ensuring they are not left behind as services become more reliant on digital platforms; and (ii) for his assessment of the provision of non-digital alternatives.
answer_text: DIGITAL INCLUSIONMy Department has a small Digital Inclusion Unit to promote online services and to give people the skills, confidence, safety, motivation and trust to engage with the online world. The aim is to promote a digitally inclusive society and to increase the use of online channels to access our public services through a range of projects by working in close partnership with several public and private organisations to help digitally excluded people get online. The Digital Inclusion team’s current Go ON NI programme enables the public to use Government services online and it continues to grow by targeting events and promotional activities at the harder to reach digitally excluded sections of the population here.These key groups include: •	those aged over 50•	rural communities•	people with physical or mental impairment•	socio-economic disadvantaged communities•	long-term unemployedThe Go ON NI programme ensures older communities are not excluded from accessing information and services by working with various organisations to recruit digital champions, provide tuition, and detail the help that is available to assist these communities in accessing online services. October is Positive Ageing Month, a month-long festival of events and activities for older people, celebrating and promoting the well-being, contributions and experience of older people. During this month the Go ON NI Programme have been attending events promoting this programme and offering free digital skills sessions specifically for older people, in local libraries, community centres and hubs. The Go ON NI programme runs all year round for people wanting to improve their digital skills. Between April and September this year, the Go ON NI programme has delivered 448 sessions to 1,810 people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on work to develop a loneliness strategy; and (ii) to detail any discussion he has had with Executive colleagues towards this aim.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer on this topic in AQW 6028/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to ensure that older people have equal access to healthcare services and are not subject to ageism in treatment and delays in diagnosis.
answer_text: My Department has a number of measures in place to ensure older people have equal access to healthcare and are protected from ageism. Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 mandates the Department to consider the promotion of equality of opportunity across several categories, including age. This is enacted through the Department's Equality Scheme and Equality Action Plan.  The Department conducts screenings and equality impact assessments to assess the potential impact of policies on equality promotion, in accordance with guidance from the Equality Commission, and publishes an annual progress report on its equality objectives.In terms of GP services for older people, GPs, as independent contractors and clinical professionals, are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of individual patients based on their assessment of patient needs. This includes clinical assessment for the risk of serious illness and referral for diagnostic tests and specialist services as clinically appropriate.GP practices are required to address patient needs within the framework of equality, disability, and human rights legislation. This might include availing of sign language interpreting services, installing premises improvements and adjustments, and working flexibly to consider patients’ circumstances, such as their level of family support and capabilities regarding technology. Practices also have a responsibility to ensure that patients have clear information on how to access their services, making reasonable adjustments for specific groups when necessary.The Department’s GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, is overseeing work to develop guidance for practices on managing patient demand and optimising workflow to maximise efficiency and improve experiences for patients. The Group is also considering how technology can help to improve the process for accessing services for both patients and practices.In terms of nursing and residential care, the Enhancing Clinical Care Framework (ECCF), launched on 7 August 2023, aims to ensure that people who live in nursing and residential care homes are supported to lead the best life possible, underpinned by the right to equitable access to healthcare provision. The Framework describes the principles and practices of what people living in care homes need to access for optimal clinical care and support to stay healthy and well. To align with these principles, the existing Care Home Standards are being reviewed to reflect current best practice and ensure compliance with the ECCF. The Public Health Agency (PHA) has also established an Ageing Well Public Health Planning Team aimed at enhancing the health and well-being of older individuals and reducing health disparities. Key initiatives include the Age-Friendly Initiative, coordinated by councils, which addresses various domains affecting older people's wellbeing and participation; Intergeneratio

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress towards implementing and resourcing age-friendly initiatives to ensure that older people are supported in their communities.
answer_text: My Officials have engaged with older people and their representative organisations to consider the potential content of any future Active Ageing Strategy.  I will be engaging with Executive colleagues to ensure that collectively we agree the best approach to prioritising and addressing the needs of older people in our society.Through the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme, funding is awarded to organisations to deliver support for older people in Belfast and the North & West region, enabling them to remain in their own homes and communities, as well as supporting social inclusion, health and wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on improved and timely access to social care services for older people, including domiciliary care packages that fully satisfy their assessed requirements.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to review the process for accessing Sure Start services.
answer_text: A recent independent review of Sure Start determined that the current arrangements for targeting support remain the most appropriate method to reach children facing disadvantage. I recently brought 22 new areas into Sure Start which means that around 48% of children aged 0-3 (and their families) now have access to Sure Start services.  Further expansion will be considered within the Executive’s Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and will be dependent on additional funding being made available by the Executive and the capacity of the early years workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any conversations he has had to enable the Housing Executive to borrow against their rents to raise income for building new houses.
answer_text: I am actively pressing for a solution that will put NIHE on a sustainable footing to increase routine & planned investment, to deal with maintenance backlogs and a program of retrofit to improve energy efficiency in homes. To that end, I have had numerous conversations on this matter.My Department is currently investigating how NIHE might be able access Government Loans, without reclassification or significant restructuring. Access to these loans would put NIHE on a more equal footing with the Registered Housing Associations, in terms of providing value for money in constructing new social housing units.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the levels of suicide in young males; and (ii) to detail the action he is taking to address this.
answer_text: Every death by suicide is a tragedy. In every year since 2001, more than 70% of suicide deaths have been male. In 2022, suicide deaths were highest for men between the ages of 40 and 44. Rates have also been high in recent years for males aged between 25-29. Suicide remains the leading cause of death in males under 50.There are a wide variety of suicide prevention services available to men and this would include Lifeline, Self Harm Intervention Programme, Flourish! Churches programme, training, Psychological First Aid and Stress Control classes, and The Rainbow Project which provides support for the mental and emotional well-being of lesbian, gay and bisexual people in Northern Ireland.Health and Social Care Trusts also provide MANifest men’s mental health training across the region. A wide range of programmes are also funded on a short term basis in local areas that have a focus on men including resilience building, mental health promotion through sport, men's shed, allotment and horticulture programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to the Committee for Justice seeking an extension to the Committee Stage of the Justice Bill, when she expects to introduce the Sentencing Bill.
answer_text: The Sentencing Bill is intended to be the second DoJ Bill in this mandate. I have been planning on the basis that a Sentencing Bill would be introduced later in 2025, and this remains my intention, though how quickly this Bill is able to progress following its introduction will be a matter for the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work from Early Years through to post-primary school age to ensure a culture of healthy relationships.
answer_text: Learning about developing relationships begins as early as the Sure Start Programmes and develops througout a young person’s education.Personal, Social and Emotional Development is one of six pre-school Areas of Learning. It covers children’s emotional well-being, understanding of who they are, and respect for others and their environment. It is also about forming and sustaining relationships, beginning to understand emotions, learning how to self-regulate, and developing positive dispositions to learn. Good personal, social and emotional development gives children the best opportunity for success in other Areas of Learning.  As they begin their pre-school education, children bring with them a variety of personal and social skills, values and attitudes. They acquire experiences and relationships within the home and their immediate surroundings, and it is important that these are recognised.Our curriculum is designed to provide opportunities to educate young people to develop healthy relationships.  Pupils have the opportunity to explore their management of a range of feelings and emotions and those of others; initiating, developing and sustaining mutually satisfying relationships, explore the qualities of a loving, respectful relationship and develop coping strategies to deal with challenging relationship scenarios.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children currently awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion surgery; (ii) how long is the current waiting list; (iii) the actions he has taken to reduce this waiting list; (iv) whether he has engaged private healthcare providers to reduce the waiting list; and (v) his assessment of the quality of life for children on the waiting list.
answer_text: On the 24th October 2024, 74 children were on the waiting list for this elective procedure.  However, this number changes regularly with some children receiving their surgery and other children being added to the waiting list for the procedure. The longest waiting patient is waiting from early May 2022 this however is excluding a number of children who have been medically or socially deferred. As stated previously my officials have been working with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) to identify measures in order to try and address this ongoing issue.  The Belfast Trust has commenced additional clinics and theatre sessions to help reduce paediatric waiting times and additional paediatric gastrostomy procedures have been approved through waiting list initiative funding.  The Trust has now scheduled additional weekend theatre sessions dates between October and December to help address the backlog.  A paediatric surgeon has now completed a validation of Peg Tube waiting lists to identify which children would be clinically suitable to transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  The clinical criteria for transfer has been agreed by the specialist team to determine this. BHSCT is currently in negotiations with Alder Hey Hospital to identify what capacity they may have to offer. However, like the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC), they are currently also experiencing unscheduled seasonal pressures. The capacity required will include pre-assessment of these complex children, theatre capacity, inpatient bed capacity (children stay between 48-72 hours), parental training to manage the peg tube, equipment and food, and a follow up appointment. Further discussions are also underway with another District General Hospital (DGH) to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon with a surgical theatre list.  Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in the DGH to help facilitate this. A number of children have been identified to be managed on these theatre lists.A further review of surgical work performed in RBHSC is underway and any work that can be redirected to DGHs will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.The Trust are also examining partnership working with the Private Sector however there are a number of areas that the Trust would need to have assurance on for the delivery of safe and effective care of these complex children many of which have other co-morbidities.I would like to acknowledge the frustration and anxiety felt by many families and young people who are waiting for procedures, treatment or surgery. Our waiting times are longer than anyone would wish them to be for this surgery and I am truly sorry. My officials at the Department of Health, the paediatric team within RBHSC and I share the families’ frustrations but I would wish

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting time for dental surgery which requires general anaesthetic; and (ii) the steps he is taking to reduce waiting lists for dental treatments which require general anaesthetic.
answer_text: (i) Children requiring dental extractions, particularly those below 10 years of age, will often not cooperate sufficiently to allow the teeth to be removed under local anaesthetic alone. General Dental Practitioners refer patients of this type to the Community Dental Services (CDS) of the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts.The CDS specialises in providing routine dental care to patients who, for various reasons are not suitable for treatment in the primary care setting. In 2023-24, the CDS saw over 3,000 children for extractions under general anaesthetic.  To ensure that the most urgent cases are treated as quickly as possible, CDS teams within HSC Trusts across Northern Ireland operate a strict triage and prioritisation process which is based on clinical need.Each HSC Trust CDS team has provided responses as detailed below for the current average wait from the time of initial referral, indicating the clinical need to be urgent, to the treatment appointment (referral to treat time).Southern Health and Social Care Trust: < 1 month.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust: 1-2 months.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust: > 1 year.Northern Health and Social Care Trust: 6-12 months.Western Health and Social Care Trust: 1-2 months.It should be noted that the average waiting times for acute patients would be less than those stated for urgent as they are given a higher priority grading; those with a routine grading would have a longer waiting time.(ii) The CDS waiting lists for dental treatment under general anaesthetic were severely impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and associated infection prevention and control restrictions. Across all HSC Trusts, CDS access to dental theatre lists have yet not returned to pre-pandemic levels.Throughout the later stages of the pandemic some waiting list initiatives were undertaken by some of the HSC CDS Trusts. These included additional lists (including weekends), as well as a successful cross border initiative in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust for children requiring timely extraction of permanent teeth. Attempts were also made to maximise the use of alternatives to general anaesthetic. These included the use of inhalation sedation (twilight sleep) and advanced sedation techniques (Penthrox). The advantage of these approaches is that they can be administered in a dental surgery thereby overcoming the issue of lack of theatre availability.Another method that has been successfully utilised by some Trusts is to have a general anaesthetic team and patient list on standby to take advantage of any free theatre space that becomes available at short notice due to list cancellation by another specialty.The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have also successfully changed their model of delivery of paediatric theatre services recently.  They have invested/ trained a Paediatric Perioperative Nurse for the Paediatric theatres in Daisy Hill Hospital as well as undertaking international recruitment to increa

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why those studying full time in further and higher education are unable to apply for carers allowance.
answer_text: The primary purpose of Carer’s Allowance is to provide a measure of financial support for people who give up the opportunity of full-time employment in order to provide regular and substantial care, of at least 35 hours per week, to a severely disabled person who is in receipt of certain disability benefits.It is a general principle that people in full-time education should be supported by the educational maintenance system of loans and grants, rather than the social security system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how statutory consultees under his Department consider the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 when considering planning applications.
answer_text: The Department provides advice about potential environmental impacts arising from planning consultations received, such as impacts on terrestrial and marine habitats and species and water quality. In relation to the Climate Act this may include advice about nature-based solutions accompanying an application or advice about proposals that may impact on peatlands, in particular active peatland which is important for its carbon capture and storage properties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why the annual rise, in line with the Consumer Price Inflation, for the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service Pension Scheme is not applied at the beginning of the fiscal year; (ii) whether this rise will be backdated to April each year; and (iii) whether there are problems accessing personal pension information via the online portal.
answer_text: Historically the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) have processed the pension increase in July or August of each year, which included any arrears owed since the April increase was confirmed.  This was due to resourcing issues, as the pension increase was calculated and processed manually outside of a pension software system. NIFRS has been undergoing a period of significant change with the pension’s administration and undertook a project to outsource to HSC Pensions’ Service.  The final stages of this project involved the transfer of the pension payroll to ensure that they would have a single, automated, end-to-end pension administration with improved resilience and support for their Firefighters and Pensioners.  This will reduce the risk of human error from manual calculations of increases and arrears and provide a more efficient service for the member in the longer term.  The pensioner payroll migrated across in February 2024.There was a need to complete additional data cleansing and reconciliation as well as testing of the software application to ensure the pension increase was accurately implemented.  Unfortunately there had been some technical issues with the software that delayed the project and NIFRS have worked with the software supplier to resolve these issues and administer the increase. These issues have now been resolved, and the pension increase together with arrears, for the vast majority of the members, were paid on 1st October 2024.  Due to the complexity of some members’ cases, it is envisaged that the remaining cases will be paid by the end of October.The annual pension increase occurs in April of each year.  As the new system has now been implemented it is NIFRS aim to process the pension increase, including arrears backdated to April, much earlier than they have been doing historically.As part of the pension’s administration project NIFRS and the HSC Pensions Service have been preparing to launch a Member Self Service Portal.  This has taken longer than initially anticipated due to technical issues with system development, allowing time for stringent testing of the portal, and the development of member videos on how to navigate registration.Communications had been issued to scheme members advising them of the upcoming service and asking them to register their interest to facilitate the implementation of the new service prior to Go Live. HSC Pension Service have advised that to facilitate a soft launch of the self-service portal the service went live on 1st October.  Any attempts by members to access the service prior to Go Live, will have failed.  Further communications to members will be issued week commencing 21st October, officially notifying them that the service is now Live and they can access their pension information via the portal.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans to include measures to address drink-spiking in the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
answer_text: One of the key objectives of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls is for women and girls to be safe and feel safe everywhere.  The Delivery Plan 2024-2026 sets out a programme of work with individual sectors to consider what action is required to address VAWG within that sector.A Safer Socialising Steering Group, which contains representatives from a range of departments and organisations including Hospitality Ulster, Translink and the PSNI, has been established to develop an action plan to address VAWG issues in the context of socialising. Spiking has been identified as a future agenda item for discussion by this Group. The PSNI Action Plan, Tackling Violence Against Women and Girls, also contains actions in relation to spiking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he directs health and social care professionals to collaborate with local voluntary sector providers, such as hospices, to address the holistic needs of patients, including their financial and emotional well-being.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of holistic palliative and end of life care for people with life-limiting conditions and for those who are important to them.  Holistic palliative care should be, and is, provided in response to individual needs taking account of clinical, psychological, emotional, spiritual and social circumstances. In line with recommendations of the Living Matters, Dying Matters Strategy, when a person is identified as having palliative and end of life care needs they will be allocated a palliative care keyworker, typically the district nurse, who will undertake a holistic assessment using tools such as the Northern Ireland Single Assessment Tool (NISAT).  NISAT is designed to capture information required for holistic person-centred assessment to enable the assessor to identify what support, care or treatment the person may require and where necessary to trigger referral to specialised services. When assessments identify individuals who have more complex or unresolved needs, or symptoms which cannot be resolved by their generalist services, they would be referred to a relevant specialist palliative care service, including those offered by hospices. When financial concerns or pressures are highlighted the individual may also be signposted to other services including voluntary partners such as local advice agencies, Macmillan Benefits Service or the Marie Curie Support Line who have advisers trained to offer tailored benefits and/or debt advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to provide financial assistance for transport for carers and family members visiting loved ones with life-limiting illnesses in inpatient care.
answer_text: Free or discounted travel is available through the Concessionary Fares Scheme on Translink services to those in possession of a SmartPass.  Financial assistance is also available in the form of the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme. The scheme is available to those members of Rural Community Transport Partnership’s who use the rural Dial-a-Lift service.  The Assisted Rural Travel Scheme allows any passenger with a current SmartPass to travel free or half fare on Dial-a-Lift.  This may include trips undertaken to visit someone in hospital within the Partnership’s operational area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that health and social care professionals regularly ask patients with life-limiting conditions about their financial concerns, especially at key stages of their care.
answer_text: Across the region Trusts use a number of methods to ask patients with life-limiting conditions about their financial concerns. As part of the Department of Health’s Case Management (2010) Guidance and using the Northern Ireland Single Assessment Tool (NISAT), an individuals’ finances are discussed on an annual basis (or more regularly as required) with all individuals. This includes those with life limiting conditions, in receipt of secondary care and known to a case manager.  The NISAT incorporates a section on finances and this is covered within the initial assessment and at follow-up reviews as required.  Finances are also discussed with individuals when required, specifically outside of assessment or planned reviews.Trusts also have established long-standing partnerships with Macmillan Cancer Support in relation to their Benefits Advice Service, which referrals can be made to. The Macmillan Benefits service can advise on benefits and grants including the Macmillan grant and others.  This service is offered via phone but a request can be made for face to face appointment. Alternatively, patients can contact the Macmillan Regional Benefits line.  The Macmillan information and support service can advise on other forms of grant and financial support and will refer back to the Benefits Team where required.Social Care professionals regularly ask patients with life-limiting conditions about their financial concerns, especially at key stages of their care; professionals are directed to do this via their assessment and review tools.  Professionals often signpost patients to community organisations for support with their benefit applications to ensure they are getting their full benefit entitlement.  In addition, social care professional staff will undertake a financial assessment with patients should they require placement in a care home environment.  Consideration is also given to individuals’ financial capacity to manage their finances and legal orders are implemented to safeguard finances, when necessary. A Holistic Needs Assessment (HNA) is offered to cancer patients by their Cancer Nurse Specialist (CNS). Using the HNA, the individual can share their holistic concerns including practical, emotional, physical, spiritual or financial concerns so that the CNS can address these needs through a bespoke personalised care plan including onward referral and support. The HNA is offered at various stages along the journey as required. At diagnosis stage each patient receives a core pack of various key support services and often most cancer patients are referred to the Macmillan Benefits at this stage by their CNS to provide access to information and support at the beginning of their journey.Clinical Health Psychology Services provide input to individuals with a number of potentially life limiting conditions, including those with diagnosis of cancer, renal, and respiratory conditions.  A biopsychosocial initial assessment is conducted which encompasses all

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department and the Health and Social Care Trusts are accounting for travel to and from charitable hospices in future patient transport planning, to ensure equitable access to palliative care services.
answer_text: The provision of non-emergency transport to facilitate individuals to access health and social care services is determined by a medical practitioner who carries out an assessment of an individual’s transport needs, as set out in Chapter Three of “The Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland”.  The published Strategy is accessible at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/transport-strategy-health-and-social-care-services-northern-irelandPatients travelling to or from hospices are not treated any differently to those patients travelling from their own homes or from a hospital setting.The current transport strategy dates back to 2007 and it is my intention to initiate a review of the policy this financial year, subject to available resources. As part of that review, the needs of all service users will be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any actions he is taking to improve access to social security advice for people with life-limiting conditions and their carers; and (ii) whether he has considered providing additional funding to hospices and other voluntary sector providers to expand their capacity to offer financial and social support services.
answer_text: My department’s Make the Call Service provides free and confidential needs assessments for individuals to check for entitlement to social security benefits, as well as additional supports and services. Make the Call has a team of community outreach officers, who conduct home visits with vulnerable people through age, disability, illness, or adverse circumstances to provide face to face assistance and support.  For those nearing the end of life because of illness, there is provision for benefits to be paid without the need for an assessment and claims are fast-tracked for payment. To ensure that customers with life limiting conditions can access the disability benefits to which they are entitled, the Department works closely with voluntary and community stakeholders.  This includes engagement with the Disability Consultative Forum and MacMillan Cancer Support.My department does not have any funding relationships in place with Hospices.In terms of other community provision my department provides significant annual funding in the region of £6.6m to the independent advice and debt sector to provide local and regional advice on a range of issues, including advice on benefit entitlements and debt support.  My department also provides direct support through the Neighbourhood Renewal programme to community-based projects working in the most deprived areas in Northern Ireland and delivering a range of support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the steps being taken to ensure a joined-up, multi-agency response to paramilitary-related coercive control, especially in relation to intimate partner violence; (ii) the measures in place to support women attempting to exit coercive relationships that involve paramilitary groups; and (iii) how trauma-informed safety plans are being integrated into this process.
answer_text: There are a range of strategic initiatives in place that contribute to a joined up, multi-agency approach to supporting women and girls affected by paramilitary violence For example, the new Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) is designed to address the whole range of gender-based violence, abuse and harm which is disproportionately experienced by women and girls, including that inflicted by paramilitaries. In addition, the Departments of Health and Justice launched (on 25 September) the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy. This aims to address all forms of domestic and sexual abuse.The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), for which I am lead Minister, also has in place a range of initiatives aimed at supporting women in areas most impacted by ongoing paramilitary activity. Programme officials are in close and regular contact with EVAWG officials to ensure that their efforts are aligned. They have also commissioned research to enable a better understanding of the impact of paramilitary harm on women, girls and young people; this has been shared widely and is available online.  EPPOC also funds the Trauma Informed Approaches work undertaken by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI).  While there is still work to be done to implement trauma informed approaches across Departments, agencies and systems, I welcome the ambition in the draft Programme for Government to understand the impact of trauma on our society, as well as the commitment in the new EVAWG Strategic Framework to taking a trauma informed approach. Whilst I am not aware specifically of trauma informed safety plans being implemented in Northern Ireland, there are various models of good practice in place within organisations that support women and children. Work will continue to ensure that trauma informed approaches are embedded so that we have a system that can listen and support people in a way that will have a lasting impact on society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the support he is providing to schools to better understand online safety; and (ii) how he is working with (a) the PSNI; and (b) industry professionals to provide specialist advice to schools in this area of work.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of supports to help schools better understand online safety, including learning resources for pupils and guidance on good practice for schools. The Northern Ireland Curriculum outlines that pupils should be provided with opportunities to develop knowledge and understanding of e-safety and acceptable online behaviour and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations Assessment (CCEA) has developed a number of Digital Well Being Resources which are available on the website. These cover all Key Stages and are monitored and updated as necessary.Department of Education (DE) Circular 2016/27 Online Safety  provides a set of guiding principles for keeping pupils and the wider school community safe online and for prioritising online safety within the school’s preventative education curriculum and overall Safeguarding Policy. Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools also includes guidance on online safety and managing risks associated with the online world.
