This guidance is reviewed as necessary with input from a range of stakeholders, including the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) which provides specialist advice in relation to online safety. The guidance also includes references to the SBNI’s Online Safety Hub and resources developed by other industry professionals, such as the Southwest Grid for Learning’s online safety audit tool and PSNI’s resources on online safety.Additionally, my Department has engaged with iNEQE Safeguarding Group to provide the Safer Schools Northern Ireland App to schools, parents/carers and pupils. The App provides advice, guidance and information on a range of safeguarding issues, with particular attention paid to risks in the online world, emerging social media platforms and online harms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he plans to create a care pathway for the early detection of liver disease; (ii) whether this pathway could be created via primary care.
answer_text: The current pathway for people identified in primary care with suspected liver disease is referral to secondary care services in their local Trust. Some initial discussions have taken place between colleagues in the Belfast Trust Hepatology Service and local primary care representatives around refinement to the local pathway for services provided in Belfast Trust, specifically around non-alcohol fatty liver disease (NAFLD).A pilot pathway has already been trialled with one GP practice.  This involved:A GP identifying a patient with NAFLD and undertaking initial screening and then sending a referral to the Hepatology ServiceThe referral is triaged by a consultant and is either forwarded for a consultant clinical appointment or if appropriate to a nurse led clinicIf the appointment is triaged to a nurse led clinic a fibroscan is undertaken and the nurse can provide lifestyle advice regarding NAFLDIf there are any abnormal findings on the scan then the patient is referred on to a consultant clinic, if not then they are discharged back to their GP with appropriate advice.Due to the success of this pilot the Regional Liver Unit is now applying the criteria to NAFLD referrals from all Belfast Trust practices and is also exploring the implications for similar pathway arrangements in other Trust areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the specific interventions in place to protect children who are vulnerable to coercion or recruitment by paramilitary groups through their relationships with affected women.
answer_text: There are a number of initiatives that aim to support and protect children who are vulnerable to paramilitary influence, including in cases where women are victims/survivors of coercion and there are children who need to be protected from the harms in their environment. This has, for example, been a focus for the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), for which I am lead Minister. The Programme includes a significant number of initiatives aimed specifically at supporting and increasing the resilience of, and protective factors around, young people who are most at risk of being coerced into becoming involved in paramilitary or criminal activity. Recognising that this is a clear case of child criminal exploitation (CCE), in 2022 EPPOC commissioned Queen’s University Belfast to detail children and young people’s experiences of CCE; this has informed my Department’s response to this issue. For example, the work plan of the Child Protection Senior Officials Group (CPSOG), led by DOJ and DOH, includes both Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) and Child Sexual Exploitation (CSE).  CPSOG provides direction on existing or emerging child protection issues requiring cross-departmental consideration and co-ordination, including those relating to paramilitary coercion. Work is ongoing on the implementation of the recommendations of a CCE Task & Finish Group which reported to CPSOG in February 2023. The majority of this is being led by the Safeguarding Board for NI (SBNI), and SBNI has reconfigured its CSE subgroup to form a Child Exploitation Committee to further strengthen and co-ordinate the multi-agency system response to all forms of child exploitation.This multi-agency SBNI subgroup is chaired by PSNI and includes Health Trusts, the Education Authority and the Youth Justice Agency among others. In addition, my Department has developed a three-year Strategy (published in July) in relation to Modern Slavery, Human Trafficking & Child Exploitation. This includes a range of commitments and actions around improving operational responses and ensuring appropriate support is available to victims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the actions being taken to counter the role of paramilitary groups in exerting coercive surveillance and control over women; and (ii) how statutory agencies are being trained to recognise and respond to such situations.
answer_text: One of the most challenging facets of paramilitary coercion of women is its hidden nature and the way it isolates people from the support they need.  I was heartened to attend the launch of research by Dr Aisling Swayne and Foyle Women’s Aid recently because it details and records publicly women’s experiences of paramilitary sexual violence. Survivor testimony is critical if we are to understand and respond effectively to harms that have been hidden for far too long. This work along with other research is helping to inform a number of initiatives that are in place which are contributing to addressing the issues you raise, including the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), for which I am lead Minister. The Programme is active across Northern Ireland to support people and communities who are vulnerable to paramilitary influence and harm, with commitments being delivered collaboratively by Government Departments, statutory agencies and partners in the voluntary and community sector. Interventions delivered under the EPPOC Programme include those aimed specifically at supporting women in areas most impacted by ongoing paramilitary activity. In addition, EPPOC funds the Paramilitary Crime Task Force (PCTF), a tripartite law enforcement agency which investigates the criminality of paramilitary groups. It is also the sole funder of the Safeguarding Board’s project on trauma-informed approaches. This ensures trauma informed approaches and toolkits are rolled out across the public, helping to ensure that all relevant agencies are aware of how trauma manifests and what can be done to address it. This is directly relevant to the issues you raise.Other strategic initiatives in this area include the Executive’s Strategic Framework to end Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG), which focuses on gender-based violence predominantly affecting women and girls; and the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, published by the Departments of Health and Justice (on 25 September) which aims to address all forms of domestic and sexual abuse. The draft Programme for Government also speaks to the importance of addressing these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what plans he has to increase funding for Community Transport to enable service users to make use of more than two journeys per week; and (ii) whether he has any plans to conduct a wider review of transport, including (a) public transport; (b) health and social care transport; and (c) community transport, to maximise travel options in rural areas.
answer_text: I recognise the value of community transport services to those who use them in our rural and urban areas.  Following consideration of the Budget Equality Impact Assessment Consultation responses, and in conjunction with the June monitoring round, I have increased funding for these services by 4.2% based on the levels awarded in 2023/24. This increase is not as much as I would like to provide but it is a significant increase and will hopefully help alleviate some of the pressures faced by service providers.Officials are currently preparing a new Transport Strategy and a new suite of Transport Plans that will set the framework for transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035.  The suite of Transport Plans will not consider changes to Community Transport operations as these matters are being considered separately by the Department at a regional level and will be integrated into the Plans as appropriate.  Health and Social Care Transport is the responsibility for the Department of Health.  However, the Transport Plans will consider access, including by Public Transport, to the main Health and Social Care facilities.I am keen to progress a NI-wide phased review of the Public Transport Network.  I am pleased that the first part of this has commenced with a review of the Ulsterbus network, including school transport provision.  The review will balance efficiency with the need to facilitate modal shift from private cars to public transport whilst taking account of our economic and transport policies to deliver a financially sustainable and accessible network.I recently met with Executive colleagues to explore if community transport can support other transport activities across government.  I am also considering the first phase of a Community Transport Review, which is examining how to support the delivery of community transport, including in rural areas, and ensure that we make the most effective use of the funding that is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail her conversations with the UK government in relation to their decision to pause City and Growth Deals for Causeway Coast and Glens.
answer_text: I refer to the Written Ministerial Statement of 18 September.https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/pausing-city-and-growth-deals-funding-commitment

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department takes consideration of the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 when assessing the impact and effectiveness of legislation related to domestic abuse.
answer_text: My department is bound by the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, which requires us to take into account the needs of rural communities in all our activities and decisions.  Therefore, when introducing legislation related to domestic abuse it will go through an assessment to evaluate the effectiveness and impact for those living in rural areas.Through the recently published Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, I am committed to ensuring that support services are available and accessible to everyone affected by domestic abuse, regardless of their circumstances or location.  The Strategy’s integrated performance framework will help us to measure success against this outcome.In addition, my Department is responsible for preparing a report on the operation of Part 1 of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings (DACP) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, scheduled for publication in June 2025.The report will provide a comprehensive overview of various data points, including the number of domestic abuse offences recorded by the PSNI, broken down by police district, the number prosecutions and convictions, and the average time taken to resolve cases.  The report will include details on the level of training provided, how court proceedings are organised, the experience of witnesses in court, and any other relevant information deemed appropriate by the Department.Additionally, the report will feature a section dedicated to examining the impact and effectiveness of the DACP Act on victims residing in rural areas.  To gather meaningful insights for this section, we will collaborate with support organisations that work directly with rural victims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the effectiveness of non-molestation orders when perpetrators with an active order are allowed to work on family farms where victims reside.
answer_text: A non-molestation order prohibits the party against whom it is made from “molesting” the party protected by the order. It is for a court to determine the terms of an order and whether any subsequent behaviour amounts to a breach of it. Non-molestation orders  are effective in that they provide victims with protection against a wide range of behaviours, including serious pestering and harassment, and in some cases they may prohibit specific acts. An order can also exclude the respondent from a defined area in which a dwelling-house is included, any other defined area, and any premises specified in the order, and this may be a way of providing additional protection to a victim. A party who has obtained a non-molestation order and is concerned that they are not adequately protected by the terms of the order, may apply to the court to seek a variation. Breach of a non-molestation order is a criminal offence punishable by a fine, imprisonment or both. It is important to note that unless specifically provided for under the terms of the non-molestation order, it generally does not prohibit all forms of contact with the victim, or being in the same general vicinity as them, unless these actions are of themselves molesting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what he is doing to support unpaid carers in rural areas (i) financially; and (ii) practically.
answer_text: Supporting unpaid carers remains a priority for my Department. I recognise that carers, particularly in rural areas, often face unique challenges, both financially and practically, due to geographic isolation and limited access to services. My Department offers support to those unpaid carers in a number of ways.  While the primary financial support for unpaid carers, such as Carer’s Allowance, is set by UK government, additional small grants are available to help carers via local Trusts. Following a Carers Assessment, based on the outcomes and assessed need, carers can receive one off payments of up to £100 to encourage them to prioritise their health and wellbeing, and to encourage them to take a break from their caring role. In addition to financial assistance, the Department provides practical support measures to unpaid carers in rural areas. The South Eastern Trust Carer Support Service, is one such example, offering information sessions, activities, and events for carers throughout the year. Recognising the isolation faced by many rural carers, these sessions are delivered both in person and online, providing flexibility for those who cannot leave their loved ones. Local carer information hubs have been established in remote areas such as Portaferry, Drumaness, and Moira, bringing resources directly to carers. In the Peninsula area, partnerships have been established between GP practices and social work teams to create support groups, organise health and wellbeing events and deliver self-care sessions. Walking tours in rural areas also offer carers an opportunity to connect with others and they can help with their mental health.Respite care remains an important part of our support for unpaid carers in rural areas. Health and Social Care Trusts provide access to various respite services including residential and home-based care, day centres and emergency respite care. Carers are encouraged to undergo a Carers Assessment, which can determine the level of support they require. Following the assessment, carers can access short-term breaks, counselling services and other practical supports. Trusts like the Belfast Trust also provide a Carers Support Service, offering resources such as a Carer ID card, complementary therapies, and support groups tailored to different carer needs.Unpaid carers, including those in rural areas, are encouraged to reach out to their local Trust’s Carers Hubs to find out more information and avail of the services available locally to them.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have considered a trauma informed and trauma resilient approach for Northern Ireland, similar to the National Trauma Transformation Programme led by the Scottish government.
answer_text: We are committed to embedding trauma-informed approaches within the work of our department and the wider public sector. In the draft Programme for Government, which is currently out for public consultation, we have committed to developing a trauma-informed public sector and understanding the impact of trauma in society.A Trauma Informed Practice team in the Safeguarding Board (SBNI) is funded by the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime. The SBNI’s Trauma Informed Practice Implementation managers will continue to support member and partner agencies to embed Trauma Informed Approaches building on existing knowledge of adversity and trauma.Within The Executive Office, the vision of our new Strategy for Victims and Survivors of the Troubles/Conflict 2024-2034 is to be ‘a trauma informed society that addresses the needs of victims and survivors.’ We are also pleased that the Victims and Survivors Service recently became the first organisation here to be accredited with a Silver ‘Trauma-Informed: Working with Trauma Quality Mark’, offering formal recognition of the VSS trauma-informed approach.In addition, a key principle of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework is that the work under the Framework should be trauma informed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration she has given to a prisoner early release scheme, similar to that in England and Wales, including the criteria for release under such a scheme and undertaking engagement with victims and survivors.
answer_text: While I regularly review these matters, particularly in relation to prison population management, I am not giving consideration to a prisoner early release scheme similar to that in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has undertaken an assessment of the impact a prisoner early release scheme will have on the aims and objectives of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
answer_text: I am not currently considering a prisoner early release scheme in Northern Ireland and therefore, have not had cause to assess any impact such a scheme may have on the aims and objectives of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the criteria for determining when prisons are considered overcrowded, including available staff and prison population; and (ii) any projections that have been carried out in relation to the likelihood of prisons becoming overcrowded in the next 12 months.
answer_text: Whether or not a prison is overcrowded takes into account a number of dynamic and interrelated factors.  These include the total and the breakdown of the population, what accommodation is open, staff availability, the level of regime that can be delivered, and the extent of cell-sharing.Whilst the Northern Ireland prison population is not presenting the same immediate risks as in other jurisdictions, it is challenging, and NIPS are highly vigilant to the population trend in the remaining months of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered (i) the impact of his schools guidance for mobile phone on neurodiverse children and those with a medical conditions who use their smartphone to manage and support their condition; (ii) a different approach for post-primary children and sixth form; (iii) programmes and apps on smartphones that were introduced during the pandemic and still in use.
answer_text: (i) The guidance focuses on restricting the use of personal mobile devices to ensure that all young people can fully engage in learning and participate in school life, free from unnecessary distractions.  The guidance acknowledges that there may be exceptional circumstances and recognises that schools will wish to develop a bespoke and tailored approach dependent on the needs of their schools and individual pupils.  The guidance does not provide an exhaustive list of exceptional circumstances and schools can assess each case on its own merits, however it could be expected that this would include pupils with medical conditions (including diabetes), Special Educational Needs or young carers.  (ii) The guidance provides examples of different approaches on restricting the use of mobile phones and highlights positives and potential issues associated with each approach.  The guidance specifically highlights the potential need for different approaches across primary and post primary.  In addition, the specific needs of pupils in years 13 and 14 are referenced.  Schools have been asked to revise existing policies accordingly to develop approaches that work for their own environment, consulting with the wider school community, including pupils and parents. (iii) The guidance is intended to focus on pupils’ personal use of mobile devices within the school environment rather than in any way prohibit or inhibit wider use of technology that have been introduced in schools to support learning. Schools are best placed to determine how such technology more broadly can effectively support learning and teaching for their pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for an update on public services transformation; (ii) whether the Public Service Transformation Board has approved the 2024-25 of £47 million to be released by the UK government; and (iii) to detail the Executive’s plans and timeline for this year’s funding.
answer_text: The Executive agreed to my proposal for an interim Transformation Programme on 9 May. I quickly established the interim Public Sector Transformation Board, who are giving consideration to proposals to utilise the transformation funding from the financial package. The interim Board’s role is to provide advice to me, after it completes its assessment of proposals, indicating those it considers to be transformative. I will then bring recommendations to the Executive. It is important that the interim Board is given the space to carry out its work.The interim Board has completed its first stage assessment. 47 proposals were received in total. 29 proposals are continuing to move forward, 11 of which have moved to a Stage 2 assessment, 18 others have been included within a Digital Landscape Review, which will conclude later this year.A final terms of reference is to be agreed for the Public Sector Transformation Board, recognising the appropriate parameters and responsibilities for transformation locally. My officials are continuing to press for progress with their British counterparts so that there is no undue delay in allocating this critical funding.Clearly £235m of transformation funding, across 5 years, will in no way tackle the magnitude of the issues at hand. However, reform and transformation cannot just be about money and how it is allocated. The focus also needs to be on what we do, how we do it, and being held to account for the results we achieve. To ensure we lay the foundations for this scale of change, a new Reform and Transformation Unit will be established by the Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQO 744/22-27, (i) whether her Department had considered potential implementation costs upon commissioning Judge Burgess’ review; and (ii) for her assessment of whether appropriate funding will be secured for an implementation plan in this mandate.
answer_text: When commissioning the review of Criminal Legal Aid the Department did not ask His Honour Tom Burgess CBE to consider proposals within any funding envelope. To do so would have constrained the scope of recommendations he could make.However, once my officials have reviewed the report and I have clarity on the resource and consequential impact of each of the recommendations, I will know if additional funding is required and will make the appropriate bid at that time.I continue to seek a fair funding model for the Department within this mandate. Where the budget provided to my Department does not support the delivery of a justice system that citizens need, I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to secure the necessary additional funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on childcare support measures for school age children.
answer_text: With the limited budget provided by the Executive for early learning and childcare in 2024/25, I have sought to target support at those who need it most.  As parents of pre-school aged children tend to be the highest users of childcare and incur the greatest costs, this is where my initial focus has been.  This is also the period when decisions are often taken by mothers to leave the labour market or reduce their hours, establishing employment patterns which have a lasting impact on their careers and incomes. 		The package of early learning and childcare measures currently being implemented will be evaluated alongside data collected from both parents and providers to inform any future interventions, including for school age childcare. 	I will be making a strong case to my Executive colleagues for the funding required to sustain and expand early learning and childcare provision in future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rationale for proposals of Health Measures in Relation to Persons in the new Public Health Bill for Northern Ireland; (ii) whether he has considered another approach that does not legally impose proposed restrictions or requirements; (iii) how these proposals impact on (a) human rights; and (b) data protection; and (iv) the risk of not moving on these proposals.
answer_text: The existing Public Health Act 1967 is no longer fit for purpose and requires modernisation to enable NI to respond appropriately to 21st century public health emergencies. The overarching principle of the proposed legislation is to protect the population against threats to public health. Health measures in relation to persons are intended for use only in the rare event that voluntary cooperation to avert a health risk cannot be secured and where other methods of control have been exhausted or are deemed ineffective, unsuitable or disproportionate to the risk involved. A decision to action such powers by way of regulations would be subject to public health advice and would require Executive and Assembly approval.Striking an appropriate balance between protecting public health and respecting personal freedom is at the core of the policy proposals. Existing public health legislation largely predates contemporary human rights and data protection legislation. The proposals comply with these laws by incorporating a robust range of protections and safeguards, in addition to an underpinning requirement that any action taken must be proportionate to the risk presented.A failure to replace our outdated public health legislation leaves NI misaligned with other regions of the UK and poorly equipped to deal with a future public health emergency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how he ensures public sector organisations are following best workplace practices in relation to misconduct of employees; (ii) how other employees and organisational stakeholders are protected when misconduct impacts upon them; and (iii) whether he has considered developing a workplace handbook for public sector organisations to ensure consistency and best practice.
answer_text: The Labour Relations Agency (LRA) provides a range of free guidance documents and templates for all employers, including public sector employers, which set out the best practice approach when developing and establishing workplace policies, and addressing workplace issues. These documents also set out the duties and responsibilities of employers to their employees when another employee is being investigated or disciplined for misconduct. Further advice and guidance for employers and employees can be found on the NI Direct and NI Business Info websites respectively. Whilst these guides are a useful resource for all employers, it is ultimately a matter for each employer to design policies and implement practices which best suit their organisational structure and needs, whilst adhering to legal requirements.Employees who wish to report malpractice or wrongdoing in the workplace can make a disclosure to the employer or ‘whistleblow’ to a prescribed person or body, which can ensure an individual’s employment rights are protected.Finally, any employee who believes they have been unfairly treated may be able to complain to an Industrial Tribunal. Anyone who finds themselves in this position may wish to consider contacting the LRA’s Workplace Information Service on 03300 555 300 for confidential and impartial information.In addition, the Law Centre NI also provides free, independent, specialist legal advice (subject to capacity) on employment rights. The Law Centre can be contacted on their advice line on 028 9024 4401 or by email: employmentadvice@lawcentreni.org.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their plans to review the office of the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse before the current commissioner’s role comes to an end.
answer_text: The HIA (NI) Act 2019 requires us to commission an independent person to review the operation of COSICA and produce a report which includes an assessment of whether there continues to be a need for the Commissioner.We have appointed Professor Alexis Jay as the independent assessor to conduct the review, which is now underway. Professor Jay is working to Terms of Reference that were agreed following feedback from COSICA.  Following some initial desk-based work, it is intended that Professor Jay will commence meetings with relevant stakeholders from October 2024, with a final report being submitted by the end of December 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consider conducting a population prevalence study of sexual abuse, including in childhood sexual abuse.
answer_text: We met the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse earlier this year. Among other topics, we discussed her proposal for research into the prevalence of non-recent child sexual abuse.This will be taken into account as part of our consideration of the overall research needs of the Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have any plans to conduct a further awareness campaign in Northern Ireland, Great Britain and internationally to encourage applications to the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Scheme before it closes on 2 April 2025.
answer_text: We continue to build on previous awareness initiatives, which have included: adverts in local papers; distribution of posters to relevant organisations, including courts and jobs and benefits offices; a leaflet drop to every household here; a digital media campaign; engagement with the Bureau in Washington to connect to organisations in America that support immigrants; and the issue of promotional material to all local councils in England, Scotland, Wales and the Republic of Ireland. A further digital media contract is to be awarded in September for more awareness raising activities over the coming months to again highlight the last date for applications to the redress scheme.  We will also continue to distribute posters and leaflets to appropriate contacts, such as local chemists and libraries, and to engage with relevant stakeholders to raise awareness of the closure of the scheme to applications.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they will work with the Minister of Health to (i) recognise the lifelong legacy for victims and survivors of Historical Institutional Childhood Abuse; and (ii) ensure adequate lifelong support from Health and Social Care services.
answer_text: One of the recommendations of the Historical Institutional Abuse (HIA) Inquiry report was that services should be made available to HIA victims and survivors for a fixed period of ten years, with a review taking place after five years.A support service for HIA victims and survivors, facilitated by the Victims and Survivors Service, commenced in December 2020. A review will therefore be due in 2025.The Commissioner is also currently undertaking a consultation to look at current and future services for HIA victims and survivors.The feedback from the consultation and the review, along with advice from the Commissioner’s Advisory Panel, once established, will inform the design and shape of future support services.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any plans they have to support victims and survivors of non-recent childhood abuse that fall outside the settings of Historical Institutional Abuse, including public facilitated foster care settings.
answer_text: We recognise the devastating impact that all forms of childhood abuse has on victims and survivors.  While foster care more broadly is a matter for the Department for Health, as part of the implementation of the recommendations being taken forward by TEO’s Truth Recovery Programme, to date the Victims and Survivors Service has provided a range of practical, emotional and therapeutic services and support to those victims and survivors affected by the pathways and practices of Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses (1922-1995). This includes those children, now adults, who were both adopted and moved into foster care settings.We have also commissioned research to examine the extent and systemic nature of Historical Clerical Child Abuse here. The purpose of the research is to enable the provision of informed, evidence-based recommendations to Ministers on any future action required regarding non-recent child abuse within the faith sector. Information relating to health and social care services support to victims of abuse, including childhood abuse, should be sought from the Department of Health.  Information relating to support available relating to criminal justice processes should be sought from the Department of Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to his Department's decision to no longer fund housing adaptations for disabled tenants, (i) whether Housing Associations were informed of this change of policy; (ii) when this decision was taken; (iii) whether this decision was subject to an equality impact assessment; (iv) when this decision was subsequently reversed; and (v) the rationale for the reversal of this decision.
answer_text: The Disabled Facilities Grant has been prescribed as a mandatory grant in legislation since 2003 and at no stage has funding for this grant been removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 is being implemented to enhance healthcare support for victims of domestic abuse; and (ii) any further measures being considered to ensure that survivors receive the necessary medical and psychological care.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any initiatives in place to support women going through perimenopause and menopause; and (ii) whether he is considering the development of a menopause strategy that includes public education, healthcare training and workplace support.
answer_text: While not all women will need medical intervention for perimenopause and menopause, and most that do should be able to receive appropriate support and treatment from their GP, it is important that appropriate advice, information and specialist care are available where needed.The Health and Social Care (HSC) Clinical Education Centre (CEC), in partnership with my Department, has this year commenced the successful delivery of a series of multi-professional educational webinars on menopause to raise awareness, share information and promote learning for health care professionals supporting women experiencing the menopause.  The webinars have focused on specific aspects of menopause including the management of menopause in relation to primary care, cardiology, pelvic health, cancer and sexual health, and have been attended by a wide range of healthcare professions and organisations.The Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) report on Gynaecology across NI, published in January this year, includes recommendations to improve and standardise advice and access to services in relation to menopause and other gynae-related issues.  There are some good examples of menopause services and support within the Health and Social Care (HSC) system, however it is recognised that access and availability currently vary across the region.  My Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and Public Health Agency representation, to oversee implementation of the report.  This will include a Regional Engagement Forum to garner expert user feedback from stakeholders, including those suffering from menopause symptoms, and a Menopause Task and Finish Group to establish service needs and develop clinical pathways.  It is recognised that some of the recommendations will require additional investment which is not currently available, and this will inevitably impact delivery.The relevant NICE Clinical Guideline (NG23) on Menopause diagnosis and management, which my Department endorsed in 2016, aimed to improve the consistency of support and information provided to women.  The Guideline is in the process of being updated, to include focus on managing urogenital atrophy, the long-term benefits and risks of hormone replacement therapy, and cognitive behavioural therapy for managing menopausal symptoms, and will be considered as part of the above work when published, however full implementation will also be subject to resources. Voluntary and community sector organisations can also play an important role in supporting women through perimenopause and menopause.  For example, a new Northern Ireland charity, Menopause Together, was established in 2023 to help address the need for more education, support and awareness and to help empower women to make informed choices and feel supported on their menopause journey.Due to the severe financial constraints facing my Department and the absence of a multi-year bu

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the steps being taken to increase awareness and support for menopause in the workplace; and (ii) how these efforts can be integrated into broader workplace health and well-being good practice.
answer_text: Women going through the menopause can experience challenges within the workplace when experiencing symptoms of menopause which require the support, understanding and compassion of their employers. It is important therefore that employers are aware of their duty of care to their employees under health and safety law and the need to make reasonable adjustments to help employees managing their symptoms when performing their job.To increase awareness and support for employers and their employees, the Labour Relations Agency, the Equality Commission and the Irish Congress of Trade Unions published a guide entitled Promoting Equality in Employment for Women Affected by Menopause. All employers are recommended to consider this guide when developing their own workplace health and well-being policies. Further guidance and support are available to employers and employees through the NI Direct and NIBusiness Info websites.	In addition, good employers will have flexible supportive working policies in place such as homeworking, flexible hours, or compassionate leave. I would encourage all employers to offer enhanced flexible and supportive working arrangements for their staff experiencing symptoms of menopause. Proposals to further strengthen the right to request flexible working though a future Employment Rights Bill are subject to a live consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration being given to the development of a comprehensive women’s health strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Due to the severe financial constraints facing my Department and the absence of a long term budget, I intend to focus during the remainder of this mandate on the actions that can be delivered within current resources to stabilise our Health and Social Care system and address the health inequalities that contribute to poor health outcomes. There are a wide range of policy and service development initiatives in progress which aim to improve women's health services and outcomes. These will be highlighted in the women's health action plan which will outline my vision for what can be delivered during the current Assembly mandate. My Department has provided funding of £10k to Derry Well Women, who in partnership with Queen’s University Belfast, are undertaking a large-scale public listening exercise to ensure that women’s voices are heard and to help shape how services are delivered in the future.  It is hoped that the first stage of this exercise – a women’s health survey – will be launched later in Autumn 2024.   This, alongside the Women’s Health Action Plan will provide a focus and an evidence base for a long-term strategy, should the Executive decide to prioritise this in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to develop a TB eradication strategy similar to the UK Government’s plan for England where badgers will be vaccinated instead of culled.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is one of the most challenging issues faced by the cattle farmers in Northern Ireland.  Herd incidence currently stands at 10.09% and over £55million was spent on the control of bTB in cattle in the last financial year.  A long-term Strategy to reduce and, ultimately eradicate bovine TB in Northern Ireland was launched in March 2022.  However, a judicial review decision in October 2023 overturned the previous Minister’s decision to undertake a non-selective cull of badgers as part of the Strategy. The Chief Veterinary Officer, Brian Dooher, has commenced a review of matters relating to bTB programme and policy, including the actions contained in the Bovine TB Eradication Strategy.  My Department is determined to tackle all factors in the maintenance and spread of this disease.   Brian and his officials are working on options for my consideration including those relating to the role of wildlife in the spread of this disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting time for children's autism assessments; (ii) whether the waiting time has increased in the last year; (iii) his plans to reduce assessment waiting times; and (iv) his assessment of delayed autism assessments on children’s development and consequent support available.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the cost of moving the winter fuel payments to a means-tested model; (ii) whether there is an application process for the new model; and (iii) how much less the new model will cost compared with the current model.
answer_text: Winter Fuel Payments are administered by the Department for Work and Pensions. Therefore, there is no cost to my Department from the move to a means-tested model in Northern Ireland.  Means tested Winter Fuel Payments will be funded directly from HM Treasury and are outside the NI Block Grant.Most people get the Winter Fuel Payment automatically if they meet the eligibility criteria.  An application is not needed if you get any of the following:Pension CreditIncome SupportIncome related Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)Income based Jobseeker’s Allowance (JSA)People born before 23 September 1958 and who receive Universal Credit  may need to claim and more information about how to do that can be found at - https://www.gov.uk/winter-fuel-payment/how-to-claimMeans-testing Winter Fuel Payments under the new model will reduce Annually Managed Expenditure (AME) costs incurred by HM Treasury by approximately £44.3m for eligible Northern Ireland recipients.  AME costs are funded directly from HM Treasury and are outside the NI Block Grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any additional (i) resources; and (ii) funding that have been provided to local councils' animal welfare departments to ensure the enforcement of legislation relating to XL Bully type dogs.
answer_text: Council dog wardens are responsible for enforcing dog control legislation in Northern Ireland, including the new restrictions on owning XL Bully type dogs. I have secured resource and will be providing support for training dog wardens, updating IT systems and other associated enforcement needs.  My officials continue to meet regularly with Northern Ireland Dog Advisory Group, which represents council dog wardens, to discuss and monitor implementation of the legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the fundamental review of criminal legal aid in Northern Ireland, reported by Judge Burgess in August 2024.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 8 – AQO 744/22-27ANSWERThe report by His Honour Tom Burgess CBE on the fundamental review of Criminal Legal Aid in Northern Ireland was received on 23 August 2024. I want to take this opportunity to thank Judge Burgess for the report and his comprehensive engagement with a wide range of stakeholders to gather the evidence necessary to make his recommendations. My officials are currently working through the report. Once this is complete and there is clarity on the resource and consequential impact of each of the recommendations, I will be engaging with Executive colleagues to bring forward an implementation plan and any necessary primary and secondary legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work he is planning to assess the scale and impact on the (i) lough; and (ii) marine environment of untreated sewage releases into the River Foyle.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates wastewater discharges through discharge consents issued under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. NIEA does not consent the discharge of untreated sewage to the aquatic environment. I can advise that my Department has no plans presently to assess the scale and impact on Lough Foyle or the marine environment of untreated sewage releases into the River Foyle. My Department monitors nutrient levels and impacts in the marine environment but it is not possible to apportion these to particular sources.   My Department however, will continue to conduct targeted studies to identify pollution sources and develop the appropriate measures for implementation to improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any discussions he has had with (i) Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council; (ii) Sport NI; and (iii) Causeway Association of Urban Sports, in relation to the creation of a skate park in Portrush.
answer_text: I have not been involved in discussions with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council nor Causeway Association of Urban Sports (CAUS) regarding the creation of a skatepark in Portrush.However, my officials worked closely with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council in recent years as part of the regeneration of the Portrush Recreation Grounds (PRG) at Ramore Avenue. The regeneration scheme, which completed in March 2023, includes an urban plaza that acts as a public space that can also accommodate skateboarding and other urban sports.While under the Recreation and Youth Service Order (NI) 1986 Article 10, local Councils have responsibility for the provision of recreational, social, physical and cultural facilities and activities, I sought the requested information from Sport NI, in relation to any discussions it may have had with either the Council or CAUS about the development of a skatepark in Portrush.As this issue is an operational matter for Sport NI, I can confirm that its Chief Executive advised me as follows:“Sport NI Infrastructure Team has had no specific discussions with Causeway Coast & Glens Council regarding a skatepark in Portrush.  During various conversations, the Council has mentioned a strong lobby (from urban sports groups) for a skatepark in Portrush, therefore Sport NI are aware of the potential demand.  However, we have no information on project proposals or what stage those proposals are at.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with Sport NI will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children who have been hospitalised with conditions other than Covid-19 in each of the last five years; (ii) the illnesses that have seen rises in frequency throughout that time; and (iii) the steps that are being taken to reduce the appearance and transmission of these illnesses.
answer_text: The number of children and young people aged under 17 who were admitted to hospital for reasons other than COVID are listed below.  Figures for 23/24 will be available in October 2024.YearTotal Admissions 2018/1949,1182019/2045,5812020/2129,0452021/2238,6642022/2341,925NB: During 2020/21 and 2021/22 admissions numbers were down due to COVID infection control measures such as lockdown and school closures. This reduced the spread of COVID and also reduced the spread of other ‘normal’ winter viruses. The main reasons for emergency admissions into hospitals for children is as a result of infections such as RSV, acute bronchiolitis, upper and lower respiratory infections including asthma exacerbation and wheezing, trauma, intestinal infections, fever, jaundice and laryngitis and for investigation for conditions such as nausea and vomiting, gastrointestinal and abdominal pain.  Children with complex / or congenital conditions with multiple co-morbidities will also be admitted to manage their care at times. With the busiest periods of year over winter periods when the system sees higher numbers of admissions due to winter respiratory viruses, in particular admissions due to RSV (Respiratory Syncytial Virus) with admission numbers varying on a year-by-year basis. There has also been a small increase in admissions for the communicable diseases of measles and pertussis which directly relates to a reduction in vaccine uptake.Vaccines are the most effective way to prevent many infectious diseases. However, over recent years there has been a reduction in the uptake of childhood vaccinations, and this is now resulting in increases in incidence in communicable diseases, such as measles and pertussis.  The PHA continues to lead on campaigns to ascertain reasons behind the lower numbers of vaccination uptake and develop means to increase vaccination rates.Each year the service puts plans in place to manage the increase in admissions as a result of RSV and a regional RSV synagis vaccination campaign is carried out to protect the most vulnerable children.  The PHA leads on a wide-ranging childhood vaccination programme that includes crucial partnership working with primary care and HSC Trusts.  This programme covers a wide range of communicable diseases and is an extremely important measure in keeping children well. This programme is regionally agreed, based upon advice from the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI) – an independent expert advisory committee that advises United Kingdom health departments on immunisation, making recommendations concerning vaccination schedules and vaccine safety.The programme includes immunisations to protects against diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, polio, haemophilus influenzae type b, (Hib) hepatitis B, rotavirus, meningococcal group B, meningococcal groups ACWY pneumococcal disease, measles, mumps, rubella, conditions caused by human papillomavirus (including cervical cancer and cancers of the mouth, throat, anus

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of crimes in which a knife or a bladed instrument was used in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on reported crimes in which a knife or a bladed instrument was used.  The recording of information in relation to offences reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI. However, the Department does hold information on the number of convictions at courts.  While convictions datasets do not contain detail on where a knife or bladed instrument was used in the commission of an offence, or on the location in which an offence took place, there are two offences which relate to the possession of a knife or bladed instrument in a school or in a public place.    Information, at Northern Ireland level, on the number of convictions for these two offences, in the five-year period up to the end of 2023, the most recent year for which statistics are available, has been provided. Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at courts where there was a conviction for at least one of the offences specified.3. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI, Harbour Police, Airport Constabulary, NCA or PBNI.4. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and magistrates' courts.5. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the support available for the diagnosis, treatment and management of post-traumatic stress disorder.
answer_text: A diagnosis of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is typically reached by a consultant (or a trainee psychiatrist under the supervision of a consultant) after a full psychiatric assessment. Psychiatrists work in multi-disciplinary teams and on some occasions a person may be assessed by a member of staff from another profession who may not make a diagnosis as such but will assess the person’s presenting symptoms and problems and will agree a management and treatment plan. Individuals with post-traumatic stress disorder will typically be offered:  a) an appropriate psychological therapy (most often trauma-focused cognitive behaviour therapy or EMDR (eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing therapy).and/or (b) appropriate pharmacological treatments (medications; most often an antidepressant). If PTSD has resulted from a Troubles/conflict-related trauma care may be provided by the Regional Trauma Network (the RTN). The RTN brings together services provided by both the statutory (through the Health and Social Care Trusts) and community and voluntary sector organisations (funded through the Victims and Survivors Service).   Furthermore, any adult who requires Mental Health Services will adhere to the standards set out in the Regional You in Mind Mental Health Care Pathway (2014). The term mental ill health covers a broad spectrum of mental health problems.  This Care Pathway is for people who experience mental ill health and for their families and friends.  The Pathway outlines how care decisions will be made and put the person, and/or family, partner or nominated friends at heart of all decision making. The Care Pathway sets out the standards expected by all mental health and psychological therapy services provided by Health and Social Care Trusts including those services commissioned from independent community and voluntary sectors organisations. The Pathway has been modelled on NICE 136 Guideline Improving Experience of People Using Adult Mental Health Services Quality Standards, and outlines how Mental Health Services will use recovery principles in providing care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to review private property rental sector regulations to make it easier for lower income households to find a home.
answer_text: The first phase of the reform of the Private Rented Sector is encapsulated in the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022. This legislation already includes measures to help low-income households to access the sector by limiting the amount of deposit that a landlord can request or retain to no more than one month’s rent. Of equal importance are those other measures derived from the Act which are focused on increasing the protections for tenants while living in a property, including my recent regulations to set the minimum standards for the installation of smoke, heat and carbon monoxide alarms, and those which my officials are currently developing to underpin electrical safety and to provide for much longer notice to quit periods. My consideration of what needs to happen in Phase 2 of Private Rented Sector reform will be focused on what further measures I can take to help ensure that the Private Rented Sector offers a good housing option for those that need it.My officials have already re-engaged with key stakeholders and reviewed what is happening in other jurisdictions as part of a policy refresh to inform the development of a draft plan for Phase 2 reform for my review. I will be meeting with officials in the near future to discuss this plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he is taking to support the Housing Executive in speeding up the process of moving tenants out of temporary housing and into permanent housing.
answer_text: The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to supporting applicants in temporary accommodation, including assisting with timeliness of move-on options, is outlined in the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation which can be found at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/fd320853-cf6e-4403-908f-d0a0b7dbbaea/Temporary-Accommodation-Strategic-Action-Plan-2022-27.pdf.  The Housing Executive will ensure that any change of tenancy works will be prioritised in any properties where an offer has been made to a household in temporary accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) his Department's course of action if a hospital patient is ready for discharge, but requires a home care package that is unable to be provided; (ii) his Department's course of action when a person living on their own home has their care package stopped or reduced to the point where their needs are not being met; and (iii) his efforts to ensure enough domiciliary care packages are available for everyone who needs one.
answer_text: (i): On the 29th September 2022 my department issued a letter to the 5 Health and Social Care Trusts outlining actions to be taken if a domiciliary care package has been offered as part of discharge planning from hospital but this is not available, the patient should be offered a place in an appropriate care home on a temporary basis if their needs cannot be met safely at home without some form of support.   Throughout the duration that an appropriate domiciliary care package is not available it was agreed that the care home fees would be waived.  This is extant.(ii): A prescribed domiciliary care package is based on an individual’s assessed need at that time.  There is evidence that for some patients when discharged to the familiar surroundings of their own home and with family and other informal supports they can make improvements in their recovery journey. All packages of domiciliary care are subject to regular professional review.  If an individual is assessed as no longer requiring a domiciliary care package or requires a reduction to their existing domiciliary care package, this will be actioned.   This in turn frees up domiciliary care hours that can then be utilised for other service users who require a domiciliary care package.(iii): There is work ongoing through the Social Care Collaborative Forum to identify how we can grow the domiciliary care workforce and also how we can maximize the current resources to reduce unmet need and put in place appropriate support for those who wish to remain living at home for as long as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to facilitate industry workshops to inform farmers, and others involved in the agriculture sector, of the latest scientific tools to help reduce emissions and farm more sustainably.
answer_text: As part of my Department’s Farm Support and Development Programme officials are currently developing new knowledge transfer and innovation programmes which will be delivered through a range of information, training, and group learning interventions. These programmes will communicate the latest scientific research and advice to support farmers to improve their technical efficiency and environmentally sustainability.	A scheme, Supporting Agri Professionals, is also being developed to raise the knowledge of professionals interacting with farm businesses to promote a wider understanding of the Farm Support and Development Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to move to a system where Housing Associations are public bodies but without their debt counting towards public debt, similar to what exists in England.
answer_text: Following the passage of the Housing (Amendment) Act (NI) in 2020, Registered Housing Associations in Northern Ireland were reclassified from the public back to the private sector. I have no plans to change the current system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to give the Housing Executive an increased input into where social housing is built.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) is responsible for the assembly and day to day management of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP), which is a three year rolling programme of planned social housing schemes based on the identification and analysis of housing need by geographical area.The formulation of the SHDP is shaped by the NIHE’s Strategic Guidelines which ensure that the Programme is distributed equitably across a range of programme policy areas and targets those areas of greatest social housing need. In managing the SHDP, the NIHE directs housing associations to identify and bring forward development opportunities in those areas of greatest unmet social housing need and administers Housing Association Grant on behalf of DfC to Housing Associations to facilitate the delivery of social housing.	Given the above, I have no plans to, nor is there a need to, give the Housing Executive an increased input.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the upcoming Programme for Government, whether they will include a Housing Executive retro-fitting programme which, similarly to their pilot programme, would see existing properties fitted with energy-saving upgrades.
answer_text: The Programme for Government is currently under development. We have previously spoken about a number of priorities including Childcare, Reducing Hospital Waiting Lists, Tackling Violence against Women and Girls, Special Educational Needs, Lough Neagh, Housing, and Developing a globally competitive economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to review the current system whereby Housing Executive rental income cannot be spent on investment in new social housing.
answer_text: NIHE’s rental income is used to pay for all the services that NIHE tenants require, including essential maintenance, improvements and investment in NIHE’s homes. At this time, I have no plans for such a review as you describe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the areas that have reached their wastewater capacity and are unable to be developed in terms of dwellings or businesses.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows: NI Water can often work with developers to try to identify a ‘no detriment’ solution, to achieve a connection.  NI Water has advised that there are currently 68 such areas across the North. These areas are listed below:-Monteith, Moneyslane, Desertmartin, Carrowclare, Killymuck, Redford, Douglas Bridge, Robinsonstown, The Loup, Florencecourt, Glasdrumman  (Annalong), Cabragh, Blackwatertown, Dunmore, Teemore, Maghera (Down), Mosside (Ballymoney), Clogh, Clarehill, Lurganare, Eglish (Dungannon), Darkley, Drumquin, Stranocum, Martinstown, Edenderry (Belfast), Lough Macrory South, Mullaghboy (Islandmagee), Garrison South, Killeen (Dungannon), Dervock, Gortin, Ravernet, Poyntzpass, Armoy Bush, Augher Blackwater,  Killygonlan, Clady (Tyrone), Lawrencetown, Ederney, Castle Archdale Integrated Constructed Wetlands, Tamnamore, Mountnorris, Clough East, Meigh, Clogher, Newtownbutler, Cloughey, Beragh, Aughnacloy, Drumbeg, Bellaghy, Belleek (Fermanagh), Killinchy, Hilltown, Glenavy, Markethill, Fivemiletown, Drumaness, Moneymore, Moy, Irvinestown, Rathfriland Drumlough, Waringstown, Greenisland, Larne, Dungannon and Cranfield.In addition, NI Water has also advised that there are 32 sub-catchments across 23 towns and villages where it is no longer able to provide a solution to developers to facilitate a new connection, given capacity issues in the sewerage network.   These are listed below:Templepatrick, Greenisland, Portadown, Belfast, Enniskillen, Carryduff, Ballymena, Dungannon, Cookstown, Downpatrick, Saintfield, Donaghadee, Dundonald, Newry, Newtownabbey, Maghera, Newtownards, Comber, Killyleagh, Kilkeel, Daraghcross, Seahill and Larne.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the work he has undertaken, and plans to undertake, with employers and industry leaders in relation to the employment of people with physical and learning disabilities, including those who had statements of special educational needs while at school; and (ii) for an update on (a) the proportion of those with physical and learning disabilities who have expressed a desire to work; and (b) the proportion of whom are currently employed.
answer_text: NISRA estimates that 41.7% of people with a disability are currently in employment. Of those who are economically inactive and classified as sick or disabled, an estimated 26,000 (20% of this classification group) express that they want to work under the right circumstances.My Department delivers a range of provision, in collaboration with many partners, to support disabled people to move closer and into employment. This includes Workable (NI), Access to Work (NI), and the Condition Management Programme; JobStart and its specialist pathway for disabled young people; our frontline Work Coaches and range of employer facing services; and provision commissioned through our Labour Market Partnerships, based on local need. My Department has also undertaken work to develop a new Disability and Work Strategy for Northern Ireland, involving extensive co-design alongside a wide and diverse range of stakeholders, including disabled people, the voluntary and community sector, and employers and their representative bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his plans for improving career and employment opportunities for those with special educational needs after they reach the age of 19.
answer_text: Every individual should be able to access opportunities to fulfil their potential, and my Department provides support through the Careers Service to help people to make fully informed choices at important transition points. For example, some young people may want to go from school into employment; the Department for Communities provides support for people with disabilities who are seeking or are in employment. Others may want to gain further skills and qualifications, and my Department has a range of provision and supports for young people with special educational needs through Further Education Colleges, Universities and across our vocational training provision.  However, I am aware that there are significant gaps in provision. I have therefore asked officials to review the current provision and to provide me with recommendations to improve support for young people with special educational needs.  I have also asked my officials to examine legislative protections and bring back advice as soon as possible.  It is my hope that practical supports and legislative protections can be strengthened in this mandate. This will require collaboration across a number of other Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the development of an active ageing strategy; and (ii) whether he will commit to securing the future of Linking Generations NI by making up the current shortfall in the organisation’s funding.
answer_text: I will be engaging with my officials in due course to understand work completed to date on developing a successor to the Active Ageing Strategy 2016-22 and to consider the next steps. Final decisions on the content and implementation of an Active Ageing Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.My Department has no funding relationship with Linking Generations NI and currently has no funding programmes open that the organisation may be eligible to apply to. However, they may wish to contact Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action (NICVA) who provide advice on securing funding alongside providing details of available grant funding through its grant tracker. Contact details can be accessed at https://www.nicva.org/services/fundraising

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the five most common reasons for those over the age of 65 to be admitted to hospital through an accident and emergency department in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to allow drivers, who passed their driving test from 1 January 1997, to tow a trailer with a maximum weight of 3,500kg without undergoing a separate test, similar to the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Legislation introduced in Britain on 16 December 2021 removed testing requirements for towing a trailer (BE entitlement), meaning that all drivers who passed a category B (car) driving test from 1 January 1997 are now entitled to tow trailers up to 3,500kg Maximum Authorized Mass (MAM).  This change was made in an effort to address the HGV driver shortage situation in England, Scotland and Wales by freeing up testing space.At that time testing was not seen as a significant reason for HGV driver shortages here. This position has not changed.In EU Member States BE testing is still a requirement, as it is here. Road safety continues to be of paramount importance and therefore I have no plans to introduce similar changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to data from the Family Practitioner Services General Dental Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023-24. to detail the reasons for the drop in NHS dental treatments being carried out on children and adults in the last ten years.
answer_text: The General Dental Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023-2024 do not provide an explanation for the trends in provision of dental treatment under the general dental services (GDS) over the past ten years. As such it is not possible to provide an evidence-based response to the question asked. The GDS is a demand-led service and the Strategic Performance and Planning Group (SPPG) of my Department are unable to control the volume of health service work versus the volume of private work provided by dentists. There will be a range of factors affecting the volume of dental treatments deliver under the GDS over the past ten years including the impact of the Covid pandemic, the types of treatment provided and the shift to a largely female workforce which has led to more part-time working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Family Practitioner Services General Dental Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023-24, how these statistics will be used to determine the level of unmet need in NHS dentistry.
answer_text: In addition to the annual publication of the General Dental Statistics, Family Practitioner Services in the Business Services Organisation also publishes quarterly updates. Officials monitor quarterly and annual publications in order to remain informed about trends. Officials also have access to real-time observation of trends via SPPG, which administers the operational aspects of General Dental Services, though this information will not have the accuracy of official statistics.My Department is aware that some areas of the country have historically lower registration and treatment rates. This is largely due to geography; areas with a low population density have a lower density of practices, which means that patients may have to travel further to access treatment. Some patients will be unwilling or unable to travel and will therefore not use the services.However, the commissioning of General Dental Services is undertaken on a regional basis, rather than within HSC Trust boundaries.Neither does the Department commission Health Service places in practices, most of which offer a mixture of Health Service and private dentistry. General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and as such are not required to register new patients. The Department does not have a role in deciding the location of new practices as this is a business decision made by practice owners.In order to provide Health Service dental care, dental practitioners must complete a year of Foundation Training in a Health Service practice in Northern Ireland or NHS practice elsewhere in Great Britain. The Northern Ireland Medical & Dental Training Agency have worked to improve the geographical distribution of Dental Foundation Training places. As a result, there will be 7 fully funded Health Service training placements in the west of the province in 2024/25, compared with just 1 in 2023/24.Despite registration rates remaining relatively high across the region, with over two-thirds of the Northern Ireland population registered with a practising health service dentist, I am aware that some practices are reducing their level of Health Service commitment.For these reasons, the focus of my Department recently has been on improving access to General Dental Service in general, and particularly for high-need patients, and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices while reform is progressed. This has resulted in the £9.2 million additional investment for 2024/25, which was announced on 27 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the work that (i) has gone into; and (ii) is going into (a) determining the features of land bordering rivers, lakes and streams that places them at greater risk of eutrophication, for instance, land use, size and any other factor; and (b) working with the owners of those more at risk in reducing the threat level.
answer_text: My Department continues to deliver a number of measures aimed at improving water quality in catchment areas. This includes a review of the current Nutrients Action Programme (NAP), which aims to improve the use of nutrients on farms and, as a result, improve water quality. My Department is also delivering the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, which provides farmers with detailed analysis of soil nutrients, acidity (pH), and run off risk maps. These maps identify areas at high risk of nutrient loss during rainfall events and help farmers to identify field areas, which may benefit from interventions, such as buffer strips, to minimise nutrient run off into water courses.  The Environmental Framing Scheme (EFS) offers five options aimed at improving water quality, with the aim of having these at placed in the right location to improve water quality. As part of a complex monitoring programme a recent published study has shown that the measures under EFS can help to reduce the causes of Eutrophication of our rivers.NIEA also provides funding to not-for-profit organisations and councils through its annual Water Quality Improvement Strand of the Environment Fund. Grantees work with land owners, putting in place measures like fencing to prevent livestock access to water bodies, leaky dams to 'slow the flow' and riparian planting and buffers to prevent nutrient run-off into waterways.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Family Practitioner Services General Dental Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023-24, how the data on the geographical variance of the number of NHS dental treatments being carried out will be used to inform policy to ensure the availability of NHS dentists across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In addition to the annual publication of the General Dental Statistics, Family Practitioner Services in the Business Services Organisation also publishes quarterly updates. Officials monitor quarterly and annual publications in order to remain informed about trends. Officials also have access to real-time observation of trends via SPPG, which administers the operational aspects of General Dental Services, though this information will not have the accuracy of official statistics.My Department is aware that some areas of the country have historically lower registration and treatment rates. This is largely due to geography; areas with a low population density have a lower density of practices, which means that patients may have to travel further to access treatment. Some patients will be unwilling or unable to travel and will therefore not use the services.However, the commissioning of General Dental Services is undertaken on a regional basis, rather than within HSC Trust boundaries.Neither does the Department commission Health Service places in practices, most of which offer a mixture of Health Service and private dentistry. General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors and as such are not required to register new patients.
