The Department does not have a role in deciding the location of new practices as this is a business decision made by practice owners.In order to provide Health Service dental care, dental practitioners must complete a year of Foundation Training in a Health Service practice in Northern Ireland or NHS practice elsewhere in Great Britain. The Northern Ireland Medical & Dental Training Agency have worked to improve the geographical distribution of Dental Foundation Training places. As a result, there will be 7 fully funded Health Service training placements in the west of the province in 2024/25, compared with just 1 in 2023/24.Despite registration rates remaining relatively high across the region, with over two-thirds of the Northern Ireland population registered with a practising health service dentist, I am aware that some practices are reducing their level of Health Service commitment.For these reasons, the focus of my Department recently has been on improving access to General Dental Service in general, and particularly for high-need patients, and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices while reform is progressed. This has resulted in the £9.2 million additional investment for 2024/25, which was announced on 27 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how the change from tax credits to Universal Credit, and the application process for free dental treatment, has impacted the number of people registered as eligible for free dental treatment in the last five years.
answer_text: The Help with Health Costs (HwHC) scheme provides individuals, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for Health and Social Care (HSC) treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist. A person is either automatically passported to the HwHC scheme if they are in receipt of certain benefits or they have to apply through the Department’s Low Income Scheme. The legislation does not currently make provision for the automatic passporting to the HwHC scheme for eligible Universal Credit (UC) claimants and therefore, legislative changes are required. Generally speaking, as the associated legislative changes have not yet been made, there should be no impact on the overall number of people eligible for help with health costs following the introduction of UC. However, there has been an impact on the method by which eligible UC claimants currently receive entitlement for help with health costs. An interim measure is in place for individuals who have migrated from a legacy benefit on to UC, they have to apply through the Department’s Low Income Scheme to have their eligibility assessed and confirmed prior to treatment. The scheme is administered by the Department for Communities. The interim measure is not an automatic process. It is paper based, and individuals must complete an application form to apply for an annual certificate for help with health costs. Currently, with the exception of having to apply manually, my Department do not expect there to be any significant change in eligibility for UC claimants receiving help with health costs.However, I recognise that the interim measure has obvious implications for some of our most vulnerable people and it has been criticised due to low public awareness and for placing an additional burden on individuals in society. It is clear that there is an urgent need to update our legislation to reinstate automatic passporting for help with health costs to those eligible. A short consultation is required which will outline the potential options that my Department is considering before proceeding to update the legislation. My Department will seek to maintain, as closely as possible, the caseload of recipients who are eligible for help with health costs, balancing the continued protection of the health of members of society who need additional support without adding pressures on HSC budgets. My officials intend to have the consultation and other necessary work completed to enable the legislation to be updated in the autumn of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the General Pharmaceutical Services for Northern Ireland Annual Statistics 2023/24, to detail the work that (i) is being done; and (ii) is planned to explore and address the link between ill health and deprivation levels.
answer_text: The link between ill health and deprivation is well established and reducing health inequalities remains a significant challenge.These inequalities arise primarily because of inequalities in the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work and age. Many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health. In fact research has shown that only 20% of health outcomes are driven by the clinical care we receive, with the rest related to the socio-economic and physical environment in which people live and a range of health and lifestyle behaviours.Making Life Better (MLB) is the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled and supported to lead healthy lives.MLB is supported by a range of public health strategies which focus on specific issues, such as substance use, suicide prevention, tobacco use and obesity prevention. The Public Health Agency (PHA) has a central role in delivery of actions identified in these strategies and targets action in specific areas where health inequalities are most prominent in our society. MLB is further complemented by the Integrated Care System for NI (ICSNI) which adopts both a population health and outcomes-based approach.Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department and the PHA continue to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally.You will be aware that health inequalities are one of my main priority areas as Health Minister and consideration is currently being given to a targeted, place-based approach to help address this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work that is planned to explore the benefits of non-pharmaceutical treatments of depression compared to pharmaceutical treatments, including potential side-effects.
answer_text: Although there are no current plans to directly compare the efficacy of pharmaceutical and non-pharmaceutical mental health treatments, the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 fully recognises the benefits of non-pharmaceutical supports, for example talking therapies and social prescribing, for individuals struggling with their mental health, including those impacted by depression.For example, Actions 5 and 19 respectively commit to expanding hubs for talking therapies and to further embedding psychological therapies across all steps of care. Preparatory work has been undertaken to review existing models and develop draft proposals for a new talking therapies hub model. However, additional resource will be required to take forward these proposals.Action 4 of the Strategy commits to the provision of social wellbeing supports, such as social prescribing, as interventions for those struggling with their mental health. Additionally, Action 16 recognises the importance of Recovery Colleges, already well established in each Health and Social Care Trust, in ensuring a recovery focus, while Action 17 commits to fully integrating the community and voluntary sector in community-based mental health services delivery.Subject to availability of future funding, delivery of all these actions will ensure that a full range of non-pharmaceutical options is available to promote good mental health and aid recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his plans for enabling and supporting areas outside Belfast to engage in, and benefit from, the seven economic sectors he identified for growth at the Digital DNA Conference.
answer_text: I have made regional economic balance one of my core priorities and I recognise the essential role of industry in enhancing the vibrancy of local economies. The sectors which have been identified as our most innovative, productive and internationally focused already represent a significant geographic spread across the north of Ireland. The sectoral action plans are designed to help these sectors grow at scale and ensure the benefits of that growth will be felt across the region. Key themes running through each of the plans include clustering and collaboration. Clustering is key in supporting regional economic development and actions are designed to foster and enhance the necessary levels of collaboration in order to maximise their impact. In autumn 2024 my department will complement the sectoral action plans through the launch of a subregional economic action plan, which will set out our subregional priorities and objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the General Pharmaceutical Services for Northern Ireland Annual Statistics 2023/24, (i) to detail any work planned to better understand the reasons for the more than 10 per cent difference in the proportion of females being prescribed anti-depressants compared to males; and (ii) for his assessment of how a better understanding of this will improve a greater prevention of depression among women.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the General Pharmaceutical Services for Northern Ireland Annual Statistics 2023/24, published on 27 June 2024, which provide a comprehensive overview of the use of prescriptions in Northern Ireland.  The statistics indicate that the proportion of the overall population currently taking anti-depressants represent 25.5% of females and 15.3% of males. It is noted that the highest single cohort being prescribed anti-depressants (39.1%) are women aged 45 – 64.There are no current plans for a formal analysis of the underlying causes of the disparity between anti-depressant prescriptions for males and females. However, it is recognised that there may be several underlying reasons for a higher proportion of females, such as caring responsibilities, menopause or more willingness to present for mental health support. The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) 2021-2031 places a strong emphasis on early intervention for, and prevention of, mental ill health. The Early Intervention and Prevention Action Plan (EI&PAP) recognises that incidences of mental ill health are higher amongst females and commits to tackling this inequality. Although the EI&PAP and MHS seek to build upon and improve services for the whole Northern Ireland population, it is recognised that targeted interventions may sometimes be appropriate, including to those individuals and families most at risk.Some examples of the ongoing work in relation to female mental health include the establishment of perinatal mental health teams in each HSC Trust and my Department’s commitment, subject to budget affordability, to establish a Mother and Baby Unit.In parallel, work is being progressed by the Department to develop a Women’s Health Action Plan, which is currently in a research and data-gathering phase. Although the action plan is not specific to mental health, mental health stakeholders (amongst others) will be engaged in its development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the General Pharmaceutical Services for Northern Ireland Annual Statistics 2023/24, to detail the reasons why the ingredient cost per prescription is nearly 30 per cent higher in Northern Ireland than in England and Wales.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the General Pharmaceutical Services for Northern Ireland Annual Statistics 2023/24, published on 27 June 2024, which provide a comprehensive overview of the use of prescriptions in Northern Ireland. The statistics indicate that the average cost per prescription item in Northern Ireland was £11.05 in 2023/24, compared with £9.01 in England and £8.15 in Wales. Northern Ireland’s cost per item was 23% higher than in England and 36% higher than in Wales. It should be noted that there are some differences in prescription dispensing and processing in Northern Ireland compared to other UK regions, e.g. in NI there is no outpatient dispensing. Varying prescribing practices may also affect the interpretation of UK comparisons and the figures may not be directly comparable across the UK due to these differences.  An example of this relates to prescribing interval and how it appears to be shorter in Wales than other UK countries resulting in lower dose units prescribed per prescription item in Wales and correspondingly higher items per head statistics for Wales. This impacts the average cost per item data for Wales by lowering it to a greater extent than other UK regions.   Other factors that may contribute to the higher number of prescription items in Northern Ireland include increased waiting list times, population trends, the number of people living with illnesses, deprivation levels and cultural and societal issues.My Department is currently undertaking policy development on a new ‘Valuing Medicines’ strategy, which will aim to inform improvements in the use of medicines within Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland to ensure they add value to health, are cost effective and sustainable. The Department is working closely with stakeholders and health service staff including GPs and Community Pharmacists, with both a steering group and departmental working group established to consider a number of strategies to improve the cost-effective use of medicines in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans he has to work with (i) banks; (ii) Post Offices; and (iii) other stakeholders to facilitate the creation of more banking hubs in East Londonderry.
answer_text: I held a meeting on 1 May 2024 with senior representatives of the local banks and building societies, LINK and Cash Access UK, where I urged them to maintain access to vital banking services in the North.I emphasised the importance of quickly rolling out further banking hubs here in the North as it is disappointing that only one banking hub, in Kilkeel, has opened to date. I understand from that meeting there are plans for four new banking hubs to open here. This includes a hub in your constituency in Portrush, where Cash Access UK indicated it is due to open in early 2025. I will continue to press on the sector to roll out banking hubs at pace. My officials are also engaging with the Consumer Council who collaborate with LINK in identifying further areas that should be considered for a banking hub here.I intend to hold a further round table discussion in the Autumn to follow up on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the impact of road safety advertising in reducing the number of deaths on our roads; and (ii) to detail the amount his Department has spent on such advertising in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Statistics demonstrate that over 95% of road traffic collisions where someone is killed or seriously injured is caused by human error/poor decision making. That means the majority of road deaths and serious injuries are avoidable. The errors are mainly, careless/inattention, drink or drug driving, excess speed for the conditions or the road and in the event of a crash not wearing a seatbelt. For this reason, my Department’s road safety campaigns focus on influencing road user behaviour leading to better choices by road users and they play a significant role in joint efforts by all road safety partners in reducing the number of people killed on roads here. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase. Last month, I launched the ‘RoadWise Up’ digital advertising campaign and road safety messaging is also promoted by  my Department on social media platforms, primarily through ShareTheRoadToZero.com I would encourage everyone to endorse and share this messaging, and to take the road safety pledge.The amount my Department has spent on road safety advertising in each of the last five years is detailed below. The amount for 2023/24 is inclusive of sponsorship funding provided by the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership. YEAR                   TOTAL SPEND2019/20             £987,767.972020/21             £1,387,647.892021/22             £1,472,397.092022/23             -2023/24             £476,000.00It has been well documented, that due to financial pressures my department was unable to allocate a budget for road safety advertising for financial years 2022/23 and a limited budget in 2023/24

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what plans he will put in place to reverse the decline of the number of adults registered with an NHS dentist; and (ii) , in relation to the General Dental Services Statistics 2023/24 that states 66 per cent of adults are registered with an NHS dentist, for his assessment of whether oral cancers are less likely to be discovered at an early and more treatable stage.
answer_text: Between 2014 and 2021 the percentage of the population registered with a dentist remained stable at around 64%. This increased to 70% by 2023, primarily because the decision was made to extend patient registrations automatically due to the impact of the pandemic.  Whilst there has been a recent decrease in total registrations to 68%, this is still higher than at any time pre-pandemic.Despite registration rates remaining relatively high, with over two-thirds of the Northern Ireland population registered with a practising health service dentist, I am aware that some practices are reducing their level of Health Service commitment.To further support practices and patients, my Department has introduced measures to ensure that patients in need can receive treatment, while options for longer term reform are developed.In March 2024, in addition to the 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package.  This includes:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Cases of oral cancer are rising across the UK and unfortunately the majority of cases are diagnosed in stages III and IV.  Opportunistic oral cancer screening during the oral clinical examination will help identify lesions of concern, but one of the most important factors for early diagnosis of mouth cancer is self-examination at home.  Many people who are most at risk do not routinely engage with healthcare services and will only attend a dentist or doctor when they are experiencing pain or other symptoms.The Department continues to work with HSC Trusts to ensure that red flag referrals are seen as quickly as possible. According to the most recently published waiting time statistics (cancer waiting times October – December 2023) 95.6% of head and neck cancers were treated within 31 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to facilitate the creation of more carers' hubs, similar to that starting at the Roe Valley Arts and Cultural Centre, Limavady.
answer_text: Buildings-based day care for people with learning disabilities are typically delivered by HSC Trusts with staff under Agenda for Change Terms. Community-based Day Opportunities are typically provided by the independent sector; therefore, my Department does not set the rate of pay.The current rate of pay in Adult Learning Disability Day Centres, depends on the band of the staff member. Most Health & Social Care HSC posts are ‘banded’ as part of Agenda of Change, depending on the tasks required and level of responsibility across a number of core and specific dimensions. Health and Social Care (HSC) members in Northern Ireland voted to accept a pay offer from the Department of Health for 2023/24. Payment of the 2023/24 pay award for all Agenda for Change HSC Staff was split with staff receiving the non-consolidated pay award of 5% in June. Payment of the non-consolidated award of £1505 for 2023/24 will follow in August.  Community-based Day Opportunities are funded by Trusts as ‘community & voluntary sector’ contracts. In March 2024, my Department announced a 3.11% uplift to these contracts to account for changes in the National Living Wage.  I recently approved a further uplift of 0.96% which effectively increased the 2024/25 interim uplift to be applied to 2023/24 contract rates, to a revised interim uplift of 4.07% in respect of these contracts from 1 April 2024.  It should be noted that this interim uplift for 2024/25 may need to be revisited in line with the element of the uplift which tracks the HSC’s Agenda for Change pay awards..My Department and HSC Trusts remain committed to expanding community-based Day Opportunities to provide people with learning disabilities with more meaningful day activities and greater choice and control throughout their lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to develop or improve legislation on tackling drug use in city centres and town centres.
answer_text: The legal framework relating to the use of drugs, including the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 and the Psychoactive Substances Act 2016, is reserved to the UK Government. The PSNI lead on enforcement of this legislation with the support partners such as Border Force.The Department of Health (DoH) lead on drug policy and the development of public health initiatives to address problematic substance use in Northern Ireland but, recognising the complexities of addressing this issue, input from key partners is critical to success. The Department of Justice (DoJ) is a member of the DoH-led Substance Use Strategy Programme Board which helps to drive forward and oversee the implementation of the 10-year strategy aiming to tackle the harms caused by substance use. There are a number of actions within the strategy that my Department is either directly or indirectly involved in delivering. Another important focus for my Department is the promotion and support of preventative approaches to substance use and the harm and vulnerabilities associated with addiction. Multi-Agency Support Hubs, part-funded by my Department, work at a local level to address vulnerabilities, including addiction, that can bring individuals into the criminal justice system. Support Hubs are in operation in all eleven council areas and each Hub helps vulnerable people get access to the right support, at the right time, from the right organisations within their local area.Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by the DoJ and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, also deliver local interventions to address harm and vulnerability. This includes Belfast PCSP funding of the Street Injectors Support Service (SISS) which targets the impact of discarded injecting equipment and delivers proactive outreach, as well as providing visible reassurance to the public and City Centre business owners.The Belfast PCSP contributes funding to the Complex Lives project which provides an integrated whole system response to support people caught in a cycle of homelessness, substance misuse, offending behaviour, and poor physical and mental health.Other PCSP projects include awareness raising around substance use, the funding of counselling services, and provision of Remove All Prescription and Illegal Drugs (RAPID) bins which provide for the disposal of all types of prescription and illegal drugs from the local community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail any plans she has to review legislation on the purchase and use of BB guns; and (ii) for her assessment of how current legislation is implemented.
answer_text: (i)	There are currently no plans to review the legislation on the purchase and use of BB guns in isolation, but this will be kept under review as part of a wider plan that my Department has to review the Northern Ireland legislative framework for firearms.  Any proposed change to legislation will require full public consultation.(ii)	A BB or pellet gun is often categorised as a low powered air gun and may also be regarded as an imitation firearm.  They are not required to be held on a firearm certificate, if their kinetic energy is less than one joule.  There is already significant regulation in place on the control of these items in Northern Ireland.It is an offence for a person under the age of 18 to purchase an imitation firearm.  It is also an offence for a person to sell an imitation firearm to someone under the age of 18.  Police have the power to seize and detain an imitation firearm if they have reasonable grounds for suspecting there is danger to public safety or to the peace.A person under the age of 17 may not purchase an air gun (those with a kinetic energy in excess of one joule) and a person under 14 may not possess an air gun unless supervised by someone who is aged 21 or over.Therefore, I am satisfied that there is sufficient legislation, particularly with regard to the age at which a person may possess or purchase such items and the age of a person to whom they may be sold.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent on private ambulances in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he plans to improve animal welfare in animal pounds and kennelling facilities.
answer_text: The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 allows District Councils to establish and maintain dog pounds and to make arrangements with Department approved kennelling facilities for their use. Kennels or Boarding Establishments are licenced under the Welfare of Animals Act 1972, with both pounds and kennels subject to the welfare rules within the robust Welfare of Animals Act 2011.	The modernisation of some of the more dated pieces of animal welfare legislation is an area I wish to address going forward and I will continue to engage with key stakeholder organisations in the time ahead which will assist me in setting the animal welfare priorities for my Department. However, my capacity to enact legislation is constrained by both the resources available to me and the time remaining in this current mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the work he is engaged in to develop and improve licensing systems, regulations and requirements for those operating animal services, including (i) rehoming; (ii) boarding; (iii) walking; (iv) day-care; (v) breeding; and (vi) grooming.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward legislation on a range of important animal welfare issues but I am also mindful of what can be delivered within the resources available to my Department and the time remaining in this mandate.Nevertheless, I will continue to engage with stakeholder organisations in the time ahead which will assist me in setting the key animal welfare priorities for the remainder of the mandate, and I am happy for this to include exploring the merits of introducing new licensing and regulatory requirements for those facilitating such animal services.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the date when the term of the current Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland comes to an end; and (ii) for an update on the recruitment process and progress of finding his replacement.
answer_text: The recruitment of a Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland is a matter for the First Minister and deputy First Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any discussions he has had with the Earl of Shaftesbury in relation to the potential transfer of ownership of the bed of Lough Neagh; and (ii) his plans to find or develop a body to whom this ownership can be passed.
answer_text: I met the Earl of Shaftesbury on 21 February 2024 and discussed a range of issues in relation to Lough Neagh including ownership.  Following his recent comments on the potential to transfer ownership of the Lough into a charity or Community Trust model, I have written to the Earl requesting a meeting to explore this further.  I welcome the Lough Neagh Heritage Resilience Project currently underway, led by the Lough Neagh Partnership, which is considering the future management of Lough Neagh including ownership.  I believe that it is appropriate for this work to be undertaken whilst also personally engaging with Earl of Shaftesbury
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work he plans to undertake with the Minister of Education in relation to addressing self-harm among young people.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) commissions the Self-harm Intervention Programme (SHIP) under the Protect Life 2 Strategy.   The PHA is currently in the final stages of re-tendering this service for a further three-year period.  SHIP provides psychological interventions for people who self-harm in line with NICE guidelines and is available to both adults and young people aged 11 years and older.    Children under 11 years who are engaging in self-harm are not eligible for SHIP and should have their needs met by other services that they are involved with.Currently school nurses and school counsellors cannot refer directly to SHIP but they do liaise with the GP or CAMH teams so that the child can be assessed and if SHIP is appropriate for their needs a referral to SHIP can be made by these professionals.  The PHA have developed a strong partnership approach with Education colleagues in helping to meet the emotional health and wellbeing needs of children and young people. This includes a number of pilot projects under the Department for Education's Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which can also provide support for self harm. The extension of the RISE NI service into Key Stage 2 aims to specifically support the emotional health and wellbeing needs of children and young people. The Text-a-Nurse service provides a secure and confidential text messaging service for young people aged 11-19, which allows them to easily and anonymously get in touch with a school nurse for advice and support they may need. There is also enhanced school nursing and regional mental health awareness programmes within schools. Mental Health First Aid training is also delivered in schools to staff to support a person who is developing a mental health problem or who is currently in a mental health crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on his facilitation to install more electric vehicle charge points; and (ii) to detail the stakeholders he has, and will, engage with in relation to this issue.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the charge point operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters such as procedural arrangements for planning and installation requirements. Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’ (ORCS), which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils and has recently published its ORCS tender in the eTendersNI portal for charge point operators to provide 127 charge points across those Council areas. Individual Councils are best placed to advise on the number of additional charge points this will provide in their area.My Department established an Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Task-Force in December 2021 to consider the EV Infrastructure requirements in the north and to help set out a clear action plan to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network. The Task-Force aims to meet quarterly, is chaired by the Department for Infrastructure, and includes representatives from the following bodies or organisations: Confederation of British Industry Consumer Council for Northern Ireland Department for the Economy Derry City and Strabane District Council (lead of local council consortium) Electric Vehicle Association Northern Ireland Electricity Supply Board NIE Networks Utility Regulator Latest statistics show there are 564 publicly available EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 45% in the total number of available EV charge points since April 2023. Individual charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for future charge point installations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the implementation of new relationships and sexuality education laws in Northern Ireland’s schools; and (ii) to detail the list of stakeholders his Department will engage with in relation to this.
answer_text: The Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023, introduced by the Secretary of State were effective from 1 July 2023. Guidance to support the changes made to the curriculum was issued to all post-primary schools on 1 January 2024. The Secretary of State’s regulations also placed a duty on my Department to publish a report by 1 September 2026 on the implementation of the changes. Officials will take this forward in due course and this will include engagement with stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in order to reduce the fees paid by his Department for private taxis taking patients to and from appointments, to detail the cross-departmental work he plans with the Department for Infrastructure to ensure better support for community transport services.
answer_text: The eligibility criteria for the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme are published on the NI Direct website:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/help-health-costs A person is either automatically entitled to a refund if they are in receipt of certain benefits or they have to apply through the Low Income Scheme. A patient may get back all or some of their travel costs depending on which certificate they have been given. Guidance for refunding travel expenses refers to ‘reasonable travel expenses’ and advises that travel costs should be calculated on the basis of the cheapest form of public transport available to the patient. Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts administer the Hospital Travel Cost Scheme on behalf of the Department of Health. HSC Trusts are responsible for informing patients of their entitlement, checking the appropriate travel costs and refunding patients travel costs to which they are entitled.If public transport is unavailable or impractical (perhaps the patient can't get to their appointment on time or their mobility is restricted), the patient is advised to contact the hospital in advance of their appointment to check if their new travel arrangements are allowed under the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme.In 2023, the Department for Infrastructure established a cross-departmental working group on Community Transport, which included active participation from the Department of Health. The working group aims:to maximise the benefits of the current services provided by community transport using existing resources and explore whether any more resources are available; andto maximise the future potential of community transport.My officials have provided an overview of HSC Transport to better define the boundaries between HSC, public transport and Community Transport. Officials are cognisant that people use Community Transport to access HSC and other public services, with further exploration required to understand the drivers behind usage.The group have collaboratively developed recommendations for how this work can be further progressed, subject to a Ministerial meeting across relevant departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to progress the adoption of Lucy’s Law.
answer_text: I intend to deliver Lucy’s Law within the lifetime of this Assembly mandate.My officials are developing options for my consideration. I will carefully consider all these options and then decide on the best one for appropriately regulating third-party sales of pups and kittens in Northern Ireland. Any proposals will be publicly consulted on.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has undertaken, or plans to undertake, an impact assessment of the impact on those older people who are moved into a nursing home or a care home due to a domiciliary care package being unavailable.
answer_text: My officials have established a Project Steering Group which has been set up to review Home Care Services specifically. One of the sub-groups within the Project Steering Group has been tasked to examine the impact waiting in a care home or at home on a new package of domiciliary care is having on the service user.I recognise that waiting in a care home for a domiciliary care package is not an ideal situation and I understand the distress and de-conditioning impact this may have on individuals.Through the Social Care Collaborative Forum my officials are working with the Statutory and Independent Sector to identify ways to grow the domiciliary care workforce and to maximize the capacity we currently have.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration he has given to better financially support students who achieve places at highly ranked universities in Great Britain but for whom the fees are prohibitively expensive.
answer_text: For academic year 2024/25 students domiciled here can apply for a tuition fee loan of up to £9,250 to pay for the undergraduate tuition fees charged at publicly funded universities in England, Scotland and Wales. Undergraduate home fees will be capped at £9,250 in England, Scotland and Wales in academic year 2024/25.The tuition fee loan available to postgraduate students was increased to £6,500 in academic year 2023/2024 following a review and consultation on the level of postgraduate tuition fee loans.Since I came into post I have been clear about my wish to strengthen the support provided to students and I will be considering a number of options going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the maintenance loan provided to students who are unable to study their chosen course in Northern Ireland and therefore study in GB where (a) fees are higher; and (b) their peers in GB receive loans closer to the cost of studying; and (ii) what consideration he has given to increasing the student loan amount for students who are unable to study their chosen university course in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Prior to my taking office, the higher education student support package for this year and the coming academic year 2024/25 had already been determined. Currently eligible students from here studying in Britain may be eligible for a tuition fee loan of up to £9,250, a maintenance grant of up to £3,475 and a maintenance loan of up to £6,776, or up to £9,492 if studying in London.Since I came into post, I have been clear about my wish to strengthen the support provided to students and I will be considering a number of options going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to (i) strengthen legislation; and (ii) undertake a sentencing review, for perpetrators of animal cruelty.
answer_text: I do not have any plans to (i) strengthen legislation; nor (ii) undertake a sentencing review, for perpetrators of animal cruelty, as policy responsibility for the welfare of animals sits with DAERA. Any decision to strengthen legislation or undertake a sentencing review for animal cruelty offences under the Welfare of Animals (Northern Ireland) Act 2011 is, therefore, a matter for DAERA. However, I have confirmed that I am open to discussing with him how best my Department can support his, if a policy decision is made by his Department to strengthen legislation or undertake a sentencing review for animal welfare offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on work that (i) has been carried out; and (ii) is planned with (a) the PSNI; (b) government departments; and (c) other organisations to assist in the prevention of livestock theft.
answer_text: My department does not have a role in preventing livestock theft.  While there is a specific legal obligation to notify my Department of the theft of cattle, and the circumstances of any theft, this is for the purpose of ensuring our identification and registration records are up to date, to allow for the maintenance of the Northern Ireland food traceability regime.  The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is responsible for enforcing legislation in relation to the theft of livestock.  My Department has a strong working relationship with the PSNI and will continue to cooperate with the PSNI in respect of any relevant investigations of livestock theft, as necessary. My Department is also happy to cooperate, where appropriate, with any other organisation and government department with an intertest in the prevention of livestock theft.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to develop a programme similar to England’s Education Health and Care plan to help the future education or training of young people with special educational needs.
answer_text: This matter is currently under consideration as part of the ongoing End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to increase the amount spent per-capita on mental health in Northern Ireland in order to bring it closer in line to that spent in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: Given the budget that my department has been allocated this year it is not possible to make firm plans to increase overall spending on HSC services. However, it is my intention to look at longer term planning for the HSC as a whole to consider how best services can be delivered to bring them onto a sustainable footing and enable unmet need to be addressed. Planning for mental health spending will be an integral part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on any further work planned to connect the Ulster Canal with Lough Neagh, and therefore the lower Bann, in order to open up this area to boating tourism.
answer_text: In July 2007, it was agreed by the North/South Ministerial Council (NSMC) to extend Waterways Ireland’s remit to include responsibility for the restoration of the Ulster Canal from Upper Lough Erne to Clones, and following restoration, its management, maintenance, and development, principally for recreational purposes. The Ulster Canal Restoration project from Lough Erne to Clones is being constructed in three phases. Phase 1: Lough Erne to Castle Saunderson was completed in 2019Phase 2: Clones to Clonfad was completed June 2024Phase 3: Clonfad to Castlesaunderson: This final section will connect the 10km between Castle Saunderson and Clonfad and link the town of Clones by navigable waterway to the Erne System and onward to the wider waterway network. Phase 3 will be the largest and most challenging and work is already underway to ensure an application for renewed planning permission will be made later this year.Waterways Ireland are working with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) in Northern Ireland on the Fermanagh elements of Phase 3.  Estimated completion of all elements, subject to funding, is 2028/29.While there are no plans currently to expand the Ulster Canal restoration project to include a connection with Lough Neagh, DfI has expressed a keenness to move this project forward in line with the expressed commitment in the Stormont House Agreement, A Fresh Start: The Stormont House Agreement and Implementation Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the rate at which bovine TB skin tests provide false negative results; (ii) the impact this has on the spread of bovine TB; and (iii) how this will influence a change in his Department’s bovine TB eradication programme.
answer_text: Tests for the identification of bovine TB (bTB) in cattle for use in Northern Ireland require the appropriate international approvals from the World Organisation for Animal Health and the European Union. The Comparative Intradermal Tuberculin Test (skin test) and the interferon gamma blood test (IFNg) are currently the only two such tests approved in European legislation (Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/689).  Bovine TB is a difficult disease to diagnose and no diagnostic test for it is perfect. The effectiveness of any diagnostic test is related to the sensitivity and specificity of the test.Sensitivity is the ability of a test to correctly identify an infected animal as positive, i.e. the higher the sensitivity of the test, the lower the probability of incorrectly classifying an infected animal as uninfected (a false negative result). The test can be interpreted at standard or severe interpretation. Studies carried out in Great Britain have shown that skin test sensitivity lies between 52% and 100% with an average of about 80% sensitivity at standard interpretation and slightly higher at severe interpretation. In practical terms this means that where infection is present in a herd, approximately 20% of TB-infected cattle can be missed by one round of skin testing using standard interpretation. Repeating the skin test and/or using severe interpretation increases the likelihood of detecting the infected animals in a herd. Specificity is the ability of a test to correctly identify an animal that is free from infection as negative, i.e. the higher the specificity, the lower the probability of classifying an uninfected animal as infected (a false positive result). The skin test is regarded as having a test specificity of 99.98%. This means that it is very rare that a non-infected animal will be wrongly classified as infected. If severe interpretation is used to increase the sensitivity of the test, the specificity is lowered, i.e. more false positive animals will be taken. That is why severe interpretation is not usually used in routine surveillance testing. The non-disclosure of infected animals is a recognised problem for eradication programmes across both the UK and Ireland. Non-disclosed infection poses a risk of spread within herds, and between herds, and recurrence of herd breakdowns. The risk of leaving residual infection in diseased herds can be mitigated to some extent by other methods including the use of severe interpretation, the removal of negative high risk animals, serial skin testing and the use of IFNg as an additional test. Severe interpretation is applied to all breakdown tests where bTB has been confirmed or where there is more than one reactor animal, and herds are subject to more frequent testing for up to a year post-derestriction. My Department’s vets are encouraged to consider if other animals in the herd pose an increased risk even if they have skin tested negative. Some breakdown herds will be offered the chance
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the recent bovine TB review, to detail (i) the terms of reference he has asked the Chief Veterinary Officer to complete in relation to developing a bovine TB strategy, including anticipated timelines; and (ii) with whom he will be engaging as part of this process.
answer_text: The review currently being undertaken by the Chief Veterinary Officer will examine all matters relating to bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) programme and policy, including the actions contained in the bTB Eradication Strategy.   This review will engage with a range of external stakeholders and departmental staff as well as considering best practice and issues in neighbouring jurisdictions.  The CVO keeps me regularly updated on progress. I anticipate that the review will result in a delivery plan to be in place from this autumn which will outline our approach to bTB eradication over the remainder of the mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how the effectiveness of differing approaches to bovine TB eradication in neighbouring jurisdictions informs his Department's work; and (ii) whether he will pursue a similar strategy as Wales, which does not use badger culls, due to its statistically greater effectiveness since 2012.
answer_text: As part of the Bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy developed by my department, the effectiveness of differing approaches to bTB eradication in neighbouring jurisdictions was considered in detail. 		My officials meet regularly with colleagues in neighbouring jurisdictions to discuss current issues, research and policy developments relating to bTB.  In March 2024, my department hosted the UK TB Liaison Group (TBLG) in Belfast, bringing together bTB officials from across the UK with officials from the Department of Fisheries, Agriculture and the Marine (DAFM) in the Republic of Ireland (RoI) also in attendance. My officials also meet separately with DAFM colleagues twice yearly to discuss bTB matters of mutual interest.    		The objective of these meetings is to share information and best practice, and, where appropriate, inform officials’ recommendations to their respective Ministers.  		The wildlife intervention element of the department’s bTB Eradiation Strategy was overturned in October 2023 following a judicial review taken by Wild Justice and the Northern Ireland Badger Group.  My officials are currently reflecting on the issues and gaps identified by the judicial review ruling in order to provide detailed advice on wildlife options for my consideration. 	Consequently, the Department does not have a wildlife intervention policy in place and this area remains policy in development which will require further public consultation. In relation to the approach in Wales, the Welsh Government is pursuing a badger vaccination policy alongside a range of measures to tackle the spread of the disease between cattle.  My officials are currently working to provide me with advice on proposals for a way forward for wildlife intervention, this will include an assessment on the Welsh approach along with the approaches in place in other neighbouring jurisdictions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools currently supplying students with free period products.
answer_text: My Department has provided funding for free period products in schools since September 2021, under the Period Dignity Pilot Scheme.  The scheme will conclude on 30 June 2024 to make way for extended, permanent provision under the Period Products (Free Provision) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (The Act).In April 2023, 88.3% (1,053) of pupils, responding to a CCEA survey on the pilot scheme, confirmed that free period products were available in their school.  Overall school engagement with the pilot scheme was excellent and is set out in the following table:SectorNumber of schoolsNumber of schools ordering through scheme supplier% Grammar736285%Secondary15013590%Special3737100%Total26023490%Adjusted *24823494%		               *Adjusted for 12 non-ordering boys’ schools796 (100%) of Primary Schools were provided with an allocation of free period products at the start of each academic year and a small number of post-primary schools made their own purchasing arrangements. These schools are not included in the above table.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department holds information on the countries that have successfully eradicated bovine TB through the use of the same Single Intradermal Comparative Cervical Tuberculin skin test as is used in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Tuberculin skin test is the main diagnostic test for bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) internationally approved for use by the World Organisation for Animal Health.  My department does not hold specific information on the use of this test in countries that have successfully eradicated bovine TB however, my officials have advised that this would have been the main diagnostic method for identifying bTB in those countries.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what measures he can take to address staff shortages in hospitality businesses along the North Coast.
answer_text: Question 8 - AQO 647/22-27I met with Hospitality Ulster earlier this month to help launch its guide to recruiting the over 50s, developed in partnership with Tourism NI. We discussed issues around staff recruitment and retention and how my Department can support the hospitality sector to have the right people with the right skills.Tourism NI delivers numerous interventions to support businesses recruit and retain good people. The most visible, the ‘Make It Here’ campaign, highlights the many positives of this sector. I am delighted to see many businesses sign up to the Wellbeing Promise, a commitment for hospitality employers to create a positive working environment.Furthermore, I have directed Tourism NI to develop an Employers Excellence Programme, supporting tourism & hospitality businesses create excellent workplaces offering good jobs.My Department is also progressing the creation of bespoke Tourism & Hospitality Academies.Last year, the Skill Up programme delivered “Get into Hospitality”, enabling participants to gain practical experience and an accreditation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to ensure the cost of farming essentials, such as seed, fertiliser and other goods and equipment, do not become prohibitively expensive for farmers, particularly family farmers.
answer_text: Input costs and farmgate prices change frequently.  These changes are mainly due to price pressures across world markets which are very much outside our control.  Consequently, my Department focuses effort on helping farmers mitigate cost and price pressures by improving productivity and efficiency.  The new agricultural support framework has been  designed to help farm businesses improve their productivity, resilience, and environmental sustainability. CAFRE continues to offer relevant training and support also.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will improve the support available for people transitioning to becoming carers for friends or family.
answer_text: My Department is committed to supporting carers. In 2023, following the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation, my Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The forum provides a formal mechanism to implement reforms to adult social care and in doing so supports opportunities for greater co-operation, co-production and joined up working between the Department, the social care sector, and across Government.The work of the Forum is being taken forward through a number of workstreams, which meet on a regular basis. A Supporting Carers Workstream, co-chaired by Carers NI and my Department, has been established with a workplan focused on improving support for unpaid carers. Whilst the workstream does not have a specific focus on individuals who are new or recently transitioning into unpaid care, its objectives include bringing forward recommendations which can help to improve support and recognition for all unpaid carers in contact with the health and social care. Work underway includes exploring options to improve information services for unpaid carers and improving identification and registration for carers. In addition, the workstream has recently established a Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group, chaired by the Department of Health. The group facilitates an integrated, cross government approach to supporting unpaid carers across society.We continue to work within the context of an extremely challenging financial environment. The work of the Social Care Collaborative Forum Supporting Carers Workstream is subject to confirmation of funding, and future implementation into service delivery of the workstream’s objectives will require prioritisation and planning, and longer-term budget stability. My Department will continue to support our carers, and we are committed to providing those supports both practical and financial for them, resources permitting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he is working with (i) developers; and (ii) wastewater management professionals to reduce the amount of wastewater entering sewerage systems to enable more buildings and properties to be built in areas where capacity is at or near its limit.
answer_text: My Department encourages developers and wastewater management professionals to use Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS), to help remove surface water from the sewer network and free up capacity for wastewater. This can also reduce flooding and pollution risks.  In addition, I frequently ask developers and wastewater management professionals to engage early with NI Water, through the Pre- Development Enquiry process, to try and find a resolution to any issues at an early stage, to help facilitate development, where possible.   NI Water has advised that within the current Price Control Period PC21 (2021/22 – 2026/27), it has a Storm Separation project to invest circa £32m to target the removal of approximately 22 hectares of impermeable area that discharges storm water into the foul/combined sewerage network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to update the 2019 traffic study of the road network surrounding the Riverside Retail Park, Coleraine, with a view to (i) assessing the impact of more recent changes to traffic flow in and out of the retail park; and (ii) being better placed to analysing the need for traffic lights at the Strand Road roundabout.
answer_text: The 2019 traffic study recorded the maximum delay experienced at the Strand Road Roundabout to be 5 minutes on the Castleroe Road with the majority of the other approaches experiencing delays of less than a minute. These finding are not considered to be excessive, nor unique, as most strategic traffic routes experience some congestion at peak times, particularly where the main road, which carries larger volumes of traffic, has priority over the lower trafficked minor roads. My officials consider that there has been no significant increase to these delays and have no immediate plans to undertake a new traffic study. However, the area will continue to be monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of Northern Ireland’s current food security; and (ii) how he will work to ensure this within future policy.
answer_text: (i) For many food commodities, production in Northern Ireland exceeds local consumption and hence the importance of export markets to the agri-food sector.  However, there are food products not produced in large quantities in Northern Ireland mostly due to climate conditions. Therefore, to meet the food needs of the Northern Ireland population, we also require imports of certain foods as well as imports of inputs such as feed, fertiliser and fuel to enable domestic production.  I am confident in Northern Ireland's food security due to a combination of a strong agri-food sector and robust supply chains with reliable trade partners.(ii)  	I am focused on ensuring longer term solutions to prevent crises and preserve our food security. I am keen to explore how we better utilise science and technology to inform future policies and ensure our food supplies are resilient.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any impact assessment carried out prior to the raising of the free-school meals threshold from £14,000 a year to £15,000 a year; and (ii) the number of families eligible for free school meals in each of the last five years.
answer_text: On 7 June I announced an interim rise in the income threshold for those applying for free school meals and uniform on the basis of being in receipt of Universal Credit. This interim rise has been implemented pending the completion of the review of the free school meals and uniform grant eligibility criteria and seeks to ensure that broadly the same number of children and young people are able to access these supports in the 24/25 academic year. All relevant impact assessments will be undertaken as part of the completion of the overall review.  The Department does not hold data on the number of families that are entitled to free school meals. Listed below are the number of pupils entitled to free school meals in the last five years taken from the school census:Academic YearNumber of pupils entitled to free school meals 19/2096,69720/2197,59221/2298,80422/2396,23123/2494,975

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she is working with (i) the PSNI; (ii) the British and Irish government; and (iii) overseas counterparts, to prevent and prosecute international scammers targeting older people.
answer_text: Scams targeting older people are particularly cruel, often preying on someone’s loneliness, vulnerability, fear, or kindness to gain access to their personal information and ultimately their money.  My Department helps to address harm and vulnerability caused by fraud and supports partnership working to tackle the threat of scams through its membership of the ScamwiseNI Partnership, which is chaired by the PSNI.  The Partnership was established eight years ago and now has over 45 partners who work collaboratively to provide valuable information and crucial advice to keep the public informed, aware and vigilant. It has an effective online presence which is managed by staff from the Office of the Commissioner for Older People (COPNI) and the Department of Justice uses its own social media channels (Twitter & Instagram) to highlight current scams and the steps people can take to protect themselves.  As part of its membership of ScamwiseNI, multi-national IT company Allstate has made its cyber safety sessions, including those focused on older adults, available to members of the public across Northern Ireland.ScamwiseNI launched its most recent campaign on 13 October 2023.  The six-month long campaign featured radio and newspaper advertisements, along with posters in public transport spaces, including bus shelters and inside buses.  Its aim was to help empower older people and those who are not regularly online to stay safe against scammers. In addition, in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, the Department provides annual funding of £4.5m to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), across each of the 11 council areas, who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level. That funding is utilised by the PCSPs across Northern Ireland to fund a range of locally identified projects and initiatives which include projects to help address scams and frauds as well as internet safety awareness raising initiatives and campaigns. For example, Lisburn & Castlereagh PCSP in partnership with PSNI delivered 20 sessions to community groups involving 705 pensioners during 2023-24. These sessions covered crime prevention including a wide range of scams including international scams. Similarly, Fermanagh and Omagh PCSP carried out 12 scam awareness presentations to elderly groups. In Derry and Strabane, Community Safety Wardens regularly cover scam awareness as part of their talks to older persons groups.  Also during 2023/24, the wardens attended 134 community engagement meetings with 1,567 residents attending. All PCSPs provide similar projects which target scam awareness, often with a focus on the elderly.Information and advice on preventing and reporting cyber crimes, and on the PSNI work to tackle fraud and scams is available in the Organised Crime Task Force Annual Report available at:  OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment 22-23 | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk)Tackling scams from int

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work he will undertake with the Housing Executive to ensure children’s human rights are not being breached by current temporary housing policies, which can result in children (i) being denied access to education due to a lack of school transport; (ii) being placed in unsuitable, uninhabitable or communal housing; and (iii) experiencing a deterioration in their mental health due to these conditions.
answer_text: My officials are working with the Housing Executive to identify alternative and more sustainable options to respond to the growing and unprecedented demand for temporary accommodation. The Chief Executive has established a Housing Solutions Task and Finish Group to address this issue and I have asked for regular updates on this work and the use of Temporary Accommodation. As the discharge of the homelessness duty is an operational matter for the Housing Executive I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is subject to Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act and the Human Rights Act in relation all its policies and functions. The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to addressing homelessness is outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 which was subject to an equality screening process which can be found at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/c674990a-3b42-450d-a7c3-337bdea94ccf/Homelessness-Strategy-Equality-Screening.pdf. The decision regarding the suitability of temporary accommodation offered in the discharge of the homelessness duty is made in accordance with the Housing (NI) Order 1988.  In determining suitability, Designated Officers within the Housing Executive will consider factors such as the individual circumstances and the needs of a given Applicant. The consideration of individual circumstances will include proximity to schools for families with children and the Housing Executive will seek to provide a placement as close as possible to facilities such as schools.It should be noted that while the Housing Executive will endeavour to provide a placement that as far as possible, meets the needs of the respective household, any provision of temporary accommodation will be dictated by availability on a given day.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the collaborative work that (i) has been undertaken; and (ii) is planned with the (a) PSNI; (b) Public Prosecution Service; and (c) other elements of the justice system, to ensure a joined-up and consistent approach to the treatment of women and girls who have experienced domestic or sexual violence.
answer_text: Collaboration is a critical to the development of informed, effective and fit for purpose policies and practices. My Department collaborates closely with the PSNI, PPS and other Criminal Justice organisations (CJOs) on a range of matters at strategic, policy and service delivery levels to ensure that there is a joined-up and consistent approach to the treatment of all victims of domestic and sexual abuse, including women and girls.  						At a strategic level this includes:			collaborative work with PSNI, PPS and other partners, to develop deliver the cross-Departmental 2016-23 Stopping Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse Strategy and associated annual action plans;  			collaborative work with PSNI, PPS and other partners to develop a new seven-year Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and initial action plan, which I hope to publish soon, in partnership with the Minister of Health; and 			ongoing collaborative work to implement the systemic reforms to the law and procedures in serious sexual offences that have been recommended by the Gillen Review. At a policy level this includes:engagement and collaboration with a range of criminal justice and wider partners to inform the development of legislation to tackle domestic and sexual violence, including most recently the introduction of a new domestic abuse offence and new offences and measures to tackle voyeurism and stalking; 		collaboration with PSNI to develop policy and guidance to support the delivery of the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS) which PSNI is responsible delivering.  Following evaluation of the scheme, my Department and the PSNI collaborated to update guidance, to reflect learning and recommendations from the evaluation report.  This included lowering the disclosure threshold from the risk of “serious harm” to “harm” and making changes to the process to make it more efficient, including reducing maximum timescales;  		collaboration with PSNI to inform the development of regulations to support the delivery of Operation Encompass; 		ongoing collaboration with a range of criminal justice and wider partners through the operation of Domestic Homicide Reviews, to examine cases where domestic abuse has resulted in death in order to help mitigate future risk by learning lessons and promoting best practice; 		ongoing collaboration with criminal justice and wider partners to consider and scope options for Barnahus-informed services in Northern Ireland, to enhance and coordinate wrap-around services to child victims of serious sexual offences; 		ongoing collaboration with PSNI and the Executive Office to inform the development of a PSNI-led campaign aimed at reducing unacceptable behaviour, sexual offending and gender-based violence; 		ongoing collaboration with criminal justice and wider partners to develop arrangements to pilot the concept of Pre-Recorded Cross Examination in serious sexual offence cases involving children; 		ongoing collaboration with criminal justice

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the discussions he has had with counterparts in the Republic of Ireland in relation to (i) the differing dog control laws between the two countries; and (ii) the action being taken to prevent dogs of breeds banned in Northern Ireland to enter this jurisdiction from the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I have been in contact with the Minister for Rural and Community Development in the Republic of Ireland (ROI), Heather Humphries TD, in relation to the XL Bully legislation I am bringing forward.  When in operation, the legislation will bring Northern Ireland (NI) into parity with the ROI in terms of the legal requirement for XL Bully type dogs to be on a lead and muzzled when in a public place.Minister Humphries has established a cross Governmental stakeholders working group to consider dog control, and in particular restricted breeds.  My officials have ongoing engagement with counterparts in the Department of Rural and Community Development and expect to meet in the coming months as the group identifies its priority actions.Anyone travelling from the ROI into NI with their XL Bully type dog will be subject to the legal restrictions and safeguarding measures my Department is planning to introduce.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting lists; and (ii) the average waiting times for children awaiting speech and language therapy, broken down by constituency; and (iii) his proposals for reducing these.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the plans he has to work with the Department of Health to introduce improved information and support resources in schools to identify, inform and support those who may be suffering from, or may be vulnerable to developing, eating disorders.
answer_text: The Education Authority provides a range of services to support pupils needing help facing difficulties that are impacting on their ability to engage with education, including those with eating disorders.  Supports are also available for pupils to access directly as part of the Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework, such as the Text-a-Nurse service.Whilst schools ensure the safety of pupils at school and raise concerns as necessary it is important that information and resources on eating disorders are informed by the Department of Health (DoH) and Public Health Agency.  My officials are working closely with DoH as it undertakes a review of the Healthy Child, Healthy Future Policy.  A key element of the review is to take account of the social and emotional wellbeing of children including those vulnerable to eating disorders.  The review document will provide guidance to School/Public Health Nurses in this respect as well as being a valuable resource available to schools.  This provides the opportunity to enhance connections between education and health in supporting the needs of our children and young people through early intervention and prevention. Further to this, the curriculum requires schools to provide learning opportunities for pupils in relation to physical and emotional health as part of personal development across the key stages of education and gives schools flexibility to cover different aspects of this learning area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he will improve the process and time taken to assess and administer enteral nutrition tubes for children, particularly when they are requested by their parents.
answer_text: This is a specialist clinical procedure and each assessment must be personal to each child.  Each assessment will therefore be patient specific.Each assessment is carried out by a multi-disciplinary team of clinical experts and they must ensure any decisions made are appropriate and safe for each child.  Where it is a deemed appropriate to then carry out this procedure the relevant child will be added to the waiting list for the procedure percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) and this will be managed through normal processes.There are just over 100 children currently on a waiting list for this procedure.  This is a complex procedure for children requiring additional support and because of this is it is only done at Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT).It must be noted though that the views and inputs of parents and carers are crucial in any decision-making process and this will be considered along with the appropriateness and safety of each clinical decision.As this is a specialist, clinical procedure we will be informed by clinical experts in this area on safe and appropriate improvements in this assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any plans he has to improve adherence to the timely payment of child support; (ii) any cross-departmental work that will be required; and (iii) what plans he has to improve temporary financial support for those parents who are not paid child maintenance regularly or on time.
answer_text: As part of my department’s commitment to improve the Child Maintenance Service in helping to secure more child maintenance for qualifying children, and by extension improve adherence to the timely payment of child maintenance, my department recently secured Executive approval to add the Child Support Enforcement Bill to the Executive’s Legislative Programme.  This Bill makes provision for the introduction of administrative liability orders, which is intended to reduce the time it takes for the Child Maintenance Service to initiate its enforcement powers.Once commenced by secondary legislation, the Bill’s provisions will allow the Child Maintenance Service to make an administrative liability order without requiring application to the Magistrates’ Court in Northern Ireland.The existing court-based liability order process takes on average 22 weeks.  It is estimated that the new administrative liability order process could be substantially shortened to 6 weeks.Officials within my department are working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions, the Department for Justice, the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service and the Enforcement of Judgments Office on the Bill, all with a view to developing an appropriate secondary legislation package.My department will continue to engage with officials in those other departments, taking a collaborative approach in developing the policy, legislation and operational delivery of new administrative processes aimed at ensuring parents adhere to their legal responsibility in supporting their children financially.My department is unable to provide temporary financial assistance for unpaid child maintenance and there are no immediate plans to do so.  However, parents who might find themselves under financial pressure, and depending on their personal circumstances, may be eligible for social security benefits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail his plans to review and implement changes to the remuneration policy with community pharmacists with a view to ensuring their sustainability; and (ii) for his assessment of the concerns of community pharmacists about the future viability of the sector.
