answer_text: At the outset, I can assure you that this Department acknowledges the very important work that community pharmacies and their teams deliver for our population.Against the backdrop of the current severe budgetary pressures and the multiple demands for additional funding across all parts of health and social care, additional investment continues to be provided to the community pharmacy sector.In correspondence to all pharmacy contractors dated 25th April ’24, it was advised that my Department has committed to the following in 2024/25:£1m per quarter in respect of inflation pressures, which will be paid as a top-up to Global Sum fees£4m non-recurrent funding for the medicines adherence service pressuresA commitment to increase the retained medicines margin of £26.5m to £31.5m, and a commitment to move towards new processes during 2024/2025. As a result of the funding measures detailed above, the revised opening funding allocation for community pharmacy services for 24/25 in Northern Ireland is £147m. This is an increase of £19m and 14.8% over the opening position of April 2023. (ii) In terms of the future viability of the community pharmacy sector, last month, the Department of Health published a new Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy which aims to fully realise the potential of community pharmacy services to support better health outcomes from medicines and prevent illness. This important piece of work was developed collaboratively by the Department of Health and Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy owners, and describes the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030.Additional funding will be required to fully deliver this Strategic Plan up to 2030. I am very conscious that the Community Pharmacy sector – like all sectors across health and social care – is keen to see further investment.The Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy is available on the Department of Health website:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/community-pharmacy-strategic-plan-2030

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail his plans to review and implement changes to the remuneration policy for NHS dentists with a view to reducing them leaving health service dentistry provision; and (ii) for his assessment of the reduction in the number of dentists who provide such treatment.
answer_text: The need for dental reform has been acknowledged for a number of years, however, the pandemic, followed by the absence of an Executive has impacted progress in this area.Most recently, efforts have been focused on addressing the immediate access issues patients are experiencing and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices while reform is progressed. This has resulted in the £9.2 million additional investment for 2024/25, which was announced on 27 March 2024.This investment includes £4.3m for a 30% enhancement to the fees associated with filings, extractions and root canal treatment which are some of the most frequently claimed fees and clinically beneficial treatments.I am conscious of the developments in the rest of the UK, with Scotland, England and Wales adopting diverging models of remuneration. Officials are liaising with their counterparts in each region to monitor the impact of these models and assess the suitability of adopting similar approaches in Northern Ireland.  In each of the last 5 years, the Department has followed the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) alongside an additional expenses element when calculating the annual uplift to the General Dental Services Statement of Remuneration. These uplifts mean that General Dental Practitioners are currently being paid 20.47% more per Item of Service than they were in 2019.In addition to these uplifts, in 2022 a permanent 25% enhancement was put in place for fees related to dentures, to reflect significant increases in costs to dental practices.  I am aware of the difficulties some patients are experiencing when trying to access General Dental Services, particularly for those who are not registered with a dentist.In Northern Ireland, there were 363 dental practices with 1,163 dentists registered to carry out health service treatments at the end of March 2023. The number of dentists registered has increased by 13% over the last decade from 1,027 in 2013 to 1,163 in 2023. There was an increase of 1.5% in the number of dentists registered between 2022 and 2023 (17 dentists).Between 2014 and 2023 there was an increase of 5.6% in the number of Health Service dentists per 100,000 population, with 60.4 dentists per 100,000 in Northern Ireland, compared to 42.3 in England.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on any available or planned schemes to encourage and support the use of renewable energy or energy efficiency when new houses are being built; and (ii) for his assessment of such schemes in the rest of the UK and Ireland and how Northern Ireland could benefit from this.
answer_text: The Department of Finance lead Building regulations in the North of Ireland which is the primary policy area overseeing energy performance in new buildings. The Executive’s Energy Strategy contains a phased plan to introduce uplifts to these regulations which will improve minimum standards with respect to carbon emissions and energy efficiency when building work is being carried out. At present, the Department for the Economy has no intention of incentivising renewable energy in new buildings as it expects the adoption of renewable energies to be driven through the 2022 Building Regulation change, with future uplifts expected to be more stringent. Incentivising adoption of renewable technology or energy efficiency improvements is extremely challenging where regulation exists in this way due to government guidance on Additionality and the need to assess the additional impacts of financial interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps he is taking to ensure adequate numbers of school support staff, including recruitment and funding, especially those involved in working with children with special educational needs.
answer_text: Responsibility for the management of staffing matters, including recruitment, is delegated to the Board of Governors of individual schools. However, the Education Authority’s (EA) Resourcing Service provides support in this area through the provision of training, tools, guidance and administrative support.  We are aware that it has become increasingly challenging to recruit school support staff, particularly Classroom Assistants and those working with children with special educational needs. Therefore, through its HR Transformation Programme, the EA has been working to optimise and improve the recruitment service provided to schools.  The EA is also seeking to progress several strategically important projects targeted at resolving challenges with the pay, grading and the employment model for school support staff.  This includes the proposed Pay and Grading Review for support staff and the joint working group with non-teaching trade unions to develop solutions to the challenges associated with the Classroom Assistant recruitment and employment model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11879/22-27, to detail the oversight his Department has, and invokes, in relation to local councils’ implementation of the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The implementation and enforcement of the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, as it pertains to non-farmed animals, is the statutory responsibility of councils.  Therefore, my Department does not oversee activity on the ground or direct how councils choose to meet these statutory obligations. Any concerns about the council delivered animal welfare service should be raised directly with the relevant organisation so that remedial action can be taken, if necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work he is carrying out, or planning to carry out, with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to address suicide in rural communities.
answer_text: Certain factors have been identified as creating risk and stress to people living in rural areas over and above the risk factors for suicide affecting general populations. These include isolation, barriers to accessing services, a more conservative approach to help-seeking, heightened stigma associated with mental health issues, LGBT populations, availability of some means of suicide (firearm ownership, pesticides), and higher risk occupational groups such as farmers and veterinarians.Rural Support provides support through a helpline which offers a listening and signposting service for farmers and rural families. Issues addressed include financial and debt problems; inheritance issues; physical and mental health concerns; farming paperwork and bureaucracy.Public Health Agency (PHA) has funded Rural Support in delivering a range of services to help promote positive mental health among isolated rural dwellers, farmers and farm families across the region. These include:Conducting information sessions to promote positive mental health, encourage farmers and rural dwellers to seek help and link with other support servicesTraining for Rural Support staff and volunteers Mentors provide one-to-one support to individuals experiencing financial distress and, where appropriate, signpost to other services.. PHA has worked in partnership with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) to develop and co-fund the Farm Families Health Check Programme. This consists of a mobile unit which is available at local livestock markets and additional community events to offer on-the-spot health checks consisting of diabetic and heart attack risk assessments, BMI checks and offers support around mental health. In addition, individual lifestyle advice is given on a range of health issues, and onward referral completed to local support services as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps he is taking to ensure prices that farmers receive for their produce ensures their sustainability.
answer_text: Farm prices are determined by the market and therefore outside the control of Government. My Department does provide considerable advice to farm businesses on matters of economic and environmental sustainability.  The Department makes around £300m annually in direct payments which form a very significant proportion of farm income and play an important role in business sustainability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of how farmers are currently being financially supported by government, in comparison to previous EU funding sources; (ii) to detail the issues this is creating for farmers; and (iii) to detail the steps he is taking to mitigate the impact on farmers of rising costs of feed, fertiliser, energy, and other costs associated with agriculture.
answer_text: EU funding for farmers and land managers in Northern Ireland has been maintained by the UK Government in cash terms since 2020 and will continue until the end of the 2024-25 financial year.My officials are bringing forward a balanced and comprehensive Farm Support and Development Programme in codesign with stakeholder organisations. There are options for every farm business under this new Programme regardless of size or scale of activity, but it requires them to change their behaviour and deliver better outcomes.      In 2023 direct payments continued to be paid earlier than normal and with a 2.32% increase to the Basic Payment component.  My Department continues to bring forward new policies and programmes designed to help farm businesses improve productivity, resilience and environmental sustainability. CAFRE also continues to offer relevant training and support.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he will improve addiction services available to people in East Londonderry.
answer_text: The Department of Health leads on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’ a key priority of this strategy is to prevent and reduce the harm related to alcohol and other drugs through a range of cross-departmental actions and support. In order to deliver the vision and outcomes of this Strategy officials within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), Department of Health, have worked with the Public Health Agency and a range of stakeholders, to co-produce a new Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan 2024-2028. This Plan has been subject to public consultation and will replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework. The Plan identifies clear strategic priority areas for improving services across the Region, for those impacted by substance use issues including families and carers.  The Plan is due to be published in June 2024 and implementation of agreed priority actions will follow subject to funding.Alongside the development of the Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, a number of other related priority areas of work are being progressed, these include;Population Based Needs Assessment relating to Substance Use in the Western Health & Social Care Trust Area, and,Independent Review of Tier 4 Substance Use Services which includes, In-patient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland.It is the intention that the recommendations from these areas of work along with the Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, will support service improvement across the Region and help to inform and prioritise, future funding decisions relating to substance use. This work will be in alignment with the new Regional Mental Health Service, and Integrated Care System.In addition, SPPG officials have been involved in supporting a collaborative proposal to the PEACEPLUS Programme to secure funding to deliver a Community Detoxification Project for those living in our border communities. It is anticipated that if successful, this project will build on and add value to existing services in the Western and Southern Health Trust areas.The breadth of the work already completed as outlined is a clear indication of the Department of Health’s commitment to implementing the vision of the Substance Use Strategy.The full delivery of the Strategy will be dependent on additional funding being made available going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to review his Department’s contract with GPs in order to facilitate an easier and simpler method for people to contact their doctor.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us. GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department to provide primary care services.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  Decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce. The GP Access Working Group is overseeing work to develop guidance for practices in the management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency and improve experience for patients. The Group is also considering how technology can help to deliver improvements to the process for accessing services for patients and practices alike.  Under the GMS contract a GP contractor is obliged to deliver services required for the management of patients in the manner determined by the practice in discussion with the patient. The Practice is required to deliver these services within core hours 8am to 6.30, Monday to Friday except Bank Holidays. As part of the recent negotiations on the GMS contract for 2024/25, my department has developed an NI Contract Assurance Framework (NICAF).  The NICAF consists of 6 domains, including one for Access, with a range of indicators in each domain. The intention is that, over time, these domains will develop to provide enhanced assurance over the quality of service being delivered across each domain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to ensure the sustainability of community transport services, especially in the north coast area.
answer_text: I recognise and value the contribution of all organisations that provide community transport services across the North, and the impact that they have on some of the most vulnerable groups in society.I have made the decision to provide funding to community transport for 2024/25 at the total levels awarded for 2023/24. This is subject to the planned Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the budget and so this allocation is indicative at this stage. I do, however, expect to be able to confirm opening budget allocations, once I have taken final decisions following consideration of responses to the Equality Impact Assessment after an initial four-week period. I am currently considering an evaluation report on the Dial-a-Lift (DAL) Scheme and Disability Action Transport Scheme (DATS) and will ensure the sustainability of community transport services is integral to the action plan for the next phase of the community transport review. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the size of the waiting list; and (ii) the average wait, for government-funded IVF sessions.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions he is taking to ensure the provision of the newest and most effective drugs to treat dementia.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with NICE under which its technology appraisals for medicines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here. Where found to be applicable they are endorsed by my Department for implementation within the Health and Social Care system. Actions to ensure the provision of new drugs will be taken forward at that time, however my Department’s initial assessment is that the deployment of novel therapeutic agents for dementia will require significant additional resources to redesign clinical pathways, increase regional imaging capacity and develop the service infrastructure needed to ensure the timely and equitable rollout of these agents, should they be endorsed for implementation in Northern Ireland.In circumstances where NICE has not yet fully appraised, or does not recommend a particular drug, it is not routinely commissioned for use in Northern Ireland. However, the Individual Funding Request process provides a mechanism to consider requests from clinical consultants for treatments for individual patients that fall outside the range of services routinely commissioned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans he has to strengthen employment laws in relation to women going through the menopause.
answer_text: Women going through the menopause can experience challenges within the workplace when experiencing symptoms of menopause. Whilst the menopause is not by itself a specific protected characteristic under equality legislation, if an employee or worker is disadvantaged or treated less favourably because of their menopause symptoms, this could be discrimination if related to a protected characteristic such as age, gender or disability.Good employers have flexible supportive working policies in place such as homeworking, flexible hours, or compassionate leave. I intend to strengthen the rights to request flexible working though a future Employment Rights Bill.In June 2023, the Labour Relations Agency, the Equality Commission and the Irish Congress of Trade Unions published a guide entitled Promoting Equality in Employment for Women Affected by Menopause. All employers are recommended to consider this guide when developing their own workplace policies. I would encourage all employers to offer enhanced flexible and supportive working arrangements for their staff experiencing symptoms of menopause.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will increase cancer screening for women.
answer_text: Currently in Northern Ireland, we have three screening programmes aimed at reducing cancer mortality in asymptomatic individuals.  These are the breast screening, bowel screening and cervical screening programmes.Invitations for screening are sent to anyone registered with a GP in Northern Ireland and who meets the eligible criteria for that particular programme. The screening programmes provide accessible, accurate and evidence-based information to individuals to ensure they understand:the condition being screened for;the testing process;the risks, limitations, benefits, and uncertainties connected to the screening process.It is up to individuals whether to accept or decline the invitation, based on their personal informed choice.To promote the importance of accepting an invitation to breast, bowel and cervical screening, the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Department supported the “Community Pharmacy Living Well,” campaign which ran in over 500 community pharmacies in April and May, this year.  Details of the programme can be found via: Living Well - Business Services Organisation (BSO) Website (hscni.net)In addition, PHA continues to commission the Women’s Resource and Development Agency (WRDA) to promote informed choice in cervical cancer screening via its peer led community-based programme. The programme targets groups where rates of participation in screening are likely to be lower, including those impacted by health inequalities and living in deprived areas.As with all screening programmes, anyone who is concerned about, or is experiencing, any unusual symptoms should seek advice from their GP rather than wait on their next screening test or screening test results.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for cutting waiting times in gynaecology.
answer_text: Gynaecology Services in Northern Ireland have been impacted by Covid, and other general overarching pressures, this has led to increased waiting times across the region. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has been asked to establish a forum to take forward the recommendations of the recent Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology. This forum has Trust, Public Health Agency (PHA) and General Practitioner (GP) representation and will oversee gynaecology service improvement and pathway development at both a local and regional level.There may be additional finance required to meet demands in this service, and in the current financial position this may be challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the percentage of households currently registered as homeless who have cited domestic violence as an issue on their housing application, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below details homelessness acceptances during 2023/24 where the acceptance reason was domestic abuse. The table also details the overall number of acceptances and the percentage of acceptances due to domestic abuse as a percentage of overall acceptances.Council AreaHomelessness Acceptances due to domestic abuseTotal Homelessness AcceptancesDomestic abuse acceptances as a percentage of all acceptancesAntrim & Newtownabbey130101312.8%Ards & North Down115101211.4%Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon8574911.3%Belfast21932266.8%Causeway Coast & Glens10984212.9%Derry & Strabane13413529.6%Fermanagh & Omagh495019.8%Lisburn & Castlereagh466157.5%Mid & East Antrim14192015.3%Mid Ulster6450512.7%Newry, Mourne & Down548026.7%Grand Total1146115379.9%”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to update guidelines in relation to how victims of domestic violence are treated in hospitals and other health and care settings.
answer_text: While no specific guidelines exist, there is considerable evidence of focused support based on operating protocols and training programmes.These include:Southern TrustSHSCT staff attend safeguarding training (adult and child safeguarding) at a level relevant to their job role. This includes recognising and responding to victims of domestic and sexual abuse within all settings, including acute, and to make appropriate onward referrals for support as applicable. Southern Trust, in collaboration with Women’s Aid, established a DASS (Domestic Abuse Safety and Support Advisor) Worker role within the Acute setting, with a dedicated focus of engaging with victims of Domestic Abuse. To provide immediate support and advice to victims of domestic abuse within hospitalTo link individuals and families to longer-term community-based supportTo provide hospital staff with expert training so that they have the confidence to ask about domestic abuse establishing clear referral routesTo create and develop immediate safety plans for the individual and an onward MARAC referral if requiredThis service has provided timely support and advice to victims of domestic abuse across both acute hospitals, Craigavon Area Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital. This has facilitated an improved response time in providing victims with support.South Eastern TrustSET Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse Partnership has developed training and awareness raising resources which have been shared both internally and externally to the Trust Trust midwives routine inquire pre and post pregnancy in recognition that domestic abuse can often start and escalate in pregnancy. Radiologists within the hospital departments have also been trained to implement targeted enquiry if they are concerned regarding a patient’s presentation The Partnership is actively exploring with the patient navigators based in the Ulster Hospital ways to support victims of domestic and sexual abuse when they present at the Emergency Department.Northern TrustTrust staff work within current Safeguarding Board NI Policies and Procedures designed to identify, treat and manage victims of Domestic Violence. Trust policies direct staff on how to respond to a disclosure and actions to take, including indirect/direct questions, onward referral, safety planning with victims, and contact numbers for support and consideration of UNOCINI (Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland) and/or MARAC (Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conference) referral.Trust staff, in hospital and community settings, have access to level 1 safeguarding awareness raising training as part of their corporate induction. Further level 2 and level 3 Domestic Abuse training is available for staff depending on their requirements for job role.  Staff responding to a disclosure of domestic abuse have also been trained in completion of UNOCINI, DASH/RIC (Domestic Abuse, Stalking and Harassment/Risk Identification Checklist) and MARAC, if required.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what engagement they have had with victims on their experience of the court system, as part of their work to bring forward the Strategic Framework Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The lived experience of women and girls has been central to the development and delivery of the draft Strategic Framework. Through co-design and other engagement, our officials have met with Victims Support NI, Women’s Aid NI, Nexus NI, as well as those individuals with lived experience. The Co-design process identified Justice as one of four main pillars of the Framework, and the key outcome as “A justice system which has the confidence of victims, survivors and the public in its ability to address violence against women and girls”.        The voices of women and girls who have lived experience will continue to inform the implementation of the Strategic Framework: this includes women and girls with experience of the court system.  We are committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework and Action Plan for consideration by the Executive Committee as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any plans he has to encourage more programmes for children to engage with older people; and (ii) his assessment of any benefits of programmes already held.
answer_text: The Department’s school improvement policy ‘Every School a Good School’ sets out the four key characteristics of a successful school, which include that a school is connected to its local community.  Where this characteristic is well embedded, there will be good relationships that facilitate engagement and communication between a school, its parents and the wider community it serves.  There are many examples of best practice throughout our school network that promote engagement between pupils and older people in the local community, such as visits to residential homes and older people volunteering within primary schools to assist pupils with their reading.In recent years, my Department and the Education Authority have liaised with Linking Generations Northern Ireland, an organisation which has been connecting generations in communities across Northern Ireland since 2009. I am also aware that the Northern Ireland Forest School Association runs an intergenerational programme which involves partnerships between nursery and primary schools and residencies selected by Radius Housing. Due to the financial pressures facing my Department and the need to prioritise frontline services, I am not in a position to develop or fund any new programmes at the present time. However, I recognise that there are many educational, social and community benefits associated with programmes which bring together children and older people. They play an important role in bridging the generational divide and have the potential to enrich the lives of all who participate in them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the emotional support provided to children who are impacted by domestic violence and have been identified by police as part of Operation Encompass, including (i) who is providing this support; (ii) how support is provided; (iii) the success of the outcomes; and (iv) the sustainability of this programme.
answer_text: The support provided through Operation Encompass to children impacted by domestic violence is incredibly important.  In the first full year of operation (May 2023 to May 2024) over 18,000 individual notifications have been made to schools and colleges.  Support is provided by a range of sources, with multi-agency working a key feature.  Notifications to schools are handled by a school’s safeguarding team, co-ordinated by the Designated Teacher.  Any incident of domestic abuse, which has resulted in a notification, is referred to Social Services by police.  Ongoing support is provided for the family by Social Services if required.  Further assistance is available for schools from the Education Authority Child Protection Support Service (EA CPSS).To provide tailored care for the child/children, limited information can be shared by the Designated Teacher/Safeguarding Team with the class teacher.  Wider oversight is maintained by the Designated Teacher and Safeguarding Team.  In all cases, schools will work with the PSNI and Social Services, as appropriate, to address any potential risks.A survey of Designated Teachers on their experience of Operation Encompass has confirmed that the initiative has been positively received by schools. The introduction of Operation Encompass has had resource implications for the EA CPSS.  To date, funding has been available from the Department of Justice (DoJ) under the Stopping Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse, 2016-2023 Strategy.  My officials are continuing to engage with the DoJ to secure funding under the new Domestic and Sexual Abuse 2023-2030 strategy.  Options are also being explored regarding the use of technologies to streamline delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to ensure the timely (i) diagnosis; and (ii) treatment of dementia.
answer_text: There is a commitment across Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland to improving timely access to accurate diagnosis and treatment for people living with Dementia.  The Department has issued a 9-week target which sets out that people should have a first assessment within that timescale. All Trusts have advised that they employ a rigorous triaging regime on receipt of dementia referrals to ensure that those individuals at greatest risk are seen first. My officials continue to engage with the Health and Social Care Trusts in order to scrutinize performance against this 9-week target and monitor the numbers of new and review clinic appointments which have been achieved. The diagnostic rate for dementia in Northern Ireland has improved to 62% comparative rates in England are reported as 64.6%. The majority of those patients receive a diagnosis at their first appointment and will start an appropriate medication. In relation to waiting times, as of 30th April 2024, there were 58.3% breaches of the 9-week target. Therefore, 2,663 people were awaiting first assessment, with 1,553 people waiting for more than 9 weeks.   In relation to treatment of Dementia, Northern Ireland was one of the first regions to make dementia medications available to citizens.  There is free prescribing of the currently available drugs- Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors and Memantine.My officials have held the inaugural meeting of a Regional Dementia Project Board on 24th May 2024. The project board aims to support the delivery of existing dementia services, as well as consider future service delivery models. Key priorities for the Regional Dementia Project Board will be to decrease waiting times for assessment and diagnosis, to consider steps to improve efficiency in systems and processes across the region, to consider regional approaches to training, to improve the experience of diagnosis for people with dementia and their relatives/carers and to formulate a clear regional vision of services into the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to increase respite services in East Londonderry for parents of children with additional needs.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust have one facility in Londonderry that offers respite services to parents of children with disabilities. The Western Trust has informed my Department that Rosebud Short Break Unit has limited short breaks being provided, due to 2 children requiring full time care placements within the unit.The Western Health and Social Care Trust has informed my department that they are aware of the stresses and anxieties for families that the limited short break provision will be creating. My Departmental officials continue to engage with the Western Health and Social Care Trust around the issue of Short breaks provision to restore the facility to its previous offer of respite care. The Children With Disability Framework is also currently being finalised by my Department in response to increasing challenges faced by families of Children With Disability and the corresponding difficulties experienced by Trusts in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. It is recognised that a review and reform of how services are structured, delivered and resourced, both from a regional perspective and locally in each of the Trusts is required. This includes the provision of Short Break.  The full implementation of the Children With Disability Framework will better enable Trusts and independent sector providers to respond to the pressures across Health and Social Care, specifically: (i) increasing demand for early and community-based support; (ii) increasing demand for respite care/short breaks; (iii) increasing demand for more bespoke residential placements; and (iv) better planning and management of complex transitions from children to adult services.A Children With Disability Workstream has also been established as part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Board.  This workstream will be looking specifically at the regional position in respect of Children With Disability, and will include a focus on short breaks / respite. The Children With Disability Workstream will seek to deliver reform within Children’s Social Care services to address a range of known service challenges, particularly in relation to an ever-increasing volume and complexity of case work. The outcomes of this work will inform future commissioning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the number of job vacancies in the agri-food industry; and (ii) what steps he is taking to address this.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the agri-food sector to our economy, and how crucial it is to rural and regional development. I am aware of the labour shortages in our agri-food sector and that the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs has written to the British Government on this issue.  In terms of the impact of the new immigration rules on labour supply for the sector, I have written to the Home Secretary to underline these difficulties and ask for consideration to be given to a change to the minimum salary threshold. I will continue to lobby British Government Ministers on this issue. My officials will continue to engage with the Migration Advisory Committee and their substantive review of the Immigration Salary List, which will result in a renewed assessment of labour market conditions and the new salary thresholds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to ensure everyone who needs it has access to an NHS dentist.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients in General Dental Services were the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers had meant that it had not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children on the waiting list for education welfare support; and (ii) the steps he is taking to increase the number of education welfare officers.
answer_text: There are currently 2,229 children on the waiting list for Education Welfare Support. A review of the Education Welfare Service has been completed which proposes the need to transform the service to provide more effective and timelier support through prevention and early intervention.  The associated business case is being finalised by the Education Authority and will be submitted shortly to the Department for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to update the Neighbourhood Renewal strategy, launched in 2003.
answer_text: A review of the Executive’s People and Place strategy is currently underway with co-design structures in place to facilitate engagement with a wide range of stakeholders including the Neighbourhood Renewal partnerships, representatives from Section 75 groups and the wider voluntary and community sector including those from rural communities.Given the complex nature of deprivation providing the right support for  communities will require a collaborative and integrated cross Departmental approach by the Executive. The drafting of a strategic document will take place over the summer months after which I will present it to the Executive for further consideration. It is then envisaged that a public consultation exercise will take place in the Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work he is carrying out to support the arts in rural areas.
answer_text: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland are the main distributor for funding for the arts on behalf of the Department and have delivered a specific funding programme targeting arts engagement in rural areas.  The National Lottery Rural Engagement Arts Programme (REAP) has operated for two years: 2022/23 and 2023/24.  Its purpose is to provide an integrated, cohesive approach to the needs of local rural communities while recognising some specific issues they face.  The primary theme of REAP is combating isolation and loneliness and promoting social inclusion.   To date 133 awards, totalling £890,080, have been made through this programme.  It is anticipated that REAP will run in 2024/25 however dates are to be confirmed.In addition to the dedicated programme above, the Arts Council run other funding programmes to which organisations in rural areas are eligible to apply.  Further to this, a number of Annually Funded and National Lottery Project funded clients will undertake outreach work in rural areas. The Department’s Community Festivals Fund, delivered in partnership with local councils, supports Culture-based festivals, many of which take place within rural areas across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he will work to maximise the economic benefits for local traders and businesses from The Open being held at Royal Portrush Golf Club in 2025.
answer_text: Tourism NI (TNI), on behalf of my department, is committed to working with key partners to deliver benefits from The 153rd Open in 2025 in excess of those of The 148th Open in 2019. The previous event generated more than £100 million of combined economic and media benefit for the North. The Causeway Coast area alone received a £26.21 million injection of new money.The Open in 2025 will bring hundreds of thousands of visitors and will showcase the destination through the huge global broadcast reach and PR coverage generated, that will deliver a legacy for years to come. In 2019, TNI’s Golf Monitor demonstrated that 53% of people who visited the region to play golf that year were directly influenced by hosting of The Open.An industry engagement programme will be delivered in the lead up to the event, in partnership with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council. This will assist and advise local traders and businesses on how best to avail of opportunities that The Open will present. It will include working with businesses to provide reasons for event visitors to extend their stay and explore the wider area, alongside the ongoing support for the development of new and immersive authentic experiences that deliver on the 'Embrace a Giant Spirit' brand.Sustainable workforce opportunities will be developed for beyond July 2025, through skills development, employment before, during and after the event, and volunteering. TNI will support the industry to recruit and retain staff through its ongoing ‘Make it Here’ careers campaign, and e-learning platform. In addition,  TNI will work with Fáilte Ireland to extend its Employer Excellence Programme into the North to foster a culture of excellence in the workplace, making tourism and hospitality sectors rewarding and attractive places to work.Aside from the world-class golf offering, other local experiences on the Causeway Coast will be promoted including high quality accommodation, restaurants, visitor attractions, and entertainment. In partnership with Tourism Ireland, a significant promotion and marketing campaign including a series of golf tour operator and media familiarisations trips in order to promote the destination to a global audience, will be delivered locally, nationally and globally, to encourage visitors to experience all our destination has to offer.My vision is to cultivate a productive, regionally balanced and carbon-neutral economy that provides good jobs. The welcome return of The Open to our shores will support that ambition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail how he is working (a) within his Department; and (b) across Departments, to reach Northern Ireland’s agreed climate targets; and (ii) for his assessment of progress made since the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 was established.
answer_text: My Department is leading on development of Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan (CAP), working together across all departments, to set out Northern Ireland’s approach to meeting the carbon budget for 2023 to 2027 and establishing a Net Zero pathway through a set of proposals and policies for emissions reductions.	Collaboration is occurring at strategic and delivery levels, within DAERA and across departments through a Strategic Oversight Group (a cross-Departmental group comprising senior officials from all NI Departments), a sector/departmental leads group, an Evidence and Analysis group (a cross-departmental group which aims to support the provision of robust evidence, analyses and advice to inform CAP development) and supported by a core CAP team in DAERA. In terms of the progress made to meet the targets set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, the most recent Northern Ireland Greenhouse Gas Inventory report shows emissions reduced by 23.2% from the base year to 2021. To meet the 2030 target, a 48% reduction is required so the rate of decarbonsation needs to substantially increase. This will require concerted effort by all.In addition to progressing the development of a Climate Action Plan, other progress made includes-bringing into operation the Climate Change (Reporting Bodies) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 on 3 May 2024; developing policy to enable regulations to be made that will set Northern Ireland’s first three carbon budgets and appropriate emission reduction targets for 2030 and 2040. Draft regulations have been prepared and I hope to be soon in a position to seek Executive approval for the regulations before laying the regulations in the Assembly for approval;developing policy around the establishment of an independent Just Transition Commission and I envisage that proposals will be published for public consultation in the coming months; implementation of a net zero aligned cap for the UK Emissions Trading Scheme with effect from 1 January 2024, acting jointly with the UK, Scottish and Welsh Governments as the UK ETS Authority; commissioning the Climate Change Committee to undertake an independent assessment of the second Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP2) which is being used to help inform the development of NICCAP3.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail plans he has to act on the Strategic Investment Board’s report into the need for a veterinary school in Northern Ireland; (ii) for his assessment of the report; and (iii) for his assessment of the current provision of vets in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: At the request of former Minister Lyons, the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) was commissioned to undertake a situational analysis and baseline report to inform any future strategic business case for a veterinary school for Northern Ireland (NI).  SIB produced reports on both, and these have been published on the DAERA website. The steering group, established to provide governance, recognised that the situational analysis and baseline report was only a small element of the overall consideration of the need for a veterinary school and that further work to advance a business case now rests with the third level educational institutes.I have recently met with Ulster University to discuss their ambitions and intentions including their plans for introducing veterinary related courses.  This included the considerations around a veterinary school within NI and the SIB situational analysis/baseline report.It should also be noted that, whilst DAERA is a key employer within the veterinary sector and as such has a vested interest in securing such a talent pipeline, issues around workforce planning for the veterinary sector sits within the remit of the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS).However, DAERA will where requested continue to discuss with and support educational institutes should they seek to progress a business case for a veterinary school in NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) primary pupils; (ii) post-primary pupils; and (iii) pupils with special educational needs that have been left without a school place for the 2024-25 academic year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The position as at 31 May 2024 is as follows:6 primary school children are still to be placed for 2024/25:Belfast East - 1Belfast South - 1Belfast West - 1Foyle - 2Mid Ulster - 1TOTAL – 663 post-primary pupils are still to be placed for 2024/25:Belfast North - 2Belfast South - 4Belfast West - 1East Antrim - 4East Londonderry - 1Fermanagh & South Tyrone - 4Foyle - 6Lagan Valley - 1Mid Ulster - 9Newry & Armagh - 7North Antrim - 4South Antrim - 1South Down - 3Strangford - 3Upper Bann - 2West Tyrone - 6Outside NI - 5TOTAL - 6360% (n.41K) of children with special educational needs (SEN) do not have a statement of SEN and apply for a school placement through the general admissions process and may, therefore, be included in the primary and post-primary figures above.  40% (n. 27K) of children with SEN have a statement of SEN. These children follow a different process for confirming their school placement whereby their school placement is named in their final statement of SEN.  This process is highly individualised and the placement is based on the assessed needs of the child, in consultation with schools and parents.  There are no opening and closing dates as with the schools admissions process, meaning that comparison with the general admission process is inaccurate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the work he has carried out with (i) farmers; and (ii) the Ulster Farmers’ Union to agree a system whereby no farmer is losing money on their produce, enabling them to continue to operate as a viable and sustainable business.
answer_text: In a market economy it is not possible to agree a system whereby no farmer fails to make a profit as the government does not control prices or decision making on farms.  This is also the case for businesses in the wider economy.  My Department does provide advice and support to farm businesses, including around £300m in direct payments annually.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the active vacancy rate of domiciliary care staff, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the amount of sewage that is released into the sea; and (ii) what steps he is taking to prevent this.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) does not hold information on the amount of wastewater related discharges released to the sea. NIEA regulates sewage discharges to the water environment under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. A regulatory regime is in place, through the issue of Water Order Discharge Consents. These Consents require dischargers to treat sewage to a level that can be sustained by the receiving environment and to maintain their treatments to avoid pollution. Discharge consents are issued to single dwellings, private sewage infrastructure serving residential / commercial properties, and NI Water. NIEA uses a risk-based approach to monitor these consents and where non-compliances are detected, NIEA will take action in line with the Department’s enforcement policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the contact and feedback he has had with farmers regarding the creation of a biomethane industry; and (ii) whether any estimates have been made as to the potential for this industry to reduce carbon output from agriculture.
answer_text: I recently met the Ulster Farmers Union to discuss the benefits of a growing sustainable biomethane sector. In addition, my department has engaged directly with farmers through on farm CAFRE slurry management events in January 2024 and with the broader industry. Feedback from stakeholders recognises the benefits of biomethane production in reducing both our carbon footprint and excess nutrients. My department also delivered a Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) in 2023 on the Sustainable Utilisation of Livestock Slurry (SULS). As part of this project, suppliers had direct engagement with farmers and issued surveys to garner feedback on the supply and management of slurry as a feedstock for anaerobic digestion to produce biomethane. Ongoing Climate Action Plan mitigation modelling has included assessments of the positive impact on agriculture sector greenhouse gas (GHG) and ammonia emissions of increasing the proportion of managed livestock manures redirected to anaerobic digestion as envisaged in recent reports produced by Queen’s University Belfast, Action Renewables and KPMG on the potential for development of a biomethane industry in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she has any plans to allow the presence of journalists in family courts proceedings, with strict reporting rules, in order to aid transparency; and (ii) for her assessment of the complaints made by victims of domestic abuse in relation to the family court process.
answer_text: (i) Following concerns raised by victims of domestic abuse, amongst others, my Department has already commenced policy scoping work to consider how the openness of family courts could be increased, whilst continuing to protect the privacy of the families and children involved. Subject to the outcome of that work, any legislative change to enable greater media access and reporting would be for the longer term. (ii) The decisions reached by courts in individual cases are matters for the independent judiciary. Generally, however, I note that, in relation to proceedings concerning arrangements for children, the legislative framework already makes the welfare of the child the court’s paramount consideration. Further, where a party applying for a residence or contact order has a conviction for a domestic abuse related offence involving the child, or is subject to a non-molestation order, a court is required to have regard to this. Looking ahead, I will be giving careful consideration to responses from victims of domestic abuse to current and planned Departmental consultations touching on family proceedings. I will also consider, in due course, the findings of the research on children’s experiences of family proceedings to be carried out for the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime. I remain committed, so far as is within my remit, to supporting victims of domestic abuse involved in family proceedings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to extend safe access zones in areas with continued protesting.
answer_text: As operators of premises where abortions services are provided, Health and Social Care Trusts are empowered under Assembly legislation to determine the size of Safe Access Zones appropriate to each location, any may extend this up to a maximum of 250 metres from entry and exits points if they consider it appropriate for the purpose of allowing safe access to “protected persons”. As outlined in my response to AQW 10680/22-27, Safe Access Zones continue to operate effectively across all premises in line with Assembly legislation to protect those accessing abortion services and staff working at those locations. In light of this, I am not aware of any Trust plans to extend Safe Access Zones at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people placed in care homes due to a lack of an available home care package in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency; and (ii) the average cost of each stay.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information centrally and this was requested from HSC Trusts. Their responses are detailed below.(i) the number of people placed in care homes due to a lack of an available home care package in each of the last five yearsHSC Trust2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Belfast7948921196704443Northern3168246442378South Eastern9825178273169Western3724435168Southern HSC Trust provided the figure of 357 people, which is their total from 01/01/2021 to 29/03/2024.(ii) average cost of each stayHSC Trust2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Northern£3,986£5,370£6,503£6,790£6,251South Eastern£4,340£5,601£6,386£5,968£4,315Western£11,964£21,226£12,515£18,911£14,135Southern HSC Trust provided the figure of £13,089, which is the average over the timeframe from 01/01/2021 to 29/03/2024.Belfast HSC Trust are unable to provide this information.Domiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the sustainable schools policy and how this impacts small, rural schools where numbers are typically low; and (ii) to detail his plans to review this in order to protect rural schools and safeguard rural communities.
answer_text: (i) The Sustainable Schools Policy (SSP) sets the vision for every learner to achieve his or her full potential at each stage of development. At its core is the aim of ensuring that all pupils have access to a high quality, broad and balanced curriculum that meets their educational needs in schools that are educationally and financially viable. So, notwithstanding publication in 2009, the vision set out in the SSP remains appropriate. The SSP is the framework, used by schools and school managing authorities, for assessing the viability and long-term sustainability of schools and as such is the cornerstone for the Area Planning process. The policy recognises the needs of rural communities and this is reflected in a lower enrolment threshold for rural primary schools, an accessibility criterion which provides guidance on home to school travel times and a ‘strong links with the community’ criterion which recognises the central place a school has for many communities.  (ii) Departmental officials have completed an internal consultation on the recommendations from the Independent Review of Education.  A paper is now being produced for my consideration outlining the findings. I expect that this will include comments on the SSP. I will consider the need to commission a full review of SSP after I have received this paper.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the cross-departmental Task and Finish Group on Childcare.
answer_text: As the member will be aware I made a written Ministerial Statement to this Assembly on 23rd May setting out a package of early learning and childcare measures I intend to introduce in the 2024/2025 financial year.The cross-departmental Early Learning and Childcare Task and Finish Group has played an important role in supporting the development of these measures for which I am grateful.   The Group includes representatives from the departments of Economy, Health, Finance, Communities and Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  Specialist advice is also provided by economists and statisticians.  The package of measures I have constructed is designed to stabilise and support expansion of the early learning and childcare sector, over time, offer all children 22.5 hours per week pre-school provision, reduce the costs of childcare for working parents and build the evidence base necessary to inform development of the longer-term strategy.  It is an ambitious programme which will ease current pressures on parents and providers and lay a firm foundation for the future.  The measures will be taken forward as a package to achieve maximum impact and ensure a balanced approach across the sector as a whole.  The focus now is on implementation.  Some elements of the package can progress relatively quickly while others will require more detailed co-design with stakeholders, IT infrastructure development and testing.  Business case approvals will also be required for the new initiatives.  The Task and Finish group will play a vital role in this next phase of the work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the work he is doing to protect the supply of vital veterinary medicines.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024, conferring powers upon the Secretary of State in relation to Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework, which includes Regulation (EU) 2019/6 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 December 2018 on veterinary medicinal products.  As the Secretary of State has not exercised discretion with respect to this regulation, it is now under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).As Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, I am committed to do whatever is in my power as Minister to ensure that a satisfactory solution is found by the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.	On becoming Minister I have discussed the veterinary medicines issue with various groups such as the Northern Ireland Chamber of Commerce and Industry and the NI Grain Trade Association, as well as representatives from the Dairy Council, Logistics NI and Ulster Farmers Union and developed an understanding of the issues for our future supply of veterinary medicines. I am a member of the Veterinary Medicines Working Group (VMWG), which aims to help find and advise the UK Government on practical solutions on the issue of future supply and access to veterinary medicines.I have written to Minister Baker, Minister of State for Northern Ireland to query the solution of a UK/EU veterinary agreement. I participated in the private members motion debate in the Assembly following the House of Lords Windsor Framework Sub-Committee’s inquiry into veterinary medicines and the Windsor Framework and I gave my commitment to speak with Minister Baker further on this subject and to write to the UK Government’s Windsor Framework Taskforce in an attempt to assist with bringing forward proposals to the UK Government and European Union authorities that would positively resolve the issues surrounding the supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.I receive regular updates from my officials who work closely with Veterinary Medicines Directorate, the competent UK authority for veterinary medicines and an agency of Defra.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to (i) improve pay and conditions for paid carers; and (ii) identify and support unpaid carers.
answer_text: I recognise the need for action to support the social care sector, and those working in it. It is clear that improving pay and conditions for those working in social care, and across the wider health and social care sector must be a key focus moving forward. My predecessor’s immediate priority on his return to post, was to see pay negotiations taken forward and concluded without undue delay, for health and social care workers in the statutory sector.  In recognition of the challenges facing the independent social care sector, on 22 March a £70m funding package, across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care, was announced. The purpose of this was to ensure independent sector providers had the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April.This funding also included an increase in the regional domiciliary care rate paid to independent sector providers increased from £18.54 to £20.01 per hour for 2024/25. Whilst any additional funding has allowed employers to offer enhanced rates of pay, and better terms and conditions, my Department has no remit to direct private companies as to the rate they pay their staff, or the terms and conditions that they offer. My officials have however written to all independent sector social care employers noting the previous Minister’s clear intent that the additional funding was to enable employers to stabilise the social care sector workforce through enhanced rates of pay, and better terms and conditions. Structures have also been established under the Social Care Fair Work Forum to consider how we could move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funding that will be required to deliver this. In addition, in recognition of the fundamental need to deliver reform and transformation in Health and Social Care, my Department has established the Adult Social Care Collaborative Forum and the Children’s Strategic Reform Board. Within these Fora, there are workstreams focused on supporting, developing, and improving the social care workforce.The workstreams are implementing a range of measures that include:Publishing a Social Care Workforce Strategy which will set out a vision to develop a sustainable social care workforce over the next 10 years. The strategy will include actions to develop career pathways, support the training and education of the workforce, and to raise the profile and recognition of the social care workforce as a skilled and competent workforce.  It is anticipated that the Strategy will be published in late 2024.In partnership with the Northern Ireland Social Care Council, work is ongoing to develop the training, career, and qualification pathways for the social care workforce.In partnership with all stakeholders’ social care campaigns are ongoing to raise the profile and recognise the significant contribution that the social care workforce provides.In re

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how Northern Ireland’s reputation as a prime golfing destination is being used to promote tourism here; and (ii) what resources are being provided to increase the quality of this asset further.
answer_text: The North is widely regarded as one of the best golfing destinations in the world, particularly following the successful hosting of The Open Championship in Portrush in 2019. In 2023 the value of golf tourism to the North was an estimated £68 million with over 29,000 visitors travelling here specifically to play golf. This has been achieved through the promotion of golf tourism at trade shows across the globe and directly to the consumer through a wide range of marketing channels.Over the past 12 months Tourism NI (TNI), working in partnership with Tourism Ireland, has undertaken consumer marketing including digital, print and in-person promotion across all of its target markets.TNI also regularly deliver consumer campaigns in partnership with major online golf travel agencies such as YourGolfTravel, GolfNow and GolfBreaks to encourage golfers to take short breaks here.The agencies’ golf teams are regularly present on promotional stands at USPGA and DP World Tour events with a focus on directly promoting golf across Ireland to golfing audiences in the US, Britain, Europe and UAE markets, as well as attending targeted trade shows in the same markets. The agencies are also joined by representatives from local golf clubs, tour operators and accommodation providers on the promotional stands.Golf influencers with large social media followings are also used to promote both golf and the wider destination directly to golfing audiences in the USA, Britain, and Europe.In October 2023, TNI hosted the International Association of Golf Tour Operators “Golf in Ireland” Convention at The Ebrington Hotel in Derry, pairing forty international golf tour operators with forty local golf partners, as part of a 3-day familiarisation trip.Finally, my Department has provided TNI with the funding necessary to secure the return of the Irish Open to Royal County Down this year and the 153rd Open Championship at Portrush next year. Hosting these events will further promote the North as a world class golfing destination to a large global audience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of student accommodation availability in and around the Coleraine area; and (ii) to detail any plans she has to encourage the increase of this provision.
answer_text: I am aware of increasing challenges in relation to the availability of private student accommodation in Coleraine.Ulster University has been working to implement mitigations where possible.  Last year the University leased 184 bedspaces, located adjacent to the campus and in Portstewart, and intends extend this by a further 30 bedspaces for September 2024.  The University continues to explore additional opportunities to increase provision of student accommodation in the Coleraine campus area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the pressures impacting hospitality businesses; and (ii) to detail the work she is undertaking to ease these.
answer_text: I understand the importance of the hospitality sector for both our tourism industry and the high street economy, with approximately 57,000 jobs in the accommodation and food service sector. Addressing the challenges of rising costs cuts across several areas of government, including the Department for Communities and local councils.  My Department is taking steps to support businesses through delivery of the Executive’s ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’ Strategy, a key objective being to ensure that all consumers have access to essential and affordable energy. Tourism NI provides a suite of advisory supports to help tourism and hospitality businesses reduce costs, improve productivity and be more sustainable, including Leaner and Greener, and Innovate Tourism.The level of VAT is also a specific issue, and I know that the Department of Finance has repeatedly raised with the Treasury the negative impacts on hospitality of high levels of VAT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the actions being taken to increase the number of foster carers; and (ii) the number of foster carers in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work being carried out to (i) quantify; and (ii) reach those who are homeless but not in contact with any government agency or non-governmental organisation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “(i) The Housing Executive is not in a position to provide any information to quantify the number of individuals who are homeless but not in contact with any government agency, as the Housing Executive only collects data for homeless presentations.(ii) Hidden homelessness is acknowledged in the Housing Executive ‘Homelessness Strategy 2022-27’, where it notes that it is necessary to ensure that these “hidden households” are provided with the means to access the housing and support services they may require.  Through the ‘Homelessness Strategy 2022-27’ we seek to ensure that relevant actions are developed around increasing awareness alongside the development of protocols for partner public services which will be delivered in order to engage with those who are considered as hidden homeless.”  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) what plans are in place in relation to the amalgamation of the Causeway Coastal Route and the Wild Atlantic Way; and (ii) what action she will take to ensure the Causeway Coastal Route receives an equal share of investment and resources when this amalgamation takes place.
answer_text: Tourism NI, Failte Ireland and Tourism Ireland are working in partnership to deliver a €7.6m Shared Island Scheme programme to improve synergies and experiences for visitors to the Causeway Coastal Route and Wild Atlantic Way, designed to increase connectivity and improved visitor experience encouraging international visitors to explore more widely, stay longer, and spend more. Discussions are underway in relation to extending the Wild Atlantic Way brand into the North.The budget will be allocated on a 50/50 basis across the North and the South.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5899/22-27, (i) whether Part 5 of the Transport Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 enables his Department to financially support the purchase, conversion and running costs for wheelchair accessible taxis; and (ii) whether he will provide financial support to taxi firms and drivers to modify their cars, in order to create more wheelchair accessible taxis.
answer_text: I would advise that there is currently no policy or budget in place to provide financial support towards the purchase, conversion or running costs for wheelchair taxis under Part 5 of the Transport Act (NI) 2011.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the work he has carried out with local councils to promote the enforcement of available processes in relation to derelict buildings.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out any such work with district councils.  Much of the current legislation available to councils to deal with dilapidated/dangerous buildings and neglected sites dates back to the Victorian era and/or applies to specific geographical areas and is limited in its scope and effectiveness. 		It is therefore my intention to introduce a Dilapidation Bill to the Assembly in the autumn, subject to the approval of my Executive colleagues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 10181/22-27, to detail how this impacts (i) work planned at Dominican College, Portstewart; and (ii) the proposed amalgamation of Dunluce College, Coleraine College and North Coast Integrated College.
answer_text: Due to the constrained capital budget position and the significant pressure in relation to Special Educational Needs, funding is necessarily being targeted to meet contractual commitments, keep schools open, pupils safe and provide education access for the most vulnerable. Other projects are being progressed in line with available budget.  The design of the agreed option for the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at Dominican College is currently at RIBA Stage 2, with the business case in development.Budget is available to take the project through the remaining planning and design stages up to pre-tender stage.  A decision to release to tender will be taken based on the budget position at that time.In relation to the new Causeway Academy, I am advised that the Education Authority has initiated a project to locate the three schools onto a single site by September 2026, subject to available funding.  Planning for a new build school will only commence should the project be included in a future Major Works Capital announcement.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when a Programme for Government will be published.
answer_text: We are working at pace to agree and publish a draft Programme for Government.The exact timing of consultation and launch is under consideration as a result of a General Election having been called for the 4th of July.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) why the Environmental Improvement Plan has not been published; and (ii) how the plan will address pollution incidents in our waterways and seas.
