answer_text: (i) I am in the process of seeking Executive approval for the draft Environment Strategy and its adoption as Northern Ireland’s first Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP). I continue to work with my Executive colleagues to  address any issues raised and it remains my wish that the EIP is published as soon as is practicable. (ii)  The Environmental Improvement Plan contains a range of measures designed to reduce both point source and diffuse water pollution, including, for example: a review of River Basin Management plans to incorporate revised consideration of the Lough Neagh blue-green algae issues; the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme, which will provide soil analysis, risk maps and nutrient management training to farmers to reduce excess run-off of nutrients to watercourses; and, ecological modelling frameworks intended to integrate catchment management with coastal water quality. Whilst the EIP will take a strategic approach to the challenges facing our environment, collective action will be required across government, private and public sector and in the community to tackle these issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the Royal College of General Practitioners’ 10-point plan, which aims to retain as many GPs as possible; (ii) for an update on the current number of GPs; and (iii) to detail the actions (a) taken; and (ii) planned to train, recruit and retain as many GPs as possible.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Royal College of General Practitioners ‘A Workforce Fit for the Future’ Retention Strategy and was pleased to speak at its recent launch.  I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice that are set out in the strategy and the impact of the increasing demand and workload pressures on GPs that it highlights.  My Department has taken, and will continue to focus on, actions that will help address a number of the workforce and workload challenges and recommendations set out in the Retention Strategy.  This includes the recently agreed 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract which is also an important step forward to achieving greater stability in General Practice.  The contract makes significant progress against key aims identified by GPs in their negotiations, including provision of £5million dedicated to GP indemnity, as an interim measure, pending identification of the long-term model for future provision.  We are focussing on building the GP workforce through the Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, and increasing the number of GP training places to its current level of 121.  We are also taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams (MDTs) and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists.In terms of the GP Fellowship Scheme, we are considering how the Scheme might be further developed, subject to available funding, to extend the range and scope of Fellowship placement opportunities and provide for Educational Bursaries to be available for all Fellows. 	With regard to the current number of GPs, this information is published on the Business Services Organisation website: General Medical Services Statistics - Business Services Organisation (BSO) Website (hscni.net)My Department is committed to working to identify solutions to the issues facing General Practice, including further roll out of MDTs, exploring digital solutions and working with GPs and HSC Trusts to promote stability in General Medical Services.  However, progress on initiatives will be constrained by the availability of the necessary funding to support this. Regrettably, the 2024-25 Budget outcome for my Department was significantly less than what we required and has resulted in 2.3% less funding in 2024-25 than we spent in 2023-24.  Health will be facing significant budgetary pressures in 2024-25 with some extremely difficult decisions required to manage within the budget allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the mental health support available for farmers and agricultural workers, including those who have suffered animal culls.
answer_text: Mental health support in Northern Ireland is available to anyone who needs it. It is recognised that the farming community is particularly susceptible to poor health and wellbeing, partly driven by the wide demands impacting on farmers across a range of social and economic factors. They often work long and anti-social hours which can lead to social isolation and often have difficulty accessing traditional health care services. I’m also acutely mindful of the pressures and disruptions a positive Bovine TB reading can bring, both to the individual as well as the wider family unit.The Public Health Agency (PHA) has supported Rural Support in delivering a range of services to help promote positive mental health among isolated rural dwellers, farmers and farm families across the region. These include:Conducting information sessions to promote positive mental health, encourage farmers and rural dwellers to seek help and link with other support services;Training for rural support staff and volunteers – to support, strengthen and build skills in mentors working on the rural support programme through coaching and skills development; andMentors providing one-to-one support to individuals experiencing financial distress and where appropriate signposting to other services. The team of mentors work closely with the Health and Social Care Trusts, Healthy Living Centres, Farm Family Health Checks and Rural GPs.  Rural Support’s Life Beyond programme provides support to farming families who have lost someone. It recognises that as well as the grief and trauma of the loss, there is also an added stress on the farm business, its day-to-day management and long-term plans.  The PHA also works in partnership with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) to develop and co-fund the Farm Families Heath Checks (FFHC) Programme. The overall aim of the FFHC Programme is to improve the health and social wellbeing of rural farmers and farm families in Northern Ireland by increasing local access to health screening services, by providing health-related advice and information and to signpost to existing services for further advice and support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5593/22-27, for an update on his way forward for Happy Health Minds and School Holiday Food Grant Payments.
answer_text: Healthy Happy Minds was a pilot which ended in March 2023.  The overall outcome of the post project evaluation was positive, however, there is no central funded service for primary school counselling services.  The challenging financial context across the education system means that budgets and investments are severely constrained.  As with all services that are not statutory, investment is unfortunately limited to budget availability.Ringfenced funding for School Holiday Food Grant payments ceased in 2023 and the payments were therefore discontinued. Food poverty is an issue for many low-income families that that affects all age groups across every day of the year. While tackling “holiday hunger” is an important aspect of this, I believe it is for the Executive to seek to address food poverty across all our society through a wider and more holistic approach within the context of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the terms of the offer from private healthcare providers to alleviate waiting lists in relation to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion; and (ii) whether he is considering this offer to ensure the 94 children waiting on this procedure are treated as soon as possible.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to make funding applications simpler and more accessible for farmers and rural communities.
answer_text: Applications developed throughout my department are unique for each individual purpose and I am committed to making them as straightforward as possible for our customers.  Officials balance this objective, when developing funding applications, against the requirement to gather the minimum amount of information to verify eligibility, identify need and to prevent or detect fraud.  		Digital platforms are used as our primary application method as this helps to streamline the application process and includes the function to pre-populate data for ease of customer reference. My Department also continues to provide helpdesk services and digital support to make online application access as simple and smooth as possible. 		At my request simplification, accessibility and bureaucracy of the future Farm Support and Development Programme will be placed as a standing item on the Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group Agenda with each scheme to be tested against a set of criteria, which is to be agreed following consultation with the Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group, to ensure that the initiatives, packages and measures are simple and easy to access.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 10274/22-27, (i) how he is liaising with local councils in relation to animal welfare updates; and (ii) how his Department assesses whether animal welfare delivery in local council areas adheres to the legislation set by his Department.
answer_text: My department has regular engagement with local councils, and this facilitates a continuous dialogue on animal welfare related matters. The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 enables local councils to appoint individuals to enforce this legislation, as it relates to non-farmed animals. My department has no remit to dictate how councils fulfil this requirement and to do so would undermine their legal and operational authority.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the pathway and timeframes for referring children with delayed developmental milestones; (ii) the impact on children’s long-term development if assessment or treatment is delayed; and (iii) the redress for parents if their child has not received the standard pathway within the appropriate timeframe.
To ask the Minister of Health (i) for their assessment of the need for an Executive-wide strategy on tackling drugs; and (ii) to detail any plans they have to develop such a strategy.
answer_text: (i) Following full public consultation and Executive approval, I launched Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery – A Strategic Framework to Tackle the Harm from Substance Use (2021-31) in September 2021 as Northern Ireland's new 10-year Substance Use Strategy. While health-led, this is an Executive-wide strategy which was co-produced as a specific commitment arising from the New Decade New Approach agreement and was prioritised in response to that commitment and also as a key element of the Executive’s overarching Programme for Government. It also has direct links with the Executive’s strategic framework for public health Making Life Better and with the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31.(ii) As part of the delivery of the strategy’s outcomes, I tasked the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group to co-produce a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework, covering all tiers of service provision. This new Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan will: •	ensure that the population of NI have access to a continuum of service with clear pathways and step-up/step-down provision;•	ensure equal access to community detoxification for alcohol and other drugs across NI;•	ensure that all services are delivered in line with the UK-wide “Drug Misuse and Dependence: Guidelines on Clinical Management” and NICE Guidelines; •	provide support to address the wider physical, mental health, and wellbeing needs of those in treatment, including housing, education, employment, personal finance, healthcare e.g. should be supported to stop smoking and address other physical health conditions; and•	recognise the importance of co-production and strengthen joint working between the community/voluntary sector, service users and peers, and the Health & Social Care Sector.Two other priority pieces of work are also due to report shortly – a Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area, and an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland. The recommendations from both reports have helped to inform the final Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, and it is the intention that all future funding decisions around substance use related services will be based on this new commissioning framework. To date an additional £750,000 has been invested recurrently in the Strategy’s implementation. The full delivery of the strategy will be dependent on additional funding being made available going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the cross-Executive Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime programme;  (ii) to detail any engagement she has had with Executive colleagues in relation to the importance of the outcomes of the programme; (iii) how the actions being taken in this programme are reducing paramilitarism; and (iv) whether any assessment has been made on community safety since the programme began.
answer_text: The Programme is now into its second phase (2021-2025) and has evolved into a comprehensive and evidence-based approach to violence and harm reduction.  Utilising a multi-agency approach to violence reduction, the Programme has been able to document and evidence the extent and nature of the problem. This helps to ensure that responses are evidence-based and effective. The Programme has developed a unique, robust framework for measuring progress across its c100 interventions, measuring positive change across 18 measures. Significant and positive outcomes are being seen across multiple domains. This includes major disruptions to criminal groups, increases in protective factors for those vulnerable to criminal exploitation and measurable reductions in risk, intent to engage in violence, and complex trauma. There are also less obvious but equally important outcomes such as increase collaboration across the public sector and within the Executive. The Programme’s work is also driving systems change in relation to trauma informed approaches and child criminal exploitation. Many more findings are detailed by the Independent Reporting Commission in their 6th report (December 2023), the Northern Ireland Affairs Committee (February 2024) and in an Independent Peer Review (March 2024) of the Programme.  There is unfortunately a persistent misconception that the Programme is focussed only on Justice-related issues.  The issues the Programme works to address may be most visible in the justice space, but they are relevant to all aspects of work undertaken by the Executive and support, particularly in the form of early intervention across the Executive, is needed to address the issue of paramilitarism and crime.The Programme’s governance structures reflect that this is a truly whole of government approach, which allows engagement across the Executive. The Political Advisory Group represents views of all the parties in the Executive. A Sponsor Group, meeting regularly, comprising of senior leads from each department (and chaired by HOCS) provides the Programme with senior leadership and links back to individual Departments and their Ministers. Through robust evaluation, projects are demonstrating how they are making a difference in dealing with the harm in the here and now as well as helping to break the cycle of harm by building individual and community resilience. Some examples of impact include:over 1200 victims of extreme paramilitary violence supported through bespoke projects. This includes young people supported in multiple ways: through youth work, including in two hospital emergency departments, and a multi-agency project, individuals supported through a bespoke project for victims of paramilitary violence and those signposted through multiple projects; Hundreds of boys and young men at risk of paramilitary harm and/or involvement were supported through social work and bespoke mentoring.  This project promotes life and social skills development, and also

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for their assessment of whether the current structures of government are conducive to an outcome-based accountability policy approach; and (ii) to detail any plans they have to review government structures.
answer_text: An outcomes-based approach is a useful tool for developing and monitoring policy. Work done over recent years in this department with regards measuring progress in an outcomes-based way has been valuable and we intend to bring this forward. Within the existing structures of government, there is room for Ministers and departments to adopt the tools which are most useful for their various business areas and functions. We do want to ensure delivery of the Executive’s priorities and have asked for proposals for a delivery-style unit that would sit at the centre and work with all Ministers to provide strategic advice to the Executive as a whole.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the rationale for moving the responsibility of the Children's Services Co-Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 to the Department for Education.
answer_text: The Execuitve Committee agreed in 2016 to a re-organisation of departments and the transfer of certain functions between them.  The functions of The Executive Office relating to policy for Children and Young People and the Childcare Strategy, and the sponsorship of the Children’s Commissioner, were transferred to the Department of Education.  The functions exercisable under the Children’s Services Co-Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 were consequently transferred to the Department of Education as being aligned with these policy responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she is addressing (i) community coercive control; and (ii) child criminal exploitation.
answer_text: Understanding and addressing community coercive control is a priority for the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime (EPPOC). The harms most commonly associated with paramilitary gangs relate to drugs, violence against the person, public order issues and shooting attacks. What is less obvious (and less obviously seen) is how paramilitary and organised crime groups negatively influence people's lives and behaviours, and how they maintain their position in communities through coercive control, intimidation and the threat of violence. ‘Gatekeeping’ is one way in which paramilitary groups cause hidden harm by controlling who speaks for and to communities. Restricting access to services is an extension of gatekeeping, as is limiting the horizons of people as to how and where they go for help.Addressing this requires leadership to invoke change and enable communities to engage with the services, organisations and bodies that they want to in a way that is not dependent on gatekeepers. The aim is not to exclude anyone from having a say but to exclude those who would seek to exert coercive control from deciding who has a say.The approach of the Executive’s Programme on paramilitarism combines robust, specialist law enforcement with projects and approaches that support individual and community resilience, and promote widespread participation and engagement. This includes: place-based community initiatives to equip particular cohorts – women, for example - with the skills, knowledge, and confidence to become involved in transformational community development; capacity building through education and training for community workers to be more effective, confident, and better equipped to manage negative influences, and; initiatives led by communities on community safety, health and wellbeing, arts and culture, area regeneration, personal transition, raising aspirations of young people and a regional project to increase the use of restorative practice in non-justice settings.The responsibility for addressing this extends beyond the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime. It is incumbent upon all of us to play our part in addressing the societal shrug that often occurs around coercive control. In relation to Child Criminal Exploitation details of this have been provided in a previous AQW 7938/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department provides any materials or guidance to support those who choose to educate on consent and healthy relationships.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to the Council for the Curriculum, Examination and Assessment (CCEA) to provide teaching and learning resources and support materials relating to the Northern Ireland Curriculum for grant-aided schools.Between 2018 and 2023, CCEA developed a Relationships and Sexuality Education Hub which has a range of resources for teachers to use or adapt to reflect the flexibility in our curriculum.  The Hub includes teaching materials on consent and healthy relationships.  The Hub is currently undergoing a quality assurance review process and should be available to schools in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what area of his budget the £8.5 million compensation paid out in 2022-23 to the public for damage caused by defects in the public road network is allocated from; and (ii) to detail his plans to fix the road network to prevent damage and save compensation costs.
answer_text: (i) Compensation paid to the public for damage caused by defects on the public road network is paid from my Department’s Resource budget.(ii) My Department has been operating in a difficult financial position for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired. The level of repairs that can be carried out in any financial year depends on the funding available to my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the cross-departmental task and finish group which aims to bring forward recommendations to the Executive in relation to childcare provision; and (ii) when he expects these recommendations to be made to the Executive.
answer_text: The Task and Finish Group was established to bring a cross-departmental perspective to the development of an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  The Group includes representatives from the following departments: Economy, Health, Finance, Communities and Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  Specialist advice is also provided by economists and statisticians.  The Group’s initial focus has been on the identification and design of interim measures which could be introduced in 2024/25 to ease current pressures on parents and providers.  I hope to bring recommendations to the Executive in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of pharmacies that closed between January 2023 and April 2024; (ii) how this figure compares to the previous ten years; (iii) how many of these where independent pharmacies; (iv) how many were located in rural areas; and (v) whether community pharmacy underfunding was the main reason given for these closures.
answer_text: A total of 9 pharmacies closed between January 2023 and December 2023, which are the latest publicly available figures. This compares to a total of 10 closures in the previous ten years (January 2013 to December 2022). Of the 9 pharmacies that closed between January 2023 and December 2023, 3 were independent pharmacies and none were located in rural areas. Closure requests are submitted in writing by letter or email correspondence. There is no requirement to state the reason for closure. In one closure request, underfunding was specifically stated as the reason for closure in the written correspondence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to amend The Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 to provide for the recommendations relating to consent outlined in the Gillen Review.
answer_text: I fully support Sir John Gillen’s conclusion in his Review of the Law and Procedures in Serious Sexual Offences that legislative steps need to be taken to underpin the key principle of consent as defined in Article 3 of the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008: “A person consents if he agrees by choice, and has the freedom and capacity to make that choice”. The Review made a number of recommendations as to how this might be achieved. I remain committed to developing the relevant policy and legislative proposals in this current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is aware that some drivers from Northern Ireland are being told by their insurance companies that they cannot drive in the Republic of Ireland due to their MOT being out of date, despite having a Temporary Exemption Certificate; and (ii) how he has engaged with (a) insurance companies; and (b) the Irish government in relation to his plans.
answer_text: From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate (TEC) automatically applied.This automatic process will be implemented from late-May onwards and once TECs have been applied this will extend the MOT certificate by 12 months from the expiry of the current MOT. This will allow motorists to continue to drive the vehicle legally, if it is kept in a roadworthy condition, is taxed and insured. This will also include driving in other jurisdictions such as Britain and the South.The DVA has consulted with the PSNI and the Association of British Insurers (ABI) who both understand the need to introduce measures to reduce waiting times.  Both organisations have also confirmed they will continue to apply the current mitigations that are in place for customers who cannot secure an MOT appointment before their current certificate expires.The view of the ABI is that a lack of a valid MOT certificate due to delays in the MOT testing regime here would not necessarily invalidate insurance but customers who find themselves in that situation are advised to check their policy documents or speak to their insurer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the support available for women who have suffered a prolapse post-birth; and (ii) any issues which cause support or treatment to be delayed.
answer_text: (i) Treatment and support for women who have suffered a prolapse post-birth prolapse may vary from non-surgical to surgical treatment options.  Non-Surgical Treatment optionsNon-surgical treatment options include pelvic floor exercises and vaginal pessaries (plastic rings or devices to support the prolapse), right through to complex pelvic floor reconstructive surgery.Surgical Treatment optionsThe following surgical inventions are available for prolapse:Vaginal hysterectomyAnterior & posterior repairEnterocele repairPerineorrhaphySacrospinous ligament fixationLaparoscopic sacrocolpopexyLaparoscopic sacrohysteropexy (womb-preserving key-hole surgery)(ii) My officials have advised that there is a recovery period after birth, therefore surgical management wouldn't usually be considered until further down the line (and usually not until women have completed their family), although conservative treatments can be initiated much earlier to improve symptoms including continence issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of social houses with four or more bedrooms available in the Causeway Coast and Glens area; (ii) his assessment of whether this is adequate to meet the current need; and (iii) his plans to increase this number.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“There are currently 317 Housing Executive properties with four or more bedrooms in the Causeway Coast and Glens area.As of 31st December 2023, there were 147 applicants on the waiting list in the Causeway Coast and Glens area with a bedroom requirement of 4 or more. The projected 5 year 2023-28 social housing need for Causeway Coast and Glens is 1,638 units. The Housing Executive’s Commissioning Prospectus indicative housing mix for Causeway Coast and Glens for this period is 17% older persons, 38% families (with focus on small family households) and 45% singles accommodation. Where there is an identified need for 4+ bed accommodation this will be reviewed with the Housing Association to ascertain feasibility on a scheme-by-scheme basis. There have been 190 new social housing Completions in Causeway Coast and Glens area the last 3 years 2021/22 – 2023/24. 11 of these units have 4+ bedrooms. There are currently 277 social housing units under construction. 13 of these units have 4+ bedrooms. There are 699 social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 - 2026/27 in the Causeway Coast and Glens area. It is not possible to provide the definitive detail on the exact housing mix across all these proposed new schemes, however each programmed scheme will be reviewed with the NIHE Place-shaping Team against the housing need for the area.Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.”The construction of new social homes will be dependent on the available budget. The Executive has recently agreed the 2024/25 budget, I will now decide how this is allocated across my department. Funding for the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for 2024/25 has yet to be finalised.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the impact misogynistic social media influencers and online content has on children and young people; (ii) for his assessment of how this impacts their behaviour towards school peers, particularly girls and young women; and (iii) what plans he has to (a) prevent this content being accessed or shared in schools; and (b) provide targeted education to prevent this content influencing young people.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) fully recognises the challenges in keeping children and young people safe from the dangers of inappropriate communication and content.  My officials are cognisant of recent research published by the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland that highlights the promotion of misogynistic ideas online as an area of concern.  I have also recently met with the Secondary Students’ Union of Northern Ireland (SSUNI), which has produced a report detailing pupil experiences of sexual harassment and sexism, the role of social media in encouraging sexist behaviour and the adverse impact of this on some of the pupils who responded to the survey.To prevent children from accessing harmful online content in schools, access is restricted on any C2k computer which is connected to the C2k managed network.  The C2k wireless provision does not allow anyone using a mobile device to access social networking sites.  Pupils, therefore, cannot access social media websites using school ICT equipment without some classroom supervision at a minimum.  If a school decides to provide an alternative internet access beyond the C2k network, the effective filtering of any inappropriate online content rests with the school’s Board of Governors.  Where schools have adopted a Bring Your Own Device policy, pupils may have unfiltered internet access available through their personal 4G/5G networks.  DE issues guidance in relation to online safety and safeguarding more generally.  My officials are currently working to update Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools to reflect emerging best practice in relation to all aspects of safeguarding, including online safety.  DE is also engaged in a range of cross-departmental strategies including the Online Safety Strategy, the Stopping Domestic and Sexual Violence and Abuse Strategy and the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy. You may be aware the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland recently launched the Online Safety hub, delivered as part of the Executive’s “Keeping Children and Young People Safe: An Online Safety Strategy”.  I wrote to all education, early years and youth settings on 26 April to encourage the use of the hub within settings and to promote its use to parents and our children and young people. In terms of educating young people on these matters, the design of the curriculum allows for flexibility for such issues to be taught in our schools.  I can also confirm that additional supports are available via the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app, which is funded by DE and provides tailored support and information to teachers, parents, carers and pupils about safeguarding issues and online harms, including extremist content and social media influencers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the provision of temporary housing in the Causeway Coast and Glens area; (ii) to detail the impact on those who are temporarily housed a great distance away from their current area; and (iii) how he is supporting the Housing Executive to provide more temporary housing to prevent disruption to families and older people.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation. As such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below details the number of temporary accommodation placements in the Causeway Coast and Glens over the past five years:Year Number of Placement in Temporary Accommodation2018/191132019/201462020/214672021/224262022/23550The figures for 2023/24 will be available upon release of the next DfC Housing/Homelessness Bulletin which is scheduled for 23rd May 2024.The Housing Executive will always endeavour to provide temporary accommodation that is close to the current area of the applicant.The Housing Executive will consider factors such as the individual circumstances and the needs of a given Applicant and will take on board any representation made on behalf of the Applicant. The consideration of individual circumstances will include proximity to their current area.Placements can be offered in emergency temporary accommodation that do not meet all of the relevant suitability factors. While an individual may feel the emergency temporary accommodation which has been made available is unreasonable, it should be noted that the Housing Executive can meet its homelessness duty by keeping an Applicant in accommodation that would not be suitable to occupy on a long term basis but it would not be unreasonable for the Applicant to occupy for a short period. This is while the Housing Executive continues to looks for a permanent housing solution, which can be a Housing Executive or Housing Association property, within their area of choice. It should be noted that while the Housing Executive will endeavour to provide a placement that as far as possible, meets the needs of the respective household, there has been a sustained increase in demand for temporary accommodation.In such circumstances, any provision of temporary accommodation will be dictated by availability on a given day.The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to assisting those in temporary accommodation is supported by a Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation which can be found here https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/fd320853-cf6e-4403-908f-d0a0b7dbbaea/Temporary-Accommodation-Strategic-Action-Plan-2022-27.pdf The Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation Year 2 Action Plan can be found here https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/e95603ed-bad9-47ca-90a2-1aef23659665/Year-2-Actions-Strategic-Action-Plan-for-Temporary-Accommodation-2022-27.pdf and contains actions that support the Housing Executive’s delivery of a sustainable supply of good quality, affordable temporary accommodation which is safe, warm and well managed.”

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to detail their plans for Good Relations; and (ii) how Good Relations will be considered in each outcome in the Programme for Government.
answer_text: Allocation of The Executive’s Office budget for 2024/25 is currently under consideration. Plans for Good Relations delivery will be finalised as part of this process.A Programme for Government is being developed at pace and will be brought to the Assembly once agreed by the Executive. It is important that the PfG reflects the agreed position of the entire Executive. We will support Good Relations and hope to see all departments play a role, but the commitments made within a PfG will be for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the announcement on 27 March 2024 of £9.2 million investment in general dental services, to detail (i) when the business cases will be completed for this investment; and (ii) the rationale for this timescale.
answer_text: Following my announcement on 27 March 2024 regarding a £9.2 million investment in General Dental Services, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has been working to develop three Business Cases in line with the process set out by Department of Finance.This is a rigorous process which requires time to facilitate input from a range of staff including Dental Commissioning, Dental Public Health and Finance colleagues with regard to setting the scope of the services, identifying objectives and mitigating potential risks, as well as financial analysis and costings calculations.  This process of due diligence is essential to ensure value for money for public funds, particularly given the value and scale of the investment.I am pleased to advise that the Business Case enabling a 30% enhancement to fees relating to fillings, extractions and root canal treatments has been approved and the enhanced rates will take effect from 1 April 2024.  The other two Business Cases are in the final stages of development, and are likely to be approved in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9590/22-27, (i) what is being done to address the lack of resources to meet capacity; (ii) the reasons for the number of re-consultations; and (iii) what is being done to improve the quality of information submitted.
answer_text: Over the last few years, the Department has undertaken a range of measures to address the lack of resources to meet capacity.  These include a review and update of guidance and internal procedures; temporary redeployment of additional caseworkers; and additional processes have been introduced such as the cessation of updates in order to allow staff to focus on the actual processing of consultations.  Analysis of consultations being receipted demonstrates that approximately 40% of these are re-consultations; the overriding reason for this is poor quality and incomplete documentation submitted with applications. Officials have initiated engagement with a variety of stakeholders including specific sectoral engagements, including with agents, to improve the quality of information submitted with a consultation. In addtion, my Department is working in collaboration with the DfI cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum which aims to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when dental practitioners will be eligible to be paid the enhanced rates for the targeted areas identified in the Dental Access Scheme; and (ii) to identify any risk to practitioners who carry out the work if business cases are not approved.
answer_text: On 27 March 2024 I announced a £9.2m investment in General Dental Services for 2024/25. Included in this investment was 30% enhancement to the fees paid to General Dental Practitioners for filings, extractions and root canal treatment.This enhancement will be applied to all eligible treatments completed in 2024/25, with arrears payments made, as required, until the fees in the Statement of Dental Remuneration are updated.The business case for the enhancement has been approved and as such there is no risk to practitioners who provide these treatments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total spend on General Dental Services in (i) 2019-20; (ii) 2020-21; (iii) 2021-22; (iv) 2022-23; and (v) 2023-24, broken down by month.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) her assessment of the impact of sickness absence in the PSNI and the ability to ensure communities are kept safe; (ii) any conversations she has had with the Chief Constable in relation to this; and (iii) what support he has asked from her Department to improve the situation.
answer_text: (i) Resourcing within the PSNI remains a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I respect the Chief Constable’s operational independence and the role of the Policing Board, therefore it would not be appropriate for me to make an assessment of what are operational matters.(ii) I met with the Chief Constable and Chair of the Poling Board on 1 May 2024 to discuss the pressures facing the PSNI, challenges in managing sick absence within the PSNI, and the pressures these create in delivering effective policing for our communities. I have confidence that it is a priority for the Chief Constable and as such is receiving the appropriate attention. (iii) My department has regular engagement with the PSNI on this and other issues and I will continue to work with the Chief Constable to ensure we have a police service that is properly resourced in all aspects for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) whether an active ageing strategy will be included in the Programme for Government to recognise the results of the recent census that we are an ageing population; and (ii) for their assessment of how an ageing population will impact public services.
answer_text: We know that the proportion of our population over the age of 65 is increasing and this will necessarily change how we deliver public services that support older people.				Discussions are ongoing regarding the development of the Programme. We are looking forward to sharing a draft with the Assembly in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to take forward Lucy’s Law as part of his legislative programme.
answer_text: I am determined to bring forward new animal welfare legislation, however, I am mindful of what can be achieved within the resources available to my Department and the time remaining in this mandate.Nevertheless, I can confirm my desire to deliver ‘Lucy’s Law’ within the lifetime of this Assembly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the negative impacts of second home ownership; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to address this.
answer_text: There is very limited data available on second homes which makes it difficult to establish their extent, location and what impacts they may have.For example, the latest Census for Northern Ireland showed that six percent of all houses had no usual residents. There are a range of possibilities for this including that they are truly vacant, being renovated, self-catering/short-term lets or second homes.My Department and the Housing Executive are beginning research on second home ownership to improve our understanding of the issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the agreed amalgamation of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College; (ii) to confirm the site for the new school; and (iii) whether capital funding will be made available in advance of day one.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has initiated a project to locate Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College on a single site by the effective date of the amalgamation, 1 September 2026.  All three existing school sites are being considered, as well as the former site of Coleraine High School on the Lodge Road.  The EA will also engage with the University of Ulster to clarify any options for a new school however, the long-term ambition of the school for a new school building will require major capital investment and at this time there is no open call for major capital works.     Any identified works required to implement the amalgamation will likely fall in 2025/26 and 2026/27 financial years. My Department does not have its capital budget beyond 2024/25 so I am unable to provide any assurances regarding the availability of capital budget at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department plans to take to ensure community pharmacies can continue to support elderly and vulnerable patients.
answer_text: I am happy to inform the member that earlier this week the Department of Health published a new Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy which aims to fully realise the potential of community pharmacy services to support better health outcomes from medicines and prevent illness. Implementation of the strategy will not only support elderly and vulnerable patients but the whole population.This important piece of work was developed collaboratively by the Department of Health and Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy owners, and describes the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030.Against the backdrop of the current severe budgetary pressures and the multiple demands for additional funding across all parts of health and social care, additional investment continues to be provided to the community pharmacy sector. The level of government funding for community pharmacies in NI compares favourably to other parts of the UK. However, more will be required to fully deliver this Strategic Plan up to 2030. I am very conscious that the Community Pharmacy sector – like all sectors across health and social care – is keen to see further investment.The Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy is available on the Department of Health website:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/community-pharmacy-strategic-plan-2030

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of delays of cases across all courts; (ii) to detail her plans to speed up the justice system in relation to this; and (iii) for a timeframe of when improvement from her actions can be expected.
answer_text: Tackling delay is one of the biggest challenges facing the criminal justice system and is a key priority for the Department and relevant justice agencies. However, reducing the time it takes to complete criminal cases is a challenging and complex issue. 	Prior to Covid, the Department’s efforts to tackle delay were having a positive effect, with the overall average time to complete a criminal case falling from 169 days in December 2018 to 149 days in March 2020, when lockdowns were first introduced. This reduction was largely driven by improvements in the magistrates’ courts.  Based on internal management information, provisional statistics show that the average time taken for Quarter 4 2023-24 was 191 days. This is down from the post–Covid highpoint of 232 days, which was recorded in Quarter 1 2022-23.  However, present demand is increasing, with a rise in the number of cases entering both the Crown Court and magistrates’ courts, and we are continuing to tackle backlogs created due to Covid.  Courts are generally disposing of more cases than they are receiving, meaning progress is being made; however, it will still be some time before the system is fully recovered and this will be dependent on the availability of resources to continue to operate at an increased level of throughput.My Department did not receive funding to address Covid backlogs and currently faces further significant budgetary pressures which will affect our ability to implement initiatives to address delays in the system. There is no doubt the future financial outlook will present challenges to the recovery of the justice system.In relation to plans to speed up the justice system, the Criminal Justice Board has prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay within the system; however, the pace and scope of change will be greatly dependent on funding.  Improved practices also take time to work through the system and so it is difficult to provide a definitive timeframe within which measurable improvements will be delivered. Nonetheless, I remain determined to reduce avoidable delay across all parts of the justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how each stage of the criminal justice process is informed to (a) recognise and; (b) approach in a sensitive manner, cases involving violence against women and girls; and (ii) the training that is provided on these matters.
answer_text: I recognise how important it is to that at every stage of the criminal justice process cases involving violence against women and girls are handled with appropriate sensitivity. Comprehensive measures have been introduced across all criminal justice organisations. It is important to emphasise that operational matters within the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) remain the responsibility of the Chief Constable. Therefore, I cannot comment on specific police procedures or practices as I have a duty to respect that independence. Similarly, the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) is a non-ministerial Department and is not accountable to the Department of Justice; their decisions and practices remain the responsibility of the Director of Public Prosecutions. The judiciary, in their handling of cases are independent of the Department of Justice: practice directions are issues by the Lady Chief Justice to guide the judiciary in various areas of case handling.As part of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (2021) and the Protection from Stalking Act (2022) the NI Assembly legislated for mandatory training for a number of criminal justice organisations and we monitor compliance with that legislation. My Department also funds Victim Support NI to provide a range of support services to all victims of crime including women and girls.  Victim Support NI are funded to undertake continuous training and all staff and volunteers must complete mandatory trauma informed training.The Gillen Review set out over 30 recommendations to improve how criminal justice organisations and the legal profession can improve the training delivered to those who deal with victims of sexual crime, the majority of whom are female.   Work in this area includes the development of a Trauma Informed Learning Framework which sets out a range of training solutions intended to result in increased and improved knowledge and greater awareness and sensitivity for those dealing with victims of serious sexual offences. A multi-agency Training Group is in place to monitor delivery and impact of this training.In addition to these developments, my Department has established an advocacy service to support victims of domestic and sexual abuse who are progressing through the criminal justice process. The service provides guidance and support to victims, tailored to individual needs.  Again, the majority of victims supported by ASSIST NI are female, reflecting the nature of these crimes which disproportionately affect women and girls. All staff undergo an induction and training plan when recruited by ASSIST NI which reflects the gendered nature of domestic and/or sexual abuse.    In addition, my Department has introduced and funds Sexual Offences Legal Advisers (SOLAs) which provides legal advice and information to adult complainants in serious sexual offence cases as they navigate the criminal justice system.  SOLAs, who receive continuous training including on trauma informed practice, ar

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department has taken to ensure unexplained wealth orders are used to their full extent.
answer_text: The Criminal Finances Act 2017 introduced the powers for specified agencies to apply to the High Court for an unexplained wealth order (UWO) to be made.  The relevant Northern Ireland provisions of the 2017 Act were brought into operation on 28 June 2021.  The role of my Department is to ensure that the legislative tools and options are there for operational partners to use.  The use of UWOs is, therefore, an operational matter, and the  National Crime Agency is the lead agency for UWOs in Northern Ireland.  The National Crime Agency have advised that they consider the potential use of a UWO in every civil financial investigation where the statutory threshold is met, in particular where there is no clear link between the property in question and unlawful conduct and/or where the respondent has accrued assets that are not commensurate with their known income/employment. Their use must be considered within the wider context of the UK’s asset recovery system.  The National Crime Agency will seek to use the most appropriate investigative order according to the circumstances of the case.  In the vast majority of cases other investigative tools, such as Disclosure Orders, are a much more effective means to progress an investigation.  Other asset recovery tools are used successfully in Northern Ireland on an ongoing basis, including applications by police and the National Crime Agency for Account Freezing Orders and Listed Asset Orders, which were also introduced by the Criminal Finances Act 2017.It is important to note that they are a useful investigatory, rather than a recovery tool.  A UWO can be sought without any civil or criminal proceedings having begun.  While they have the potential to enhance the ability of enforcement agencies to take steps to recover the proceeds of crime, using a wider range of asset recovery powers, they are not in themselves a means to recover the proceeds of crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for choosing the specific ages of private vehicles to be eligible for Temporary Exemption Certificates.
answer_text: From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate (TEC) automatically applied from the date their current MOT certificate expires. Historically, the youngest vehicles presenting for test have had the highest pass rates. As legislation requires that private cars must have an initial roadworthiness test when they are 4 years old these cars are ineligible to receive a TEC.  I have therefore limited the eligibility criteria to younger cars which have a valid MOT certificate in place.  Vehicle licensing information shows that around 115,000 cars may be eligible for a TEC, which should make a notable improvement to waiting times. I appreciate that external factors can negatively impact the DVA’s plans for service delivery.  A 12-month gap between the registration dates of the two eligible groups of cars will provide flexibility for the review of the use of TECs in the future.I am satisfied that the selected dates ranges for TECs strikes the right balance between managing waiting times and the impact on road safety.As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  Vehicle owners also have a legal responsibility for maintaining their vehicles in a roadworthy condition, regardless of MOT status.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department’s policy on freeing up hospital beds occupied by patients ready to be discharged but who decline to return home due to the absence of a care package; (ii) the average daily cost of a home care package; and (iii) the average daily cost of a day in a care home.
answer_text: (i) At any one time across Northern Ireland there are a number of delayed discharges from hospital sites.  These delays affect patient flow through the hospital system and the ability of Trusts to admit sick patients into beds.  It also has a direct impact on those requiring a bed for elective surgery and negatively impacts ambulance waits at Emergency Departments as there are no beds to admit into.  Ambulances delayed at hospitals, in turn negatively impacts on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s capacity to provide essential emergency care in the community.Due to the necessity to free up hospital beds all Trusts have brought into effect contingency measures, whereupon if a package of care essential to manage the person safely at home is not available, they are offered a contingency bed.    Contingency beds offer the opportunity of safe continuing care in a care home setting.  Contingency placements are provided free of charge to the service user and care home fees are waived temporarily.  The use of contingency beds is appropriate at this time and fully supported by the Department of Health.(ii)  The daily cost of a home care package varies as each package of care is based on the individual's assessment of need which varies from person to person. The Northern Ireland average cost for one hour of home care is £20.36. The average weekly package is 12.7 hours. (iii) The daily cost of a day in a care home is dependent on the individual’s assessment of need which varies from person to person.  There are different categories of care within care home and some individuals may need enhanced care.   The Northern Ireland average cost of a contingency bed is £220 per day.Also, to note that in recognition of the regional shortage of domiciliary care, I have recently announced a £70m support package for social care providers and hospices.  This funding will help independent sector organisations deal with rising costs from the start of the new financial year, particularly forthcoming minimum wage increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent on repairing potholes and similar road defects between April 2018 and the end of March 2023, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information in the format requested, however I can provide you with details of Structural Maintenance spend by council area which includes the cost for repairing potholes.Table 1 below sets out the amount of money the Department has spent on Resource and Capital Structural Maintenance, in the period 1 April 18 to 31 March 2023 by financial year.Table 1: Total Structural Maintenance (Resource & Capital) by District Council Area – Financial Years 2018-19 to 2022-23District CouncilStructural MaintenanceYear18-19Year19-20Year20-21Year21-22Year22-23Figures in £mBelfast City CouncilResource1.41.21.81.71.8Capital7.87.15.36.18.4Total 9.28.37.17.810.2Lisburn & Castlereagh City CouncilResource0.80.70.81.01.0Capital5.55.95.17.96.5Total 6.36.65.98.97.5Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough CouncilResource1.30.51.11.00.6Capital5.24.82.86.17.3Total 6.55.33.97.17.9Mid & East Antrim Borough CouncilResource1.31.11.81.41.8Capital7.28.67.210.18.6Total 8.59.79.011.510.4Causeway Coast & Glens Borough CouncilResource1.51.11.21.31.1Capital9.19.910.310.110.3Total 10.611.011.511.411.4Derry City & Strabane District CouncilResource1.31.11.01.21.6Capital7.88.26.76.59.8Total 9.19.37.77.711.4Mid Ulster District CouncilResource1.91.92.31.61.7Capital9.710.59.88.619.8Total 11.612.412.110.221.5Fermanagh and Omagh District CouncilResource2.11.93.11.71.6Capital12.211.011.09.711.1Total 14.312.914.111.412.7Ards & North Down Borough CouncilResource0.40.50.50.50.7Capital4.94.14.55.42.3Total 5.34.65.05.93.0Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough CouncilResource2.62.32.72.52.4Capital12.010.39.710.99.5Total 14.612.612.413.411.9Newry, Mourne and Down District CouncilResource2.11.81.92.22.1Capital10.511.99.97.217.4Total 12.613.611.89.419.5Capital Structural Maintenance includes resurfacing and reconstruction, asphalt patching, surface dressing, capital investment in both drainage infrastructure and in the structural stability of embankments. Resource Structural Maintenance covers patching of carriageway and footways.Expenditure will have been determined by budget availability and capacity in a given year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how a comprehensive childcare strategy will reduce levels of child poverty.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities, including our young people, right across Northern Ireland.I believe that children experiencing poverty, or at risk of experiencing poverty deserve equal access to services and supports, including appropriate childcare and education.  Ensuring the availability of appropriate and affordable childcare that meets the needs of parents and children will be a vital aspect of the Executive’s work to address both poverty and gender equality and will be a key aspect of the wider Anti-Poverty Strategy. I am, therefore, very supportive of the broader Childcare Strategy and believe that work on the development of the Anti-Poverty and Childcare Strategies should be closely linked and complementary.In addition, the Department for Communities also promotes the value of work, and the positive impact it can have on poverty, health and wellbeing, social inclusion, and our economy. Difficulties accessing childcare can present significant barriers for parents, and particularly mothers, on lower incomes in accessing the labour market. For example, lone parents are the most likely family type to experience poverty. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement as it is an Executive Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps being taken to reduce waiting lists for children’s outpatient appointments, particularly in relation to the recommendations from the Royal College of Paediatrics and Children’s Health; (ii) the reasons behind the 172.6 per cent increase in these lists from June 2016 to June 2023; and (iii) his assessment of the long-term risks to a child’s future mental and physical health if conditions are not treated as a child, relative to those of an adult.
answer_text: The issue of children on prolonged outpatient waiting lists is something that is of high importance to me.  Unfortunately, longer waiting lists are something which has been experienced by paediatric and adult services regularly in Northern Ireland.  The RCPCH “Worried and Waiting” Report highlights the key issues facing the service but also means to address same.  Many of these recommendations also reflect the key priorities of my department.the steps being taken to reduce waiting lists for children’s outpatient appointments, particularly in relation to the recommendations from the Royal College of Paediatrics and Children’s HealthMy department published the Paediatric Palliative and End of Life Care Strategy and the Strategy for Paediatric Healthcare Services in Hospitals and in the Community in 2016.   Both strategies were completed after considerable consultation with all relevant stakeholders across the sector in NI and beyond to ensure they were as comprehensive as possible;In each strategy there are 23 ambitious objectives that cover a wide range of issues: -Within paediatric palliative care, from diagnosis of a palliative or life limited condition through to bereavement support for families of those children who have passed away;Within Hospital Care and Community – from issues in community/primary care, urgent and emergency care, paediatric surgery to the need for networked approaches and accurate data to better manage the needs of children and young people in NI;The strategies were introduced in part to introduce a new way of working in children’s health care services to meet the demands of a system in the 21st century with increasing demands upon the system and advancements in how conditions are now treated.  These strategies offer the means to change how our healthcare services for children and young people are delivered and ultimately improve waiting list demands.the reasons behind the 172.6 per cent increase in these lists from June 2016 to June 2023;Whilst both strategies were published in 2016 additional funding to help implement the objectives were not available until 2019, and of course the funding provided was only to start to implement the strategy, more funding is required to fully implement the strategy. From this point the system worked collectively to find means to implement systemic change on how services are delivered for children and young people which would have had also had positive implications on waiting lists.However, with the arrival of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 this had a huge impact on children and young people across NI.  Whilst the pandemic didn’t have serious clinical implications for the vast majority of children and young people they were impacted by many of the indirect implications of the pandemic.  This included access to school and many of the societal things that children need to thrive.  During the period many children’s health care services were suspended and only the most urgent/emergency clinics

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any ongoing work with the Department for Infrastructure to remedy issues with wastewater limitations and planning procedures; and (ii) the areas where these issues are less problematic so that the development of private and social housing in alternative areas can be pursued in the short-term.
answer_text: Through my Department’s work to develop a draft housing supply strategy, wastewater constraints and the planning system were identified as key issues. Department for Infrastructure officials have been involved throughout the strategy development process, including through representation on the project board overseeing strategy work. I am working to finalise the strategy with the intention of bringing it to the Executive as soon as possible.  Prior to this, I intend to write to the Infrastructure Minister, seeking support for the strategy and a commitment for the Departments to work closely together on these issues.In terms of areas potentially impacted by wastewater constraints, NI Water provides online updates advising of catchments where negative planning responses may be provided by it, in consideration of applications. These updates can be accessed at: www.niwater.com/capacity-information/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on steps to improve pay and conditions for care workers; and (ii) for his assessment of how these improvements will make the job more attractive to the most appropriate, trained and experienced carers.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answers to AQW 8598/22-27 and AQW 10765/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the repercussions, from this summer onwards, for (i) Lough Neagh; (ii) downstream waterways from Lough Neagh; and (iii) the north coast should plans to address pollution and blue-green algae levels not be initiated.
answer_text: My Department is already taking forward a range of work aimed at addressing the problems at the Lough. I advised you of some of this work in my response to AQW 5594/22-27.It is widely accepted that the blue-green algae blooms which were seen in watercourses across Northern Ireland last year, particularly in Lough Neagh, are highly likely to recur both in 2024 and in subsequent years. & (iii) This will have serious implications for not only the Lough itself but for downstream waterways and north coast beaches.I have made it clear that my assessment is that there are no quick fixes to the occurrence and probable re-occurrence of blue-green algae blooms and that we need to work collectively to secure longer term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what steps he is taking to ensure that increasing profits made by major supermarket retailers are reflected in the prices paid to Northern Ireland farmers; and (ii) for his assessment of the current economic situation of family-owned farms.
answer_text: The regulation of contractual relationships, and other related competition issues (e.g. pricing) is a reserved matter. The UK government has been undertaking a series of reviews to help improve transparency and fairness in agreements between UK buyers and farmers and Defra has recently introduced new legislation to mandate minimum contractual terms in the UK dairy sector. My department will continue to work closely with Defra, and with the Scottish and Welsh governments, as this supply chain work progresses.The economic situation of family-owned farms will be shown within my Departments ‘NI Agricultural Incomes 2023’ release which is due to be published soon.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any plans to adopt and oversee a joined-up approach to security at hospitals to best protect staff from aggressive or violent patients or visitors; and (ii) how a policy of more prevention rather than cure in terms of violence in hospitals may be established.
answer_text: (i)    All HSC Trusts strive to provide a joined-up approach to security on hospital sites. This includes employing dedicated security staff who maintain static posts and perform routine patrols to help deter incidents but also to respond urgently to all requests for assistance with violent and aggressive persons as best possible. CCTV and access control systems are also in place on many sites as an additional deterrent to detect and deny unauthorised entry. Security staff, supervisors and management participate in meetings and sit on specific groups with internal stakeholders such as clinical staff, ward and department managers, clerical and reception staff, estates, pharmacists, social workers, H&S teams, trainers and Unions etc, where security policies are considered and reviewed.Trusts also engage with external stakeholders such as local community groups, drug and homelessness outreach organisations and PSNI Neighbourhood Policing teams along with crime prevention liaison officers. Challenging behaviours and incidents are examined to provide practical advice and support to staff.(ii)   On 12 December 2023 my Department launched a new Management of Violence and Aggression in the Workplace HSC Framework. The purpose of this framework is to outline the HSC commitment in partnership with staff representatives, to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace, and to ensure associated structures, policies and support are in place to enable staff to work safely. This regional framework must form the basis of any policies and processes developed locally by the various HSC bodies in relation to dealing with such incidents in a diverse range of working environments. Each HSC employer will have differing arrangements in place, supported by appropriate risk assessments according to the setting.The regional framework is accessible on the Department’s website.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-afc-6-2023_0.pdf

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the steps they (i) have taken; and (ii) will take to ensure the relevant religious institutions contribute financially to the redress scheme in recognition of their role in the historical institutional abuse of children.
answer_text: On 28 July 2021 Mr Paul Sweeney was appointed as an Independent Facilitator to take forward negotiations with the six voluntary institutions.  Following a meeting between Ministers, the institutions and the Independent Facilitator in October 2021, Mr Sweeney drafted a framework for reaching an agreement between the Executive and the six voluntary institutions. Mr Sweeney facilitated meetings with officials and the six institutions in April and May 2023 to discuss contributions and his proposed framework.  Since then, there has been regular official-led engagement with the six institutions.   This engagement has included the production and scrutiny of accurate individual datasets for each institution, consideration of complex determinations where a victim or survivor may have been resident in multiple institutions, and consideration, on the basis of the Hart Report, of each institutions’ ability to pay and any payments or other outlay that the institutions may wish to be taken into account.We met the Independent Facilitator last month for a positive discussion regarding the next stages of the process. Constructive discussions on appropriate contributions from the institutions are ongoing.  As recommended by the Hart Report, if an agreement as to the amount to be paid cannot be reached, we will submit all issues to mediation.  If mediation fails, then all remaining issues will be dealt with by agreeing with the relevant institutions to submit to binding arbitration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans for integrating education on understanding and addressing intergenerational loneliness in the schools curriculum.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Curriculum provides a range of opportunities to address the issue of loneliness, in all its forms. Personal Development and Mutual Understanding (PDMU) and Learning for Life and Work (LLW) are statutory Areas of Learning within the curriculum.  From foundation stage pupils can explore a range of topics including responsibilities and respect for self and others, social responsibility, forming healthy relations and the importance of family as a caring unit. As with all Areas of Learning within the curriculum, it is a matter for teachers to decide which specific topics, including the incorporation of contemporary issues, should be covered to best suit the needs of their pupils. This flexibility presents opportunities to equip students with skills such as empathy, understanding and relationship building that can help to combat loneliness within our society through fostering meaningful connections across different age groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 31197/17-22, (i) when the outline business case for HMP Magilligan was submitted; (ii) to provide details on the outcome of this, including actions that will result and the associated timeframe; and (iii) whether funding is available and in place for the planned work.
answer_text: Approval for the associated expenditure in the Outline Business Case 1 (OBC) for the Redevelopment of Magilligan Prison Project was granted by Department of Finance (DoF) in May 2022.  However, due to significant cost inflation, NIPS has determined that this work should proceed on a phased approach, determined by urgent business need, commencing with the preparation of OBC1s for a New Kitchen and Café and an additional wing at Halward House.  Funding requirements for these projects will be informed by the OBC1s which are being progressed.  Funding allocations will be determined by budget availability.  All contractual commitments will have to be prioritised before other projects can be funded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of whether concerns about the spread of zebra mussels, and their relationship with trout-stocking, could be mitigated by restocking in colder months when the water temperature drops below the 12 degree Celsius breeding level required for zebra mussels; and (ii) to detail the difference in risk between the currently halted movement of trout from fish farms and that of the currently permitted relocation of elvers to Lough Neagh from areas such as the Severn and Loire, which are considered to have high number
answer_text: A stocking protocol is being developed and reviewed for periods when zebra mussels veligers are not present. However, there is also a gap in our knowledge as to the life cycle of zebra mussels in Northern Ireland with scientific evidence suggesting zebra mussels may be spawning at temperatures exceeding 9oC. DAERA is currently considering how to address the knowledge gap.The major risk associated with stocking live trout is in the river water that they would be transported in, as it may contain zebra mussel veligers. Elvers used for stocking from France are caught in the marine environment. Bio-security measures are also in place to transfer elvers to Lough Neagh. After capture, elvers are held in potable water.
