They are then removed from the water and placed into polyboxes. This is done "dry" with ice in their own slime before being transferred. Only one stocking has taken place from France for glass eels since the suspension of stocking from Movanagher was put in place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the reason behind the cessation of trout stocking from the Movanagher hatchery; (ii) the impact this has on trout numbers in the Lower Bann; (iii) the impact this has on private fisheries such as the Loughinsholin Fish Farm; and (iv) what consideration he has given to reducing the fee charged for angling permits.
answer_text: After the identification of zebra mussels at Movanagher fish farm in June 2023, the Department suspended the movement of live fish from Movanagher to comply with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985.		 The Department does not stock the Lower Bann; it is a wild fishery. 	The Department is currently withholding permission to move any live fish or aquatic species from the Lower Bann in order to comply with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985. This includes Loughinsholin Fish Farm. Private angling fisheries can still purchase live fish from other fish farms in Northern Ireland or the Republic of Ireland.		The permit fee has not increased since 2016. A review of permits and licences is underway, and I will be considering options towards the end of 2024. I would also highlight that the DAERA game permit gives permission for the permit holder to fish a Public Angling Estate water, but it does not guarantee the stocking of fish into a water or the level of fish stocks within a water. This information is available to anglers on the Department’s website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the work that has been carried out on addressing intergenerational loneliness; (ii) the work that is planned; (iii) the policies from other parts of the United Kingdom, in particular Wales and Scotland, that could be adopted in developing a loneliness strategy; and (iv) how this work will be funded and included in the forthcoming Programme for Government.
answer_text: Work to develop a Programme for Government which meets the needs of everyone in our society is moving forward quickly.We know that everyone is likely to experience feelings of loneliness at some point in their lifetime and the effects of feeling lonely can negatively impact a person’s health, wellbeing and overall quality of life. This policy area is being led by the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the opportunities for older people living in rural areas, in particular in East Londonderry, to access help and support to improve online skills such as (a) cyber security; and (b) online banking; and (ii) how he is ensuring that older people are not disadvantaged in terms of accessing goods, services and facilities due to a lack of this knowledge.
answer_text: DIGITAL INCLUSIONMy Department has a small Digital Inclusion Unit to promote online services and to give people here the skills, confidence, motivation and trust to navigate the online world. The aim is to promote a digitally inclusive society and increase the use of online channels to access our public services through a range of projects by working in close partnership with a number of public and private organisations to help digitally excluded people get online. The Digital Inclusion team’s current Go ON NI programme enables the public to use Government services online. The programme has been very successful, and it continues to grow by targeting events and promotional activities at the harder to reach digitally excluded sections of the population here.These key groups include: •	rural communities•	those aged over 50•	people with physical or mental impairment•	socio-economic disadvantaged communities•	long-term unemployedThe Go ON NI programme ensures rural and older communities are not excluded from accessing information and services by working with various organisations to recruit digital champions, providing tuition, and detailing the help that is available to assist these communities in accessing online services. The programme provides free digital training sessions in libraries, community centres and hubs. These digital skills sessions can help anyone go online for the first time or improve their skills. The Digital Inclusion Team are also pro-active members of the Digital Assist Steering Group, a cross-sectoral body chaired by Business in the Community which helps ensure that government services are not delivered in isolation through appropriate interaction with organisations from across the private, voluntary and community sectors. There is also a working partnership with the Age Friendly Networks in all council areas.A number of events have taken place recently, including sessions in Dungiven (Glor Hall) and in Limavady Library.  The three events in Dungiven covered online shopping, online safety and a session with Danske Bank.During Positive ageing month and Get Online week in October 2023, as well as Spring Online week in May 2024, digital information events were held in several libraries. In addition, all 96 libraries run Go ON & GOT IT sessions which are free to attend, throughout the year.My Department also has responsibility for the NI Cybersecurity Centre, which works with public, private, third sector organisations and citizens to improve their ability to defend against cyber attacks, increase their knowledge of cyber threats, and become more cyber resilient. The website contains useful advice and guidance to help improve cyber security awareness for the general public.We recognise that public services are increasingly moving towards the provision of information and delivery of services through online, web-based and digital platforms, and the Digital Inclusion team aims to help and support people through this transition. Fo

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they will (i) acknowledge the ageing population by including proposals to meet their needs in the Programme for Government, with specific regard to (a) healthcare; (b) loneliness; (c) poverty; (d) healthy ageing; and (e) public transport; and (ii) include older people and their representative groups in creating such policies.
answer_text: Work to develop a Programme for Government which meets the needs of everyone in our society, whatever their age, is moving forward quickly.We know that the proportion of our population over the age of 65 is increasing rapidly, and this will change how we deliver public services that support older people.We hope to make it available to the Assembly for scrutiny soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to (i) ensure older people can access GP services in a timely way; and (ii) allay fears that they may not be able to do so.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for all patients, including older people.  GP services have been experiencing sustained, high levels of demand since before the COVID-19 pandemic.  Practices have therefore continued to adapt how services are delivered in response to increasing pressures. In the region of 55% of consultations remain face to face, accommodating those patients less familiar with or without access to alternative technology.  However, in response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care.  Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  As part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  It is essential that we continue to use of all the tools at our disposal to ensure that we can help our services to work as effectively as possible to meet the need for access to primary care support and treatment.  It is also worth noting that GPs are independent contractors who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on steps being taken to assist older people to access (i) local banks in person; and (ii) online banking, following cross-departmentally facilitated and funded classes on internet banking.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy does not have direct responsibility for financial services, as the regulation of financial services is a reserved matter.The Department of Finance has a limited role in directly influencing decisions by local banks for the same reason, however, does have a lead role in engaging with them on banking matters. Minister Caoimhe Archibald MLA held a roundtable meeting with senior representatives from local banks on 1 May 2024. The Minister of Finance used this meeting to urge local banks to protect the local branch network and ensure communities, including older people, impacted by branch closures, are able to access banking services. The Minister also emphasised the importance of quickly rolling out further banking hubs in those areas left without bank branches.The Finance Minister will continue to engage with banking representatives as well as the Credit Unions, Post Office, trade unions, business bodies, consumer and community organisations and also the Treasury and the regulatory authorities to see how the sector can best support customers.In addition, the Department of Finance has a small Digital Inclusion team to promote online services and to give people the skills to navigate the online world. In 2023/24 The Go ON NI programme helped almost 7,000 people with digital training including sessions called “Organise Your Life Online” which included the topic of online banking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he will ensure workplaces (i) adopt age-inclusive policies; (ii) identify the factors that support older people in the workplace; and (iii) work to comprehensively establish these.
answer_text: Workers who are supported and valued in the workplace are happier, more productive and more likely to remain with their employer. As people continue to live longer, the number of older people within the workforce will continue to grow. Age is a protected characteristic under our human rights and equality legislation, therefore employment rights, entitlements and protections apply equally, regardless of age. My department funds the Labour Relations Agency which provides an advice, guidance and early conciliation service for employers and their employees. This includes an employment document toolkit resource to assist employers in the drafting of workplace policies. We also provide information and guidance to support employers and employees through the NI Direct and NIBusiness Info websites.Fundamental employment and equality rights are ultimately enforceable through the Industrial and Fair Employment Tribunals if no agreed resolution of disputes can be reached, including matters concerning age related discrimination.As part of the consultation to support the development of a Good Jobs Bill, I will be seeking views on enhanced rights to make a statutory flexible working request from the first day of employment and the introduction of a right to carer’s leave. These important policies will be available to employees of all ages, however they will be an important means of supporting older people to remain within or re-enter the workplace. Communication and engagement with groups representing the rights of older people, employees, employers and their representative bodies will be important to ensure any legislative changes are adopted by all employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps he is taking to strengthen the security of tenure for residents of private sector care homes and their human rights protections, within the Regional Care Home Contract, particularly in relation to the termination of residency; and (ii) any changes planned for the Regional Care Home Contract with a view to better safeguard the rights and wellbeing of care home residents.
answer_text: The current Regional Care Home Contract sets out the circumstances under which a Provider can  terminate a residency. These include instances where Providers assess they are no longer able to meet the needs of a Resident or where the behaviour of the Resident or any other circumstances relating to the Resident are materially detrimental to the safety and welfare of other Residents or the conduct of the Provider. Termination of any residency is always a last resort and all possible efforts will be made between the Resident (their relatives or representatives), the respective Trust and the Provider to resolve any issues that have arisen. Where  this is not achievable and termination is the only remaining option,  the Trust and the Provider will work together to ensure the needs of the Resident are met during this process.My Department has established a Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF), the purpose of which is to provide a formal mechanism for the Department and representatives of the Social Care Sector, across the statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors, to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements in social care.A Commissioning and Contracting Workstream has been set up under this Forum to review the existing Regional Care Home Contract which has been in place for a number of years. While this work is looking at the contract in its entirety it will also consider how a culture of human rights can be embedded in a revised Contract.The review of the contract will consider all relevant learning from COPNI’s recently published report entitled, ‘Respecting Residents’ Rights in Care Homes: A Review of the Regional Care Home Contract’, along with similar recommendations relating to tenure agreements as set out within the report issued by the Independent Review Team into events at Dunmurry Manor Care Home. Alongside the work of the Forum, and resulting from the Independent Review Team’s suite of Reports into events at Dunmurry Manor Care Home, the NI Public Services Ombudsman (NIPSO), through their Complaints Standards Team, intends to develop a range of training packages and guides for public bodies to support all those involved in complaints handling from senior managers through to front line staff. This training will be rolled out to care home providers as well as a number of other organisations, as a way of supporting and encouraging best practice.  This work will also see compliance monitoring ensuring those public sector bodies subject to the new arrangements are fulfilling all their responsibilities and complying with the new standards.  My officials are in regular contact with NIPSO in relation to the development and implementation of this work.It remains fundamental that residents and their families are able to complain where they have genuine concerns about the care provided within a care home without fear of retribution by a care home or its staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how he is engaging with and facilitating older people who want to work, remain in work or re-enter employment; (ii) how workplaces have been advised to better facilitate the needs and requirements of older workers; and (iii) his assessment of how, and in what ways, older workers can uniquely contribute to modern workplaces.
answer_text: Our ability to achieve a more prosperous and inclusive society is dependent upon the success of our economy. As people are living longer, the proportion of older people who are represented in our workforce is continuing to grow. Improving the quality of work through my good jobs agenda will provide for a more inclusive workforce.  My department has delivered age-specific interventions, such as the Ignite Your Skills pilot initiative, which targeted those aged 40+, and continues to support several initiatives which enable people of all ages to upskill or reskill, and thus improve their employability. My department-led Skills, Equality Diversity and Inclusion (SEDI) sub-group considers the access and participation in skills and education of under-represented groups. The older age cohort is a specific focus of this sub-group.DfE Careers Service provides impartial and personalised advice and guidance on an all-age basis, delivered by professionally qualified Careers Advisers.  The aim is to ensure that all individuals, regardless of age or ability, have access to independent careers guidance and advice which helps them to maximise their potential and contribute to their community and the local economy.Careers Advisers can help adults to explore their personal strengths, skills and abilities and explore opportunities for re-skilling and up-skilling, informed by the anticipated needs of the labour market. This includes opportunities such as All Age Apprenticeships which were introduced in September 2023 with the aim of encouraging more adults to undertake an apprenticeship.  Opening apprenticeships to all will help to create more careers options for people of all ages, attract more adults back in the workplace, supporting inclusion and a culture of lifelong learning.   It can also help address skills gaps in key sectors such as Health & Social Care, where the life experiences of older workers can be of particular benefit.Older workers bring a wealth of experience which helps to foster a more diverse working environment and will help us to achieve our economic ambitions of increased employment and productivity. It is right therefore that those older people who wish to remain within or re-enter the workforce are able to do so freely.Age is a protected characteristic under our human rights and equality legislation: therefore employment rights, entitlements and protections apply equally, regardless of age. These rights are enforceable through the Industrial and Fair Employment Tribunals. My department funds the Labour Relations Agency which provides an advice, guidance and early conciliation service. We also provide information and guidance to employers and employees through the NI Direct and NIBusiness Info websites.Following a co-design and consultation process, it is my intention to bring forward an employment rights Bill within the current Assembly mandate to ensure the employment law framework remains fit for purpose and operates effectively for b

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the complaints procedure and analysis of complaints within a care home; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to create an independent advocacy service to oversee a resident’s complaints to ensure the complaints procedure is not solely overseen, assessed and acted upon by the care provider.
answer_text: Care home residents currently have the right to make a complaint under the extant HSC Complaints Procedure. Where a complainant is dissatisfied with the outcome of the HSC Complaints Procedure, they will be advised of their right to refer their complaint to the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman (NIPSO).  NIPSO can carry out independent investigations into complaints about poor treatment or service or the administrative actions of HSC organisations.  NIPSO also has powers to investigate complaints about social care decisions.   The Patient and Client Council can also assist if people are unhappy with the outcome of a process or review or the way in which it was conducted and can assist people to make a complaint to a Health and Social Care organisation or a submission to NIPSO.Separately, NIPSO is also progressing a programme of reform to create common complaints handling standards for all bodies across the public sector.  My Department is committed to working closely with NIPSO, where appropriate, to deliver on this important programme of work.The Department has no current plans to create an independent advocacy service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she is taking to prevent the use of good character references in relation to those accused of sexual offences.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns around the use of good character references in court proceedings and am giving this matter my consideration.  It will be important to take views from key stakeholders and also to learn from experiences in other jurisdictions to help inform my thinking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how he will ensure residents of care homes do not have their residency terminated for registering a complaint regarding the care they are receiving; and (ii) the obligations on Health and Social Care Trusts to advocate for and challenge care homes where residencies are terminated for non-valid reasons.
answer_text: The current Regional Care Home Contract sets out the circumstances under which a Provider can  terminate a residency. These include instances where Providers assess they are no longer able to meet the needs of a Resident or where the behaviour of the Resident or any other circumstances relating to the Resident are materially detrimental to the safety and welfare of other Residents or the conduct of the Provider. Termination of any residency is always a last resort and all possible efforts will be made between the Resident (their relatives or representatives), the respective Trust and the Provider to resolve any issues that have arisen. Where  this is not achievable and termination is the only remaining option,  the Trust and the Provider will work together to ensure the needs of the Resident are met during this process.My Department has established a Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF), the purpose of which is to provide a formal mechanism for the Department and representatives of the Social Care Sector, across the statutory, voluntary/community and private sectors, to work together as partners to build shared values and deliver improvements in social care.A Commissioning and Contracting Workstream has been set up under this Forum to review the existing Regional Care Home Contract which has been in place for a number of years. While this work is looking at the contract in its entirety it will also consider how a culture of human rights can be embedded in a revised Contract.The review of the contract will consider all relevant learning from COPNI’s recently published report entitled, ‘Respecting Residents’ Rights in Care Homes: A Review of the Regional Care Home Contract’, along with similar recommendations relating to tenure agreements as set out within the report issued by the Independent Review Team into events at Dunmurry Manor Care Home. Alongside the work of the Forum, and resulting from the Independent Review Team’s suite of Reports into events at Dunmurry Manor Care Home, the NI Public Services Ombudsman (NIPSO), through their Complaints Standards Team, intends to develop a range of training packages and guides for public bodies to support all those involved in complaints handling from senior managers through to front line staff. This training will be rolled out to care home providers as well as a number of other organisations, as a way of supporting and encouraging best practice.  This work will also see compliance monitoring ensuring those public sector bodies subject to the new arrangements are fulfilling all their responsibilities and complying with the new standards.  My officials are in regular contact with NIPSO in relation to the development and implementation of this work.It remains fundamental that residents and their families are able to complain where they have genuine concerns about the care provided within a care home without fear of retribution by a care home or its staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the options available to his Department for reducing speeding in Drumsurn, County Londonderry; and (ii) the cross-departmental work he is planning to address this issue.
answer_text: Departmental officials are currently considering what, if any further engineering enhancements would help address the traffic issues in the village. Any proposals will be agreed with the PSNI Roads Policing Unit prior to implementation.Meanwhile concerns about driver behaviour such as speeding or other inconsiderate behaviour should be directed to the PSNI on their non-emergency 101 telephone number.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work that has been undertaken on assessing (i) pollution; and (ii) congestion levels in Ballykelly, to discern the views of locals and commuters in relation to the need and benefits of the proposed Ballykelly bypass.
answer_text: The preferred route for Ballykelly Bypass was announced in 2010, however due to the lack of funding this scheme has not been progressed and no further development work has been undertaken. At that time an assessment on pollution and traffic was undertaken, however given the passage of time these will need to be reassessed. The A2 Ballykelly Bypass Stage 2 Assessment Report, published in 2010, contains further information and can be viewed using the following link: www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/A2-ballykelly-bypass-stage-2-assessement-report.pdfMy Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the volume of sewage spills into waterways in each of the past five years, broken down by location; (ii) the reasons behind these spills; (iii) the work ongoing and needed in future to avoid these spills; and (iv) the impact such spills are having on (a) wildlife; (b) the environment; and (c) water quality for bathing.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on the volume of wastewater discharges into waterways from Combined Sewer Overflows (CSOs), Emergency Overflows (EOs) nor Wastewater Treatment Works (WwTWs). These storm overflows are designed to operate in heavy rainfall conditions, when the wastewater in sewer networks is significantly diluted with stormwater, thereby preventing out of sewer flooding in properties.Climate change and heavy rainfall events exacerbate problems from sewerage infrastructure and agricultural runoff, presenting real challenges for both farmers, sewage infrastructure and water quality managers. The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is working with NI Water on its programme of installing and commissioning storm-overflow monitors and to ensure the data received is accurate and reliable. It is NIEA and NI Water’s intention to publish annual performance reports once accurate and reliable monitoring data is available.It is recognised that both wastewater and agricultural run-off affect bathing water quality. Impacts of discharges from WwTWs contribute to excess nutrient loading (e.g. phosphorus), in our water environment and the above work will greatly assist in better understanding the impact of discharges on water quality and actions to address the issue. My Department will also continue to conduct targeted studies to identify the sources at failing sites and ensure appropriate measures are implemented to improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps that have been taken to train, recruit and retain care staff during the current Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Staffing levels continue to be a significant challenge across the health and social care sector. My officials continue to work to maximise current capacity in social care and to engage with service providers in regard to addressing those ongoing issues of recruitment and retention.  This is only possible with a skilled and dedicated workforce. 		In recognition of the challenges facing the independent social care sector, I recently announced a £70m funding package across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care. The purpose of this funding is to ensure independent sector providers have the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April.  I have also established structures to consider how we move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funding that will be required to deliver this.	In recognition of the fundamental need to deliver reform and transformation in Health and Social Care, my Department has established the Adult Social Care Collaborative Forum and the Children’s Strategic Reform Board. Within these fora, workstreams have been established focusing on supporting, developing and improving the social care workforce. The Adult social care workforce workstream is also scoping how we can improve recruitment of international social care staff.These workstreams are implementing a range of measures that include:Publishing a Social Care Workforce Strategy which will set out a vision to develop a sustainable social care workforce over the next 10 years.  The strategy will include actions to develop career pathways, support the training and education of the workforce, and to raise the profile and recognition of the social care workforce as a skilled and competent workforce.  It is anticipated that the Strategy will be published in late 2024.In partnership with the Northern Ireland Social Care Council, work is ongoing to develop the training, career and qualification pathways for the social care workforce.In partnership with all stakeholders’ social care campaigns are ongoing to raise the profile and recognise the significant contribution that the social care workforce provides.In addition, the Social Care Fair Work Forum has also been established which will seek to embed Fair Work initiatives and improve the terms and conditions for everyone employed across all parts of the social care sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of people (i) charged; and (ii) convicted in relation to drug crime in the Limavady area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the numbers of persons charged for drugs offences.  Information on persons charged for drugs offences is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in part (i) of the question to the PSNI.Information in relation to part (ii) of the question has been provided in relation to the number of convictions at courts for drugs offences in 2018 – 2022, the most recent five years for which information is available, in relation to defendants who had an address in Limavady at the date their case was dealt with in court.  Convictions at courts for drugs offences where defendant had address in Limavady, 2018 - 2022YearConvictions2018                  33 2019                  34 2020                  20 2021                  34 2022                  25 Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was a conviction for at least one drugs offence.3. Figures relate to defendants who were recorded as having an address in Limavady settlement area at time court case was dealt with.4. Limavady settlement area is as defined in the NISRA report ‘Review of the Statistical Classification and Delineation of Settlements 2015’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the plans to extend Sandelford School, Coleraine, including (i) the extra number of pupils it will be able to accommodate; and (ii) the funding available for (a) construction; and (b) the recruitment and retention of staff.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it is advancing a three-classroom modular extension at Sandelford School, with the tender due to issue in May 2024. To meet demand for placements for September 2024, the EA is also currently investigating the procurement of five rental modular units.Sandelford School is also advancing in design under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP).  The proposed SEP project will add approximately 1,400m2 of accommodation to the school, together with some refurbishment of the existing school. Following my announcement (30 April 2024) for a Special Educational Needs (SEN) Capital Investment Programme, the EA will undertake a review of all special schools, including Sandelford School.  The EA will undertake an Optimum Pupil Number Exercise which, together with the Department of Education (DE) projections for the area, will inform the longer term need for all special school places.  This will allow the EA to take forward workstreams to ensure a sufficient number of places are available as part of workstreams to be detailed in the Operational Plan 2: 2024-2026 (OP2).  Until the Optimum Pupil Number Exercise and site master planning exercises are carried out, it is not possible to determine how many extra pupils Sandelford School can accommodate. (ii) (a) As announced on 30 April 2024, the scope and budget in respect of the existing SEP could be expanded to deliver accommodation at the scale required by the school.  This is currently being explored further by the EA and the school. (ii) (b) A resource allocation meeting will be convened between the EA and the Chair of Governors and principal of Sandelford School in advance of additional pupils being placed.  An allocation of teaching and non-teaching staff will be agreed with the school to resource additional classes and ensure that the special education needs of pupils are met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he is considering to assist hospitality businesses that are struggling due to the significant number of such businesses, particularly on the north coast.
answer_text: I understand the importance of the hospitality sector for our tourism industry, particularly on the north coast, and recognise that rising costs represent a challenge at this time. Addressing these challenges cuts across several areas of government, including the Department for Communities and local councils.  My Department is taking steps to support such businesses through delivery of the Executive’s Path to Net Zero Energy Strategy, a key objective being to ensure that all consumers, including small businesses, have access to essential and affordable energy.The level of VAT is an additional issue for the sector.  The Department of Finance has repeatedly raised with the Treasury the negative impact of high levels of VAT on the hospitality sector, and these representations will continue.  While town centre regeneration is a matter for the Department for Communities, the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce are also relevant as successful hospitality businesses are important features of vibrant town centres.  I therefore intend to work with Executive colleagues to consider the High Street Task Force recommendations to move this agenda forwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how Safe Access Zones around hospitals can be better enforced in order to avoid suffering of families who have lost babies and potential conflict with protestors.
answer_text: Safe Access Zones were introduced at all health service locations providing abortion services across Northern Ireland from 29 September 2023, and continue to operate effectively in line with Assembly legislation to protect women and girls accessing abortion treatment services, information, advice and counselling, and to protect staff working at these locations.Enforcement of a Safe Access Zone is a matter for the Police Service of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support it provides to adults with suspected but undiagnosed special educational needs, particularly with issues such as reading and writing and when preparing for or seeking employment.
answer_text: My Department offers a wide range of support to customers based on personal assessments and tailored to individual needs, operating in conjunction with other supports delivered by Central and Local Government, and the Voluntary and Community Sector. The Department offers Access to Work NI, Workable NI, and the Condition Management Programme which are flexible provisions that support management of their conditions, movement towards, and sustainment of employment. Additionally, the JobStart Scheme offers paid placements for young adults, alongside a training and upskilling bursary.  In respect to reading and writing provision for adults, while this is primarily the remit of the Department for the Economy, Work Coaches across my Department’s Jobs and Benefits office network refer and connect claimants to local provision. They also offer schemes such as the Advisor Discretionary Fund which can provide non-repayable grants to remove any financial barriers to participation within this provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the work his Department has undertaken with Causeway Neurodiversity in identifying, diagnosing and supporting adults with suspected special educational needs (SEN); and (ii) to detail how his Department can better work with charity groups such as this to facilitate a better understanding of, and better support for, adults with suspected SEN.
answer_text: The Department of Health and HSC partners work closely with the Department of Education and the Education Authority to support children with special educational needs throughout their time in school and transition into adult services.  Those transitioning to adult services may present with health and social care needs, accessing a range of supports dependent on assessment.Causeway Neurodiversity is one of the partner members, of the cross sector Northern Area Autism Forum and the working groups established to deliver an Action Plan.  The Forum is chaired by Northern Health and Social Care Trust and provides a multi-agency approach to all issues, including health, employment and housing, impacting on people with ASD and their families in line with the Autism Strategy.   The Department engages with this Forum on a quarterly basis.  The Trust has provided seed funding for Causeway Neurodiversity to help with set up costs and to help them offer support for neurodivergent children, young people, adults and their carers/ families.   My Department continues to engage with autistic people and the sector through the Department’s Autism Forum established in 2021, which is co-chaired by autistic advocates, to consider issues and challenges on a thematic basis.  The Forum comprises of people with lived experience of autism and the community and voluntary sector organisations who represent them.  My Department also continues to attend regional Autism Fora and Stakeholder Groups on a quarterly basis to enable continued engagement on the aims and improvements to be achieved in the Autism Strategy.  More recently my Department worked with the voluntary and community sector in the development of the Learning Disability Strategic Plan.  This work sets out to finalise a service model for learning disabilities and a framework for disabled children.  This work is being progressed alongside a refreshed approach to transitions for children with learning disabilities or autism, which is linked to the ongoing End to End Review of SEN, progressed by the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many planning applications are awaiting assessment by NIEA as a statutory consultee; (ii) how many applications did NIEA respond to in (a) February 2023; and (b) February 2024; and (iii) how many officers are resource allocated for responding on behalf of NIEA as a statutory consultee.
answer_text: When consulted on a planning application the Department provides a single collated response covering all relevant Departmental divisions, as such the number of planning consultation responses are recorded at a Departmental level which covers 7 teams within NIEA & Marine & Fisheries Division (MFD). 	(i) Week commencing 22 April 2024, there were 280 statutory planning consultations and 97 non-statutory consultations awaiting assessment with the Department. 	(ii) NIEA & MFD responded to 448 planning consultations in February 2023; and 415 planning consultations in February 2024. 	(iii) There are 47.4 full time equivalent officers working on planning consultations within NIEA & MFD.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the challenges of performing major elective surgeries when (a) an overnight stay is not possible; (b) there is no access to emergency theatre; and (c) there is no on-site surgical assessor; and (ii) how these challenges will impact where elective surgeries take place.
answer_text: The hospital infrastructure in Northern Ireland operates as an integrated network in the delivery of elective care to the region. The nature and complexity of the elective surgery required, and the patient’s clinical circumstances, will determine where a surgery takes place.  More complex or specialist elective surgery will be carried out in a hospital which has the necessary supporting clinical infrastructure.Our two dedicated regional Day Procedure Centres at Lagan Valley and Omagh Hospitals perform planned day surgery and mainly focus on high volume, low complexity surgery across a range of specialities.  Elective Overnight Stay Centres have also been established at Mater, Daisy Hill and South West Acute Hospitals for intermediate complexity elective surgery that sometimes, but not always, requires an overnight stay in hospital.  There is no doubt that we need to maximise the use of all the hospital infrastructure available to address the waiting lists. Work is ongoing to maximise the effectiveness and throughput of surgical theatres and diagnostic services by driving performance and efficiency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what consideration is being given to how the demography in Northern Ireland will change in the next 20 years, in particular the age of our population; and (ii) how an ageing population will impact services across all health and social care settings.
answer_text: It is widely recognised that Northern Ireland has a growing and ageing population, and with this comes the increased risk of people living with multiple conditions.  This adds to the complexity of needs that our services must manage and places additional demands and pressures on the system.Consequently, there is a fundamental need to change the way we work if we are to address these demands and pressures.The development of the Integrated Care System NI will support us in doing this, enabling collaborative working across the HSC sector and beyond, placing a focus on people keeping well in the first instance, providing timely, co-ordinated care when they are not, and supporting people to self-care when appropriate. This approach will help support us to ensure that we are maximizing the resource we have available to deliver the best outcomes for our population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what options he has considered to encourage surgeons and other personnel to work in hospitals outside Greater Belfast, including merit awards, targeted training, and other forms of recognition.
answer_text: I value the contribution provided by all HSC personnel in all clinical settings to the delivery of care to our population.My Department is working with the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency and all HSC Trusts right across Northern Ireland to implement practical measures that will enhance the quality of medical training provided.Recruitment into HSC is a matter for each Trust individually as the employer. Where any part of the workforce is under pressure, each employer may seek the Department’s approval to apply a Recruitment and Retention Payment (RRP). The use of RRPs is, however, limited due to the potential for impacts in neighbouring Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the reasons NIEA suspended assessment of planning applications as a statutory consultee in March 2023; and (ii) his assessment of how this suspension impacted on applications related to the rural and agricultural economy.
answer_text: (i) The decision to suspend the provision of advice to planning authorities in spring 2023 was based on concerns around the efficacy of NIEA’s extant advice to support robust decision-making on sustainable development.  This was informed by the Planning Appeal Commission revoking a planning decision that relied on NIEA’s advice, following a legal challenge by Fermanagh and Omagh DC; an increase in planning enforcement cases related to air pollution; recognition that planning authorities were increasingly reluctant to base their decisions on the Standing Advice; and DAERA’s ongoing review of the air pollution standing advice in light of ever increasing ammonia emissions and associated risks to biodiversity.(ii) I recognise that the decision to pause the issuing of advice delayed decisions in a number of planning cases, which my officials have been working hard to expedite.  I can assure you that we have sought to increase capacity in this work area and are trying to respond to all planning consultations as quickly as possible.  However, it must be noted that issues around the previous advice were causing significant difficulties for the planning system, planning authorities and applicants alike in being able secure sound planning decisions that safeguard our natural capital and comply with environmental law.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the increased perinatal services available in Causeway Hospital since the maternity unit was moved to Antrim Area Hospital; and (ii) for his assessment of access to services for expectant mothers who must travel further since the move.
answer_text: (i) Following our acute maternity services transformation work which saw births and inpatients consolidate onto the Antrim Area Hospital site, the Trust have worked to increase antenatal and postnatal services in the Causeway Maternity Hub by improving local access to antenatal and postnatal services for women within the Causeway locality. Complex antenatal clinics have been established in Causeway so as to mitigate the distance women have historically been asked to travel to receive this care. These clinics include;Perinatal Mental Health Clinic There is a dedicated joint perinatal mental health and obstetric clinic.  Since the reconfiguration of services, the community perinatal mental team now provide fortnightly clinics in the Causeway Maternity Hub.  This has facilitated an improved pathway and increased co-ordination in care between the multidisciplinary teams and have reduced the need for women with perinatal mental health concerns to travel to Antrim as had been the case prior to the reform. Social Complexities Antenatal/ Postnatal Clinic There is a dedicated obstetric clinic with support from the Midwife specialist for Social Complexities.Third trimester Midwifery Led Scanning Clinic Weigh to Health Pregnancy Clinic for women with an increased BMI in pregnancy.Specialist infant feeding clinicThe Trust also have a Maternity Care clinic, which is a dedicated provision for scheduled ambulatory care.  Since the opening of this unit, until end of March 2024, 733 women have benefited from the utilisation of this service.  Scheduled Care provisionExamples of scheduled care provision includes iron infusions, caesarean section pre-operative care, screening tests, including those for diabetes and fetal and maternity wellbeing checks.  Being developed;Gestational Diabetic ClinicPathways are currently being developed to provide to a cohort of risk-assessed women who present with gestational diabetes to receive antenatal care, thereby further increasing perinatal services to the woman in the locality of Causeway without the need to travel to the Antrim site.Neurodiversity antenatal/ postnatal clinicA further pathway currently under development is an antenatal space/clinic to provide women with a diagnosis of neurodiversity, that may find it difficult to attend a traditional antenatal clinic an alternative setting. (ii) In relation to an assessment of access to services for expectant mothers who must travel further since the move, one of the concerns which had been expressed was that the change could result in additional babies born before arrival to hospital (BBAs).  Since the consolidation of inpatient maternity services and births in Antrim Area Hospital, six women (accounting for seven babies as there was one set of twins) have experienced birth before arrival to hospital. Three women were within the Causeway locality and four resided closer to Antrim. There has been no significant increase in the average number of BBAs, which is approximately one

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to introduce compensation schemes for farmers whose crops fall victim to poor weather, including during recent heavy rainfall.
answer_text: I am aware that this year’s very wet and late Spring is presenting challenges for farmers operating in almost every sector within the industry. I am conscious of the wider impact if wet weather continues in the time ahead and I have made representations to the UK Government about the need for central government intervention if the situation lasts for much longer.Meanwhile, my officials in the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) continue to monitor the situation very closely, maintaining contact with stakeholders and farmers across the range of sectors within the industry.CAFRE has released a number of advisory articles on the management of farming operations following this period of prolonged inclement weather and tailored advice is also being offered at group meetings and through one-to-one discussions with individual farmers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any cross-departmental work or discussions in relation to potential changes to taxi operators’ licensing regulations, specifically in relation to (i) the impact it will have on taxi numbers; and (ii) the knock-on effects on (a) older people who rely on taxis for medical appointments; and (b) families with children with special educational needs or disabilities who rely on taxis to take them to school.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry, in relation to taxi driver, taxi operator and vehicle licencing. Within this regulatory role the department has no remit to influence the specific types of work taxi drivers or taxi companies undertake. The same applies to driver recruitment and retention polices, this is a matter for taxi operators. With regard to the proposal to amend the current Taxi Operator Licensing Regulations (NI) 2012  you may wish to note that I have not yet made a final decision on the need for subordinate legislation here.  However, I should emphasise that any new regulations would not alter the current taxi regulatory framework.  Rather, they would simply clarify the existing requirements of the taxis Act (NI) 2008. I can also confirm that in considering the proposal my officials did engage with Transport for London and the Department for Transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to introduce legislation to ban the hunting of wild animals with dogs.
answer_text: I understand that this is an important issue, and much work has been undertaken to date to take forward a ban, most notably, by John Blair, MLA, in the last Assembly mandate.  I am supportive of the need to ban the hunting of wild animals with dogs.However, I need to consider the priority of any relevant legislation against other priorities within the Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement he has had with Ulster University about the future of the Coleraine campus.
answer_text: I have met with Ulster University for wide-ranging discussions in which the University’s commitment to its multi-campus mission, including the future of its Coleraine campus, has been very clear. I am supportive of that commitment as a means to deliver regional balance in the provision of higher education in the North. 		I will continue to work with all of our higher education institutions to help ensure all their campuses are thriving, sustainable centres for higher education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will report on the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015, as is his statutory responsibility.
answer_text: The first report on the operation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 has been prepared and will be presented to the Executive for consideration within the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for her assessment of no-fault divorce; and (ii) to detail any plans to introduce no-fault divorce.
answer_text: The policy of no-fault divorce has the potential to improve how such important matters are dealt with. I appreciate there are competing views on the issue and am keen to engage with those who may be able to make contributions in relation to no-fault divorces.I have had exploratory discussions with my officials in the Department who are engaged on a range of civil law reform areas including marriage and civil partnership reform and other important policies. I will consider this issue within the available resources and overall legislative priorities, and officials will begin preparatory work with the aim to see progress on this policy area during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he is aware of any road traffic collisions or incidents involving cyclists or other road users caused by (i) potholes; and (ii) other damaged roads, in the last three years.
answer_text: My department does not collect data on road traffic collisions.  In general, road traffic collision data and related incidents is collected by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) where personal injury has occurred. This data is subsequently made available to my department and is also available to the public on access portal ‘OpendataNI.’  This data can be accessed via the below link.https://admin.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset?organization=police-service-of-northern-ireland&tags=injury+collisionsA review of  the current information on OpendataNI, records would indicate that there were 12 collisions within the last three years of available data where a ‘defective road surface’ has been recorded as ‘special condition at site’.  Of these, there were 3 instances where a pedal cycle had been recorded as a vehicle involved in the collision.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will include an outcome for older people in the Programme for Government, to reflect the ageing population and the impact that has on public services.
answer_text: We are working at pace to develop a Programme for Government which meets the needs of everyone in our society, whatever their age. We know that the proportion of our population over the age of 65 is increasing rapidly, and this will change how we deliver public services that support older people.Any commitments made within a Programme for Government must be agreed by all members of the Executive. We are together working to develop a Programme and hope to make it available to the Assembly for scrutiny soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of what percentage of the planning application for a major development is allocated to the (i) planning authority; and (ii) statutory consultees.
answer_text: Any fee payable under the Planning (Fees) Regulations (NI) 2015 (as amended), for an application for planning permission is allocated in full to the relevant planning authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to appoint independent persons to undertake hearings and examinations to help address the current backlog faced by the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: I recognise the resourcing challenges of the Planning Appeals Commission and it is my intention to discuss the appropriate resourcing of the Commission with the Minister of Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) guidance provided by; and (ii) conversations between, his Department, Tourism NI and local councils in relation to making public car parking easier for tourists, specifically the variety of methods required to pay, often within the same town or council area.
answer_text: Public parking is the responsibility of local councils. Tourism NI has not provided any guidance to local Councils on the specific methods required to pay for public parking by tourists. Tourism NI regularly engages with all 11 Councils on a range of services for tourists. For instance, they are working closely with Causeway Coast and Glens Council on the Connected Causeway programme, which proposes to create four new park and ride/stride hubs across the area to alleviate congestion and promote sustainable transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how he is liaising with local councils to ensure the proper application of animal welfare rights and policy; (ii) how performance, and the application of legislation and policy by animal welfare officers is monitored and assessed; and (iii) whether there are plans to strengthen animal welfare laws to counter increasing instances of animal cruelty.
answer_text: My Department liaises regularly with local councils to discuss the direction of animal welfare policy and any potential legislative developments. However, the implementation and enforcement of non-farmed animal welfare remains the statutory responsibility of each council and they are best placed to determine how this function should be discharged to meet the specific requirements of their region. I am acutely aware of the calls for reform of animal welfare in Northern Ireland and I will be considering options in the time ahead, to determine what is needed, and what is deliverable, within the remainder of this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the creation of provisions for Relationship and Sexuality Education as referenced by the Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023; and (ii) to detail the level of importance given to addressing misogyny, consent and the development of toxic attitudes towards women.
answer_text: Relationships and Sexuality Education in the Northern Ireland Curriculum is not new; there were requirements for schools to deliver RSE before the introduction of the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 and before the Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023 which were made by the Secretary of State and came into force on 1 July 2023.From 1 July 2023, comprehensive, age-appropriate and scientifically accurate education about sexual and reproductive health and rights covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion became a mandatory element of Learning for Life and Work at key stages 3 and 4.  Schools have therefore been required to provide that education from the start of the current school year.My Department issued guidance to schools on 1 January 2024 on this change and from that date made provision by Regulations for the circumstances in which a parent can request a pupil be excused from the new education or specified elements of it.The design of our curriculum means that schools have flexibility around what they teach and the resources they can use.  This means that teaching can be tailored to discuss issues such as misogyny, consent and attitudes to women at a time when they are important to pupils and are part of wider debate in society.My officials have also established as task and finish group to consider the findings of the Education and Training Inspectorate’s Evaluation of the Preventative Curriculum in Schools and Education Other Than At School EOTAS) Centres.  The report found that some sensitive topics were not covered sufficiently or at all in some schools.  I will consider whether it is appropriate to issue further guidance to schools in response to that report.Officials are also engaged with the Executive Office on its Strategy for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.  Schools are very well placed to lead the way in attitudinal and behavioural change, with a whole school environment which equips and supports the empowerment of future generations to develop healthy, respectful relationships and gender equality.  Addressing the root causes of violence against women and girls will be a central part of achieving this outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on efforts to provide classroom assistants with a fair pay deal.
answer_text: The Budget allocations provided for 2024-25 do not include a specific allocation for the Pay & Grading Review for classroom assistants and other support staff.  However I, together with Executive colleagues, am committed to working collectively to find a resolution for these important workers.The Executive has agreed to seek approval from the HM Treasury to reprofile some of the repurposed funds provided in the Executive restoration financial package for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) his plans to reinitiate the dilapidation bill; and (ii) the stage the bill is currently at in its creation.
answer_text: Subject to the Executive approving the 2024/25 legislative programme and also agreeing the Bill, it is my intention to introduce a Dilapidation Bill to the Assembly as soon as possible after summer recess.  		The drafting of the Bill is now complete and officials are currently finalising the associated Regulatory Impact Assessment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what steps he (a) has taken; and (b) will take to enable schools to plan multi-year projects; and (ii) any engagement he has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to confirming the ability to do so in relation to his Department's budget.
answer_text: My Department already has several multi-year programmes (Majors, FSA, SEPs) and indeed some larger minor works currently in planning.Unfortunately with single year budgets, it is difficult to plan.  As 2024-25 is the last year of the current Spending Review period, a multi-year budget has not been possible this time.  I am advised that the Finance Minister has pressed both the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and the shadow Secretary of State on the need for a multi-year settlement.  It goes without saying that I fully support her efforts in relation to this key issue.As I have previously stated, it is my intention to continue to make the case for significant additional and sustained capital budget to invest right across the schools’ estate.I have made the case for additional and sustained capital investment in our education infrastructure and I had a constructive bilateral meeting with the Department of Finance Minister on 21 March and will continue to engage at both Executive and with the DoF Minister as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the budget to meet and maintain the recent pay deal for teachers has been, and will be, ring-fenced to guarantee this rise in future years.
answer_text: Following the agreement of the financial package from HM Treasury earlier in the year, my Department was allocated additional funding to contribute towards the settling of three years of outstanding non-contractual teaching pay awards in 2023-24.  The recurring impact of settling these pay awards, is estimated at £166m in 2024-25. All Department’s opening budgets for 2024-25 were agreed last week by the Northern Ireland Executive.  I will very shortly make final budget decisions with regard to the pressures my Department currently faces.  Key education stakeholders, such as the Education Authority and schools, will be notified of their final opening allocations for 2024-25 shortly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to change the Housing Executive points system to include those who are survivors of historical institutional abuse.
answer_text: I acknowledge the trauma experienced by survivors of historical institutional abuse. The Housing Selection Scheme awards points based on housing need. Points are considered on the grounds of health and social well-being, including consideration of complex needs and any social, support and care needs that the applicant or any member of their household may have. Each case is considered on its own merit, based on the applicant’s housing needs under the provisions of the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the amount raised by schools through fundraising in each of the last five years; and (ii) for his assessment of any increased reliance by schools on fundraising and support from parents for simple operating costs.
answer_text: Under current legislation raising additional funds by schools through fundraising is a matter for Boards of Governors. For this reason, my Department does not collect data on school fundraising activities.  However, in seeking to fully answer your question, the Education Authority has provided information on the levels of income that controlled and maintained schools have lodged into their delegated budgets, which these schools have classified as fundraising. The amounts for the last five years are outlined below.Financial YearAmount £2019-202,096,4622020-211,453,5562021-221,103,1362022-231,718,5852023-24 (Provisional)2,118,161Total8,489,900During my frequent visits to schools, I hear firsthand about the significant financial pressures facing schools and, in some instances, schools having to fundraise or seek voluntary contributions from parents in order to fund basic operating costs.  Clearly, this is a serious concern to me.Additional and much needed funding for our schools is an issue that I continue to highlight to my Executive colleagues. Going forward, I will continue to take every opportunity to make the case for additional investment in our children and young people’s education.  However, you will appreciate that my Department does not determine what its final budget outcome will be. Rather, education pressures are considered alongside the competing pressures of all departments with my Department’s overall budget allocation being decided collectively by the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) when funding for the education of pupils with special educational needs or disabilities will be made available for the next academic year; and (ii) whether every child with special educational needs or disabilities will have a suitable place in a school that will comprehensively meet their needs from the start of the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: Departments’ opening budgets for 2024-25 have only recently been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive and I will very shortly make final budget decisions with regard to the pressures the department currently faces.  Key stakeholders (e.g. the Education Authority (EA) and schools) will be notified of their final opening allocations for 2024-25 shortly thereafter.  Children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) follow a different process (to the open enrolment schools admissions process) to secure a school placement which considers the most appropriate school placement to meet their needs as set out in their statement, in consultation with parents and schools.  This is on an individual basis for each child with a statement of SEN.The situation for September 2024 remains very challenging with latest EA planning assumptions indicating that almost 5,800 children with a statement of SEN require a change of placement for September 2024, with an estimated 1,000 additional places needed across Northern Ireland to meet this demand.  There has been significant progress made in agreeing solutions with schools to meet this demand through the creation of additional capacity in special schools and new specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS).   Focused work continues to secure solutions for September 2024 and the EA continues to work at pace on a significant programme of work to create additional specialist education places in both special schools and SPiMS for the 2024/25 academic year.  Meeting this demand will require the widespread support of schools and my officials recently wrote to all nursery, primary and post-primary schools requesting that they consider their capacity to establish specialist provision and what support they require to do so.  They are awaiting the results of a short survey which will help identify further options for exploration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why GP pharmacists are not paid the same as other members of GP multi-disciplinary teams; (ii) whether there are plans to include GP pharmacists in the Agenda of Change pay scaling; and (iii) for his assessment of how the current disparity in pay is impacting the recruitment and retaining of GP pharmacists.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the various issues regarding remuneration for GP practice pharmacists employed by GP Federations which you have raised. I can assure you that such issues are already subject to ongoing discussions between the Department of Health (DoH) and key stakeholders.The GP Pharmacist Oversight Board was established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the DoH in December 2023. The group brings together representatives from all key stakeholder groups, including SPPG, DoH, GP Federations and GPC (General Practitioners Committee) to facilitate collaborative working to optimise the working arrangements of the GPP workforce, in line with strategic priorities of the DoH Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework. The group will oversee the delivery of the service and facilitate discussion and resolution to issues relating to the GPP workforce employed in General Practices. The group also aims to develop the General Practice Pharmacist workforce, in line with DoH strategic direction and the Pharmacy Workforce Review, which is being progressed through Service Delivery and Service Development subgroups.DoH will continue to work closely with stakeholders over the following months to address the issues to which this question refers

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any correlation observed between those suffering from orthopaedic pain and alcohol or drug addiction issues.
answer_text: The misuse of prescription medication has been shown to be associated with a wide range of substance use related harms. As well as people illicitly seeking out prescription medicines, there can also be issues with involuntary addiction to prescription medications if they are not taken or prescribed in line with guidelines – this can particularly occur in relation to sedatives/tranquilisers and opioids in relation to managing chronic pain.A review was undertaken in England in 2017 to identify the scale, distribution and causes of prescription drug dependence, and what might be done to address it. It showed that in the year 2017-18, 1-in-4 adults in England were prescribed benzodiazepines, z-drugs, gabapentinoids, opioids for chronic non-cancer pain, or antidepressants.There are also cases where individuals try to access medication online if prescriptions they feel they require are not increased or stopped too quickly. This increases their chances of getting lower quality medication and incorrect dosage quantities which can lead to an increased risk of harm.Under the NI Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery, DoH Strategic Planning & Performance Group will produce an updated Prescription Drug Misuse Action Plan which, building on current processes, will include additional support to monitor prescribing levels, as well as support for prescribers to better understand who may be at risk of harms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive has a responsibility to adequately train its housing advisors to better understand and work with people experiencing issues such as addiction, poor mental health, domestic abuse, elder abuse, homelessness and other issues relevant to housing and homelessness.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Upon recruitment, all Housing Advisors are provided with 15 days of functional induction training, with this training covering a breadth of issues ranging from NIHE’s statutory duties in respect of housing and homelessness assistance, to the ability to provide support to vulnerable households presenting for assistance.The functional induction training programme for Housing Advisors is designed to provide NIHE staff with a basis of the key skills and knowledge required to carry out their role to best meet the needs of customers at the time of presentation to the Housing Executive.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any initiatives his Department provides to identify and help people escape a long-term period of homelessness.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation, as such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s approach to identify and help people escape a long-term period of homelessness is outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 which can be found on https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/73313718-aa0e-4aae-b122-6573dcab88c7/Ending-Homelessness-Together-Homelessness-Strategy-2022-27.pdf “

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people who are (i) receiving; or (ii) applying for Personal Independence Payments because they are unable to work due to being on a waiting list for orthopaedic treatment.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested. The latest published Personal Independence Payment (PIP) statistics for Northern Ireland were published in February 2024, covering up to November 2023 and can be accessed via the link below: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/personal-independence-payment-statistics-november-2023

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of how wastewater limits are preventing the opening of new businesses in town centres; (ii) whether the Department or local council’s understanding of the wastewater situation in the area is, or can be, shared with the applicant prior to them paying for a wastewater impact assessment; and (iii) to detail the difference in cost between private and NI Water wastewater impact assessments.
answer_text: The issues you raised are operational matters for NI Water.  You should therefore direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. In terms of my Department’s responsibilities, I can advise that NI Water has received substantial allocations of public money during the current and previous Price Control periods, to enable it to deliver on water and wastewater services for all areas across the North. Like all public services, NI Water is facing challenges, but as demonstrated by the figures below, it has received significant public investment in recent years, and indeed in 2023-24 it was provided with the highest levels of capital budget allocated to the company to date to enable it to keep delivering essential improvements. Price Control PeriodResource DEL Capital DEL PC15 (2015-2021)£683.8m£947.3mPC21 (2021-2024) £491.6m£849.5m
