question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the changes to the level of security of the personal details of people in occupations such as those in the PSNI or Prison Service, that will accompany the change from tax credits to Universal Credit; (ii) whether an impact assessment on this issue has been carried out; and (iii) whether he has raised this issue with the Department of Work and Pensions.
answer_text: The Universal Credit system is owned and managed by the Department for Work and Pensions and used by my Department for the administration of Universal Credit in Northern Ireland.  Both the Department for Work and Pensions and the Department for Communities are subject to professional and legal expectations surrounding the protection of personal data and take these responsibilities very seriously. Beyond the legal requirement for protecting personal data, it is also recognised that claimants will carry higher sensitivities for a variety of reasons, both personal and professional, and these will have been considered as part of the Universal Credit design.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps he will take to mitigate the economic impact on businesses from NIE Networks' power outages, planned during office hours, including (i) the provision of free, short-term loans of generators; (ii) planning the outages for times when the businesses are not operating; and (iii) providing some level of compensation.
answer_text: NIE Networks is a privately owned company operating under a conditional license issued and enforced by the Utility Regulator.  Reliability of supply requirements and targets are a matter for the regulator to address.	NIE Networks website has information and advice for any customer affected by Planned Work in their area and seeks to minimise disruption to business and domestic customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s responsibilities to protect an existing road network, where a planning application has been approved and built, but with recommendations from the local planning office that steps be taken to mitigate anticipated negative impacts on the road network that were subsequently brought to fruition.
answer_text: DfI Roads is a statutory consultee to the planning process and provides advice to Council planning to enable progression of applications. When consulted by the planning authority, my department assesses the proposed development to determine its transport impact and how best to mitigate any adverse impacts on the road network.Where applicable, my department will recommend conditions to be included as part of an approval. The planning authority is responsible for ensuring a Developer complies with any conditions recommended by the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 9468/22-27, to detail potential resolutions to the issues faced in securing providers for rural primary schools in the Healthy Happy Minds pilot.
answer_text: I will take the time to assess the findings of the evaluation and the recommendations contained therein, including rural proofing.  It will also be important to consider other relevant research and feedback from stakeholders, and the availability of these health professionals across all services, when developing a longer-term approach to supporting children and young people’s emotional health and wellbeing in education.  Of course, this must be delivered within the budgetary resources available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, when using private resources to carry out NHS treatment, whether a patient’s Health and Social Care Trust has a responsibility to maximise efficiency, convenience and cost for the patient.
answer_text: When a reasonable offer of treatment is made by a Trust to a waiting list patient, the availability of service capacity will normally take precedence over the convenience and travel time to the provider.  Patients have the option to accept or decline the offer. Reference: Integrated Elective Access Protocol Reasonable Offers: 4.5.3 A reasonable offer is defined as:• an offer of admission, irrespective of provider or location, that gives the patient a minimum of three weeks’ notice and a choice of two To Come In dates, and • at least one of the offers must be within Northern Ireland., except for any regional specialties where there are no alternative providers within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether allowing people, whose vehicle has recently passed an MoT test, to book an appointment for approximately one year’s time, would reduce waiting lists and backlog issues and enable people to get an appointment at a reasonable date.
answer_text: Annual vehicle roadworthiness inspections are conducted in accordance with The Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 and the Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003.  The regulations do not permit an MOT to be booked more than three months before it is due.Extending the window for customers to book a test in advance of their MOT expiry date would increase demand on the service reducing the availability of appointments for those customers who require one more urgently. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to develop an ADHD strategy, similar to the Autism Strategy.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there are currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The Department is committed to working closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.Department of Health officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of times Article 6 of the Pollution Control and Local Government (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 has been used by local councils to force owners to make improvements to a dilapidated property, broken down by council area, in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the efforts his Department has made, and will make, to encourage councils to use the powers they have in this regard, particularly in reference to the impact on the neighbouring properties and area of increased insurance premiums, reduced property v
answer_text: This information is not available to my department;	District councils are encouraged to use all legislation at their disposal to deal with the blight of dilapidation. The 1978 Order is only one of a number of pieces of disparate legislation currently available to councils, much of which dates back to the 19th century and / or applies to specific geographical areas and is limited in its scope and effectiveness. 	Subject to Executive approval, I intend to introduce a Dilapidation Bill to the Assembly this year. The Bill will consolidate and enhance the existing legislation and improve councils’ cost recovery abilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive has a responsibility to explore both social and private rental options when sourcing a property for a homeless tenant.
answer_text: The NIHE offers tailored advice and assistance to applicants, assessing their long-term housing needs as well as any immediate housing issues and providing a range of tenure-neutral housing options and choices. These options may include Social Housing, Private Rented Accommodation or Home Ownership, with the primary aim to help applicants make informed choices.While it has been custom and practice for the NIHE to discharge the statutory homeless duty in the social housing sector, existing legislation also allows for the homelessness duty to be discharged in the private sector. Proposal 4 (NIHE can meet their duty to homeless applicants on a tenure-neutral basis) of the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations aims to explore options to enable NIHE, where appropriate, to meet its homelessness duty by securing suitable accommodation in the private rented sector, subject to certain safeguards. For instance, such accommodation will be reasonable for the household to occupy; of the appropriate standard; and available for a reasonable period of time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to ensure that (i) everyone with dementia receives an official diagnosis; and (ii) the type of dementia and the ethnicity of the patient is recorded by NISRA.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) pilot schemes; and (ii) temporary trials that are (a) in place; and (b) planned involving adult ADHD diagnosis and treatment.
answer_text: pilot schemes; and (ii) temporary trials that are (a) in place.In relation to pilot schemes and temporary trials Northern Trust are currently the only Trust with a pilot in place. A number of factors prompted the Northern Trust to progress with a small, unfunded pilot that saw those who have been referred to the Trust for Adult ADHD diagnosis and treatment, being placed on a waiting list and then being offered an appointment with the Trust. Part of the Pilot process is detailed pre-screening to ensure assessments are offered, where there is a high likelihood of Adult ADHD. All patients on any waiting list remain under the care of their GP.This pilot approach has taken time to establish given the preparatory work required. Communication with individuals on the register commenced in December 2023, in chronological order of date of original referral.planned involving adult ADHD diagnosis and treatment.Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals, currently there are no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision of services between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals having to wait longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.Department of Health officials are regularly reviewing the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. Any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability. Department of Health officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any correlation between those with ADHD and an increased risk of developing drug or alcohol dependency, or experiencing trauma; and (ii) the estimated percentage of the (a) Northern Ireland population; and (b) UK-wide population that currently have ADHD.
answer_text: any correlation between those with ADHD and an increased risk of developing drug or alcohol dependency, or experiencing trauma;There is a body of evidence on the increased risk of substance misuse in people with ADHD.  This is noted in the NICE guidance on ADHD diagnosis and management (last updated 2019) as follows:“Be aware that people in the following groups may have increased prevalence of ADHD compared with the general population: people with a history of substance misuse… that untreated ADHD can have far-reaching, long-lasting negative impacts on a child or young person's life (for example, affecting academic performance, interpersonal relationships, work, personal issues, substance use and driving).Symptoms of ADHD can overlap with those of other related disorders. Therefore, care in differential diagnosis is needed. ADHD may also coexist with other disorders. Common coexisting conditions in children include disorders of mood, conduct, learning, motor control, language and communication, and anxiety disorders; in adults, they include personality disorders, bipolar disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder and substance misuse”.(ii) the estimated percentage of the (a) Northern Ireland population; and (b) UK-wide population that currently have ADHD.The Department does not hold specific data or information on this but from literature available, please see the following:The global prevalence of attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children is estimated to be around 5%, while studies based on US populations (where rates of diagnosis and treatment tend to be highest) estimate the rate at between 8% and 10%. ADHD is more commonly diagnosed in boys than girls. Prevalence ratios are generally estimated at 2–5:1, while clinic populations show a ratio as high as 10:1. This sex difference may be due to the fact that boys present more often with disruptive behaviour that prompts referral, whereas girls more commonly have the inattentive subtype and have lower comorbidity with oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) and conduct disorder.In the UK, the prevalence of ADHD in adults is estimated at 3% to 4%, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 3:1Youth Wellbeing Survey in NI 2020 (carried out by UU and commissioned by HSCB) states the following but note it is not referring specifically to ADHD:Overall, the findings indicate that approximately 1 in 8 children and young people in Northern Ireland experience emotional difficulties, 1 in 10 experience conduct problems and 1 in 7, problems with hyperactivity. These prevalence estimates are broadly in keeping with findings from other UK surveys which have used the SDQ (Fink et al., 2015; University of Essex & Institute for Social & Economic Research, 2020), although it is notable that problems with peers and prosocial behaviour were substantially lower in NI than those identified in recent UK surveys There is further information available in the NICE evidence review (2018) on risk factor

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the process under the Shared Care programme whereby adults obtain a diagnosis for ADHD which requires regular prescriptions of medication; (ii) whether some or all NHS GPs are (a) currently writing; and (b) allowed to write, prescriptions for the necessary medication following such a diagnosis; and (iii) the conditions under which NHS GPs are able to provide such prescriptions.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland shared care arrangements may be put in place where a medicine initiated by a hospital doctor is regarded as a ‘specialist medicine’  due to the rare use of the medicine in the community and/or the requirement for complex prescribing not normally undertaken in general practice by a GP. Patients for whom such medicines are prescribed, may also have particular complex monitoring requirements, which require specialist knowledge for the appropriate interpretation of results. There are a number of medications which are prescribed for ADHD which are regarded as specialist medicines.Shared-care arrangements for specialist medicines enable GPs to prescribe specialist medicines initiated by the hospital specialist. Such arrangements should be made with the agreement of all parties. The GP should have sufficient knowledge, experience and information to support them to undertake the prescribing and the hospital specialist should provide the GP and patient with sufficient information to permit the safe management of the condition. If the GP is not satisfied to take prescribing responsibility this should be explained to the specialist and patient and alternative arrangements provided by the hospital specialist.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of figures from Carers NI that show 220,000 people, mostly older and female, care for a friend or family member unpaid, half of whom care more than 90 hours per week; (ii) how this situation has changed over the last five years; (iii) for his assessment of the impact on the physical and mental health of unpaid carers; (iv) what steps have been taken, or are planned, to improve the lives of unpaid carers and the ease of accessing the services available to them; and (v) to detail the ability, including funding-wise, of his Department to take s
answer_text: I acknowledge the vital role played by family carers in Northern Ireland, and I recognise the significant role and contribution that family carers make, not only to the people they care for, but within the whole system of adult social care. I understand that research carried out by Carers NI showed that around 60% of unpaid carers in Northern Ireland are women, with more than 1 in 6 women in employment providing unpaid care. This research also noted that women have a 70% chance of providing care in their adult life and are more likely to care earlier in their life. Following the Covid-19 pandemic, the number of people providing some form of unpaid care will likely have increased, and this may increase further given our growing ageing population and people living longer. I am committed to raising awareness of the role that unpaid carers play in our society and ensuring they are supported and valued. Given population demographics, it is clear that the number of family carers will continue to increase in years to come. It is therefore vital that the supports are in place to ensure that carers are supported, whether on a physical, financial, emotional or mental health basis. This is an issue that is wider than just Health, other Departments will need to consider how they can best support our family carers, now and in years to come. In 2019, a State of Caring report carried out by Carers NI showed that around 1 in 5 people in Northern Ireland were providing some form of unpaid care, and that women made up the majority of carers with 20% of women aged 45-54 providing the most unpaid care. In comparing these findings to the State of Caring report carried out in 2023, these figures have increased over the last five years, with 81% of respondents identifying as female, and 35% of those within the age range of 55-64 providing the most unpaid care. I also understand that a higher state pension age will mean people are working for longer and they therefore are more likely to be working whilst also carrying out their caring role.I am aware of previous research by Carers NI which outlined the detrimental impact felt by unpaid carers, on their physical and mental health, as a result of their caring role. We need to find better ways to support family carers, to allow them to continue to provide care to their loved ones, but also to ensure that their own quality of life is improved and that their needs are being met. in order to support carers with their physical and mental health. Our Health and Social Care Trusts continue to grow and adapt their services to enhance supports using online activities, increasing the information available to them and via face-to-face events. Trusts continue to proactively reach out to identify unpaid carers to ensure they have the support they need. My Department is committed to supporting family carers. In 2023, my Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum to provide a formal mechanism to support opportunities for

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the reasons why further education lecturers receive lower pay than teachers at both primary and post-primary school level.
answer_text: Further Education lecturers’ pay, as well as the terms and conditions of employment, are matters for the Further Education colleges as employers. The pay, and the terms and conditions, for teachers are the responsibility of the Department of Education. As I am committed to ensuring that the pay and conditions provided to Further Education lecturers fully recognises the range of teaching and learning that they deliver to our students, I have approved a budget envelope which has allowed the Further Education colleges to make a revised pay offer consisting of an increase of lecturers’ starting salary to £30,000 plus a pay increase of 8.4% and a consolidated £1,000 on all other pay points. This is a first step towards parity with teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of A Fair Start programme for improving educational underachievement.
answer_text: Progress in respect of the forty-six actions taken forward in the 2023/24 financial year has been good despite the significant budget constraints faced by the education sector as a whole. I am currently reviewing the fifth “A Fair Start” progress report and this will be published when Executive colleagues have had an opportunity to consider it.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they are leading on a trauma-informed approach across all Departments and statutory agencies.
answer_text: While each agency has its own responsibilities, work to support trauma-informed practice (TIP) across the public sector has been funded since 2021 by the cross-Executive Tackling Paramilitary Activity, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme.  A report commissioned to review progress across the 21 Safeguarding Board member agencies and partners in developing a systemic and integrated approach to TIP was published in February 2024, and highlights successes, barriers and enablers to progress: it is available on the Safeguarding Board’s website: https://www.safeguardingni.org/resources/we-are-journey-implementing-trauma-informed-approaches-northern-ireland-evidence-report  Further research is now underway to establish the prevalence of trauma and adversity in the adult population, and will report later this year.Within the Civil Service, a resilient, engaged and productive workforce that is able to recognise, address and be resilient to trauma has never been more important.  The Head of the Civil Service has committed to reviewing how to embed trauma informed approaches across the NICS and deliver trauma informed services. This will be underpinned by a trauma-informed training framework that is currently in development. As regards TEO’s responsibilities, the new draft Strategy for Victims and Survivors of the Troubles/Conflict has a vision of a trauma-informed society that addresses the needs of victims and survivors. The T:BUC review process also included a review of international best practice in trauma-informed approaches to good relations and community building.  TEO’s arm’s-length bodies established a task and finish group to consider how TIP might influence the work of TEO and its ALBs, and a training session for the Department and ALBs is now being set up.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to bring forward legislation for compulsory microchipping in cats.
answer_text: My Department’s Code of Practice for the Welfare of Cats already recommends that owners ensure their cats are identified, using either a collar or microchip. However, I am aware that from 10 June 2024, the microchipping of pet cats in England will become mandatory.    I am considering the compulsory microchipping of pet cats in the context of all pressing needs for animal welfare reform in Northern Ireland.  However, ultimately, my capacity to enact legislation will be constrained by the resources available to me and the time left in this shortened mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an explanation of the delay of the Northern Ireland Environment Agency in responding to planning applications as a statutory consultee.
answer_text: The primary reason for NIEA delay in responding to planning applications is that the number of complex planning consultations receipted exceeds the capacity that staff in post have to respond. Contributing factors include the number of re-consultations received / the quality of information submitted, and the level of non-statutory work associated with the delivery of our planning role and responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of compensation paid out by his Department due to vehicle damage caused by public roads in disrepair, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20182019 Table 7.1 in the above link provides details of vehicle damage compensation paid during the last five full financial.  I would, however, advise the member and other members using the above link to be cognisant of the caveats noted in respect to the information provided on roads related public liability claims.   Please also note that the figures for the 2023/2024 financial year are not currently available as the end of year financial adjustments have yet to be finalised, together with the end of year data management activities on my Department’s Claims Management System.The link to this information is also available via the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to his approval of the teachers’ pay awards, (i) when he will announce a review of the pay and grading structure; and (ii) whether the review will include teaching assistants’ pay.
answer_text: Department officials have been in discussion with the Education Authority regarding a Pay & Grading Review business case for support staff.  This was submitted to the Department of Finance and approval was granted on 4 April 2024.  Given the significant cost associated with the Pay & Grading Review, my Department will need additional funding from the Executive in order to implement its recommendations.  I have therefore submitted a bid for this funding as part of the Executive’s Budget 2024-25 process.The Pay & Grading Review covers school support staff, including classroom assistants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to reverse the decision to halt work on proposals for a bypass at Ballykelly.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which includes the A2 at Ballykelly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding granted to sports clubs in each of the last five years, broken down by sport.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to reinitiate counselling services for primary schools.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023.  An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and is now published on the Department’s website.  I will take the time to assess the findings of the evaluation along with wider research / feedback from stakeholders in developing the Department’s longer-term approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, since its inception, to detail (i) the number of calls made to the Phone First number; (ii) a breakdown of the various directions given following these calls, broken down by constituency; and (ii) his assessment of the impact this has had on emergency departments.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the future provision of general surgery at Causeway Hospital; and (ii) to detail the reasons behind the exploration into the potential of ending general surgery there.
answer_text: In March 2024, the Northern Trust published “Causeway Hospital A Strategic Vision.” The vision outlines the Trust’s ambition to develop and enhance the Causeway site, focussing on providing a 24/7 Emergency Department and acute inpatients services, same day emergency care, elective care and cancer services, and reshaping mental health provision to meet the needs of the local population. It also highlights how Causeway Hospital is perfectly positioned to become an elective and diagnostic hub for the entire North West area.  On Thursday 28 March 2024, I visited the newly established surgical ambulatory service. This service which is opened each week - Monday to Friday - 9am to 5pm, assesses, diagnoses and treats surgical patients referred from the ED Department and it is planned that the service will be open to GP referrals in the near future. This is a great new service and it means that emergency and urgent surgical patients are streamlined to treatment in a safe and effective way.I appreciate the work of our doctors, nurses and healthcare staff and acknowledge the positive impact that this model for the emergency surgery service will bring for patients presenting with surgical conditions such as; Diverticulitis were no abscess is present, mild cholecystitis, perianal abscess, pilonidal abscess, early discharge patients’ post-surgery who can be safely managed via the surgical “hot” clinic for further treatment and review.The need to change in how we deliver surgical services in Northern Ireland is necessary as we develop service models that can meet/best mitigate the essential standards set within the Review of General Surgery in Northern Ireland. I appreciate the changes and recent developments in Northern Trust, to improve services for the local population, particularly the new ambulatory services and pathways which should improve outcomes and make services more accessible and am delighted to support the need for this new model for emergency ambulatory surgery in the Causeway area and the benefits it brings to the population of the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department’s policy for households that are eligible for the Housing Executive's Disabled Facilities Grant, but do not receive the required funding to complete the work to enable the individual to receive the support or equipment to stay in their own home.
answer_text: Since the Housing Executive (NIHE) has responsibility for administering the Disabled Facilities Grant, my Department does not have a specific policy in this area. In practice, the needs of each individual are assessed by the relevant HSC Trust’s Occupational Therapy Service, with recommendations on the nature of the adaptation work required then shared with the NIHE for processing.  Works are normally subject to a statutory maximum level of grant aid (currently £70k), and only in very exceptional circumstances will NIHE funding exceed that level.Cases in which the required works exceed the maximum funding are referred for consideration by a Regional Interagency Escalation panel, which will take account of the type and nature of the adaptation work, the level of funding required, and the individuals means to contribute. These panels are coordinated by my Department’s Housing and Health Lead, who is jointly employed by the Department of Health and Housing Executive.Ultimately, the statutory responsibility and decision to provide any additional funding will rest with the HSC Trusts as the Department have no identified resources to support in these cases.This approach has been endorsed by the Interdepartmental (Department of Health/Department for Communities) Housing and Health Adaptations Programme Board, and my Department does intend issuing guidance to HSC Trusts to reinforce the position to ensure a consistent and equitable approach.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the schemes his Department has been involved in with charities in East Londonderry that help dementia sufferers continue to socialise and mix with others outside their home.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Supporting People programme grant funds Floating Support provision that can help dementia sufferers continue to socialise and mix with others outside their home.15 units of Floating Support in the Coleraine area are provided by a charity where the primary client group is ‘Older People with Mental Health Problems / Dementia’. This service is provided by Praxis Care – Coleraine Dementia SPFS 45.The following Floating Support services provided by charities were the primary client group ‘Older People’, can also support people affected by dementia.Radius Housing Association provide 560 units of floating support via Floating Support Larne, Carrickfergus and Newtownabbey in the Northern Health & Social Care Trust area.The Cedar Foundation provide 40 units of floating support via Elderly Floating Support Scheme, Moyle in the Moyle and Coleraine area.First Housing Aid & Support Services provide 60 units of Floating support via Floating Support Scheme for Older People Limavady/Derry/Strabane, in the Limavady/Derry/Strabane area.Praxis Care provide 77 units of Floating support via Foyle SPFS 43 in the Londonderry and Strabane area.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the testing of water in the lower Bann is carried out on a regular basis to ensure harmful chemicals and toxins are not present in the waterways.
answer_text: My Department undertakes monitoring across four sites of the Lower Bann under The Water Environment (Water Framework) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017.   Monthly or quarterly monitoring is undertaken for 29 chemicals and trace metals.  During each six-year River Basin Plan cycle, additional monitoring is undertaken each month for a twelve month period. This additional testing looks for a wide range of pollutants that are considered potential threats to water quality.  During 2023 ad-hoc monitoring was also undertaken to verify the presence of Blue/Green algae.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether there is an upper limit for the Housing Executive's Disabled Facilities Grant; and (ii) the process when the cost of the work rises after the grant is allocated, but the family or individual cannot afford to make up the deficit.
answer_text: (i) Article 2 of The Housing Renovation etc. Grants (Grant Limit) Order (Northern Ireland) 2003 informs the Housing Executive of the maximum grant-aid amount which it can approve for eligible works.  As of 1 February 2023, the maximum amount is £35,000.  There is provision under Article 64(3)(b) of the Housing (NI) Order 2003 for the Housing Executive, under certain circumstances, to provide additional grant aid for mandatory adaptation works.  The additional amount has been agreed at £35,000 so the total payable for eligible works is up to a maximum of £70,000. (ii) The Housing Executive has advised that the grant award is calculated using a Schedule of Rates for construction works informed by the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors Building Cost Information Service Cost Indices (RICS BCIS Indices).  These rates are reviewed and, if necessary, individual rates adjusted should market forces dictate. During the normal life-time of a case the Housing Executive may complete multiple recalculations for a number of different reasons.  This includes, and is not limited to, for example;an applicant’s OT recommendation may change and have additional eligible works added; there has been an increase in the Schedule of Rates - where work has not started the applicant’s case may benefit from the uplift in rates; orunforeseen works emerge after the work has started on site.The Housing Executive will review each case as need arises and at the end will complete a recalculation by way of a final re-cost to ensure amounts of all eligible works are accounted for.Where appropriate the Housing Executive may sign-post an applicant to potential bursary and other grant schemes which may consider contributing towards the works being completed if they are not fully met by the grant award.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the powers his Department has to legally compel (a) private; and (b) public owners of derelict buildings to bring them up to a habitable standard; and (ii) the number of times this has been done in the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not have specific powers to compel property owners to bring derelict buildings up to habitable standards, however Councils have authority to deal with privately owned derelict buildings that are insecure and open to public access.The powers which local Councils have to address dilapidated buildings fall under the responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/dilapidation	Environmental Health Officers have powers to enforce the statutory minimum fitness standard for housing, as set out in Article 46 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1981.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to (i) make PSA tests routine as part of blood tests being taken by men over 50-years-old; and (ii) lower the age at which these tests are carried out, particularly for those with a family history of early-onset prostate cancer.
answer_text: There are no plans to make PSA test routine as part of blood tests taken by men over 50 years old.  The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee which advises the UK Health Ministers, does not currently recommend screening for prostate cancer.  This is because the Prostate Specific Antigen (PSA) test is not accurate enough to detect prostate cancer that needs treatment. It can falsely identify men who do not have prostate cancer. It can also miss some cancers. This means that many men might have to undergo unnecessary and often unpleasant tests and/or unnecessary treatment. It is currently unclear if other tests such as an MRI scan, with or without PSA, are accurate enough. Research is looking at whether a method for predicting prostate cancer risk using a combination of a blood test and other information about a man could be more accurate. But more studies are necessary to confirm the early results. My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount paid out in claims to drivers whose vehicles have been damaged by potholes in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) motorways; (ii) A roads; and (iii) B roads.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online or via the at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20182019  Table 7.1 in the above link provides details of vehicle damage compensation paid during the last five full financial. Please note, however, the NICS accounting system does not record the road class for incidents that lead to compensation payments. Therefore, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by road class as requested. I would also advise the member and other members using the above link to be cognisant of the caveats noted in respect to the information provided on roads related public liability claims. The link to this information is also available via the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount of funding provided to each amateur boxing club in East Londonderry in each of the last five years; (ii) how clubs apply for funding; and (iii) the amount of funding available for small community sports clubs.
answer_text: As this issue is an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive.  As the information received is extensive, I have arranged for this to be provided in the Assembly library. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly. Making direct contact with Sport NI will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the steps he has taken since assuming office to roll-out the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not been delivered and I am committed to taking this forward as a matter of priority.Since taking office, I have:visited a wide range of football clubs to hear about their needs directly; met the Irish FA and Northern Ireland Football League to hear their feedback on the sector’s needs; received a Strategic Outline Case setting out high level proposals for my consideration and approval; held discussions with the Irish FA regarding the development of a National Training Centre;asked for options to invest at grassroots level in parallel to undertaking the research and evidence gathering needed to underpin a comprehensive package of support; and hosted a visit by the UK Sports Minister to Belfast on 4 March, and I have also written to him with background and my Department’s assessment of need for investment to modernise local football facilities. Pointing out that the current NI Executive ringfenced budget of £36.2m falls far short of the required needs. It is important to note that ambitious, large scale capital infrastructure investments such as this take time to deliver. My officials are working closely with stakeholders including clubs themselves to ensure robust proposals are developed. Investments must address clearly identified needs and create future proofed facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether the growth of electric vehicle charge points is keeping pace with the growth in the number of electric vehicles.
answer_text: The North has currently around 1.27 million vehicles registered with circa 50,000 new registrations per year. Of these, there are currently in the region of 20,450 EVs registered. The proportion of new cars registered here using alternative fuels has risen to a high of 41% of all new cars sold in quarter one of 2023/24. Whilst most charging of electric vehicles is carried out at home charge points, there are now 549 publicly available charge points available to EV drivers. This represents a 63% increase in publicly available charge points since August 2021. There have been 80 new chargers installed in the past two months alone, which is a rise of 17%. The provision and operation of those publicly accessible EV Charge Points is a commercially led operation in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of any commercially operated charge point rests solely with the Charge Point Operator. Current providers operating here include BP Pulse, Easygo, ESB, Maxol and Weev. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure.In this commercially led market, my officials continue to provide advice to Charge Point Operators on operational matters such as procedural arrangements for planning and installation requirements.Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils and are currently preparing a tender for charge point operators to provide 127 charge points across those Council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the impact of a ban on amalgam fillings in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail how he can prevent this ban from being implemented here.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the practical implications of the revised law.  I think they will be significant and persisting both in terms of cost and capacity.  In 2022/23 dentists placed 201k amalgam fillings.  Approximately 31% of Health Service fillings are amalgam and this number rises to 64% for posterior teeth, where the larger fillings are needed.In particular, I am concerned about the impact on capacity, which is already struggling as a result of the pandemic, as non-amalgam fillings can take two or three times longer to place, depending on a number of factors.We have estimated the cost as being £3.6m to the Department per annum and £2.75m in total for patients.  However, increases in the price of amalgam and non-amalgam are anticipated, so the overall costs could be more.The law will come into effect in Northern Ireland in January 2025 without the successful deployment of the Stormont Brake.  The Democratic Scrutiny Committee have an important role in assessing the significance of any impact of the changes in the law.  My Department has briefed them of our concerns, will be providing further evidence to them in April 2024 and will continue to support them as much as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many schools have (i) transformed to integrated status; and (ii) expressed an interest transforming to integrated status, in the last year.
answer_text: Last year there was one school which transformed to integrated status. Development Proposal (DP) 690 – which proposed that “Sion Mills Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status, with effect from 1 September 2023, or as soon as possible thereafter” was approved on 11 September 2023 and officially transformed to integrated status on the 1 January 2024.The table below details a number of development proposals currently with the Department for transformation to integrated status. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the spread and impact of the invasive zebra mussel species; and (ii) the areas in East Londonderry that have been affected by this.
answer_text: (i)  Zebra mussels were first reported in Northern Ireland in 1996 from Lower Lough Erne with records from Upper Lough Erne the following year.  The first confirmed record in Lough Neagh was at Kinnego Marina in 2005 and it has now spread to the Lower Bann and tributaries. It thrives in eutrophic waters but does not survive in acidic water bodies.Zebra mussels have been implicated in a range of impacts including changes in nutrient cycles, reduced plankton, increased water clarity, increased plant growth around lake margins, population declines of native mussels, and changes in fish populations. Not all of these interactions are fully understood. In addition, they can block water intake pipes and boat engines, and foul the hulls of boats.(ii)  Within the East Londonderry constituency, there are reports of zebra mussels being present in the Lower Bann at The Cutts and Kilrea Bridge. There are further records from the Articlave River at Ardina Bridge and the Bannbrook at Quilley Road Bridge.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to increase (i) the use of; and (ii) funding for, Acute Care at Home services.
answer_text: In March 2022, my Department published the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care. Under the recommendations of the review, a Task and Finish group was established, through an Intermediate care sub-group to examine the current challenges regarding hospital at home services, particularly how to ensure consistency of approach and outcomes of a high performing model. The outworkings of this group are at the later stages and a final report will be submitted to the Chief Nursing Office, as lead sponsor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Consumer Council report that shows a quarter of households are left with less than £32 per week after all essentials are paid for; and (ii) to detail any policies he is aiming to develop to support those in financial need.
answer_text: The Consumer Council’s report underscores the need for long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland. Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me. I look forward to working with my Executive colleagues to develop a sustainable, deliverable and affordable Anti-Poverty Strategy which prioritises the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives.My officials have developed draft debt respite policy proposals in preparation for a public consultation, which I expect to consider in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail progress that has been made regarding the list of animal medicines at risk of being discontinued in Northern Ireland because of post-Brexit arrangements.
answer_text: There is currently a grace period for veterinary medicines until 31 December 2025. This grace period was granted on condition that the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (VMD), an agency of Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) and United Kingdom (UK) competent authority, comply with measures in a joint European Union (EU)/UK Action plan. This action plan is a matter for the UK Government and requires the UK to provide details of veterinary medicines at risk in Northern Ireland (NI) including those in danger of being discontinued as well as information on measures which could be undertaken to ensure the continued availability of veterinary medicines to NI. VMD submitted documents to the EU Commission to meet Milestone 3 of the EU/UK Action plan on 31 January 2024 and are currently working on Milestone 4.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the repercussions of the NHS England ban on puberty blockers for those in Northern Ireland already on these drugs; and (ii) how he will respond to this ban.
answer_text: (i) Currently the decision of NHS England to ban puberty blockers has had no repercussions for those in Northern Ireland. Puberty blockers are not banned in Northern Ireland. They are available on NHS prescription for those under 18s, who were accepted and remain in the Belfast Trust's Knowing Our Identity Service  endocrine pathway prior to March 2020.  (ii) My officials have been working with the Gender Identity Services Pathway Review Group and the Gender Identity Services Liaison Panel (which includes representatives from transgender organisations) to develop the Gender Identity Service.  At the next quarterly meeting, they will have the opportunity to discuss the ban on puberty blockers by NHS England.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how she will protect owners of new buildings which are subsequently found to be unsafe or defective; and (ii) any buildings, other than Victoria Square, Belfast, that are being explored to determine whether they are unsafe.
answer_text: I have instructed my officials to develop legislation to offer residents in Northern Ireland the same protection as currently offered in other UK jurisdictions.  My Department has been made aware of one other building that is currently undergoing continued investigations as recently highlighted in local media, namely Coopers Court.  I am unaware of any other buildings currently under review for structural safety concerns.DfC’s Residential Building Safety Division are currently working to implement the recommendations of the Department of Finance Expert Panel’s report into the safety of high-rise residential buildings in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the options that exist for creating an offshore windfarm; and (ii) the difference in cost between these and the options that exist for an onshore windfarm.
answer_text: My Department established the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group to develop an action plan to deliver offshore renewable energy in the north. Working collaboratively across various government departments and with partner organisations, officials have developed a draft OREAP. The Draft OREAP aims to enable the deployment of 1GW of offshore energy in our marine area from 2030. As you are aware Planning is within the remit of the Department for Infrastructure (DfI), therefore my Department does not hold the cost information you have requested. DfI have provided me with the following information in relation to your question:Planning fees are prescribed under the Planning (Fees) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, and schedule 2 of this sets out the fee levels by category of development (Scales of Fees) Currently, fees for onshore windfarms are considered under Category 6 ‘The erection, alteration or replacement of plant and machinery including telecommunications/data communications equipment, a single wind turbine and wind farms’. The fee payable is calculated as such: (a) where the site area does not exceed 5 hectares, £409 for each 0.1 hectare of the site area; (b) where the site area exceeds 5 hectares, £20,450; and an additional £121 for each 0.1 hectare in excess of 5 hectares, subject to a maximum in total of £304,316

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to increase the (i) number; and (ii) spread of electric vehicle charge points, particularly the ultra-rapid variety.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercial operation in the North of Ireland. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of any commercially operated charge point rests solely with the charge point operator. Current providers operating in the North include BP Pulse, Easygo, ESB, Maxol and Weev. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure.  My Department has no plans to intervene in this commercial market but will continue to provide advice to Chargepoint Operators (CPOs) on operational matters such as procedural arrangements for planning and installation requirements.My Department does not hold information detailing electrical vehicle charging points or their locations.  Relevant up to date electric vehicle charge point locations can be found on online platforms, such as https://www.zapmap.com.  The relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of charge points in your constituency.   Local Councils have also been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emission’s, On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme which my Department match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council are leading a consortium on this work having been successful in securing funding to install 124 charge points across nine Council areas and may be in a position to outline their plans should you wish to contact them. My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider the barriers to enhancing our EV charging infrastructure requirements.  An Action Plan to deliver a commercially led, fit for purpose, modern EV charging network was agreed in autumn 2022. The Action Plan can be found here:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/electric-vehicle-ev-infrastructure-action-plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the reasons behind obliging councillors to attend meetings in person; (ii) whether there are mitigating circumstances in which councillors may still attend remotely; and (iii) to detail the remote working policy in the Department for Communities.
answer_text: The flexibility for councillors to attend meetings remotely was introduced using enabling powers in section 78 of the Coronavirus Act 2020.  This was intended as emergency legislation, and I do not believe there is justification in prolonging provisions made under the Coronavirus Act.   The decision not to extend provisions of the Coronavirus Act means that remote attendance at council meetings has temporarily ceased, however, I am committed to bringing forward regulations under the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act 2021, within as short a timeframe as possible,  to provide councils with the flexibility to hold meetings by remote or hybrid means. There are no mitigating circumstances which would currently allow councillors to attend remotely. It will require new legislation to reintroduce arrangements for hybrid/remote meetings.  The Department for Communities follows the NICS Hybrid Working Policy, a copy of which can be found here https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dfp/NICS%20Hybrid%20Working%20Policy%20-%20MASTER%20clean%20v1.3%20Oct22%20with%20links.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps he is taking to (i) increase the number of Education Welfare Officers; (ii) reduce the backlog of children waiting to be allocated one; and (iii) equate the pay of Education Welfare Officers with social workers.
answer_text: My officials are working with the Education Authority on a Business Case to resolve the on-going industrial action within the Education Welfare Service which is having a negative impact on waiting lists. The Business Case will need to be agreed by the Department of Finance and funding will be required to resolve the pay and grading issues.  Following a Transformation programme conducted in 2021/22, it was agreed that the Education Welfare Service would take a proactive approach to the management of pupil attendance and all the associated issues.  Once implemented, a transformed Education Welfare Service will provide a timelier, consistent, and more effective approach which will support schools and pupils in meeting their specific needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the policy of offering places in care homes for those awaiting discharge from hospital, but for whom a required care package is not in place.
answer_text: This policy has been operating since September 2022. Where a person that may otherwise have been offered a domiciliary care package as part of their discharge is unable to be provided with such a package on a timely basis, they will be offered a place in appropriate residential/nursing accommodation on a temporary basis if their needs cannot be met safely at home without some form of support.Where this offer is accepted, and the individual moves into residential/nursing accommodation on a temporary basis, the relevant charging regulations will not be applied while their preferred option of a domiciliary care package is not available. However, should they later be offered, and choose to decline, a domiciliary care package, when it can be provided, then the charging position will change at this point in line with the relevant charging regulations.Since September 2022, 347 patients in the South Eastern Trust have availed of this offer at a cost of £2.3m. 68 patients in the Western Trust have also taken up this offer at a cost of £1.4m. The remaining Trusts were unable to provide the information sought within the set time frame.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the percentage of recyclable materials generated in Northern Ireland that is recycled in (a) Northern Ireland; (b) the Republic of Ireland; (c) the UK; and (d) elsewhere; and (iii) what safeguards are in place to ensure the material sent outside of Northern Ireland is recycled as opposed to disposed of in another way.
answer_text: Robust data is collected from all 11 councils on municipal recycling. At present there is no mandatory requirement for any other sectors to report on the recycling of waste generated in Northern Ireland, therefore, the data available is limited largely to household waste. From the Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste (LACMW) data in 2022/23:75% of this material was recycled in Northern Ireland2.4% of this material was sent to Republic of Ireland for recycling.5.6% of this material was sent to Great Britain for recycling with the total UK figure being 80.6%.17% of this material was sent elsewhere for recycling.   For waste movements outside of NI but inside the UK i.e. to GB there is a Duty of Care and associated code of practice in NI. Waste destined for international recycling must comply with the requirements of the 1013/2006 Waste Shipment Regulations. There are assurances and checks in place as part of this process which is regulated by NIEA. Assurances include quality checks on NI waste prior to export, verification checks of destination recycling sites and liaison with the competent authorities in destination and transit countries.Yours sincerely
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has a role to encourage individuals from a Catholic community background to join the PSNI.
answer_text: It is incumbent upon me, as upon all elected representatives, to encourage people from all walks of life, including but not limited to religious background, to consider a career in policing in order to ensure that the Police Service of Northern Ireland is representative of the population which it serves. My role as Minister is to provide a legislative framework with appropriate measures in place to ensure accountability in the recruitment process in addition to the normal oversight arrangements operated by the Policing Board. There is currently provision for targeting advertising to reach under-represented groups and the recruitment process is open to all those who meet the minimum criteria regardless of their religious background.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he will take to (i) reverse the decline in jobs growth; and (ii) reduce the number of redundancies.
answer_text: While the number of employee jobs here decreased in the quarter to December 2023 by 0.6%, it is more appropriate to consider the year-on-year change, which has increased by 0.8% in the year to December 2023, with  the growth of over 6,400 new jobs. I aim to continue and accelerate this trend by working to raise productivity through a focus on skills, support to business and recognition of the specific productivity issues in some areas. I will refocus Invest NI to ensure it delivers locally designed interventions to support our small and medium-sized enterprises, work in collaboration with local partners to enhance existing areas of strength and seek to attract higher productivity Foreign Direct Investment. 		My department has been notified of 2,430 confirmed redundancies in the twelve-month period to February 2024, which is a significant increase on the 940 confirmed redundancies in the previous twelve months, although both are similar to pre-pandemic data. I intend to bring forward a Good Jobs Bill, which will provide flexibility and protect workers’ rights, and strengthen the role of trade unions, particularly in low paying sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what plans she has to participate in discussions in relation to the prospective move away from criminality by the UDA.
answer_text: I am not aware of any such discussions taking place with the UDA and as Minister of Justice it would not be appropriate for me to participate in discussions with a proscribed organisation.  Where people are engaged in criminality, they should either stop or expect to face the force of the law. PSNI and other law enforcement agencies have my full support in pursuing those who use the cloak of paramilitarism as cover to carry out harm in communities. As well as criminal justice measures, I am focused on supporting cross-Government initiatives to address the vulnerabilities in communities that are often exploited by paramilitaries.  This is being addressed through the Executive’s Tackling Paramilitarism and Organised Crime Programme and many other interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to develop an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me and I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for our communities right across Northern Ireland.	I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the work that is being undertaken to increase the number of ultra-rapid electric vehicle charge points.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercial operation in the North of Ireland. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of any commercially operated charge point rests solely with the charge point operator. Current providers operating in the North include BP Pulse, Easygo, ESB, Maxol and Weev. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure.  My Department has no plans to intervene in this commercial market but will continue to provide advice to Chargepoint Operators (CPOs) on operational matters such as procedural arrangements for planning and installation requirements.My Department does not hold information detailing electrical vehicle charging points or their locations.  Relevant up to date electric vehicle charge point locations can be found on online platforms, such as https://www.zapmap.com.  The relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of charge points in your constituency.   Local Councils have also been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emission’s, On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme which my Department match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council are leading a consortium on this work having been successful in securing funding to install 124 charge points across nine Council areas and may be in a position to outline their plans should you wish to contact them. My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider the barriers to enhancing our EV charging infrastructure requirements.  An Action Plan to deliver a commercially led, fit for purpose, modern EV charging network was agreed in autumn 2022. The Action Plan can be found here:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/electric-vehicle-ev-infrastructure-action-plan.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any expressions of interest that have been (i) sought; or (ii) received as part of the development of the previous Shackleton Barracks site.
answer_text: MJM Renewables was selected as the preferred purchaser of the Shackleton site following an assessment process in 2016. 	The Executive Office no longer owns the Shackleton site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work that has been undertaken to emphasise the north coast’s surfing conditions in attracting tourists to the area.
answer_text: Tourism NI (TNI) supports the development and promotion of surfing along the Causeway Coastal Route through Experience Development, Investment, Marketing Campaigns and International Media Visits. TNI provides assistance for tourism organisations to support them in creating experiences that embrace our giant spirit and stimulate demand for tourism in the North to the benefit of the wider economy.  TNI is currently working with Alive Adventures Surf School in Portrush, to onboard the business to the destination brand and support its wider activities which include e-biking, to support year-round sustainability. Alive Adventures received a total investment of £132,341.79 from the Experience Development Programme 21/23 to fund activity equipment and the development of a safety briefing area.  In 2023, TNI produced a new online toolkit for all outdoor activity business titled “Developing Experiences in the Outdoors”, which supports growth into new markets and encourages businesses to work in partnership to grow their destination. TNI's consumer website - discovernorthernireland.com provides a surf specific landing page, currently hosts tailored information about each of the following key surf providers: Long Line Surf School (Limavady), Troggs Surf School SUB6 Surf & SUP School, Portrush Surf School and Alive Adventures (all Portrush). In addition, in promotes many of the key surfing beaches which often provide information relating to existing surfing providers, facilities and services, and link to nearby attractions and accommodation.The website also promotes the wide range of water-based activity on the Causeway Coastal Route to compliment the surf offer for example Freedive NI, Revive North Coast, Active Adventures NI, Causeway Coasteering, Get up & Get on Paddleboarding and Eagle Divers NI. This content is often curated in partnership with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council. Each year, TNI work closely with Tourism Ireland to curate itineraries for International Media Visits. The itineraries are themed by interest area such as golf, culture arts and heritage etc The 2024 soft adventure programme includes a Causeway Coast surfing experience. TNI's Marketing Campaigns target consumers across Ireland, including TV, Broadcast Video On Demand, Radio, Press, Social & Digital, Email, PR and Influencer activity. Long Line Surf School is featured in the current 2024 Spring Campaign and are part of our Active Maximiser segment product offering, for which we commissioned photography last year.  In addition to campaign activity, surf schools are featured seasonally i.e. inclusion in bank holiday bucket lists, activities for families etc. Similarly, TNI digital campaign promotes Long Line Surf School which has featured continuously at both Belfast Airports since Summer 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the mechanism via which the £20 million regeneration funding for Coleraine will be distributed; (ii) whether groups or organisations may apply for funding; and (iii) the geographical limit where the money may be spent.
answer_text: This funding was announced by the UK Government.  My department has not been involved in any discussions relating to this funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the loss of Coleraine’s enterprise zone, whether the recently announced new prospective client is still in the process of locating to the Atlantic Link area.
answer_text: The prospective client has withdrawn its interest in the Atlantic Link site.Work is ongoing between officials in my Department and the British government to develop proposals for the Enhanced Investment Zone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of (i) the changes in the recent budget regarding the scrapping of tax incentives for owners of holiday lets; and (ii) how this could impact the north coast area.
answer_text: My department has not undertaken an assessment of these changes. That would be a matter for the British Treasury, which is responsible for taxation and the removal of this relief, and the Department for the Economy, which has local policy responsibility for tourism.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the scale of illegal moneylending; and (ii) what tools she is using to address this practice.
answer_text: Whilst illegal money lending is an under-reported crime in Northern Ireland, it is one that we know is causing significant harm within communities. Many reasons underpin underreporting: some victims are concerned about reporting their own involvement with those involved in illegal activity while others fear reprisals if they provide information to the police. The possibility of victims being drawn into criminality due to unpaid ‘debts’ ‘owed’ to paramilitary groups also prevents some victims from seeking help or reporting concerns.Addressing the harm from illegal money lending requires a system-wide response and PSNI work with a range of partners to assist those affected, including the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, foodbanks and community fridges, Northern Ireland Association for the Care and Resettlement of Offenders (NIACRO) (Base2), Christians against Poverty (CAP) and other community groups including addiction support and women's groups. The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) has helped to raise awareness of the issue through its current ‘Ending the Harm’ public awareness campaign on this topic and also provides a wide range of supports to people who are at risk of many types of paramilitary exploitation. The Department for Communities funds Advice NI and its affiliated front-line community-based centres to deliver a free, independent, and impartial debt advice service. Their Debt Advisers can support victims of illegal money lending by offering free, independent debt advice.PSNI have been upskilling their officers in identified hotspot locations to be more aware of illegal money lending and, also, how to maximise potential investigative opportunities. Illegal money lending has also recently been added as a specific crime type for the purposes of police recording, helping to ensure that there is a greater understanding of the prevalence of this activity in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he will take to ensure planning officials are kept up to date and fully trained in the latest renewable energy technology, so as to better make recommendations and decisions within councils.
answer_text: I recognise that training is an important component of improving the planning process.The identification of training needs for planning officers across local government is primarily the responsibility of each council. However, my Department has been engaging with the renewables industry and other stakeholders, including Councils, with a view to working collaboratively on learning opportunities in relation to the latest renewable energy technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of vehicles without a valid MOT certificate; (ii) the number of vehicles both without valid car tax and not declared as SORN; (iii) the number of vehicles that have an MOT certificate due to expire within one month and do not have an MOT appointment booked; (iv) the impact of the cracked vehicle lifts in MOT testing centres; and (v) the resultant reduction in test frequency during the period of repair or replacement of the lifts.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not hold this information requested at points (i) and (ii).  Whilst the DVA holds the information requested at point (iii), it is not held in a readily accessible format and would take a significant amount of time and cost to produce.There has been no impact on MOT testing as a result of small hairline cracks being identified in the welds of the main scissor bushing in a number of vehicle lifts.  The lifts are independently assessed, with the assessor attributing their lowest category of fault to this issue.  The lifts remain serviceable and safe to use.  The inspection frequency for the affected lifts has been increased from an eight-weekly inspection to a four-weekly inspection.  Where possible, any repairs required to vehicle testing equipment are completed during DVA’s planned preventative maintenance programme to minimise any disruption to the delivery of MOT services.
