question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work being done to attract, train and retain carers.
answer_text: Staffing levels continue to be a significant challenge across the health and social care sector. My officials continue to work to maximise current capacity in social care and to engage with service providers in regard to addressing those ongoing issues of recruitment and retention.  This is only possible with a skilled and dedicated workforce. 	I recognise the need for action to support the social care sector, and those working in it.  My immediate priority on return to post, was to see pay negotiations taken forward and concluded without undue delay, for health and social care workers in the statutory sector.  		In recognition of the challenges facing the independent social care sector, I recently announced a £70m funding package across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care. The purpose of this funding is to ensure independent sector providers have the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April.  I have also established structures to consider how we move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funding that will be required to deliver this.	In recognition of the fundamental need to deliver reform and transformation in Health and Social Care, my Department has established the Adult Social Care Collaborative Forum and the Children’s Strategic Reform Board. Within both of these Forums, there are workstreams focused on supporting, developing and improving the social care workforce.These workstreams are implementing a range of measures that include:Publishing a Social Care Workforce Strategy which will set out a vision to develop a sustainable social care workforce over the next 10 years.  The strategy will include actions to develop career pathways, support the training and education of the workforce, and to raise the profile and recognition of the social care workforce as a skilled and competent workforce.  It is anticipated that the Strategy will be published in late 2024.In partnership with the Northern Ireland Social Care Council, work is ongoing to develop the training, career and qualification pathways for the social care workforce.In partnership with all stakeholders’ social care campaigns are ongoing to raise the profile and recognise the significant contribution that the social care workforce provides.In addition, the Social Care Fair Work Forum has also been established which will seek to embed Fair Work initiatives and improve the terms and conditions for everyone employed across all parts of the social care sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how he is helping to winter-proof social and low-income housing; and (ii) how he is informing those eligible of the services available.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and the relevant Housing Associations.  The Department does not hold information in relation to winter-proofing for Housing Associations.I have however sought the requested information from the Housing Executive Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:(i) Annually the Housing Executive deliver an External Cyclical Maintenance programme, a primary aim of which is to ensure the integrity of the external fabric to ensure properties are wind and watertight.  It also delivers a heating upgrade programme and a window replacement programme.The Housing Executive recently completed the ERDF funded Energy Efficiency in Social Housing programme, installing External Wall Insulation (EWI) in some 1,400 non-traditionally constructed homes.The first schemes of NIHE’s innovative 300 Low Carbon Retrofit Programme will be completed in 2024/25; this involves the installation of energy efficiency measures in 300 homes.  The Housing Executive has a number of further EWI schemes to be delivered, and has developed an initial Cavity Wall Insulation remediation/replacement programme of over 9,000 dwellings that will be rolled out across the next three to four years. (ii)  The Housing Executive’s freephone NI Energy Advice Service actively promotes energy efficiency advice and grants across all tenures including social housing and low-income households.  The advice service is promoted in partnership with a variety of stakeholders including community groups, political representatives and local Councils.The Affordable Warmth Scheme provides grants for energy efficiency measures within the home and is currently open to applications.  The scheme assists low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with an annual household income of less than £23,000.  To make an application to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, customers can contact the NI Energy Advice Service via: 0800 1114455 or email: NIenergyadvice@nihe.gov.uk.  Details of the Scheme are available on the NIHE and DfC website and also via NI Direct.  A press release was also issued by DfC in September 2023 advising of changes to the application process and how those eligible could apply.Housing Executive tenants are informed of energy related services via its Making Your Money Work Scheme, its Financial Inclusion team, and through liaison with the Energy Advice team to promote Oil Buying Networks to Housing Executive tenants via text messaging and the customer portal.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the average yearly costs for taxi drivers before any outgoing payments are made, such as payments for (a) required courses; (b) average insurance costs; (c) medical costs; and (d) any other legally required expenses; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact of these fees on the reduction in taxi driver numbers in recent years.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry, in relation to taxi driver, taxi operator and vehicle licencing. Many costs associated with being a taxi driver such as, fuel, depot charges, car rental/hire purchase are outside the remit of my Department. For your reference I have provided detail below in relation to the various aspects of licencing.   (i) (a) Taxi drivers are required to complete 35 hours training across the 5-year validity of their taxi licence.  Courses are delivered by DVA approved training providers who set the cost of their training.  Costs vary, with some costing as little as £35.(b) Insurance costs are a commercial matter and outside the remit of my Department. (c) Drivers must provide a Taxi Medical Report when applying for or renewing their Taxi Driver Licence, with drivers aged 45 and over required to provide one annually.  Any charge for these reports, is at the discretion of the GP Practice. My officials have advised me that the BMA’s suggested rate for a Taxi Medical Report is currently £120.(d) The fee for a first-time application for a taxi driver licence is £140, and £105 for a renewal.  A taxi driver licence is normally valid for 5 years. A taxi vehicle licence costs £147 and is valid for one year.  Class A and B taxis can incur a taxi meter test charge of £42. This charge is not incurred again unless the taximeter needs to be retested.A Taxi Operator Licence costs £140 and once awarded each operator must pay an annual Grant Licence fee, which is dependent on the size of the fleet which they operate.(ii) The taxi industry operates within the wider context of the existing economic situation and there are many factors that contribute to the current shortage of taxi drivers.  I have no evidence to suggest that the fees detailed above have any direct influence on the reducing the number of drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of how loneliness among older people may be exacerbated by living rurally; and (ii) to detail the steps he will take to ensure loneliness among older and more vulnerable rural dwellers is addressed.
answer_text: While the Health Survey (NI) 2022/2023 showed that people living in urban areas were more likely to show signs of loneliness than those living in rural areas, loneliness among older people in rural areas can be exacerbated due to factors such as geographical isolation, poor connectivity and transport links and reduced social opportunities. Other circumstances such as bereavement, ill health or caring responsibilities can also contribute to loneliness among older people.My Department supports a number of initiatives under its Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework which help improve health and wellbeing among vulnerable groups in rural areas.The Rural Support charity is proactive in seeking to improve the mental health and wellbeing of rural dwellers by providing programmes and services which help address loneliness and social isolation among farmers and farm families. This includes offering a confidential telephone helpline and signposting service for farmers, farm families and other rural dwellers seeking support and promoting connectivity through the ‘Across the Hedgerow’ and ‘Plough On’ projects. The Farm Families Health Checks Programme provides farmers, farm families and other rural dwellers attending markets and community events with the opportunity to avail of a comprehensive physical and mental wellbeing assessment. It also provides a signposting service to ensure that those with particular needs receive the support they are entitled to. The Rural Support Networks across Northern Ireland help to promote social inclusion and to tackle social isolation by helping to sustain community venues which provide space and opportunities for rural dwellers to meet.I recognise that loneliness in rural areas is not solely the responsibility of DAERA and that a collaborative approach to tackling loneliness is required. My Department therefore continues to work with other public authorities in seeking to tackle loneliness in rural areas including being represented on the Loneliness Forum which is facilitated by the Department of Health and includes representatives from from the Public Health Agency, the Northern Ireland Social Care Council, the 5 Health and Social Care Trusts and the Institute of Public Health.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on establishing a childcare strategy to provide free, or more affordable childcare, to support those aiming to enter the workforce.
answer_text: My department continues to progress the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy as a matter of priority. Work is now focusing on the development of a bespoke Northern Ireland package of measures to meet the outcomes we want to achieve, both in the short and longer-term. These include consideration of affordability and enabling parental employment. To assist with this, all departments have been invited to allocate staff to a Task and Finish Group which will ensure cross-departmental input across the range of issues the strategy needs to address. I have bid for funding to enable me to take forward short-term actions in the 2024/25 financial year.  I will work with my Executive colleagues on this but progress will ultimately depend on the funds being made available to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the impact of the delay in implementing a childcare strategy on families and women who wish to be able to work; and (ii) to detail his next steps in working with other Executive departments resolve this issue.
answer_text: The Department of Education leads on the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  The overarching policy aims of the strategy are to support child development, improve the affordability of childcare for families and, thereby, enable more parents, particularly mothers, to work. Implementation of the Strategy will be taken forward with the support of a cross-departmental Task and Finish Group, which includes my officials, and will involve further targeted engagement with stakeholders, to inform and test operational planning.I am committed to supporting working parents to enable them to take up, return to and remain in work.  My Department currently provides a range of financial and practical support, including:Universal Credit - help with childcare costsAdviser Discretion Fund – help with upfront childcare costsEmployability programmes – including Local Labour Market Partnerships (LMP) and JobStart.Additional information on help with childcare costs through Universal Credit and the Adviser Discretion Fund is available at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-payments-children-and-childcareAdditional information on LMP is available at:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/employment-and-training-opportunities-your-areaAdditional information on JobStart is available at:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/jobstart-schemeIn support of the development of the Strategy, my officials have shared details of this support with colleagues in the Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to work with local councils in identifying properties that have fallen into (i) dereliction; and (ii) disrepair, so that owners may be encouraged to bring them back into use as part of either private or social housing stock.
answer_text: The powers which local Councils have to address dilapidated buildings fall under the responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.However, my Department has worked with various local councils to regenerate towns and villages by addressing derelict buildings and bringing them back into use, including as homes.  For example, the Department contributed £400,000 to Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council “Empty to Occupied” scheme.  One of the objectives of this Scheme is to create an opportunity for town centre living and will include bringing eight vacant properties in urban centres across the Borough back into residential use.The Village Catalyst grant scheme is a partnership initiative between the Department for Communities, the Department for Agriculture Environment and Rural Affairs, the Architectural Heritage Fund, and the Housing Executive. Applications are invited from charities, social enterprise and other not for profit groups in rural villages of less than 5,000 people, who want to tackle rural poverty and social isolation in their area by developing a sustainable use for a disused historic building in their village. 4 pilot projects have been completed to date and 13 villages are actively developing proposals. New projects are being sought for the scheme, which could include housing opportunities.Heritage in Housing is a match funding programme from the Housing Executive, developed to support the Heritage Lottery Fund Townscape Heritage Initiative (THI), by reinstating empty upper floors of landmark buildings within conservation areas for private rent. Between 2015 and 2023, the Housing Executive has provided funding totalling £588,425 for the Heritage in Housing programme, which has contributed to the restoration of 25 empty properties and the provision of rental accommodation within five designated townscape conservation areas.  It is anticipated that there is one year remaining of the THI in its current form.  During this period, it is proposed that investment will be focused in Armagh and Lurgan.The Housing Executive engages with statutory Community Planning partners including Councils, on an ongoing basis, to develop and deliver the actions and outcomes within each of the Community Plans. This engagement has included the development of co-designed ‘Place Shaping Plans’ which aim to identify opportunities to increase the supply of shared, accessible, and affordable housing in areas of housing need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the Driver and Vehicle Agency still operates as a trading fund, specifically in relation to MOT services; and (ii) to what extent both driver and vehicle testing is tax-payer subsidised.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency still operates as a Trading Fund under the provisions of the Driver & Vehicle Agency Trading Fund (Northern Ireland) Order 2016. MOT services are accounted for within this Trading Fund.The operational running costs for driver and vehicle testing services are funded from fees charged to customers for these services. Fees for driver and vehicle testing services were recently reviewed and increases were implemented from 1 October 2023. These increases were necessary to ensure these services can be provided without the need for a taxpayer subsidy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to identify people, particularly the older generation, who may be more vulnerable to loneliness and isolation, and to put them in touch with groups that promote greater social engagement and inclusion.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action research (2020) concluded that one in 20 people experience chronic loneliness in Northern Ireland.  The rising prevalence of loneliness amongst both older and younger people continues to be identified in such research and in practice and my Department recognises the impact of loneliness on health and wellbeing. My Department has taken the following actions.  In July 2020 an initial scoping study was completed across the HSC organisations.  This aimed to capture policies, strategies and activities in place to help tackle loneliness from a health and wellbeing perspective.  Subsequently the Department established  a DOH/HSC Loneliness Forum involving representatives from the 5 Trusts, NISCC, PHA and DOH policy leads. DAERA has also worked with the forum, recognising many older people may experience loneliness living in rural areas.  Through the forum, my Department has proactively supported key initiatives that encourage older people and others to connect in their local communities, such as “chatty cafes” and “chatty benches” in local parks.  We have also encouraged Trusts to establish, support and fund local loneliness networks, again in rural areas to provide a more sustainable infrastructure in local communities including rural areas.  More recently we have commissioned NISCC to produce a loneliness learning resource for the social work and social care workforce(s). This is being designed to enhance the knowledge and skills of social workers and social care workers in practice, so that they can intervene at the earliest stage possible and provide support for older people and others who may experience loneliness and social isolation.   The resource will complement other strategic and policy work that the Department is progressing including the Reform of Adult Social Care;  social care and social work workforce policy , the Mental Health Strategy and the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, amongst others.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the absence of a loneliness strategy, how he will work with the community and voluntary sector to reach out to people, promote greater social engagement and inclusion, and support opportunities and activities that do so.
answer_text: The voluntary and community sector is diverse and complex and includes many organisations which deliver on key social outcomes with the support of a range of statutory bodies and non-statutory organisations.  My Department recognises the vital contribution of the sector in representing the interests of people and communities and in promoting societal wellbeing, resilience and inclusion.  My Department supports the work of the sector to deliver wellbeing and inclusion outcomes through investment under a range of programmes including community empowerment, housing support, employability, sport, culture and heritage.  My Department also supports the Joint Forum between government and the voluntary and community sector, a mechanism where the contribution of the sector to tackling cross-cutting issues such as loneliness and social isolation can be highlighted and promoted across government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6163/22-27, and in light of rising expenses and fixed NHS fees that mean dental remuneration does not correlate with the cost to deliver care, how he will ensure fees are set at a level that meet the true cost to deliver modern health service dentistry.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans (i) to develop a loneliness strategy; and (ii) to help embed loneliness and resultant policies cross-departmentally and in tandem with other relevant strategies.
answer_text: Northern Ireland does not currently have a specific strategy to address loneliness nor one organisation identified to lead on this work. However, it is widely acknowledged that it is an important cross cutting issue that no one organization can tackle independently. It requires a collective and collaborative approach across sectors including, central government, statutory, voluntary and community sectors. Recognising that as a society, the issue of loneliness needs to be given greater prominence, my department carried out a scoping study in 2020 to identify existing and emerging policies, strategies and initiatives that are in place across the HSC that make a positive contribution to tackling loneliness.  Subsequently a Loneliness Forum was established that  includes HSC Trusts, the Public Health Agency, the Northern Ireland Social Care Council and DoH staff. DAERA also participate in the forum.  The DOH/HSC forum focuses on promoting and sharing best practice.  My Department is committed to continuing to lead the DOH/HSC Forum and we will liaise with all stakeholders, including other Departments, to further develop plans to tackle loneliness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what advice his Department provides to local councils in order for them to maximise the amount of money they can raise from buildings such as town halls and civic buildings.
answer_text: My Department does not provide advice to Councils in order for them to maximise the amount of money they can raise from buildings such as town halls and civic buildings. The raising of money from council buildings is an operational matter for District Councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what work his Department has been involved in to determine the cause of the large number of deceased frogs found recently on the beach in the East Strand and Whiterocks area of Portrush.
answer_text: My Department is aware of reports of dead frogs in Portrush area. We have not been involved in any work to determine the cause. Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council has investigated this incident but has not sought any assistance from the Department
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process by which councils obtain the power to require a change of planning permission application, when a property changes from being occupied full-time to being occupied on a part-time basis, either as a holiday home or as a short-term let.
answer_text: There are no specific planning regulations or planning policy in place relating to holiday homes or short-term lets in an existing domestic property.  Planning permission may be required to use a house, apartment, student accommodation or other premises for a short-term let or holiday home, however this will depend on the extent to which the short-term let or holiday home changes the use and character of the property and the degree to which it impacts on its surroundings.		Planning applications seeking the change of use of an existing building or for a new build to provide a short term let or self-contained holiday accommodation will normally be determined by the  council.  Each application will be assessed on its own merits, having regard to the local development plan, regional planning policy, and all other material considerations. The relevance and weight to be given to all material considerations is a matter for the council to decide.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 6976/22-27, for her assessment of the trend of prisoners who, while not addicted to drugs prior to being sentenced to HMP Magilligan, become so while serving their sentence.
answer_text: The vast majority of people coming into Magilligan Prison with addiction issues will have developed that within the community.  While there is data that indicates some prisoners develop addictions in prison, the indications are this number is in decline.  The data used to establish these trends is extrapolated from the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland (CJINI) prisoner survey, which forms part of the inspection process. In their report of an unannounced inspection of Magilligan Prison, published in February 2022, in response to the question: ‘Have you developed a problem with illicit drugs since you have been committed to this prison,’ forty-six of the one hundred and forty three respondents indicated yes (32%).A further prisoner survey was conducted in October 2023.  In response to the question: ‘Have you developed a problem with illicit drugs since you have been committed to prison,’ forty-five of the two hundred and nine respondents indicated yes (22%).  Fourteen respondents indicated that the issue began while in Magilligan.This indicates that any trend of developing an addiction whilst in Magilligan is declining.  However Prison Staff are determined to prevent illicit substances from entering the Prison and will continue to work with partners, including the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to tackle abuse of prescription medication, and to support prisoners to address their addictive behaviour.You are welcome to visit Magilligan Prison to discuss these issues with the Governor and his team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the intensive physiotherapy options that exist in (i) Northern Ireland; and (ii) elsewhere for young patients suffering from conditions such as cerebral palsy, with the aim of enabling them to walk.
answer_text: Paediatric Physiotherapy services are available through our local Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) to all such children in Northern Ireland who require assistance to enable them to walk. Regardless of their presenting condition, these children will have a personalized treatment plan developed to address their specific needs. Plans are created in partnership with the child and their parents or carers, and to achieve the desired outcomes, may require input from other professionals such as Orthotists or Occupational Therapists. The intensity of the service provided will be determined firstly by the initial assessment and then by the progress that the child makes at follow up appointments.  If the desired outcomes are not achieved despite these interventions, where appropriate, the child can be referred on for further interventions which could include Orthopaedic or Neuro surgery.The Neuro surgery, specifically any requirement for a Selective Dorsal Rhizotomy - an operation used to reduce spasticity (muscle stiffness) in cerebral palsy - would be performed in Alder Hey children’s hospital in Liverpool, with follow up care provided back in Northern Ireland as part of a revised treatment plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 6976/22-27, to detail her assessment of how the presence of illegal drugs is maintained in HMP Magilligan.
answer_text: Regrettably, many people entering our prisons have long-standing issues regarding addictions, with associated engrained drug seeking behaviours. These behaviours continue whilst in custody. The Independent Review of Progress of Maghaberry and Magilligan Prisons undertaken by the Criminal Justice (NI) Inspectorate and His Majesty’s Chief Inspectorate of Prisons in late 2023 evidenced:“Medications were the most commonly found substances, indicating a level of success in preventing the ingress of illicit substances from the community.”Analysis of Magilligan’s drug test figures for February 2024 further underpins these findings; of 119 prisoners tested, 12 failed - all for the presence of medications not prescribed to that individual.This represents a fall of 7 % in failures, from the time of the Independent Review of Progress, and against a higher number of individuals tested.  This coupled with the use of X-Ray body scanners and other search aids, which have thwarted efforts to traffic prohibited items, demonstrates the effectiveness of Magilligan’s Drug Strategy in stopping the entry of drugs into the prison.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether funding for the perinatal mental health services operational in all Health and Social Care Trusts is ring-fenced for future years.
answer_text: My Department allocated funding in 2021 for the development of Perinatal Mental Health Services in Northern Ireland, which included establishing  Community Perinatal Mental Health Teams in each Trust. The project has two phases. Phase One prioritised a core staffing model for a Community Team, with phase two providing the enhanced service provision. Funding came from the Department at various stages throughout the year.  Phase one of the project is now complete and recurrent funding has been provided to all Trusts at a total cost of £4,186,280.A number of workshops have been held to look at lessons learned from the Phase 1 implementation and what staffing and additional funding is required for Phase 2.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of whether the £267 million Single Application and Map Service and the Entitlements Transfer Service is adequate to cover the needs of the farming sector; and (ii) to detail a comparison between this year’s funding and that of each of the last five years.
answer_text: The UK Government in the 2020 autumn spending review committed to maintain the total funding available to farmers, land managers and rural society in cash terms (measured against previous CAP Pillar I and Pillar II provision from EU funds) in every year of this Parliament, (to 2024/25). This provided ring fenced Resource DEL funding of £315.6m in 2021/22, £312.8m in 2022/23, £327.2m in 2023/24 and £329.4m for 2024/25 in each of the four years.  CAP Pillar 1, Direct Payments and Pillar 2 EU allocations to Northern Ireland in 2019 and 2020 EU financial years was £321.6 m and £322.5 m respectively.Future provision of funding for agricultural, environmental and rural support will be a matter for the next UK Government but I will certainly be seeking a settlement that reflects our policy objectives and ambitions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support and funding which is provided by his Department to the Royal National Lifeboat Institution.
answer_text: My Department does not provide any funding to the Royal National Lifeboat Institution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the 2024 Rivers Trust report State of Our Rivers; (ii) to detail the cross-departmental work he has identified as necessary to improve the quality of our waterways; and (iii) to detail the financial and ecological cost of failing to act on the evidence and warnings of the numerous reports and the water-based ecological incidents of recent years.
answer_text: The findings highlighted by the Rivers Trust report were published by NIEA in 2021 as part of the Water Framework Directive Statistics Report, which included the information that no river water bodies in Northern Ireland achieved overall good status. At that time, NIEA explained that this was due to a change in the status reporting to include so-called ‘forever’ chemicals in the assessment.The work required is summarised in the draft third River Basin Management Plan for Northern Ireland. Following my consideration and review in light of the challenges posed by the occurrence of blue-green algal blooms in Lough Neagh, I will bring the Plan to the Executive for consideration in due course. The department does not presently hold the data. It is difficult to track data citied but is a matter which my Department is exploring with Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any research that has been commissioned into assessing the number of higher education students that could be eligible for the additional support for those facing financial hardship; and (ii) whether this funding will be sufficient to support all those eligible and in need.
answer_text: Additional student support funding of £1m was allocated following the receipt of updated forecast demand analysis undertaken by the higher education institutions. Demand will continue to be monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of whether the introduction of bovine electronic identification will help with the prevention and prosecution of livestock theft; (ii) whether this system can be used with other species of livestock in future; and (iii) what other ways of preventing and prosecuting for livestock thefts are being explored.
answer_text: Although not currently in use, the Department may consider the introduction of bovine electronic identification at some point in the future.  While bovine electronic identification can deliver many benefits, including a reduction in paperwork and direct animal contact, it is not designed to specifically assist with the prevention and prosecution of livestock theft.Since 2010, electronic identification has been required for sheep and goats, however, there are currently no plans to roll this out to any further livestock species.  The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is responsible for enforcing legislation in relation to the theft of livestock.  My Department will continue to cooperate with the PSNI in respect of any relevant investigations of livestock theft, as necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any conversations he has had with the new chair of Invest NI in relation to (i) opportunities; and (ii) proposals that exist in East Londonderry for investment and economic growth, supported by Invest NI.
answer_text: I have had several discussions with John Healy OBE, the new Chair of Invest NI, about how my priority of a regionally balanced economy will be delivered. Invest NI will have a particularly important role in achieving this, and I have tasked the Chair with ensuring that the organisation increases its focus and resources at a sub-regional level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how his Department will increase funding to the community and voluntary sector to enable it to return to pre-pandemic and pre-cost-of-living-crisis levels of service; and (ii) for his assessment of how investment in this sector by his Department saves Executive departments' money at rates beyond the funding provided, by supporting or filling gaps in public services.
answer_text: The voluntary and community sector is diverse and complex and organisations receive funding from across a range of statutory bodies and non-statutory organisations.  My department has heard consistently from sector leaders in recent months that operating conditions are difficult and that the cost-of-living crisis is affecting the sustainability of services.For those organisations that my department funds, no cuts to community and voluntary sector grants were made as part of last year’s budget exercise and my department has previously provided funding uplifts to support costs associated with increased inflation, with some potential to provide similar support before the end of this financial year.Public consultation has recently ended on policy proposals from my department for future support for the Sector, setting out a commitment to continued investment in core sectoral infrastructure.  Following consideration of responses, I will bring forward proposals for future investment in sectoral infrastructure, alongside commitments to fair and sustainable funding approaches and sustained investment in the voluntary and community sector. I cannot make an evidence-based assessment of how investment in the voluntary and community sector saves money across the Executive, however I appreciate that all government departments currently benefit from the work of the Sector, in terms of the design and delivery of public services and through advocacy across a very wide range of policy interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the rules regarding the movement of cattle from Great Britain to Northern Ireland; and (ii) for his assessment of these rules.
answer_text: Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/692 supplementing Regulation (EU) 2016/429 (Animal Health Law) and Regulation 2017/625 (Official Controls Regulation), which apply in Northern Ireland (NI) by virtue of their listing in Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework, sets out the rules for the movement of cattle between Great Britain (GB) and NI.The rules vary depending on the reason for movement (Breeding & Production (B&P) or Direct Slaughter (DS)) but in summary include:Pre movement Requirements.Disease Freedom, e.g. Bovine TB.GB level residency - DS 3 months, B&P 6 months.GB Establishment level residency – 40 days.Age – Born after 14 Feb 2015.the animals must be pre-notified to the NI port of entry at least 24 hours in advance via a CHED-A electronic notification on TRACES NTMovement Requirements.During movement the animals must be accompanied by the relevant Export Health Certificate and be presented to the SPS Inspection facility for documentary, identity, physical and welfare checks.Post Movement Requirements.Re-identification if applicable (not required for DS moves).30-day restriction at destination (not required for DS moves).In October 2021, the EU Commission amended Regulation (EU) 2020/692 providing a derogation enabling NI cattle return directly from approved shows and sales in GB without needing to fulfil the six-month residency period. Please note that due to the ongoing outbreak of Bluetongue in GB, movement of cattle and other ruminants from GB to NI is currently suspended.The Windsor Framework is an international agreement between UK Government and the European Union.  Any change to the agreement or to the applicability of Animal Health Law, Official Controls Regulation and supplementing legislation in NI is a matter for the UK Government and the European Union.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) what work she is planning to carry out to protect children from being exploited by criminal and drug-dealing gangs, for instance, when they have drug debts; and (ii) what work she will do with other departments in order to protect children from being exploited by these gangs.
answer_text: Those who seek to exploit children in this way commit child abuse. The prevention of, and protection from, child criminal exploitation is a complex matter requiring input from a range of statutory and non-statutory partners. It requires a strong multi-agency approach if we are to successfully tackle this heinous crime and protect children.The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) has been at the forefront in Northern Ireland of developing evidence-based understanding and service provision for young people at risk of, and experiencing, child criminal exploitation. It has developed into a coherent proposition involving direct service provision to young people, multi-agency initiatives, and development of initiatives to enable the wider system to support young people at the risk of harm from paramilitary groups. Details of some of the key projects have been provided in AQW 6852/22-27. The Child Protection Senior Officials Group (CPSOG) provides cross-Departmental strategic direction on existing and emerging child protection issues. CPSOG is chaired by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice is a core member of the Group. In 2023, CPSOG agreed a two-year action plan to address child criminal exploitation (CCE) in NI. This cross-Departmental work, jointly led by Departments of Justice and Health, builds on the work of EPPOC to create a system wide response to child exploitation, including exploitation from criminal and drug dealing gangs. The ultimate aim is to improve the wellbeing of children and young people experiencing or at risk of criminal exploitation and to improve their longer-term life chances.The Department’s Modern Slavery, Human Trafficking and Child Exploitation Branch has engaged on issues concerning child exploitation in the development of the draft three-year Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy, including commitments and actions around developing operational responses to child exploitation and ensuring appropriate support is available to victims. The Strategy will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a timeline of when childcare providers will be provided with financial support to fully address the needs of parents prior to the end of this financial year.
answer_text: I am aware of the significant financial strain on both families and childcare providers and the calls to urgently put in place support due to cost increases from 1 April.  Options are currently being scoped, however, any scheme(s) we put in place must be based on a robust business case assessment, provide value for money, be targeted to achieve the outcomes required and have appropriate governance and accountability mechanisms built in.  It is also necessary to consider the potential impact of a bespoke childcare intervention on other sectors.  My officials are engaging with their counterparts in other departments on these issues.   I have bid for funding to enable me to take forward short-term actions in the 2024/25 financial year.  I will work with my Executive colleagues on this but progress will ultimately depend on the funds being made available to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cross-departmental work that he will facilitate between the Mental Health Champion and Executive departments to ensure staff and their service users are supported with their mental health.
answer_text: As outlined in the Terms of Reference for the Mental Health Champion (MHC), there is Executive wide support for the role. In particular, the MHC is a Government advocate to help and support all NI Departments and officials, acting as a positive conduit between Government and mental health service delivery. The MHC exercises her role in an independent manner, free from direction by the Department. The MHC has recently written to the Health and Education Committees to request an opportunity to brief them on the implementation of the Mental Health Strategy and her priorities for Health and Education. She has also requested meetings with each newly appointed Minister to discuss her role and how she can support each Department.  In addition, the MHC has written a letter to the NI Executive in which she sets out her priorities for addressing mental ill health throughout NI.I am highly supportive of the MHC’s mission to improve the mental health of the people in NI and have planned to meet her in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that children with special educational needs, and their family, are provided with the same educational opportunities as any other child and family.
answer_text: The challenges faced by children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) and their families as they try to navigate a complex system in order to access support are well rehearsed.  The various reports since 2017 and the numerous recommendations for change, point to the failings within the SEN system in its current form. There is a clear requirement for transformation of our education system that will ensure high quality, child centred and sustainable SEN support and the End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN has been established to bring forward early actions as well as longer term actions for systemic reform that will lead to improved outcomes for children and young people with SEN. My department’s vision is that EVERY CHILD is happy, learning and succeeding and this is no different for those children and young people with SEN.  I am committed to securing support from my Executive colleagues for any additional investment that is required to ensure that children with SEN get the right support, delivered by the right people, at the right time and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to introduce sub-24-week pregnancy and baby-loss certificates to parents, similar to the system in the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that pregnancy loss at any stage can have on parents and welcome the recent introduction of pre-24-week pregnancy loss certificates in England, as recommended by the Independent Pregnancy Loss Review commissioned by the Westminster Government. My Department will continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the new system is embedded there, with a view to understanding whether it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland, including any associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the process by which his Department will implement the ban on live animal exports.
answer_text: The Department has followed the progression of the Animal Welfare (Livestock Exports) Bill throughout all its stages; however, this bill is not applicable in Northern Ireland.The welfare of animals during transport is a devolved matter and due to differences in our legal framework we will not be seeking to extend this bill to Northern Ireland.My department will, however, remain focussed on ensuring that exports of live animals, when they take place, adhere to all regulatory requirements and animal welfare protections.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's progress to create more specialist surgery hubs.
answer_text: Dedicated capacity for elective surgery has been enhanced through continued development of elective care centres across Northern Ireland. Day Procedure Centres are operational at Lagan Valley Hospital and Omagh Hospital supporting a range of surgical specialties.  We have three Elective Overnight Stay Centres at the Mater Hospital, South West Acute Hospital and Daisy Hill Hospital for intermediate complexity surgery that may require an overnight stay in hospital.  In addition, there are cataracts centres at Downe, South Tyrone and Mid Ulster Hospitals and an orthopaedic hub at Musgrave Park Hospital which includes the Duke of Connaught Unit, a dedicated orthopaedic Day Procedure Centre.Considerable progress has been made through the development of dedicated elective care centres with positive impacts on waiting times. Ongoing work in these centres will be closely monitored to ensure continued progress on delivery of the intended objectives.Continued development of dedicated elective care centres will be considered in the wider context of ongoing transformation of elective care services across the HSC system and will be subject to availability of sustainable funding.  In the meantime, we continue to ensure that the HSC is making best use of the resources currently available to provide maximum benefit for patients, whilst ensuring high quality, effective and efficient, value for money services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her plans to reintroduce a drug-free wing in Magilligan Prison.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail treatment options for both adults and children who suffer from hydrocephalus.
answer_text: Hydrocephalus is treated through the surgical implantation of a shunt or by an endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV) procedure. The procedures are available for adults at Royal Victoria Hospital and for children at Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will implement the GPP NI 2030 strategy without the review and revaluation of the remuneration that community pharmacies receive from his Department.
answer_text: Following positive evaluation of the benefits of the current general practice pharmacist (GPP) service, the publication of “GPP NI 2030 – A strategy for general practice pharmacy in Northern Ireland” sets out ambitious plans for future development of the sector. The “GPP NI 2030” strategy outlines how general practice pharmacists can contribute fully to optimising the health outcomes of the population as clinical leads for medicines within the general practice team. It also sets out an ambition to develop and expand the workforce through the introduction of new advanced pharmacist practitioner roles, which will allow general practice pharmacists to enhance their clinical skills and expertise with greater benefit to patient care.Realising the vision set out in “GPP NI 2030” will require collaborative working and new innovative approaches to the planning and delivery of general practice services, and additional investment to stabilise and develop service capacity and capability is needed to realise the full potential of the general practice pharmacy workforce. My Department is committed to working with the sector to progress the recommendations outlined in “GPP NI 2030” over the coming months and years, however it is not possible to fund full implementation from within the Department’s existing resources and so delivery is dependent on securing the additional resources required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the legal process for north coast tourism businesses, with particular regard to water-based businesses, to claim compensation when toxic algal blooms prevent their businesses from operating.
answer_text: My Department has no legal process for businesses to claim compensation when impacted by blue green algae.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what steps he will take to encourage employers to hire people with disabilities; and (ii) how he will better support those with disabilities in securing work.
answer_text: I am keen to ensure all people in our society are given equal opportunity to use the skills they have and obtain good jobs.  My department provides several programmes to support people with disabilities to access employment or access training which will help them secure employment.  For instance, the Disability Support Service helps those with disabilities participate in the vocational training programmes Skills for Life and Work and ApprenticeshipsNI.  Skills for Life and Work also has an extended age eligibility for young people with a disability while a disability supplement is payable to allow training suppliers to provide additional support to apprentices participating on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme.  Separately, the Disability Access to Education and Employment programme (DARE) is a strand of the Department’s Step Up project which offers support to those with a disability to progress into work.  It also encourages employers to review their policies to ensure that these are effective in recruiting people with disabilities.  Within Further Education Colleges the Additional Support Fund helps provide the necessary additional technical and/or personal support required to allow students with learning difficulties or disabilities to participate in mainstream Further Education courses or in discrete programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the difficulties being faced by farmers in relation to the Northern Ireland Food Animal Information System; and (ii) what steps he will take to improve this.
answer_text: The latest stage of NIFAIS went live on 4th September 2023, for bovine functionality. Alongside this software release DAERA introduced changes around how access to individual herds was managed. This caused frustration for some farmers and agents who could previously access a specific herd but now could not. This change was implemented to comply with GDPR requirements and was not linked to NIFAIS software. Communications have been issued to advise farmers, and agents, how access to herd information online can be granted.Whilst NIFAIS did initially have some performance issues the main cause of these issues was identified as an NICS infrastructure issue and was fixed.On a weekly basis, approximately 8500 bovine animals move to markets and 9500 bovine animals move to abattoirs across Northern Ireland. All these moves are created or recorded on NIFAIS which operates continually on a 24/7 basis.My NIFAIS team is in constant contact with all external stakeholder groups to make system improvements where required. Farmers having issues with NIFAIS know to contact their local DAERA Direct Office, where help will be provided, or the issue will be escalated to the NIFAIS team if necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is any proposal to review the process in cases where there is a lengthy ongoing planning application, with multiple periodic changes involving notifications to neighbours and objectors, who must then repeatedly respond.
answer_text: The two-tier planning system established in 2015 transferred responsibility for processing the majority of planning applications to local government. Although my department has an oversight role in respect of the planning system and reports on system activity and performance, it does not interfere with councils’ rights and responsibilities in terms of the processing of planning applications.  Applicants often seek to amend development proposals prior to their determination, and it is for planning authorities to consider if such changes can be accommodated.  Equally, the Planning Act requires that in determining any application for planning permission a council or the Department must take into account representations relating to that application which are received by it. I remain committed to ensuring that our planning system continues to work in an efficient and effective way and intend to take some time to review the progress that has been made to date. Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what consideration has been given to setting out a three year budget from 2024; and (ii) to detail any issues preventing this.
answer_text: It is not possible to set a three year budget because 2024-25 is the last year of the current Spending Review period. The Executive does not have a budget allocation beyond this period.In these circumstances, setting a multi-year budget would not comply with Section 64 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 which states that:1A - At least 14 days before laying a draft budget for a financial year, the Minister of Finance and Personnel must lay before the Assembly a statement specifying the amount of UK funding for that year notified to the Minister by the Secretary of State.1B - At the same time as laying a draft budget for a financial year, the Minister of Finance and Personnel must lay before the Assembly a statement showing that the amount of UK funding required by the draft budget does not exceed the amount specified under subsection (1A) for that year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the number of dog attacks on sheep in each of the last five years; and (ii) what steps he will take to prevent these.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the data you have asked for, this information is held by local councils. The request has therefore been passed to them to provide a response to you directly. The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 provides local councils with significant powers to investigate and prosecute all incidents of dog attacks, My Department has also published guidance for dog owners and landowners in relation to livestock worrying on the DAERA website and it continues to take every opportunity to stress the need for owners to exert proper control over their dogs.I take the matter of livestock worrying very seriously and my Department encourages farmers to report all incidences of livestock worrying to their local council dog wardens who are responsible for investigating them.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assistance his Department provides to groups supporting those suffering from endometriosis.
answer_text: Covid, and general overarching pressures within the Gynaecology Service in Northern Ireland have delayed the development of Endometriosis Services. As you may be aware, my Department recently launched the Women’s Health Strategy for Northern Ireland and we have also published the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology – this signals our commitment to working towards improving services for women which includes those suffering from endometriosis.The GIRFT  report has identified 20 general recommendations to improve services through coordinated regional action, as well as a list of specific recommendations for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to take forward. These recommendations have been accepted in principle with a view to implementing the required improvements as soon as it is feasible; however, it is acknowledged that some recommendations may require additional funding and lead-in time to deliver fully.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what financial support his Department can provide to establish multi-use games areas in the north coast area, including, Castlerock, Portrush, Limavady and Dungiven.
answer_text: My Department has previously provided approximately £144,000 part-funding to Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council as part of the COVID Recovery Small Settlement Scheme. This funding was used to establish a number of multi-use games areas in the North Coast villages of Dernaflaw, Dervock and Magilligan and are expected to be completed within the coming months. This initiative was developed in collaboration with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department for Infrastructure, along with local councils including Causeway Coast and Glens, to provide a Recovery Plan designed to support smaller settlement, with populations under 5000 to recover from the impact of the pandemic and address regeneration needs.At this time there no current programmes within my Department to provide support to establish further multi-use games areas in the north coast area.Any support which may be available from Sport NI, my Department’s arm’s length body which provides support for the sports sector, would be an operational matter.  As such, I would advise that you should seek this information directly from Sport NI and direct your question to Richard Archibald, Interim Chief Executive Officer at assemblybusiness@sport.net.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the impact of the Electronic Travel Authorisation on tourism to Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail any steps he has taken to mitigate any issues regarding this.
answer_text: I have strong concerns around the British Government’s introduction of its Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme and its potential impact as a disincentive to tourists.  I’m aware these concerns are shared by many within our local tourism industry, including businesses on the North Coast.Tourism is a key priority for me, and I will meet with the NI Tourism Alliance in the very near future to discuss this vital issue and to hear at first hand the industry’s concerns.  Following these discussions, I will lobby hard with the British Home Office Minister for Legal Migration to raise my strong concerns and urge consideration of an ETA exemption package of short-term mitigations for tourists travelling to the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the steps she is taking to ensure disabled access in all government buildings.
answer_text: My Department is committed to equality and ensuring our government offices are accessible.All newly constructed offices and major refurbishments on the DoF Office Estate comply with access requirements in the Building Regulations. The design of the office will also consider the recommendations contained in the 2018 BS 8300 ‘Design of an accessible and inclusive built environment Part 2: Buildings’ Code of Practice, whenever feasible. All existing buildings are as accessible as is practicable, given our Estate includes some listed and heritage buildings but, as a minimum, comply with the requirements of the Disability Discrimination (Providers of Services) (Adjustment of Premises) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003, to ensure that our staff and visitors, including those with disabilities, can access and work safely in our buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what discussions he has had with Tourism NI and Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council regarding proposals for a new world-class golf course being developed at Magilligan and a museum of US presidents with local links; (ii) how his Department can be involved in the creation of these; and (iii) for his assessment of how this could positively impact the tourism in the north coast.
answer_text: To date there have been no formal discussions with Tourism NI (TNI) and the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council on these specific proposals. The proposals which have been presented at this stage are still in the exploratory stage and no detailed assessment has been carried out on either project. It should be noted that whilst there was a golf course on Magilligan Point a little over 100 years ago, the land is a protected nature reserve today and the development of the site into a golf course will need to be include an investigation into the environmental protections currently in place to determine whether any development is possible. TNI will be happy to work with the Council and other stakeholders to establish the viability of these proposals as they develop. No formal feasibility study has been conducted into the economic value of a new championship links course, however due to the continued demand for links golf in the North the economic value has the potential to be substantial. It would be advisable that careful thought is given to any museum development as very few museums operate without substantial public sector subvention and the project sponsors would want to be assured that such a proposal is commercially viable in the longer term before any commitment is made to such a development.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on a redress scheme for victims and survivors of historical institutional abuse.
answer_text: As of 31 January 2024, the Redress Board has received 4,283 applications, of which 9 remain non-compliant or incomplete.  Redress Board panels have considered 4,211 applications making award determinations totalling £83,656,250, with £81,042,472 having been paid directly into the applicant or applicant’s solicitor’s account.  As of 31 January 2024, 63 compliant applications remained to be considered by a panel. Electronic versions of each of the three Board Annual Reports are available via the Assembly Library.   The Redress Scheme is due to close to applications in April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) which roads in the East Londonderry constituency will benefit from the (a) £1 million funding for potholes; and (b) £8.1 million for road maintenance; and (ii) the funding that will be allocated to the East Londonderry constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not operate within a constituency basis, rather road maintenance section office boundaries mirror local council boundaries.  Both the additional £1m of funding for potholes and the further £8.1m of funding for structural maintenance have been allocated to each of the four roads divisions. A proportion of the funding for potholes will now be used in the Causeway Coast and Glens Council area, with plans already in place for work on the Frosses Road.The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so a full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the postnatal mental health resources available for new fathers.
answer_text: It is recognised that the postnatal period may be a time of considerable psychological and social stress for the baby’s father and this may contribute to depressive symptoms developing. My Department along with the Public Health Agency established Community Perinatal Mental Health services across all HSC Trusts in June 2023.  While this service is for mothers, our teams can offer support and psychoeducation to their partners and direct them to appropriate services to seek help. There are a range of services available for men within general mental health services and within the community and voluntary sector. As Community Perinatal Mental Health teams grow and evolve, it may be possible that they are able to offer more by the way of support and intervention to the partners of the mothers they are seeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what plans he has to progress a sheep scheme.
answer_text: In January 2023, the Sheep Industry Taskforce produced its report to identify the needs of the sheep sector in Northern Ireland and a proposed package to encourage its development.  I look forward to receiving a briefing on this report to better understand the strategic needs of the sector and will consider deploying levers to meet those needs. Any decision to introduce new policies or programmes will be in the context of budget availability within the ring-fenced farm support funding envelope.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the number of people being forced from their houses due to paramilitary intimidation is determined.
answer_text: The Department for Communities does not determine the number of people who may have left their homes because of paramilitary intimidation. The Housing Executive will hold information for homeless presenters to it and acceptances due to intimidation, with a sub-category of ‘Intimidation – Paramilitary’. This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of regulations covering the beauty treatment sector; and (ii) to detail any steps he is taking to improve regulations, in particular those involving invasive procedures such as injections.
answer_text: The risk to public safety posed by the unlawful distribution and misuse of unauthorised medicinal products within the non-surgical cosmetic sector in Northern Ireland is a serious issue. Such breaches relating to Northern Ireland can be referred to my Department for investigation.My Department continues to take decisive action to combat illegal promotion, supply or misuse of medicines and to alerting the public about the dangers of using unauthorised medicines outside of the regulated supply chain. We remain vigilant and continue to work with key partner agencies to monitor the illicit marketplace and will continue to take effective action where necessary to protect public safety in Northern Ireland.My Department is aware of the wider, changing health and social care service delivery environment, including in relation to cosmetic treatments. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required, including consultation on the draft policy.  The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to recent commitments to building the economy in the north west, to detail his plans to grow the economy in areas beyond Londonderry city, particularly in towns and areas in East Londonderry.
answer_text: Regional economic balance is one of my key objectives and I recognise that building the economy is critical for all areas of the North-West.  My recent announcement of just over £1m for Derry City airport and future investment through the Causeway Coast & Glens Growth Deal will all play an important part in delivering regional balance. Work is also underway with all Councils to identify their economic priorities and to co-design targets in the Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a timeline on the delivery of funds to departments to resolve ongoing pay disputes.
answer_text: At its meeting on 15 February 2024, the Executive agreed the final departmental funding envelopes for the 2023-24 financial year, utilising the additional £1046.5 million from the financial package.It is now for individual Ministers to manage pay awards within the funding envelope provided to their department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail his plans to resolve the funding deficit that exists for community pharmacies, including reviewing the current drug tariff and clawback model; and (ii) for his assessment of the current operating conditions for pharmacies, given that many are operating at a loss, are having to use their own personal savings and are unable to employ and train staff.
answer_text: I very much appreciate the extremely important work that community pharmacies deliver for our population.My Department undertook a consultation seeking to improve the Drug Tariff arrangements in April 2022 and work is currently in train to take forward the first element of reform involving discount clawbacks.In light of representations from the sector, I held a meeting with representatives of CPNI and I have asked that further work is undertaken to review financial arrangements which includes elements of the Drug Tariff.  I encouraged CPNI to continue to work with Department officials to seek ways to ensure the pharmacy service can develop in line with our shared strategic objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of coastal flooding modelling, such as Climate Central’s Coastal Risk Screening Tool, and its identification of areas of the north coast that are likely to become vulnerable to flooding within the next decade; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to explore options for coastal flooding defences in the identified areas.
answer_text: My Department has published flood maps for all of the North.  These maps identify areas which may be vulnerable to both present day and projected climate change impacts associated with river, surface and coastal flooding. The maps are available to view at: Flood Maps NI | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).Climate Central is an American independent group that use their own sources and methods to apply climate change scenarios to nations worldwide. The Department does not contribute to these models and cannot comment on the accuracy of the outputs. However, a regional Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP) for 2021 – 2027 has been developed by my Department. The development of this plan has, utilised industry accepted flood modelling techniques, and identified areas of existing and potential flood risk, including area subject to coastal flood risk. The FRMP is readily available on the Department’s website at: 2nd Cycle - Flood Risk Management Plan 2021-2027 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).The FRMP includes a range of interventions to manage flood risk from a range of sources. As part of this work my Department is currently progressing various detailed flood studies and is also currently progressing the Belfast Tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme (BTFAS). The scheme aims to provide a long-term approach to tidal flood risk management for the City. The scheme takes into account the latest projections on climate change and also allows for future adaption to mitigate against potential sea level rises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any work being planned (i) by his Department; and (ii) on a cross-departmental basis, to attract more people into town centres, particularly in the East Londonderry constituency.
answer_text: Sub-regional economic development, including addressing regional imbalances, is one of my core priorities.  I will work with a range of local partners including local enterprise agencies, local businesses, the community and voluntary sector, Invest NI and councils, who have a lead role in progressing and promoting local economic development policies to improve town centres.  I also intend to work with Executive colleagues on taking forward the High Street Task Force recommendations to benefit town centres. This will necessarily require agreement from other Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of private residential properties and units that were not inhabited in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency; and (ii) the proportion of these properties that were in an uninhabitable condition.
answer_text: ANSWER	Details on properties not inhabited is not recorded within the Land & Property Services rating system. However, the table below sets out the number of Housing Stock* properties recorded as vacant within the rating system, by District Council in each of the last five years.  		As rates are payable on all domestic properties irrespective of whether the property is occupied or vacant, information provided may not reflect the actual number of vacant properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of times Article 6 of The Pollution Control and Local Government (NI) Order 1978 has been used by local councils, in each of the past 10 years, to force owners to make improvements to a dilapidated property; (ii) the council areas this has been done; and (iii) the efforts his Department has, and will, make to encourage councils to use the powers they have in this regard, particularly in reference to increased insurance premiums, reduced property value and anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information, and I understand that the relevant legislation falls under the auspices of DAERA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the efficacy of laws which give powers to local councils to oblige owners to better maintain their properties when they fall into disrepair, dereliction or ruin; (ii) to detail the most recent changes to laws of this kind; and (iii) to detail any plans he has to update these laws.
answer_text: I am aware that dilapidated properties can have a detrimental impact on the lives of people who live in adjoining properties and also on the amenities of neighbourhoods.  There is a range of existing legislation across a number of Departments that is available to district councils to deal with dilapidation and to evaluate its effectiveness. The Planning Act (NI) 2011 contains a number of provisions that provide councils with the powers to issue a “completion notice” to require a development which has a time bound planning permission, and which has been commenced, to be completed within a time limit of at least 12 months; to issue an order requiring a particular land use to stop or require the alteration or removal of any building or works in the interests of proper planning or amenity; and to serve an ‘Urgent Works Notice’ (UWN), where it appears to a council that works are urgently necessary for the preservation of a listed building (or building in a Conservation Area).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for her assessment of the number of private properties that are currently uninhabitable due to dereliction and neglect; and (ii) to detail any (a) cross-departmental work; and (b) work with the private sector, she has planned to bring these properties back into use.
answer_text: ANSWER	Property that is uninhabitable because it is derelict does not have a rateable value, and Land & Property Services (LPS) does not hold information for such property. Property that is uninhabitable due to neglect may still attract a rateable value because the property is capable of repair, however LPS does not hold details in its systems to identify all such property.		I have no plans at this time. Housing strategy is led by the Department for Communities and I look forward to working with that Department within the context of my Ministerial portfolio.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the obligations, set out in the recent deal with the UK government, on the Executive to raise revenue to help pay for public services
answer_text: ANSWERThe financial package which accompanied the return of the Executive, while welcome, does not put the Executive’s finances on a sustainable footing. The Executive is committed to delivering sustainable public services.
