A pre-requisite of that must be that the right level of funding is provided.I have a number of significant concerns in relation to the financial package, not least the conditions attached to the write -off of the £559 million provided in the package for ‘debts’.My view is that these ‘debts’ only arose due to the underfunding of our public services. To make this funding repayable unless the Executive raises a £113 million through locally generated revenue in 2024-25 is unacceptable. I am meeting the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 28 February to discuss my concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the anticipated timeline of the offer of a pay deal to transport workers, with a view to settling the ongoing dispute and preventing further industrial action.
answer_text: Following the Executive’s agreement to allocate the Department additional resource budget of £87.8m, an additional budget allocation was provided to Translink to enable it to negotiate a 2023-24 pay award with trade unions.  The unions are currently balloting their members and I await an announcement of the result.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his plans to facilitate the wider roll-out of stereotactic radiotherapy for cancer patients; (ii) the number of patients who would benefit from this treatment; and (iii) the number of patients that can be offered this treatment option.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the potential to grow Northern Ireland’s adventure tourism sector; and (ii) any plans he has to create a specific body that oversees and develops this sector.
answer_text: I am considering these matters as part of the tourism strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on any work that is (i) underway; and (ii) planned, to explore Northern Ireland’s potential for geothermal energy production.
answer_text: Geothermal energy is widely used in Europe and globally, however, there are very few installations in the north.  The Energy Strategy recognised the potential for geothermal energy to support the Energy Strategy’s vision of net zero and affordable energy and the 2022 action plan committed to commencing a geothermal demonstration project.In February 2023, the Department commenced delivery of the geothermal demonstration project “GeoEnergy NI”.  The project is aimed at encouraging demand for and unlocking investment in geothermal technology.  The project comprises two geothermal exploratory and feasibility studies which will help unearth the heat beneath our feet.  It is anticipated that the project will be delivered incrementally in the coming years with current feasibility studies due to be completed in early 2025.The project is supported by a communications and marketing strategy aimed at engaging and educating the public and more information can be found at: GeoEnergy NI - Unearthing The Heat Beneath Our Feet.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what plans he has to reduce the time taken to process planning applications for renewable energy projects, for instance onshore windfarms; and (ii) how this may be achieved.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of facilitating the drive to meet the targets for renewable energy set out in the Energy Strategy and the Climate Change Act and believe that a continuing engagement is important with the renewables sector.I am also committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way.  I intend to take some time to review the progress that has been made to date and will continue to work closely with key stakeholders to collectively improve processes and increase the efficiency of the system.  Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the prevalence of mother and baby car parking spaces in public car parks; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to encourage an increase in this number.
answer_text: The management of the majority of publicly provided off-street carparks transferred from the Department to local Councils on 1 April 2015.  The provision of dedicated parking bays for specific purposes is therefore a matter for the local Council which operates the facility.The off-street parking places operated by the Department are “park and ride” or “park and share”.Park &Ride  sites are primarily a facility to encourage and promote modal shift by utilising public transport as an alternative to the private car.  Park & Share sites are primarily directed at commuters and are generally located at key interchange junctions to provide an off-road facility for sharing of car borne journeys to a common destination.Parent and Baby spaces are mostly found in 'attractor' car parks, particularly supermarkets. The wider bays are designed to give parents enough space to manoeuvre their young children in and out of their cars, especially when they have bulky carriers or pushchairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of whether the time and effort required to obtain a convenient and timely MOT appointment, represents an acceptable value for money service; (ii) to outline the steps he will take to (a) improve; and (b) expedite the MOT booking and testing process; and (iii) what consideration he has given to (a) temporarily extending the MOT exemption period for new cars; (b) establishing a separate, expedited process for car dealerships; and (c) creating a specific position or think-tank for resolving this issue.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the most recent recruitment competition launched on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.  The DVA also uses overtime to provide cover for leave and they offer vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.Whilst demand for vehicle testing services remains high, the DVA has taken a number of steps to improve the customer experience.  For example, in September 2021 the DVA implemented a new online booking system to replace the previous booking system, which was 20 years old. The new system has simplified the MOT and driving test application process through the introduction of easy-to-use screens for customers, with clear guidance on how to complete the booking process.  The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at  Change, cancel or view your MOT/ vehicle test appointment online | nidirectTo further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.The circumstances that provided for the issue of Temporary Exemption Certificates (TECs) have significantly changed, along with the return of normal testing services following the pandemic.  It is, therefore, important from a road safety perspective, that all vehicles are tested at the ear

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of how drug (a) availability; (b) misuse; and (c) addiction is being tackled in HMP Magilligan; and (ii) to detail what further steps she will take to reduce the number of inmates who are able to (a) maintain; or (b) develop a drug habit while in HMP Magilligan.
answer_text: Many people coming into prison have existing issues regarding addiction and display drug-seeking behaviours. Magilligan disrupts the availability of illegal drugs into and within the prison using X-Ray body scanners and other search aids to thwart efforts to traffic prohibited items.  Healthcare in Prisons teams also work to support those prisoners with drug addictions.Key to this are partnerships with North West Regional College, South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, AD:EPT (Alcohol and Drugs: Empowering People through Therapy) and other stakeholders. This integrated approach has been designed to boost purposeful activity, maintain family links and empower individuals’ self-esteem to break the cycle of substance misuse. Positive results have already been evidenced from these interventions.The recent Independent Review of Progress at Magilligan Prison noted that a revised Drug and Alcohol Strategy had been introduced in September 2023. This Strategy is now embedded and a plan is in place to restrict supply, reduce demand and build recovery.The Northern Ireland Prison Service also continues to work closely with the Police Service of Northern Ireland to tackle the flow of drugs into prisons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what areas of healthcare could be treated in the community through pharmacies, opticians, dentists and other professional settings; and (ii) what plans he has to facilitate this.
answer_text: Western Urgent Care (WUC) is commissioned to provide the GP Out of Hours (OOHs) service across the Western area.  The GP OOHs service is for people who need urgent medical treatment but cannot wait until their doctor's practice reopens. The local population is encouraged to utilise the service appropriately to meet any urgent health care needs which cannot wait to be seen by their registered practice when it reopens.  The GP OOHs service is available Mon-Fri from 6pm to 8am the next morning, as well as 24 hours at weekends and during public holidays.  All Patients can contact WUC via a single telephone number, there are no telephone numbers available to contact bases directly.  Patients who contact the service will be triaged by a telephone or video call by clinical staff who could be located in any one of the service’s bases in Derry/ Londonderry, Enniskillen, Limavady, Strabane or Omagh.  Many patients can be safely and effectively clinically triaged by either a Nurse Advisor, Prescribing Pharmacist, an Advanced Nurse Practitioners (ANPs) or GP.  The majority of patients who contact WUC OOHs service do not need to be seen face-to-face.  From Apr-Dec 2023, 71% of WUC OOHs patient contacts were given advice by telephone or video call, 25% of patient contacts required a base visit and 4% of contacts required a home visit. The GP OOH service provided by WUC faces considerable challenges in consistently populating the GP rota, despite best efforts.  Reasons for this include the fact that GPs are not contractually obliged to work in GP OOH; shifting attitudes in relation to achieving work life balance and preparedness or willingness of doctors to cover out of hours’ sessions; and busier and more complex day time general practice reducing the inclination or ability of GPs to work additional out of hours sessions.  When GPs book to work in out of hours clinics, the majority prefer to work within their local area - close to their own practices and homes.  The majority of clinical staff are retained by WUC on an ‘as and when required’ basis and for the reasons outlined above DoH does encourage the development of a multidisciplinary workforce.  Provision of a multidisciplinary workforce allows WUC to meet patient needs and make effective use of the available clinical resource.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail his plans to reimplement the Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service Plus scheme; and (ii) for his assessment of how this scheme is able to reduce waiting lists and times for treating eye conditions.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service Plus (NI PEARS Plus) pilot commenced in 2023/24 and ran for thirty-three weeks, with 834 patients accessing the community optometry-based service with acute eye conditions which would otherwise have presented at Belfast HSC Trust Eye Casualty. NI PEARS Plus seeks to build on the successful NI PEARS scheme which currently manages ca. 4,000 patients in community optometry settings, closer to home.As per commissioning specification, the pilot is currently being evaluated for impact, assessing metrics around activity, performance, clinical safety, patient experience and cost-effectiveness.i) Following evaluation, and in consultation with stakeholders, Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) will make a commissioning decision on whether the service should be mainstreamed and introduced regionally, subject to available funding.ii) From the initial data analysis approx. 25 patients per week were diverted from BHSCT Eye Casualty to the NIPEARS Plus service thus releasing capacity in Eye Casualty. Approximately 93% of these patients were managed to completion in primary care with approx. 7% subsequently requiring referral back into Eye Casualty as deemed too complex to be managed in primary care. These figures are subject to validation, and the management and outcomes audited for quality and safety. Any subsequent regional roll-out would be expected to have scaled positive impact on waiting lists and times for acute eye care.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) their plans to implement a loneliness strategy; (ii) their assessment of the scale and nature of loneliness in Northern Ireland; and (iii) the anticipated cross-departmental work and responsibility that will be required in the formation and roll-out of such a strategy.
answer_text: At this time we have no plans for the Executive Office to develop and implement a cross-departmental loneliness strategy. No assessment has therefore been made of the resources likely to be required to do so.In relation to The Executive Office, we are particularly aware of how issues such as social isolation and loneliness impact victims and survivors. Befriending services for victims and survivors are supported through the Victims and Survivors Service’s Health and Wellbeing Casework Network, and we are committed to improving access to services for rural and socially isolated victims and survivors through the new Victims and Survivors Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what (i) support mechanisms; and (ii) legal safeguards are (a) in place; (b) planned, to help those who find themselves in debt to criminal gangs and are forced to undertake criminal activity on their behalf.
answer_text: Addressing the harm that criminal gangs inflict on individuals, communities and our whole society is a key focus for my Department. My earlier response to AQW 6162/22-27 referred to the work of the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) which supports those who are vulnerable to all types of such harm. EPPOC data has been instrumental in highlighting previously ‘hidden harms’ such as illegal money lending and coercive control. I also mentioned EPPOC’s ‘Ending the Harm’ campaign (www.endingtheharm.com) which, as well raising awareness, signposts support for those who have become victims. Some examples of EPPOC-funded projects that support those who may find themselves in debt to criminal gangs are:The Mid and East Antrim youth support project which provides multi-agency support for young people (up to 25 years) who are at risk of being involved with, influenced by, or exploited by paramilitary gangs. ‘Engage’ which is an Education Authority-led intensive, specialist outreach and mentoring project working with young people within communities at risk of being harmed by paramilitaries.'Aspire' which is Probation Board-led project targeting marginalised men who are most susceptible to paramilitary/criminal influence to help them develop alternate coping mechanisms and increase their resilience.A specific project that works with victims to provide trauma or counselling support, including with issues such as drug debt.In addition, in 2024/25 financial year, EPPOC will commence new scoping projects to address areas of concern identified by the programme. This includes work with colleagues in the Department of Health to identify a service response to the issue of drug-related intimidation. Learning from other jurisdictions will be considered in the Northern Ireland context to recommend a comprehensive and appropriate way forward. A similar approach will be taken for a project that looks across the system at how threats to life are currently dealt with, working closely with the range of relevant partners.The current phase of EPPOC is due to conclude in March 2025. In due course, consideration will be given across the Executive as to next steps, ensuring that the lessons learned through the programme are embedded and scaled up.In Northern Ireland the Child Services Cooperation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 requires statutory agencies to share information in safeguarding cases with the aim of improving outcomes for individuals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the mechanism by which recipients of housing assistance will be protected from the planned 7.7 per cent rise in social housing rent in 2024-25.
answer_text: Housing assistance is provided through Housing Benefit and Universal Credit.  From April 2023, most social security benefits, including personal allowances and premia in Housing Benefit, were increased by 10.1%.  From April 2024 they will be increased by a further 6.7%.  Additional support is available through Social Sector Size Criteria Welfare Supplementary Payments and the Discretionary Housing Payment Scheme.  Collectively these measures will help protect recipients of housing assistance from the planned rise in social housing rent in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of (i) the number of vacant commercial properties in town centres across East Londonderry; (ii) the trends seen in this number, over the last five years; and (iii) the issues dissuading traders from (a) maintaining; and (b) establishing commercial premises in these centres; and (iv) what steps his Department has (a) taken; and (b) planned, to encourage and incentivise more businesses, particularly independent traders, into commercial premises in the constituency’s town centres.
answer_text: (i) the number of vacant commercial properties in town centres across East Londonderry; (ii) the trends seen in this number, over the last five years; I have consulted with colleagues in the Department of Finance and the Department for Communities and the number of vacant commercial properties in town centres is not recorded by constituency.(iii) the issues dissuading traders from (a) maintaining; and (b) establishing commercial premises in these centres; (iv) what steps his Department has (a) taken; and (b) planned, to encourage and incentivise more businesses, particularly independent traders, into commercial premises in the constituency’s town centres.While development of policies to improve town centres and draw in more traders is primarily a matter for the relevant district council to take forward, the Department for Communities also has responsibility for regeneration of towns and I understand it has undertaken work in this constituency in recent years. I intend to work with Executive colleagues to consider the recommendations of the High Street Task Force report to address some of the issues facing high street businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what role his Department plays, or can play, in (i) preventing livestock thefts; and (ii) assisting with subsequent prosecutions, particularly in East Londonderry.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is responsible for enforcing legislation in relation to the theft of livestock.While there is a specific legal obligation to notify my Department of the theft of cattle, and the circumstances around this theft, this is for the purpose of ensuring our identification and registration records are up to date.  My Department has a strong working relationship with the PSNI and will cooperate with the them in respect of any relevant investigations of livestock theft, as necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the increasing cost of household goods, what steps he will take to support those reliant on (i) benefits; (ii) state pensions; and (iii) government and community support schemes to help ease pressures during the current cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has been at the forefront of delivering support to households impacted by the rising cost of living.  In response to the cost-of-living and energy crises, the UK Government announced a package of one-off payments for disabled claimants, pensioners and those on income-related benefits. Northern Ireland claimants are included in these payments which are intended to provide additional financial support in response to the rise in the cost of living. The most recent Cost of Living Payments have been delivered throughout 2023/24 with a final payment of £299 to eligible people in receipt of an income-related benefit to be paid in February 2024. Further information on all cost of living help and support available, including details of cost of living support payments issued, is available at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/cost-livingFrom April 2023, benefits and state pension were increased by 10.1%, in line with the September 2022 rate of inflation. From April 2024 benefits will be increased by 6.7%, in line with the September 2023 rate of inflation, whilst the state pension will be increased by 8.5% in line with the increase in average earnings between May and July 2023.My Department also provides help through the Affordable Warmth scheme, as well as the Winter Fuel Payments to those who are eligible. Funding has been allocated to our Social Supermarket Scheme to help those in or at risk of food poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the increasing cost of household goods, to detail any correspondence he has had, or plans to have, with large supermarkets and retailers, with a view to ensuring consumers get the best possible prices to help ease pressures during the current cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My Department’s Trading Standards Service has been focusing its enforcement efforts on price transparency and ensuring consumers get what they pay for, especially on essential items. The Consumer Council, a Partner Organisation of my Department, engages with key stakeholders and representative bodies and shares its research and insights relating to the impact of rising food prices for consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she plans to take in relation to (i) the prevention of livestock thefts; and (ii) subsequent prosecutions in (a) East Londonderry; and (b) Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is a member of the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP), along with the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DEARA) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). The partnership shares information on emerging or reoccurring crimes, and will continue to do so, to develop a coordinated response and take preventive action to issues such as livestock theft. There has been recent engagement between PSNI and relevant partners on this issue. The policing response however is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of (i) the importance of north coast tourism in helping to achieve Tourism NI’s target of an NI-wide 75 per cent increase in tourism revenue by 2033; (ii) the factors which may negatively impact north coast tourism, including (a) a recurrence of toxic algae blooms; (b) the cost of living crisis; and (c) controls on tourists entering Northern Ireland from the Republic; and (iii) what his Department must do to (a) mitigate these and any other barriers to north coast tourism; and (b) to further develop and increase it.
answer_text: My Department’s draft Tourism Strategy aims to increase the value of tourism to our local economy by 50-75% over the next decade and to support the tourism industry to realise its potential for the future. As an important part of our regional tourism offering, not least due to its iconic experiences and heritage sites, north coast tourism will be an important contributor to that strategic aim.Tourism NI (TNI) acknowledges the vital role of tourism on the North Coast in fulfilling its objectives of achieving a 75% increase in tourism revenue across Northern Ireland by 2033.  By fostering strong partnerships with all eleven local authorities across the North including Causeway Coast and Glens Council (CC&GC), TNI convene regular meetings to address priorities aimed at enriching the tourism sector throughout the North Coast region. The Causeway Coast and Glens (CC&G) has the second largest number of all overnight trips (after Belfast) and the highest proportion of overnight holiday trips in all Council areas.   ??TNI will once again work closely with key partners to plan for and support delivery of the 153rd Open Championship at Royal Portrush in 2025, including engagement with the Council and the business community to optimise the economic benefits of hosting such a prestigious tournament.  TNI are also working in partnership with Tourism Ireland and Fáilte Ireland to deliver a Shared Island programme to develop linkages between the Wild Atlantic Way (WAW) and Causeway Coastal Route (CCR) This work will be carried out in 2024 and all key stakeholders will be engaged before recommendations are supplied. There are significant opportunities that exist around CC&G’s role as part of the £72m Causeway Coast & Glens Regional Growth Deal, meaning that further investment in the area as an already successful tourism destination is being considered.  All of these initiatives will further develop the north coast tourism offering and help achieve the 75% target increase in tourism revenue.  No doubt factors outside the control of my Department including the cost of living crisis and the recurrence of toxic algae blooms may well have some impact on tourism throughout the North including the north coast but I will be working alongside my Executive colleagues to ensure these impacts are mitigated in a proportionate way.I am concerned about the impact of the introduction of the British Government's Electronic Travel Authorisation Scheme on our tourism sector. I have met with the NI Tourism Alliance to discuss this vital issue and to hear at first hand the industry’s concerns.  I intend to raise this matter with the Home Office to try to find a solution that protects our tourism industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the number of pupils with special educational needs increasing by 5,000 between 2019 and 2024.
answer_text: School census data indicates that the number of pupils with statements of special educational needs (SEN) has increased from 19,208 in 2019/20 to 24,283 in 2022/23, a rise of around 5,000.It is important to understand causes and links to inform and shape future policy decisions.  In the absence of epidemiological studies, increased trends in SEN are not unique to Northern Ireland.  However, the challenge is more acute as evidenced by the Independent Review of Education (December 2023), which pointed to the proportion of pupils with a statement of SEN in Northern Ireland being 2.5% higher by comparison to England with an estimated additional cost that must be found from within the overall education budget of approximately £136m.Research has considered possible causal factors behind the rising numbers of children with SEN and it is clear that education alone cannot address all of the issues.  In addition to advances in medical science, evidence would suggest that there is a range of other relevant contributory factors unique to our circumstances.  The ongoing legacy of the troubles can manifest itself through collective community trauma which sees those areas with high levels of poverty, disadvantage, poor health, low educational attainment and high unemployment moderately correlating with high levels of SEN and complex needs.  Importantly, research points to weaknesses in the current system as being key contributory factors to rising needs, some of which are a consequence of the under-investment in education. The lack of an effective model of early intervention that meets presenting needs at the right time may lead to more complex levels of need that often require costlier interventions.   The school estate also fails to provide suitable learning environments for children whose needs are such that they require appropriate breakout space to enable them to self-regulate or to learn in smaller class settings. The End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN and associated workstreams have been established to ensure that the systemic reform needed to provide quality outcomes for our children and young people with SEN is implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has to introduce pre-recorded evidence gathering from victims, to prevent repeated questioning and re-traumatisation.
answer_text: My Department is leading multi-agency preparatory work to support a pilot project to test pre-recorded cross-examination in serious sexual offence cases involving very young complainants.  There are interdependencies with other child focused workstreams and work is ongoing to resolve outstanding issues, however, significant progress has been made.  Launch of the pathfinder project will be subject to the agreement of partner organisations and to securing funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Affairs Committee’s report The Effect of Paramilitary Activity and Organised Crime on Society in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how he will work with the Minister of Justice to protect people and communities from becoming vulnerable to, and victims of, criminal gangs.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Affairs Committee report outlines the complexity of the challenges we face and how important a co-ordinated approach across government is to addressing the effects paramilitary activity and organised crime have on vulnerable individuals, communities and wider society.  The recommendations in the report highlight issues such as illegal lending, the targeting and exploitation of vulnerable individuals, including those in financial hardship, and notes the correlation between paramilitary activity, deprivation, and mental health issues.  I agree that the approach to tackling paramilitarism and organised crime must be co-ordinated and has to involve all the relevant Government Departments, statutory bodies, local community organisations and wider society.With regard to working with the Minister of Justice I can confirm that my officials will continue to work closely with their colleagues in the Department of Justice to deliver elements of the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC).  This includes working with other Government Departments, the PSNI, voluntary and community sector organisations, sporting and cultural bodies and local groups to increase awareness, build confidence and develop skills that create greater community resilience and confidence. In addition, my Department will continue to support vulnerable individuals and communities through a range of programmes and services including the Neighbourhood Renewal programme, advice on debt management, funding for emergency food provision, training and employment support.  These interventions, and the ongoing support for community infrastructure, all make a positive contribution to the Executives long term goal of addressing the complex issues and challenges associated with protecting  vulnerable people and communities from paramilitarism, criminality and coercive control.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he is taking to minimise duplication of paper correspondence to members of the public, while ensuring an appropriate form of correspondence for those not comfortable using digital solutions.
answer_text: My Department works closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to continually develop and modernise our benefit systems to reduce clerical and paper based processes.  Recent examples of enhanced digitalisation in benefit administration include the new Get Your State Pension service and Universal Credit, where claimants can apply for benefit, maintain their claim and interact with staff online. Work continues to develop increasingly digital solutions to modernise many of its complex range of older benefit systems, which will further improve digitalisation and the potential to reduce incidences of duplicate paper correspondence. Claimants who are uncomfortable with, or not able to use, digital channels can continue to access support through our benefit specific telephony teams or in person at one of our 35 local Jobs and Benefit offices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the increasing number of people on NHS waiting lists who subsequently opt to pay privately for treatment.
answer_text: Waiting lists in Northern Ireland are wholly unacceptable.  I am aware that as a consequence of this, some patients opt to pay privately for their assessments and treatment rather than waiting. My Department does not hold the detail on the number of patients that this applies to.Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  The Elective Care Framework published in 2021 sets out a detailed plan for addressing hospital waiting lists. Much good work is underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and mega clinics.  In addition, my Department is working with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of best practice service improvements.  Tackling waiting lists is however undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the potential benefits of the pilot scheme to film the delivery of judgments in the Court of Appeal, both in this setting and any future use of filming in the wider courts system.
answer_text: The pilot scheme by the Lady Chief Justice for filming in courts represents an important step forward in opening up our courts and engendering a better understanding of the legal system.  Allowing broadcasting in courts would increase transparency and enable the public to view decisions being made by the judiciary. I look forward to seeing the evaluation of the pilot scheme and engaging with the Lady Chief Justice on any recommendations that might be brought forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what public health programmes and policies he will initiate to help people protect themselves from developing cancer.
answer_text: My Department leads on a number of strategies that are designed to help improve population health and wellbeing through encouraging people to live healthier lives and reduce the prevalence of cancer risk factors such as tobacco and alcohol consumption, obesity, nutrition and lack of physical activity, and ultraviolet radiation exposure.These strategies are implemented by the Public Health Agency through a number of supporting policies, public information campaigns, community pharmacies, as well as online resources and other specific services such as specialist stop smoking services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what plans he has to make the £1 million in funds previously unspent by Sport NI available to sporting clubs and organisations; and (ii) whether this money can be used to safeguard the short-term future of the North West 200.
answer_text: As it is not possible to carry monies from one financial year into the next, the Sport NI easements were used to address other Departmental pressures in the relevant financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans his Department has to (i) reinstate the Affordable Warmth, boiler replacement and other schemes; and (ii) create new schemes; (iii) change eligibility thresholds of existing schemes in order to reduce the impact of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The Affordable Warmth Scheme is currently open to applications and is set to run until March 2026. The Scheme continues to assist low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with an annual household income of less than £23,000. To make an application to the Affordable Warmth Scheme, customers can contact the NI Energy Advice Service via: 0800 1114455 or email: NIenergyadvice@nihe.gov.uk My Department is currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty intervention that will replace the current Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends. This new scheme will align with the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, Climate Change Act and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy. My officials are also exploring the option of low carbon heating solutions along with other energy efficiency measures and will consider the eligibility and income thresholds in the development of the new scheme. The consideration of new thresholds will form part of planned stakeholder engagement workshops.In September 2023 my Department announced the closure of the Boiler Replacement Scheme due to budgetary constraints. Prior to the budget decision, the Scheme had seen a significant decrease in both the number of enquiries and application forms received in the previous two financial years, which was further evident again this year.  Additionally, the current Scheme replaced fossil fuel heating systems without the option of more efficient, low carbon heating.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many people sought (a) treatment; and (b) support for eating disorders, in each of the past five years; (ii) for his assessment of how successfully eating disorders are being identified and treated by his Department; and (iii) to outline the (a) challenges involved; and (b) future steps that will need to be taken to reduce and eradicate eating disorders.
answer_text: how many people sought (a) treatment; and (b) support for eating disorders, in each of the past five years;Community Adult Mental Health Referrals Accepted by Eating Disorder Services by Fiscal Year and HSC Trust, from 2018/19 to 2023/24 year to date (January 2024)HCS Trust2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/2323/24 to January 2024Belfast and South Eastern 16215612313911491Northern10610478*105*198*98Southern133148140182124102Western121126123118119125NI Region522534464*544*555*416Sources: Monthly Non Inpatient Mental Health Returns from HSC Trusts*Proxy Figures have been included for the Northern Trust figures for Adult Eating Disorders as shown in red. This is due to limitations in data availability arising from Information System migration from EPEX to PARIS:2020/21 - Full Year effect based on data up to 24th February 2021 only.2021/22 - Proxy figures for full year based on average of 3 years data.2022/23 - Full year effect based on data for February and March 2023.Proxy figures are include in the regional totals. Child and Adolescent Mental Health (CAMHS) Referrals Accepted by Eating Disorder Services by Fiscal Year and HSC Trust, from 2018/19 to 2023/24 year to date (January 2024)Fiscal Year2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/2323/24 to January 2024Belfast and South Eastern 456799967337Northern406167745612Southern363453794316Western48454765388NI Region16920726631421073Sources: Monthly CAMHS MDS Returns from HSC TrustsGeneral Caveats /Notes - Please read1.The figures provided above are the numbers of referrals accepted into Eating Disorder Services for Adults and Children, for the fiscal years 2018/19 to 2023/24 year to date (January 2024).
