It is not possible to determine whether there are multiple referrals for individuals as the information submitted by HSC Trusts is in an aggregated format and cannot be disaggregated.3. Belfast Trust provides an Eating Disorder service for residents of the South Eastern Trust area.for his assessment of how successfully eating disorders are being identified and treated by his Department;There are currently four adult Eating Disorder Specialist Teams across the five Health and Social Care Trusts.  The Belfast Trust and South Eastern Trust is represented by one team.  These teams have developed community based psychological therapy services using evidence-based treatments as per National Institute for Clinical Excellence (NICE) guidance.  They work to the Regional Care Pathway for the Treatment of Eating Disorders (2016). The care pathway outlines what people can expect if they are referred for treatment and provides summary of clinical advice derived from evidence-based practice. It also includes information to assist people with self-care and accessing other supports before, during and after treatmentThe Regional Eating Disorder Network Group (REDNG) ensures lo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to reduce farm-related accidents, injuries and deaths.
answer_text: I am committed to ongoing engagement with colleagues in the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) who have lead responsibility for workplace Health and Safety, including on farms. 		Significant improvement in understanding of the factors associated with incidents on farms has developed in recent years and this has informed the work of the Farm Safety Partnership (FSP). The key aim of the partnership is for all stakeholders to work together to develop and deliver a ‘Farm Safety Action Plan’ that will drive improvement of safety standards on Northern Ireland farms. All partners have been working collaboratively to develop a new Action Plan that will span the years 2024 to 2026.My department contributes directly to the Farm Safety Action Plan across a number of themes, including the FSP’s 'Stop and Think SAFE' farm safety multi-media campaign, which was developed by the Partnership to help tackle the high rates of serious accidents and deaths on Northern Ireland's farms. This campaign draws attention to the four main causes of fatalities on our farms - Slurry, Animals, Falls (from height) and Equipment.DAERA also supports the HSENI Farm Safety calendar. The calendar features images from posters created by primary school children with the theme of ‘Avoid Harm on the Farm’. Over 43,000 calendars are produced annually and are distributed to all rural primary schools in Northern Ireland. The College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) ensures that farm safety is a key component of all agriculture education programmes that are delivered. For example, all new full-time, Further Education agriculture students participate in the ‘Yellow Wellies’ Farm Safety initiative which is delivered by the Farm Safety Foundation.  Health and safety and the requirement for students to carry out risk assessments is also integrated into the practical activities undertaken by students studying at all levels at the College. CAFRE also provide tractor driving courses to young people aged between 13 and 15 years old. This course has a particular emphasis on health and safety. Health and safety also features across CAFRE’s industry training provision. Farmers participating in Business Development Groups (BDG) and Farm Family Key Skills (FFKS) programmes receive regular opportunities to discuss health and safety issues at BDG meetings and during bespoke FFKS training events. DAERA has also recently commissioned a three-year research project to determine how behavioural and cultural change can be influenced to effect safety improvements in the NI farming sector. In conclusion, my department maintains a firm focus on reducing the incidence of injuries and fatalities on farms and we will continue to actively engage in the Farm Safety Partnership to improve health and safety outcomes for farmers, their families and their employees on all farms across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he is taking to ensure people making a claim for Universal Credit receive their full entitlement ahead of the benefit uprating in April.
answer_text: The next annual uprating of benefit will occur on 8 April 2024 and new rates of the various Universal Credit entitlements will be automatically applied by the UC computer system to monthly assessment periods that commence on or after this date. My Department is confident that all new eligible claims to Universal Credit with a monthly assessment period that ends in advance of 8 April 2024 will be paid in full and on time.  Thereby ensuring they will automatically receive the financial benefit of uprating on or after 8 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to reintroduce the Right to Buy scheme for residents of Housing Association properties.
answer_text: The Housing Amendment Act (NI) 2020 ended the House Sales Scheme for tenants of Registered Housing Associations. The Act made provision for Housing Associations to offer a voluntary House Sales Scheme to their tenants. This will be a decision for individual Housing Associations and their Boards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how health workers’ pay increases will be sustained in future years if money from the additional £3.3 billion deal recently agreed with the UK government is not replicated in future years.
answer_text: The Executive is united on the need to resolve public sector pay disputes and rebuild public services. To that end, we have jointly written to the Prime Minister to seek an improved financial settlement. I will continue to make the case for much-needed additional funding for health and social care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what steps he will take to ensure farmers who rear 100 per cent grass-fed cattle will not be disadvantaged by the lowering of the age at which cattle go to slaughter in the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme; and (ii) for his assessment of the potential and inadvertent risks, of raising the carbon footprint per kilo of beef, should more cattle farmers switch to feeding their animals a meal-based diet.
answer_text: The age at slaughter of finished beef cattle is strongly influenced by management decisions and the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme does not confer a specific disadvantage on any one system of production.  Evidence collated by my Department shows that 80% of animals are already slaughtered at or below the Year 1 target for the Scheme of 30 months.  The targets set in the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme by no measure represent or require an intensive production system.  Additional purchased feedstuffs can be avoided through better grassland management within a grass-based production system, as well as better attention to animal health and better genetics.  This will be the focus of the knowledge transfer programmes that my Department’s College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise, (CAFRE) will be providing, as well as other aspects of the Farm Support and Development Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his plans to ensure that everyone can join and access a local NHS dentist in a timely way; and (ii) whether he will put in place a revised contract with dentists to ensure it is financially viable for dentists to take on and treat new NHS patients.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I know that an additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.In relation to contract reform, I am conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK in recent months, with Scotland, England and Wales adopting diverging models of remuneration. I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. I also know that officials are liaising with their colleagues in the devolved governments to monitor the results of these models and the views of practitioners and patients, in order to inform decision making in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for reducing waiting times for hip replacement operations.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective care, including orthopaedics, is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need, such as hip replacements.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.In April 2022 my Department commissioned an independent external review of the regional elective orthopaedic service in Northern Ireland to identify proposals for the immediate recovery of the service. Since the completion of the review significant progress has been made across the region, with some Trusts now exceeding pre-Covid levels of activity.  For example, in Musgrave Park Hospital, more than 1,700 additional patients, over and above activity planned prior to the review, have been treated to date, and the newly refurbished Duke of Connaught Unit at the Hospital is now delivering high volume, low complexity treatments for patients, with over 1,700 procedures and 750 outpatient appointments delivered in the unit thus far. However, I accept that there is more to do and providing further improvement in orthopaedic waiting lists is important to improve the health outcomes of our population.I recognise that the transformation of HSC services alone is not enough to remove our unacceptably long waiting times. While it will help bridge the existing capacity / demand gap and in some specialities/procedures, may also reduce waiting lists, more needs to be done. Going forward, we therefore need to invest in additionality to reduce waits. Any future funding allocation to help reduce waiting lists in orthopaedics will, however, need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the impact on the economy due to parents being unable to work due to the absence of sufficient and affordable childcare; and (ii) the work of his Department in any expansion of free childcare.
answer_text: The Early Years and Childcare Strategy which is being developed by the Department of Education is an Executive priority.  I recognise that there is a need for high quality affordable childcare to enable parents to work and my department will support the development of this strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the impact of the cost of living crisis on students’ financial wellbeing; and (ii) what steps he will take to improve this.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the impact that cost of living increases are having on students and I will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available in future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the links between the cost of living crisis and the increase in organised criminal action, including (a) illegal money-lending; (b) drug dealing; and (c) violence; and (ii) to detail any plans she has to develop strategies to combat the exploitation by criminal gangs of (a) people in need; and (b) disadvantaged communities.
answer_text: Tackling organised crime continues to be a priority for my Department. Criminals will exploit any opportunity for their own means and the ongoing  cost of living crisis is no exception. It can increase the risk of cash thefts as well as the likelihood of consumers to consider counterfeit goods from which criminal gangs can profit.  Demand for illicit drugs remains high, with the current cost of living crisis manifesting in a growing use of alternative substances, including synthetic drugs, increasing abuse of prescription medicines and multi substance abuse.  Addressing the harm caused by paramilitarism and organised crime now, and preventing it happening in the future, is a critical part of the overall response. The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) is active across Northern Ireland to support communities and individuals who are vulnerable to paramilitary influence and harm. Over one hundred projects are delivered collaboratively by government departments, statutory agencies, and partners in the voluntary and community sector. These help keep people safe, reduce the risk of exploitation and over time, help to build community resilience to criminality and paramilitarism. This is a long-term endeavour that requires action across the Executive. On the specific issue of illegal money lending, EPPOC’s ‘Ending the Harm’, public awareness campaign (www.endingtheharm.com) has led the way in consciously highlighting the devastating impact that criminal gangs have on victims, families, communities, and wider society. The current campaign focuses on how gangs use illegal money lending to control and coerce vulnerable people. It has been instrumental in raising understanding through preventative messaging and provides signposting to support for those who have become victims. EPPOC, which is a cross Executive programme co-ordinated from within the Department of Justice, works in tandem with my Department’s Organised Crime Team and with a range of operational partners through the Organised Crime Taskforce.  Work is underway to develop an addendum workplan to the current Organised Crime Strategy to further tackle organised criminality and more effectively respond to this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how parents are contacted in relation to their child’s vaccination schedule and appointments; (ii) for his assessment of the risks of these methods being unsuccessful in contacting parents; and (iii) for his assessment of ways of how to best ensure parents are kept informed about their child’s vaccination requirements and appointments.
answer_text: In relation to part (i), the Child Health System (CHS) centrally schedules vaccinations for children according to the routine schedule. For preschool vaccinations, an appointment card is sent to the parents either with a specific time and date for their child’s appointment, or it will ask them to contact their GP practice to make an appointment. It is up to each GP practice to choose the option used for their appointment cards. For school-based vaccinations, the CHS will schedule vaccinations according to school year and will liaise with the school nursing team to offer vaccination in the school setting. Letters are sent out from the school nursing service about the scheduled vaccine together with information about the vaccine being offered and a consent form. Parents are given the telephone number of the school nursing service and invited to phone if they have any questions.In relation to part (ii), as the CHS works on a central and regional basis, it has been shown to be successful in contacting children, where the contact details are kept up to date. Parents are encouraged to keep their contact details up to date with their GP and this information is shared with CHS to issue appointments. The Public Health Agency undertakes a number of actions to raise awareness of the importance of maintaining the CHS record.  As with all systems, the CHS relies on the information it holds being updated, but parents can contact their GP, health visitor or school nurse if they want to review their child’s vaccination status.It is recognised that some parents, for example who do not have English as a first language, may find it difficult to understand the various communications issued. In the case of the primary immunisation appointment cards, these are discussed with parents when the health visitor calls for scheduled visits. In situations where parents do not speak English, the translator accompanying the health visitor will be able to translate the information provided. In relation to part (iii), parents are kept informed in a number of ways  regarding the vaccinations available for their child, this starts with the pregnancy book and the “birth to five” books, which are available to all parents to inform them of what vaccines their child should receive and when. The Personal Child Health Record (PCHR), given to parents at the time of birth, also lists the vaccinations given in childhood and has a facility to record when their child has had their vaccinations. GPs and other healthcare professionals are encouraged to promote vaccination at every encounter a child/parent has with services. Health visitors will also promote the uptake of vaccinations with parents at the “healthy child, healthy future” contacts which are scheduled when a child is 10-14 days old, 8 weeks, 14-16 weeks, 6-9 months, 1 year, 2 years, 3+ review.  In addition, vaccination is encouraged through School Nursing at the P1 ‘Health Appraisal’ and in advance of the vaccinations offered in sch

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the risk posed by an extension of the landfill site at Maghery Road, County Armagh; and (ii) whether, given its regional significance, this application should be called in for decision by his Department under the 2011 Planning Act.
answer_text: I understand that the planning application (ref: LA08/2019/0129/F) referred to at Maghery Road, County Armagh is currently under consideration by Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council. As decision maker, it is for the council to assess any potential risks from the proposed development as part of the planning assessment, in the first instance. My Department has received recent requests for the application to be ‘called in’ to it for determination. My Officials are currently assessing the requests and I will make a decision as to whether or not the application should be referred to my Department for determination once this assessment has been finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the estimated number of children vaccinated with the MMR vaccine; and (ii) the estimated proportion of parents who (a) have intentionally not had their child vaccinated; and (b) have not done so because they were not aware of appointments, had not been successfully contacted or were not registered with a doctor, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The latest quarterly coverage (July-Sept 2023) reports Northern Ireland uptake of one dose of MMR at 2 years of 88.8% and 93.6% at 5 years of age. Uptake of two doses of MMR vaccine at 5 years is 85%. This information is available at Vaccination coverage | HSC Public Health Agency (hscni.net). Information on the estimated proportion of parents who have intentionally not had their child vaccinated, have not done so because of unawareness of appointments, or because of lack of registration with a GP within the last five years is not held.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his plans to implement a loneliness strategy; (ii) his assessment of the scale and nature of loneliness in Northern Ireland; and (iii) the anticipated cross-departmental work and responsibility that will be required in the formation and roll-out of such a strategy.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has no responsibility for loneliness policy and has no plans to implement a loneliness strategy. A decision to develop a strategy, and identify a lead Department, would be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his plans to increase the provision of free and affordable childcare in line with the rest of the UK.
answer_text: Since my appointment as Education Minister, I have made the development and implementation of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy a priority and while I have stated that our ambition should not be limited, it is important that we take time to carefully consider our options and to secure the necessary funding.  I want to deliver a bespoke affordability scheme for Northern Ireland that addresses the challenges we face and delivers the outcomes we want to achieve.  This will include taking on board the learning from other jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of current women’s health services, particularly gynaecology; and (ii) to detail plans he has to deal with lengthy waiting lists and times for these services.
answer_text: As I outlined during a debate on the development of a Women’s Health Strategy on 13 February, current waiting lists are simply unacceptable.I also advised that my Department is in the process of developing a short-to-medium-term women’s health action plan that builds on ongoing work and identifies the priority actions across women’s health services that can be taken forward within our current budget, as well as those actions for which additional investment will be required.  In addition, I want to prioritise implementation of the recently published Gitting it Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology report. My Department has already written to all Trusts about the report and will engage with relevant stakeholders to progress implementation as far as possible within the available resources. A copy of the Hansard report on the debate can be found at Official Reports (niassembly.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he anticipates his previously released cancer strategy is to be fully funded with the restoration of the Assembly and Executive.
answer_text: Additional funding for the cancer strategy will be considered along with the many demands for extra funding across the health and social care system at this time. It is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether it is possible for a patient to opt-out of the digitalisation of their medical records.
answer_text: Information has been held in digital medical records for many years, Clinicians require this information to provide all patients and service users with safe and appropriate care. In very rare circumstances restrictions on access to selected parts of a record have been implemented.Information held on HSC computer systems is used for direct patient care only and any other use, such as research and development, requires additional patient consent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps that (i) have been made; (ii) are planned (a) to increase the number of wheelchair accessible taxis across Northern Ireland; (b) to ensure wheelchair accessible taxis are not more expensive for passengers; (c) to ensure taxi companies do not contravene the Equality Act; and (d) to put in place an individual or organisation to oversee the application of disability rights, in particular those relating to taxi businesses.
answer_text: Under current taxi legislation there is no requirement for taxi operators to have a minimum number of wheelchair accessible vehicles, and the option to include these in their fleet is at the discretion of the operators and drivers.  Neither does my Department under current taxi legislation have the powers to deliver financial support for the purchase or operation of a Wheelchair Accessible Taxi. Taxi fares are consistent for all passengers who can be charged up to the maximum fare tariff allowed. Taxi companies are responsible for complying with equality legislation.  My officials previously met with the Consumer Council, IMTAC and the Equality Commission, which led to direct contact between the Commission and the three largest taxi operators in NI.   As responsibility for disability rights relating to taxi businesses rests with the taxi companies my Department has no plans to oversee their application.If a passenger feels there has been a contravention of the Equality Act they can make a complaint firstly to the taxi operator and then to DVA through the complaints process:-https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/making-complaint-about-taxi-service#toc-0

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the process by which a person may change their medical history records; (ii) plans he has to improve this process; and (iii) the conditions or evidence required for this process to go ahead.
answer_text: Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations should make every effort to keep patient records accurate. However, occasionally information may need to be amended about the patient or their care.If a patient feels that the health or care information in their record is factually inaccurate Article 5 of UK General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) legislation provides a legal right to rectification. For example, a patient can ask for their home address to be changed because they moved house. They may also ask for something they feel has been inaccurately recorded, such as a diagnosis, to be corrected. However, it may not be possible to agree to all requests.A request can be made either by speaking to staff in the relevant HSC organisation (for example Health and Social Care Trust or GP Practice) or by making a request in writing. The patient may need to provide evidence of the correct details, for example proof of address or change of surname after marriage. The organisation will then consider the request. Where organisations agree to make a change, they should make it as soon as practically possible and within one calendar month. In certain circumstances, if the rectification is deemed complex, timescales could be extended to a maximum of three months for this rectification to happen.On occasion, a patient may disagree with information written in their record, but the information could still be factually correct. For example, the patient may disagree with a diagnosis they were given in the past. Whilst they can still ask the organisation to amend the entry that they feel is inaccurate, an organisation will consider with the care professional whether they believe it is factually correct. There may also be times when information was correct at the time the entry was made but has since changed. For example, there may be an initial working diagnosis which was, at the time of entry, clinically possible, but is later ruled out with a different confirmed diagnosis. Retaining the original diagnosis does not make the record inaccurate, so it is important that this is not amended. There are exceptions to this, for example, where there is a court order requiring an amendment.In cases where all parties agree that the information is inaccurate, it may still be necessary to retain the information. For example, health and care professionals may have taken the information into consideration when making decisions about treatment or care. This information would therefore be needed to justify and explain health and treatment decisions or to audit the quality of care received. The patient can, however, request for a comment or entry to be made in the record to show that they disagree with the content and what they think it should say.If the patient is unhappy with the decision of a health or care organisation to retain information they wish to have deleted or rectified there are steps a patient can take. In the first instance, the patient can make a formal complaint thro

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans for implementing the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his plans to tackle the spread of Bovine Tuberculosis.
answer_text: Bovine Tuberculosis is one of the most challenging issues faced by the cattle farmers in Northern Ireland.  I share Members’ concerns regarding the current high rate of TB incidence and the impact this is having on hard working farm families.  With herd incidence currently at over 10%, one farm in ten has experienced the difficulties associated with a new TB breakdown in the past year and I have already heard directly from farmers the impact this is having.Tackling this disease is also having a significant impact on my Department’s budget.  53 million pounds was spent on the control of TB in the last financial year and a similar amount is expected this year.  The high cost of controlling TB is not sustainable- not to farm businesses nor to my Departmental budget.  I can assure the house that tackling this disease and all the factors which contribute to its spread and endurance will be a top priority for me in my new role.  I will be working closely with my officials in the coming weeks and engaging with stakeholders to examine the best way to help lower the rate of TB in our herds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans to introduce stand-alone age discrimination legislation for accessing of goods, services and facilities.
answer_text: Consideration of extending age discrimination legislation to the provision of goods, facilities and services was the subject of consultations in a previous mandate.  A decision was not made in respect of the scope of that legislation. The issue requires further work to inform the potential scope of any legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the current availability of home carers, and with families being advised by Health and Social Care Trusts to use the direct payment system, whether he would appeal to available family members to become carers and receive direct payments via this route.
answer_text: HSC Trusts continue to promote and support the use and uptake of direct payment as a means of meeting individuals needs in a more flexible and innovative manner, ultimately with the aim of giving individuals greater choice over the care they receive. This in turn can sustain wellbeing and improve quality of life for many individuals, but I acknowledge that they are not the preferred option for everyone. Direct payments are not intended to replace existing support networks with family members, rather they are intended to supplement this family care. If a service user or family member feels that their individual circumstances would benefit from a receiving a direct payment to employ a family member, I would urge them to discuss this with their local HSC Trust who will consider a request on a case-by-case basis and will take into consideration the assessed needs of the service user.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps she will take to (i) meet the New Decade, New Approach target of 7,500 PSNI officers; (ii) improve conditions so as to limit the number of officers experiencing protracted periods of mental and physical ill-health; and (iii) increase the attractiveness of a career as a police officer for potential new recruits.
answer_text: Police officer numbers are a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northen Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.  These are primarily matters for the PSNI; however, my department will continue to engage with the PSNI where appropriate to ensure safe and effective policing.Whilst the policy and legislative framework which governs police officer terms and conditions falls under the remit of the Department of Justice, the PSNI has complete operational autonomy.  It is, therefore, primarily for the Chief Constable to determine how best to attract potential new recruits to choose a career in the police service. For my part I will endeavour to support him in achieving those objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timeline on the implementation of the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not been delivered. Work is progressing to develop proposals for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Football and I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority. Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she will continue with a previously proposed early-release scheme for prisoners in order to free-up space in prisons; and (ii) to provide details around eligibility criteria.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) operated a temporary release scheme during the Covid 19 pandemic.  This provided for the temporary release of certain categories of prisoners during the last 3 months of their sentence.  Its purpose was to provide each prisoner with single cell accommodation, an important aspect of the steps NIPS took to prevent the spread of coronavirus in its establishments.  There are no plans to re-start this scheme to deal with the current increase in population.NIPS has also operated a Conditional Early Release Scheme since June 2015.  This provides for the early release of some prisoners on a supervised licence.  Applicants must meet a range of criteria, including a number of statutory and non-statutory exclusions.  Successful candidates must also demonstrate that they pose a low likelihood of re-offending; that they have been of good behaviour whilst in custody; that they have approved, stable and supportive accommodation in the community; and that they have complied fully with all conditions imposed during any periods of temporary release.  I currently have no plans to expand this scheme however, the increase in the prisoner population and the difficulties that is placing upon the operational effectiveness of the Prison Service is a matter I am keeping under close review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the economic health of the community and voluntary sector; (ii) what plans he has to improve this; and (iii) for his assessment of how this sector could be used to support other public services.
answer_text: There is no single source of evidence on the economic health of the voluntary and community sector, which is diverse and complex.  However, my department has heard consistently from sector leaders in recent months that the cost of living crisis and unstable or reduced public sector funding is affecting the sustainability of services and of some organisations within the Sector.  The Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action (NICVA) has reported widespread financial pressures in the Sector based on extensive engagement and surveys of its membership. My department has recently consulted on policy proposals for future support for the Sector, setting out commitment to continued investment in core sectoral infrastructure and to improving the way that government works with and in support of the Sector.   Following consideration of responses, I will bring forward proposals for future investment in sectoral infrastructure, alongside commitments to strengthening future relationships between government and the Sector, including fair and sustainable funding approaches. I appreciate the critical role played by the voluntary and community sector in supporting our communities and delivering important social, environmental and economic outcomes across Northern Ireland.   All government departments currently benefit from the work of the Sector, in terms of the design and delivery of public services and through advocacy across a very wide range of policy interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current applications for the funding of new school buildings in East Londonderry.
answer_text: There are currently four School Enhancement Programme (SEP) projects, one Fresh Start project and one Major Capital project advancing in design and planning in the East Londonderry constituency. Sandelford School was announced in May 2018 under SEP. The proposed project involves a new build extension to provide general and specialist classrooms, with associated ancillary spaces. The project is currently at RIBA Stage 2 (Concept Design). Coleraine Grammar School was announced in May 2018 under SEP. The project will bring the school on to one site at Castlerock Road. It involves the construction of a new part single, part two-storey extension for specialist classrooms, reconfiguration and refurbishment of existing accommodation and car parking and bus drop off and collection. Construction of the project started in June 2023 and the estimated completion date is Summer 2024. Dominican College, Portstewart was announced in January 2019 under SEP. The proposed project involves demolition of existing accommodation and a new build extension. The project is currently at RIBA Stage 2 (Concept Design). St Patrick’s and St Brigid’s College, Claudy was announced in January 2019 under SEP. The proposed project involves the construction of a new standalone sports block to include: sports hall, PE Classroom, fitness suite and drama suite; extension and refurbishment of existing accommodation; and a 3G synthetic pitch. The project is currently at RIBA Stage 3 (Developed Design).Mill Strand Integrated Primary School was announced as a major capital project under the Fresh Start Programme in March 2016. Works on site commenced February 2023 and the school is currently expected to handover Mid-May 2024.Loretto College, Coleraine was one of twenty-eight major capital projects announced in March 2022. Following my statement to the assembly on Monday 12 February is one of the seven of those announced projects that I have released to progress in planning.  Work on early feasibility and design will commence on appointment of an Integrated Consultant Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to limit the formation of toxic algae blooms in Lough Neagh and to prevent them from travelling down the River Bann to north coast beaches.
answer_text: It has become clear that there are no quick fixes to the occurrence and probable re-occurrence of blue green algal (BGA) blooms at Lough Neagh. This is a problem that has been decades in the making and it could take decades to resolve.		Nutrient surplus entering the aquatic environment has been identified as one of the key drivers of water quality issues that led to the prolific BGA blooms in Lough Neagh in 2023 and the subsequent downward transmission of these blooms. DAERA is taking action on several fronts to address this.		My officials are working with The Agri-Food and BioSciences Institute (AFBI) to agree a programme of scientific work for Lough Neagh, including the development and validation of models to apportion nutrient loading from agricultural and non-agricultural sources. A review of potential in-lake future management options is also being taken forward by an Independent Science Advisory Group (SAG), chaired by my Chief Scientific Advisor. This work will complement a Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) scheme that aims to identify viable technical solutions to deal with the immediate effects of BGA within the Lough. 		One further action my Department is taking is through the College for Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), where a programme to deliver training for farmers / land managers at various levels targeted towards Lough Neagh catchment areas has already started. 		I hope my response provides you with an insight into the range of work being undertaken. I look forward to working with all stakeholders including my Executive and Assembly colleagues in finding solutions to address this complex issue facing Lough Neagh and our beaches.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what progress has been made in identifying and securing a site for the planned new integrated post-primary school in Coleraine.
answer_text: The Education Authority has initiated a project to establish the new school on one or more of the existing school sites, subject to available funding. The long-term accommodation needs to educate all pupils on one site will require major capital investment.  There is currently no open call for major capital works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans he has to reintroduce withdrawn programmes for primary school pupils and their families, such as holiday hunger payments and in-school counselling services.
answer_text: Following a decision by the Executive, the Department of Education, made, on an interim basis, School Holiday Food Grant payments from July 2020 to March 2023.  The ringfenced funding for this ceased in 2023 and the payments were therefore discontinued. I will be considering the way forward on this issue in the time ahead. The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023.  An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and I will assess its findings as I seek to better support the emotional health and wellbeing of learners.  The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) remains available to post-primary age pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will retain free bus and rail travel for those aged over 60 in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The 12-week public consultation on changes to the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme closed on 24th August 2023, with the Department receiving over 20,000 responses. These responses have been invaluable in understanding the benefits of the Scheme not only for individuals, but for our society and economy as a whole, and I thank everyone who took the time to respond or get involved.In the coming weeks I will be considering the responses the Department has received to the consultation and while I will do all I can to protect the concessions which matter most to people, any decisions on whether to keep free travel for those aged 60-64 must be based on the funding made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how he will ensure the fair distribution of funding across Northern Ireland, provided by Invest NI, particularly outside of Belfast.
answer_text: Promoting regional economic balance is a priority for me and I have instructed Invest NI to increase its focus and resources at a sub-regional level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how she plans to resolve (i) the issues barristers have with the timely and adequate payment of Legal Aid; and (ii) the backlog of cases due to be processed by our criminal justice system.
answer_text: Officials in my department have worked tirelessly throughout the year and have secured additional funding for legal aid to reflect the level of demand for payment in respect of cases completed. The opening allocation for 2023/24 was £93.385m and with additional funding the budget now stands at £106.3m. The value of bills received continues to be high and this level of demand will be factored into discussions on the 2024/25 budget. This is an example of the impact of inadequate funding of the department. Additional resources will be required to address the demand for payment of bills submitted. Current operational performance can be found at https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/lsa-operational-performance-2023-2024In terms of plans to resolve the backlog of cases due to be processed by the criminal justice system, at the outset of the pandemic, the criminal justice system responded rapidly and innovatively so that confidence in the system was maintained and to ensure the safety of staff, those who use the system and those in our care.  Key justice organisations (including PSNI, PPS, NICTS and the Department) and the judiciary have worked collaboratively to a system wide shared priority of recovery, in addition to individual recovery plans. New ways of working were introduced, for example making better use of technology and digital platforms, which have had long term benefits for the justice system. Justice organisations successfully bid for additional resources during the years post Covid-19 up to and including 2023/24.  The additional resources enabled collaborative efforts to be continued to sustain an increased throughput in courts of up to 115% capacity.  Bids for funding in 2024/25 have been included in the recent Future Year Budget planning exercise.The Criminal Justice Board has prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay within the system. This incorporates the efforts to recover the system, reducing backlogs and progresses work for long-term change.This work is built around five key workstreams and will take forward existing work on committal reform, existing and emerging work in the digital arena and explore other areas for efficiencies such as early engagement, court remits and out of court disposals. The system is presently under considerable pressure, dealing with increased caseload and although progress is being made, it will take time and resources to resolve this issue. There is no doubt the future financial outlook presents challenges to the resolving the backlog.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans for better safeguarding the wellbeing of homeless people with or without addiction issues.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail within this answer a copy has been placed in the Assembly Library to which I refer the member.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for acting on the recommendations of the Bengoa report in reforming our health service.
answer_text: Reforming our Health Service to become more efficient and deliver a better service to all our citizens, young and old, is a key priority for my Department.  Health and Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together sets out my Department’s response to the recommendations included in the Bengoa Report.  Delivering Together sets out to stabilise, reconfigure and to transform Health and Social Care services.Since the launch of Delivering Together in October 2016, three reports on the progress of the ring-fenced transformation funded projects have been published in 2017, 2019 and 2021.  These reports are available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/progressreportintroduction.  The next progress report is currently being drafted.There are several major areas of transformation and reconfiguration already underway and in the implementation phase, however, implementing health service transformation will require additional, sustained recurrent funding and support for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to increase the provision of social housing and facilitate the building of new, affordable private developments.
answer_text: The ability to increase the supply of social housing is constrained by budget.  My Department continues to work with the NIHE to bring forward measures to maximise the Social Housing Development Programme.  Officials are also working with the NIHE and a range of public bodies to look at ways in which we can make delivery timelier; bring more stability to the programme; and explore how issues and pressures associated with existing delivery might be addressed.In relation to new affordable homes, my Department continues to work with the sector to look at how we can increase the range of affordable options and products. This includes how we can support the delivery of more affordable homes through the local development plan process.Intermediate Rent is one such new affordable housing product.  The Intermediate Rent Policy was published in March 2023 and a supply of homes will come forward under a future funding package which is in development.My Department also funds Co-Ownership, which provides an alternative route into home ownership for people on low incomes. Importantly, around 20% of Co-Ownership homes are new build; offering an alternative affordable tenure in new housing developments.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on steps to provide childcare facilities for staff.
answer_text: At its meeting on 5 November 2019, the Assembly Commission was advised that the then Permanent Secretary of the Department of Finance (the Department), had liaised with the Assembly’s Clerk/Chief Executive, to explore the feasibility of opening a pre-school childcare facility on the Stormont Estate. The intention was that such a facility could be used by Members, Members’ staff, Assembly Commission staff and Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) staff working on the Estate.While some exploratory options and designs had been developed, considerable work was still needed to prepare a fully-worked business case.  This was being developed by the Department, and it was intended that once a detailed proposal was developed, it would be brought to the Assembly Commission for its consideration.Given the many interruptions to normal work activities since March 2020, arising from the Department’s response to COVID-19, the project was paused at that time. The Department has advised that, at the time, the feasibility of a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate could have been considered as a potential action for inclusion in a draft Childcare Strategy. The Department has also advised that work on developing a Childcare Strategy has restarted with an indicative date of March 2023 for completion of a draft Strategy. Consultation is planned for summer 2023 and Executive approval is anticipated in late 2023/early 2024.The Department envisages that a new action plan would be developed when the Strategy receives Executive Approval, however, it is not known at this stage if the Department’s response will include the provision of a childcare facility within the Stormont Estate, as consideration would be given to the significant changes within the working arrangements of NICS staff on the Estate (i.e. the implementation of Hybrid Working and the use of Connect2 hubs) which may impact on the requirement for an on-site location. It is worth noting that since 2017, Members and Assembly staff can avail of HMRC’s Tax-Free Childcare scheme. This scheme is administered centrally by HMRC, and allows the payment for approved childcare (for example, from a childminder or nursery), to be made via an online childcare account. Up to £2,000 per year for each child is available to help with the costs of childcare. As with all schemes set up by HMRC, it has specific rules and regulations that must be met before it can be used.In addition, some Members who met the relevant conditions, were able to purchase childcare vouchers using a salary sacrifice scheme. This arrangement meant that childcare vouchers could be purchased in a tax efficient manner. While the salary sacrifice arrangement ended in 2017, the scheme has remained open to Members who were already included in the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline what planned work it has to improve disability accessibility in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: As noted in the reply to AQW 773/22-27, the Assembly Commission has already undertaken a considerable number of accessibility initiatives in Parliament Buildings. While there are no current plans to carry out any additional work, the Assembly Commission’s commitment with regards to accessibility is reflected in its Disability Action Plan 2022-25 which was approved in February 2022. The Assembly Commission will continue to give due consideration to opportunities to improve the facilities in Parliament Buildings to ensure it is accessible to everyone.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the energy efficiency measures that have been put in place in Parliament Buildings in the last five years.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response given to AQO 2774/17-22 which provides details on previous improvements works (some of which fall outside the five-year time frame).The Assembly Commission strives to be an exemplar organisation in terms of sustainable development and has implemented a range of measures to enhance energy efficiency and to improve the environmental performance of Parliament Buildings.To reduce our energy consumption the following works have been carried out:Installing additional metering systems to allow for better management of usage;Installing LED lighting on Basement, 2nd, 3rd and 4th floor corridors and in offices on the 2nd, 3rd and 4th floors;Installing more energy efficient LED external lighting around the building;Installing more energy efficient LED lighting in the Assembly Chamber;In collaboration with the Department of Finance, replacing the lights in the Upper and Lower car parks with new LED lights; andReplacing the TVs in Parliament Buildings with more energy efficient models. Staff in the Assembly Commission’s Sustainable Development Unit work closely with partners to introduce energy-saving solutions and ensure continuous improvement in our energy efficiency.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the amount spent on (i) heating; and (ii) electricity in Parliament Buildings, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the annual cost spent on gas (for heating) and electricity for Parliament Buildings for the period 2017-18 to 2022-23.  Costs for 2022-23 are available up to the end of October 2022.YearElectricityGasTOTAL2017-18£173,663£86,702£260,3652018-19£198,170£91,063£289,2332019-20£199,999£82,104£282,1032020-21£203,780£81,948£285,7282021-22£237,203£246,365£483,5682022-23*£297,924£98,306£396,230* Figures updated to the end of October 2022It should be noted that the increase in costs seen in both 2021-22 and 2022-23 to date are mostly due to pricing increases in the wholesale markets.  Usage levels for both gas and electricity are published on the Assembly website:http://www.niassembly.gov.uk/about-the-assembly/corporate-information/sustainability/table-versions/

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the amount paid in fuel allowance to MLAs in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Members may claim expenses related to their Constituency Offices, which includes amounts for heating fuel.  While there is no specific “fuel allowance” available to Members, Constituency Office Operating Expenses (COOE) covers the ongoing, day to day expenses that may be claimed for running an office and this includes heating oil or gas.The amounts paid to Members for oil and gas for the past five complete years, together with the amounts paid for this financial year from April 2022 to October 2022, are set out in the table below.YearGasOilTotal2017-18£10,990£15,576£26,5662018-19£14,693£16,079£30,7722019-20£14,030£15,018£29,0482020-21£9,026£8,114£17,1402021-22£14,715£14,398£29,1132022-23*£8,030£5,972£14,002* April 2022 to October 2022

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the ways in which he will translate the success of the regional Foot and Ankle Waiting List Initiative into waiting lists in other areas of healthcare.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail what plans he is developing to reduce waiting lists and times for children’s health services; and (ii) to outline what large scale structural changes that are being considered within the health service in order to facilitate this.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the Northern Ireland Carbon Intensity Indicators 2022 report can be an indication that farmers are reducing their carbon emissions in specific processes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the estimated number and percentage of properties where average internet speeds will remain below 30Mbps, following the completion of Project Stratum.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail what steps she will take to ensure working people are never financially disadvantaged by working; and (ii) to outline the cross-departmental work she will do to ensure policies do not discriminate against working people.
answer_text: Universal Credit (UC) is now the main working age benefit in Northern Ireland and is available to both in-work and out of work claimants.UC was designed to ensure that claimants are better off in work. In contrast to the more rigid rules of older legacy benefits, UC does not restrict the number of hours that a claimant can work, claimants can take on temporary work without having to make a new claim to UC, and UC will continue to provide support between periods of work.It is a key principle of UC that working claimants on low pay should be supported to find more work, or better paying work. Claimants with earnings below a prescribed threshold receive additional support from Work Coaches to help achieve this. Furthermore, when UC claimants undertake training to enhance their employment prospects and receive a qualifying training allowance to meet specific costs for training or development, the amount of UC they receive may not be impacted by that training allowance. As the amount of a UC claimant’s employed earnings increases, the amount of UC they receive decreases. This is called the ‘earnings taper.’  The taper has been reduced from 65% to 55%. This allows working claimants to keep more of their UC along with their earnings.UC also makes provision for a ‘work allowance’ which is a prescribed amount of income that claimants, in specific circumstances, can earn before the earnings taper is applied. Where a qualifying claimant’s earnings are below this amount, there is no reduction to their UC, and the taper will only apply to earnings above the work allowance.I am aware that the cost of childcare has increased significantly in recent years and can form a real barrier to work for people with responsibility for children. UC also makes provision for 85% of qualifying childcare costs to be reimbursed to working parents in an effort to remove this barrier.	Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities, including working people, right across Northern Ireland.		The Department for Communities promotes the value of work, and the positive impact it can have on poverty, health and wellbeing, social inclusion, and our economy.		In drafting the Anti-poverty strategy, I will be engaging with my Executive colleagues, to ensure that as far as possible we encourage people seeking employment and remove barriers that prevent working people from staying in work. 		I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Strategy, and final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement as it is an Executive Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of how the functions of her Department, which require ministerial approval, will be impacted by a vacancy in the Ministerial office, post 28 October 2022; and (ii) to list the upcoming issues of importance that will be impacted by a vacancy in the Ministerial office.
answer_text: Following my appointment as Minister of Justice on 3 February 2024, I look forward to being able to advance a number of important issues relating to the work of the Department of Justice.  In the absence of a Minister since 28 October 2022, it was not possible to progress a number of issues within the Department including inter alia:Agreement of a way forward following public consultation on the future use of audio and video links within courts and completion of Assembly procedures relating to six-monthly extensions of current provisions.Agreement of a way forward following public consultation on the future use of audio and video links within courts and completion of Assembly procedures relating to six-monthly extensions of current provisions.Agreement of a way forward following a Call for Evidence on Abuse of Position of Trust. Decision on policy options in relation to coronial investigations into deaths abroad.A number of decisions were taken, however, by senior Departmental officials under the provisions of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 when considered in the public interest.   Details of decisions taken by senior officials that would normally have been taken by a Minister are available from here: www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/articles/reports-decisions-taken-by-northern-ireland-departments

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the information that recommends people continue to receive COVID vaccinations.
answer_text: Throughout the course of the COVID-19 vaccination programme, we have followed the advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccinations and Immunisations (JCVI), an independent expert advisory committee.  JCVI consider all the available scientific evidence, both published and unpublished, before offering their advice to the four UK Health Ministers.  JCVI published their advice in relation to the autumn booster programme: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/jcvi-updated-statement-on-the-covid-19-vaccination-programme-for-autumn-2022/joint-committee-on-vaccination-and-immunisation-jcvi-updated-statement-on-the-covid-19-vaccination-programme-for-autumn-2022COVID-19 is more serious in older people and in people with certain underlying health conditions.  This winter it is expected that many respiratory infections, including COVID-19 and flu may be circulating at high levels, this may put increasing pressure on hospitals and other health care services.  For these reasons, people aged 50 years and over, those in care homes, and those aged 5 years and over in clinical risk groups are being offered an autumn booster of COVID-19 vaccine, in line with JCVI advice.A booster is also be offered to front-line health and social care staff, those who care for vulnerable individuals and families of individuals with weakened immune systems.The autumn booster is being offered to those at high risk of the complications of COVID-19 infection, who may have not been boosted for a few months. As the number of COVID-19 infections increases over the winter, this booster should help to reduce their risk of being admitted to hospital with COVID-19.  I would therefore encourage all eligible individuals to continue to follow the expert advice of JCVI and take up the offer of vaccination

I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Service of Northern Ireland maintain their own statistics on persons arrested and charged by them. You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in parts (i) and (ii) of the question to the PSNI.In relation to the information requested in part (iii) of the question, prosecutions and convictions datasets held by the Department do not contain detail in relation to the location in which an offence occurred, so it is not possible from information held, to identify cases where there has been a successful prosecution in relation to gun attacks in the Causeway Coast and Glens area, in the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether (i) he will enable the reintroduction of direct payments to family members, or people in the same household, acting as personal assistants to a care recipient; and (ii) the staffing and care provision shortfalls being experienced in the sector constitute exceptional circumstances.
answer_text: The temporary flexibilities, which included the possibility of employing a family member as a personal assistant, were introduced as a temporary response to the unprecedented circumstances being faced during the Covid-19 pandemic.The Personal Social Services and Children’s Services (Direct Payments) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 set out provisions relating to Direct Payments. These state that Direct Payments should not be used to pay spouses/partners or family members and their partners who may be living in the same house as the recipient unless it is necessary to meet assessed needs to an appropriate standard. This is broadly analogous to the position in other parts of the UK.As noted above, usually a close family member who lives in the same household as a person requiring care is not permitted to be employed via Direct Payments. However, allowing temporary flexibility was seen to be reasonable given the very exceptional circumstances and risks of infection that existed at the time in relation to the Covid-19 pandemic. A short-term, temporary arrangement could therefore be considered in discussion with the named worker and with prior, written permission from the local Trust. The flexibilities contained in the guidance were initially due to come to an end in March 2022 but were subsequently extended to 30 September 2022. My Department wrote to the Trusts on 26 September 2022 to advise the phasing out of such arrangements, and to revert to previous practice. In general terms, Direct Payments are not intended to replace existing support networks with family members, and for that reason only in exceptional circumstances can they be used to buy a service from a spouse or civil partner, relative or anyone who lives in the same house. If a service user feels that their situation falls into this category, they should discuss it with their relevant HSC Trust who will consider on a case by case basis.I recognise that staffing issues, along with increased demand for services have been, and continue to be a challenge across the care sector. Work has been ongoing to deliver additional funding, both in-year and on a recurrent basis, to try to alleviate the ongoing pressures on the system, particularly in regards to recruitment and retention of staff.I was therefore pleased to announce an uplift of £7.1m for 2022-23 for Direct Payments on 6 October. This should see personal assistants receive an uplift of around 12% in their hourly rate and allow individuals who employ personal assistants via Direct Payments to offer improved rates of pay and, in turn, support the continued provision of care in the context of an increasingly competitive employment market. My officials have written to Trusts advising them of the uplift and asking that the payments are progressed as quickly as practicable.Individuals employed through Self-Directed Support play a vital role in enhancing the quality of live for the person they are employed by, and I am pleased to be able to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the recent outbreak of avian influenza and the threat it poses to the Northern Ireland poultry industry; and (ii) to detail the financial safety nets in place to support any farmers who experience an outbreak.
answer_text: In recent weeks, a significant surge of avian influenza cases has been seen across Great Britain (GB) and the figures continue to rise on a daily basis. Since 1 October 2022 there have been over 60 cases of HPAI H5N1 in poultry and captive birds across GB.HPAI H5N1 has also been confirmed in captive birds at Castle Espie, Wildfowl and Wetland Trust (WWT). A 3km disease control zone has been put in place and my officials are working closely with WWT representatives on the disease control measures required. There have also been over 50 wild birds detected with HPAI H5N1 from across Northern Ireland (NI) in recent months.An outbreak of bird flu will have a devastating impact on farmers and their families, not to mention the wider impacts on the NI poultry flock and international trade. As a precautionary step to help protect the NI poultry flock, an Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) has been declared for all poultry and captive birds across NI from noon on 17 October 2022. 7.	The AIPZ places a legal requirement on all bird keepers in NI to follow strict biosecurity measures.  This applies if you keep pet birds, commercial flocks or just a few birds in a backyard or hobby flock.	In relation to compensation The Disease of Animals (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 (the Order) provides that DAERA will pay compensation at current market value for all birds culled, at the Department’s direction, to mitigate against the risk of avian influenza spread from an infected premises.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether community pharmacies regularly pay more for medicines than they are reimbursed for by his Department; and (ii) what plans he has to make changes to the drug tariff to enable community pharmacies to dispense medicines more affordably and without cost to themselves.
answer_text: Contractors are not reimbursed on an individual dispensed medicine basis. Under current funding arrangements for community pharmacies, there is an agreed level of retained purchased profit (also known as “margin”) within the system. This figure is currently a minimum of £26.5 million. In order to determine that the remuneration to pharmacy contractors is both fair and reasonable, (and delivering the agreed level of retained purchase profit), the Ongoing Margin Survey is carried out each year. The aim of the margin survey is to estimate the level of “margin” community pharmacies are able to retain from purchasing medicines. The Ongoing Margin Survey is managed by a multidisciplinary group which has representations from Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the DoH and both the operational and analytical teams from the Business Services Organisation (BSO). An Independent Assessor verifies calculations and attends all meeting of the multi-disciplinary group.As previously stated, the current agreed level of retained purchase profit (“margin”) is a minimum of £26.5 million per annum. Results from the last two years which have been completed (19/20 and 20/21) indicates levels of margin significantly in excess of this figure.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) in England consulted on a number of community pharmacy drug reimbursement reform proposals back in 2019. DHSC are now beginning to implement these changes to the English Drug Tariff, and as the NIDT is currently reflective of the English Drug Tariff, a consultation on potential changes to the Northern Ireland Tariff was issued in March 2022.My Department will be working together with our colleagues in CPNI on the outworkings of this consultation over the coming months, including consideration of potential impacts and mitigations

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 3962/22-27, (i) to detail the earliest potential date when decisions to change the eligibility criteria for free school meals and the school uniform grant will come into effect; and (ii) whether she has plans to expedite this process in light of the cost of living crisis and its impact on families’ ability to pay for school meals and uniforms.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to progress a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.  I will be considering the next steps for this review in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the £5.3m support package for community pharmacies, to help meet the rising costs of medicines, will cover all the price increases.
answer_text: Community pharmacy representatives have highlighted inflationary pressures in relation to the cost of medicines. In the last number of months there has been a marked increase in the market prices of a small number of medicines versus the reference Drug Tariff price. Where this is identified, community pharmacies are reimbursed at the higher price and this is known as a concessionary price.While relatively small in number, the cost of concessionary prices has risen each month and at September was £1.7m.This additional cost is fully reimbursed. However, as community pharmacies are having to manage the risk in advance of concessionary prices being awarded, my Department has increased the amount of concessionary price advance by £0.5m to £1.5m and will keep this under review.Furthermore, my Department had provided a special advance payment of £35m to community pharmacies at the beginning of the COVID-19 emergency.  This has been reclaimed on a gradual basis be retracting £1.4m each month. Given the cashflow issues being reported, my Department has suspended this retraction for a period of three months.Finally, my Department is processing additional funding of £0.6m to community pharmacy from existing programme funds. It is anticipated these measure will alleviate the issues being faced and my Department will keep this issue under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what conversations she has had with the Chief Constable regarding potential cost-efficiency measures in the PSNI, including the scaling back of services, pay and staff.
answer_text: I have regular meetings with the Chief Constable and we discuss a range of policing issues, including the impact of in-year pressures.  I last met with the Chief Constable as part of our regular stocktakes on 11 October and we discussed the PSNI’s financial position amongst other matters.In the absence of an Executive and a budget, the financial position for the justice system as a whole is extremely challenging. PSNI has provided my Department with regular updates of the in-year pressures they are currently facing which, as with all parts of the justice system, are significant. However, the prioritisation of policing resources is ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting his operational independence and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the steps being taken to introduce a Universal Basic Income.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland.I am aware that Universal Basic Income has been raised by stakeholders during the Anti-Poverty Strategy co-design process. I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Strategy and final content will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how is she ensuring that the needs and rights of children in families, who rely on benefits, are being met.
answer_text: The Department is committed to supporting people through the welfare benefits system.A range of measures have been introduced, including:47% increase in the maximum amount of childcare costs available under Universal Credit from 28 June 2023, 10.1% increase in working age benefits from April 2023, a further 6.7% increase from April 2024, and an increase in Local Housing Allowance rates to the 30th percentile of local market rents from April 2024.A package of Cost of Living support payments was paid over the course of the 2023/24 financial year to those in receipt of certain means-tested benefits, disability benefits and pensions.In addition, in Northern Ireland, families with children are protected from the effects of the Benefit Cap and Social Sector Size Criteria (Bedroom Tax) through existing welfare mitigation schemes. Further information on all support available can be found at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/information-and-services/benefits-and-money

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) when an applicant is informed of their Personal Independence Payment entitlement amount, is it described as an offer or entitlement; and (ii) for her assessment of whether describing it as an offer could be misleading to applicants.
answer_text: (i)The notification issued to applicants refers to an award of Personal Independence Payment and does not make reference to an offer. The notification confirms whether the applicant will receive the benefit, specifies the components and rate payable, the duration of the award and the total number of points scored. 		(ii)The information provided, therefore, is not misleading.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for her assessment of the interim report of the Independent Review of Education; and (ii) whether the interim report contains recommendations that are actionable now.
answer_text: I welcome the Independent Review of Education panel’s Interim Report and am pleased that the panel has made progress. I am aware they have carried out extensive engagement with stakeholders and I look forward to considering the Final Report in due course.This Interim Report sets out the work undertaken by the Panel and the key themes identified for further exploration and analysis.  It does not include recommendations for action at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 3628-22/27, to detail what steps are being taken to bring the 16 Actionable voids in East Londonderry back into use.
