answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for The Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:     “The previous response relates to 16 Actionable voids as at October 2022.  At the end of April 2024, Causeway Area’s East Londonderry Actionable voids totalled 24.  Actionable voids are defined as properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated/awaiting change of tenancy repairs.  These may also include properties deemed to be difficult to let.  20 of these properties are awaiting routine change of tenancy repairs (17 of these are currently offer accepted and awaiting sign up with the remaining 3 under offer).  2 properties have been extensively damaged and are undergoing significant change of tenancy repairs. The remaining 2 properties are subject to a legal dispute regarding the liability for reinstatement / demolition costs”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the increase in the number of sexual offences committed in the past five years; and (ii) the length of time taken by the justice system to clear sexual offences.
answer_text: I am conscious that there has been an almost 69% increase in the number of convictions for sexual offences in the courts over the last five years which, although disturbing, is indicative of the success of the work of the PSNI, the Public Prosecution Service and the Courts in bringing perpetrators to justice.  While PSNI advise that there are no clear trends, in respect of locations, offenders or patterns in reporting, which would indicate the reasons for an increase in recorded crimes, it is, of course, a matter of concern for me and not something about which I am complacent. PSNI statistics are attached at Annex A and are available on the PSNI website.I recently introduced a number of new sexual offences in the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. While there is already a robust legislative framework of sexual offences to deal with a wide range of sexual offending, where it is clear there is a gap in the law, I take steps to address that gap.  I have also introduced a number of measures to increase victims’ confidence to report sexual offences, knowing that they will be supported and protected through the criminal justice system. Measures include a new advocacy support service, the establishment of remote evidence centres; work to provide trauma informed training to justice professionals and; the provision of free legal advice to adult complainants. Tackling delay is one of the biggest challenges facing the criminal justice system. My Department is working with key justice partners to take forward a number of initiatives to help speed up the justice system.On 17 October, I introduced the first phase of Committal Reform provided for in the Criminal Justice (Committal Reform) Act (NI) 2022. These changes will help support victims and witnesses in Crown Court cases by ensuring that they cannot be called to provide oral evidence as part of committal proceedings in the magistrates’ court in advance of trial.Reducing the time it takes to complete criminal cases is a complex issue and reforms take time to embed and for their impact to be seen. Like many other areas of public service, COVID has had a significant impact on efforts to speed up the justice system, resulting in significant backlogs, which will take time to clear.  										            Annex AStatistics Information on the number of sexual offences reported to police are included in recorded crime statistics published by the Police Service of Northern Ireland.  Figures for sexual offences recorded by police in the years 2017/18 – 2021/22 have been provided in the following table.   Police recorded crime - sexual offences, 2017/18 to 2021/22Year2017/182018/192019/202020/212021/22Sexual offences     3,430      3,535      3,551      3,328      4,059 Note:Statistics on police recorded crime in Northern Ireland are collated and produced by statisticians seconded to the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) from the Northern Ireland Statistics and Res

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the future of the Cross-Border Healthcare scheme.
answer_text: The scheme you are referring to is the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) which has now closed.In my written assembly statement on 20 June 2022, regarding the reform of stroke services and the recovery of elective orthopaedic services in Northern Ireland, I announced my decision to extend the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (ROIRS) by allocating a further £5m, to continue it beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until the £5m of new funding had been committed.New applications were accepted from 1 July 2022 and the scheme closed to new applications at 5pm on Wednesday 21 September as the additional £5m funding had been committed.I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I have continued to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement. However, because of these pressures, I regret that it has not been possible to assign funding to further extend the RoIRS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why, in the Northern Trust area, an educational psychologist’s statement of educational need and recommendation of support for a child awaiting an official autism assessment is not sufficient to enable the child’s family to access the Autism Advisory Service; and (ii) to outline the steps he is taking to expedite the delivery of autism assessments and corresponding support in this area.
answer_text: Part (i) of this question is an issue for the Department of Education as the Autism Advisory Service is an Education Authority service. In response to part (ii) it should be acknowledged, that there is an excess of demand for the Paediatric ASD service in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust that exceeds capacity for assessment and diagnosis & in turn for providing specialist intervention following any diagnosis of ASD & intervention to those with a historical diagnosis. Referrals to the Paediatric ASD service in total (assessment and intervention) has increased by 55.9% between 2020/21 and 2021/22 (2,272 to 3,542 referrals) .  These figures do not include the telephone consultation service, which receives on average 170 enquiries per month.In the Northern Health and Social Care Trust a recovery plan has been initiated to attempt to address the backlog in referrals for autism assessment. Actions within this plan include:Continuing to offer the Telephone Consultation Service - This service was established during Covid to support all families known to the Paediatric ASD service (those awaiting ASD assessment and those with ASD) to provide advice to manage immediate needs impacting on daily life, to share resources and signpost to appropriate supports.  Continuing offering overtime clinics for diagnostic assessment  To apply diagnostic referral criteria to allow appropriate triage into correct assessment care pathwayTo design a streamlined person centred care pathway for Looked After Children who require ASD assessment  and to provide intervention & support to those with ASD/ those awaiting assessmentFurther extend a pilot joint CAMHS/ ASD assessment model to all trust areas which provides a streamlined person-centred process for accessing  ASD assessment for young people with significant mental health difficulties that require on-going input / support from Specialist CAMHSWorking in partnership with multi-disciplinary teams (MDT) supporting GP surgeries to streamline GP referrals where MDT will assist in the recording of development histories as part of a child/young person’s referral to the ASD diagnostic service (increase capacity within ASD service) Further developing online intervention supports and webinars to release staff for further diagnostic work

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what work that is being undertaken to prepare for the potential scrapping of EU environmental laws, including the creation of similar legislation for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following EU Exit my Department delivered a significant programme of legislation to ensure the preservation of environmental protections and standards in domestic legislation. The UK Government has recently introduced the Retained EU Law (REUL) (Revocation and Reform) Bill which proposes that retained EU Law is repealed, amended or extended from 31 December 2023. My officials are considering the potential impact of the REUL Bill along with colleagues across the NICS and in other UK jurisdictions.It is my intention going forward that Northern Ireland continues to have a clear statutory basis for protecting the environment as has been the case throughout the changes resulting from the UK’s withdrawal from the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on plans to introduce consistent and mandatory relationship and sex education in schools.
answer_text: Teaching about Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age and it is the responsibility of the Board of Governors of each school to ensure that a comprehensive programme is delivered which meets the needs of its pupils and aligns with its RSE policy.The curriculum gives schools flexibility to adapt what they teach and enables them to use a variety of approaches and curricular resources that reflect, and are responsive to, the learning needs of their pupils.  This ensures that young people are both safe and well-informed about all of the issues they face in modern society.There is no evidence to suggest that detailed lists of compulsory subject content within a curriculum guarantees that a subject will be well or appropriately taught.  Furthermore, in a subject such as RSE, prescribed content may rapidly become out of date when new issues emerge.  A wide range of resources have been developed and are available on the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s RSE Hub, which will include the future publication of a RSE Progression Framework.  This approach is designed to improve confidence and capacity in the teaching of RSE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the increase in the number of students dropping out of university courses, in particular his assessment of whether the cost of living crisis may have contributed to this trend.
answer_text: Recent figures published by the Student Loans Company (SLC) show that student withdrawal notifications for academic year 2021/22 are higher than the previous 2 academic years. This recent increase in withdrawals applies across the UK and not just in Northern Ireland. However, the SLC report also indicates that while Northern Ireland student withdrawal notifications for 2021/22 are higher when compared with 2019/20 and 2020/21, they are broadly in line with the (pre-pandemic) figure for 2018/19. The number of Northern Ireland and other UK domiciled students dropping out of higher education courses during the pandemic was unusually low. The Higher Education Statistics Agency (HESA) suggests there are likely to be multi-faceted reasons underpinning this decrease, rather than a single, direct explanation for the drop. However, during periods of economic uncertainty, there is often a trend for increased higher education enrolments. The most recent increase is therefore likely to be a ‘normalising’ of the withdrawal rates following the pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Strategic Investment Board’s report into the need for a veterinary school in Northern Ireland, including its anticipated publication date.
answer_text: There is an increasing acceptance that Northern Ireland requires a more assured supply of veterinarians in the long term, than is available from historical sources.  There are a number of options for achieving this which merit further exploration, and last year I commissioned, from the Strategic Investment Board (SIB), a Baseline Report and Situational Analysis of Veterinary Services in Northern Ireland.  The report completed this year by the SIB is part of the overall analysis as to whether a veterinary school is needed for Northern Ireland and focused on just one element, the need for veterinary personnel here. I am keen that the report is published alongside other relevant evidence, and work is ongoing between my Department and representatives from Queens University, Ulster University and the Department for the Economy in that regard.  I will confirm the timescale for publication as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what support exists for schools that are unable to afford to keep their classrooms at a temperature suitable for teaching children; and (ii) what direction has been given to schools for which this is the case.
answer_text: (i) In the 2022-23 financial year, an additional £12.7m was allocated to schools to mitigate the impact of increased energy costs.  In addition, from the 1 October 2022 to March 2023 schools benefitted from the Energy Bill Support Scheme, which protected them from excessively high energy bills over the winter period.  For the period 1 April 2023 to 31 March 2024 schools will again benefit from a revised Energy Bill Discount Scheme, which provides a discount on gas and electricity unit prices subject to a capped discount. Schools do not need to do anything to obtain this discount, as it will be applied automatically to energy bills.Since August 2020, the EA has proactively sought to mitigate the impact of increasing utility and energy costs by engaging an independent energy advisor to provide advice on purchasing energy, tariff changes and hedging purchase prices for electricity and gas on behalf of our schools. In the most recent update from the EA, it has confirmed that following a review of 2023-24 energy costs by the energy advisor, energy costs are forecast to reduce significantly compared to the prior year.   (ii) Any school which is facing financial difficulty is encouraged to contact the EA, as funding authority for grant aided schools, in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what plans he has to bring a scheme similar to Project Girona in Coleraine to other areas.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the level of co-operation that exists between her Department and the Home Office in the sourcing and securing of temporary accommodation for both local homeless people and those arriving via refugee programmes.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Department for Communities (DfC) and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) are key partners in the delivery in Northern Ireland of the Syrian Vulnerable Person Relocation Scheme, the Afghan Relocation and Assistance Policy and the Afghan Citizens Relocation Scheme. Under these schemes temporary accommodation is directly sourced for refugees in advance of arrival in Northern Ireland.DfC and NIHE are actively involved in the delivery of support for Ukrainian nationals that have arrived in Northern Ireland under the various visa schemes provided by the UK government in response to the Russian invasion. The NIHE directly deliver a response to breakdowns in sponsorship arrangements under the Homes for Ukraine Scheme that could involve sourcing new sponsorship hosts or providing assistance under statutory homelessness including providing temporary accommodation.  In Northern Ireland the Executive Office has responsibility for refugee and asylum integration and has established structures at strategic and operational levels through which DfC and NIHE engage on a range of issues, including the provision of temporary accommodation. The Housing Executive also works with the Home Office and the Mears Group in relation to refugees moving on from the asylum process that require homelessness assistance and temporary accommodation.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 3060/22-27, whether he plans to bring forward legislation to protect the rights of working women experiencing symptoms of menopause or fertility treatment which may impact their ability to work.
answer_text: In order to meet its statutory obligations, the immediate focus and priority for my Department, on employment related entitlements, is the progression of miscarriage leave and pay and domestic abuse safe leave.I have previously said that I believe an Employment Bill is needed to ensure our employment law framework continues to meet the needs of workers and businesses.Whilst there are no plans at present to legislate beyond the existing legal and equality obligations currently in effect, I encourage all employers to treat their workers fairly and sympathetically. Any proposals for inclusion in a future Employment Bill will be subject to the views of the Executive, stakeholder engagement, public consultation and the relevant Assembly processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of social housing units that exist in the (a) Macosquin; and (b) Castlerock areas; and (ii) what planned social housing projects are being developed for these areas.
answer_text: The table below details the number of Housing Executive properties in the Macosquin and Castlerock Common Landlord Areas (CLAs) as of the 18th October 2022. Please note that the Housing Executive do not hold corresponding information for housing association stockCLAProperty CountMacosquin77Castlerock42The Housing Executive is currently in the process of nominating a housing association to take forward a social housing development of five homes on Housing Executive-owned land at Ramsey Park, Macosquin. Subject to acceptance of the nomination by a housing association, this proposal is anticipated to start on-site as part of the 2023/24 Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). The Housing Executive has recently responded to a planning application for 14 homes at Freehall Road, where housing need was confirmed along with the suitability of the proposed housing mix to meet the needs of households on the waiting list.  It is anticipated this will be brought forward by a housing association and added to the SHDP in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what efforts are being made to connect houses added retrospectively to Project Stratum at the same time as nearby houses that were included in the initial plan.
answer_text: As you aware, Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30Mbps or greater, primarily across rural areas of Northern Ireland. The project was allocated £165m of public funding, £150m of which was secured under the Confidence and Supply Agreement between the DUP and the Conservatives. Fibrus Networks was appointed as the contractor in November 2020 to deliver gigabit-capable broadband to 76,000 premises in rural areas across Northern Ireland.In January 2022, my Department secured additional capital funding of £32 million to bring a further 8,500 premises into Project Stratum. Fibrus Networks is now delivering gigabit-capable broadband to 85,000 premises throughout Northern Ireland.Wherever possible Fibrus Network aim to roll out the additionally contracted premises alongside the originally contracted premises. However, as would be expected, planning and deployment to the originally contracted 76,000 premises was well progressed when the additional 8,500 premises were added to the project and several areas had been delivered, with many more underway and on track for completion by 31 March 2024. As a result, it was necessary for Fibrus Networks to add a further four delivery quarters to the project to allow for additional planning, resources, network build and time to deliver. All 85,000 premises will have access to gigabit capable full fibre broadband by 31 March 2025. This approach has allowed Project Stratum to continue to proceed at pace, ensuring the best value for money is delivered and progress with the deployment is maintained.If not already done so, citizens should register their interest at  https://hyperfastni.com/contact, and Fibrus Networks will keep them informed of progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether the School Holiday Food Grant will be available for children over the autumn mid-term and Christmas breaks; and (ii) to detail what steps have been taken to extend free school meals provision to all nursery and primary school pupils.
answer_text: I announced on 20 October that I have decided to extend the School Holiday Food Grant to cover the Halloween 2022, Christmas 2022 and February half term 2023 school holiday periods. This will provide financial assistance to families whose children are entitled to  free school meals with families receiving £13.50 per week for each entitled child. The Department is continuing to progress a review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria. The review is considering a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meal for some/all year groups as well as raising the current income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit, Working Tax Credit or Child Tax Credit. It is planned that a public consultation will commence later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the locations of the anticipated 1,400 new homes to be built by Clanmil Housing following recent investment; and (ii) the amount to be provided by her Department as part of this project.
answer_text: My Department already provides significant funding for social housing and this private financing will enable Clanmil, one of our leading providers of social housing, to build more social housing for those in most need across Northern Ireland.  Should Clanmil bring forward any additional social housing schemes, they will be assessed by NIHE and/or my Department and appropriate level of Housing Association Grant (HAG) awarded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the number of school pupils in receipt of free school meals in each of the last five years; (ii) what this number represents as a percentage of all school pupils; and (iii) for her assessment of any change in these figures.
answer_text: The table below shows the (i) number and (ii) percentage of pupils entitled to free school meals attending all schools in Northern Ireland.  The figures show a broadly consistent pattern over the five year period despite the disruption to education caused by the pandemic during this time. The number and percentage of pupil entitled to free school meals 2017/18 to 2021/22Academic Year(i) Number of pupils(ii) Percentage of pupils2021/2298,80428.5%2020/2197,59228.3%2019/2096,69728.4%2018/1999,16529.3%2017/18100,27529.9%Source: Annual School Census

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what advice has been given to schools’ catering departments regarding making school dinners affordable but nutritious in the face of rising food and energy costs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the provision of school meals in grant-aided nursery, primary, post-primary and special schools with the exception of voluntary grammar or grant-maintained integrated schools where those schools are responsible for this provision. The price of a school meal in the schools for which the EA is responsible is determined by the EA. In determining this price, the EA is conscious of the potential impact that any rise in price would have on families paying for school meals. As a result, the EA has not raised the price of a school meal since 2017. The EA continues to keep this price under review.Nutritional standards apply to school lunches as set by the Department which include the recommended portion sizes and nutritional requirements within each food category. This ensures that all pupils can access a healthy and nutritious school lunch that is suitable as their main meal of the day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 3630/22-27, (i) whether these timeframes align with her expectations and targets; (ii) to outline any excess costs associated when cases go beyond a reasonable timeframe; and (iii) to detail what plans she has to review the process with a view to expediting these kind of cases.
answer_text: (i) The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service publish a range of targets and performance information in their Annual Report and Accounts.  In relation to the Kings Bench cases, the figures for 2021-22 show that the target of listing 98% of Clinical Negligence claims for review before the Master within 13 months of receipt of the writ was met, while 97% of claims for Personal Injury were listed for review within three months of setting down compared to the target of 98%.  There are no separate targets for Personal Injury or Clinical Negligence cases in the County Court.  The target of 95% Civil bills to be allocated a hearing date within 15 weeks of receipt of the certificate of readiness was not met during 2021/22 (85%).(ii) The cost of individual court cases is not recorded.  In civil matters the onus is on the parties involved to prepare the case and provide the Court with the relevant information.  The costs are largely dependent on the complexity of the case and factors such as the length of time involved in case preparation, the number of legal representatives and other experts involved, and on the number and length of court hearings.  Delays in providing the relevant information or the failure to progress the case by the plaintiff or respondent may not necessarily lead to additional court hearings or impact on the costs.(iii) The Judiciary have published a series of Pre-Action Protocols and Practice Directions in relation to setting out best practice and procedures required to meet the courts expectations and ensure that litigation is dealt with expeditiously and fairly.  The Civil & Family Justice Review made a number of recommendations to streamline processes and expedite cases, and a Shadow Civil Justice Council has been established to oversee the co-ordination of a draft plan for their implementation.  The Council are currently working on a range of proposals including a Personal Injury and Damage Only Road Traffic Accident Pre-Action Protocol. In relation to claims for criminal injuries compensation, as I indicated in the response to AQW 3630/22-27, each claim is unique and often complex and so setting targets for the assessment of claims is not straightforward.  I am content, however, that officials are focused on processing compensation claims as expeditiously as possible, bearing in mind the dependence on information from external sources and more recently the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic.   While there are no specific excess costs related to the timeframe for completing the assessment of cases, the scope for making any changes to administrative arrangements for processing of claims is always kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how her Department considers (i) accommodation; (ii) relationships; and (iii) employment issues when supporting people to not reoffend.
answer_text: The point at which an individual leaves the care of the Prison Service can be a particularly difficult and testing time. My Department is focused on improving outcomes, reducing reoffending and making communities safer by ensuring people resettle successfully on leaving custody.  I have met with the Ministers of Health and Communities to discuss how the housing, health and justice sectors can improve collaboration to tackle homelessness for those with complex lives. I am committed to working with others to ensure the accommodation needs of those being released from prison are appropriately addressed. As part of current pre-release planning arrangements, support is provided by both prison staff and partner organisations to ensure that people in our care have accommodation in place on release. However I recognise that there is a need to improve the provision of sustainable accommodation and my officials are working with the Department for Communities, Department of Health and NI Housing Executive, to consider the potential expansion of the Housing First model.In November 2019, the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) launched a new Strengthening Family Relations Strategy for 2019-2024. The aim of the strategy is to set out how NIPS, working in partnership through a multi-agency approach, will strengthen family relationships. Due to Covid progress was delayed however, work is now ongoing to deliver the actions emerging from the Strategy.  I recognise that securing employment on release from prison can be one of the critical success factors in reducing a person’s likelihood of reoffending. My Department is taking forward a range of initiatives linked to enhance employability opportunities for people with convictions.  Aside from the investment in learning and skills provision through Belfast Metropolitan and North West Regional Colleges, these include the introduction of Department for Communities work coaches into the prison setting; utilising social clauses and employment schemes to provide work experience and employment for those with convictions, and supporting the Business In The Community Ban the Box campaign, which aims to remove the criminal record checkbox from job applications at the initial stage of applying for a job.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) whether he has considered the impact of family and relationships on mental health; and (ii) how public services provide support and take account of the family unit when providing therapy for mental health.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to introduce no-fault divorce in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As my term as a caretaker Minister ends on 28 October I do not plan to legislate to introduce no fault divorce here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to outline the process for ensuring Relationships and Sex Education is part of the curriculum and delivered in all schools from all sectors; and (ii) whether this requires additional funding to deliver effectively.
answer_text: Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is already a compulsory part of the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding area of learning in primary schools and the Learning for Life and Work area of learning in post-primary schools.  It is a matter for schools to decide what and how they teach a range of curricular issues across all areas of learning provided they meet the legal minimum requirements set out in legislation.  A school’s teaching of RSE should reflect its ethos and RSE policy which should be agreed by its Board of Governors in consultation with parents and pupils.The Education and Training Inspectorate, through its inspection of child protection and safeguarding policies and practices, routinely assesses whether or not schools have an appropriate policy for the delivery of RSE within the curriculum.  Funding for the delivery of RSE in schools, as with any curricular subject, is provided through the Common Funding Formula. Schools are therefore responsible for meeting the cost of delivering the curriculum from within their delegated budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to streamline the process for divorce in Northern Ireland, comparable with other regions of the UK.
answer_text: Responsibility for the legislation relating to divorce in Northern Ireland sits with the Minister of Finance.Under the NI Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) modernisation plans, NICTS will undertake the development and delivery of a new core technology platform for courts and tribunals and a web portal for use by citizens and the legal profession. This will consist of the development of a range of new and enhanced online services, including divorce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the reasons for the delay in progressing the planned amalgamation of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College into a single, integrated school.
answer_text: On 9 March 2022, the Education Authority (EA) published a series of Development Proposals (DPs) in relation to the Causeway Area.  DPs 676–680 propose the closure of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College (IC) and the establishment of a new Controlled IC which will include Autism specific and Learning Support Classes.  The statutory two-month objection period concluded on 23 May 2022.My officials are currently collating all responses received by the Department, along with all other pertinent information and will provide me with a detailed submission to assist me in taking decisions that are in the best interest of all learners.Unfortunately, I am unable to offer a specific date for a decision.  I appreciate the importance of a timely decision regarding the proposals and I am acutely aware of the need for certainty for the interested parties.  I will not delay a decision any longer than necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 3207/22-27, to detail the ways in which she will improve the Private Rented Sector, so that it provides a safe and secure housing option for those that need it.
answer_text: Phase 1 of Private Rented Sector reform is encapsulated in the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022. Those areas that are particularly focused on safety and security relate to the introduction of: Minimum standards for electrical safety and the installation of smoke, heat, and carbon monoxide alarms; and Much longer notice to quit periods. Subject to the appropriate Committee and Assembly consideration, it is anticipated that regulations to operationalise all these improvements will be made by the end of the year. My future consideration of what needs to happen in Phase 2 of the reform programme will be focused on what further measures I can take to improve the safety, security, and quality of this sector. My officials have already started to re-engage with key stakeholders to review what is happening in other jurisdictions to inform this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what bereavement counselling services are currently available across Northern Ireland; (ii) where these are located; and (iii) what steps are being taken to offer and provide bereavement counselling services to anyone who has lost a loved one.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of recent excess death figures.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what plans he has to encourage and facilitate the expansion of renewable energy production.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer given to AQW 3805/22-27 answered on 12th October 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) how the disability employment services currently funded by the European Social Fund will be paid for after March 2023; and (ii) what conversations he has had with the Westminster government regarding its involvement in future funding of these services.
answer_text: The UK Government’s Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) pledges to replace EU Structural Funds. The Department for Communities (DfC) has responsibility for employability policy, including for those  people with disabilities. I understand that DfC is developing a new Disability Employment Strategy which will include  a long-term commitment to reducing the disability employment gap. I have had no conversations with the Westminster government regarding future funding of disability employment services as this responsibility rests with my ministerial colleague, the Minister for Communities. More generally, the Minister of Finance leads on the negotiation of EU replacement funding with the UK Government. However, going back as far as 2018, DfE has been involved extensively with the series of Westminster officials involved in UK SPF. We have provided a substantial volume of evidence, information and data in an attempt to direct funding and have always been clear that replacement of ESF is a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the current criminal assets recovery powers, in particular that relating to drug crime; and (ii) to detail what plans she has to seek to strengthen these powers.
answer_text: Financial gain remains the principal driver of organised criminality and therefore asset recovery powers are vital tools in the fight against organised criminal activity in all its forms, including drug-related crime.  The asset recovery powers available to law enforcement bodies to deprive criminals of their proceeds of crime and prevent the further funding of crime are substantial and used effectively by relevant partners in Northern Ireland.  The Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (POCA) is the principal legislation for criminal and civil asset recovery powers and anti-money laundering provisions across the UK.  The powers available facilitate the recovery of assets by the confiscation of the proceeds of crime following a criminal conviction and by civil powers such as cash seizure and forfeiture, listed assets seizure and forfeiture, freezing and forfeiture of money in accounts, civil recovery action and tax recovery.  Operational organisations with asset recovery powers in Northern Ireland are represented on the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF).  The OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment (https://www.octf.gov.uk/publications/type/octf-annual-report-threat-assessments) records the operational response to disrupting the importation, distribution and sale of drugs by organised crime groups and reports on the asset recovery performance of all relevant partners.  The OCTF partners routinely prioritise asset recovery in both proactive and reactive operations.  The Home Office publishes an asset recovery statistical bulletin covering asset recovery performance in England and Wales and Northern Ireland.  Data linked to crime type/activity is not available: https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/asset-recovery-statistical-bulletin-financial-years-ending-2017-to-2022/asset-recovery-statistical-bulletin-financial-years-ending-2017-to-2022. Utilising the full suite of asset recovery powers is essential in the fight against organised crime; NI must be as robust as other jurisdictions in its response. I prioritised the commencement of the outstanding provisions of the Criminal Finances Act 2017, which in June 2021 introduced the asset recovery powers to seize and forfeit listed assets and freeze and forfeit money in accounts.  The use of these powers in Northern Ireland continues to grow and will be reported in the OCTF Annual Report and the Home Office statistical bulletin.  My Department continues to work with the Home Office on strategic and legislative approaches to enhance asset recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for an update on what conversations she has had with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council regarding the installation of CCTV in Portrush, with a view to increasing public safety and reducing crime in the town; and (ii) to outline what funding is available from her Department for such a project.
answer_text: I have had no conversations with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council regarding the installation of CCTV in Portrush and I do not routinely have conversations about the provision of CCTV as my Department does not fund town centre or public-facing CCTV cameras in any council area and has no funding mechanisms available for CCTV related projects.My Department in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board provides annual funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level. PCSPs through local engagement and working in partnership with designated partners including PSNI; local statutory bodies/agencies; the voluntary sector and local communities identify and prioritise local community safety and policing concerns and develop and deliver initiatives that help alleviate those local concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what work she has undertaken to create a minimum income for artists programme, similar to that in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware of the Basic Income for the Arts (BIA) programme currently being piloted in the Republic of Ireland. I am interested to see further reports on impacts, both in relation to individual artists and to the sector as whole.  Any future commitment as regards a similar scheme for Northern Ireland would depend on robust supporting evidence and funding availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the level of drug-related activity in Limavady; (ii) how this has changed in recent years; and (iii) to detail what resources her Department has provided to the area in order to reduce drug-related activity.
answer_text: Data on drug related and drug misuse deaths at local government district level is published by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency on behalf of the Department of Health and can be found at: Drug-Related and Drug-Misuse Deaths 2010-2020 | Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (nisra.gov.uk). The PSNI also provide statistics on drugs seizures across district council areas on a quarterly basis: Drug Seizure Statistics | PSNI From a criminal justice perspective, it is not possible to accurately assess the full extent of drug related activity as not all those engaged in such activity will come into contact with the justice system. Moreover, the assessment of, and response to, any criminal activity is primarily an operational matter for the Chief Constable. The PSNI has advised that under Operation DEALBREAKER, it will continue to work with partners to prevent drugs coming into our communities, protect the most vulnerable from harm, and pursue those involved in drugs criminality. My Department, through the work of the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF), supports the ongoing efforts of the OCTF drugs subgroup, which provides a forum for operational partners to monitor trends and respond to issues relating to illegal drugs.  	At a community level, Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board, take a lead on the operational response to these issues. 	Actions currently being delivered by Causeway Coast and Glens PCSP  include early intervention initiatives, increased engagement with local Drug and Alcohol Coordination Teams (DACTs), information sharing between the PCSP and PSNI, the holding of public PCSP meetings to raise awareness of issues, public information campaigns, educational programmes delivered to post-primary schools (including on drink-spiking) and the installation of RAPID bins.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether a person on the social housing waiting list, who subsequently secures a private rental property, is still able to use previously awarded Housing Executive points for homelessness in an application.
answer_text: The Housing Executive will have discharged its statutory duty to all Full Duty Applicant Households (those “accepted” as statutorily homeless) once a household secures a permanent tenancy with all associated points removed. Should the household subsequently find themselves homelessness or threatened with homelessness, they can present to the Housing Executive where a new assessment of their circumstances will be undertaken and if they meet the tests to be “accepted” as statutorily homeless will again be awarded full duty status and appropriate points.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how, amid cuts to the Rebuilding Support Scheme fees being paid to NHS dentists, he will ensure that (i) no NHS dentist is operating at a loss; (ii) everyone who needs NHS dental treatment is able to register with a dentist and receive timely care; and (iii) health service dentistry is future-proofed and sustainable.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average length of time taken by his Department to determine the outcome of a major planning application, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: From 1 April 2017 - 30 June 2022, there have been 32 major applications processed by my Department with 29 decisions issued and 3 applications withdrawn. The average processing time for these applications was 187.4 weeks. The data below from 2017/18 provides information for the last five full years and the first three months of the current year (April-June 2022), showing major planning applications processed to decision by my Department or withdrawn by the applicant.YearApprovedRefusedWithdrawnAverage processing time (weeks)22017/181020169.02018/19410383.22019/2020030.32020/21201350.42021/22600160.82022/23 (Apr-Jun 22)1112610.0Total2543187.4Source: NI Planning PortalNotes:1 Quarterly estimates for 2022/23 are provisional and will be subject to revision2 The time taken to process a decision/withdrawal is deemed valid to the date on which the decision is issued, or the application is withdrawn.  The median is used for the average processing time as any extreme values have the potential to inflate the mean, leading to a result that may not be considered as "typical".

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the average response time taken by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to review consent for effluent discharge for a property; and (ii) what actions are being taken to improve these times, particularly when the review is needed for a house sale completion.
answer_text: The average time taken to process applications for new consents, variations to existing consents or consent transfers in 2021/2022 was 13.5 weeks. This is a high-volume business area (averaging over 280 applications each month) and NIEA is continually seeking to improve service delivery.  In November 2020, NIEA introduced a portal for applying online for new consents and transfers.  Further reviews of processes and systems are ongoing under the Department’s Digital Transformation Programme to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of data management and service delivery.Applications linked with house sales may involve a new application for consent; a variation, or a transfer of an existing consent and the NIEA will seek to process urgent requests as soon as possible, dependant on the type of application and information required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for her assessment of the number of older people currently presenting and being accepted as homeless; and (ii) to detail what specific support exists for older people who find themselves homeless.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below outlines the number of presentations and acceptances from households within the pensioner household category. Pensioner Households Presenting/Accepted as Homeless 2022/23 & 2023/24Year2022/232023/24Presenters22002414Acceptances17291926  Any household who presents as homeless to the Housing Executive will be provided with support and assistance through Housing Solutions and Support Teams. This will include the identification of any support needs and subsequent engagement with other localised services to provide, as far as possible, wrap around support. This support can include floating support to prevent homelessness or temporary accommodation in cases where emergency accommodation is required.Floating Support is delivered on a short-term basis into the home and can prevent older people becoming homeless by resolving a housing crisis or ensuring one doesn’t develop.  For those who are seeking to move or find accommodation, floating support can also assist with applications, taking on a new tenancy and support with move in and setting up a new home. Peripatetic Support, delivered on a long-term basis, provides help and assistance to older people to enable them to continue to live independently in their own home for as long as possible.If an older person has a low-level support need, then they can also apply for Sheltered/Continued Independent Living Accommodation.  Sheltered/Continued Independent Living Accommodation usually includes a group of flats or bungalows, specially built and designed for older people, with support provided on site by a scheme coordinator during weekday office hours.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what plans his Department has to legislate on party walls and structures as recommended by the Northern Ireland Land Commission in its 2010 Report on Land Law; and (ii) to detail the existing laws that protect homeowners when an adjoining building is being demolished.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to legislate on party walls and structures.Homeowners and those carrying out works here continue to rely on rights they enjoy at common law, which include the right to repair any defective party structure, to pull down any party structure which is not strong enough to support any building which is to be built, and to replace it with a structure of sufficient strength. They may also cut away or take down any wall or building that overhangs a building owner’s ground in order to erect an upright wall.Homeowners are also given an opportunity to object to certain proposed building projects as part of the standard planning permission process

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total number of schools that have transitioned (i) to; and (ii) from, integrated status.
answer_text: Since 1991, 31 schools have transformed to Integrated status.  The detail in respect of the schools that successfully transitioned to Integrated status is set out in the table below:Nursery School Primary SchoolPost PrimarySchool NameTransformedSchool NameTransformedSchool NameTransformedBrefne 2021Carhill 1992Brownlow 1991Bangor Central 2022Portaferry 1995Fort Hill 1998Rathenraw 1996Priory 1998Annsborough 1997Crumlin 2007Bangor Central 1998Parkhall 2009Kilbroney1998Glengormley 2021Kircubbin 1998Carnlough 2001Glengormley 2003Round Tower 2003Glencraig 2004Groarty 2005Ballycastle 2007Cliftonville 2008Ballymoney 2009Fort Hill 2009Crumlin 2010Mallusk 2015Loughries 2016Killyleagh 2016Harding Memorial 2021Carrickfergus Central 2021Seaview 2021TOTAL 2TOTAL23TOTAL6There are no schools that have transitioned from Integrated status.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the volume of human milk donated in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on donations of human milk made in Northern Ireland is not held centrally within my Department. Western Health and Social Care Trust, which provides a milk bank service for neonatal and seriously ill babies to all five of the Health and Social Care Trusts, as well as some hospitals in the Republic of Ireland, was asked for input. The figures they provided are tabulated overleaf.Human Donor Expressed Breast Milk (DEBM) received in litres from 2018 to date1YearDEBM received (in litres)2018378.47220191136.020201315.220211919.720221284.8Source: Women and Children’s Directorate, Western Health and Social Care Trust12022 data is up until 2 October 2022 only.2During 2018, operations in the facility were suspended because of the discovery of pseudomonas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the volume of blood donated in each of the last five years.
answer_text: As the data requested is not held centrally within my Department, the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service (NIBTS) was asked for input and their data is provided in the table below.Units of whole blood donated to the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service from 2017/18 to 2021/22Donation2017/182018/192019/202020/212021/22Whole Blood43,20743,31940,25336,76440,067Source: Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service.I hope that you will find this information useful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) what other completed or planned visits by foreign travel agents have been facilitated by Tourism NI, further to that with representatives from the Indian travel agent sector; and (ii) how the success of such visits can be assessed.
answer_text: Tourism NI, working in partnership with Tourism Ireland, undertakes an extensive programme of fact-finding or familiarisation visits for tour operators and travel agents from around the world, so that they can experience the superb tourism product in Northern Ireland for themselves at first-hand. The objective is to increase awareness of Northern Ireland among influential travel professionals, to help Northern Ireland secure a greater share of their business, and to ensure that they can sell the destination to their customers with more enthusiasm and authority when they return home. During 2022 so far, Tourism NI has welcomed tour operators and travel agents from various global markets. In addition to the Indian familiarisation visit to which you refer, Tourism NI hosted 65 tour operators in March 2022 on a programme of 5 familiarisation trips right across Northern Ireland as part of Tourism NI’s annual flagship travel trade event, Meet the Buyer.  In May, Tourism NI facilitated a week - long familiarisation trip for the Education Travel Consortium’s Executive Advisory Council. The Council members include senior travel planners from nonprofit educational travel providers, and US tour operators.    In September, Tourism NI supported 4 operators from the Gulf Cooperation Council market to promote luxury products and experiences in Northern Ireland to target their high-net-worth clients.  In addition, earlier this month, Tourism NI welcomed a further 4 separate familiarisation trips for 48 travel agents from Australia/New Zealand, Austria, Belgium & Canada.Next month, planned familiarisation visits will include 23 travel agents from the Canadian and Spanish markets. November will also see Tourism NI once again host the ITOA (Incoming Tour Operators Association of Ireland) Workshop which will take place in County Fermanagh. There will be 2 familiarisation visits associated with the event which will see approximately 50 operators taking in products and experiences across Northern Ireland.The success of activity with the travel trade overseas can be measured by increased awareness and knowledge of Northern Ireland; increased programming of the destination in the tour operators’ programmes and brochures; and by increased bookings. Tourism NI in partnership with Tourism Ireland, conducts an evaluation of all such activity. The feedback is invaluable for informing product and industry development activities by Tourism NI, but also in gauging the future impact of the programme.  In 2022, a survey of buyers who had attended Meet the Buyer familiarisation trips indicated that:•	97% said the visit would encourage them to promote NI to their clients•	95% said it would allow them to better programme NI•	97% said they will increase programming of NI in future as a result of the visit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of a loneliness strategy.
answer_text: Currently, Northern Ireland does not have a specific strategy to address loneliness, however it is acknowledged as an important but cross cutting issue that no one organization can tackle working alone. It requires a collective response and a collaborative approach involving all sectors, central government, agencies, voluntary and community sector and beyond. As a society we need to give the issue of loneliness greater priority, given its impact on society’s health and wellbeing.  My department has established a small loneliness working group who are responsible for collecting information and consulting with colleagues in the DoH, other NICS departments as well as the wider HSC family. We continue to engage with these departments and organizations through an established interdepartmental and regional HSC Trust loneliness forum.My Department has completed a scoping exercise inclusive of a literature review across the Northern Ireland to identify how the issue is being addressed in existing policies and services across a variety of agencies. This work will now continue in the form of a loneliness framework, which will have a focus on health and well-being and will support HSCTs and those they work with to address loneliness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what plans he has to improve road and pedestrian safety along the A2 near Dunluce Castle, where people often stop cars and coaches in order to take photographs; and (ii) what conversations he has had with the PSNI regarding this.
answer_text: My Department was a key stakeholder in the development of the Causeway Coast Visitor Management Strategy led by Tourism NI which was looking at how visitors to a number of attractions around the Causeway Coast, including Dunluce Castle could be better managed. I understand proposals are now being developed as part of the Regional Growth Deal and my officials are fully engaged in this process.  In the past my Department has highlighted the safety issues in consultation responses to licence applications from cross-border tour operators and has had some success in influencing their actions at this and other locations.  Discussions have taken place with PSNI Road Policing over several years regarding concerns about safety at this location and it was concluded that the introduction of restrictions at locations where tourists currently stop could potentially result in them stopping in more hazardous positions with associated implications for road safety. My Department is not aware of any change to the PSNI’s views on this issue.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the review into eligibility criteria for free school meals and the school uniform grant; and (ii) to detail what consideration she has given to adopting a ceiling price for school uniforms, similar to rules made in England.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria. The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. My Department continues to develop a costed options paper and it is intended that a public consultation exercise will take place later in the year.  In relation to the cost of school uniforms, I have instructed officials to identify an approach that will seek to ensure, on a statutory basis, that schools are required to make their uniforms more affordable for families. As part of this process, officials will be considering the introduction of a maximum price on the costs of uniforms, however the statutory guidance that now applies on the costs of school uniforms in England and Wales does not include any such price restrictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what safeguards are in place to ensure the fairness of health assessments carried out for Personal Independence Payment claims by private companies.
answer_text: Departmental officials monitor and manage the Personal Independence Payment (PIP) assessment provider’s (Capita) assessment process.A monthly Independent Customer Satisfaction Survey is undertaken. The Department’s Health Assessment Advisory Team also undertake a programme of clinical audits and assessment observations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the current asbestos removal regulations, in particular in relation to the cost to the property owner; and (ii) how these costs impact the level of illegal and potentially hazardous removals and disposals.
answer_text: (i)The Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012, associated with the removal of asbestos from privately owned properties, is the responsibility of the Health & Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI), an Executive Non-Departmental Public Body sponsored by the Department for the Economy. Consequently, it is not for my Department to give an assessment of these regulations.The movement of hazardous wastes, including those containing asbestos, is regulated under Hazardous Waste Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005 for which my Department is the enforcing authority. These regulations set out a system for identifying and then recording the movement of waste deemed to be hazardous. The aim of this is to ensure that waste is recovered or disposed of whilst safeguarding human health and without using processes or methods (including its transportation) which could harm the environment Under these regulations my Department (NIEA) charges a fee of £24 for the supply of each serial consignment number to consignors of hazardous waste (such as asbestos containing wastes) for use on the consignment note that tracks the waste movement. This fee has not been increased since it was inaugurated in 1998.Further, as noted in my previous related reply (AQW 2135/22-27) a private householder may bring properly packaged asbestos containing wastes to a licensed site themselves without the need for a consignment note (they will need to contact the site in advance of travelling to find out whether any specific conditions apply) in which case no fee would apply.All other costs associated with the removal and disposal of asbestos, i.e. contractor labour costs incurred in dismantling/removal and packaging the asbestos, its carriage costs and waste transfer station/landfill site charges are set on a commercial basis by the waste industry. (ii) Asbestos is a product historically used in the Building Industry. As such illegal disposal of asbestos or asbestos containing wastes (ACW) reflects general trends in the building industry. The more building/renovation/demolition activity taking place the more ACW is generated to be disposed of, either legally or illegally.NIEA operates a Flytipping Partnership with 7 Councils. Under this ACW that has been fly-tipped may be reported to NIEA for collection and disposal. NIEA can see no discernible trend in the amount of fly-tipped ACW collected and disposed of year on year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) foster carer allowance rates in Northern Ireland, when compared to those in other parts of the UK and with reference to the current cost of living crisis; and (ii) how these rates are affecting the number of (a) new; and (b) continuing foster carers.
answer_text: (i) An uplift to foster care allowances in Northern Ireland was applied in August 2022, aimed at closing the gap between rates payable in Northern Ireland and comparable rates in other parts of the UK. This resulted in an increase of approximately 3 per cent on 2021/22 rates, backdated to 1 April 2022. This brings allowances in Northern Ireland in line with national minimum allowances rates payable in areas of England outside London and the South East. In addition to basic allowances, foster carers also receive additional allowances, such as for birthdays, Christmas, holidays and outings. These other allowances have also been uplifted in line with the increases applied to the basic rates. I recognise that foster carers, like all families, will be experiencing increases in the cost of living. Regrettably, my Department is facing a significant financial shortfall and the uplifts applied to foster care allowances in 2022/23 represent what is affordable within these significant financial constraints.(ii) There are approximately 2,800 foster carers in Northern Ireland, providing much needed care and support to the vast majority of children in care. At a time when we have more children in care than ever before, it is vital that new carers are encouraged to come forward, and that those currently caring for a child are supported to continue in their role.  In 2021/22, the regional Fostering and Adoption service received 652 enquiries from prospective foster carers, an increase of 2.5% on the previous year. In addition, more than 500 enquiries were prompted by a recent campaign seeking carers and hosts for refugee children and young people. During my time as Health Minister I have supported a number of recruitment campaigns to encourage new carers to come forward and have provided additional financial support to help foster carers meet the challenges of the pandemic and sustain foster placements. The foster carers I meet are clear that financial rewards are not their primary motivation, and it is important to recognise that a range of other factors—such as the support provided by social workers and being seen as a valued member of the team around the child—are also essential in promoting and sustaining foster care.  Nevertheless, I understand that rises in the cost of living will cause additional challenges for many. My Department is continuing to engage with foster carers and their representatives—including through the ongoing Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services—about their experiences, but I regret that within the current budget constraints it is not possible to provide additional financial support at this time. I will, of course, keep this under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1151/22-27, for an update on the Public Health Agency’s urgent formal review of the pathway to remobilise adult day-care, short breaks and transport services.

To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to liaise with the Minister for the Economy, prior to the Open golf tournament in Portrush in 2025, with a view to limiting the number of local people evicted from long-term rental properties by landlords wishing to take advantage of short-term lets during the tournament.
answer_text: I am aware that short term lets can impact on the availability and affordability of properties for longer term rent by individuals and families.  I plan to meet the Minister for Infrastructure in the near future about my Housing Supply Strategy. One of the issues that I will raise with him during this discussion will be if and how Councils may be given the power to restrict the volume of short term lets via their planning responsibilities, particularly in areas of high housing need and demand. I am also undertaking work to improve security of tenure for households in the private rented sector by introducing much longer notice to quit periods. The requirement to provide these longer notice periods should also act as a disincentive for landlords to switch between long term and short term lets in the circumstances you describe. The Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 specifies that regulations on exceptions must be in place before these longer notice periods can come into operation. I anticipate these regulations will be ready for consultation in late Summer and, subject to a positive response, will be in place by the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1467/22-27, to detail what feedback he has had from the domiciliary care sector regarding the recent increase in secondary rate mileage.
answer_text: Whilst there has been no formal feedback received to date, I am aware that increased fuel costs remain a concern across the region.Staff may access the secondary rate once they have exceeded the 3,500 mile threshold, which will not be the case for all domiciliary care workers. I have been advised that this secondary rate has been welcomed and gone some way in stabilising services.With the lack of a current Executive budget, any decision I make to commit further funding is at risk. I fully appreciate that staff employed within HSC may feel that all mileage allowances should be increased, however I took the decision to target an intervention at those who travel the most frequently and who are often the lowest paid.As you are aware, these mileage rates do not apply to staff employed in the independent sector, where mileage rates are set at individual providers’ discretion. Trusts are in regular contact with representatives from Domiciliary Care Independent Sector Providers, where providers have the opportunity to highlight pressures on service delivery.Independent sector providers continue to raise the impact that fuel costs are having on the ability to maintain existing staffing levels, as well on the recruitment of additional staff to the workforce.I recognise that the domiciliary care sector continues to face significant difficulties. My officials continue to engage with Trusts and the sector on ongoing challenges, including those arising from travel and the cost of living. However, given the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty, there is limited room for manoeuvre in terms of additional funding

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what plans he has to introduce cancer prehabilitation programmes into all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: There is a growing evidence base to suggest that prehabilitation (i.e. multiprofessional interventions ahead of treatment) can lead to reduced length of stay, reduced complications of treatment, improved patient experience and outcomes over the long-term.  The commissioning of prehabilitation services across the region is a priority and as such is identified as a key action within the Cancer Strategy (Action 17).   While there have been no prehabilitation services commissioned to date, there are a number of pilots ongoing at trust level.  As part of strategy implementation, Macmillan Cancer Support is supporting the development of a new post within the Northern Ireland Cancer Network, the Regional Patient Pathway Integration Lead (currently recruiting – hope to have in post by the new year).  Macmillan has also invested in prehabilitation leads in each trust (all to be in post by the new year).  These posts will work with the regional lead to engage with stakeholders, including patients, to shape the development of prehabilitation services in Northern Ireland.  The regional lead will:scope current trust pilots to understand what is currently available and to identify the regional learning;to work across the professional groups to agree the appropriate model for provision in Northern Ireland and to understand how this might be prioritised and phased; and to develop a clear understanding of the what is required to support the development of regional prehabilitation services in terms of pathway development, changes to current working practices, governance, workforce and funding. This work will allow wider engagement on the issue and importantly will provide a roadmap and plan for the delivery of services within Northern Ireland.  That plan will allow us to understand what opportunities there may be to provide services within existing resources and to inform how we might prioritise future funding that may be made available to support strategy implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the capacity level that respite and daycare services for learning disabled adults are operating at, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Information regarding service provision is currently being collected by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to support the remobilisation of services. The current capacity levels regarding respite and day care services are not available broken down by constituency, however the Learning Disability data on the DoH website provides information regarding current service provision/capacity levels in terms of daycare and respite/short breaks per Trust.As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, respite/short breaks and transport. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. Data is baselined against service provision in February 2020, which is defined as pre-pandemic capacity. Data across all Programmes of Care is analysed with the data for Learning Disability currently being published monthly on the Department of Health website as noted below:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  It is regretable that services have not yet been fully restored to pre pandemic levels and I sincerely apologise to those individuals, families and carers. Service remobilisation will continue to be an area of focus.A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. Aligned to this process, SPPG engage in performance meetings with Trusts to discuss any noted barriers to full day care, respite/short break service remobilisation and to agree mitigating actions to support continued progress towards full remobilisation. Furthermore, I have committed to issuing periodic communication to MLAs to outline the progress made by Trusts towards a return to full service remobilisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the region plans to attract the level of large scale renewable energy projects that are needed to meet Northern Ireland’s Climate Act target of 80 per cent renewable electricity consumption by 2030.
answer_text: A crucial mechanism to meet the Climate Change (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 target of 80% renewable electricity consumption by 2030 will be establishing a support scheme to incentivise renewable electricity development in Northern Ireland. As per the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022, my Department intends to consult on renewable electricity support mechanism options this year, with a view to delivery in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy considering the energy and cost of living crisis, how Northern Ireland will encourage the build-out of renewable energy required to increase indigenous energy generation and secure future energy independence and protection from volatile global markets.
answer_text: My Department is working towards a target of 80% renewable electricity consumption from a diverse range of renewable sources by 2030 to deliver clean, affordable, locally produced electricity for Northern Ireland. To encourage sufficient investment to meet this target we will be establishing a support scheme to incentivise renewable electricity development in Northern Ireland. As per the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022, my Department intends to consult on renewable electricity support mechanism options this year, with a view to delivery in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the review of the Community Development element of the Community Support Programme; (ii) to outline the terms and conditions of the review; and (iii) to detail her plans and timeframe to progress this area of community and voluntary support.
answer_text: The review of the community development element of the Community Support Programme paused when Council and departmental officials were working with voluntary and community sector partners to deliver the  community response to the pandemic.Officials are now actively reviewing our evidence base, working with Councils to increase our understanding of Sectoral needs and consider new ways of working together, to inform the future delivery of support to the Sector through improved relationships and partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of how availing of the power to set corporation tax level could improve Northern Ireland's economic forecast.
answer_text: The Independent Fiscal Commission’s report published in May 2022 indicated that, in its view, there is value in completing the devolution of Corporation Tax, but that there are a range of issues that would first need to be agreed between a restored Executive and the British Government if this were to be pursued. This would need to include an updating of previous estimates of both the potential costs and benefits of a devolved Corporation Tax policy for the North given that these are now extremely dated. And where the economic, political and global landscape has also changed greatly in recent years. Significant analysis would be required in this regard before any assessment of the economic impact could be made. It will be for an incoming Finance Minister to bring recommendations on the Commission’s report to a restored Executive for its considerations on the full suite of devolved tax proposals, including Corporation Tax. These considerations will also be informed by the evidence gathered from the recent consultation I launched on the 4th October (www.finance-ni.gov.uk/consultations/devolution-fiscal-powers) on the devolution of fiscal powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the direct and knock-on effects of the cost of living crisis on rural communities; and (ii) to detail what plans he has to address the effects.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
