question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what plans he has to provide funding or assistance to charities and councils that relied on the former Dogs Trust neutering scheme for the neutering of surrendered or stray dogs; and (ii) for his assessment of the knock-on effects of the cessation of this scheme.
answer_text: I am aware of the changes to the neutering scheme operated by Dogs Trust in Northern Ireland which follows similar changes made in the rest of the United Kingdom.  I understand that the neutering voucher scheme in Northern Ireland has been re-focused to direct help to dogs in the rescue system. This re-focused scheme is being piloted for one year and reviewed by Dogs Trust. The decision to change the scheme is a matter solely for Dogs Trust. My Department has no plans to provide funding or assistance to charities or councils for neutering dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the size of the social housing waiting list in each constituency; and (ii) the estimated time the current waiting list in East Londonderry will take to clear, given the current level of investment, new builds and re-housing rates.
answer_text: The Housing Executive have provided the table below detailing the number of waiting list applicants by Parliamentary Constituency. Figures are as at the end of June 2022.Parliamentary ConstituencyAll ApplicantsBelfast East2097Belfast East/South404Belfast North4383Belfast South2834Belfast South/Strangford124Belfast West3821Belfast West/North134Belfast West/South24East Antrim1721East Derry 2534Fermanagh South Tyrone1924Foyle4463Lagan Valley1984Mid Ulster1337Newry & Armagh2223North Antrim2288North Down1887South Antrim1589South Down2123Strangford1642Upper Bann2777West Tyrone1916Grand Total44229Please note that where there are less than 10 applicants or allocations, they have been listed as <10 in compliance with Data Protection.It is not possible to put a timeframe on the eradication of the social housing waiting list. The rate of progress in reducing numbers of households in housing stress will be determined by long term policy and investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans she has to introduce or re-introduce domestic energy saving grant schemes and initiatives to help people use less oil, gas and electricity while keeping their homes warm.
answer_text: My Department continues to deliver energy efficiency grant schemes in partnership with the Housing Executive and Local Councils. 	The Affordable Warmth Scheme targets low-income households in the owner-occupied and private rented sectors most at risk of fuel poverty.  It includes multiple energy efficiency measures such as loft and cavity wall insulation, installation of gas or oil, boiler replacement, replacement windows and solid wall insulation. The Boiler Replacement Scheme, open to owner-occupiers with an annual household income of less than £40,000, provides a grant of up to £1,000 towards the cost of replacing old and inefficient boilers over 15 years old.The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding and support that has been provided by his Department to the Air Ambulance, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Helicopter Emergency Medical Service (HEMS) service is provided by way of partnership between Air Ambulance NI (AANI) and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS). AANI is responsible for the non-medical running costs of the service, such as aircraft lease, pilots and airbase.  NIAS provides the clinical support to the HEMS service.NIAS contribution to the HEMS service includes Medical and Paramedic staff and associated medical equipment and supplies.  The service operates broadly 7 days a week, 365 days of the year during daylight hours and weather permitting.  The costs are variable but the total is in the order of  £1m per annum since 2017-18 to date.AANI is a charity that relies on fundraising to meet the running costs. Over the last 5 years Air Ambulance NI received direct funding of £1m from DOH as grant funding to support the service in 2020/21 during the Covid-19 pandemic. In addition, in 2016 AANI was successful in an application to HM Treasury for a start-up grant and received £4.49m (2016/17 £1.09m, 2017/18 £1.44m and 2018/19 £1.96m), this funding was received from HM Treasury and subsequently was issued to AANI from NIAS.

answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the table below and it shows the number of unoccupied Housing Exec properties, as of 1/9/22, broken down by Parliamentary Constituency:ConstituencyActionable VoidsNon-Actionable VoidsTotalsBELFAST EAST88148236BELFAST NORTH83197280BELFAST SOUTH5154105BELFAST WEST362561EAST ANTRIM15102117EAST DERRY161632FERMANAGH AND SOUTH TYRONE91120FOYLE135164LAGAN VALLEY60101161MID ULSTER161026NEWRY AND ARMAGH104252NORTH ANTRIM373774NORTH DOWN131427SOUTH ANTRIM23932SOUTH DOWN151328STRANGFORD162036UPPER BANN96069WEST TYRONE835435189451463* Actionable voids are properties which are vacant while in the process of being allocated/awaiting change of tenancy repairs. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the steps he is taking to ensure adequate numbers of doctors and nurses within the NHS following the recent British Medical Association survey highlighting dissatisfaction within the workforce, in particular regarding the issue of pensions.
answer_text: My Department published the comprehensive Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People in May 2018.  The aim of the Strategy is that ‘by 2026, we meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce’.The Strategy’s second action plan was published on 15th June 2022 to cover the period 2022/23 to 2024/25. The second action plan was developed collaboratively with stakeholders during 2021/22 and provides the strategic direction for the next three years to meet our workforce needs and the needs of our workforce through 32 identified actions and 94 associated outputs that focus upon the attraction, recruitment, training and retention of our workforce.  In this regard, I am pleased to report that, despite the current financial constraints facing my Department, I was able to secure in 2022/23 expansion in a number of medical specialty programmes delivered by NIMDTA. This included the Oncology, Radiology, Paediatric Cardiology, Vascular Surgery, Transplant Surgery, Palliative Medicine and Dermatology programmes.I was also able to increase the GP training programme this year by 10 places; building on continuing work to invest in our GP workforce which has seen the number of GPs trainees increase by over 70% from 2015 levels.In addition, my Department is committed to sustained investment in growing the local nursing and midwifery workforce to meet ever increasing demands.  This year we have commissioned the highest ever number (1,335) of pre-registration nursing and midwifery training places. This includes the final 300 places, who are due to commence their studies in September 2022, of the additional 900 New Decade New Approach training places; the first cohort (300) of the additional 900 nurses are due to have completed their training and be available for use across HSC in June 2023"While retention of staff is an intended outcome of the actions identified across the Strategy, the second action plan contains a specific action to develop and deliver retention initiatives that are tailored to the requirements of individual healthcare professions including doctors and nurses.One issue which can impact doctors later in their career is pension tax charges and work is underway to introduce guidance for flexibilities which may help those who are negatively impacted.  There are currently a number of policy issues being considered regarding the impact of inflation rates and possible flexible retirement measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what plans he has, or work he has done, to explore linking the redeveloped Ulster Canal with Lough Neagh and the Lower Bann.
answer_text: Waterways Ireland has North South Ministerial Council approval to explore the restoration of the Ulster Canal from Lough Erne to Clones and work is currently underway on that stretch of canal.The restoration of the Ulster Canal from Clones to Lough Neagh remains a long-term goal and is an expressed commitment in both the Stormont House Agreement, A Fresh Start: The Stormont House Agreement and Implementation Plan and in the New Decade New Approach agreement.In the absence of an Executive and the North South Ministerial Council there are no plans, at present, to restore the stretch of canal from Clones to Lough Neagh, this matter will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what assurances he has been given that the Shared Prosperity Fund will comprehensively meet the gap in Northern Ireland’s business funding left by the cessation of EU funding sources, such as the European Social Fund and European Regional Development Fund.
answer_text: No such assurances have been given in recent times. The Department of Finance (DoF) leads a cross-departmental Future Policy & Finance Workstream that provides input to the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) on funding streams associated with the Levelling Up agenda, including the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). With our support, DoF continues to press for a substantive role for the NI Executive in design and delivery of these funds.We have continually repeated the message that activity currently funded by the European Social and Regional Development Funds should be prioritised under the SPF. NI Executive Ministers have also articulated that the SPF would be best delivered through local Departments, ensuring continuation of the success of these programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average time taken for personal injury settlements to be made to victims, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The average time from issue to disposal for personal injury cases in the Kings Bench and County Court in each of the last five years are detailed in the tables below.Kings Bench 20172018201920202021Personal Injuries Cases967711859604861Issue to Disposal (weeks)173157149157169County Court20172018201920202021Personal Injuries Cases5,2674,8974,7381,9463,801Issue to Disposal (weeks)5856525367The information is taken from the Integrated Courts Operating System.  Personal Injury Cases include Road Traffic Accidents, Negligence, Breach of Statutory Duty and Clinical Negligence.  The average time between issue and disposal excludes Default Judgements and Office DisposalsThe Northern Ireland Criminal Injuries Compensation Scheme provides for the payment of compensation to, or in respect of, persons who have sustained criminal injury. Each claim for criminal injuries compensation is unique and often complex.  In some instances, for example, significant medical information and other external information can be required to assess the application.  The ability to process those applications is limited by the ability of organisations to return information in a timely way.  The following table provides details of the percentage of initial decisions and review decisions that were made within 24 months of an application to the scheme or a request for a review decision in each of the last five years. Percentage of initial decisions and review decisions made in each financial year that were made within 24 months of an application to the scheme or a request for a review decision2017/1886%2018/1984%2019/2087%2020/2184%2021/2274%When a decision is made on an application for criminal injuries compensation an applicant has up to 90 days to ask for the decision to be reviewed.  If unhappy with the review decision, the claimant has up to a further 90 days to appeal the decision to the Criminal Injuries Compensation Appeals Panel for Northern Ireland (CICAPNI) which is independent of the Department of Justice.   It is therefore not possible to provide details of the average time taken for an award to a claimant as that will depend on when a review is requested by an applicant and on processes within CICAPNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in addition to the current schemes available, to outline her plans to ensure social and private homes are properly insulated to reduce energy costs.
answer_text: Improving the energy efficiency of our homes is a vital part of addressing fuel poverty and carbon reduction. Cavity wall insulation is recognised as a cost-effective way to reduce home energy bills, improve thermal comfort, and contribute to lower carbon emissions.My Officials are currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty energy efficiency scheme that will aim to assist low-income owner occupiers and households in the private rented sector. This new scheme will align with the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, Climate Change Act and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy. Insulation will be considered as a priority along with other energy efficiency and decarbonisation measures within the design of the new scheme.In relation to social homes, the Housing Executive has provided the following input.“The Housing Executive acknowledges that the thermal performance of its stock is poor and believes that the answer to this is a Fabric First approach to energy efficiency that focuses on better insulating its homes.  At present NIHE are addressing the insulation of stock in a number of ways:Upgrading loft insulation in tandem with replacement of heating systemsNIHE have just completed an external wall insulation (EWI) programme for 1,400 of its non-traditionally constructed properties via the Energy Efficiency in Social Housing programme that was part-funded by the European Regional Development Fund, and are following this with a small EWI programme as funding permitsDeveloped an initial cavity wall insulation (CWI) remediation/replacement programme of some 9,000 dwellings that will be delivered over the next three years in a drive to address instances where the condition of the CWI is having a detrimental impact on the structure of a property.The first schemes in NIHE’s innovative 300 Low Carbon Retrofit Programme have recently started on site and will involve the installation of new CWI along with other energy efficiency measures in some 300-400 homes in a number of locations across Northern Ireland by the end of 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she plans to provide support to community and voluntary groups to support their local communities during the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she has plans to (i) offer free school meals to all children during the cost of living crisis; and (ii) support schools to offer or extend breakfast and after-school clubs.
answer_text: The Department is continuing to progress a review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria. The review is considering a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meal for some/all year groups as well as raising the current income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit, Working Tax Credit or Child Tax Credit. It is planned that a public consultation will commence later this year. The Extended Schools programme budget is £9.1m per annum, which allows the most disadvantaged schools to provide a range of interventions and additional learning opportunities outside of the traditional school day.  Many schools choose to offer breakfast and after-school clubs to pupils.The budget is fully committed at present and there are no plans to expand the programme.  Any decisions regarding funding of the programme in future years must be taken in the context of available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline services which are offered at Treatment Rooms in Primary Care practices; (ii) whether each service has been additionally resourced; and (iii) what feedback he has received from primary care practitioners about Treatment Room services.
answer_text: (i) While some practices manage their own treatment room the majority of treatment rooms are managed and supported by local Trusts. Treatment rooms across Northern Ireland offer a range of service to the GP practices and patients locally.  In general Treatment rooms may provide some phlebotomy, urine testing, ECGs, wound management/dressing. Some will carry out base line observations such as blood pressure readings. Some might assist with immunisation (children and adult) and near patient testing for INR monitoring. Some support GPs with minor surgery. All would be trained and supportive to manage a cardiac arrest scenario with basic life support. Practices may employ their own health care assistant (HCA), Practice Nurses (PN), Advanced Nurse Practitioners to compliment the treatment room service. Often practice employed HCAs and PNs will be involved in the monitoring and management of patients with known chronic disease (Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease, Asthma, COPD etc as well as ensure safe monitoring more complex medication such as Disease Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARDS) and Warfarin.(ii) Treatment Rooms are not resourced for individual services provided, but rather for the full range of services to patients.  Treatment Rooms are resourced by local Trusts.(iii) GP Practices value their nursing and Health Care Assistant colleagues within Treatment rooms.  However, in some localities across the region, practices have reported difficulty with access to Treatment Rooms for their patients. Some practices have also reported that complex dressings from secondary care can take up significant time in Trust treatment rooms impacting on primary care access.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has plans to cancel or defer domestic and non-domestic rates to reduce living and business costs during the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: Given the ongoing absence of an Executive it is not legally possible for me to act unilaterality to take forward new legislation to cancel or formally defer rates bills for domestic or non-domestic ratepayers. Such legislation would, as a minimum, require Executive agreement because these proposals could reduce the revenue that is required within central government budgets, or stop vital funding to Councils that rely upon rates as their primary source of income.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether all Multi-Disciplinary Teams across Northern Ireland include a social worker; and (ii) to outline reasons why some Multi-Disciplinary Teams do not have a social worker.
answer_text: To date, 68 practices have a Social worker (Band 7). This equates to 53.1% of practices within the current MDT footprint.Further roll-out of the MDT model is subject to available funding to support the delivery of the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his review into General Practitioner pensions and the ability to opt out of such schemes.
answer_text: As with all HSC Pension Scheme members, General Practitioners have the ability to opt out of the scheme at any point.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline reasons why pathology result wait times have, on average, increased; (ii) for his assessment of the impact of longer wait times; and (iii) what action he is taking to reduce these times.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he has plans to introduce e-prescribing in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead on a programme of work which is considering options for the replacement of the current written prescription system with an electronic prescribing system. Work is underway to identify and implement a suitable IT solution to progress this programme of work including ongoing engagement with the NHS in England over the potential use of their Electronic Prescribing Technology solution for the HSC in Northern Ireland. My Department continues to operate under significant budgetary and financial constraints at the present time however I remain hopeful that implementation of this project will be able to proceed, once a suitable IT solution can be identified and funding secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) how many GP surgeries are at risk of closing across Northern Ireland; (ii) the reasons why GP surgeries are at risk of closure; and (iii) the impact for patients if a GP surgery closes.
answer_text: As at 30 September 2022, 16 GPs surgeries are considered as being at risk of closure across Northern Ireland.  Reasons as to why this may be the case vary but include staffing difficulties, workload pressures and issues with the fabric/size of premises.  I recognise that any closure of a GP surgery is concerning for patients and potentially places additional pressures on neighbouring surgeries.I am committed to continuing to work to address the pressures facing GP practices.  On 26th September, I announced a £5.5m package of support for GPs.  This package provides targeted help to parts of the service that are most under pressure by investing £1m in a new Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme to help attract GPs in hard-to-recruit areas. It also boosts the support team for GP practices who are experiencing difficulty by an additional £680k.  In addition, it also includes investment of £3m to support practices across Northern Ireland through the winter period, giving them the capacity to provide additional in-hours sessions to help meet heightened demand through the winter; up to £800k additional investment in Out of Hours services; and a commitment to progress work to address the issue of GP indemnity.I recognise that being able to access GP services is very important for people across Northern Ireland.  My Department has therefore convened a working group to explore issues around access to services, including how technology can be better used to provide improved access for patients.  An initial report from the group is anticipated in the autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration he has given to making it mandatory for all new-build houses to include renewable energy sources, such as solar panels.
answer_text: An interim uplift of the Department’s Technical Booklet guidance to Part F (Conservation of fuel and power) of the Building Regulations was published in March 2022. This uplift supports regulation 43B (Nearly zero-energy requirements for new buildings) and has been carefully set so that on-site renewables, such as solar panels, are expected to form a key component of new houses subject to a building regulations application made from 30th June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what conversations he has had with the Westminster government regarding the potential for future windfall taxes on companies that have recorded an increase in profits as a result of rising food and energy prices.
answer_text: I have been urging the Treasury to implement a windfall tax on energy companies to help fund interventions to support vulnerable households and small businesses for some time. I re-iterated this point directly to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury when I met him just after the British Government’s fiscal event on 23rd September, and where I followed up in writing on this on 27th September. This also followed an unheeded joint letter that I, along with my Scottish and Welsh counterparts, sent to the Chancellor shortly after he was appointed calling for a windfall tax. More action is clearly needed to support both businesses and households through this cost of living crisis and I believe that this this should be funded in part by capturing the excess profits from the energy sector rather than passing the cost on to hard-pressed workers and businesses through higher borrowing. I will continue to press the British Government on this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people newly registered as homeless, per constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The most recent official statistics relating to homelessness were published on 15 September 2022;https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/system/files/publications/communities/ni-homelessness-bulletin-jan-june-2022.pdfHomelessness figures presented in this report can be read in conjunction with those published in the quarterly Housing Bulletins which can be found at;  https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what powers he has to seek the granting of greater repayment flexibility for businesses that availed of Covid Bounce Back loans, but are currently in arrears.
answer_text: I have previously urged Treasury and the local banks to ensure that the repayment of debt, such as Bounce Back Loans, is managed carefully to ensure that those who relied on this finance during the pandemic are supported and treated fairly.  It is also important for businesses who find themselves in the situation of not being able to make their repayments, to engage with their lender and discuss their particular circumstances and options.However, this is a Reserved Matter, with the rules and regulations for Bounce Back Loans falling under the responsibility of the British Government, and any decision to grant repayment flexibility for Bounce Back Loans will ultimately be for them to make.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people who called the Make the Call Service helpline, broken down by month, in the last five years.
answer_text: The earliest data available for calls made to the Make the Call Service helpline is from November 2017.From November 2017 to August 2022, Make the Call Service received 232,501 calls on the freephone advice line number. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the potential repercussions for teachers’ pay if they take (a) strike action; and (b) industrial action short of a strike; and (ii) the reasons for these repercussions.
answer_text: Teachers’ terms and conditions, including pay, are negotiated by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC).  Management Side of the TNC includes representatives from the employers, sectoral interests and the Department of Education.  Teachers’ Side of the TNC includes representatives from the five main teaching unions (INTO, UTU, NAHT, NASUWT and NEU).I understand that Management Side of the TNC has carried out an assessment of NASUWT’s industrial action and it has formed a view that teachers participating in the action short of strike as directed by NASUWT are not fulfilling the full extent of their contractual obligations and are thereby in breach of contract.My Department is not responsible for the management of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee although they are a key representative on it. Discussions are ongoing and I very much hope that Management Side and Teachers’ Side will come to a resolution as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in addition to the construction of more properties, to detail what other initiatives she is planning in order to house the approximate 44,000 households currently on the social housing waiting list.
answer_text: I have set out an ambitious and long-term plan to deliver for those in most housing need.  My plan includes: Revitalising the Housing Executive so that it can borrow, invest in its homes and, ultimately, start building again. Introducing reform of the social housing allocations system with the aim of ensuring that it is effective, fair and based on objective need; Improving the Private Rented Sector so it provides a safe and secure housing option for those that need it; Expanding the range of affordable intermediate housing options available, including developing a new intermediate rent product to provide an additional supply of good quality, well managed and maintained homes, which are affordable for lower income families; andDeveloping a Housing Supply Strategy to provide a framework for addressing those whole system issues that impact on housing supply and the delivery of the right volume and types of homes to meet our changing housing need and demand. Most recently this work has included a joint workshop with DFI and other stakeholders to examine potential strategic and tactical solutions in relation to wastewater capacity issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the options available to her to avoid budget deficits in her Department in the absence of an agreed Executive budget.
answer_text: My Department is very restricted in its ability to reprioritise or reduce spend due to the vast majority of its budget being required to address statutory and demand-led obligations. About 80% of the total Education budget is subsumed by staff costs.While work is ongoing to identify programmes of spend that could be scaled back or stopped, this will be extremely limited; and obviously any reduction in spend would invariably require cuts to essential frontline services that support the most vulnerable within our society, as well as risking breaching statutory obligations.My Department started the year with inescapable or pre-committed pressures estimated at a minimum of c£409m against the indicative baseline. This figure has now risen to c£550m. Therefore, without confirmed in-year budget cover well in excess of the indicative baseline, the Department is heading towards a significant overspend up to this amount. Even if the Draft Budget proposals are agreed to (which would give DE an additional c£161m above the indicative baseline), this would still lead to a very significant Departmental overspend of up to c£390m. Some of the most significant pressures relate to pay (c£188m) and Special Educational Needs (c£122m).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of (i) his Department’s final survey on the effectiveness of the Spend Local Scheme; and (ii) the overall success of the scheme.
answer_text: The NISRA COVID-19 Opinion Survey helped inform the Department on how well the various aspects of the Scheme were performing whilst it was operating and the findings clearly demonstrate the positive experiences applicants had applying for their Spend Local card as well as the overall benefit to local businesses of spend on the cards throughout Northern Ireland.The full set of survey results can be found at Northern Ireland High Street Scheme Survey October 2021 to March 2022 ReportThe High Street Scheme was a huge success injecting more than £136.5 million into the local economy, providing a much needed economic boost to our retail, hospitality and service sectors to help them recover from the devastating impact of the pandemic. Of this spend, £84.9m was spent in businesses required by Covid Regulations to close during 2020, providing a much needed boost to these businesses. The Department commissioned an independent research report by Retail Economics to assess the perceived value of the Scheme during its operation and found that it was comprehensively welcomed by retail, hospitality and leisure businesses in NI and was seen as an effective way to stimulate the economy, restore consumer confidence and safeguard jobs. The report concluded that 80% of retail and hospitality businesses agreed that the Scheme helped support small independent businesses with the majority reporting that the Scheme had a positive or very positive impact on sales against their expectations for the time of year; 80% of small retailers thought that the Scheme encouraged spending that was otherwise unlikely to have occurred and two-thirds (66%) of small businesses thought that it was a good use of public money.The Scheme was also well received by our local business representatives, with the Ulster Bank stating that the Scheme had undoubtedly contributed to the pick-up in demand after retail sales in Northern Ireland rose in November for the first time in four months. In addition, figures published by the Northern Ireland Retail Consortium (NIRC) in December 2021 showed a boost to the number of people going into shops or businesses in October being continued in November following the introduction of the High Street Scheme. The Report demonstrated that Northern Ireland’s footfall in November increased by 5.6 percentage points on the previous month. Northern Ireland’s shop visits were 10 percentage points higher than the UK average.My Department has commissioned a Post-Project Evaluation (PPE) of the Scheme.  The PPE has commenced and will assess the extent to which the Scheme was successfully implemented, achieved its original objectives and provided an economic stimulus for the local economy taking into account the challenges of delivering a scheme of this scale within a very tight timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what initiatives he is progressing to build on Coleraine High Street’s joint success in Retail NI’s High Street Heroes awards.
answer_text: I was honoured to be involved once again in the High Street Heroes campaign this year. Our retailers are the lifeblood of our economy, and it is right that they are celebrated for their excellence. I was delighted to see Coleraine awarded ‘High Street of the Year’ alongside Newtownards.My Department’s Vision for a 10x Economy was published in May last year. The vision places innovation at the centre of our economic action with the aim of enabling businesses of all sizes, stages and in all locations, to expand and grow. I also want to see more people and places participating and benefitting from this economic growth with better opportunities, quality jobs and higher wages available to all our people. The vision was only a first step in setting out our economic priorities. It will be supported by a series of publications to outline, in more detail, how we will transform our economy over the next decade. Officials are currently undertaking work to develop policies and interventions that will deliver the Economic Vision. The success of our high streets requires the involvement of many agencies and stakeholders. The Executive Office established a High Street Task Force, while other departments have lead responsibility for town regeneration, planning and licensing. From the perspective of my Department, I am confident that the actions arising from the vision will enable Coleraine to flourish economically and build on its well-deserved success.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail any positive outcomes resulting from conversations with the UK Government regarding the easing of immigration regulations for agri-food workers; and (ii) for an update on his assessment of labour supply in this sector.
answer_text: I met with the then, Defra Secretary of State George Eustice MP on 08 June 2022, to discuss a number of issues facing the NI agriculture sector, including labour challenges as I understand fully the impact that labour shortages are having on our agri-food sector. Disappointingly, the Home Office continue to be steadfast in their view that their immigration policy will not change and that all sectors requiring labour should focus their efforts on recruiting domestic workers and in investing where possible in automation. Over the past 18 months, I have had extensive and regular engagement with our agri-food stakeholders on the issue of labour shortages and they have indicated that despite offering competitive wages and other incentives, they have struggled to recruit all the workers they need.  They have collectively expressed concerns that insufficient appetite exists among our domestic workforce for the types of jobs they are able to offer. This is why, I continue to press the UK Government for changes to existing immigration policy because I realise that aspirations for increased domestic workers and use of automation will only be possible with time and investment.Despite the UK Government’s insistence that they are not open to considering new visa routes or easing their immigration regulations, they did announce a week after this meeting that they would be expanding their Seasonal Agricultural Workers (SAW) scheme from 30,000 working visas to 40,000.  They also agreed that a fifth of these additional visas would be allocated to the seasonal poultry sector. I greatly welcomed this development because I had made a recommendation for the inclusion of the seasonal poultry sector in this scheme based on my own engagements with NI stakeholders. Furthermore, I believe it does highlight that if there is enough evidence to justify a change that the UK Government have shown they are willing to make adjustments. On the second point regarding my assessment of labour supply in the NI agricultural sector, I want to be clear that unfortunately there is still a shortage problem, albeit that recently there has been less requests from stakeholders to meet and discuss the matter. However, this is because for many of these businesses the immediate focus has now switched to soaring energy and fertiliser costs and potential issues with Carbon Dioxide supplies.The labour shortages in the agri-food sector have been exacerbated by the fact that there are labour shortages being experienced in all sectors of our economy and this is coupled with a very low unemployment rate. The most recent published statistics show that UK unemployment rate for May to July 2022 was 3.6% which is the lowest rate since May to July 1974.  In Northern Ireland, our unemployment rate is even lower at 2.7% as of June 2022.  While it is great to see unemployment rates so low it is problematic for those businesses trying to fill shortages because there is a smaller pool of potential domestic empl
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what steps he is taking to ease production costs for agri-food business owners.
answer_text: Northern Ireland producers are facing unprecedented rises in input costs. We continue to monitor the impacts and have a range of actions in place to mitigate these impacts on primary production businesses.   		The ‘Rising Cost Taskforce’ (led by College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise with cross industry representation) continues to ensure the consistency of advisory messages and provides practical help and support. It is currently surveying farm businesses and the information gathered will inform targeted messaging over the winter months. Further practical on-farm information sessions to consider the continuing increasing input costs will be delivered by my officials over the winter months. The key concerns are energy costs, fertiliser cost and availability and expected increase in compound feed costs.		I recognise that there continues to be volatility in the market and the overall situation remains difficult given the scale of the input cost rises. From 01 	September 2022 I issued full Direct Payments of £286.9million to 97% of eligible applicants. This was 6 weeks earlier than previous years and should help assist our local agri-industry with cash flow. I have also delivered a pig sector support scheme to address some of the challenges affecting pig producers, with payments of over £1.2m issued to approximately 130 producers.		My Officials have significant ongoing engagement with industry stakeholders, Defra, other devolved administrations and the Department of Agriculture, Food and Marine (ROI) on those cross-cutting issues relating to reducing the impacts of volatility in price and supply of key industry inputs. I continue to engage regularly with UK Government at a senior level and drive home the importance of national collaboration on dealing with the impacts of the Ukraine crisis on our agri-food industry and future food security. I have written and met with the former Secretary of State, Rt Hon George Eustice on a number of occasions to reiterate my concerns regarding the unprecedented increases in input costs and the need for a range of measures, including financial assistance for UK farmers to support them through the crisis. I will be restating those concerns to the new Secretary of State for DEFRA, Ranil Jayawardena MP.		DAERA produces a weekly Food Observatory Report and a supporting Food Supply Impact Assessment to continuously assess the health of the food supply system for Northern Ireland. An escalation policy, to raise issues of concern through TEO, is in place so that we are prepared for both future crisis and long term sustainability issues associated with our food supplies.  A cross departmental food forum supports this work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps he is taking to relay to the Westminster government and EU the views of Northern Irish business groups and owners regarding the best way to resolve the issues around the NI Protocol.
answer_text: My officials have regular contact with NI businesses and business representative groups to understand the issues they are facing around the NI Protocol. I have consistently escalated these concerns directly to UK Government Ministers to ensure NI’s trading relationships with the rest of the UK and with the EU are well considered. 	It is the responsibility of the UK Government to negotiate with the EU.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans he has to introduce work policies for women experiencing symptoms of menopause or fertility treatment which may impact their ability to work.
answer_text: Good employers recognise the challenges that many women can face within the workplace when experiencing symptoms of menopause. They similarly recognise the potential impact undergoing fertility treatment can have on the ability of employees to work during this period. They have flexible supportive working policies in place such as homeworking, flexible hours, or compassionate leave. I would encourage all employers to offer such flexible and supportive working arrangements for their staff. The Labour Relations Agency, the Equality Commission and the Irish Congress of Trade Unions have published a guide to help promote equality in employment for women affected by menopause. All employers are recommended to consider this guide when developing their own workplace policies. 	The Labour Relations Agency, the Equality Commission and websites such as NIBusinessInfo and NIDirect all provide valuable advice and guidance for employers and workers on these and a range of other workplace issues. Employers are recommended to take these sources of information into account when developing their own workplace policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what steps he is taking to make conducting business more affordable for business owners amid the cost of living crisis, in order to limit the number of business closures.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressure businesses are facing across the board, primarily as a result of soaring wholesale energy costs associated with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. In the last year, since September 2021, the wholesale cost of natural gas has trebled (+194%), while the cost of coal (+104%) and home heating oil (+107%) has doubled. Coupled with ongoing global supply chain disruptions, these unprecedented increases in energy costs are driving inflation across the economy, including food and other commodities.As we are seeing across Europe and beyond, the magnitude of these global inflationary pressures means that it can only be tackled meaningfully by significant interventions at a national government level. That is why in recent weeks and months I have taken every opportunity to engage with UK Government to highlight the inflationary pressures being felt by households and businesses in Northern Ireland. Following these engagements, I welcome the UK Government’s commitment to deliver the much needed £400 Energy Support Payment Scheme in Northern Ireland. In addition, the announcement by the Prime Minister of an Energy Price Guarantee and her continued commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their bills over the coming months.  My Department will continue to engage with the UK Government and others to ensure these support measures reach Northern Ireland households and businesses as soon as possible.The outlook remains challenging but I am hopeful for the future. I am investing in those areas where we can gain the best long-term outcomes, which will help us build greater economic resilience for the future.  For example, I have published an energy strategy which will deliver a step change in how we move away from our reliance on overseas, imported fossil fuels. Alongside our investment in skills this will support the delivery of our ambitious 10X Economic Vision for the benefit of all our people.In practical terms, Invest Northern Ireland’s Energy and Resource Efficiency Support for businesses provides a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable local businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) why the total funding available via maintenance loans and means-tested non-repayable maintenance grants to Northern Irish students studying in Great Britain is much lower than that for a student from a similar economic background studying at the same university but domiciled in Great Britain; and (ii) what plans he has to review this.
answer_text: I have confirmed a 40% increase in the maximum student maintenance loan available to full-time undergraduate students, providing in the region of £55million in additional support to approximately 40,500 NI-domiciled students studying in the UK and Republic of Ireland from academic year 2023/24.In addition, my Department will undertake a full review of the higher education funding system, including the support provided to students such as maintenance loans, grants and other student support products, and the support provided to the sector, such as the level and mix of teaching grant and fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what guidance has been given to haulage companies bringing privately bought goods from Great Britain to Northern Ireland in relation to the obligation to request EORI numbers from the buyer.
answer_text: The Trader Support Service (TSS) provides functionality via the TSS portal that permits hauliers to act as the importer and use their EORI to move privately bought goods from Great Britain to Northern Ireland. There is no requirement for a buyer to provide an EORI number where the end user is a private citizen.TSS has shared guidance on this process directly with hauliers. Hauliers which have not received guidance should contact the TSS Helpline or raise a case via the TSS Portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) why the maximum maintenance loan available to Northern Irish students studying in Great Britain is lower than that for a student from a similar economic background studying at the same university but domiciled in Great Britain; (ii) whether raising the loan limit would come at any cost to his Department; and (iii) whether he plans to review this.
answer_text: I have confirmed a 40% increase in the maximum student maintenance loan available to full-time undergraduate students, providing in the region of £55million in additional support to approximately 40,500 NI-domiciled students studying in the UK and Republic of Ireland from academic year 2023/24.In addition, my Department will undertake a full review of the higher education funding system, including the support provided to students such as maintenance loans, grants and other student support products, and the support provided to the sector, such as the level and mix of teaching grant and fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of preschool provision, particularly in rural areas.
answer_text: Pre-school provision is an important phase of early education and learning for all children.  My Department’s aim through the Pre-School Education Programme is to provide a year of funded pre-school education for every target age child whose parents want it.  Pre-school education is non-compulsory, however, each year most parents - approximately 93% - apply for a funded pre-school place for their child. In each of the last eight years my Department has achieved its aim with at least 99.8% of children whose parents remained with the admissions process to the end receiving an offer of a funded pre-school education place in a setting of their preference.  A similar percentage of children living in rural areas (based on the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency definition of urban/rural) were placed in a setting of their choosing.    In the 2021/22 academic year, a total of 22,790 children attended funded pre-school education places across 699 pre-school settings.  Based on the NISRA definition, approximately 32% of funded places were in rural areas, across 302 pre-school settings. This is broadly in line with the population distribution based on the same definition.Information from the Education and Training Inspectorate also demonstrates the high quality of the pre-school provision we offer our young children.  Moving forward my intention is to build on this through the standardisation of the length of pre-school sessions and the implementation of the early years actions in the Fair Start Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether each child between the age of three and four years is entitled to a funded preschool place; (ii) why preschools have a limit on the number of funded places they can offer; (iii) why preschools may have to cut the number of funded places they offer; and (iv) how preschools who decline government funded children are then able to accept children if their parents pay for a place.
answer_text: Pre-school provision is an important phase of early education and learning therefore, it is my Department’s aim to provide one year of funded pre-school education for every target age child whose parents want it.  In the last eight years, at least 99.8% of parents who stayed with the admissions process to the end received the offer of a funded pre-school education place in a setting of their preference.  Funded pre-school places are provided through the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP), and accessed via a preference based admissions process. Operational decisions about the allocation and funding of non-statutory pre-school education places is the responsibility of the Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG).  This group comprises representatives from the statutory, voluntary and private sectors and is chaired by an officer or member of the EA Board.  It operates within strict parameters, allocating only sufficient funded places to meet the level of demand in local areas.  All non-statutory settings are allocated places for one year at a time, and no setting is guaranteed a minimum allocation, or one that will accommodate all first preference applications.  The EA PEG keeps the provision of funded places under constant review and may contact non-statutory pre-school settings directly to allocate additional funded places should they identify a need, e.g. where there is a shortfall of provision in a local area.  All non-statutory settings are registered as childcare providers under The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 and as such, have the flexibility to offer fee paying childcare places to parents if they wish, under the stipulation that the relevant requirements of the Minimum Standards are adhered to.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps he has taken to encourage more members of the Irish Traveller Community to pursue tertiary education.
answer_text: I believe tertiary education opportunities should be available to all, regardless of social or economic background.  Further education, higher education, apprenticeship and careers policy and procedure support this ambition.  The Careers Service, in particular, has a focus on addressing the needs of young people and adults facing, or vulnerable to, social exclusion – including those from the travelling community.  Through partnership working arrangements with all post-primary schools in Northern Ireland young people who are most at risk of leaving school early, or, at risk of not successfully progressing to education, training or employment are identified early in school and actively supported by the DfE Careers Service until they are settled into a positive and appropriate destination. The Careers Service has also established effective working relationships and referral arrangements with a broad range of statutory, voluntary and community sector bodies who have a shared interest in supporting clients vulnerable to social exclusion, including those from the travelling community.  Widening participation in higher education continues to be a priority for my Department.  We want to ensure that those most able but least likely can access and benefit from the higher education that is right for them.  To help facilitate this all higher education providers are required to spend a minimum of 10% of their additional tuition fee income on activities relating to widening participation.  While the Department has identified specific target groups for widening participation, as referred to in the ‘Access to Success’ strategy, higher education providers can also identify further under-represented and disadvantaged groups to target if there is an identified need.  Research commissioned by the Department looking at the widening participation landscape in Northern Ireland is currently being finalised and will inform future widening participation policy and actions.  I also want to continue to ensure the widest possible take-up of our Further Education and Apprenticeship offers, helping to link individuals to work.  I was therefore very pleased that in April 2021, a member of the Irish Traveller Community won the Department for the Economy’s Apprentice of the Year Award.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the guidance given to planners and councils regarding the potential over development of properties such as apartments, which are likely to be sold as tourist rentals or second homes.
answer_text: Relevant policy and guidance is contained in a range of Departmental publications, including the Regional Development Strategy 2035; the Strategic Planning Policy Statement; retained Planning Policy Statement 12: Housing in Settlements and Planning Policy Statement 7: Quality Residential Environments (and its associated addenda); Development Control Advice Note 8 “Housing in Existing Urban Areas; and, ‘Creating Places: Achieving Quality in Residential Developments’. These documents will be taken into account by councils in plan-making and decision-taking for all types of housing, including apartment development. As you will be aware, here in the North, housing policy is the responsibility of the Department for Communities. DfC has advised that there is limited up-to-date information available as to the extent and in what way second homes or short term holiday rentals impact on housing stress and homelessness. The need for improved housing-related data collection and analysis forms part of the ongoing work of the Housing Supply Strategy which is being led by DfC and on which DfI officials are and will continue to be engaged. Ultimately, councils have the important responsibility for setting a vision for the long term future development of their areas through the preparation of local development plans. In doing so councils have the flexibility to bring forward bespoke approaches to dealing with any local issues which affect their communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) full time; (ii) part-time; and (iii) agency staff nurses employed by the Health and Social Care Trusts in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline what steps he is taking, and advice he is giving, to reduce sedentary behaviour in desk and office-based workplaces, in particular those in the public sector; and (ii) for his assessment of those industries most affected by sedentary behaviour.
answer_text: (i) My Department, in conjunction with the Public Health Agency, works cross-departmentally with the Department for Communities in relation to their Active Living: Sport and Physical Activity Strategy and with the Department of Infrastructure’s Active Travel strategy. Alongside my Department’s A Fitter Future For All strategic framework, these strategic partners work together to promote the Chief Medical Officer’s Physical Activity Guidelines, which aims to improve physical activity across all parts of the population and to increase the numbers of people choosing to travel to schools and workplaces through active travel means. Programmes in place to address sedentary behaviours include the workplace health programme - ‘Work Well Live Well’ - which works with 250 businesses in Northern Ireland, with sedentary workers being a key target group. The workplace active travel programme ‘Leading the Way’ in Belfast and the North West supports people to use active travel modes of transport and to use this as a means to attain the CMO Physical Activity Guidelines. As part of this, the annual Active Travel Challenge invites workplaces across Northern Ireland to sign up to compete with other employers and to log active travel journeys during the period of the challenge.Finally, the Regional Health and Social Care Workforce Wellbeing group launched a Regional Staff Health and Wellbeing website to help promote the Chief Medical Officers’ Physical Activity Guidelines and hosts resources and recommendations to support staff and their families to incorporate physical activity into their daily activities.(ii) There is evidence to show that office workers are at risk of excess sedentary behaviour. A recent study using data from 360,047 adults from UK showed participants who reported more than 6 hours/day compared with less than 2 hours/day sedentary time had higher risks of 12 of 46 non-communicable diseases. These included ischemic heart disease, diabetes, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, asthma, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, thyroid disorder, depression, migraine, gout, rheumatoid arthritis & diverticular disease. Another study addressing health and sitting in 850,000 adults showed that, without changes to the levels of sedentary behaviour, 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous activity per day would be required to compensate for the added mortality risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what plans he has to make baby heel prick tests for spinal muscular atrophy available through the Health Service.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) advises ministers across the UK on making decisions related to screening and spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) is included in the list of conditions that they regularly review. Based on evidence considered by the UK NSC in 2018, the Committee does not currently recommend screening for this condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what steps he is taking to protect the Northern Ireland to Republic of Ireland milk trade in anticipation of any changes to the Northern Ireland Protocol.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the potential threat of rising avian influenza cases in wild birds to human and animal health.
answer_text: Avian influenza (AI) is primarily a disease of birds and the risk of transmission to humans is low. There is a very low food safety risk for UK consumers and properly cooked poultry and poultry products, including eggs, are safe to eat.		The need for stringent and effective biosecurity measures are of paramount importance to help prevent the spread of the disease from wild birds or another source to poultry. Individual site biosecurity remains a crucial mitigation factor.  		Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) officials continue to work closely with the Public Health Agency, local councils and the environmental non-governmental organizations on the issue of collecting and safely disposing of birds and providing advice to the public.		All keepers of birds are urged to critically review their biosecurity measures using the checklist provided on the DAERA website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average construction cost of a Housing Executive property in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the table below I have provided details of the total cost to deliver a new social housing unit based on the Departments Total Cost Indicator (TCI) methodology. It is based on the acquisition and works costs for a general needs 2 storey 3 person/2 bedroom house. I have broken the cost down by the 3 components that make up the TCI.Land Costs - This is the purchase price of land/property. It will include any developer contributions such as a levy to finance essential infrastructure works such as road improvements. Works - Main works contract costs, these include, soil stabilisation and decontamination, piling, road and sewer construction, major demolition works, as well as statutory agreements and associated charges e.g. road bond, connection charges.On Costs – These will include legal fees, disbursements and expenses, stamp duty, bank/building Society or other valuation and associated administration fees, fees for building control and planning permission.  Furniture provision including loose fittings and furnishings would also be included in this. TCI are regularly reviewed by the Department and are subject to consultation with the Federation of Housing Associations.
