question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of farm safety; and (ii) to detail what steps he is taking to improve safety on farms and throughout agriculture.
answer_text: Agriculture remains our most dangerous industry and has consistently held a poor safety record in relation to deaths and serious injury.Whilst Health and Safety is the remit of the Health and Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI), my Department continues to show commitment to the safety of our farm families and employees through significant work in a number of different ways. 	The Farm Safety Partnership (FSP), established in 2012, is currently undertaking the 2020-2023 Farm Safety Action Plan, concentrating on specific ‘Farm Safe Essentials’ aimed at reducing the number of serious and fatal incidents relating to frequently recurring causes. The FSP is comprised of representatives from my Department, HSENI, the Ulster Farmers’ Union, the National Farmers’ Union Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Clubs of Ulster and the Northern Ireland Agricultural Producers Association. The 'Stop and Think SAFE' farm safety multi media campaign was developed by the FSP to help tackle the high rates of serious accidents and deaths on Northern Ireland's farms. It raises the issues of the four main causes of fatalities on our farms - Slurry, Animals, Falls (from height) and Equipment. My Department continues to provide funding for this ongoing high profile and often hard hitting campaign.The FSP also developed FarmSafeNet, an online learning tool designed to raise awareness about farm safety. It provides a range of useful resources along with practical information, all to encourage farmers and those working on farms to think seriously about safety on their farm. In addition, the ‘Making it Safer’ tool, included in FarmSafeNet, allows farmers to carry out a simple risk assessment that can help them manage their farm in such a way that it is safer for themselves, families and employees. These online farm safety tools are an integral part of the application process for the Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme Farm Business Improvement - Capital Scheme. I strongly recommend that all farmers, farm family members and farm workers access these online tools which can be found at www.farmsafenet.org.Farm safety is a high priority across all programmes that are delivered through the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) and Health and Safety is a key component of these programmes. 	All new full-time Further Education agriculture students participate in the Yellow Wellies Farm Safety initiative which is delivered by the Farm Safety Foundation.  Health and safety is also a key feature of all practical activities undertaken by students during their course at CAFRE. In addition, the education and training provision on the safe operation of machinery at CAFRE has been further enhanced through the use of simulated driving technology. During July and August each year CAFRE provides a tractor driving training course for 13 to 16 year old young people. 132 young people participated on this course during July and August 2022. This course includes Health and Saf
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the knock-on effects of the availability of taxis; and (ii) to detail what steps he is taking to mitigate these effects.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North. This role requires balancing the needs of taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers, as well as drivers and operators.   My Department remains committed to working within this remit to play its part, alongside those in the taxi industry, to address the range of issues the industry faces.The taxi industry operates within the wider context of the existing economic situation and there are many factors that contribute to the current shortage of taxi drivers.  As we recover from the Covid pandemic potential drivers will compare the industry to other driving jobs, including those that do not require them to be responsible for managing running costs, or that involve unsociable hours. All of these factors influence the attractiveness of the industry, both to existing licenced taxi drivers that are choosing not to drive for the industry at present, as well as potential new recruits. You may wish to note that as part of its regulatory role the Department is currently consulting with the public on a review of taxi fares, which will finish on the 7th of October 2022.  This review has factored in a large increase for depot charges, 2022 fuel prices, and other increased costs such as insurance and labour.   We are actively encouraging all of those with an interest to respond to the consultation.  After this process has concluded the outcome of the review can be implemented, to help address the needs of drivers, operators and taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the disability employment gap in Northern Ireland, which currently stands at 42 per cent, compared to the UK average of 28 per cent.
answer_text: The disability employment gap in Northern Ireland is significant and needs to be addressed.  My Department already provides a wide range of support for people with disabilities across the further and higher education sectors, in our training, reskilling and upskilling programmes, as well as through the Careers Service.  We recognise that more needs to be done and my officials are working closely with the Department for Communities as they develop a Disability Employment Strategy for Northern Ireland. My Department’s 10X economic vision strives for growth through innovation and for this growth to be inclusive. It recognises the need to capture how our economic policy improves the standard of living and wellbeing throughout society. We are clear that we want to design our policies and programmes so that the rewards are distributed fairly across people and places, creating opportunities for all.In addition, my Department’s recently published Skills Strategy provides us with a key platform for driving forward responses to this type of under representation. The Skills Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion (SEDI) sub-committee of the Northern Ireland Skills Council has been established to look at access and participation for those in under-represented or disadvantaged groups. The aim is to identify barriers and advise on policy solutions and actions to help drive change. Disability and access to skills has been one of the first substantive issues considered by the SEDI sub-committee and this work is continuing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail any training or employment schemes currently available for people with disabilities; (ii) for his assessment of the success of previous schemes; and (iii) what other support his Department offers disabled people seeking employment.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) has lead responsibility for employment policy and employment programmes, including for those with disabilities, and is currently developing a Disability Employment Strategy for Northern Ireland, which my Department is contributing to. Minister Hargey would be best placed to respond on issues relating to existing or previous employment schemes and support for those seeking employment.In relation to training and skills, my Department provides a range of practical, financial, and other support for people with disabilities to support them as they obtain the qualifications and skills to compete in the employment market. Support is available within the further and higher education sectors, as well as for those participating on the vocational training programmes funded by the Department. Examples include the Disabled Students Allowance for Higher Education students, a focus on disability through the Higher Education Widening Access and Participation Plans, the Additional Support Fund in Further Education, extended eligibility and flexibilities on the Skills for Life and Work programme, and specialist support through the Disability Support Service contract. In addition, the 2014-2020 Northern Ireland European Social Fund Programme has a dedicated theme to support those with Disabilities with the projects designed to promote employment opportunities and enhance social inclusion among people with disabilities. My Department’s Careers Service also offer careers guidance interviews, during which any barriers to training or employment are discussed. Careers Advisors also participate in Transition Meetings, including significant follow-up work for young people with a disability at key transition points throughout their education and training journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he is aware of any central database or register of people banned from owning or working with animals, held by individual councils; (ii) for his assessment of how maintaining such databases could help better enforce such bans; (iii) to detail the legal obligations of the bodies responsible for enforcing these bans; and (iv) for his assessment of how animal ownership bans could be better enforced.
answer_text: Local councils are responsible for prosecutions in respect of the welfare of companion animals. They do not hold a database or register of all persons in Northern Ireland banned from owning animals. These details are recorded on the criminal records system which is overseen by the Department of Justice. Local councils do, however, use their own information system to record details of any companion animal welfare investigations they carry out, including any conviction secured and the penalties imposed. The criminal records system holds the information that would be needed to establish a register of people prohibited from owning or working with animals. However, as any register would be animal welfare related, the Department of Justice indicated that my Department should lead on exploring whether or not establishing one is feasible. Detailed research by my Department and its liaison with the Department of Justice has recently regrettably confirmed that the creation of a register of animal welfare offenders is untenable.In particular, data supplied by the Department of Justice from the criminal records system does not indicate that the number of persons breaching bans on the owning or keeping animals is in anyway significant; during the period from 2016 until 2020, the rate of re-offending in respect of animal welfare offences was very low at 3.35%, with only three breaches of court disqualifications on the actual owning or keeping animals during that period.  I am satisfied that, where there is sufficient evidence of a breach, statutory enforcement agencies have powers to investigate and pursue a prosecution.My officials have, however, been exploring the potential to enhance current ways of working and information sharing between the relevant enforcement agencies to ensure continued compliance with bans on the owning or keeping of animals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why, in 2021-22, (a) the number of people who have seen a dentist; and (b) the range of treatment delivered, has not returned to pre-pandemic levels; and (ii) to detail any benefits that exist for dentists treating NHS patients over private patients.
answer_text: Throughout much of 2021, we were seeing record levels of infection, and measures were in place in all industries and in all regions of the UK to minimise the risk of transmission. Infection Prevention and Control (IPC) measures in healthcare settings have had a significant adverse effect on activity levels, with additional Personal Protective Equipment, social distancing, pre-appointment patient screening, and fallow time after aerosol generating procedures all contributing to a lower patient throughput in General Dental Services (GDS).  IPC restrictions were relaxed in January and May 2022, and this, along with the introduction of the Rebuilding Support Scheme, which rewards dentists based on activity levels, has seen an increase in activity levels from 58% to 76% of pre-pandemic levels over the last 2 months.Due to the increasing waiting times in areas for dental check-ups and treatment, there has also been an unprecedented demand for more lucrative private dental treatment, which dental practices have been keen to address.  Undoubtedly too, there are ongoing concerns from practitioners regarding the existing contract, which some feel undercompensates them for the Health Service work they are undertaking.  A GDS Contract Reform Group has been established to help address these concerns.Dentists in Northern Ireland continue to undertake a significant amount of Health Service dentistry every month.  In addition to the payment for Items of Service (that makes up around 60% overall GDS spending) there are also a range of allowances available to dentists and practices, as well as a very generous pension scheme and maternity, paternity and sick pay benefits.  Since the beginning of April 2022, the GDS Rebuilding Support Scheme has also been paying dentists an additional 25% on all dental treatments undertaken on Health Service patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) Northern Ireland’s current disability employment strategy; and (ii) her plans to update this strategy.
answer_text: i)  Northern Ireland’s current disability employment strategy - The Employment Strategy for People With Disabilities - was developed by the former Department for Employment and Learning in 2015. Responsibility for disability employment strategy transferred to the Department for Communities upon its formation in May 2016.ii)  This strategy is being replaced by a new disability and work strategy which is currently in preparation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether dentists are, on average, dedicating a smaller proportion of their time to seeing and treating NHS patients than in previous years; and (ii) to detail any figures collected or held by his Department regarding the proportion of their time dentists spend treating NHS patients as opposed to private patients.
answer_text: In recent months general dental activity levels have increased from around 55-60% or pre-pandemic levels to 76% as a result of new payment approach and the reducing impact of the pandemic.  Specific information however is not collected by Business Service Organisation or the Department on the length of time dentists spend providing HSC or private treatment and is therefore not available. NHS Digital however carry out a biennial Dental Working Patterns Survey of primary care NHS/Health Service dentists in England, Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales. The survey is based on a sample of all dentists. Information on average weekly hours, weeks of annual leave, the division of time between NHS/Health Service and private dentistry, and clinical and non-clinical work, is presented as well as measures of motivation and morale. The latest Dentists' Working Patterns, Motivation and Morale report for 2018/19 and 2019/20 can be found at the following link: https://digital.nhs.uk/data-and-information/publications/statistical/dental-working-hours/2018-19-and-2019-20-working-patterns-motivation-and-morale

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the areas where the available sewerage capacity is currently at a level that prohibits the construction of new housing developments; and (ii) the work his department is doing to increase capacity to facilitate construction of more homes.
answer_text: Work undertaken to increase sewerage capacity to facilitate the construction of more homes is an operational matter for NI Water, who will also retain information in relation to areas where sewerage capacity is currently prohibiting new housing development. You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for her assessment of the impact that limited local seweage capacity has on house construction in areas in need of both private and social housing; and (ii) what conversations she has had with the Minister for Infrastructure to ensure that adequate sewerage capacity exists in areas in need of additional housing.
answer_text: The potential implications for the development of social housing due to infrastructural issues has been of concern, particularly given the increasing levels of housing stress and need and the risks to the achievement of target delivery in future years.  Capacity restrictions of water/sewerage infrastructure are routinely highlighted to my Department, as an issue impacting on the delivery of social housing particularly in areas of high housing need.I have led work to develop a fundamental Housing Supply Strategy which is aimed at identifying and unlocking major levers for increasing supply across all housing tenures. I recognise that wastewater infrastructure is one of those key levers that can address our housing supply issues and my officials have been engaged with DfI officials throughout the Strategy development process.My officials have and will continue to engage with DfI and NI Water as well as key stakeholders to explore what actions can be taken to find solutions to these issues.  I have also recently met with the DfI Minister to discuss issues which impact on both our Departments. Following this it has been agreed that a further meeting will take place with officials from both Departments to examine this issue in more depth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total number of hours that dentists spent providing NHS treatment in each of the last five years; and (ii) these figures as a percentage of the total time dentists spent treating all patients.
answer_text: Information is not collected by Business Services Organisation on the length of time dentists spend providing Health & Social Care treatment and is therefore not available. Business Services Organisation does not hold any information in relation to private treatments that are carried out by dental practices. NHS Digital however carry out a biennial Dental Working Patterns Survey of primary care NHS/Health Service dentists in England, Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales. The survey is based on a sample of all dentists. Information on average weekly hours, weeks of annual leave, the division of time between NHS/Health Service and private dentistry, and clinical and non-clinical work, is presented as well as measures of motivation and morale. The latest Dentists' Working Patterns, Motivation and Morale report for 2018/19 and 2019/20 can be found at the following link: https://digital.nhs.uk/data-and-information/publications/statistical/dental-working-hours/2018-19-and-2019-20-working-patterns-motivation-and-morale

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the process for anyone wishing to purchase their Housing Association property before the House Sales Scheme for Tenants of Registered Housing Associations ends in August 2022; (ii) how the valuation of the property is determined; (iii) who conducts the valuation; and (iv) whether an independent valuation obtained by the buyer will be taken into consideration when determining a property’s value.
answer_text: Full details of the process for anyone wishing to purchase their Housing Association property before the House Sales Scheme for Tenants of Registered Housing Associations ends in August 2022 can be accessed on the Department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/disposal-housing-association-property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail her long-term plan to support more lower-income households who are facing price increases for food, goods and services.
answer_text: The fact we still have no functioning Executive or an agreed Executive budget means Departments are facing serious challenges. Despite this, I am committed to doing all within my power to put money into peoples’ pockets. I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis. I have also reconvened the Emergencies Leadership Group as part of a strategic response to the cost of living crisis. The group brings together leaders from the voluntary and community sector who are on the frontline, assisting workers, families and communities who are enduring real hardship as the crisis deepens. The group will advise, inform and shape the emerging policy and operational response to the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to raising the minimum age for purchasing tobacco each year by one year.
answer_text: The recent independent review into smokefree policies, led by Dr Javed Khan OBE, recommended that the UK Government raise the age of sale for purchasing tobacco each year by one year. The aim of the recommendation is to create a tobacco free generation where young people below a certain age are legally prevented from buying tobacco products throughout their entire lifetime. Whilst the Khan recommendations do not relate directly to NI the recommendations, along with the UK Government response, will be considered within a NI context particularly during the ongoing review of our tobacco control strategy and in the development of our subsequent strategic approach.  I am keen to explore all avenues that reduce the unacceptable harm and health disparities caused by smoking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of dental practices that have (i) stopped; and (ii) started, treating NHS patients in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Between 1 April 2016 and 31 March 2022, 23 practices are no longer contracted to carry out health service dental work. Table 1 details this information, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current provision of psychiatrists; and (ii) what steps he is taking to (a) fill current vacancies; and (b) increase the number of training places for psychiatrists.
answer_text: The supply of Consultant Psychiatrists is a concern for all Trusts across NI, with more roles available than individuals to fill them.  Issues in relation to medical workforce planning, limited commissioned training places, differences in pay and conditions and early retirements are resulting in a reduced available consultant psychiatry workforce, both on a permanent and locum basis.  A review of the Mental Health workforce, one of the enabling actions in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, is currently underway, and a range of workforce issues, including recruitment and retention, is included as part of that review.  The final report is anticipated by early autumn.There are currently 94 training places across psychiatry training programmes in Northern Ireland, including core and higher level programmes.  Psychiatry training programme places – 2022/2023Position at 3 August 2022SpecialtiesPlacesCore Psychiatry Training (Core Trainee 1-Core Trainee 3)51Child & Adolescent Psychiatry (Specialty Trainee 4+)9Forensic Psychiatry (Specialty Trainee 4+)4General Adult Psychiatry (Specialty Trainee 4+)22Psychiatry of Intellectual Disability (Specialty Trainee 4+)4Psychiatry of Old Age (Specialty Trainee 4+)4Total places94A strategic workforce review of the psychiatric specialties was recently carried out.  The resulting Medical Workforce Plan for Psychiatry Specialties 2022 – 2031 is currently being considered by my Department.  When this process has been completed, the report will be published on the Department’s website.I have stated my ongoing commitment to implement recommendations made as part of the strategic workforce planning process, including any that arise from this report.  However, as I have noted on many occasions, planning is ultimately meaningless without the necessary financial resources in place to supply and develop our workforce.Unfortunately, the current funding being made available to my Department is not sufficient to meet existing or new workforce planning recommendations.  In addition, single-year budgets constrain the assessment of affordability, frustrating long-term investment in the multi-year training programmes.  It is therefore essential that a multi-year budget is confirmed at the earliest opportunity, so that the difficult, long-term decisions about the prioritisation of finances for issues such as psychiatric training, can be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the support available for patients with arthritis awaiting surgery, including pain-management and emotional support; (ii) how a patient’s expected timeline for surgery is communicated to them; and (iii) what steps he is taking to reduce waiting times for orthopaedic surgery.
answer_text: (i) 	In order to support the large number of people living with pain, while awaiting surgery, the Northern Ireland Pain Forum has been established.  The aim of this forum is to develop and improve services across Northern Ireland (NI) for people living with a wide range of painful health conditions.  The membership of this forum spans voluntary organisations such as Versus Arthritis, Healthy Living Centres, service users and clinicians from many disciplines and organisations including primary and secondary care.All HSC Trusts offer pain and condition management programmes when access criteria are met for those individuals with a range of physical and mental health conditions, including chronic pain, and GPs continue to provide core services to their registered patients including access to allied health professional services, such as physiotherapy.  Funding is also being provided to Versus Arthritis and the Healthy Living Centre Alliance to deliver self-management programmes in order to support adults living with all forms of persistent pain and long-term health conditions, including those on waiting lists.(ii)	When a patient is told that they require surgery, they are advised of the indicative timescale in which that surgery is likely to take place.  In NI patients are managed chronologically, based on their clinical need, with urgent patients seen and treated first and management of waiting lists being one of the mechanisms that upholds the principle of equity of access to HSC services. Patients can ask for an updated indicative timescale at any time, and Trusts will normally make contact with the patient close to the time when a date for surgery can be offered. (iii)	One of the key actions of the Elective Care Framework which I published in June 2021 is the delivery of a recovery plan for orthopaedics by the Northern Ireland Orthopaedic Network, which will set out the priority actions, targets and timescales to bring orthopaedic activity back to commissioned levels and to increase activity as rapidly as possible. In April my Department commissioned a rapid independent external review of the regional orthopaedic service in NI, to identify proposals for the immediate recovery of the service.   The Review was undertaken by a team from the Royal National Orthopaedic Hospital (RNOH), Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Projects Directorate.The report detailed 21 recommendations, which were designed to increase activity in the short term with the overall aim of building a sustainable service for the future.  All 21 recommendations have been fully accepted by the Department, and work is already well underway to implement these.  The recommendations describe the need to ring fence staff and beds for elective orthopaedics, and for the creation of an Orthopaedic Elective Surgery Recovery Board to provide oversight to Trusts throughout the rebuild process.  The Recovery Board is now established and operational, and through collaboration across all Trusts,

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to developing up to date infrastructure plans for areas that experience high numbers of tourists in the summer months.
answer_text: The planning system has a key role in managing tourism related development through planning policies and in integrating tourism provision with necessary infrastructure. Seasonal impacts of high numbers of tourists on areas are likely to be limited to specific towns and attractions and are likely to be limited to roads and parking issues rather than other impacts. Due to these localised impacts Local Development Plans (LDPs), prepared by councils, are best placed to set out planning policy in respect of new tourist development. The preparation of an LDP provides the opportunity to assess the transport needs, problems and opportunities within the plan area and to ensure that appropriate consideration is given to transportation issues in the allocation of land for future development. The preparation of local transport studies by councils will assist in this process. The Department is in the process of developing a new suite of Transport Plans alongside the councils’ Local Development Plans (LDPs) that will set out the framework for transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035. The Transport Plans will take account of transport needs across the region and will reflect councils’ ambitions in their LDPs, including as it relates to tourism. Furthermore the Transport Plans will facilitate engagement with other stakeholders to ensure that a wide range of views are taken into account as they are developed. As the Transport Plans are being produced alongside the councils’ Local Development Plans their timescales will reflect that of the councils’ plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the care and support made available to pregnant and pre-conception women who have suffered (a) one; (b) two; and (c) three or more miscarriages; and (ii) what steps he is taking to introduce a specialist care pathway for high risk women wishing to conceive.
answer_text: Women who have suffered miscarriage may be referred by their GP to the gynaecology service for investigation.  The criteria for referral currently are:all women with 3 or more consecutive miscarriages;all women with 1 or more second trimester miscarriages;all women with a history of miscarriage and an associated condition such as antiphospholipid syndrome, PCOS, chronic medical disorder; andall women over 35 years old with 2 or more miscarriages.In addition to trying to establish the cause of a miscarriage, all Trusts in Northern Ireland have a bereavement midwife, early pregnancy unit and services available for women who have experienced miscarriage.In January 2021, I announced funding to establish a new specialist perinatal mental health community service model, to respond to the varying mental health needs of mothers, including those who have experienced a miscarriage.  Specialist perinatal mental health services are being implemented on a phased approach, with phase one focussing on the roll out of community perinatal mental health teams across the region.  Services in the Belfast Trust and the South Eastern Trust are operational and accepting referrals, with work being progressed in the other Trust areas to commence service delivery as soon as possible.  In parallel, preliminary scoping work has also commenced on the establishment of a Mother and Baby Unit for the region which includes engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts.The existing Antenatal Core Care Pathway helps to identify those women, including those women at high risk, who require some or all of their care by an obstetrician and ensures that this care is provided in line with individual needs. In addition, a Pre-Conceptual Care Sub-Group of the Maternity Strategy Implementation Group has been established which will address the need for a care pathway for women with long-term conditions who are planning to conceive or who are pregnant.Furthermore, the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence Guideline NG 126, ‘Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management’, which was endorsed by the Department in June 2019, was updated in November 2021 to reflect new recommendations on the use of progesterone in cases of threatened miscarriage.  It is expected that the HSC will implement these new recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for her assessment of the Neighbourhood Renewal funding system; and (ii) to detail any plans she has to review this system.
answer_text: Neighbourhood Renewal Programme and investment remains the Executive’s main place based strategy to target those communities suffering the highest levels of deprivation.  In recognition of the value of the services supported through the programme, I have protected the NR revenue budget since taking office in 2020. The review of the existing ‘People and Place - a strategy for Neighbourhood Renewal’ is currently underway with an inclusive co-design approach

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what work he has done to investigate and optimise tourist opportunities arising from the north coast’s links with the Spanish Armada, including wrecks such as La Girona.
answer_text: Tourism NI are tasked with growing the tourism economy in Northern Ireland and as such must take into account which parts of the heritage portfolio have the strongest potential to appeal to domestic and international markets, generate revenue, increase visitors and provide employment in the process.Whilst the story of the Spanish Armada is compelling, it is a lesser-known part of the history of the North Coast and in terms of revenue generation would not be in a position to compete with the key sites of the Giant’s Causeway, Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge, Ballintoy Harbour or Dunluce Castle. As such, at this time Tourism NI do not hold specific market intelligence on the tourism potential of the Girona and the Spanish Armada.However, like many parts of our culture and heritage, provides a further opportunity for visitors to explore more and stay longer. A range of Girona and Spanish linked experiences are available across Northern Ireland, including the North Coast.The history of the Spanish Armada is covered in the La Trinidad Valencera permanent exhibition located within the Tower Museum in Londonderry. There are discussions underway to promote the gateway linkage of Londonderry and the Causeway Coastal Route and beyond. As part of this work the links with the Spanish Armada could be further explored.  In addition, Tourism NI are also supporting Derry City & Strabane District Council on their Derry on the North Atlantic (DNA) museum project as part of their City Deal programme. It is proposed that the new museum will comprise galleries dedicated to ‘Discoveries of the Deep’ and ‘Sunken Treasures’, relating in part to the La Trinidad Valencera, the Spanish Armada and the exciting stories, discoveries and artefacts uncovered by the City of Londonderry Sub Aqua Club in 1971. Tourism NI has supported the development of a bespoke sea-tour, Giant Shipwrecks of the North Coast, operated by Aquaholics which covers a number of wreck sites around the North Coast and Rathlin Island. This tour specifically covers the story of the Spanish Armada and visits the Girona wreck location.Tourism NI has provided videography and photography assets for this experience to allow for the marketing and promotion of the offer. Tourism NI has also provided training and mentoring to the operator and market and quality testing of the experience as part of the Embrace a Giant Spirit experience brand journey. Aquaholics also received £42 920 from the Experience Development Programme 2020/21 for investment into diving equipment.Furthermore, Tourism NI are supporting Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council as part of their £72m Growth Deal.  One of the key tourism projects being considered is Dunluce Castle and an opportunity exists to feature stories and information relating to Girona and the Spanish Armada, however this project is still at a very early conceptual stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the progress of the single post-primary transfer test, due to be in place for 2024; and (ii) what details she can provide to schools, parents and pupils regarding preparation for the test.
answer_text: The Schools’ Entrance Assessment Group (SEAG) was formally established earlier this year to oversee a single common entrance assessment.  Following a competitive tender process, GL Assessment Ltd in England have been awarded a 3 year contract to provide the tests.  Staff have been appointed locally to oversee the administration.  The new testing format will consist of two papers held on two Saturdays, two weeks apart (provisionally scheduled for Saturday 12 November 2023 and Saturday 26 November 2023). Entry will cost £20 per pupil but will be free for pupils entitled to Free School Meals.  Both tests will contribute to candidates’ scores.  I have been advised that the tests will focus on the literacy and numeracy content of the curriculum at Key Stage 2.  There will be a mixture of multiple choice and open questions.   In terms of preparation for the tests, I am advised that, as the test is still being developed, practice papers will not be available to schools, parents and pupils.  My officials will be meeting with representatives from the Group in due course and the SEAG’s website will be updated as progress is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work his Department has undertaken, and is planning to undertake, to reduce disruption and distress to locals caused by car cruise events in Portrush and Portstewart.
answer_text: Since August 2021, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) has assisted the PSNI in carrying out four enforcement operations in the Portrush and Portstewart areas aimed at addressing road safety concerns associated with car cruise events.During these operations a total of 63 modified cars were inspected by examiners, resulting in 51 vehicles (81%) being subjected to prohibition action due to breaches of the Construction & Use Regulations. 14 modified cars (22%) were immediately banned from use on our roads due to dangerous mechanical defects.DVA will continue to provide technical support to the PSNI in such operations to help address the problem of unsafe modified cars being used on our roads.Officials from Roads Northern Division have also had discussions with the PSNI regarding other measures to discourage anti-social driving and are proposing to provide raised table pedestrian crossings at Kerr Street, Portrush; additional speed cushions at Main Street, Portrush; a raised table at the pedestrian crossing on Portstewart Promenade; and explore the possible introduction of a part-time ban on right turns out of Harbour Place, Portstewart.  Development work on these measures is currently ongoing with the intention of consulting formally on the legislation for these proposals later this year.Should you have any further questions regarding the traffic calming measures, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have sought Health Service medical care as a result of privately administered Botox and lip-filler cosmetic treatments in each of the last five years.
answer_text: As such data is not collected centrally by my Department, each of the five Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were asked for input to this question. Responses have been collated below:Belfast HSCT Co-Director of Surgery stated that they were not aware of any cases of medical care required for Botox / lip-filler cosmetic treatments in the last 5 years.Northern HSCT stated that there have been no known cases of medical care required for Botox / lip-filler cosmetic treatments in the last five years.South Eastern HSCT cannot provide a response, as the Trust does not collate such information and would therefore need to conduct a manual trawl through the records of all the emergency admissions in the last 5 years, at disproportionate cost.Western HSCT stated that they are unable to advise if a patient has previously been treated privately for such procedures or has either been admitted to hospital or attended at A&E as a result of such procedures.Southern HSCT did not provide a response and it is assumed that they could not provide a response within the time-frame provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the required processes and costs associated with the removal and disposal of asbestos in privately owned buildings; and (ii) any plans he has for reducing these costs.
answer_text: The required processes associated with the removal of asbestos from privately owned properties are the responsibility of the Health & Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI), the independent body responsible for health and safety policy and the enforcement of health and safety law in Northern Ireland.  HSENI strongly encourages the use of trained professionals for removing and disposal of asbestos containing materials (ACM).  This is especially the case if the ACM is a higher risk material such as asbestos insulation board (AIB), ceiling tiles or thermal insulation, as these materials contain a higher percentage of asbestos and readily release carcinogenic fibres to the atmosphere if disturbed or damaged. Any handling or removal of ACMs requires the right personal protective equipment to be worn and safe working methods followed, to prevent fibres from becoming airborne and being inhaled. In the event that asbestos is being removed by a contractor or other person for gain, then they must comply with the Control of Asbestos Regulation (NI) 2012.Asbestos containing materials are classified as ‘Hazardous Waste’ and must not be disposed with household waste. The material should be double wrapped in labelled polythene sacks or sheeting before they are transported for disposal at a licensed waste disposal site. Where asbestos waste is produced by the householder, or by someone acting on behalf of the householder, and not for gain, the householder may either:Employ a licensed waste carrier or a licensed asbestos contractor (as long as he is also a licensed waste carrier) to arrange disposal on their behalf to a suitably licensed site. A statutory hazardous waste tracking document called a consignment note with a unique serial number purchased from NIEA must be obtained by the carrier/contractor in accordance with the Hazardous Waste Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005 and must accompany the movement of the asbestos material to the licenced disposal site. The carrier/contractor must leave a copy of the consignment note with the householder; or     The Householder may bring the ACM to the licensed site themselves. The householder will need to contact the site in advance of travelling to find out whether any specific conditions apply. A private householder does not require a waste carrier’s license nor a consignment note.Where asbestos waste is produced by a contractor (plumber, joiner, builder or other party acting for gain) they are responsible for disposing of the waste.  If they are taking the waste themselves to a licenced site then they must hold a waste carrier’s license (issued by NIEA) and use a consignment note. The asbestos can only be taken to a site specifically licensed to accept it. Alternatively, the contractor can employ another contractor who is licensed to carry and dispose of such waste.Regarding costs associated with the removal and disposal of asbestos from privately owned buildings, the Department (NIEA) charges a fee of £24 fo
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of current regulations surrounding non-surgical cosmetic treatments, including the minimum age a person can avail of these treatments; and (ii) to detail the qualifications and training required to administer Botox and lip filler treatments.
answer_text: Under the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005, the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) regulate clinics providing certain laser or intense pulsed light cosmetic treatments and these providers are required to undergo the appropriate training etc. However, those carrying out treatments such as Botox or dermal fillers are not required to register with RQIA.  There are no qualifications legally required to administer Botox or dermal fillers.My Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) carried out an enforcement and compliance programme into the provision of Botox over a 6 month period. Initial investigations and assessments have been conducted into over 200 individual providers of Botulinum Toxin Type A.  Shortcomings in prescribing processes and breaches of advertising legislation have been identified.  Those in breach of legislation have been issued formal advice and warning notices where appropriate and have moved into legal compliance in the vast majority of cases investigated.  In addition, formal referrals have been made to professional bodies, e.g. GMC where appropriate.  The MRG continues to monitor the situation.My Department is aware of the changing health and social care environment, notably around cosmetic treatments, and has carried out a fundamental review of health and social care regulatory framework.  My officials have developed a new draft regulatory policy that includes the principles of regulation, along with the broad scope of services to be regulated and the proposal that the regulator should have wider powers of enforcement etc.   This work has been the first phase of the process before moving on to phase 2. As part of my Department’s response to the pandemic the launch of the consultation on phase 1 was delayed.  Before a draft policy is consulted upon it will be subject to further review taking into account any additional lessons from the pandemic and any other regulatory and quality issues that have emerged over the past few years.It is my intention that a Review of Regulation will be taken forward during the current mandate of the Assembly.  This Review of Regulation will also include an assessment of current standards in order to consider whether any further assurances are required.

answer_text: In tables 1 and 2 below I have provided ‘Total Applicants by Calculated Bedroom Entitlement’, and ‘Applicants in Housing Stress by Calculated Bedroom Entitlement’, for the Coleraine Central, Limavady Town, Portrush and Portstewart Housing Need Assessment (HNA) areas. Table 3 provides the total annual allocations for each HNA area. At table 4 below I have detailed the total numbers of new social housing units completed to-date in 2022/23, under construction and programmed to start in Coleraine, Limavady, Portrush and Portstewart as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval.
