question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 170/22-27, to detail the reasons that make the creation of a banned offenders register untenable and what evidence he used to come to this conclusion.
answer_text: The Department of Justice holds the information that would be needed to establish such a register. However, as any register would be animal welfare related, it indicated that my Department should lead on exploring whether or not establishing one is feasible. Following detailed consideration and clear advice from DoJ, my Department has regrettably concluded that the creation of a register is untenable because a clear evidential need has not been identified, the costs of establishing and maintaining a register would be significant and the data protection implications involved are prohibitive. In term of the evidence used to come to this conclusion, my Department considered the relevant re-offending rates, data protection law, the position in other parts of the British Isles and research conducted there.  During the period from 2016 until 2020, the rate of re-offending in respect of animal welfare offences was 3.35%, with only three breaches of court disqualifications on the actual owning or keeping animals during that period.  As such, my Department concluded that there was no overwhelming need for a register to be created based on current evidence, considering the number of instances when those convicted regained access to animals, and the value for public money.  Under data protection law, any information about criminal convictions is considered sensitive personal data. This means that it enjoys enhanced legal protection and can only be shared if certain conditions set out in law are met.  My Department is not convinced that, without the consent of the person convicted, disclosing animal cruelty convictions to any non-statutory bodies and or the public would meet any of the relevant conditions. No register of animal welfare offenders exists in any part of the United Kingdom or the Republic of Ireland and there are no plans to create one.  The Task and Finish Group established by the Welsh Government, and led by the Royal Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals, to examine the establishment of an animal cruelty register there was unable to identify any evidence to prove that such registers are effective or identify a way to overcome the data protection and costs barriers also identified by my officials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what consideration she has given to changing the threshold limits for the school uniform grant, so that parents who are struggling to afford uniforms, but who are just outside the eligibility threshold, can get support; and (ii) to detail any alternative support outside of this grant that is available for parents in this position.
answer_text: The Department has commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The purpose of this review is to ensure that FSM and uniform grants are available to support the most vulnerable children in our society. The review is considering a number of options for uniform grants, including the options for the removal/adjustment to current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit, the current rates of uniform grant as a contribution to the cost of school uniforms as well as the potential to legislate on uniform policy.   My Department continues to work to develop costed options and a consultation exercise will take place later in the year.  In the interim period, I have agreed an increase of 20% to the existing uniform grant allowances for the forthcoming academic year.In addition I am aware of various initiatives run by schools and community organisations that offer previously worn uniforms and that can provide an alternative source for parents.The Department does not offer any additional support outside of the grant but does provide guidance to schools on uniforms which stresses the need for schools to be mindful of the costs:https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/circular-201104-guidance-schools-school-uniform-policy-revised-5-june-2018

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the amalgamation of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College, including (i) the financial, developmental and operational role of Ulster University; (ii) progress on funding; (iii) what further liaison there has been with parents and the existing schools; and (iv) the anticipated timeline for (a) location; (b) planning; (c) development; (d) construction; and (e) operation.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) published five linked Development Proposals (DPs 676-680) relating to an area solution for post-primary provision in the Causeway area on 9 March 2022.  The statutory two-month objection period concluded on 23 May 2022. A submission containing all pertinent information on the proposals will be presented to me for my decision in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to outline the progress that has been made towards the formation of an integrated education strategy within six months of 26 April 2022, in line with the requirements of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; (ii) for her assessment of the challenges of doing so; (iii) to detail any timeline she has for its creation and publication; and (iv) to detail the potential legal, political and educational repercussions of failing to meet the six-month deadline.
answer_text: I am aware of the requirement under section 9 of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 for the Department to prepare, publish and maintain the integrated education strategy.  I should however clarify that this strategy is to be published within six months of 26 October 2022 – the date the Act comes into operation – not 26 April 2022 which is the date it received Royal Assent.A key challenge presented by the requirements of the Act is the impact on resources within the Department of Education, staff resources and the financial cost to deliver the requirements of the Act within a very constrained education budget.  It is also worth noting the range of consultation requirements associated with various sections of the Act, including for the action plan to be included in the integrated education strategy.  This places additional time constraints on the statutory timescale for publication of the integrated education strategy.My Department is currently engaged with the Departmental Solicitor’s Office on a range of matters requiring legal clarity, and is working to redeploy sufficient staff to undertake the work required to implement the Act in its entirety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 1072/22-27, whether he has prepared future changes to the rating of empty homes exclusions to include houses that have been vacated by an unwell or infirm person being cared for in the home of a relative or friend.
answer_text: There are no current plans to make future changes to the rating of empty homes exclusions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what work she has undertaken, or plans to undertake, with other Executive departments, to assess what correlation there is between the number of second homes in an area and the demand for school places.
answer_text: The planning of education provision to ensure there are enough places of the right type and size and which are located in the right place at the right time is a matter for the Education Authority (EA), supported by the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) and in collaboration with the various bodies established to represent the interests of the other sectors.In undertaking this role they will assess all relevant evidence, including population data relevant to any area subject to planning considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) action she has taken to investigate the potential for social or private housing on a 4.9-acre site on the Ballywillan Road in Portrush, that is adjacent to the future site of the new Mill Strand Integrated Primary School; and (ii) other potential sites she has identified in Portrush for this purpose.
answer_text: The site on the Ballywillan Road is contained within the development limits of Portrush, and the Housing Executive have advised they would support a suitable scheme at this location in principle to address  housing need in Portrush.The Housing Executive’s North Place Shaping team has received preliminary enquiries from several housing associations regarding support for the development of c. 60 new social homes on the Ballywillan Road, Portrush. Therefore, it is expected that, at this stage, housing associations are carrying out initial feasibility work to review the potential to bring forward new social homes at this location through the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). A further two sites at Glenmanus Road (15 units) and Parker Avenue (1 unit) are programmed to start in 2022/23 as part of the current Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) what work he has undertaken to assess the seasonal economic boost to towns on the north coast; (ii) the findings of any assessments; and (iii) what work he has undertaken, and plans to undertake, to improve the economic benefits.
answer_text: Parts (i) and (ii) -  Accommodation statistics provided by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) for the Causeway Coast & Glens local government district area for 2019 indicate that:Hotels and guesthouses, bed and breakfast and guest accommodation in the area experienced substantially higher room occupancy rates during April-September 2019 compared with the main autumn/winter period.Average room occupancy for hotels was 77% for April-June 2019 and 82% for July-September 2019 compared with around 50% for the remainder of the year.Guesthouses, bed and breakfast and guest accommodation experienced higher average room occupancy during July-September 2019 (45%) than they did in April-June 2019 (31%), with average room occupancy for the rest of the year dropping to around 11%.Note: Small sample sizes.  Full year data is unavailable for 2021 due to Covid-19 related closures.Tourism Northern Ireland analysis of cardholder spend on key tourism-related categories (bars, eating places & restaurants and hotels) in the Causeway Coast and Glens area in 2019 indicates: Tourism related spend in quarter one (January-March 2019) accounted for just 13% of tourism related cardholder spend for the year, compared with 40% for July-September 2019.Quarters two (April-June 2019) and four (October-December 2019) each accounted for just under one-quarter of tourism related cardholder spend in 2019.Note: Analysis is based on available cardholder spend in Ballycastle, Ballymoney, Bushmills, Coleraine, Limavady, Portrush and Portstewart.(iii) Tourism NI has a close working relationship with each of the eleven local authorities across Northern Ireland, including Causeway Coast & Glens (CC&G) council, and are meeting regularly with those councils to discuss areas such as marketing, business support and key local tourism projects.  Understandably, the Covid 19 pandemic has had a hugely negative impact on tourism in the CC&G area, other neighbouring council areas, and indeed across all parts of Northern Ireland, but the CC&G area has established significant market growth and brand recognition over several years.  There has been ongoing business activity in the Causeway Coastal Route area in relation to business collaboration working that includes the tourism private sector, public sector and voluntary sector.  A number of formal tourism cluster groups have been operating for a number of years now under the Invest NI’s Collaborative Growth Programme.  Tourism NI and CC&G council are supportive of these independently facilitated cluster groups, namely the Causeway Coast, Limavady/Binevenagh and Taste Causeway in terms of industry and business support provision.  There are significant opportunities that exist around CC&G’s role as part of the £72m Causeway Coast & Glens Regional Growth Deal, meaning that further investment in the area as an already successful tourism destination is being considered.  Tourism NI is working clo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline how increased visitor numbers to the north coast during summer months impacts on the demand for services at Causeway Hospital; and (ii) to detail any seasonal or yearly boost in funding or resources the hospital receives, in order to meet demand.
answer_text: During the months of July and August, Emergency Department attendances at Causeway Hospital are 10% higher than the average across the year.  The assumption is that this is due to the increased visitor numbers to the area during this period.The Northern Health and Social Care Trust does not receive any additional seasonal funding to meet this demand, however seasonal fluctuations in demand are taken into account in the commissioning and planning of health and social care services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in the absence of an Executive, what steps he is able to take to facilitate the full implementation of the Period Products (Free Provision) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and the provision of free period products.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward the necessary preparatory work to progress our duty under Section 2(1) of the Act, to specify via regulations which of the public service bodies within our functions must make period products obtainable free of charge in its premises. Under Section 6 of the Act, the body is required to make sufficient products available to meet the needs of persons while they are on the premises, but not products for use elsewhere.Section 1(1) of the Act places a duty on The Executive Office (TEO) to make period products obtainable free of charge within Northern Ireland by all persons who need to use them, in accordance with arrangements established and maintained by The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of facilities offering respite care for adults with learning disabilities in each constituency.
answer_text: Remobilisation of adult daycare and respite is coordinated at a regional level to ensure consistency across all 5 Trusts. Data on day care, short breaks and transport is provided on the Department’s website and is updated on a monthly basis. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesTo improve the level of provision my officials have worked with the Public Health Agency to develop a Pathway for remobilisation and issue updated public health guidance, which will enable Trusts to better balance Covid-19 considerations with wider risks to the mental health and wellbeing of service users, families and carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the changes in the workforce of General Practitioners over the last ten years; (ii) to detail the impact that these changes have in meeting the demand within primary care; and (iii) to detail his Department’s workforce planning to take into account changes over the last 10 years.
answer_text: Primary care services are a cornerstone of our health and social care provision. Unfortunately, even prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, primary care services were already coming under increasing pressure. It remains my assessment that GP-led services cannot continue to meet the rising demand and expectations without urgent action to address the pressures facing the service. To do this, means that we have to focus on both the GP workforce and on developing new ways of working that were already being implemented but which have accelerated during the pandemic. Focussing on the workforce of General Practitioners specifically, recent figures from the Business Services Organisation show that at March 2022, there were 1,419 GPs working in Northern Ireland. It should be noted, however, that not all GPs work full-time hours so changes in headcount may not reflect the change in full time equivalent GPs.  This is against a backdrop of rising demand for GP services, compounded by the ongoing pressures created by COVID-19.  Further details on the specific demographics of the GP workforce including its evolving gender and age profile are also set out in the report from BSO which is available at:  FPS - General Medical Statistics Annual Report (hscni.net)Our workforce planning approach has sought to take account of the need to ensure that we have a GP workforce that is supported, motivated and sustainable and that continues to provide quality care to patients.   The General Practitioner Medical Workforce Planning Group Final Report – June 2015 considered demand for GP services, comparison of GP medical workforces, GP training, workload and workforce trends.  It recommended a phased increase in GP training places to 111.  The Department achieved this expansion in 2019/20, an increase of 71% from 2015 levels. Work is underway  on a review of GP trainee places with a working group established to explore issues relating to the GP workforce to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst its work is still ongoing, I have accepted the working group’s recommendation of an immediate increase of 10 additional GP training places as an interim measure for the 2022/23 academic year bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year. Any future increases will be informed by completion of the work of the working group and subject to the availability of funding.In addition to the work outlined above, a number of support schemes are also in place.   The GP Induction and Refresher Scheme provides an opportunity for GPs who meet the required criteria to safely return to general practice following a career break or time spent working abroad. It also supports the safe introduction of overseas GPs and provides support to GPs returning to or entering clinical practice in Northern Ireland. Similarly, the GP Retention Scheme is designed to assist in the retention of GPs who may be considering leaving or reduci

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail any conversations she has had with caravan and caravan park owners regarding the consumer protections of the Caravans Act (Northern Ireland) 2011; and (ii) for her assessment of the current regulations.
answer_text: My Department formally launched the 2021 Review of the Caravans Act 2011 on 14 December 2021.  As the legislation is cross-cutting an inter-departmental Working Group was set up to include the Department for Infrastructure and the Department for the Economy.  Working Group members are now analysing approximately 717 responses received from caravan owners (both holiday and residential), caravan park owners and caravan trade organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his Department’s policy on providing puberty blocking medication for people under 18 years old who are transgender; (ii) the current availability of these drugs; and (iii) the process by which people under 18 years old, or their parents, can avail of them.
answer_text: (i) The Knowing Our Identity Service based within the Belfast Trust provides a range of treatment and support provided, which are designed to be clinically appropriate and reflect a staged approach in terms of psychological support and assessment. Subsequent physical assessment and intervention in the form of puberty blockers were provided in keeping with national and international guidelines.  (ii) Knowing Our Identity has offered access to gender affirming endocrine interventions (e.g. puberty blockers) for young people following psychological assessment and consideration since 2014. However, due to limited Adult Gender provision with growing waiting lists to be seen in the Adult Gender Identity Service there was no readily available transition pathway for young people at age 18 to allow for safe continuation of endocrine treatment. There was also a lack of psychological support alongside medical interventions. This led to changes to the service specification for Knowing Our Identity in March 2020 whereby new referrals after this date would no longer have access to the endocrine path.  (iii) Any young person who had been referred to Knowing Our Identity before March 2020 will have access to these drugs through the endocrine pathway. This pathway was closed after March 2020, due to the limited Adult Gender provision. There is therefore no access to these drugs for this cohort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the steps she is taking to increase spend on education, per pupil, to bring it closer in line with other parts of the UK.
answer_text: There are inherit difficulties in trying to compare per pupil funding across jurisdictions, examples of which include, regional differences in school starting ages, data sources, how different types of schools are funded and how funds, including support services to pupils, are accounted for.That said, I welcome last year’s report from the Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS), Comparisons of School Spending Per Pupil Across the UK, which highlighted that spend per pupil is expected to be the lowest in Northern Ireland, in the current financial year, compared to the other UK regions. This must be viewed in the context of the historic funding squeeze on the education budget where, between 2010-11 and 2019-20, the Northern Ireland education budget reduced by £229m in real terms. This reduction in funding was against a backdrop of a 7% increase in pupil numbers; that is, 20,000 additional pupils.  It is also important to note that Northern Ireland has the largest school-age population as a proportion of the total population of any of the UK nations.  Our relatively large school-age population contributes to a lower spend per pupil. All of this is a serious concern to me.  In my time as Minister, I have continually emphasised the issues to the Finance Minister in my work with him and my fellow Ministers in seeking to secure the necessary funding for Education as we move forward.It is also likely that the Independent Review of Education will consider the distribution of funding to education settings and the quantification of any potential funding gap, including a consideration of factors which may create funding disparities in Northern Ireland relative to the rest of the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the renewable energy technology options he is exploring to assess their potential to contribute to Northern Ireland’s electricity production.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy established a target of at least 70% of all electricity consumed in Northern Ireland to come from a diverse range renewable sources by 2030. This target was increased to 80% renewable electricity consumption by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. My Department is considering diversifying the renewable electricity technology mix in the round to provide clean, secure and affordable energy for Northern Ireland. This will mean expanding our renewables portfolio into offshore wind and other technologies in a manner that avoids negative impacts on security of supply and consumer costs. The Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022 states that the Department will bring forward an action plan to deliver 1GW of offshore wind from 2030.There is a statutory process necessary to enable offshore renewable energy deployment in NI waters involving multiple stakeholders and legislative, regulatory, technical and economic issues that must first be resolved. My Department has set up an Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan Steering Group, bringing together all relevant key stakeholders, to address these issues and see offshore wind energy deployed in NI waters as soon as practicably possible. Departmental officials are also working alongside BEIS and the other devolved administrations on the development of the UK-wide £240m Net Zero Hydrogen Fund and the Hydrogen Business Models, which BEIS hope to launch this year. It is hoped these funding mechanisms will provide financial stimulus to support the deployment of low carbon hydrogen production across the UK.My Department also publishes a regular ‘Funding Innovation for Net Zero’ E-Zine, which highlights funding opportunities in clean energy and a low carbon economy relating to the £1billion UK Net Zero Innovation Portfolio, to support the ongoing development of renewable energy and stimulate innovation towards my Department’s vision of a “10X Economy” for Northern Ireland. More information on this E-Zine can be found here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the steps people can take to reduce domestic fossil fuel consumption; and (ii) the government support and grant schemes that exist to encourage this transition.
answer_text: ANSWER (i) the steps people can take to reduce domestic fossil fuel consumption.		Steps outlined within the NI Energy Strategy to reduce fossil fuel used by domestic energy consumers include the Department’s approach to the decarbaronisation of power, heat and transport. Policies being considered include: 	a support scheme to bring forward investment in renewable electricity generation;	phasing out fossil fuel based home heating oil;	phasing out coal and certain solid fuels for home heating;	support for low carbon heat technologies including heat pumps;	trials and demonstrations on emerging heat solutions; and	a roadmap to a cleaner, greener transport system.		Decarbonising heat remains one of the biggest challenges in achieving the Energy Strategy vision as more than two thirds of homes still use oil fired central heating.  My Department remains committed to supporting trials and pilots of lower carbon forms of heating to ensure they work for our homes and buildings. Further research and engagement with stakeholders and consumers will inform a Decarbonising Heat Consultation, which my Department will publish in the coming months.		Clear direction in transport has been established by the UK government’s proposed ban on the sale of all new petrol and diesel cars from 2030 and all plug in hybrids from 2035. This ban will also apply in Northern Ireland.  A tailored approach in Northern Ireland to support vehicle users with these changes is being developed.	Energy demand reduction through retrofitting existing homes with energy efficiency measures provides both an immediate and long-term means of saving energy and thus provides householders with a means of reducing their domestic fossil fuel consumption.	ANSWER (ii) the government support and grant schemes that exist to encourage this transition.The Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) is an annual programme of energy efficiency schemes funded by all electricity consumers. The £8 million/year fund is administered by the Energy Saving Trust on behalf of the Utility Regulator. The majority of funding (80%) is ring fenced for vulnerable (‘priority’) customers in Northern Ireland who are typically able to access a package of energy efficiency measures including insulation, heating system replacement, low energy lighting, hot water cylinder jackets and smart controls. Customers who do not fall into the ‘priority’ group can access grants towards the cost of insulation.The Housing Executive delivers the Northern Ireland Energy Advice Service through funding from the Department for Communities. This service provides free and impartial energy advice to all NI households and information such as energy efficiency grant signposting, renewable energy advice, energy saving tips, how to switch energy provider, debt assistance, fuel poverty advice and oil-buying club signposting for householders.  The Housing Executive’s Affordable Warmth Scheme, which is delivered in partnership with local Councils

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the transport options that exist for people in rural areas to travel to hospital appointments, in particular when very short notice of an appointment is given.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory responsibility to provide non-urgent transport to hospital appointments in certain circumstances.  Under our 2007 Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland, this service is provided to those who have a medical need, as determined by a medical practitioner.  Medical need is defined in the strategy as being: “Where the patient’s condition could be significantly worsened if there were a failure to provide transport or, where the patient’s condition is of such severity that it renders them unfit to travel by any other means, except non-emergency ambulance transport, or some other form of non-emergency transport”. Medical practitioners assessing eligibility for transport have the flexibility to consider the needs of people with particular mobility problems, or individuals who are rurally isolated, where there is no other form of available and suitable transport.  Advice on community transport options that exist for people in rural areas to travel to hospital appointments is a matter for the Minister for Infrastructure, who has responsibility for funding community transport schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what continuity of care provision exists to enable a client user, who has moved to a neighbouring Health and Social Care Trust area, to maintain the services of their existing respite provider; and (ii) for his assessment of the importance to a child with severe learning difficulties of maintaining a relationship with a respite provider they have used for many years.
answer_text: Each transition across the region is considered on a case by case basis. (i) Trusts work collaboratively to ensure continuity of care for clients transitioning across regional boundaries. There is a standardised approach to contact the client and family, ensure assessments of need are shared and updated, and provide opportunities to meet new staff and independent sector providers. In addition, multi-disciplinary input is sought to consider options in maintaining existing respite and wider health and social care providers. (ii) For those with complex needs, adults or children, it is important to maintain relationships with staff and respite providers throughout transitions. This must be balanced against the assessment of need, growing complexity and value for money considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 630/22-27, (i) to detail any schools in East Londonderry that she has assessed as being in need of capital funding; and (ii) whether she has plans to assess again the schools eligible for the Major Capital Works Programme and release more funding for it.
answer_text: There are currently two Major Capital Works Projects in the East Londonderry constituency.  Loreto College, Coleraine, was announced in my recent Major Capital Works Call and Our Lady of Fatima Primary School, Tamnaherin, was announced in the 2013 Call.  The list associated with this call is now closed and there will be no further announcements made from this call. The need for Calls to each of the Department’s Capital Programmes, that is the Minor Works Programme, the School Enhancement Programme and the Major Capital Programme will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she is taking to encourage owners of properties that are derelict or unsuitable for habitation to bring them up to a habitable standard.
answer_text: My Department is progressing a Housing Supply Strategy that sets out a long term framework for the delivery of supply to meet our housing needs and demands. The Strategy includes a number of long term commitments and actions including looking at adaptive reuse of existing assets and developing policies for regeneration supported by residential development. I am currently considering options for progressing the Strategy in the continuing absence of an Executive and agreed budget. Derelict properties can be reported to local councils who have responsibility for investigating derelict, dangerous and insecure buildings in their area. They have the authority to seek a remedy with the property owner.  The Department of Agriculture Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) under their remit to “Protect the Environment” have overall responsibility for Dilapidation. DAERA officials are currently assessing the legislation used by councils to deal with dilapidated/dangerous buildings and neglected sites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the current regulations on properties, or parts of properties, being used for AirBnB lets; (ii) if landlords are changing from operating long-term lets to holiday lets; and (iii) any plans he has to improve or initiate regulation relating to this.
answer_text: To answer each question in turn:	The Department of Finance does not have responsibility for regulating AirBnB lets. 	My department does not hold this information.	My Department is not responsible for regulating AirBnB lets, therefore I have no plans in that regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what conversations she has had, or plans to have, with the Minister for the Economy regarding the potential for vacant spaces above shops and businesses to be used for social or private housing.
answer_text: I have developed a Housing Supply Strategy that sets out a long term framework for the delivery of supply to meet our housing needs and demands. As part of the consultation process for this strategy all departments were invited to comment. I am currently considering options for progressing the Strategy in the continuing absence of an Executive and agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans she has to introduce price caps for rental properties.
answer_text: I have stated my intentions to make renting in the private rented sector fair and affordable for tenants. The first stage in addressing the issue of affordability was the introduction of the Private Tenancies 2022 which introduced a restriction on the frequency of rent increases and limited the amount of a deposit which a landlord can charge. In addition the Act introduced powers to bring forward regulations to decrease rents by up to 10% and freeze those lowered rents for up to 4 years. The Act commits the Department to carrying out a consultation on whether it should exercise these powers and to lay the resulting report before the Assembly by 27 October 2022.This consultation work was commissioned to the Chartered Institute of Housing in June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail any plans he has to revise planning regulations so that homes more suited to families are prioritised for development, ahead of apartments targeted towards second homes and short-stay rental properties; and (ii) for his assessment of current rates of planning permissions being granted for apartments in areas with high levels of tourism but low numbers of long-term housing options.
answer_text: The Department’s Regional Development Strategy (RDS) 2035 and the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) support the creation of balanced communities and, in relation to housing, facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality housing to meet the needs of everyone. Council Local Development Plans (LDPs) are the main vehicle for delivering this by assessing future housing land requirements and managing housing growth to achieve sustainable patterns of residential development. This process allows councils to address local housing issues within their communities, such as the demand for family housing; apartments; holiday homes; and short-stay rental properties. Where appropriate, LDPs can include specific policies to protect the distinctive nature and character of settlements. Councils are also responsible for operating effective development management in respect of planning applications for housing and in doing so, the provisions of regional planning policy must be taken into account.  I have no plans to revise the Department’s planning policy approach on housing at this time.  In relation to part (ii) whilst my Department publishes statistics on planning activity and performance on a quarterly and annual basis, the disaggregation being referred to in the Member’s question is not available through existing statistical databases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for her assessment of the impact on Housing Executive tenants of the closure of the House Sales Scheme; and (ii) to detail any plans she has to create an alternative to this scheme, such as the Right to Buy Scheme which operates in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: I refer you to response to AQW 1648 / 22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the trend in the number of dentists providing NHS treatment; and (ii) what steps he is taking to ensure there is adequate provision of NHS dental care.
answer_text: Information is not held on dental practices and dentists that are currently accepting new Health Service (HS) patients or if they have any self-imposed limits on HS patient registrations. Information is however available on the number of active dental contracts, where dentists have entered an arrangement with the Department to provide HS dental services.Data provided by the Business Services Organisation shows that since 2016/17 there has been an increase in the number of dentists who accepted at least one new HS patient. There has however been a slight fall in this number since 2019/20 (see below table).Financial YearGDS DentistsNumber active at 31st MarchNumber at 31st March that accepted at least one new HS patient12016/171,2111,1422017/181,2521,1892018/191,2601,2002019/201,2871,2162020/211,2871,1922021/221,3011,190 The absence of any new HS patient registration in-year does not necessarily mean that the dentist is not currently accepting new HS patients. The Department has made unprecedented investment into the industry in order to maintain the provision of HS dentistry, with over £76m being paid to independent General Dental Practitioners over the past two years in return for increasing levels of activity. However, given the pressures of the pandemic, it is to be expected that some practices have found their businesses unsustainable and have either had to close, or seek more profitable options.Further support has been committed for the first half of 2022/23, with dental fees being enhanced by 25% as part of the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), to reflect the ongoing pandemic pressures practices are facing. This investment has been supplemented by an additional £6.5m made available to practices via Revenue Grant Schemes in 2020/21 and 2021/22.  This funding is designed to improve patient safety, practice efficiency and improve the resilience of practices in order to mitigate against future transmission of aerosol based respiratory diseases, and other infectious diseases.Recent easing of Infection Prevention and Control procedures within all health care settings, along with the introduction of the RSS in April 2022 has already resulted in an increase capacity within practices from 58% to 76% of pre-covid levels.  This is expected to continue to improve over the coming months, as dentists work through their backlogs.The Department is also developing options to increase the availability of appointments for non-registered patients who have urgent and emergency treatments needs.  Patient registration timeframes have also been extended since the beginning of the pandemic to ensure that patients registration status is unaffected by any difficulties encountered in attending. In addition, Urgent Dental Care Centres continue to provide cover for patients with dental emergencies who cannot be seen in practice, and these centres will be reverting to Relief of Dental Pain Clinics, with increased capacity, in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the mechanisms via which the various Cost of Living Payments announced by the UK Government will be distributed to people in Northern Ireland; (ii) when they will be delivered; and (iii) who will receive each payment.
answer_text: Information on the Cost of Living payments for people here can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/cost-living-support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the process for anyone wishing to purchase their Housing Executive property before the House Sales Scheme ends in August; (ii) how the valuation of the property is determined; (iii) who conducts the valuation; and (iv) whether an independent valuation obtained by the buyer will be taken into consideration.
answer_text: The Housing Executive’s House Sales Scheme is not ending and therefore there is no change to the existing process for a Housing Executive tenant to purchase their home.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail for each constituency (i) the number of second homes; and (ii) the number of these that are (a) rented long-term: (b) rented short-term; and (c) not rented.
answer_text: The management information available to my Department, in relation to those properties rented out to private tenants, is taken from the Landlord Registration Scheme database and is produced by council area. We do not have information on the numbers of properties which are rented long term or short term or those which currently are without a tenant.  In addition not all landlords own another home, for example if they are renting their current residence due to working abroad.Table below shows the number of rental properties on the database as of 6th June 2022 and the number of registered Landlords – CouncilLandlord CountProperty CountUnallocated postcodes (due to error in registration data)421291440Antrim and Newtownabbey3,0014,723Ards and North Down4,3806,158Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon4,1108,109Belfast5,90120,673Causeway Coast and Glens3,0885,629Derry City and Strabane2,5545,706Fermanagh and Omagh2,4454,050Lisburn and Castlereagh3,7744,135Mid and East Antrim3,3446,071Mid Ulster2,9384,157Newry, Mourne and Down3,7145,040Totals39,30574,620

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 631/22-27, to detail the number and nature of any enquiries by businesses interested in becoming tenants at Coleraine’s Atlantic Link Enterprise Campus, since its creation.
answer_text: As I stated in my previous written response, Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council is responsible for the ongoing promotion and marketing of the Atlantic Link Enterprise Campus. My Department does not therefore hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 671/22-27, to detail what conversations she has had with the Minister for the Economy and Tourism NI to (i) assess housing provision and need; and (ii) develop policies to address housing issues.
answer_text: I have developed a Housing Supply Strategy that sets out a long term framework for the delivery of housing supply to meet our needs and demands. One of the key issues identified in the Strategy is that of ensuring we have the right skills to deliver on our housing needs, in particular in addressing climate change commitments, retrofit and improved housing quality. My officials have engaged with DfE colleagues on this issue throughout the development of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 671/22-27, to outline the options she has to improve housing provision in the absence of an official Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: I have and will continue to take measures to improve housing provision.  I have secured investment of almost £380 million to deliver new social housing starts in the past two years. Indeed, the 2021/22 budget expenditure of £171.8m represented the highest level of Social Housing Development Programme related investment activity since the Housing Executive took on responsibility for managing its delivery in 2007. I have provided £158m to Co-Ownership to help those who wish to own their own home and have progressed work to deliver new affordable housing options, such as, intermediate rent.I have also taken action to increase the safety and security of the Private Rented sector, including by progressing legislation through the Assembly. Despite the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget, all options will continue to be explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of active short-term holiday let licences, in each constituency, in each of the last five years; and (ii) what conversations he has had with Tourism NI regarding the number of short-term holiday let licences granted in holiday areas, which are experiencing a shortage of long-term rental properties for local residents.
answer_text: All tourist accommodation in Northern Ireland requires inspection and certification by Tourism NI, irrespective of the duration the business is being used as a tourist let. A certificate is renewable every four years and ensures a minimum standard is met by all accommodation providers.  For tourism businesses within the self catering sector, a tourist let may not be the primary use, with many properties also being utilised as student accommodation or personal holiday homes during the off-peak season. Tourism NI is not legally required to hold a record of how many trading weeks tourism businesses operate but does record accommodation stock within each council area. The latest figures for self catering stock by Council area including bedspace capacity are set out in the table below.	CouncilBed SpacesRooms UnitsTotal PremisesAntrim and Newtownabbey3287454Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon4879872Belfast2679677452Causeway Coast and Glens1244221481849Derry and Strabane787170117Fermanagh and Omagh2564474265Lisburn and Castlereagh50411484Mid and East Antrim942205166Mid Ulster805161117Newry, Mourne and Down2624521401North Down and Ards985221195 Total2514748633772			There are other statutory requirements, such as planning permission or change of use requests for tourist accommodation, which fall outside the scope of Tourism NI’s legislative remit. Such responsibilities sit with individual councils. Tourism NI is not therefore responsible for controlling the number of tourist lets in any given area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 683/22-27, to detail the Assembly and Executive mechanisms preventing her from progressing the Sub Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: Legal advice has indicated that the updated proposals for the Sub Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer require consideration and approval by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the number of vacant properties in town centres; and (ii) what steps he is taking to support town centre businesses to prevent closures.
answer_text: This is not within the remit of my department. The Executive Office has established a High Street Task Force, whilst other departments have lead responsibility for town regeneration, planning and licensing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people awaiting domiciliary care packages that have chosen to avail of free residential, care or nursing home accommodation offered by his Department, in each constituency in the last 12 months; (ii) the estimated total monthly cost of the care packages required; and (iii) the estimated total monthly cost of the residential, care or nursing home accommodation provided.
answer_text: (i) In the past twelve months there are a recorded 1,027 people awaiting domiciliary care packages that have chosen to avail of free residential care or nursing home accommodation. However this is believed to be an under estimation of the total number of people, as one Trust, The Western Health and Social Care Trust, were unable to provide the data in the required timeframe. The breakdown of the known people awaiting a domiciliary care package that have chosen to avail of free residential or nursing home care as per Trust locality is provided in the attached table (column 2). (ii)  From the information available, the known estimated total monthly cost of the care packages required for the 1,027 people awaiting domiciliary care packages across the region equates to £180,234 (based on an hourly rate of £18). The breakdown of the known total monthly cost of the care packages required per Trust locality is provided in the attached table (column 3). However this is believed to be an under estimation of the  total monthly cost of the care packages required, as two Trusts, The Western Health and Social Care Trust and the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, were unable to provide this data in the required timeframe.(iii) From the information available, which is based on returns for four of the five Trusts, the estimated total monthly cost of the residential and nursing home care provided for the 1,027 people awaiting domiciliary care packages, equates in the region of £3 million (based on the 2022/23 tariff rates). As information was not available in the required timeframe, as to the exact length of stay for each person, this calculation is based on the assumption that each person resided in the care home for the full month. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 632/22-27, how the absence of a rise in fuel allowance rates for domiciliary care workers is impacting the delivery of care packages in rural areas.
answer_text: Home care workers are raising concerns daily regarding the impact of increased fuel prices. For all trusts, it is becoming challenging to cover staff absence, with some staff choosing not to make themselves available to cover extra shifts as they advise the cost is impacting their own personal finances. The cost of fuel may also have an impact on future recruitment, which would have a negative impact on the ability of trusts to sustain services, particularly in rural areas. It is estimated, for example that a Domiciliary Care Worker will spend between 10 & 20% of their working day driving, depending on whether they are urban or rural based.  To mitigate any service disruption, each trust has a service continuity plan, which has been used during the Covid surge periods, whereby available resources will be targeted at those most critical cases based on a dynamic risk assessment approach.I recognise the difficulties faced by our healthcare staff due to rising fuel costs and I have approved a 50% increase to the secondary rate on annual mileage over 3,500 miles to bring it up to 30p per mile. This measure, which will be for an initial six month period, to be reviewed, will benefit those who are required to use their vehicles most frequently.Paying this increased rate through staff payroll systems will take some time to implement, in the interim, I have asked for further work to be undertaken to explore the feasibility of issuing fuel cards to eligible staff when they reach the 3,500 threshold.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 303/22-27, to detail any direct, on the ground assistance he is giving to businesses to help them fill staff vacancies.
answer_text: The Department for Communities’ Employer Engagement Service provides a service to businesses to help fill staff vacancies. My Department’s Assured Skills academies provide demand led, pre-employment training programmes which offers new and expanding local companies the opportunity to work closely with the DfE, Invest NI (INI) and the Further and Higher Education sector to recruit high quality employees.  All participants who successfully complete an Academy will be interviewed for a guaranteed job vacancy with the company.  To date, in the 2021/22 financial year, 43 of the 48 academies scheduled have been completed, resulting in 552 of the 625 participants upskilled, accepting an offer of full-time employment.Apprentices participating on the ApprenticeshipsNI Programme are in paid employment from day one and follow a national training framework which leads to the attainment of industry approved competence and knowledge based qualifications, at Level 2 or 3.  My Department’s Higher Level Apprenticeship (HLA) programme provides funding for the delivery of off the job training at levels 4 to 7, which is equivalent to Foundation Degree through to Masters level study.  HLAs are demand-led, work-based programmes designed around the needs of employers, offering recognised Higher Education qualifications to new employees and existing employees taking on new roles which require significant training and development.My Department also provides assistance through the provision of Traineeships and Advanced Technical Awards at level 3 which prioritise those skills that are most economically relevant and contribute to increasing the proportion of working age population with qualifications at level 2 and level 3.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to highlight the importance of men’s health and to encourage men to seek medical diagnoses when symptoms are present.
answer_text: A number of initiatives aimed at promoting and improving men’s health are funded/co-ordinated by the Public Health Agency. These include  male-specific health checks in Healthy Living Centres; a Farm family health check programme; investment in sexual health clinics for Men who have sex with Men (MSM); Men’s health checks online, as well as Low Threshold Services as part of drugs and alcohol services; a wide range of health promotion based activity programmes specifically for males such as horticulture, angling, walking, football, music, arts, and sports; training programmes in HSC Trusts focusing on engaging men on mental health and emotional wellbeing; and Men’s Health Week support by local Connections teams and PHA-funded Council staff. The PHA also provides funding for the Men’s Shed movement which supports key priorities of tackling health inequalities, reducing isolation and loneliness, and promoting positive mental health.The Diabetes Prevention Programme NI supports men at risk of developing type 2 diabetes to reduce their risk through lifestyle choices supported by health coaching, and an early diagnosis cancer campaign was delivered in 2021 through the Living Well service. This campaign engaged with men to provide information on cancers, including male-specific cancers, and their signs and symptoms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what new strategies and initiatives he is planning in order to aid suicide prevention efforts; and (ii) what support his Department gives to the community and voluntary sector organisations that are involved in this issue.
answer_text: The Protect Life 2 Strategy contains a wide range of actions to reduce suicide and self-harm in Northern Ireland. The implementation of the Strategy is taken forward through the Protect Life 2 Steering Group, and the Public Health Agency lead on the implementation of the Health Actions. A smaller number of actions are delivered through HSC Trusts.Updates against all actions of the Protect Life 2 Strategy can be found at:Protect Life 2: Action Plan brief update (health-ni.gov.uk)Around £10m is invested each year by DoH on suicide prevention. Additional funding was sought on suicide prevention in the 2022-25 Draft Budget exercise to enable the commissioning of new suicide prevention services. However, the availability of funding will be determined by the budget allocation my Department receives once a budget is agreed. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.Subject to future funding Protect Life 2 aims to expand existing services including Multi Agency Triage Team, Self Harm Improvement Programme, Towards Zero Suicide, Training and awareness raising. New services are also planned around support for those bereaved by suicide, safety planning and promotion of self-care.£3.49 million was given to Community and Voluntary Organisations under Protect Life 2 in 2021/22. £1.32 million was given to Community and Voluntary Organisations through small grants in 2021/22. Since March 2021 the Department has made available £16m through the Mental Health Support Fund which supports Community and Voluntary organisations that aim to improve the mental health and emotional wellbeing of the population. A full list of supported projects can be found at: Mental Health Support Fund Round 1 Successful Applicants Mental Health Support Fund Additional Funding and Successful  Applicants

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the varying percentage of energy created by renewable sources in Northern Ireland impacts the price consumers pay for electricity.
answer_text: There are many factors that impact on the price of electricity including the availability of renewables on the system.  In periods of high wind, prices tend to drop with the highest prices associated with a lower wind forecast.  When renewable energy is available on the system, it has a dampening effect on the wholesale price, pushing down the price set by traditional thermal generation.  However, renewable energy availability is intermittent and the system remains reliant on traditional fossil-fuelled generation to ensure security of supply.  Therefore, the predominant influence on electricity prices remains the wholesale cost of fuel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the outcome of the consultation on reshaping stroke care will be published; and (ii) to outline his latest proposals for improving stroke care, including timelines.
answer_text: On 20th June, I made a statement to the House confirming publication of the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis report and subsequent Action Plan which sets out the way forward for the reform of stroke care in Northern Ireland. Both documents are available on my Department’s website at www.health-ni.gov.uk/reshaping-stroke-care

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how adults with learning disabilities, who do not have a social worker, can receive a referral to day services.
answer_text: An adult with a learning disability can receive a referral to day centre following a referral to Social Services and subsequent assessment of need.  If an adult with a learning disability does not have a social worker, a referral to Social Services could be made by any professional e.g. GP, Nurse or family member/carer for assessment in respect of day centre service provision. There may also be opportunity for adults with learning disabilities who do not have a social worker to access day activities through their local community or voluntary groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of staff shortages in the frontline health and social care sector; (ii) to detail the areas most affected; and (iii) to detail the steps being taken to address the shortages.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the impact of staff shortages across the whole health and social care system, including on front line service provision.  Health and social care organisations are under unrelenting pressure to deliver safe and effective care.  This has been exacerbated over the recent period by the pandemic and the associated increased levels of absence due to sickness and self-isolation of staff.The following table shows the latest published figures on HSC vacancies (at 31 March 2022).  There were 6,778 HSC vacancies actively being recruited to, equating to an overall vacancy rate of 8.1%.  The largest number of vacancies (2,067 – 9.9%) related to registered nurses and midwives, while the highest vacancy rate was for social care workers (10.3% – 997).HSC Vacancies by Staff GroupStaff GroupVacancies actively being recruited to at 31 March 2022% Vacancy rate for each Staff Group and TotalAdmin & Clerical 9436.1%Estates Services Staff738.2%Support Services/User Experience3695.1%Registered Nursing & Midwifery2,0679.9%Nurse/midwifery support4908.4%Social workers4859.6%Social care workers99710.3%Professional & Technical 1,0028.3%Medical & Dental3235.8%Ambulance90.6%Total6,7788.1%Source: HSC organisationsSimilar challenges exist in the independent social care sector with, for example, continuing staffing pressures in care homes having knock on effects in delayed hospital discharge.Action to address operational staff pressures is led by health and social care employers who have developed frameworks to address these issues.  The starting point is seeking targeted additional voluntary resource, which can take the form of additional bank shifts, voluntary redeployment and, where necessary, employment of agency or locum staff.Active recruitment to permanent HSC Trust vacancies is proceeding at pace.  This is targeted at existing trained staff, newly qualified professionals, and doctors and nurses who have joined the HSC through our successful international recruitment programmes.  At 31 May 2022, 1,023 international nurses have been recruited and remain in post.  I approved a further phase of international nurse recruitment in February 2022.  This has the potential to deliver a further 3,000 international nurses over the next five years, subject to the provision of the necessary funding.  In November 2021 I announced a support package of up to £23m to enable social care employers to offer improved rates of pay and attract staff for the domiciliary care and care home workforces.  To date, there has been positive feedback on how this additional funding has helped to stabilise the sector and supported recruitment and retention.  Despite a very challenging financial position, I have prioritised funding this year to continue supporting this sector.  Long-term, transformational change is needed in this essential area and is the subject of the current consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care (26 January 2022 – 1 July 2022). Growing our

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children currently (i) being treated for; and (ii) on a waiting list to receive treatment or support for, mental health issues.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline the challenges in providing domiciliary care within the community in the Western and Northern Health and Social Care Trusts; (ii) how many service users are currently without their (a) full assessed care package; or (b) part of their assessed care package; and (iii) for his assessment of the capacity of care providers in these areas to fully provide services.
answer_text: You will be aware that on 24 November 2021 I announced a support package of up to £23m for the independent domiciliary and wider social care sector. This additional funding has been provided with the expectation that providers will not only maintain current service levels, but increase capacity and be in a position to reach out particularly into rural areas.  Covid-19 related and non Covid-19 related absences throughout the pandemic have been a challenge for all domiciliary care providers.  The demand for domiciliary care has increased as well as the complexity of need of service users.  Recently fuel cost increases have put additional pressures on the domiciliary care sector.  Providers operate rolling recruitment exercises to fill workforce vacancies. The table below summaries the current numbers of people who are waiting on a full or part packages of care in the Northern and Western Trust areas.TRUSTDATEFULL PACKAGE  WAITSPARTIAL PACKAGE WAITSNumber of ClientsNumber of ClientsNHSCT9.6.22498349WHSCT6.6.22261108I will continue to support the domiciliary car sector in their attempts to meet the current demand for packages of care in the community.  The local HSC Trusts are promoting Direct Payments as an alternative to traditional packages of care and we would encourage as many people as possible in the community to avail of this option.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of jobseekers, trainees, apprentices and other individuals that have taken part in employment and skills programmes, funded in whole or part by her Department, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the number of service users awaiting care packages in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) changes that have been made to the number of hours of care provision calls; (iii) his assessment of the current challenges for domiciliary care and the sustainability of services; and (iv) the steps he is taking in response to these challenges.
answer_text: As of 13 June, 1480 service users were reported as awaiting domiciliary care packages in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, of these 1077 were awaiting a full package of care and 403 were awaiting a partial package of care.(ii) The table below outlines the total number of hours of care delivered, over the past 5 years, within the Northern Trust area. To year endHours of Care DeliveredMarch 2017                 245,577 March 2018                 245,085 March 2019                 256,160 March 2020                 259,191 March 2021                 260,921 March 2022                 254,071 Note: This includes independent sector and internal Homecare service provision. Independent sector hours of care are reported in 13 periods throughout the year as opposed to months. The Independent sector hours have been adjusted to months based on manual calculation and the in house hours delivered have been adjusted based on the average hours delivered per month over the entire year to negate the impact of 5 week months.(iii)	I recognise that there continue to be a range of difficulties facing the domiciliary care sector including for providers in retaining their staff, with rising costs being one element of this. There are also continued challenges presented by staff absence due to Covid-19, as well as a competitive wider recruitment environment.While domiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures, my Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service. Trusts endeavour to provide care packages where they are required, however, ultimately there are a range of factors which can impact upon this delivery, including staffing issues. Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure delivery of services continues to be provided.(iv)	In regards to action being taken, where a temporary care home placement is accepted for individuals awaiting implementation of a care package, my Department has advised Trusts to waive the usual care home placement fees until a suitable home care package has been developed. Trusts are continuing the messaging of encouraging a timely discharge process wherever possible through the use of care options such as care home placements, or informal support from family and friends.The Department also reviewed and re-issued the regional Direct Payments guidance, to simplify the process, make it less restrictive (i.e. allowing for the employment of family members where no other workers can be identified) and make the option more attractive to prospective users.In regards to support to the independent sector, you will be aware that in November last year I announced a support package of up to £23m, which supported independent domiciliary care providers to improve recruitment, employee retention, and Terms and Conditions for staff. This investment has allowed providers to deliver enhanced rates of pay and impro

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) current waiting time; and (ii) number of women waiting, for gynaecology appointments in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Outpatient waiting times are published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  These publications refer to the position at the end of a given quarter, detailing how many patients are waiting for a first consultant led outpatient appointment broken down by HSC Trust, Specialty and length of time waiting. The most recent Quarterly National Statistics publication of Outpatient Waiting Times for the position at 31st March 2022 are available at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-outpatient-waiting-times-march-2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what engagement he has had with the Minister of Education in relation to improving childcare provision for working parents.
answer_text: I have not had any engagement with the Minister of Education on this subject, as childcare provision is not within the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of staff shortages across all industry sectors; and (ii) what new steps he is taking to help (a) businesses find employees; and (b) people who are unemployed to find work.
answer_text: (i) Data published by the Office of National Statistics highlights that approximately two in five businesses (38.2%) in Northern Ireland were experiencing difficulties recruiting employees in April 2022 which is broadly in line with the UK average of 38.9%. Whilst no sectoral breakdown is provided and no official data exists, I am aware of worker shortages reported across a number of sectors including Agri-food, Hospitality, Construction, Manufacturing, ICT and Health and Social Care. 		As evidenced through the monthly economic updates produced by my Department, unemployment has continued to decline over the last six months to the joint lowest on record (2.3%). This is coupled with higher than normal levels of advertised job vaccancies, highlightling an increasingly tight labour market in Northern Ireland.		(ii) (a) In March 2022, my Department published the new Skills Strategy for Northern Ireland, ‘Skills for a 10x Economy’. The Strategy focuses on three major policy objectives: 	Addressing skills imbalances, driving economic growth; 	Creating a culture of lifelong learning; and	Enhancing digital skills, developing our digital spine. 		Successful implementation of ‘Skills for a 10x Economy’ over the next decade will ensure that Northern Ireland’s citizens and businesses increasingly have access to modern economically relevant skills that will empower them to reach their full potential and improve competitiveness on the global stage.	 		(ii) (b) My Department  continues to work collaboratively with the Department for Communities in the development and provision of skills-based interventions which support the unemployed to engage/re-engage with the labour market. This includes support and participation in a range of emerging initatives such as ‘Labour Market Partnerships’, the Disability Employment Strategy and refreshed approaches to addressing economic inactivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of day centre provision for (a) older people; (b) people with mental health issues; and (c) people with physical disabilities; and (ii) to detail the steps he is taking to increase this provision.
answer_text: In February 2022, I wrote to the HSC Trusts in regard to my decision to approve the Public Health Agency (PHA) Pathway to remobilise adult day-care, short breaks and transport services.  I was clear that the Pathway should be implemented with immediate effect across all programmes of care including these services for older people, those with physical and sensory disabilities, and those with ill mental health. Since May 2022, my department has been receiving updated rebuild plans on a fortnightly basis from each HSC Trust which outline the current position across each adult programme of care and which provides indicative timeframes for the full remobilisation of day centre provision to pre-pandemic capacity.To date HSC trusts are continuing to build upon their day centre activity on a phased basis across all the programmes of care with progression to pre-Covid-19 activity.  However, there remains a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care and this will continue to be an area of focus.It should be noted that for a percentage of service users, their assessed needs have changed from pre-pandemic levels which has resulted in the need for differing care requirements. Also, a percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have currently chosen not to uptake their day care entitlement to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. Current barriers to full remobilisation of adult day centres include staffing availability, reduced estate, service user / carer choice to reduce /not return at present due to their CEV status, challenges with aspects of the guidance in regard to social distancing, isolation requirements, testing and vaccination. Some facilities have experienced Covid-19 outbreaks.While it is clear that the Pathway has been an enabler to remobilise services, there remains further adjustments to public health guidance required before services can be fully restored to pre-pandemic levels.  I have therefore asked PHA for an urgent formal review of the pathway which is due to be completed by the end June 2022. Furthermore, I have committed to issuing a periodic communication to all MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts. It is regrettable that day centre provision has not yet been fully restored to pre-pandemic capacity.  I sincerely apologise to those affected service users and their families and wish to offer the assurance that the full remobilisation of day care remains one of my key priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he has had with the MJM Group in relation to the development of the former Shackleton military base in Ballykelly.
answer_text: I met with MJM group on 12th January 2022. One of the topics discussed was their recent acquisition of the Shackleton site in Ballykelly and the plans for the site going forward. These plans were commercial in confidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what consideration he has given to including houses that have been vacated, by an unwell or infirm person being cared for in the home of a relative or friend, into the rating of empty homes exclusions; and (ii) how this can be brought into line with rating of empty homes exclusions for houses that have been vacated by a person who has gone into a registered care facility.
answer_text: As advised in AQW 30860/17-22, the rating of empty homes policy was introduced in October 2011 to assist with wider policy objectives related to housing affordability and supply, as well as to raise additional revenue.  One of the exclusions to this policy is that the person who owns the empty property has their only or principal residence in a hospital, nursing home or residential care home.  As previously advised this policy cannot be amended in the absence of an Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the impact on hospitality businesses as a result of the reintroduction of Pavement Café Licence fees by some councils; and (ii) to outline any support he is planning to provide hospitality businesses in these areas to continue trading and serving customers outside.
answer_text: From the Office of the Minister 	Gordon Lyons MLA									Use of outside spaces is not within the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for her assessment of the impact, on artists and entertainment venues, of the reintroduction of entertainment licence fees by some councils; and (ii) to outline any support she is planning to provide artists and venues in these areas.
answer_text: In response to the impact of the COVID 19 pandemic on the hospitality industry, last year I introduced a temporary nominal fee of £1 for renewal applications for all categories of entertainment licence for the duration of the 2021/2022 financial year.Earlier this year, following consultation with the councils, I decided to continue with this measure for the 2022/2023 financial year, again subject to review.
