answer_text: The number of patients at Causeway Hospital that have had to travel to other locations to access MRI facilities in each of the last five years is detailed in the table below:YearReferrals2017/183432018/194522019/205322020/217362021/22849Source: Northern Health and Social Care Trust

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health which Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service stations have been assessed for their potential inclusion in the programme for firefighters to respond to cardiac arrests alongside the Ambulance Service.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) intend to include all stations in the programme for firefighters to respond to cardiac arrests alongside the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service, following appropriate engagement and agreement with Representative Bodies and delivery of training to NIFRS firefighters. Currently Carnlough Fire Station has been progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of (i) the current demand for independent support services such as foodbanks; and (ii) how this demand has changed over the previous three years.
answer_text: My current assessment of the demand of independent support services such as foodbanks is that whilst demand has fallen from the peak of the pandemic it is on the rise again as a result of the cost of living crisis The Trussell Trust operates the largest network of foodbanks here and gathers and publishes statistics on usage. https://www.trusselltrust.org/news-and-blog/latest-stats/end-year-stats/ The figures show that in 2019/20 just over 45,000 food parcels were issued. This increased to almost 79,000 during 2020/21 and whilst usage in 2021/22 shows a decrease to just under 62,000 from they remain 36.5% higher than in 2019/20.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the efforts she is making to explore the installation of renewable energy-generating technology on (i) social housing; and (ii) associated land, with a view to reducing energy costs long-term.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is currently investigating a range of renewable energy options to provide more affordable energy services to tenants.  These include ground source and air source heat pump systems, and geothermal energy production and heat storage.In its role as the Home Energy Conservation Authority the Housing Executive has engaged with power network providers, the Utility Regulator and the Department for the Economy about how to best develop technical options suitable for both Housing Executive and privately owned homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether Cross-Border Elective Healthcare will be extended beyond the current 30 June 2022 deadline; and (ii) what percentage of the cost of treatment will be reimbursed to the patient.
answer_text: You will be aware that in June 2021, I announced a new Cross-Border Healthcare Scheme (ROI Reimbursement Scheme) for a 12 month period from 30th June 2021 as the Cross Border Healthcare Directive no longer applies to the UK.  This initiative, part of the Elective Care Framework, is aimed at reducing Northern Ireland waiting lists.  My officials are preparing a paper for me to consider options for the end of the current 12 month scheme.The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme allows patients to seek and pay for routinely commissioned treatment in the private sector. The reimbursement of costs is not based on a percentage of the actual payment made by the applicant to the private provider. The applicant can have the costs, up to the cost of the treatment to the HSC in Northern Ireland, reimbursed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29259/17-22, to detail the efforts she is making to explore the installation of renewable energy generating technology on (i) school land; and (ii) school buildings, with a view to reducing energy costs long-term.
answer_text: The Department of Education is working with all Executive Departments to develop Climate Action Plans that will reduce energy consumption long-term. The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs is leading on the Northern Ireland Executive’s Green Growth Strategy, a multi-decade strategy that will balance climate, environment and economic issues in Northern Ireland. When the Climate Action Plans have been agreed, the Department, in partnership with the Education Authority, will put in place the necessary framework to allow these to be delivered in the schools estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work she is doing to collect data on people accessing foodbanks and other support services provided by the community and voluntary sector, to better inform the policies of her Department and other Executive departments.
answer_text: Officials monitor available Trussell Trust statistics on the numbers accessing their services here alongside statistics produced in the Family Resources Survey on food security. Where there is a funding relationship between my Department and the Voluntary and Community Sector my officials gather data to monitor numbers supported, progress, success and value for money to inform future funding decisions and opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the establishment of a cross-departmental steering group, chaired by her Department, to develop and deliver actions to reduce fuel poverty; and (ii) which organisations and bodies (a) have been engaged to date; and (b) will be engaged with, in this process.
answer_text: My Department continues to have engagement with the Utility Regulator, Consumer Council and other stakeholders in relation to increased energy costs and wider issues relating to fuel poverty. These include exploring further options and actions to mitigate the problems being faced

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health i) for his assessment of the current level of eating disorder training among health professionals; and (ii) what steps he is taking to increase training.
answer_text: Training on eating disorders is delivered through a range of mechanisms. Staff within Adult Eating Disorder Service (AEDS) work as part of a regional network through which experience, learning and evidenced based management is shared for learning and training purposes.  Staff working within the AEDS are encouraged and facilitated to attend training delivered by providers such as the HSC Clinical Education Centre and the Eating Disorder Youth Service – scenario based teaching and meal supervision training.CAMHS clinicians have developed core competencies and skills through specialist training in eating disorders such as the Eating Disorder practitioner course, Body Image work, CBT-E and family based interventions in keeping with NICE core interventions and Junior MARSIPAN guidelines. There is ongoing staff awareness training in house completed by Eating Disorder Practitioners and more recently, voluntary agencies such as BEAT have delivered training for both carers and professionals.Each Trust has developed its own training and awareness approach for staff working outside of the specialism. The range of options is too extensive to list but the most commonly used approaches are: the use of a course provided by the Clinical Education Centre; the provision of modules by Recovery Colleges; and training of medical students.A number of actions within the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 are focused on addressing issues around skills and training.  Action 32 of the Strategy, one of 4 enabling actions which has received funding in 2021/22, commits to a comprehensive workforce review, considering existing workforce need, training and development of new workforce.  This work is already underway.Action 29 also commits to provide further investment so that eating disorder services can achieve optimum staffing levels and skill mix to deliver effective care across the pathways.  A business case for regional eating disorder services is currently being developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the process by which the economic factors of (i) skills gaps; and (ii) staff shortages are actioned into policies by his Department.
answer_text: The new Skills Strategy, ‘Skills for a 10x Economy’, was published in March 2022. It is built around three major policy objectives which aim to boost the supply of skills that will be required to support our economic ambitions over the next decade. Central to the Strategy’s development is the Northern Ireland Skills Barometer, which details the imbalance between the supply and demand for skills under a high-economic growth scenario. It also highlights the oversupply of people entering the labour market with low or no skills, who consequently face an insecure future in a rapidly changing labour market. With this in mind, ‘Addressing skills imbalances, driving economic growth’ has been established as the Strategy’s overarching policy objective.The Barometer is updated every two years by the Ulster University’s Economic Policy Centre and provides an evolving evidence base that identifies which sectors are growing and where demand for skills will arise. This evidence will continue be fed into the work of the nine projects which have been designated to deliver the Strategy’s 49 recommendations, and to help inform how they will address the skills challenges over the ten year life of the Strategy. The Strategy was developed through extensive consultation with stakeholders from business, trade unions, education institutions, the community and voluntary sector and key government departments (Education and Communities). This collaboration will continue as the Strategy work progresses, including through the Northern Ireland Skills Council and engagement with stakeholder groups relevant to each project. Their involvement will be key to ensuring the Strategy is keeps pace with changes in the labour market.The key strategic priorities to be addressed through the Strategy are:addressing the prevalence of low or no skilled individuals in our labour force, where a refreshed approach to lifelong learning will be essential;improving the supply of advanced digital skills (related to cybersecurity, software engineering and computer science) into our economy. There is evidence that undersupply is already constraining business development in key sectors of our economy; andimproving the supply of mid-level professional and technical skills in our workforce, from those leaving education for the first time and through adult learning provision. 	Through this continued collaborative approach, the Department aims to deliver on these priorities to help ensure the skills gaps and shortages are addressed and the Strategy’s objectives achieved.

answer_text: The number of referrals accepted to adults eating disorder services and to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) across Northern Ireland for the previous five years are presented in Table 1. This information is not available by constituency and to do so would be at a disproportionate cost; therefore, the figures have been provided at Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust level.Table 1: Number of referrals accepted to adults eating disorder services and CAMHS by HSC Trust (2017/18 – 2021/22)HSC TrustYear ending 31 March20182019202020212022Belfast*229207223222235Northern**153146165144167Southern192169182193261Western188169171170183Northern Ireland762691741729846Source: Monthly Non Inpatient Mental Health Returns and Monthly CAMHS MDS Returns from HSC Trusts, SPPG.*The Belfast HSC Trust provides an eating disorder service for residents of the South Eastern HSC Trust.**Proxy figures for 2021 and 2022 have been provided for the Northern HSC Trust due to limitations in data availability as a result of ongoing Information System migration.Note 1: The figures provided are in relation to referrals, not 'people'; therefore, it is possible one individual was referred multiple times.Note 2: It is not possible to disaggregate referrals received for eating disorder services in children as this is through a single point of entry for all CAMHS Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the efforts, facilitated by Invest NI, to encourage investment in East Londonderry.
answer_text: Partnership Working & DeliveryMore so than ever the importance of strong partnership working to encourage investment in the region, indigenous or international, cannot be understated. This includes Invest NI, Causeway Chamber, Council, University, Regional Colleges, and all other relevant stakeholders in the area.  Invest NI’s North West Regional Office has been actively working with the key economic development stakeholders in the area. An Economic Advisory Group has been set up as an action from the Community Plan to develop and deliver strategic projects through a collaborative approach. Operationally, a partnership plan has been agreed, with monthly partnership meetings to steer delivery.Key areas of focus include post COVID recovery/rebuild, Entrepreneurship, New Exporters, Innovation, & Skills.A specific partnership initiative to encourage local businesses to consider exporting for the first time was recently completed; locally branded “Causeway Exporters” and under the theme ‘The Export Journey’, six monthly webinars were delivered between September ’21 & February ’22 with support from Invest NI’s Export Division. Causeway Chamber of Commerce led on this initiative. Other partners included Causeway & Roe Valley Enterprise Agencies, Ulster University, both Regional Colleges, and the local Council. In addition to the monthly webinars, 1:1 follow on support was provided to 52 local businesses. Similar partnership initiatives are in progress in the area to encourage higher levels of innovation amongst local businesses as well as the promotion of self-employment as a career option. Growth Deal Announced in July 2019 by the government, £300m ‘Growth Deal’ funding will be shared across Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. The government outlined that the funding for Northern Ireland would support ‘Growth Deals’ in Mid-South West Northern Ireland and the Causeway Coast and Glens area. Circa £36m has been made available from central government for the CC&GC area for projects focused on Innovation, with match funding of same expected from NI Executive for enabling infrastructure projects.  Invest NI has put dedicated resource in place to support the Council who are overseeing the delivery of the ‘Causeway Deal’. Following extensive consultation, a short list of investment projects has recently been completed by the Council; of particular interest to Invest NI are; the proposed Centre for Drug Discovery at the Ulster University campus; Innovation Hub proposal at the designated Enterprise Zone; as well as the NW Regional Colleges proposed ‘Foodovation’ proposal at their Limavady campus.Investment in the last five yearsInvest NI provided support to businesses, which will lead to the creation of 793 new jobs within East Londonderry Parliamentary Constituency. Jobs arising from these projects being created over the next 3 to 5 years, and will result in an increase in annual salaries of £17.72m.Invest NI supported these businesses wi

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the benefits of animal assisted therapy; and (ii) to detail the current prevalence of this therapy in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Animal assisted therapy is considered as an innovative and effective intervention.  It has shown that the interaction with animals can improve social behaviours and communication and can benefit service users for whom the more traditional forms of therapy are not appropriate or do not work. Animal assisted services are offered in a number of facilities throughout Northern Ireland to assist children and adults in improving their mental health, those who suffer from a range of conditions including autistic spectrum disorder, attachment, trauma issues, sensory processing and communication difficulties. The facilities include social farms, animal rehoming centres and equine assisted services facilities.  Referrals have been supported through the Department of Health, Health and Social Care Trusts as well as Department of Education (as part of their Happy Healthy Minds Project).  In addition the Victims and Survivors Service have provided equine therapy services to victims and survivors from Northern Ireland.  There are also instances where an individual or family member will make a personal referral for such services.   The Department of Health are working closely with central government departments including DAERA, Education and Justice along with other organisations to further explore the benefits of this type of intervention.  This partnership is part of an overarching plan for developing a new Equine Strategy for Northern Ireland with Health (equine assisted services) being one of the 3 key strands of work.A number of equine assisted service pilots have taken place for Trust staff to help assess the benefits of this type of intervention for the workforce who have been on the front line delivering services during Covid-19.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current system of NHS dentistry funding; and (ii) for an update on efforts being made to reform NHS dentistry funding to protect frontline services and provision of dentistry.
answer_text: I am pleased to have been able to commit record levels of investment into General Dental Services (GDS) in recent years.To help sustain GDS during the Covid-19 pandemic, my Department has provided £76m in financial support to independent General Dental Practitioners over the last 2 years. Further funding has been committed for the first half of 2022/23 to support dental practices, with dental fees being enhanced by 25% as part of the Rebuilding Support Scheme, to reflect the ongoing pandemic pressures practices are facing. This investment has been supplemented by an additional £6.5m made available to practices via Revenue Grant Schemes in 2020/21 and 2021/22.In addition to this, for the last 5 years, the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration pay uplift recommendations have been implemented in full, including a 3% uplift in 2021/22, representing a total pay uplift of 11.24% since 2017.My Department is committed to working with the British Dental Association and other organisations to develop a new GDS contract that will help build a sustainable, financially viable future for GDS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 29639/17-22, to detail (i) the implications of the current migrant labour laws on the success of the potential Attract In/Attract Back programme; and (ii) what conversations he has had with the UK Government in relation to any changes to these laws that may be necessary to launch this programme.
answer_text: The Attract In/Attract back programme, currently under development by Invest NI, will take full account of the current immigration rules as set by the UK Government.		While my offcials and I continue to engage with the UK Government on the immigration system there are no specific changes that are required to lauch the Attract In/Attract Back programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Education Welfare Officers employed in each constituency, in each of the last five years
answer_text: The Education Welfare Service does not organise its regional service around parliamentary boundaries rather through main offices based in Armagh, Ballymena, Belfast, Dundonald and Omagh. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why Northern Ireland does not take part in the Warm Homes Discount Scheme, which is available in all other regions of the UK.
answer_text: It is not possible for Northern Ireland to join the Warm Homes Discount Scheme as the underpinning legislation makes the scheme specific to GB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30249/17-22, to detail the simplification that has been made to regional Direct Payments guidance.
answer_text: Direct Payments are made to a service user or carer following an assessment of their needs, in order to achieve an agreed outcome. It should be noted that this principle remains unchanged. In response to requests from carers, Northern Ireland Guidance for People Receiving Direct Payments was published on 3 August 2020. The guidance encourages Health and Social Care Trusts to apply flexibilities in the use of Direct Payments, in order to better meet the needs of service users and carers during the COVID-19 pandemic. The guidance can be accessed at: www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidance-direct-payments HSC Trusts are expected to support those people in receipt of Direct Payments by applying a flexible approach to meeting their needs and agreed outcomes, with the underpinning guiding principle being to ensure individuals safely receive the care and support they require.The flexibilities and simplification included in the guidance include paying a close family member where care has broken down, use of unspent funds and paying for PPE if required. Usually, a close family member who lives in the same household is not permitted by current guidance to receive payment, however given the exceptional circumstances arising from the COVID-19 pandemic, a short-term, temporary arrangement may be considered in discussion with the named worker and with prior, written permission from the local Trust.Health and Social Care Trusts will adopt this more flexible approach to the use of Direct Payments until 30 September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any funding that exists for community and voluntary groups to memorialise unmarked graves or former institutional settings where many people lived and died.
answer_text: My department does not provide any specific funding for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what provisions are available to support children and their families, assessed with special educational needs, when the Special Educational Needs Coordinator in the school is absent for a lengthy period of time.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs Coordinator (SENCo) is responsible for many day to day operational duties for special educational needs (SEN) within a school. All relevant staff will also be aware of the provision in place for those pupils with assessed SEN.  In the absence of a SENCo, their duties will need to be delegated, and as with all staff absences, it is up the school Principal to designate a staff member to carry out SENCO duties, or assume the role themselves to ensure provision for children with SEN continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what forms of redress are available for patients whose surgeries, or other medical appointments, for serious illness were cancelled or delayed due to the pandemic and led to worse health outcomes.
answer_text: There is no specific form of redress in relation to patients whose surgeries, or other medical appointments, for serious illness were cancelled or delayed due to the pandemic and led to worse health outcomes.Generally, redress is available where:negligence is admitted or proven and compensation is paid; the Public Services Ombudsman finds that there has been maladministration or service failure in the case of a complainant; ora public inquiry recommends that a redress scheme should be set up, and the relevant authority accepts that recommendation. Whilst I fully accept that the pandemic has had a devastating impact on health and social care capacity, access to surgery and medical appointments continued to be available and prioritised for the most seriously ill people.  Unfortunately a number of theatre lists had to be postponed by Trusts at very short notice to allow staff to treat those seriously ill with COVID-19, and patients affected were rebooked for treatment at the earliest opportunity.  Those circumstances, whilst highly regrettable, were unavoidable in the context of the pandemic.  As Health Minister I am committed to increasing capacity to reduce waiting times within the budget available to my Department.  As recently as last month I announced a further £46m for immediate capacity boosting in year and £16m in recurrent funding to reduce waits across elective care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the steps she has taken to ensure greater security for private tenants in rental contracts; and (ii) for her assessment of the pressures on the private rental market in areas where there is a large tourism market, such as parts of East Londonderry.
answer_text: The Private Tenancies Act 2022 will make the private rented sector a safer and more secure housing option for people living in it.  The Act increases the Notice to Quit (NTQ) periods Landlords must provide to tenants which will increase further in the longer term once regulations regarding exceptions have been considered.Policy responsibility for short term tourist accommodation lies with the Department for Economy with holiday lets regulated by Tourism NI subject to licensing requirements. Housing need and demand models, across all tenures, take into account the demand for such accommodation.  		The need for improved housing related data collection and analysis forms part of ongoing work on my Department’s Housing Supply Strategy.  In this context, it is worth highlighting that the Housing Executive is already proposing to conduct research in the near future which will aim to assess effect of holiday homes and short term lets on the homes in the housing market.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the steps she has taken to alleviate financial stress on the estimated 50,000 people of pensionable age living in relative poverty; and (ii) the efforts she has made to highlight the financial support available for older people in financial difficulties who are eligible but unaware of the support available.
answer_text: My Department provides the Make the Call Service which aims to ensure that all individuals and their families are receiving the benefits, supports and services they are entitled to claim. This Service offers free and confidential needs assessments for individuals to check for entitlement to social security benefits, additional supports and services. Make the Call also has an extensive range of partnerships with community-based organisations and other departments which extends its reach and accessibility. Sitting alongside this partnership offer, the Make the Call community-based Outreach Officers work with local community, charitable and voluntary groups, to raise awareness of potential benefit entitlements and provide practical help in accessing them.In addition, my Department continues to work collaboratively with Age NI and other trusted key stakeholders in the community to raise awareness and seek to increase Pension Credit uptake.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what conversations she has had with football clubs and bodies regarding Northern Ireland’s expected joint hosting of Euro 2028; and (ii) for her assessment of the impact that the delay in the funding for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer will have on Northern Ireland's ability to host matches, training camps and training matches.
answer_text: My Department along with other Government partners are continuing to engage with the football associations, including the IFA, on the joint ‘Expression of Interest’ to host the UEFA European Championship Finals in 2028. 	In terms of the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme, the challenges created by not having a functioning Executive or agreed Executive budget means I cannot bring new proposals for endorsement and agreement as is required.	 	I met with representatives from IFA and NIFL, and have agreed to work with them to progress this project to application stage.		I remain committed to moving this Programme forward and will continue to work to progress it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to outline the current youth services available in East Londonderry; and (ii) to detail the amount spent by her Department on youth services, in each constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Policy responsibility for Youth Services rests with the Department for Education.  However, my Department currently funds a number of youth initiatives in East Derry.  I have provided an update as below on youth services.The Coleraine Education Community Project (CECP) is a schools based project that has been successfully operating across Coleraine for over ten years, which links nine schools that work within or have significant numbers of pupils from the two Neighbourhood Renewal Areas (NRAs) of Coleraine East (Ballysally and Millburn) and Coleraine West (The Heights and Killowen). This initiative was allocated £ 45,053.16 in the current financial year.The second project is the Ballysally Integrated Nurturing Project (BINP) which provides an effective nurturing service for families and individuals of all ages, with a cradle to grave approach of supportive developmental work. This project was allocated £159,613.28 in the current financial year.The Dry Arch Children’s Centre – Enjoying, Learning and Achieving in Limavady provides a Youth Mentor/Family Support Worker for 25 hours per week to target children, families and young people at critical stages in their lives, so that support and interventions are available to those in danger of becoming involved in antisocial behaviour or are marginalised thus effecting their educational attainment. Additionally in 2021/22 the Neighbourhood Renewal programme, awarded funding of £17,880 to Roe Valley Residents Association in Limavady to externally revamp the newly opened Youth Hub in the town centre.As I explained earlier the provision of funding to youth services has a wider remit than my Department, but overall information pertaining to funding awarded can be accessed on the Government Funders Database which is accessible via the link below:https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk/

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of units of social housing created in each constituency, in each of the last five years; (ii) the total number of social housing units in each constituency; and (iii) the actions she is taking to increase the number of social housing units in East Londonderry.

A further 25 units have been completed to date in the current programme year.A further 255 social housing units are programmed to start through the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2022/23 – 2024/25 in the constituency.Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval.
