question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on action being taken to find tenants for Coleraine’s Atlantic Link Enterprise Campus.
answer_text: Causeway Coast & Glens Borough Council (CC&GBC) is responsible for the ongoing promotion and marketing of the designated area for the Pilot Enterprise Zone in Coleraine, called the Atlantic Link Enterprise Campus (ALEC). My Department’s role is to ensure that the ALEC is developed by CC&GBC in a way which will attract capital intensive companies.		As of the end of May 2022 the following activity has been undertaken by CC&GBC to market the site:Promotion of the ALEC via a Development Brief to prospective businesses, investors, developers etc. A copy is available on the dedicated ALEC website: http://atlanticlinkcampus.com/wp-content/uploads/atlantic-link-developement-brief.pdf Annual full-page advertisements in all regional and local newspapers (16 newspapers); and front cover page articles in both Agenda NI and Eolas.Regular monthly wide-spread mailshot of both new hard and soft copies of the ALEC Brochure and Development Brief to prospective investors regionally, nationally and internationally.Exhibition stands at large scale digital focused conferences in Belfast and Dublin.ALEC information events/roadshows in Belfast, Dublin and London.   Investigation of proposed Growth Deal opportunities.A new property agent has been appointed. They are currently preparing a strategy going forward. Invest NI has assisted with promotional events to potential investors at the British Embassy in Dublin and the Guildhall in London. Invest NI’s International Investment Division continue to facilitate introductions to relevant events, conferences, and contacts in GB, RoI, and the US. Invest NI also promote the Campus on the property section of its website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of (a) paid; and (b) unpaid sports coaches; and (ii) the support that her Department provides to encourage new coaches, and to retain existing coaches, particularly those working with young people.
answer_text: My Department does not keep a record of the number of paid or unpaid sports coaches as the recruitment of sports coaches is a matter for Clubs, Sports Governing Bodies and Councils.  Sport NI provides support to the sector to encourage new coaches, and to retain existing coaches, including those who work with young people, through a number of programmes including the Club Environment Transition Programme, the Club Coach Developer Programme, the Performance Coach Developer programme, the annual SportMaker Awards and Sport NI Learning Provision.  In addition, Tollymore National Outdoor Centre (TNOC) provides a range of courses for coaches in adventure sports and physical recreation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process by which she determined the 28 schools that are to benefit from the Major Capital Works Programme.
answer_text: The eligibility and scoring criteria were set out in the Protocol which was published in advance of the call for Major Capital Works being launched. The latest call closed on 14 January 2022 and by the closing date, thirty four school nominations were received. The nominated schools were assessed against the Gateway criteria in the Protocol and six schools which did not meet the eligibility criteria were subsequently ruled out during this assessment. On 21 March, I was pleased to announce that all of the remaining 28 schools which had passed the eligibility assessment would be taken forward for major capital investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps have been taken to increase the number and capacity of crematoriums in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is a matter for a councils to decide whether they wish to provide this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps are being taken to increase fuel allowance rates for domiciliary care workers.
answer_text: In light of the recent significant increases in fuel costs, my Department, Employers and Trade Unions have been involved in national and local discussions regarding a potential review of the current Agenda for Change (AfC) Mileage Rates.The national discussions have been led through the NHS Staff Council and have been exploring the options in relation to mileage reimbursement rates. Unfortunately it has not been possible to conclude a National agreement on an increase in fuel reimbursement rates.In light of no nationally agreed position, temporary increases have been agreed in Scotland and Wales until the end of July and June respectively. At the time of those agreements, no decision could be made locally due to election period restrictions. My Department is, however, considering options for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of healthy eating habits in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail any current or proposed cross-departmental schemes to make healthy food cheaper and more easily accessible for households.
answer_text: (i)  The 2020/21 Health Survey asked “in general, would you say that your eating habits are?”. Survey results showed that 61% of responders (aged 16+) assessed their healthy eating habits as ‘good’ or ‘very good’.  Response2020/2195% confidence intervalsVery good19%16.6 to 20.7Good42%39.1 to 44.3Fair29%26.5 to 31.2Bad9%7.5 to 10.4Very bad2%1.2 to 2.6However, the latest data from the National Diet and Nutrition Survey (NDNS) for Northern Ireland https://www.food.gov.uk/research/national-diet-and-nutrition-survey/national-diet-and-nutrition-survey-ndns-report-for-northern-ireland (2012/13 – 2016/17) showed that:the consumption of 5 A Day fruit and vegetable portions was below the recommendation in all age/sex groups;the average consumption of fruit and vegetables for children aged 11 to 18 years in Northern Ireland was 2.5 portions per day with 94% not meeting the 5 A Day recommendation; andon average adults aged 19 to 64 years consumed 3.4 portions per day and adults aged 65 years and over consumed 3.3 portions per day with around 80% not meeting the 5 A Day recommendation.Recent take home food and drink purchasing data provided by Kantar FMCG Purchase Panel (https://www.food.gov.uk/research/wider-consumer-interests/northern-ireland-take-home-food-and-drink-purchases-2016-2019-2020) demonstrates there was an increase in energy (kcal), total sugars (g), total fat (g) and saturated fat (g) purchased per person per day during 2020, compared to a decrease from 2016 to 2019(ii) My Department is currently working with stakeholders to develop a new obesity strategy to follow on from the current ‘A Fitter Future For All 2012-2022’ obesity prevention strategic framework. We also continue to work with the Department of Communities on food poverty and Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on the new Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework which has a principal to increase access and affordability to healthy nutritious food.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) his plans to make better use of the skills and experience of the community and voluntary sector to deliver health and social care; and (ii) how he will support this sector to do so.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current levels of diabetes; (ii) to outline the current policy on prevention and treatment; and (iii) to detail what new treatment options are being considered.
answer_text: I recognise that diabetes is one of the most challenging chronic conditions facing people in Northern Ireland and impacts both at a population and individual level. Raw disease prevalence information for Northern Ireland 2021/22, recently published by my Department, indicates that, at 31st March 2022, there were 108,223 patients recorded on the General Practice Quality and Outcomes Framework diabetes register, which is a register of the number of patients aged 17 years and over with diabetes mellitus (specified as type 1 or type 2 diabetes).My Department published a Strategic Framework for Diabetes in Northern Ireland in 2016. The Framework, which was developed in partnership with a range of stakeholders, including Diabetes UK and people living with diabetes, included a 3-year action plan which aimed to improve outcomes and services for people living with diabetes or at risk of developing diabetes. The Diabetes Network supports the implementation of the Diabetes Strategic Framework and action plan and provides strategic direction for diabetes treatment and care in Northern Ireland. It brings together people living with diabetes, carers, and health and social care professionals working in partnership with Diabetes UK. The Network is based on an ethos of collective leadership and aims to enable people living with diabetes and frontline experts in diabetes care to work in partnership to co-design services for future diabetes care and improvements in the long term health for people living with the condition.  Key achievements and initiatives by the Diabetes Network to support prevention and treatment of diabetes include: Development of a regional integrated foot-care pathway. The pathway is based on a multi-disciplinary approach that operates across primary care, community and acute hospital services.  It is focussed on the prevention of amputations and includes foot assessment and risk assignment, regular review for those with foot complications, delivery of foot health education as part of a structured education programme, acute phase management and specialist investigations for new episodes of diabetic foot disease, and management of acute diabetic foot disease and diabetic foot emergencies, including the care of patients pre- and post-amputation; In January 2022, the launch of a new regional insulin pumps service, developed in partnership alongside Diabetes UK and people living with diabetes.  The new model has been supported by £2million funding from my Department and aims to ensure equitable access to pumps and support across Northern Ireland for people with Type 1 diabetes. The pathway, ethos and approach for the service have been agreed and this will be developed over the next 3 years.  When in place the service will help support people to get the best outcomes from their pump therapy and ensure that diabetes teams across the region can share good practice and optimise care;  The development of standardised protocols for the management of dia

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the existing policies that require schools to (a) teach children the importance of healthy eating; and (b) provide healthy meals and snacks; and (ii) what efforts are being made to improve the policies.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of young people being educated to adopt a healthy lifestyle.  The Personal Development and Mutual Understanding area of learning at primary level and the Learning for Life and Work (LLW) area of learning at post-primary level are statutory elements within the Northern Ireland curriculum.  These areas of learning provide opportunities for pupils, from Foundation Stage to Key Stage 3, to learn effectively how to keep themselves healthy and safe, aligned to the importance of healthy eating.  The Home Economics strand of LLW aims to further equip pupils with the knowledge, skills and values that will help them make appropriate lifestyle choices.  This includes understanding food choices and nutritional health in a society where obesity is an increasing problem and diet-related illness is prevalent.  Pupils also have the opportunity to pursue qualifications at Key Stage 4 and post-16 that explicitly cover issues relevant to healthy eating, for example, the GCSE in Food and Nutrition and the A Level in Nutrition and Food Science.To support schools in the delivery of the curriculum, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) develops and produces curricular guidance and teaching support materials for use in schools.  This includes resources to address the broader issues of obesity, over-consumption of junk food and general health.  The CCEA Wellbeing Hub also supports the teaching of young people to learn about the role of exercise, healthy eating and healthy habits as part of good health.The Food in Schools policy sets out the Departments’ policy in relation to all food in schools, support available, roles, responsibilities and funding. https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/food-schools-policyNutritional Standards for food in schools are currently being updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to (i) outline what work has been undertaken to assess levels of loneliness in Northern Ireland; and (ii) detail any steps that are being taken to address the issue.
answer_text: The rising prevalence of loneliness, especially amongst older people and more recently younger people has been identified in practice and research. In 2020, Northern Ireland Council for Voluntary Action (NIVCA) research concluded that one in twenty people experience chronic loneliness in Northern Ireland. In 2021, the Wellbeing In Northern Ireland Report produced by Northern Ireland Statistics Research Agency (NISRA) produced data from the Continuous Household Survey which assesses the levels of loneliness, self-efficacy, personal wellbeing an locus of control in Northern Ireland. The Department of Health loneliness team has met with NISRA and has contributed to their final report.  Northern Ireland does not have a specific strategy to address loneliness, however it is acknowledged as an important but cross cutting issue that no one organization can tackle working alone. It requires a collaborative approach involving all sectors, central government, agencies, voluntary and community sector and beyond. My Department is represented on the All Party Group for loneliness. A number of meetings have been held following its formation in 2019. Many current policy areas and programmes in the Department lead by HSC organisations do contribute significantly to tackling loneliness and social isolation. These include the Reform of Social Adult Care, the Social Work Strategy, the Mental Health Strategy, the work undertaken by the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams.A Department Of Health loneliness co-ordination team has been established to work alongside the wider HSC organisations and an HSC Forum has been set up.  These structures will help to raise awareness and destigmatise these important issues as they impact on the health and wellbeing for those who are vulnerable or at risk.    My Department continues to work in partnership with the ROI, UK and beyond, to learn from other jurisdictions and share information on innovative approaches to tackle loneliness in support of positive outcomes for health and wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of current melanoma rates in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what steps he has taken to both reduce rates and treat melanoma.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the current incidence of skin cancer has been rising in recent decades and it is now the most common form of cancer.  Northern Ireland Cancer Registry’s most recent figures on malignant melanoma indicate that at the end of 2019, there were 4,787 people (Males: 1,914; Females: 2,873) with malignant melanoma who had been diagnosed in Northern Ireland during 1995 to 2019.   My Department’s Health Improvement Policy Branch launched a Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy for Northern Ireland in 2011, which aimed to reduce the incidences and deaths from skin cancer. While skin cancer is now the most common form of cancer in Northern Ireland, in the majority of cases it can be effectively treated if detected at an early stage.  The Strategy identifies children and young adults, as well as people who spend a significant amount of time outdoors, as key target groups. Over exposure to UV radiation is widely accepted as the underlying cause for skin cancer and experts believe that four out of five cases could be prevented by adopting simple precautions. Skin cancer prevention initiatives have included general “care in the sun” awareness raising; specific targeting of at-risk groups such as outdoor workers and sports clubs; educational resources for schools and for summer schemes. A mid-term review of the Strategy was completed in April 2017.  Overall, the review found that significant progress had been made in terms of implementing the strategy and action plan during the period 2011/12 to 2016/17.  However, given the nature of skin cancer and long lead in period, rates remain high and are predicted to increase. An extension to the Strategy (which was due to expire in July 2021) was agreed to allow for an end review to take place when pressures allow.In addition, the Sunbeds Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 provided for tighter regulation of sunbeds and in particular, prohibits the use, sale or hire of a sunbed to anyone under the age of 18.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated number of unregistered carers; and the amount of money that their work saves the public purse each year.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he has taken to improve treatment for people with epilepsy.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group, formerly known as the Health and Social Care Board are working with my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services to improve services to patients with neurological conditions including epilepsy.The Review, which resumed in June 2021, is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years, determining the associated investment required and outlining an implementation plan. As part of the Review, a specific work stream has been established to develop a neurology care pathway to support those with long-term neurological conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on his plans to implement the recommendations of the Bengoa report; and (ii) for his assessment of how implementing these recommendations would impact on the efficiency and delivery of the health service.
answer_text: Health and Wellbeing 2026: Delivering Together sets out my Department’s response to the recommendations included in the Bengoa Report.  Delivering Together sets out to stabilise, reconfigure and to transform Health and Social Care services.  (i) Since the launch of Delivering Together in October 2016, three reports on the progress of the ring-fenced transformation funding projects have been published in 2017, 2019 and 2021.  These reports are available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/progressreportintroduction (ii) The evidence contained within these three progress reports, particularly the most recent report, confirms that this programme of activity continues to be successful in stabilising, reconfiguring and improving our health and social care services.I have also published strategies for mental health, elective care and cancer services and have launched a consultation on urgent and emergency care.  Together, with Health and Wellbeing 2026, these strategies articulate a clear trajectory to stabilise, reconfigure and improve our Health and Social Care Services.  I have however made it clear that delivering this agenda will require sustained recurrent funding and support for change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the specific support that exists for people who have experienced trauma.
answer_text: SPPG can only comment on Major Trauma i.e. serious multi-system injury which requires multi-specialty input and has an Injury Severity Score (ISS) between 9 and 15+. All other trauma (ISS <9-15+, minor injury, or psychological trauma) is outside our ability to answer.When a person sustains trauma and if they meet certain clinical criteria then NIAS will bring them to the Major Trauma Centre (MTC) at the RVH for multi-specialty care; this is true for patients if they are within 45 minutes' drive of the RVH. If a person sustains trauma but it does not meet the clinical criteria or if they are outside of a 45 minute drive time to the MTC, then they will be taken to their local Type 1 trauma-receiving unit. The clinical criteria is set out in the Northern Ireland Major Trauma Triage Tool (NIMTTT) and the governing SOP is called 'The Referral and Reverse Referral of Patients to the Major Trauma Centre...'. This type of major trauma system has been shown to improve survival rates by up to 20%.There are seven sites that are classified as Type 1 hospitals in Northern Ireland: Altnagelvin (ALT), South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), Causeway (CAU), Antrim Area Hospital (AAH), Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH), The Ulster Hospital Dundonald (UHD), and Daisy Hill Hospital (DHH).  However, the latter (DHH) has recently removed acute surgical provision and will be routinely bypassed by NIAS for cases of significantly injured patients.At the Type 1 receiving unit the patient will avail of the full range of commissioned services associated with the management of physical trauma at that site. This will include emergency department input, imaging and diagnostics, acute surgical intervention and in-patient recovery. AAH and CAU will not provide orthopaedic surgical input as it is not available in the Northern Trust; in such cases they may directly refer orthopaedic patients to RVH if required. Additionally, if it is subsequently determined at a type 1 unit that a patient hits predetermined major trauma markers then they will be automatically sent to the MTC under the 'Call & Send' protocol. At the Major Trauma Centre (MTC) in Belfast arriving patients will also avail of the full range of commissioned services for major trauma. These include emergency department input; imaging and diagnostics; interventional radiology; acute surgery (neuro, vascular, cardiothoracic, orthopaedic, general); plastics are located at UHD but a local agreement provides early access to services at the RVH site.The MTC also provides a dedicated major trauma unit/ward which provides a consultant-led multidisciplinary team of trauma specialists. The major trauma unit provides direct input from non-medical consultants in rehabilitation medicine; a regional nerve block room; a major trauma review clinic; and a fracture clinic. Clinical psychology is currently not commissioned for major trauma at the MTC and any input from that specialty has been on a good will basis to date.The Royal Belfast Ho

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the publication of the Strategic Investment Board report into the need of and potential for a veterinary school in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Strategic Investment Board for Northern Ireland (SIB) was commissioned to undertake a formal assessment of the need and an independent analysis of the various options for better assuring the supply of veterinarians, and an associated centre of excellence for innovation and research, in Northern Ireland. The Department has received the SIB’s assessment of need and officials are considering next steps.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of bowel cancer (i) prevalence; (ii) survival rates; and (iii) treatment waiting times, with reference to how these compare to other parts of the UK and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: PrevalenceNorthern Ireland Cancer Registry prevalence data suggests that at the end of 2019 there were approximately 8,861 colorectal cancer survivors living in Northern Ireland.  (Colorectal Fact Sheet, Northern Ireland Cancer Registry-Factsheet colorectal.pdf (ncri.ie))SurvivalMost recent data from the NI Cancer Registry data suggests that 81.1% of people diagnosed with colorectal cancer in NI survive for one year and 61.5% survive for 5 years (based on patients diagnosed 2010-2014).  Office for National Statistics data for patients diagnosed in England 2013-17 suggests one and five year survival of approximately 80% and 60% respectively.  One year survival data is not available for the Republic of Ireland but the 5 year survival is comparable at 62.2%. (The 31 day standard aims to ensure that patients wait no longer than 31 days between the decision to treat and commencement of first definitive treatment. The 62 day standard aims to ensure that patients wait no longer than 62 days from receipt of referral to the commencement of first definitive treatment).It is acknowledged that performance against cancer waiting times in Northern Ireland is poor.  Performance on the 31 day standard for colorectal cancer consistently sits at around 95% against a target of 98%.  In 2021/22, 31 day performance was 95%; the equivalent figure in England was 93%.  Performance against the 62 day standard has been consistently low ranging from 47% in 2018/19 to just 18% in 2020/21 (the equivalent figure for England for 2020/21 was 50%),  Essentially the 31day standard reflects the treatment pathway, and performance, whilst still below the stated target of 98%, is consistently high.  The fact that we are significantly underperforming on the 62 day pathway therefore reflects challenges, not within the treatment pathway, but within the diagnostic pathway.Within colorectal cancer pathway the main diagnostic tools are colonoscopy and CT scans.  COVID impact aside, Northern Ireland has had significant demand capacity gap within scopes for some years which until recently Trusts have attempted to manage year to year using non-recurrent funding.  I have recently announced recurrent funding to support 20 additional regional scopes sessions at LVH and 10 additional sessions at Omagh.  It is hoped that some of these sessions will commence by October. When fully implemented this investment will give capacity for 9,000 additional scopes per year. It is anticipated that this will have a significant impact on regional waiting time for scopes.In addition to this the service is taking forward a range of other measures to help address back logs.Theatre Utilisation for endoscopy lists –  At a regional level we are currently delivering 85% run time against a 90% target.   Improvement work is underway to look at how we increase efficiency to allow more capacity within current resources.Independent Sector insourcing – Belfast and South Eastern Trusts have established insourcing arrangement

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) actions he has taken to better support parents who have lost a child to stillbirth or neonatal death; and (ii) work undertaken to better understand the causes of stillbirth and neonatal death.
answer_text: (i)  All Trusts recognise the importance of supporting parents who have lost a child to stillbirth or experienced a neonatal death. In these sad circumstances, a wide range of support services for bereaved parents are available across each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust and delivered in partnership between clinicians, bereavement coordinators and the community and voluntary sector. In cases of stillbirth or early neonatal death, all Trusts in Northern Ireland have a Bereavement Midwife who provides support and guidance for bereaved women, partners and their families. This support and guidance will be tailored to meet the needs of the individual woman, her partner and family as appropriate. The Bereavement Midwife also works to ensure that all staff within the relevant healthcare settings are supported and enabled to provide compassionate and respectful care for families who experience such a loss. Each Trust has also had a Trust Bereavement Coordinator in place since 2006, when the HSC Bereavement Network was established to provide information, guidance and resources for those who care for and support people around the time of death and bereavement, as well as a Bereavement Forum which meets regularly with a wide membership comprising staff and service users. In addition, the Department has commissioned a Palliative and Life Limited Service (PALLS) from the NI Children’s Hospice. With the support of a dedicated specialist nurse and access to a bed in the Children’s Hospice (or the child’s own home), there is wrap around support for those families where the baby is expected to die shortly after birth. PALLS allows the parents to spend time together as a family in a supportive environment with time for memory making before the baby passes away. Follow up bereavement care and support is also provided to help the family in these difficult circumstances.(ii) Reducing stillbirth and neonatal death is a priority for the HSC system. A number of work streams are in place across the region with the aim of reducing perinatal mortality:Implementation of the Department of Health England’s Saving Babies’ Lives Care Bundle which first issued in 2016 with an updated version published in 2019 is underway in Northern Ireland. The focus is on reducing the incidence of stillbirth and neonatal death.  Saving Babies Lives 2 saw the addition of work on preventing premature births where possible. The Public Health Agency (PHA) was asked by the Department to complete an assessment of the extent to which work to reduce stillbirth is embedded across the HSC. A regional action plan to reduce stillbirths has been put in place and a regional group, led by the PHA with representation from the five Trusts, has been tasked with overseeing and co-ordinating this important work.UK Perinatal surveillance has been in place since 1992 and a consortium called MBRRACE-UK (Mothers and Babies: Reducing risk though audit and confidential enquiries) led by the National Perinatal Unit i

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) number of people living with a neuro-disability, broken down by constituency; (ii) support that exists for sufferers; and (iii) recent scientific, medical and therapeutic advances that have been made in treatment and diagnosis.
answer_text: My Department does not hold figures on the number of people living with a neuro-disability in Northern Ireland, however, the average number of annual admissions and individuals admitted to neurology and neurosurgery specialties in Northern Ireland by Assembly constituency (2018-19 to 2020-21) is provided below. Figures relate to the average number of admissions or number of individuals for the last 3 years of complete data (2018-19 to 2020-21). Data has been rounded to the nearest whole person/ admission:Average annual number of admissions and individuals admitted to Neurology and Neurosurgery Specialties in Northern Ireland by Assembly Area of residence (2018-19 to 2020-21) AdmissionsIndividuals Neurosurgery SpecialtyNeurology SpecialtyTotalTotalBelfast East64154218125Belfast North116241357201Belfast South85223308166Belfast West149173322211East Antrim77176253135East Londonderry88196284130Fermanagh And South Tyrone73202275127Foyle81207288107Lagan Valley93313406202Mid Ulster77182259135Newry And Armagh31578840North Antrim100252352171North Down111206317146South Antrim84264348137South Down92212304144Strangford86269355156Upper Bann78217295137West Tyrone77320397174Unknown33215437Northern Ireland1,5953,8855,4802,681Neuro-disabilities are a very wide ranging group of congenital or acquired long term conditions, including brain injury and autism. The Regional Specialist Acquired Brain Injury In-patient Unit (RABIU) at Musgrave Park Hospital serves the whole of NI.   Staff within RABIU link with the Multi-disciplinary Brain Injury Teams within each of the five HSC Trusts, and these teams provide rehabilitation and support following a period of in-patient treatment.  My Department also has two regional contracts, one to provide vocational training and support to people with an acquired brain injury and the other is to provide recreational support to people and their carers.   My Department has established formal links with NICE whereby guidance published by NICE is locally reviewed for applicability to NI and, where appropriate, endorsed by the Department for implementation in Health and Social Care.  This link has ensured that NI has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions.  My Department has also recently contributed to the call for evidence for the development of an Acquired Brain Injury Cross-Government Strategy lead by Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) England.  In addition, the development of an Autism Strategy for Northern Ireland for the period 2023-2028 is also currently underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the (a) financial; and (b) respite support that his Department is providing to all carers; and (ii) what steps he is taking to contact unregistered carers to provide access to the support they need.
answer_text: (i) (a) Financial support for carers is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.  While my Department does not make direct financial payments to carers, the HSC Trusts do have the facility to make a cash grant payment to carers following the completion of an assessment of their need.  Such payments can be made to replace a broken domestic appliance or to pay for a short break for the carer.In recognition of the need to support all carers, I was pleased to launch the Support for Carers Fund last year, with £4.4m allocated to the fund.  The aim was to provide grant funding to organisations with charitable status that can deliver outcomes to improve the lives and experiences of carers.  The second round of awards, totalling more than £1m, was announced in March 2022.  This announcement followed the allocation of almost £600,000 in the first round of funding, announced last September, and I am pleased to advise that a third call for applications for funding is currently open, with a closing date of 20 June 2022.A wide range of projects have received funding to date, including the provision of practical support, wellbeing events, respite, advocacy support, and mental health support. Projects also cover both adult and young carers. I look forward to seeing how they make a difference in the lives and experiences of individual carers in the coming months and years.(b) Significant efforts have been made by Trusts to implement the Public Health Agency Pathway to remobilise respite services to manage risk, while increasing service provision on a phased basis, throughout a period of high community prevalence of Covid-19.Short Breaks provision across statutory health & social care settings has significantly increased.  However, overall capacity has reduced as a number of respite beds available in care homes have been withdrawn by independent sector providers. There remains further adjustments to public health guidance required before services can be fully restore to pre-pandemic levels.  Therefore, officials have written to the PHA asking them to commence an urgent formal review of the Pathway and associated health protection guidelines.Aligned to this process, the Department has requested performance and capacity data across all Trusts, which will be published on the Department’s website in June 2022.(ii) In 2021, in recognition of the need to reach out to unpaid carers in particular, my Department organised a leaflet drop through the door of every home in Northern Ireland, in an attempt to encourage those in a caring role to seek the help and support they need and may be entitled to.Trusts continue to proactively reach out to identify informal, unpaid carers to ensure they have the support they require in their caring role, and are able to access appropriate advice and support.  This is done by working closely with GPs, pharmacists, and the community and voluntary sector, especially condition specific agencies and other public agencies such

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail what steps she is taking to enable pre-schools and nurseries in the voluntary and community sector to take in more children and operate to their full capacity; and (ii) for her assessment of how this sector can be best supported to cater to those parents and children who live long distances from the nearest statutory pre-school or nursery.
answer_text: It is my Department’s aim to provide one year of funded pre-school education for every child whose parents want it.  Funded pre-school places are provided through the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP), and accessed via a preference based admissions process.The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) has responsibility for securing non-statutory pre-school education provision in local areas and operates within strict parameters in doing so, allocating sufficient funded places to meet the level of demand in local areas, whilst avoiding over provision which can lead to unnecessary costs and destabilise the pre-school education programme overall. All non-statutory settings are allocated places for one year at a time, and no setting is guaranteed a minimum allocation, or one that will accommodate all first preference applications.  My Department plays no role in the making of operational decisions about the allocation or funding of non-statutory pre-school education places in local areas. The EA PEG keeps the provision of funded places under constant review as the pre-school admissions process progresses and may contact non-statutory pre-school settings directly to allocate additional funded places should they identify a need, e.g. where there is a shortfall of provision in a local area. Non-statutory settings also have flexibility to offer additional services if they wish to do so, such as wrap-around and fee paying childcare provision should they choose to do so.All funded pre-school settings follow the same curricular guidelines and are inspected to the same educational standards. When applying for a funded pre-school education place, parents are encouraged to apply for a range of pre-school settings they would be content for their child to attend in order to maximise their opportunity of being offered a funded place in a pre-school setting of their preference.  It may reassure you to know that in each of the last seven years, at least 99.8% of parents who stayed with the admissions process to the end received the offer of a funded pre-school education place in a setting of their preference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of rural crime levels; and (ii) to detail the efforts being made to prevent and prosecute offences.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) has primary responsibility for preventing and detecting rural crime in Northern Ireland.  Rural crime covers a wide range of crime including theft of machinery, burglaries of isolated farms and livestock crime.The PSNI issues regular updates on agriculture and rural crime statistics and trends.  The latest report is available at Agriculture and Rural Crime in Northern Ireland Monthly Update 31st March 2022 (psni.police.uk).  The is shows that from 1st April 2021 to 31st March 2022 there were 196 agricultural crimes recorded in Northern Ireland, a fall of 95 on the previous 12 months and continuing the downward trend since 2010/11.My Department’s Welfare and Enforcement Branch (WEB) liaises very closely with the PSNI and the Department of Justice (DOH) who have primary responsibility for preventing and detecting rural crime in Northern Ireland. WEB represents DAERA on a number of strategic and operational rural crime prevention related groups.WEB is a founder member of the Rural Crime Partnership (RCP). Work on this partnership includes shared responsibility for delivery of the detailed RCP Rural Crime Action Plan which has strands covering Prevent & Protect; Partnership Working; and Enforcement.Officials also work with the charity Crimestoppers to encourage reporting and ask the public to give information anonymously about rural crime across Northern Ireland.In addition, my Department is a member of the operational cross-border multi-agency Rural Crime Taskforce which includes the PSNI, an Garda Síochána, Her Majesty’s Revenue & Customs, Revenue & Customs (RoI) and the Food Standards Agency. This taskforce targets, investigates and prosecutes serious rural crime, often with a cross border dimension including livestock theft and smuggling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any cross-departmental work she has overseen with the Department for Infrastructure to ensure that the planning system addresses housing need.
answer_text: I recently met with the newly appointed Infrastructure Minister John O'DowdMy officials continue to engage with Department for Infrastructure colleagues to explore how to unlock and prioritise planning for sites in key areas of social housing need. Alongside this I have led work to develop a fundamental Housing Supply Strategy which is aimed at identifying and unlocking major levers for increasing supply across all housing tenures. I recognise that the planning system is one of those levers that can address our housing supply issues and my officials have been engaged with DfI officials throughout the Strategy development process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what steps he is taking to improve the availability of skilled staff across all industries where need exists.
answer_text: My Department published the new Skills Strategy ‘Skills for a 10x Economy’ in March 2022 which focuses on three major policy objectives to boost the supply of skills that will be required to support our economic ambitions over the next decade, namely:Addressing skills imbalances, driving economic growth;Creating a culture of lifelong learning; andEnhancing digital skills, developing our digital spine. When complete, ‘Skills for a 10x Economy’ will help ensure that Northern Ireland’s citizens and the business community have access to modern, economically relevant skills that will empower them to reach their full potential and improve our competitiveness on the global stage.The establishment of the Skills Council provides a forum where stakeholders will discuss the key challenges and priorities for the skills system, providing strategic advice on the implementation of the Skills Strategy. Collaborative work between government Departments will be crucial, alongside engagement with local government to ensure the necessary support for individuals.  My Department is working collaboratively developing labour market partnerships with the Department for Communities and enhancing digital skills and developing our digital spine with the Department of Education.		The Skills Strategy also highlights the need for a for a full review of investment in the skills system and recommends a ring-fenced skills fund to provide new opportunites in the labour market and and support skills development.	As an example of Departmental provision, in 2021/22, 53 Assured Skills academies were launched, including sixteen academies in the IT sector, nine in Financial Services, eleven in Business, one in Legal and sixteen in Manufacturing & Engineering. To date 43 academies have completed, resulting in 625 people being upskilled, and 552 of those gaining full time employment with participating companies.'

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for her assessment of the football training facilities at Portrush Football Club Youth’s Parker Avenue site; and (ii) to detail any conversations she has had with stakeholders at the club and the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council regarding facilitating improvements.
answer_text: My officials have engaged with a range of stakeholders, including Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council, to identify the current and future needs for soccer facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the efficacy of the Rural Needs Act; and (ii) to outline the cross-departmental work that is carried out when decisions are being made on (a) health care; (b) education; (c) childcare; and (d) transport in rural areas.
answer_text: The Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 (the Act) aims to ensure that public authorities consider the particular needs of people in rural areas in their decision making processes and to increase transparency on how rural needs have been considered. The Department published the 4th Rural Needs Annual Monitoring Report in December 2021 which contains information on how public authorities have had due regard to rural needs when exercising their functions. This report demonstrates that the Act is now embedded within public authorities and that public authorities are undertaking Rural Needs Impact Assessments routinely. The report also provides stakeholders with greater transparency on how public authorities have considered rural needs in their decision making processes. Responsibility for decisions on health care, education, childcare and transport in rural areas lies with the Minister in the department with statutory responsibility for the particular matter and the Rural Needs Impact Assessment process requires departments to takes steps to identify the needs of people in rural areas which are relevant to the decision and to give appropriate consideration to those needs in the decision making process. My Department continues to work with other public authorities to help ensure that the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 is implemented effectively.My Department also works in partnership with other public authorities to deliver a range of measures under  its Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework including the Farm Families Health Checks programme which is delivered in partnership with the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme which is delivered in partnership with the Department for Infrastructure and the Rural Community Transport Partnerships across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any work she has done to explore ways of releasing emergency support funding for people struggling to pay energy bills.
answer_text: I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents measures being introduced.However, I am committed to continuing to support individuals, families and communities who are experiencing real hardship. To giving them the helping hand they need to heat their homes, to put a meal on the table.I am determined that my department will continue to deliver for all those who are in need.I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all of the options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the ways in which he will help farmers diversify their businesses in order to improve sustainability.
answer_text: My Department is designing a more tailored agricultural policy, which better meets the needs of Northern Ireland. We have developed a Future Agricultural Policy Framework Portfolio that is based on four key outcomes of increased productivity, environmental sustainability, improved resilience and an effective functioning supply chain, alongside measured transition to new schemes.  Following a period of public consultation, on 24 March, I announced 54 policy decisions on Future Agricultural Policy for Northern Ireland.  These policy decisions will be followed as soon as possible with new policies and systems to ensure that we will have a future sustainable agricultural industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his plans to create a banned offenders register to prevent people found to have committed acts of animal cruelty from owning or working with animals.
answer_text: Significant work has been taken forward by my Department to explore the feasibility of creating a register. Following extensive consideration of the relevant legal and financial considerations, establishing a publically accessible register is considered untenable.Instead, the Department is developing guidance which will encourage animal rehoming organisations to require prospective animal owners to disclose the results of Access NI checks before transferring ownership to them. This will ensure that these entities do not place animals with those convicted of animal cruelty offences. The Department is also investigating whether there is potential to further enhance information sharing between animal welfare enforcement organisations and whether there is any way of augmenting the checks that are already carried out to ensure that there is compliance with court imposed bans on the owning or keeping of animals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the ways in which he will improve business connectivity and infrastructure.
answer_text: Business infrastructure/connectivity is a complex landscape referring to a wide range of facilities and services including transport infrastructure, broadband, networking, business services, energy and technology. Project Stratum, funded under the DUP/Conservative Confidence and Supply Deal, represents a total investment of almost £200million that is transforming the broadband connectivity bringing gigabit capable service to 85,000 premises in rural areas right across Northern Ireland. Plans associated with Department’s rollout of future broadband connectivity initiatives, in line with UK Government policy, will be announced in the coming months. A strategic vision has been set out in the 10X Economic Vision that focuses on Northern Ireland’s strengths in priority technologies and clusters where we can be world leaders; investing in and supporting our people; diffusing innovation across our economy; taking a new approach to funding; and focusing and capitalising on what makes Northern Ireland unique. A series of announcements have been made including the NI City and Growth Deal programme will unlock £1.5billion of investment from the public and private sectors, £170million of funding for three cutting edge innovation projects at Queen’s University Belfast, a multimillion pound project focussing on creative industries, data analytics, robotics and digital health lead by Ulster University.  Having produced an energy strategy, The Path to Net Zero, and associated 2022 Action Plan which sets out a vision to deliver self-sufficiency in affordable renewable energy for Northern Ireland. These actions will grow and evolve our local economy to the new and emerging challenges Northern Ireland faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he has taken to improve care and outcomes for patients who have a brain injury.
answer_text: The Specialist Regional Acquired Brain Injury In-patient Unit (RABIU) is located at Musgrave Park Hospital and serves all of Northern Ireland. RABIU works closely with the Multi-disciplinary Brain Injury Teams within each of the five HSC Trusts to provide rehabilitation and support following a period of in-patient treatment.My Department also holds contracts with community and voluntary sector organisations to provide ongoing support to people with an acquired brain injury.  Currently there are two regional contracts; one is to provide vocational training and support to people with an acquired brain injury and the other is to provide recreational support to people and their carers.   My Department has a close working relationship with all the main brain injury organisations in Northern Ireland who help to raise awareness of acquired brain injury. Recently, during acquired brain injury week from 9-15 May, a number of events were organised and videos posted on social media to raise awareness.In addition, the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) England is leading on the development of an Acquired Brain Injury Cross-Government Strategy. My Department and the HSC Trusts will respond to their call for evidence which closes on 6 June at 12.45pm. The call for evidence can be accessed via this link:https://www.gov.uk/government/consultations/acquired-brain-injury-call-for-evidence

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of foster carers, broken down by constituency in each of the last five years; and (ii) for his assessment of the trend of the prevalence of foster carers.
answer_text: The number of foster carers in Northern Ireland is routinely collected at Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust level only; therefore, to obtain this information by constituency would be at a disproportionate cost. The number of foster carers in Northern Ireland, by HSC Trust, is set out in Table 1. Table 1: Number of foster carers in Northern Ireland by HSC Trust (2017-2021) HSC TrustYear ending 31 March20172018201920202021Belfast403449482472572Northern483444467465485South Eastern302311388383334Southern380417438420474Western431468517479563Northern Ireland1,9992,0892,2922,2192,428 Source: Health and Social Care Board, Delegated Statutory Function/Corporate Parenting Returns.The data in Table 1 refers to kinship and non-kinship foster carers who have been approved by a panel, as well as specialist fee-paid foster carers.  In addition, at 31 March 2021 there were 211 foster carers who had been approved by independent foster agencies; 45 specialist foster carers who provide care only to children with a disability; and 212 carers who were undergoing a process of being assessed as kinship carers.  (ii) At 31 March 2021, there were 3,530 children in care, of whom 2,857 (81%) were in foster care.  From 31 March 2017 to 31 March 2021, the number of children in care increased by 18%.   Over the same period, the number of HSC Trust-approved foster carers increased by 21%.  Although it may seem that the number of foster carers is keeping pace with the number of children who require to be cared for, it is notable that—while the number of kinship carers has increased by 46% between March 2017 and March 2021—the number of non-kinship foster carers has increased by only 8% over the same period.  It is essential that the Health and Social Care system continues to provide support to existing carers and encourage much-needed new carers to come forward.  Efforts to recruit and retain foster carers are led by the regional HSCNI Adoption and Foster Care service.  In addition to ongoing recruitment activity throughout the year, my Department has also lent support to a number of targeted campaigns, most recently an appeal for foster carers and supported lodgings hosts for young refugees.  In addition, to help support foster carers and sustain foster placements, £1.39m of Departmental funding was made available to foster carers over two 12 week periods in 2020/21.  In March this year, I also announced an additional support package for foster carers; this was backdated to cover a 12 week period from January 2022 to March 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on efforts to provide timely MOT testing slots to motorists in need of one; and (ii) to detail the number of motorists currently driving with expired MOTs.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.Official vehicle testing statistics are published on a monthly basis and can be accessed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-and-vehicle-agency-monthly-tests-conducted-statistics.  The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The DVA does not currently hold information to enable it to accurately report on the number of motorists currently driving with expired MOTs.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of volunteers in Northern Ireland, broken down by constituency; and (ii) the support she is giving, and plans to give, to assist these volunteers.
answer_text: Using the data gathered in the continuous Household Survey 2019/20, my Department has concluded that approximately 28% of the population aged sixteen and over regularly volunteer. In addition a further survey completed in 2019 showed that 49% young people had volunteered with clubs, campaigns or organisations.While unable to provide a breakdown of volunteers at a constituency level the table below provides a percentage of the adult population aged sixteen and over who volunteered within each council area during the year 2019/20.District Council AreaCarried out any voluntary work - 12 months 2019/20%Antrim and Newtownabbey32Ards and North Down39Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon27Belfast City24Causeway Coast and Glens20Derry City and Strabane36Fermanagh and Omagh27Lisburn and Castlereagh City31Mid and East Antrim32Mid Ulster28Newry, Mourne and Down21The Department provides funding to six local Volunteer Centres and to Volunteer Now under the Volunteering Infrastructure Support Programme.		The Department also provides funding to three sporting bodies – Ulster Gaelic Athletic Association, Irish Rugby Football Union, Ulster Branch, and the Irish Football Association to work in partnership to support volunteering through sport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she is taking to tackle the current housing crisis, particularly in areas of East Londonderry.
answer_text: I refer you to the response to AQW 15816/17-22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the issues that have caused the delay to phase two of the £500 COVID recognition payment for health and social care workers; and (ii) for an update on when these workers will receive this payment.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been temporarily paused to allow officials to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23 therefore I am not in a position to provide a date as to when all payments will be made.I can confirm that as at 11 May 2022, a total of over £16.6m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,314 individuals. As at the same date £2.1m has been paid out to 5,027 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £300k has been paid to 863 individuals. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment will be paid to independent sector staff as soon as it is practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the current debt levels of schools across Northern Ireland; and (ii) to outline the current policy on schools taking on debt.
answer_text: As at 31 March 2021, being the latest date for which finalised data is available, the value of accumulated deficits held by Controlled and Maintained schools was £87.3 million (across 478 schools). Similar data is not available for Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated schools, as these schools are separate bodies and their financial positions are reported in the individual schools’ annual accounts. The level of schools’ accumulated deficits evidences the long-term funding squeeze on the education budget, which reduced by £229m (12%) in real terms between 2009-10 and 2019-20.  £148.4m of additional funding for the schools’ delegated budget across the 2020-21 and 2021-22 financial years was secured.  Despite this welcome increase in schools funding, a gap clearly remains.  It should also be noted that this funding shortfall is against a backdrop of a 7% increase in pupil numbers. Without further significant additional funding, the budget positon for our schools will continue to be extremely challenging due to the substantial cost and demand pressures which the sector now faces. (ii) Guidance on Financial and Management Arrangements for Controlled and Maintained Schools Funded Under the Common Funding Scheme outlines the policy on Controlled and Maintained schools going into deficit.  These schools must seek permission from the Education Authority (EA) prior to incurring a deficit. In relation to Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated schools, these schools must engage with the Department and obtain permission before entering into any long-term loan arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what advice can he offer to people with rising energy costs, including electricity; (ii) what steps he is taking to mitigate these costs; (iii) to outline the process for people who cannot afford to pay their energy bills; and (iv) what conversations has he had with energy companies to provide support to their customers.
answer_text: Significant increases and ongoing volatility in international wholesale fuel prices witnessed across the globe have continued to impact negatively on our electricity and gas markets. Directly impacting consumers across Northern Ireland, these high prices are forecast to continue for some time with further increases a real possibility.I would encourage any customer that is struggling to pay their energy bill, or is in debt, to contact their supplier for help. Through their regulated licence conditions, energy suppliers should support their customers by working with them to develop a manageable repayment plan; providing additional protection to consumers who are of pensionable age, disabled or chronically ill; and providing energy efficiency advice which may reduce their usage and therefore their overall bill. The Department for Communities (DfC) has lead responsibility for fuel poverty and any mitigations that may be put in place to assist with rising fuel costs. There are a number of government support schemes and independent advice agencies that can provide consumers with additional help. This information can be found on NI Direct.The Utility Regulator (UR) is responsible for regulating gas tariffs charged by the dominant suppliers in the Greater Belfast and Ten Towns license areas and also the largest electricity supplier in Northern Ireland. The UR scrutinises the energy supplier submissions in relation to price changes and ensures that customers pay no more than the efficient costs of purchasing and supplying the energy plus an agreed profit margin set by the UR. The Department has an oversight role in this process. The new Energy Strategy sets out the intention to introduce minimum standards for the energy efficiency of buildings and to substantially increase funding and support for retrofitting buildings. This will help consumers make changes that reduce their energy use and subsequently the amount they need to spend on energy. I am also planning to have a round table meeting with energy companies in the coming weeks to discuss rising energy prices for domestic consumers in the context of affordability issues leading into next winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for her assessment of whether pupil numbers are expected to increase in the coming years; and (ii) how this forecast aligns with current and planned school closures.
answer_text: (i) My Department produces school population projections each year and these estimate that pupil numbers are expected to decrease by 2030/31.  These projections are guided by birth and population projections produced by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency as well as input from Policy Branches within the Department of Education.  (ii) Planning of education provision is a matter for the Education Authority in liaison with the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools and representatives of the other school sectors.  My Department is engaged in this process only at the point where a Development Proposal (DP) is published.  The Sustainable Schools Policy describes various solutions available, to be explored with a view to ensuring there is a viable and sustainable level of quality educational provision in an area.  While closure may be the only realistic option to ensure that the educational interests of children are addressed in a sustainable setting, I have set out my expectation in a written statement to the Assembly that the planning authorities will develop planning options and bring forward innovative, shared and creative solutions that provide sustainable provision in communities.  A full list of published DPs currently under consideration by my Department is accessible at the following link:  Current development proposals | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)My written statement to the Assembly is accessible at: Written Ministerial Statement - Setting the Priorities for the Next Regi....pdf (education-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps are being made to use digital technology to mitigate the disruption to learning caused by temporary school closures as a result of staff absences.
answer_text: School is clearly the most appropriate setting for our children and young people to learn and they benefit most from face to face teaching in the classroom.  While the scale of staff absences due to COVID 19 had placed significant pressure on schools to maintain face-to-face teaching, remote learning ensured that our children and young people were able to continue with their education.  Remote learning is now only being implemented in exceptional circumstances.  My Department carries out a weekly survey to assess the level of remote learning, and from the responses received each week, it is encouraging to note that the percentage of schools using remote learning has reduced from 50% in January to 7.34% in week commencing 16 May.Throughout the period of the COVID-19 pandemic, my Department made a significant investment in the provision of digital technology and IT equipment to enhance remote learning.  Almost 25,000 new devices were procured and provided to schools to support, in particular, our most disadvantaged and vulnerable learners. Schools were informed by the Education Authority (EA) that they could retain these devices for the 2021/22 academic year to provide continued support to disadvantaged and vulnerable children.  My Department also invested £1 million during 2021/22 to purchase digital devices for children from more disadvantaged backgrounds.  This has been used by the EA to purchase 1,400 iPads, 1,400 Chromebooks and 1,400 Windows Laptops.  This investment will continue during the current and future financial years.  This significant and ongoing investment will ensure children from poorer backgrounds have improved access to digital devices.  Through C2k, the EA delivers a comprehensive range of digital tools to support teaching and learning.  Platforms such as G Suite, MS Teams and Collaborate Ultra are readily available to support learning.  As part of the Continuity of Learning programme, additional training was provided to teachers in relation to both the functionality of the platforms and how they are used to improve learning and teaching.Since 2020, £7m has been allocated to provide devices, enhance broadband, upgrade existing platforms on C2k and procure new applications, including applications to support communications between school and home.
